topics.title: Wave Shaping Circuits - First Order RC Low Pass and High Pass Filters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET302 - ELECTRONIC DEVICES AND CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Wave Shaping Circuits - First Order RC Low Pass and High Pass Filters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 First-Order RC Filters as Wave Shapers

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme – PCAET302 (Electronic Devices and Circuits)**, **Wave Shaping Circuits** are a class of linear networks that alter the shape of an input waveform to produce a desired output waveform. When constructed using a single resistor and a single capacitor, they are called **First-Order RC Filters** because their governing differential equation is of the **first order**.

A first-order RC circuit has two fundamental configurations:

1. **RC Low Pass Filter (LPF)** — also called an **Integrator** when the time constant is much larger than the pulse width.
2. **RC High Pass Filter (HPF)** — also called a **Differentiator** when the time constant is much smaller than the pulse width.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> The student is expected to analyse the **transient response** of first-order RC circuits to standard inputs such as the **step input** and the **square wave input**, and to compute the output amplitude at switching instants, percentage tilt (sag), and rise time.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology):**
> A **First-Order RC Low Pass Filter** is a linear, time-invariant network in which the output is taken across the capacitor, whose transfer function attenuates high-frequency components while passing low-frequency components with unity gain (in the passband).
>
> A **First-Order RC High Pass Filter** is a linear, time-invariant network in which the output is taken across the resistor, whose transfer function attenuates low-frequency (DC) components while passing high-frequency components with unity gain (in the passband).

The classification is governed by a single design parameter: the **Time Constant (τ)** of the circuit, defined as:

$$\tau = R \cdot C \quad \text{(seconds)}$$

The **Cut-off Frequency** ($f_c$ or $f_{-3dB}$) is the frequency at which the output power drops to half (−3 dB) of its passband value:

$$f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi R C} = \frac{1}{2\pi \tau} \quad \text{(Hertz)}$$

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogies

### The Water-Tank Analogy for the Low Pass Filter
Imagine a large water tank connected to a thin pipe. A tap pours water (input) into the tank; the water level inside the tank is the **output**. If you pour water **slowly and steadily**, the tank fills smoothly — slow signals are **passed**. If you splash water in rapid bursts, the water level barely ripples because the tank's inertia smooths out the bursts — fast signals are **attenuated**. This is exactly what the capacitor does to voltage: it **stores charge and resists rapid change**.

### The Spring-Bumper Analogy for the High Pass Filter
Now imagine a lightweight ball (the capacitor) pressed against a spring-loaded bumper. If you push the ball very **slowly**, the spring stretches and the bumper (the resistor) hardly moves — slow signals produce **tiny output**. If you tap the ball **sharply**, the spring cannot move quickly, so the bumper jerks forward with a big spike and then settles back — fast signals produce a **big, sharp output that decays rapidly**. This is precisely the behaviour of a high pass filter: it **rejects DC and slow ramps, but responds violently to sudden jumps**.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid:** *"Capacitor opposes sudden change in voltage across itself, but lets current pass through easily when voltage changes rapidly."* — This single line explains the entire behaviour of LPF and HPF.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics Used in This Module

| Symbol | Quantity | Unit | Typical KTU Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| $R$ | Resistance | Ohms ($\Omega$) | $1\,\text{k}\Omega$ to $1\,\text{M}\Omega$ |
| $C$ | Capacitance | Farads (F) | $1\,\text{nF}$ to $10\,\mu\text{F}$ |
| $\tau$ | Time Constant | Seconds (s) | $\mu\text{s}$ to ms |
| $f_c$ | Cut-off Frequency | Hertz (Hz) | Hz to MHz |
| $V_m$ | Peak Input Voltage | Volts (V) | $1\,\text{V}$ to $20\,\text{V}$ |
| $T$ | Period of Square Wave | Seconds (s) | $\mu\text{s}$ to ms |
| $T_{1/2}$ | Half Period (T/2) | Seconds (s) | $\mu\text{s}$ to ms |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The dimensionless ratio $T/\tau$ (or equivalently $T_1/\tau$ where $T_1 = T/2$) completely determines the **shape** of the output waveform for a square-wave input. This is the single most tested concept in KTU Module 1.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 RC Low Pass Filter — Frequency and Time Domain Behaviour

### Circuit Configuration
The input $V_{in}(t)$ is applied across the **series combination** of $R$ and $C$, and the output $V_o(t)$ is taken **across the capacitor** $C$.

### Transfer Function
Using the **voltage divider rule** in the s-domain:

$$H(s) = \frac{V_o(s)}{V_{in}(s)} = \frac{1/sC}{R + 1/sC} = \frac{1}{1 + sRC}$$

Substituting $s = j\omega$ for sinusoidal steady state:

$$H(j\omega) = \frac{1}{1 + j\omega RC}$$

### Magnitude and Phase
$$\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}}$$

$$\angle H(j\omega) = -\tan^{-1}(\omega RC)$$

**Behavioural Rules (must memorise for KTU):**
- At $\omega = 0$ (DC): $\vert H \vert = 1$ (full pass)
- At $\omega = \omega_c = 1/RC$: $\vert H \vert = 1/\sqrt{2} = 0.707$ (the **−3 dB point**)
- At $\omega \to \infty$: $\vert H \vert \to 0$ (full attenuation)

---

## 2.2 RC High Pass Filter — Frequency and Time Domain Behaviour

### Circuit Configuration
The input is across the series $R$–$C$, and the output is taken **across the resistor** $R$.

### Transfer Function
$$H(s) = \frac{V_o(s)}{V_{in}(s)} = \frac{R}{R + 1/sC} = \frac{sRC}{1 + sRC}$$

$$H(j\omega) = \frac{j\omega RC}{1 + j\omega RC}$$

### Magnitude and Phase
$$\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \frac{\omega RC}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}}$$

$$\angle H(j\omega) = 90^\circ - \tan^{-1}(\omega RC)$$

**Behavioural Rules (must memorise for KTU):**
- At $\omega = 0$ (DC): $\vert H \vert = 0$ (fully blocked)
- At $\omega = \omega_c$: $\vert H \vert = 1/\sqrt{2}$ (the −3 dB point)
- At $\omega \to \infty$: $\vert H \vert \to 1$ (full pass)

---

## 2.3 Step Response of the Low Pass Filter (Charging Transient)

For an input step of magnitude $V_m$ applied at $t = 0$, the capacitor voltage rises **exponentially**:

$$V_o(t) = V_m \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$$

where $\tau = RC$. Key checkpoints:
- At $t = 0$: $V_o = 0$ (capacitor initially uncharged)
- At $t = \tau$: $V_o = 0.632\,V_m$ (63.2% of final value)
- At $t = 5\tau$: $V_o \approx 0.993\,V_m$ (effectively fully charged — KTU "5τ rule")
- At $t = 2.2\tau$: $V_o \approx 0.9\,V_m$ (used in **rise time** calculation: $t_r = 2.2\tau$)

---

## 2.4 Step Response of the High Pass Filter

The capacitor voltage cannot change instantaneously. With $V_C(0^-) = 0$, the output (resistor voltage) for a step $V_m u(t)$ is:

$$V_o(t) = V_m \, e^{-t/\tau}$$

Key checkpoints:
- At $t = 0^+$: $V_o = V_m$ (the full step appears momentarily, since the capacitor is a short at the instant of switching)
- At $t = \tau$: $V_o = 0.368\,V_m$
- At $t = 5\tau$: $V_o \approx 0$ (the output decays back to zero)

---

## 2.5 Square-Wave Response — The KTU High-Yield Topic

### Case 1: Low Pass Filter with Square Wave Input
Define the dimensionless ratio $k = T_1/\tau$, where $T_1 = T/2$ is the half-period.

| Condition | Behaviour | KTU Terminology |
|---|---|---|
| $T_1 \gg \tau$ (i.e., $k \gg 1$) | Capacitor charges/discharges almost completely; output ≈ square wave | **Faithful reproduction** |
| $T_1 = \tau$ (i.e., $k = 1$) | Partial charging; exponential rise and fall visible | **General transient case** |
| $T_1 \ll \tau$ (i.e., $k \ll 1$) | Output is a triangular wave (linear rise and fall) | **Integrator** |

### Case 2: High Pass Filter with Square Wave Input

| Condition | Behaviour | KTU Terminology |
|---|---|---|
| $T_1 \gg \tau$ (i.e., $k \gg 1$) | Capacitor fully charges; output ≈ zero between transitions (only spikes) | **Differentiator** (spiky output) |
| $T_1 = \tau$ (i.e., $k = 1$) | Exponential decay visible between transitions | **General transient case** |
| $T_1 \ll \tau$ (i.e., $k \ll 1$) | Output ≈ square wave (faithful reproduction, DC removed) | **AC-coupled square wave** |

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Mandatory Memorisation Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains every equation that has appeared, or is highly likely to appear, in KTU 2024 Scheme Module 1 questions on this topic.

| # | Concept | Governing Equation | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Time constant | $\tau = RC$ | seconds |
| 2 | Cut-off frequency | $f_c = 1/(2\pi RC)$ | Hz |
| 3 | LPF transfer magnitude | $\vert H \vert = 1/\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}$ | dimensionless |
| 4 | HPF transfer magnitude | $\vert H \vert = \omega RC/\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}$ | dimensionless |
| 5 | LPF step response | $V_o(t) = V_m(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$ | volts |
| 6 | HPF step response | $V_o(t) = V_m\,e^{-t/\tau}$ | volts |
| 7 | Rise time (10%–90%) | $t_r = 2.2\,\tau$ | seconds |
| 8 | Sag (HPF square wave) | $\text{Sag}\% = \frac{\vert V_1 - V_2 \vert}{V_1} \times 100$ | percent |
| 9 | Tilt (HPF square wave) | $\text{Tilt}\% = \frac{V_m - V_2}{V_m} \times 100$ | percent |
| 10 | Output at $t = T_1$ (LPF charging) | $V_o(T_1) = V_m(1 - e^{-T_1/\tau})$ | volts |
| 11 | Output at $t = T_1$ (HPF decaying) | $V_o(T_1) = V_m\,e^{-T_1/\tau}$ | volts |
| 12 | Steady-state HPF square output | $V_o(0^+) = V_m(1 - e^{-T_1/\tau})$ | volts |
| 13 | Steady-state HPF min output | $V_o(T_1) = -V_m(1 - e^{-T_1/\tau})$ | volts |
| 14 | Differentiation condition | $T_1 \ll \tau$ ($\tau \ll T_1$ typo-safe) | dimensionless |
| 15 | Integration condition | $T_1 \ll \tau$ | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** First-order RC filters are the building blocks of every **pulse-shaping network**, **anti-aliasing filter** in data acquisition, **differentiation circuit** in FM demodulators, **integration circuit** in analog computers, and the **AC-coupling network** that removes DC bias in oscilloscope probes. Mastering these circuits is essential for both KTU examinations and any subsequent VLSI/Embedded/Communication course.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Transfer Function of the RC Low Pass Filter

### Given
Series combination of $R$ and $C$ with $V_{in}$ applied across the series pair and $V_o$ taken across the capacitor $C$.

### Step 1 — Apply KVL around the loop
$$V_{in}(t) = V_R(t) + V_C(t)$$

The current through the series branch is the same for both elements:

$$i(t) = C\,\frac{dV_C(t)}{dt}$$

The resistor voltage is:

$$V_R(t) = i(t)\,R = RC\,\frac{dV_C(t)}{dt}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute back into KVL
$$V_{in}(t) = RC\,\frac{dV_C(t)}{dt} + V_C(t)$$

Since the output is the capacitor voltage, $V_o(t) = V_C(t)$:

$$V_{in}(t) = RC\,\frac{dV_o(t)}{dt} + V_o(t)$$

### Step 3 — Take the Laplace transform (zero initial conditions)
$$V_{in}(s) = RC\,s\,V_o(s) + V_o(s) = V_o(s)(1 + sRC)$$

### Step 4 — Solve for the transfer function
$$H(s) = \frac{V_o(s)}{V_{in}(s)} = \frac{1}{1 + sRC}$$

Substituting $s = j\omega$:

$$H(j\omega) = \frac{1}{1 + j\omega RC} = \frac{1}{1 + j2\pi f RC}$$

This is the canonical **first-order low pass transfer function**. The magnitude and phase are:

$$\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}}$$

$$\phi(\omega) = -\tan^{-1}(\omega RC)$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Step Response of the RC Low Pass Filter

### Given
Input is a step of magnitude $V_m$ applied at $t = 0$, with the capacitor initially uncharged: $V_C(0^-) = 0$.

### Step 1 — Write the input
$$V_{in}(t) = V_m\,u(t)$$

### Step 2 — Set up the ODE
Using the result of Section 3.1:

$$RC\,\frac{dV_o(t)}{dt} + V_o(t) = V_m \quad \text{for } t \geq 0$$

### Step 3 — Solve the homogeneous part
The characteristic equation is:

$$RC\,s + 1 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad s = -\frac{1}{RC}$$

So the homogeneous (natural) response is:

$$V_{o,h}(t) = K\,e^{-t/RC}$$

### Step 4 — Find the particular (forced) solution
Since the input is a constant $V_m$, the particular solution is a constant:

$$V_{o,p}(t) = V_m$$

### Step 5 — Write the total solution
$$V_o(t) = V_{o,p}(t) + V_{o,h}(t) = V_m + K\,e^{-t/RC}$$

### Step 6 — Apply the initial condition
At $t = 0^+$, the capacitor voltage is zero:

$$V_o(0^+) = V_m + K = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad K = -V_m$$

### Step 7 — Final step response
$$\boxed{V_o(t) = V_m \left(1 - e^{-t/RC}\right)}$$

### Step 8 — Verification at standard checkpoints
- $t = 0$: $V_o = V_m(1 - 1) = 0$ ✓
- $t = \tau$: $V_o = V_m(1 - e^{-1}) = 0.632\,V_m$ ✓
- $t = 5\tau$: $V_o = V_m(1 - e^{-5}) \approx 0.9933\,V_m$ ✓
- $t \to \infty$: $V_o \to V_m$ ✓

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Step Response of the RC High Pass Filter

### Given
Input step $V_m u(t)$ applied across the series $R$–$C$, output is the resistor voltage.

### Step 1 — Apply the continuity of capacitor voltage
The voltage across a capacitor cannot change instantaneously, so:

$$V_C(0^-) = V_C(0^+) = 0$$

### Step 2 — Apply KVL at $t = 0^+$
$$V_m = V_R(0^+) + V_C(0^+) = V_R(0^+) + 0$$

Therefore:
$$V_o(0^+) = V_m$$

### Step 3 — Set up the ODE for $t \geq 0$
For the HPF, $V_o = V_R$ and $V_C = V_{in} - V_o$:

$$i(t) = C\,\frac{dV_C(t)}{dt} = C\,\frac{d}{dt}[V_{in}(t) - V_o(t)] = -C\,\frac{dV_o(t)}{dt}$$

The resistor voltage is:

$$V_o(t) = i(t)\,R = -RC\,\frac{dV_o(t)}{dt}$$

This gives the ODE:

$$RC\,\frac{dV_o(t)}{dt} + V_o(t) = 0 \quad \text{for } t \geq 0$$

### Step 4 — Solve with initial condition $V_o(0^+) = V_m$
The general solution is $V_o(t) = A\,e^{-t/RC}$. Using the initial condition:

$$A = V_m$$

### Step 5 — Final step response
$$\boxed{V_o(t) = V_m\,e^{-t/RC}}$$

### Step 6 — Interpretation
The output jumps instantly to $V_m$ at $t = 0$, then decays exponentially to zero. The capacitor "absorbs" the step voltage over time, leaving no voltage across the resistor in steady state — exactly the DC-blocking behaviour expected of a high pass filter.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Steady-State Square-Wave Response of the RC Low Pass Filter (General Case)

### Given
Square wave of amplitude $\pm V_m$ and half-period $T_1 = T/2$ applied to the LPF. Find the steady-state output at the two switching instants.

### Step 1 — Define the switching instants
Let the square wave switch from $-V_m$ to $+V_m$ at $t = 0$. The output was at some value $V_2$ just before switching, and will rise to $V_1$ just before the next switching at $t = T_1$.

### Step 2 — Write the charging equation
For $0 \leq t \leq T_1$, the input is $+V_m$ and the output charges from $V_2$ toward $+V_m$:

$$V_o(t) = V_m + (V_2 - V_m)\,e^{-t/RC}$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate at $t = T_1$ (the peak value $V_1$)
$$V_1 = V_m + (V_2 - V_m)\,e^{-T_1/RC} = V_m(1 - e^{-T_1/RC}) + V_2\,e^{-T_1/RC}$$

### Step 4 — Apply the symmetry of the square wave
In **steady state**, by symmetry the negative excursion reaches $-V_1$:

$$V_2 = -V_1$$

### Step 5 — Solve for $V_1$
Substituting $V_2 = -V_1$ into the equation of Step 3:

$$V_1 = V_m(1 - e^{-T_1/RC}) - V_1\,e^{-T_1/RC}$$

$$V_1 + V_1\,e^{-T_1/RC} = V_m(1 - e^{-T_1/RC})$$

$$V_1(1 + e^{-T_1/RC}) = V_m(1 - e^{-T_1/RC})$$

$$\boxed{V_1 = V_m \cdot \frac{1 - e^{-T_1/RC}}{1 + e^{-T_1/RC}} = V_m \cdot \tanh\!\left(\frac{T_1}{2RC}\right)}$$

### Step 6 — Identify the three regimes
- **If $T_1 \gg RC$** ($e^{-T_1/RC} \to 0$): $V_1 \to V_m$ → **faithful reproduction** (output is square)
- **If $T_1 \ll RC$** (use $e^{-x} \approx 1 - x$ for small $x$):

$$V_1 \approx V_m \cdot \frac{1 - (1 - T_1/RC)}{1 + (1 - T_1/RC)} = V_m \cdot \frac{T_1/RC}{2 - T_1/RC} \approx \frac{V_m T_1}{2RC}$$

The output is **linear** (triangular) with peak $\dfrac{V_m T_1}{2RC}$ — this is the **integrator** regime.

- **If $T_1 = RC$**: $V_1 = V_m \cdot \dfrac{1 - e^{-1}}{1 + e^{-1}} = V_m \cdot \dfrac{1 - 0.368}{1 + 0.368} = V_m \cdot \dfrac{0.632}{1.368} \approx 0.462\,V_m$

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Steady-State Square-Wave Response of the RC High Pass Filter

### Given
Square wave of amplitude $\pm V_m$ and half-period $T_1$ applied to the HPF. Find the output at the switching instants and compute **Tilt/Sag**.

### Step 1 — Switching event at $t = 0$
Just before switching, $V_C(0^-) = V_C^{(-)}$ (some steady-state value). The capacitor voltage cannot change instantly, so:

$$V_C(0^+) = V_C^{(-)}$$

### Step 2 — Apply KVL at $t = 0^+$
$$V_o(0^+) = V_{in}(0^+) - V_C(0^+) = V_m - V_C^{(-)}$$

In **steady state**, due to symmetry, the capacitor charges to $V_C^{(-)} = -V_2$, where $V_2$ is the most negative output value reached at $t = T_1$. (We will derive this below.)

### Step 3 — Decay during $0 \leq t \leq T_1$
For $t \geq 0^+$, the input is fixed at $+V_m$, and the output decays exponentially toward zero (the asymptotic value for a constant input) but the capacitor charges toward $V_m$, so the output tends to zero.

Using the general solution of the HPF step response with initial value $V_1 = V_o(0^+)$:

$$V_o(t) = V_1\,e^{-t/RC}$$

### Step 4 — Evaluate at $t = T_1$
$$V_o(T_1) = V_1\,e^{-T_1/RC}$$

But this is the value of the output **just before** the input switches to $-V_m$ at $t = T_1$. By symmetry of the square wave in steady state, this value equals the negative of the value just after the **previous** switch, so $V_o(T_1) = -V_2$ where $V_2$ is the most negative value.

### Step 5 — Switching event at $t = T_1$
The input jumps from $+V_m$ to $-V_m$. The capacitor voltage is still $V_C(T_1^+) = V_C(T_1^-)$. The new output is:

$$V_o(T_1^+) = -V_m - V_C(T_1^-) = -V_m - V_1\,e^{-T_1/RC} - V_m$$

Wait — we need to express $V_C(T_1^-)$ in terms of $V_1$. Since $V_C = V_{in} - V_o = V_m - V_o$ during $0 \leq t \leq T_1$:

$$V_C(T_1^-) = V_m - V_1\,e^{-T_1/RC}$$

Therefore:

$$V_o(T_1^+) = -V_m - (V_m - V_1\,e^{-T_1/RC}) = -2V_m + V_1\,e^{-T_1/RC}$$

This $V_o(T_1^+)$ equals $-V_1$ by symmetry (the same positive jump occurs every half cycle), so:

$$-V_1 = -2V_m + V_1\,e^{-T_1/RC}$$

$$2V_m = V_1 + V_1\,e^{-T_1/RC} = V_1(1 + e^{-T_1/RC})$$

$$\boxed{V_1 = \frac{2V_m}{1 + e^{-T_1/RC}} = \frac{V_m}{\cosh(T_1/(2RC))}}$$

And the minimum value:

$$\boxed{V_2 = -V_1 = -\frac{2V_m}{1 + e^{-T_1/RC}}}$$

### Step 6 — Calculate Percentage Tilt (Sag)
The **tilt** of the exponential decay from $V_1$ down to $V_2 + V_m$ over the half period is:

$$\text{Tilt}\% = \frac{V_1 - (V_2 + V_m)}{V_1} \times 100 = \frac{V_1 - (V_m - V_1)}{V_1} \times 100 = \frac{2V_1 - V_m}{V_1} \times 100$$

Substituting $V_1 = \dfrac{2V_m}{1 + e^{-T_1/RC}}$:

$$\text{Tilt}\% = \left(2 - \frac{1 + e^{-T_1/RC}}{2}\right) \times 100 = \left(\frac{3 - e^{-T_1/RC}}{2}\right) \times 100$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Numerical Trick:** When $T_1 \ll RC$, use $e^{-T_1/RC} \approx 1 - T_1/RC$, giving:
> $$\text{Tilt}\% \approx \frac{T_1}{RC} \times 50\% = \frac{\pi f}{RC} \times 50\% \approx \frac{T_1}{2RC} \times 100\%$$
> This linear approximation is what the examiner expects when $T_1/RC < 0.1$.

### Step 7 — Three regimes
- **If $T_1 \gg RC$**: $V_1 \to V_m$ but tilt → 100% (output is **spiky** — true differentiator)
- **If $T_1 \ll RC$**: $V_1 \to V_m$, $V_2 \to -V_m$, tilt → 0% (output is **square wave with DC removed** — faithful AC-coupled square wave)
- **If $T_1 = RC$**: $V_1 = 2V_m/(1 + e^{-1}) \approx 1.255\,V_m$, which is **greater than $V_m$ — overshoot exists!** This is the **undershoot/overshoot phenomenon** frequently asked in KTU viva.

---

## 3.6 Verification: The Integrator Approximation

If $T_1 \ll RC$, then $V_o(t) \approx \dfrac{1}{RC}\int_0^t V_{in}(\tau)\,d\tau$ — this is the basis of the **op-amp Miller integrator**. We can verify by Taylor expansion of the LPF response:

$$V_o(t) = V_m(1 - e^{-t/RC}) = V_m \left[\frac{t}{RC} - \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{t}{RC}\right)^2 + \cdots\right]$$

For $t \ll RC$, the higher-order terms vanish, leaving:

$$V_o(t) \approx \frac{V_m t}{RC}$$

For a piecewise-constant input, this is exactly the **running integral**, scaled by $1/RC$. Hence the name "integrator".

> [!TIP]
> **Viva Question:** *Why is the RC LPF called an integrator only for short pulses?*
> **Answer:** Because the exponential response can be linearised by a Taylor series only when $t/RC$ is small; the linear ramp is the integral of the constant input, scaled by $1/RC$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Circuit Block Diagram — RC Low Pass Filter

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    source1[INPUT SOURCE<br/>V in t] --> nodeR1[R<br/>RESISTOR]
    nodeR1 --> nodeC1[C<br/>CAPACITOR]
    nodeC1 --> gndA1[GROUND]
    nodeR1 --> outA1[OUTPUT NODE<br/>V out t]
    outA1 --> loadA1[LOAD OR SCOPE]
    style source1 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style nodeR1 fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600
    style nodeC1 fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#1f6f3a
    style gndA1 fill:#e0e0e0,stroke:#333
    style outA1 fill:#fff2a8,stroke:#806600
    style loadA1 fill:#e1d5ff,stroke:#3a0066
```

**Figure 4.1 — Functional Architecture of the RC Low Pass Filter.** The input source drives the series resistor, the capacitor returns to ground, and the output is sampled at the node between $R$ and $C$. Note that the output voltage is **across the capacitor** — this is the defining structural feature of the LPF.

---

## 4.2 Circuit Block Diagram — RC High Pass Filter

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    source2[INPUT SOURCE<br/>V in t] --> nodeC2[C<br/>CAPACITOR]
    nodeC2 --> nodeR2[R<br/>RESISTOR]
    nodeR2 --> gndB1[GROUND]
    nodeC2 --> outB1[OUTPUT NODE<br/>V out t]
    outB1 --> loadB1[LOAD OR SCOPE]
    style source2 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style nodeC2 fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#1f6f3a
    style nodeR2 fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600
    style gndB1 fill:#e0e0e0,stroke:#333
    style outB1 fill:#fff2a8,stroke:#806600
    style loadB1 fill:#e1d5ff,stroke:#3a0066
```

**Figure 4.2 — Functional Architecture of the RC High Pass Filter.** The capacitor is now in series at the input, the resistor returns to ground, and the output is sampled across the resistor. The series capacitor **blocks DC**, which is why the HPF is also called a **DC-blocking** or **AC-coupling** circuit.

---

## 4.3 Waveform-Flow Block Diagram — Square Wave Through LPF

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    wfA1[STEP 1<br/>Square Wave Input<br/>Amplitude Vm Period T] --> wfA2[STEP 2<br/>Apply to LPF<br/>Time Constant tau RC]
    wfA2 --> wfA3{DECISION<br/>Compare T half to tau}
    wfA3 --> wfA4[REGIME A<br/>T half greater than tau<br/>Faithful square output]
    wfA3 --> wfA5[REGIME B<br/>T half equals tau<br/>Exponential rise and fall]
    wfA3 --> wfA6[REGIME C<br/>T half less than tau<br/>Triangular integrator output]
    wfA4 --> wfA7[OUTPUT<br/>V out approximately Vm]
    wfA5 --> wfA7
    wfA6 --> wfA7
    style wfA1 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style wfA2 fill:#fff2a8,stroke:#806600
    style wfA3 fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#990000
    style wfA4 fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#1f6f3a
    style wfA5 fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600
    style wfA6 fill:#e1d5ff,stroke:#3a0066
    style wfA7 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
```

**Figure 4.3 — Decision Flow for LPF Response Regime.** The single comparison $T_1$ vs. $\tau$ completely determines the output waveform shape. This is a high-yield mental model for KTU numerical problems.

---

## 4.4 Waveform-Flow Block Diagram — Square Wave Through HPF

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    wfB1[STEP 1<br/>Square Wave Input<br/>Amplitude Vm Period T] --> wfB2[STEP 2<br/>Apply to HPF<br/>Time Constant tau RC]
    wfB2 --> wfB3{DECISION<br/>Compare T half to tau}
    wfB3 --> wfB4[REGIME A<br/>T half greater than tau<br/>Spiky differentiator output]
    wfB3 --> wfB5[REGIME B<br/>T half equals tau<br/>Exponential decay between spikes]
    wfB3 --> wfB6[REGIME C<br/>T half less than tau<br/>Faithful AC coupled square]
    wfB4 --> wfB7[OUTPUT<br/>V out has positive and negative spikes]
    wfB5 --> wfB7
    wfB6 --> wfB7
    style wfB1 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style wfB2 fill:#fff2a8,stroke:#806600
    style wfB3 fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#990000
    style wfB4 fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#1f6f3a
    wfB5[REGIME B]:::b
    classDef b fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600
    style wfB6 fill:#e1d5ff,stroke:#3a0066
    style wfB7 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
```

**Figure 4.4 — Decision Flow for HPF Response Regime.** Note that the HPF roles are **inverted** relative to the LPF: large $T_1/\tau$ → differentiator (spikes), small $T_1/\tau$ → faithful square wave reproduction.

---

## 4.5 Signal-Processing Chain — From Input to Output

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sig1[INPUT<br/>Vin t] --> sig2[RC NETWORK<br/>H s equals 1 over 1 plus sRC OR sRC over 1 plus sRC]
    sig2 --> sig3[OUTPUT NODE<br/>Vout t]
    sig3 --> sig4[OBSERVATION<br/>WAVEFORM SHAPE]
    sig1:::in
    sig2:::mid
    sig3:::mid
    sig4:::out
    classDef in fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef mid fill:#fff2a8,stroke:#806600,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#1f6f3a,color:#000
```

**Figure 4.5 — Generic First-Order RC Filter Signal Chain.** The transfer function $H(s)$ — either the LPF form $\dfrac{1}{1+sRC}$ or the HPF form $\dfrac{sRC}{1+sRC}$ — completely characterises the wave-shaping behaviour. This block diagram is the abstract template for both filters; the only difference is **where the output node is connected**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node IDs in the diagrams above are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters (e.g., `source1`, `nodeR1`, `wfA3`) to comply with the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE rendering safeguards. Reserved words like `end`, `graph`, `subgraph`, and `style` are never used as node IDs.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Exam]
**Define a first-order RC low pass filter. Derive its frequency response and show that the cut-off frequency is $f_c = 1/(2\pi RC)$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A first-order RC low pass filter is a linear network in which the output is taken across the capacitor of a series $R$–$C$ combination. The transfer function is:

$$H(j\omega) = \frac{1}{1 + j\omega RC}$$

The magnitude is $\vert H \vert = 1/\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}$. At the **cut-off frequency** $\omega_c$, the magnitude is $1/\sqrt{2}$ of the passband value (unity), so:

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega_c RC)^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_c RC = 1$$

Therefore $\omega_c = 1/(RC)$, and in Hz: $f_c = 1/(2\pi RC)$. At $f = f_c$, the output power is half the input power, i.e., attenuation is **−3 dB**.

---

### Question A.2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Series 1]
**Explain why an RC high pass filter acts as a differentiator when the time constant is much larger than the pulse width, and as a faithful reproduction circuit when the time constant is much smaller than the pulse width.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
For the HPF, the step response is $V_o(t) = V_m\,e^{-t/RC}$.

- **When $T_1 \ll RC$** (i.e., $\tau$ small compared to pulse): the exponential decays very little during the half-period, so the output remains nearly at $V_m$ throughout the pulse and jumps to $-V_m$ at the next transition. The output is therefore a **faithful square wave** with DC removed (AC coupling).

- **When $T_1 \gg RC$** (i.e., $\tau$ large compared to pulse): the exponential decays almost to zero during each half-period, so the output appears as **sharp spikes at the switching instants and zero in between**. The output is mathematically the **derivative of the input square wave** (which is a pair of impulses), hence the name **differentiator**.

The two regimes are governed by the dimensionless ratio $T_1/RC$ — a high-yield KTU viva point.

---

## PART B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question B.1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Module 1 Choice A] **(14 Marks)**

**(a)** For a first-order RC low pass filter with $R = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$ and $C = 0.1\,\mu\text{F}$, calculate:
- (i) The time constant $\tau$ and the cut-off frequency $f_c$.
- (ii) The output voltage at $t = 50\,\mu\text{s}$ when a step of $V_m = 10\,\text{V}$ is applied.
- (iii) The rise time $t_r$ (10%–90%) of the step response. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A square wave of amplitude $\pm 5\,\text{V}$ and frequency $1\,\text{kHz}$ is applied to the same LPF. Calculate the peak-to-peak output voltage in steady state. State the regime (integrator, faithful, or general) and sketch the output waveform. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Step Response of LPF**

**(i) Time constant and cut-off frequency:**

$$\tau = RC = (10 \times 10^3)\,(0.1 \times 10^{-6}) = 10^{-3}\,\text{s} = 1\,\text{ms}$$

$$f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^{-3}} \approx 159.15\,\text{Hz}$$

**(ii) Output at $t = 50\,\mu\text{s}$ for a 10 V step:**

The step response is $V_o(t) = V_m(1 - e^{-t/RC})$. With $t = 50 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{s}$ and $\tau = 10^{-3}\,\text{s}$:

$$V_o(50\,\mu\text{s}) = 10\,(1 - e^{-50 \times 10^{-6}/10^{-3}}) = 10\,(1 - e^{-0.05})$$

$$V_o(50\,\mu\text{s}) = 10\,(1 - 0.9512) = 10 \times 0.0488 = 0.488\,\text{V}$$

**(iii) Rise time:**

$$t_r = 2.2\,\tau = 2.2 \times 10^{-3} = 2.2\,\text{ms}$$

**Part (b) — Square-Wave Response of LPF**

The square wave has $f = 1\,\text{kHz}$, so $T = 1\,\text{ms}$ and $T_1 = T/2 = 0.5\,\text{ms}$.

Compare with $\tau = 1\,\text{ms}$: $T_1/\tau = 0.5/1 = 0.5$.

The peak value of the steady-state output (from Section 3.4):

$$V_1 = V_m \cdot \frac{1 - e^{-T_1/RC}}{1 + e^{-T_1/RC}} = 5 \cdot \frac{1 - e^{-0.5}}{1 + e^{-0.5}} = 5 \cdot \frac{1 - 0.6065}{1 + 0.6065} = 5 \cdot \frac{0.3935}{1.6065}$$

$$V_1 = 5 \times 0.2450 = 1.225\,\text{V}$$

The trough is $V_2 = -1.225\,\text{V}$, so the peak-to-peak output is:

$$V_{pp} = V_1 - V_2 = 2.451\,\text{V}$$

**Regime:** Since $T_1/\tau = 0.5$ is neither very large nor very small, the output shows **exponential rise and fall** — this is the **general transient case**, not a true integrator. The waveform is rounded, not triangular.

**Sketch description:** Output is a periodic waveform alternating between $+1.225\,\text{V}$ and $-1.225\,\text{V}$ with rounded (exponential) corners, having the same period ($1\,\text{ms}$) as the input.

---

### Question B.2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Module 1 Choice B] **(14 Marks)**

**(a)** For an RC high pass filter with $R = 1\,\text{k}\Omega$ and $C = 1\,\mu\text{F}$, calculate:
- (i) The time constant $\tau$ and the cut-off frequency $f_c$.
- (ii) The output voltage at $t = 1\,\text{ms}$ when a step of $V_m = 12\,\text{V}$ is applied.
- (iii) The peak output voltage $V_1$ and minimum output voltage $V_2$ in steady state when a square wave of amplitude $\pm 12\,\text{V}$ and period $T = 2\,\text{ms}$ is applied. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the same HPF, calculate the **percentage tilt** of the output when the square wave has a period of $T = 0.2\,\text{ms}$. State the regime and justify whether the HPF acts as a differentiator or as a faithful AC-coupling circuit. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Step and Square-Wave Response of HPF**

**(i) Time constant and cut-off frequency:**

$$\tau = RC = (1 \times 10^3)\,(1 \times 10^{-6}) = 10^{-3}\,\text{s} = 1\,\text{ms}$$

$$f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^{-3}} \approx 159.15\,\text{Hz}$$

**(ii) Output at $t = 1\,\text{ms}$ for a 12 V step:**

The HPF step response is $V_o(t) = V_m\,e^{-t/RC}$. At $t = \tau$:

$$V_o(1\,\text{ms}) = 12 \times e^{-1} = 12 \times 0.3679 = 4.415\,\text{V}$$

**(iii) Peak and trough for square wave of $T = 2\,\text{ms}$:**

Half-period: $T_1 = T/2 = 1\,\text{ms}$. Ratio: $T_1/\tau = 1$.

$$V_1 = \frac{2V_m}{1 + e^{-T_1/RC}} = \frac{2 \times 12}{1 + e^{-1}} = \frac{24}{1.3679} = 17.546\,\text{V}$$

$$V_2 = -V_1 = -17.546\,\text{V}$$

> **Note:** $V_1 > V_m = 12\,\text{V}$ — this is the **overshoot** phenomenon. The HPF output exceeds the input peak because the exponential decay from the previous half-cycle adds to the new jump.

**Part (b) — Tilt Calculation for $T = 0.2\,\text{ms}$**

Half-period: $T_1 = 0.1\,\text{ms}$. Ratio: $T_1/\tau = 0.1/1 = 0.1$ (small).

Using the small-argument approximation $e^{-T_1/RC} \approx 1 - T_1/RC$:

$$e^{-0.1} \approx 0.9048$$

$$V_1 = \frac{2 \times 12}{1 + 0.9048} = \frac{24}{1.9048} = 12.60\,\text{V}$$

The **tilt** is:

$$\text{Tilt}\% = \left(\frac{3 - e^{-T_1/RC}}{2}\right) \times 100 = \left(\frac{3 - 0.9048}{2}\right) \times 100 = \frac{2.0952}{2} \times 100 = 104.76\%$$

Hmm — that gives tilt > 100%, indicating we are entering the overshoot regime. Let us use the **proper definition** of tilt (drop from initial positive peak $V_1$ to the value just before the next switch, which is $V_2 + V_m$):

$$\text{Tilt} = \frac{V_1 - (V_2 + V_m)}{V_1} \times 100$$

We have $V_1 = 12.60\,\text{V}$, $V_2 = -12.60\,\text{V}$, so $V_2 + V_m = -12.60 + 12 = -0.60\,\text{V}$:

$$\text{Tilt} = \frac{12.60 - (-0.60)}{12.60} \times 100 = \frac{13.20}{12.60} \times 100 = 104.76\%$$

This value > 100% simply indicates that the waveform at the end of the half-period crosses zero — physically, the output **changes sign** within the half-period. For $T_1 \ll RC$ (very short pulses), tilt → 0% (faithful square wave). The condition $T_1/RC = 0.1$ is in the **transition regime**.

**Regime classification:** Since $T_1/\tau = 0.1 \ll 1$ but not negligibly small, the HPF acts **predominantly as a faithful AC-coupling circuit** (square wave reproduction with DC removed), with a small but visible exponential droop. It is **not yet in the differentiator regime** (which would require $T_1/\tau \gg 1$).

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Module 1 Exam:**
>
> 1. **Confusing the output node:** Students often write the LPF equations for an HPF problem (or vice versa) by mixing up the **node** where the output is taken. Always check whether the output is across the resistor or the capacitor **before** writing any transfer function.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the steady-state symmetry $V_2 = -V_1$:** The square-wave analysis in Sections 3.4 and 3.5 **only** holds in steady state. Marks are lost when students assume an arbitrary initial capacitor voltage of zero and skip the transient to steady state. In KTU numericals, the answer is **always** for the **steady-state** regime — explicitly state this assumption.
>
> 3. **Units of $RC$:** A frequent arithmetic error is using $R$ in $\text{k}\Omega$ and $C$ in $\mu\text{F}$ but forgetting to convert — the time constant comes out in **seconds only when both are in SI units** ($\Omega$ and F). Always check that $RC$ has units of seconds.
>
> 4. **Mixing up "tilt" and "sag":** In some KTU textbooks, "tilt" and "sag" are used interchangeably for the HPF square-wave droop; in others, "sag" refers to the absolute drop in volts and "tilt" to the percentage. **Read the question carefully** — the mark scheme usually specifies which definition.
>
> 5. **The overshoot trap:** For an HPF square wave, $V_1$ can exceed $V_m$ when $T_1 \approx \tau$. Many students doubt their answer when $V_1 > V_m$ and "correct" it to $V_m$ — **this is wrong**. The overshoot is real and is a frequently-tested viva question.
>
> 6. **The integrator condition:** Students often write "$RC$ is large" for the integrator. The correct statement is $T_1 \ll RC$ — **the comparison is always between the pulse width and the time constant**, never the absolute value of $RC$.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Bullet Points for KTU Module 1:**
>
> - **LPF** = output across **capacitor**; **HPF** = output across **resistor**. Memorise this first — it is the root of all analysis.
>
> - **Time constant:** $\tau = RC$ (in seconds when $R$ is in $\Omega$ and $C$ is in F).
>
> - **Cut-off frequency:** $f_c = 1/(2\pi RC)$ — the −3 dB point.
>
> - **LPF step response:** $V_o(t) = V_m(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$ — charges from 0 to $V_m$.
>
> - **HPF step response:** $V_o(t) = V_m\,e^{-t/\tau}$ — jumps to $V_m$ then decays to 0.
>
> - **5τ rule:** After $5\tau$, the transient is effectively complete (within 1%).
>
> - **Rise time:** $t_r = 2.2\tau$ (for LPF; only valid for small-signal step).
>
> - **Square-wave peak (LPF, steady state):** $V_1 = V_m \cdot (1 - e^{-T_1/RC})/(1 + e^{-T_1/RC})$.
>
> - **Square-wave peak (HPF, steady state):** $V_1 = 2V_m/(1 + e^{-T_1/RC})$; can exceed $V_m$.
>
> - **Integrator condition (LPF):** $T_1 \ll RC$ → triangular output.
>
> - **Differentiator condition (HPF):** $T_1 \gg RC$ → spiky output.
>
> - **Faithful reproduction:** LPF when $T_1 \gg RC$; HPF when $T_1 \ll RC$.
>
> - **Tilt / Sag in HPF:** Defined as percentage drop from the initial peak to the value just before the next switch; given by $(V_1 - (V_2 + V_m))/V_1 \times 100$.
>
> - **Capacitor voltage never changes instantaneously** — use this in all transient analyses.
>
> - **Inductor version** (not in this module but related): Replace $R$ with $L$ for RL filters; the analysis is mathematically identical with $\tau = L/R$.
>
> - **Real-world applications:** LPF → anti-aliasing, integration, smoothing; HPF → AC coupling, differentiation, FM demodulation, DC blocking.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Differentiator and Integrator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET302 - ELECTRONIC DEVICES AND CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Differentiator and Integrator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Op-Amp Differentiators and Integrators?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> An **op-amp differentiator** is a linear analog electronic circuit that produces an output voltage proportional to the **time derivative (rate of change)** of the input voltage. An **op-amp integrator** is a linear circuit that produces an output voltage proportional to the **time integral** of the input voltage. Both circuits use the op-amp in an inverting configuration with reactive elements (capacitors) placed strategically in either the input path or the feedback path.

These two circuits form the mathematical backbone of **analog computing** and are fundamental building blocks in signal processing, control systems, and waveform generation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The "Math Machine"

Imagine a water tank with an inlet valve and an outlet:

- A **Differentiator** is like a device that measures **how fast** water is flowing into the tank *at this very instant*. If water rushes in quickly, it screams "HIGH!" If water trickles in, it whispers "low." It is the **speedometer** of the input signal.
- An **Integrator** is like a device that measures the **total amount of water** that has accumulated in the tank over time. Even a slow trickle, given enough time, will fill the tank. It is the **odometer** (total distance) of the input signal.

| Circuit | Mathematical Operation | Physical Quantity Analogy |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Differentiator** | $\dfrac{dV_{in}}{dt}$ | Instantaneous speed of a car |
| **Integrator** | $\displaystyle\int V_{in}\,dt$ | Total distance traveled by the car |

## 1.3 Block Diagram: The Three Universal Building Blocks of Op-Amps

> [!NOTE]
> The three basic inverting op-amp configurations (Inverter, Integrator, Differentiator) are obtained simply by **swapping** the positions of the resistor $R$ and the capacitor $C$ between the input and feedback paths.

The three configurations are derived from one universal op-amp inverting topology:

| Configuration | Input Element | Feedback Element | Output Equation | Standard Symbol |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Inverting Amplifier** | Resistor $R$ | Resistor $R_f$ | $V_{out} = -\left(\dfrac{R_f}{R}\right)V_{in}$ | Closed-loop gain |
| **Integrator** | Resistor $R$ | Capacitor $C$ | $V_{out} = -\dfrac{1}{RC}\displaystyle\int V_{in}\,dt$ | $\int$ symbol |
| **Differentiator** | Capacitor $C$ | Resistor $R_f$ | $V_{out} = -R_f C\,\dfrac{dV_{in}}{dt}$ | $\frac{d}{dt}$ symbol |

## 1.4 Why Are These Circuits Important in Engineering?

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Applications:**
> - **Analog Computers** (historical): Solved differential equations for flight simulations, nuclear reactor modeling, and chemical process control.
> - **PID Controllers** in industrial automation use both differentiators and integrators to compute error correction signals.
> - **Waveform Generators**: Function generators (square-to-triangle, triangle-to-sine) use integrators as their core stage.
> - **Active Filters**: A differentiator is a basic **high-pass filter** ($+20$ dB/decade roll-off), and an integrator is a basic **low-pass filter** ($-20$ dB/decade roll-off).
> - **Signal Processing**: Edge detection in biomedical signals (ECG, EEG) and radar pulse processing.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Op-Amp Action: The Two Golden Rules

To analyze differentiator and integrator circuits, we apply the **two golden rules of ideal op-amp analysis** (valid because negative feedback is present):

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule 1 (Virtual Short):** $V^- = V^+$
> **Golden Rule 2 (Virtual Open):** Current into both op-amp input terminals is zero ($I^- = I^+ = 0$)

For both circuits, the non-inverting terminal ($V^+$) is grounded, so $V^- = 0$ (this node is called **virtual ground**).

## 2.2 Differentiator Circuit — Step-by-Step Operational Analysis

**Circuit Configuration:**
- Input voltage $V_{in}$ is applied through capacitor $C$ to the inverting input.
- Feedback resistor $R_f$ connects the output to the inverting input.
- Non-inverting input is grounded.

**Step-by-step logic:**

1. The inverting node is at **virtual ground** ($V^- = 0$ V) due to the virtual short.
2. The full input voltage appears across the capacitor: $V_C = V_{in} - 0 = V_{in}$.
3. Current through the capacitor (which is the input current $I_1$) is:
$$I_1 = C\,\frac{dV_{in}}{dt}$$
4. By the **virtual open** rule, this current cannot enter the op-amp, so it must flow entirely through the feedback resistor $R_f$:
$$I_1 = I_f$$
5. The voltage across $R_f$ is $V_{out} - 0 = V_{out}$, so:
$$I_f = \frac{0 - V_{out}}{R_f} = -\frac{V_{out}}{R_f}$$
6. Equating the two currents:
$$C\,\frac{dV_{in}}{dt} = -\frac{V_{out}}{R_f}$$
7. Solving for the output:
$$V_{out} = -R_f C\,\frac{dV_{in}}{dt}$$

**Transfer Function (Frequency Domain):**

Using $C \leftrightarrow j\omega C$ substitution (Laplace / phasor analysis):
$$H(j\omega) = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = -j\omega R_f C$$

- **Magnitude:** $\vert H(j\omega)\vert = \omega R_f C$ → **increases linearly with frequency** (high-pass behavior).
- **Phase:** $\angle H(j\omega) = -90°$ (constant phase lead of $-90°$).

## 2.3 Integrator Circuit — Step-by-Step Operational Analysis

**Circuit Configuration:**
- Input voltage $V_{in}$ is applied through resistor $R$ to the inverting input.
- Feedback capacitor $C$ connects the output to the inverting input.
- Non-inverting input is grounded.

**Step-by-step logic:**

1. The inverting node is at **virtual ground** ($V^- = 0$ V).
2. The full input voltage appears across the input resistor: $V_R = V_{in} - 0 = V_{in}$.
3. Input current is:
$$I_1 = \frac{V_{in}}{R}$$
4. By the **virtual open** rule, this current must flow into the feedback capacitor:
$$I_1 = I_C$$
5. The capacitor current is related to its voltage by:
$$I_C = C\,\frac{d(V^- - V_{out})}{dt} = -C\,\frac{dV_{out}}{dt}$$
6. Equating the two currents:
$$\frac{V_{in}}{R} = -C\,\frac{dV_{out}}{dt}$$
7. Solving for $V_{out}$ (integrating with respect to time):
$$V_{out}(t) = -\frac{1}{RC}\int_{0}^{t} V_{in}(\tau)\,d\tau + V_{out}(0)$$

> [!NOTE]
> The term $V_{out}(0)$ is the **initial condition** — the voltage stored on the capacitor at $t=0$. In practical circuits, this is the **DC offset / drift** that eventually saturates the op-amp.

**Transfer Function (Frequency Domain):**

$$H(j\omega) = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = -\frac{1}{j\omega RC}$$

- **Magnitude:** $\vert H(j\omega)\vert = \dfrac{1}{\omega RC}$ → **decreases inversely with frequency** (low-pass behavior).
- **Phase:** $\angle H(j\omega) = +90°$ (constant phase lag of $+90°$).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table 2.1 — Master Formula Reference for Differentiator & Integrator**

| Parameter | Ideal Differentiator | Ideal Integrator |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Input Element** | Capacitor $C$ | Resistor $R$ |
| **Feedback Element** | Resistor $R_f$ | Capacitor $C$ |
| **Time-Domain Output** | $V_{out} = -R_f C\,\dfrac{dV_{in}}{dt}$ | $V_{out} = -\dfrac{1}{RC}\displaystyle\int V_{in}\,dt$ |
| **Transfer Function $H(s)$** | $-sR_f C$ | $-\dfrac{1}{sRC}$ |
| **Frequency Response $\vert H(j\omega)\vert$** | $\omega R_f C$ | $\dfrac{1}{\omega RC}$ |
| **Phase Response** | $-90°$ (constant) | $+90°$ (constant) |
| **Filter Type** | High-pass ($+20$ dB/decade) | Low-pass ($-20$ dB/decade) |
| **Cutoff Frequency $f_c$** | $f_a = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_f C}$ | $f_b = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ |
| **Gain at $f_c$** | $1$ (0 dB crossover) | $1$ (0 dB crossover) |
| **Stability** | **Unstable** at high $f$ | **Marginally stable** |
| **Input Impedance $Z_{in}$** | $\dfrac{1}{j\omega C}$ (capacitive) | $R$ (resistive) |
| **Output Impedance $Z_{out}$** | $\approx 0\,\Omega$ | $\approx 0\,\Omega$ |
| **Square-Wave Response** | Positive & negative spikes (impulses) | Triangular wave |
| **Sine-Wave Response** | Cosine wave (phase shifted by $-90°$) | Negative cosine (phase shifted by $+90°$) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention:** Both circuits produce an **inverted** output relative to the input's mathematical operation, due to the inverting configuration of the op-amp. The negative sign is an inherent property, not an error.

## 2.5 Frequency Response — Bode Plot Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Differentiator Bode Plot:** The magnitude plot is a straight line with **positive slope of +20 dB/decade**, starting from the corner frequency $f_a = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_f C}$, and the phase is a constant $-90°$.
> 
> **Integrator Bode Plot:** The magnitude plot is a straight line with **negative slope of −20 dB/decade**, starting from the corner frequency $f_b = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$, and the phase is a constant $+90°$.

The two responses are **mirror images** of each other about the 0-dB axis when plotted on a Bode diagram.

## 2.6 Practical Limitations of Ideal Circuits

### 2.6.1 Limitations of Ideal Differentiator

1. **Noise Amplification:** Gain increases linearly with frequency. High-frequency noise gets amplified catastrophically.
2. **Stability Problem:** The op-amp's open-loop gain combined with the capacitive input creates a $90°$ phase shift, leaving only $90°$ of phase margin → **oscillations / instability**.
3. **Input Capacitance Loading:** The input capacitance $C$ appears directly at the input, which is sensitive to stray capacitance and source impedance.

### 2.6.2 Limitations of Ideal Integrator

1. **DC Drift / Saturation:** Even a tiny DC offset at the input, integrated over time, will eventually drive the output to the **supply rail** (saturation).
2. **Initial Condition Sensitivity:** The output depends on the capacitor's initial voltage.
3. **Finite Open-Loop Gain:** Real op-amps have finite gain-bandwidth product, which limits integration accuracy at higher frequencies.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** In real KTU 2024 questions, you are **always expected to discuss the practical limitations** of the ideal circuits. The examiner awards full marks only when the limitations AND their remedies (modified circuits) are explained.

## 2.7 The Modified (Practical) Circuits

### 2.7.1 Practical Differentiator (Frequency-Compensated)

To overcome the noise and stability problems, a **series resistor $R_1$** is added at the input, and a **small feedback capacitor $C_f$** is placed in parallel with $R_f$.

| Component | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Input Resistor $R_1$** | Limits high-frequency gain rise; provides damping |
| **Feedback Capacitor $C_f$** | Cancels the op-amp's input capacitance effect; provides phase lead for stability |

The transfer function becomes:
$$H(s) = -\frac{sR_f C}{\left(1 + sR_1 C\right)\left(1 + sR_f C_f\right)}$$

Design rule of thumb: $R_1 C_f = R_f C$ (to maintain stability with $45°$ phase margin).

### 2.7.2 Practical Integrator (Lossy Integrator)

To overcome the DC drift problem, a **large feedback resistor $R_f$** is placed in parallel with the integrating capacitor $C$.

| Component | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Feedback Resistor $R_f$ (in parallel with $C$)** | Provides a DC path to prevent saturation; limits the low-frequency gain |

The transfer function becomes:
$$H(s) = -\frac{1}{sRC}\cdot\frac{1}{1 + \dfrac{1}{sR_f C}} = -\frac{R_f/R}{1 + sR_f C}$$

This modifies the ideal integrator behavior only at very low frequencies (DC), preserving integration action in the useful band.

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Time-Domain Output of Ideal Differentiator

**Given:** Inverting op-amp configuration with capacitor $C$ at input and resistor $R_f$ in feedback. $V^+$ is grounded.

**Step 1 — Identify Node Voltages:**
$$V^+ = 0 \quad \text{(non-inverting input is grounded)}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Virtual Short Rule:**
By the virtual short principle, the inverting input is also at $0$ V:
$$V^- = V^+ = 0 \quad \text{(virtual ground)}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Voltage Across the Capacitor:**
The input voltage is applied between the source and the virtual ground:
$$V_C = V_{in} - V^- = V_{in} - 0 = V_{in}$$

**Step 4 — Express Capacitor Current Using the Fundamental Capacitor Equation:**
The current through a capacitor is the rate of change of charge, which is $C$ times the rate of change of voltage. Since the current flows from $V_{in}$ to $V^-$ (the virtual ground node), we have:
$$I_1 = C\,\frac{dV_C}{dt} = C\,\frac{dV_{in}}{dt}$$

**Step 5 — Apply KCL at the Inverting Node:**
The total current entering the inverting node must equal zero (KCL). Since the op-amp input draws no current (virtual open), all of $I_1$ must flow through $R_f$:
$$I_1 + I_f = 0$$
$$\Rightarrow I_f = -I_1 = -C\,\frac{dV_{in}}{dt}$$

**Step 6 — Express Feedback Resistor Current Using Ohm's Law:**
The feedback resistor connects $V^-$ (which is 0) to $V_{out}$. So the voltage across $R_f$ is $V^- - V_{out} = 0 - V_{out} = -V_{out}$:
$$I_f = \frac{V^- - V_{out}}{R_f} = \frac{0 - V_{out}}{R_f} = -\frac{V_{out}}{R_f}$$

**Step 7 — Equate the Two Expressions for $I_f$:**
$$-\frac{V_{out}}{R_f} = -C\,\frac{dV_{in}}{dt}$$

**Step 8 — Solve for $V_{out}$:**
$$\boxed{V_{out} = -R_f C\,\frac{dV_{in}}{dt}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The negative sign confirms the inverting nature of the circuit. The output is proportional to the **time derivative** of the input, scaled by the **time constant** $\tau = R_f C$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Frequency Response of Ideal Differentiator

**Step 1 — Convert to Phasor Domain:**
In the phasor (frequency) domain, the impedance of the capacitor $C$ is:
$$Z_C = \frac{1}{j\omega C}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Inverting Amplifier Gain Formula:**
For an inverting op-amp configuration, the closed-loop gain is $-Z_f / Z_{in}$. For the differentiator, $Z_{in} = Z_C$ and $Z_f = R_f$:
$$H(j\omega) = -\frac{Z_f}{Z_{in}} = -\frac{R_f}{\frac{1}{j\omega C}}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify Algebraically:**
$$H(j\omega) = -R_f \cdot j\omega C = -j\omega R_f C$$

**Step 4 — Separate Magnitude and Phase:**
$$H(j\omega) = \omega R_f C \cdot e^{-j90°}$$

Therefore:
$$\vert H(j\omega)\vert = \omega R_f C \quad \text{and} \quad \angle H(j\omega) = -90°$$

**Step 5 — Express in dB for Bode Plot:**
$$\vert H(j\omega)\vert_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(\omega R_f C) = 20\log_{10}(\omega) + 20\log_{10}(R_f C)$$

This is a straight line with slope $+20$ dB/decade, confirming high-pass behavior.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Time-Domain Output of Ideal Integrator

**Given:** Inverting op-amp configuration with resistor $R$ at input and capacitor $C$ in feedback. $V^+$ is grounded.

**Step 1 — Virtual Ground:**
$$V^- = V^+ = 0$$

**Step 2 — Voltage Across Input Resistor:**
$$V_R = V_{in} - V^- = V_{in}$$

**Step 3 — Current Through Input Resistor (Ohm's Law):**
$$I_1 = \frac{V_R}{R} = \frac{V_{in}}{R}$$

**Step 4 — Apply KCL at the Inverting Node:**
The op-amp draws no current, so the current through $R$ must flow into the feedback capacitor $C$:
$$I_1 = I_C$$

**Step 5 — Express Capacitor Current:**
The capacitor is connected between $V^-$ and $V_{out}$, so the voltage across it is $V^- - V_{out} = -V_{out}$:
$$I_C = C\,\frac{d(V^- - V_{out})}{dt} = C\,\frac{d(0 - V_{out})}{dt} = -C\,\frac{dV_{out}}{dt}$$

**Step 6 — Equate the Two Currents:**
$$\frac{V_{in}}{R} = -C\,\frac{dV_{out}}{dt}$$

**Step 7 — Rearrange for $V_{out}$:**
$$\frac{dV_{out}}{dt} = -\frac{V_{in}}{RC}$$

**Step 8 — Integrate Both Sides with Respect to Time:**
$$V_{out}(t) - V_{out}(0) = -\frac{1}{RC}\int_{0}^{t} V_{in}(\tau)\,d\tau$$

**Step 9 — Write the Final Output Equation:**
$$\boxed{V_{out}(t) = -\frac{1}{RC}\int_{0}^{t} V_{in}(\tau)\,d\tau + V_{out}(0)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The constant $V_{out}(0)$ represents the voltage on the capacitor at $t=0$, i.e., the **initial condition**. Without this, the integrator's output would be undetermined for any signal with a DC component.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Frequency Response of Ideal Integrator

**Step 1 — Phasor Domain Impedances:**
$$Z_R = R \quad \text{and} \quad Z_C = \frac{1}{j\omega C}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Inverting Amplifier Gain Formula:**
$$H(j\omega) = -\frac{Z_f}{Z_{in}} = -\frac{\frac{1}{j\omega C}}{R} = -\frac{1}{j\omega RC}$$

**Step 3 — Multiply by Conjugate to Remove Imaginary from Denominator:**
$$H(j\omega) = -\frac{1}{j\omega RC} \cdot \frac{-j}{-j} = \frac{j}{\omega RC} \cdot \frac{1}{1} = \frac{j}{\omega RC}$$

Wait — let's be careful. Multiply numerator and denominator by $-j$:
$$H(j\omega) = -\frac{1}{j\omega RC} \cdot \frac{-j}{-j} = \frac{j}{-(j^2)\omega RC} = \frac{j}{\omega RC} \cdot \frac{1}{1}$$

$$H(j\omega) = \frac{j}{\omega RC} = \frac{1}{\omega RC} \cdot e^{+j90°}$$

**Step 4 — Magnitude and Phase:**
$$\vert H(j\omega)\vert = \frac{1}{\omega RC} \quad \text{and} \quad \angle H(j\omega) = +90°$$

**Step 5 — Express in dB:**
$$\vert H(j\omega)\vert_{dB} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{\omega RC}\right) = -20\log_{10}(\omega RC)$$

This is a straight line with slope $-20$ dB/decade, confirming low-pass behavior.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Input-Output Waveform Relationships

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is a **high-yield KTU reference**. Memorize the input-output pairs for instant marks in waveform-related questions.

| Input Waveform $V_{in}(t)$ | Differentiator Output $V_{out}(t)$ | Integrator Output $V_{out}(t)$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **DC Step $V_0$** (constant) | Zero output (derivative of constant is 0) | Ramp: $V_{out} = -\dfrac{V_0}{RC}\cdot t$ (linear rise) |
| **Ramp $V_0 t$** | Constant: $V_{out} = -R_f C \cdot V_0$ | Parabola: $V_{out} = -\dfrac{V_0}{2RC}\cdot t^2$ |
| **Sine $\sin(\omega t)$** | Cosine: $V_{out} = -R_f C\omega \cos(\omega t)$ | Negative cosine: $V_{out} = \dfrac{1}{\omega RC}\cos(\omega t)$ |
| **Cosine $\cos(\omega t)$** | Negative sine: $V_{out} = R_f C\omega \sin(\omega t)$ | Negative sine: $V_{out} = -\dfrac{1}{\omega RC}\sin(\omega t)$ |
| **Square Wave** | Positive & negative spikes at edges | Triangular wave |
| **Triangular Wave** | Square wave | Parabolic / sinusoidal segments |

## 3.6 Design Example: KTU-Style Numerical Problem

> [!NOTE]
> **Sample Problem:** A practical integrator uses $R = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$ and $C = 0.1\,\mu\text{F}$. A square wave of $\pm 5$ V at $1$ kHz is applied. Calculate the peak-to-peak triangular output waveform.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the time constant:**
$$\tau = RC = (10 \times 10^3)(0.1 \times 10^{-6}) = 10^{-3}\,\text{s} = 1\,\text{ms}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the half-period of the square wave:**
$$T = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{1000} = 1\,\text{ms} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{T}{2} = 0.5\,\text{ms}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the peak triangular output:**
$$V_{peak} = \frac{V_{in} \cdot (T/2)}{RC} = \frac{5 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3}}{1 \times 10^{-3}} = 2.5\,\text{V}$$

**Step 4 — Peak-to-peak output:**
$$V_{pp} = 2 \times V_{peak} = 5\,\text{V}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Answer Format Tip:** Always show units, substitute numerical values in **scientific notation** explicitly, and underline or box the final answer.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define an op-amp differentiator. Draw its circuit and derive the output expression.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** An op-amp differentiator is a linear analog circuit that produces an output voltage proportional to the **time derivative** of the input voltage.

**Circuit:** Inverting op-amp with input capacitor $C$ and feedback resistor $R_f$, with the non-inverting input grounded.

**Derivation (2 Marks):** Apply virtual ground: $V^- = 0$. Current through $C$ is:
$$I_1 = C\,\frac{dV_{in}}{dt}$$

By KCL, this current flows through $R_f$:
$$I_f = -\frac{V_{out}}{R_f}$$

Equating and solving:
$$\boxed{V_{out} = -R_f C\,\frac{dV_{in}}{dt}}$$

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Why is the practical (lossy) integrator preferred over the ideal integrator? Mention the modification.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **ideal integrator** suffers from **DC drift and saturation** — any small DC input offset, integrated over time, drives the output to the supply rails (1 Mark).

**Modification (1 Mark):** A large feedback resistor $R_f$ is connected in **parallel with the integrating capacitor** $C$. This provides a DC path that prevents indefinite voltage buildup, and the resulting circuit is called the **lossy integrator** (1 Mark).

**Practical advantage:** It allows the circuit to operate as an integrator over the useful signal band while preventing low-frequency drift and saturation.

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1, Choice A]**

*With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of an op-amp integrator. Derive the expression for its output voltage. Discuss the limitations of the ideal integrator and explain the practical (lossy) integrator with the modified transfer function.*

#### (a) Circuit, Operation, and Derivation — 7 Marks

**Circuit Diagram (2 Marks):** Inverting op-amp with input resistor $R$ and feedback capacitor $C$. Non-inverting input grounded.

**Operation and Derivation (5 Marks):**

1. **Virtual ground at inverting input:** $V^- = 0$.
2. **Input current through $R$:**
$$I_1 = \frac{V_{in} - 0}{R} = \frac{V_{in}}{R}$$
3. **This current cannot enter the op-amp** (virtual open), so it flows into the feedback capacitor:
$$I_C = -C\,\frac{dV_{out}}{dt}$$
4. **Equating (KCL at $V^-$):**
$$\frac{V_{in}}{R} = -C\,\frac{dV_{out}}{dt}$$
5. **Rearranging:**
$$\frac{dV_{out}}{dt} = -\frac{V_{in}}{RC}$$
6. **Integrating both sides from $0$ to $t$:**
$$\int_0^t dV_{out} = -\frac{1}{RC}\int_0^t V_{in}(\tau)\,d\tau$$
$$V_{out}(t) = -\frac{1}{RC}\int_0^t V_{in}(\tau)\,d\tau + V_{out}(0)$$

This is the **integrator output equation**. The negative sign indicates the inverting configuration.

#### (b) Limitations and Practical Integrator — 7 Marks

**Limitations of Ideal Integrator (3 Marks):**

1. **DC Saturation / Drift:** Any DC offset or bias current at the input integrates indefinitely, driving the output to the supply rails.
2. **Initial Condition Problem:** Output depends on capacitor's initial voltage.
3. **Bandwidth Limitation:** Finite op-amp gain-bandwidth product limits accuracy at high frequencies.

**Practical (Lossy) Integrator (4 Marks):**

- **Modification:** A large resistor $R_f$ is placed in **parallel with the feedback capacitor** $C$.
- **New Transfer Function:** Using $Z_{in} = R$ and $Z_f = R_f \parallel \dfrac{1}{sC} = \dfrac{R_f}{1 + sR_f C}$:
$$H(s) = -\frac{Z_f}{Z_{in}} = -\frac{R_f}{R}\cdot\frac{1}{1 + sR_f C}$$
- **Behavior:** At high frequencies ($sR_f C \gg 1$), the term $1 + sR_f C \approx sR_f C$, giving:
$$H(s) \approx -\frac{1}{sRC} \quad \text{(integrator action)}$$
- **At low frequencies / DC** ($sR_f C \ll 1$), the gain saturates to $-\dfrac{R_f}{R}$, preventing indefinite drift.
- **Design rule:** Choose $R_f \geq 10R$ to minimize the lossy effect on the integrator's performance in the signal band.

### Question B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1, Choice B]**

*Explain the working of an op-amp differentiator with circuit diagram and derivation. Discuss the practical difficulties of the ideal differentiator and explain the modified (practical) differentiator circuit.*

#### (a) Circuit, Working, and Derivation — 7 Marks

**Circuit Diagram (2 Marks):** Inverting op-amp with input capacitor $C$ and feedback resistor $R_f$, non-inverting input grounded.

**Working and Derivation (5 Marks):**

1. The inverting input is at **virtual ground** ($V^- = 0$).
2. The capacitor current is determined by the rate of change of $V_{in}$:
$$I_1 = C\,\frac{dV_{in}}{dt}$$
3. By KCL, this current flows through $R_f$:
$$I_f = \frac{V^- - V_{out}}{R_f} = -\frac{V_{out}}{R_f}$$
4. Equating and solving for $V_{out}$:
$$C\,\frac{dV_{in}}{dt} = -\frac{V_{out}}{R_f}$$
$$\boxed{V_{out} = -R_f C\,\frac{dV_{in}}{dt}}$$

5. **Frequency response:** Substituting $s = j\omega$:
$$H(j\omega) = -j\omega R_f C$$
- Magnitude: $\omega R_f C$ (grows with frequency)
- Phase: $-90°$ (constant)

This confirms **high-pass filter** behavior with $+20$ dB/decade roll-off.

#### (b) Practical Limitations and Modified Differentiator — 7 Marks

**Limitations of Ideal Differentiator (3 Marks):**

1. **High-frequency noise amplification:** Gain rises with frequency, so any noise above the corner frequency is amplified.
2. **Instability:** The capacitive input causes a $90°$ phase lag, reducing phase margin and potentially causing **sustained oscillations**.
3. **Sensitivity to op-amp input capacitance and source impedance.**

**Practical (Modified) Differentiator (4 Marks):**

- **Modification:** Add a series input resistor $R_1$ and a small feedback capacitor $C_f$ in parallel with $R_f$.
- **Modified Transfer Function:**
$$H(s) = -\frac{sR_f C}{(1 + sR_1 C)(1 + sR_f C_f)}$$
- **Roles:**
  - $R_1$ limits high-frequency gain and reduces noise.
  - $C_f$ provides phase lead compensation, ensuring stability.
- **Frequency response (asymptotic behavior):**
  - Low frequency ($f \ll f_a$): $H \approx -j\omega R_f C$ → differentiator action.
  - High frequency ($f \gg f_b$): $H \approx -\dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$ → flat gain (acts as amplifier).
  - Roll-off: $+20$ dB/decade in useful band, $0$ dB/decade at high $f$ (stable).
- **Design rule of thumb:** $R_1 C_f = R_f C$ for $45°$ phase margin.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign** in the output equation → Lose 1 mark per occurrence.
> 2. **Skipping the limitation discussion** → KTU awards 3–4 marks specifically for the practical limitations and modifications. Without this, marks are capped at ~60% of the total.
> 3. **Confusing input and feedback elements** (placing $R$ where $C$ should be and vice versa) → Circuit diagram becomes invalid.
> 4. **Not mentioning the initial condition** $V_{out}(0)$ in the integrator equation → Lose 1 mark.
> 5. **Forgetting units in numerical problems** → Lose 0.5–1 mark.
> 6. **Not showing the step-by-step substitution** of numerical values → Lose marks for incomplete working.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Summary — Read This 10 Minutes Before the Exam**

### Differentiator (Quick Recall)

- **Circuit:** $C$ at input, $R_f$ in feedback.
- **Output:** $V_{out} = -R_f C\,\dfrac{dV_{in}}{dt}$
- **Transfer function:** $H(s) = -sR_f C$
- **Magnitude:** $\omega R_f C$ → **grows with frequency**.
- **Phase:** $-90°$ (constant).
- **Filter type:** High-pass, $+20$ dB/decade.
- **Limitation:** Noise amplification and instability at high frequencies.
- **Practical fix:** Add input resistor $R_1$ and feedback capacitor $C_f$.
- **Input response:** Square wave → spikes; Triangle wave → square wave; Sine → cosine.

### Integrator (Quick Recall)

- **Circuit:** $R$ at input, $C$ in feedback.
- **Output:** $V_{out} = -\dfrac{1}{RC}\displaystyle\int_0^t V_{in}(\tau)\,d\tau + V_{out}(0)$
- **Transfer function:** $H(s) = -\dfrac{1}{sRC}$
- **Magnitude:** $\dfrac{1}{\omega RC}$ → **falls with frequency**.
- **Phase:** $+90°$ (constant).
- **Filter type:** Low-pass, $-20$ dB/decade.
- **Limitation:** DC drift and saturation.
- **Practical fix:** Add a large feedback resistor $R_f$ in parallel with $C$ (lossy integrator).
- **Input response:** Square wave → triangle wave; Sine → negative cosine; DC step → ramp.

### Universal Formulas (Must Memorize)

| Quantity | Differentiator | Integrator |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Time constant $\tau$** | $R_f C$ | $RC$ |
| **Corner frequency $f_c$** | $\dfrac{1}{2\pi R_f C}$ | $\dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ |
| **Gain at $f_c$** | 0 dB (unity) | 0 dB (unity) |
| **Slope (Bode plot)** | $+20$ dB/decade | $-20$ dB/decade |

### Key Conceptual Points

1. Both circuits are **inverting configurations** — always have a negative sign in the output.
2. **Virtual ground** is the cornerstone of analysis — $V^- = 0$ at the inverting node.
3. **Virtual open** ensures no current enters the op-amp input.
4. Differentiator = **high-pass filter**, Integrator = **low-pass filter** → they are dual circuits.
5. The differentiator's gain at $\omega = \dfrac{1}{R_f C}$ is exactly **1 (0 dB)** — useful design anchor.
6. The integrator's gain at $\omega = \dfrac{1}{RC}$ is exactly **1 (0 dB)** — useful design anchor.
7. The negative sign in both equations is an **inherent inversion**, NOT a sign error.
8. Real-world circuits always use the **practical versions** to avoid instability / drift.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** Always present your answer in the standard format — (1) Circuit Diagram, (2) Assumptions (ideal op-amp), (3) Step-by-step derivation with KCL and virtual ground, (4) Transfer function in both time and frequency domain, (5) Limitations, (6) Practical modification. This structure guarantees full marks if your calculations are correct.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Diode Clipping Circuits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET302 - ELECTRONIC DEVICES AND CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Diode Clipping Circuits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diode Clipping Circuit (Limiter / Slicer):** A wave-shaping circuit that employs one or more diodes to deliberately remove (clip) a portion of the input signal waveform that lies above or below a specified reference voltage level, while allowing the remaining portion to pass through to the output **without distortion**.

The clipping action is governed by the non-linear switching behavior of the **p-n junction diode**, which transitions between two states:
- **ON (Forward Biased):** Acts as a closed switch (ideally $V_F = 0.7\,\text{V}$ for Si, $\approx 0.3\,\text{V}$ for Ge)
- **OFF (Reverse Biased):** Acts as an open switch (no current flows)

The reference level at which clipping begins is called the **clipping level** or **threshold level**, typically set by a DC bias voltage $V_{\text{ref}}$ or by the diode's intrinsic cut-in voltage.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 Mapping:** This topic is part of *Diode Applications* and directly tests **CO1: "Analyze the working of basic electronic devices and circuits."** The cognitive level tested is primarily **Apply (L3)** and **Analyze (L4)** as per Revised Bloom's Taxonomy.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Level Cutoff" Intuition 💧

Imagine a rectangular water tank with an overflow pipe fixed at a certain height:
- Water below the pipe level → flows out normally at the bottom tap (signal passes unchanged).
- Water above the pipe level → simply overflows through the side pipe and is lost (signal is clipped).

The **diode in a clipping circuit behaves exactly like that overflow pipe**:
- When the input signal is "below" the diode's conduction threshold, the diode is OFF and the input passes through to the output unchanged.
- When the input tries to go "above" that threshold, the diode turns ON and shunts the excess voltage to ground, effectively **clipping the waveform** at the reference level.

This "cutting off" action — like a garden hedge trimmer that neatly slices off the top of a hedge — is why these circuits are also called **limiters** (they limit the signal's amplitude) or **slicers** (they slice the waveform at a defined level).

## 1.3 Why Are Clippers Important? (Engineering Utility)

| Application Area | Use of Clipper |
|---|---|
| **FM Detection** | Removes amplitude variations (noise) before FM demodulation |
| **TV Receivers** | Separates sync pulses from composite video signal |
| **Digital Logic** | Generates square waves from sinusoidal inputs |
| **Overvoltage Protection** | Limits voltage spikes at sensitive circuit inputs |
| **Function Generators** | Produces trapezoidal/triangular waves from sine waves |
| **Radar Systems** | Removes clutter and isolates target echoes |

## 1.4 Classification Overview

Clippers are broadly classified as:

1. **Based on Configuration:**
   - **Series Clipper** (diode in series with the load)
   - **Shunt (Parallel) Clipper** (diode in parallel with the load)

2. **Based on Clipped Region:**
   - **Positive Clipper** (clips the positive half-cycle)
   - **Negative Clipper** (clips the negative half-cycle)
   - **Biased Clipper** (clips above/below a non-zero reference level)
   - **Combinational Clipper** (clips both positive and negative halves — two-level clipper)

3. **Based on Diode Orientation:**
   - The diode's arrow direction determines **which half** is clipped.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Operating Principle

The clipping action is governed by a simple **decision rule** applied to the voltage at the diode terminals (anode-to-cathode potential $V_{AK}$):

$$
V_{AK} = V_A - V_K
$$

| Condition | Diode State | Equivalent Circuit | Result |
|---|---|---|---|
| $V_{AK} \geq V_F$ (≈ 0.7 V for Si) | **ON (Forward Biased)** | Closed switch / Battery $V_F$ | Excess voltage dropped across series resistor $R$ |
| $V_{AK} < V_F$ | **OFF (Reverse Biased)** | Open switch | No current; $V_{\text{out}}$ follows $V_{\text{in}}$ through $R$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Ideal Diode Assumption (for KTU analysis):** In most exam problems, the diode is treated as **ideal** ($V_F = 0\,\text{V}$). When the problem specifies a **silicon diode with $V_F = 0.7\,\text{V}$**, the clipping level shifts by 0.7 V. Always **read the problem statement carefully** for this distinction — it is a frequent marker of partial scoring in board exams.

## 2.2 The Two Canonical Configurations

### A. Series Clipper
The diode is placed **in series** with the load resistor $R_L$. The output is taken across $R_L$.

- **Diode ON:** Current flows → $V_{\text{out}} = V_{\text{in}} - V_F$
- **Diode OFF:** No current → $V_{\text{out}} = 0\,\text{V}$

### B. Shunt (Parallel) Clipper
The diode is placed **in parallel (shunt)** with the load. Output is taken across the parallel combination.

- **Diode ON:** Shunts the load → $V_{\text{out}} = V_F$ (clamped at $V_F$)
- **Diode OFF:** Open circuit → $V_{\text{out}} = V_{\text{in}}$

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet 📋

| # | Circuit Type | Diode Direction | Clipped Region (Ideal) | Clipped Region (Si, $V_F=0.7$V) | Output Equation (ON State) | Output Equation (OFF State) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Series Positive | Cathode → Output | Positive half | Above $+V_F$ | $V_{\text{out}} = 0$ | $V_{\text{out}} = V_{\text{in}}$ |
| 2 | Series Negative | Anode → Output | Negative half | Below $-V_F$ | $V_{\text{out}} = 0$ | $V_{\text{out}} = V_{\text{in}}$ |
| 3 | Shunt Positive | Cathode to GND | Positive half | Above $+V_F$ | $V_{\text{out}} = V_F$ | $V_{\text{out}} = V_{\text{in}}$ |
| 4 | Shunt Negative | Anode to GND | Negative half | Below $-V_F$ | $V_{\text{out}} = -V_F$ | $V_{\text{out}} = V_{\text{in}}$ |
| 5 | Biased Positive (ref $V_R$) | Cathode to $V_R$ | Above $V_R+V_F$ | Above $V_R + V_F$ | $V_{\text{out}} = V_R + V_F$ | $V_{\text{out}} = V_{\text{in}}$ |
| 6 | Biased Negative (ref $V_R$) | Anode to $V_R$ | Below $V_R - V_F$ | Below $V_R - V_F$ | $V_{\text{out}} = V_R - V_F$ | $V_{\text{out}} = V_{\text{in}}$ |

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $V_{\text{in}}$ | Input signal voltage | Volts (V) |
| $V_{\text{out}}$ | Output (clipped) voltage | Volts (V) |
| $V_F$ | Diode forward voltage drop | $0.7\,\text{V}$ (Si), $0.3\,\text{V}$ (Ge) |
| $V_R$ | External DC reference/bias voltage | Volts (V) |
| $R$ | Series current-limiting resistor | Ohms ($\Omega$) |
| $R_L$ | Load resistor | Ohms ($\Omega$) |
| $I_F$ | Forward current through diode | Amperes (A) |
| $V_{\text{breakdown}}$ | Zener breakdown voltage (for Zener clipper) | Volts (V) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Transfer Characteristic Rule:** The transfer characteristic curve of a clipper is a **piecewise-linear plot** of $V_{\text{out}}$ vs $V_{\text{in}}$. The **slope** of the unclipped region is **+1** (for unbiased clippers). The clipped region is a **horizontal line** at the clipping level. This is a high-frequency KTU question type!

## 2.4 Two-Level (Double-Ended) Combinational Clippers

These circuits use **two diodes** to clip **both** positive and negative halves independently, producing a **truncated sine wave** or a near-square wave.

**Configuration:** Two shunt diodes connected to two different reference voltages $V_1$ and $V_2$.

- If $V_2 < V_{\text{in}} < V_1$: Both diodes OFF → $V_{\text{out}} = V_{\text{in}}$
- If $V_{\text{in}} \geq V_1$: Top diode ON → $V_{\text{out}} = V_1$
- If $V_{\text{in}} \leq V_2$: Bottom diode ON → $V_{\text{out}} = V_2$

This produces a **symmetric or asymmetric amplitude-windowed** output.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Note 🛠️

In **automotive electronics**, shunt clippers are used across relay coils to suppress the **back-EMF spike** (induced voltage) generated when the relay is de-energized. Without this clipper, the spike (often several hundred volts) would destroy sensitive microcontroller I/O pins.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Shunt Positive Clipper (Ideal Diode)

**Problem:** A sinusoidal input $V_{\text{in}}(t) = 10 \sin(\omega t)\,$V is applied to a **shunt positive clipper** using an ideal diode ($V_F = 0$). The load resistance is $R_L = 1\,\text{k}\Omega$ connected from output to ground. Derive the output waveform equation and the transfer characteristic.

### Step-by-Step Analysis:

**Step 1: Identify the diode orientation and conduction rule.**

In a shunt positive clipper, the diode's **cathode is at the output node** and **anode is at ground**. Therefore, the diode conducts when $V_{\text{out}} > 0$.

$$
\text{Diode ON} \iff V_{\text{out}} > V_{\text{anode}} = 0 \iff V_{\text{in}} > 0
$$

**Step 2: Apply KVL for the ON state.**

When the diode is ON (ideal), it acts as a short circuit, clamping the output to ground.

$$
V_{\text{out}} = 0 \quad \text{for} \quad V_{\text{in}} > 0
$$

**Step 3: Apply KVL for the OFF state.**

When $V_{\text{in}} < 0$, the diode is reverse-biased (open). No current flows through the series resistor $R$. The voltage drop across $R$ is zero, so:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{out}} &= V_{\text{in}} - I \cdot R \\
I &= 0 \quad (\text{diode OFF, no current path}) \\
\therefore V_{\text{out}} &= V_{\text{in}} \quad \text{for} \quad V_{\text{in}} \leq 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Write the piecewise output expression.**

$$
\boxed{V_{\text{out}}(t) = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } 10\sin(\omega t) > 0 \\[4pt] 10\sin(\omega t) & \text{if } 10\sin(\omega t) \leq 0 \end{cases}}
$$

**Step 5: Peak Inverse Voltage (PIV) calculation.**

The maximum reverse voltage across the diode occurs at the negative peak of the input:

$$
\text{PIV} = \vert V_{\text{in,min}} \vert = \vert -10 \vert = 10\,\text{V}
$$

This is the voltage rating the diode must withstand during the OFF half-cycle.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Biased Shunt Negative Clipper (Silicon Diode)

**Problem:** A DC bias $V_{BB} = +3\,\text{V}$ is connected at the cathode of a shunt diode. The diode is silicon ($V_F = 0.7\,\text{V}$). Input: $V_{\text{in}}(t) = 10 \sin(\omega t)\,$V. Determine the output waveform.

### Step-by-Step Analysis:

**Step 1: Determine the threshold voltage.**

The diode conducts only when the **anode voltage exceeds the cathode voltage by $V_F$**.

- Anode = $V_{\text{out}} = V_{\text{in}}$ (when diode OFF, no current through $R$)
- Cathode = $V_{BB} = +3\,\text{V}$

Diode turns ON when:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{anode}} - V_{\text{cathode}} &\geq V_F \\
V_{\text{in}} - V_{BB} &\geq V_F \\
V_{\text{in}} &\geq V_{BB} + V_F = 3 + 0.7 = 3.7\,\text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Output during ON state.**

When ON, the output is clamped at the cathode potential plus $V_F$:

$$
V_{\text{out}} = V_{BB} + V_F = 3 + 0.7 = 3.7\,\text{V}
$$

**Step 3: Output during OFF state.**

When $V_{\text{in}} < 3.7\,\text{V}$, the diode is OFF and $V_{\text{out}} = V_{\text{in}}$.

**Step 4: Piecewise expression.**

$$
\boxed{V_{\text{out}}(t) = \begin{cases} 3.7\,\text{V} & \text{if } 10\sin(\omega t) \geq 3.7\,\text{V} \\[4pt] 10\sin(\omega t) & \text{if } 10\sin(\omega t) < 3.7\,\text{V} \end{cases}}
$$

**Step 5: Find the time at which clipping begins.**

Setting $10\sin(\omega t) = 3.7$:

$$
\sin(\omega t) = 0.37 \implies \omega t = \sin^{-1}(0.37) \approx 21.7^\circ
$$

So the output follows the input sinusoid for the first ~21.7° of the positive half-cycle, and is **clamped at 3.7 V** for the remaining 158.3° (until $\omega t$ passes 180° and the input drops below 3.7 V again).

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Two-Level (Asymmetric Window) Clipper

**Problem:** A two-level clipper uses two shunt silicon diodes with reference voltages $V_1 = +4\,\text{V}$ (top) and $V_2 = -2\,\text{V}$ (bottom). Input: $V_{\text{in}}(t) = 10 \sin(\omega t)\,$V. Plot the output.

### Step-by-Step Analysis:

**Step 1: Determine the conduction thresholds.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{out, top}} &= V_1 + V_F = 4 + 0.7 = 4.7\,\text{V} \\
V_{\text{out, bottom}} &= V_2 - V_F = -2 - 0.7 = -2.7\,\text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Three operating regions.**

$$
