# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define a voltage doubler. List its two main types and state one advantage of each.**

**Model Answer:**

A voltage doubler is a rectifier circuit that produces a DC output voltage approximately equal to **twice the peak value (2V_p)** of the applied AC input voltage. It is commonly used when a high DC voltage is required from a low-voltage AC source without employing a step-up transformer.

**Two main types:**

1. **Half-Wave Voltage Doubler (Villard / Greinacher cascade):** Uses two diodes and two capacitors in a cascade arrangement. **Advantage:** It does not require a center-tapped transformer, making it compact and economical.

2. **Full-Wave Voltage Doubler (Delon doubler):** Uses a center-tapped transformer with two diodes and two capacitors connected in series across the load. **Advantage:** It has a ripple frequency of 2f (twice the line frequency), resulting in **better filtering and lower ripple** compared to the half-wave doubler.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: What is the Peak Inverse Voltage (PIV) of each diode in a half-wave voltage doubler? Justify your answer.**

**Model Answer:**

The Peak Inverse Voltage (PIV) of each diode in a half-wave voltage doubler is **2V_p**, where V_p is the peak value of the AC input voltage.

**Justification:**

- **Diode D1:** When the AC source swings to its negative peak (−V_p), the cathode of D1 is held at +V_p by the pre-charged capacitor C1. Hence, the reverse voltage across D1 is:

$$
PIV_{D1} = V_{C1} - v_{s,min} = V_p - (-V_p) = 2V_p
$$

- **Diode D2:** When the AC source swings to its positive peak (+V_p), the cathode of D2 is held at +2V_p by capacitor C2, while its anode is at +V_p. Hence, the reverse voltage across D2 is:

$$
PIV_{D2} = V_{C2} - v_{s,max} = 2V_p - V_p = V_p
$$

However, in practical design, a **conservative PIV rating of 2V_p is used for both diodes** to account for transient overshoots and ensure reliability. This conservative rating is the value expected in KTU board examinations.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern:** In the KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Examination (ESE), Module 1 questions are presented in pairs (Choice A and Choice B). Students must answer **either** Choice A **or** Choice B. Each question carries **14 marks** distributed across two sub-parts (typically 7 + 7 marks).

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`

**Q: (a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **half-wave voltage doubler**. Derive the expression for its DC output voltage and the PIV rating of each diode. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A half-wave voltage doubler is supplied with 230 V, 50 Hz AC. The load current is 10 mA and each capacitor has a value of 50 µF. Calculate the **(i)** DC output voltage, **(ii)** PIV rating of each diode, and **(iii)** ripple voltage. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Circuit Description:**

A half-wave voltage doubler consists of an AC source $v_s(t) = V_p \sin(\omega t)$, two diodes D1 and D2, and two capacitors C1 and C2. The junction of D1's cathode and D2's anode is connected to the top of C1 (which is grounded at its bottom plate). The output is taken across C2.

**Operation — Positive Half-Cycle (0 to π):**

During the first positive half-cycle, the source voltage rises positively. Diode D1 is forward-biased and conducts, charging capacitor C1 to the peak input voltage V_p. The top plate of C1 is at +V_p and the bottom plate is at ground (0 V). Diode D2 is reverse-biased during this interval because its cathode is held at the source potential while its anode is also at source potential, but in a non-conducting state (the path through C1 is to ground).

**Operation — Negative Half-Cycle (π to 2π):**

During the subsequent negative half-cycle, the source voltage swings to −V_p. Diode D1 becomes reverse-biased (its cathode at +V_p is more positive than its anode at −V_p). The voltage at the junction node (anode of D2) is now the series sum of the source voltage and the voltage stored on C1:

$$
V_{junction} = v_s + V_{C1} = -V_p + V_p = 0 \text{ (at the start of the half-cycle)}
$$

However, as the source voltage reaches its negative peak (−V_p), the junction potential relative to the source becomes:

$$
V_{C2} = -v_s - V_{C1} \text{ (relative to source)}
$$

Wait — let me correct this using proper KVL. At the negative peak, the source is at −V_p. The voltage across C2 (from its top to ground) equals the source potential plus the C1 voltage (since they are in series-aiding configuration):

$$
V_{C2} = V_{C1} + |v_{s,min}| = V_p + V_p = 2V_p
$$

Diode D2 forward-biases during this peak, charging C2 to 2V_p. The output across C2 is therefore:

$$
\boxed{V_{out} = 2V_p}
$$

**PIV Derivation:**

- **PIV for D1:** During the negative half-cycle, D1 must block the series sum of the source negative peak and the C1 voltage. Hence, $PIV_{D1} = 2V_p$.
- **PIV for D2:** During the positive half-cycle, D2 must block the difference between the C2 voltage and the source positive peak. Hence, $PIV_{D2} = 2V_p$ (conservative design value).

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:** $V_{rms} = 230 \text{ V}$, $f = 50 \text{ Hz}$, $I_L = 10 \text{ mA} = 10 \times 10^{-3} \text{ A}$, $C = 50 \text{ \mu F} = 50 \times 10^{-6} \text{ F}$.

**(i) DC Output Voltage:**

$$
V_p = V_{rms} \times \sqrt{2} = 230 \times 1.414 = 325.22 \text{ V}
$$

$$
V_{out} = 2V_p = 2 \times 325.22 = 650.44 \text{ V}
$$

**(ii) PIV Rating:**

$$
PIV = 2V_p = 2 \times 325.22 = 650.44 \text{ V} \approx 651 \text{ V}
$$

A diode with PIV rating ≥ 800 V is recommended for safety.

**(iii) Ripple Voltage:**

$$
V_r = \dfrac{I_L}{fC} = \dfrac{10 \times 10^{-3}}{50 \times 50 \times 10^{-6}} = \dfrac{10 \times 10^{-3}}{2.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 4 \text{ V}
$$

**Final Answer:** $V_{out} = 650.44 \text{ V}$, $PIV = 650.44 \text{ V}$, $V_r = 4 \text{ V}$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the working of a **Cockcroft-Walton voltage multiplier** with a neat diagram. Derive the expression for **(i)** the no-load output voltage and **(ii)** the ripple voltage for an n-stage multiplier. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare a **voltage tripler** with a **voltage quadrupler** in terms of circuit topology, output voltage, number of components, and typical applications. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Circuit Description:**

A Cockcroft-Walton multiplier is constructed as a ladder network of n diodes and n capacitors connected in a specific cascade arrangement. The AC source feeds the bottom of the ladder, and the output is taken at the topmost capacitor's top plate. The diodes are arranged so that alternate capacitors charge on alternate half-cycles, progressively stacking up the voltage.

**Operation:**

- **Stage 1 (Bottom):** Capacitor C1 charges to V_p via diode D1 during the positive half-cycle.
- **Stage 2:** During the negative half-cycle, C2 charges to 2V_p via D2 (the voltage of C1 plus the source's negative peak).
- **Stage 3:** During the next positive half-cycle, C3 charges to 2V_p above the C2 voltage, reaching 3V_p at the top.
- This process continues for n stages, with each pair of stages contributing an additional 2V_p.

**Derivation of (i) — No-Load Output Voltage:**

Each pair of stages (one diode-capacitor pair per half-cycle) adds 2V_p. For n stages:

$$
\boxed{V_{out,NL} = 2nV_p}
$$

**Derivation of (ii) — Ripple Voltage:**

The ripple voltage arises because each stage capacitor must supply the load current I_L for half a cycle (T/2 = 1/(2f)) before being recharged. The droop across a single capacitor in one half-cycle is:

$$
\Delta V = \dfrac{I_L \cdot (1/2f)}{C} = \dfrac{I_L}{2fC}
$$

However, the topmost stage experiences the droop of all n capacitors, the next stage experiences the droop of (n−1) capacitors, and so on. The total ripple is the sum of an arithmetic series:

$$
V_r = \Delta V + 2\Delta V + 3\Delta V + \ldots + n\Delta V = \Delta V \cdot \dfrac{n(n+1)}{2}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\boxed{V_r = \dfrac{I_L}{2fC} \cdot \dfrac{n(n+1)}{2} = \dfrac{I_L \cdot n(n+1)}{4fC}}
$$

The **simplified alternate form** used in many textbooks and KTU references is:

$$
V_r \approx \dfrac{I_L}{fC} \cdot \dfrac{n(n+1)}{2}
$$

depending on whether the droop is counted per half-cycle or per full cycle. Both forms are accepted by examiners; the key is to clearly state the assumed cycle.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

| Parameter | Voltage Tripler | Voltage Quadrupler |
|-----------|----------------|---------------------|
| **Output Voltage** | $V_{out} = 3V_p$ | $V_{out} = 4V_p$ |
| **Number of Diodes** | 3 (D1, D2, D3) | 4 (D1, D2, D3, D4) |
| **Number of Capacitors** | 3 (C1, C2, C3) | 4 (C1, C2, C3, C4) |
| **Topology** | Half-wave doubler + one extra stage | Two cascaded half-wave doublers |
| **PIV of each diode** | $2V_p$ | $2V_p$ |
| **Ripple frequency** | $f$ (line frequency) | $f$ (line frequency) |
| **Typical Applications** | Bias supplies for specialized tubes, moderate-voltage laboratory supplies | High-voltage test equipment, electron beam sources |
| **Cumulative voltage on capacitors** | C1: V_p, C2: 2V_p, C3: 3V_p | C1: V_p, C2: 2V_p, C3: 2V_p, C4: 4V_p |

**Key Difference:** A tripler is essentially a doubler with one extra stage that adds V_p, while a quadrupler adds another full 2V_p stage. The quadrupler requires double the components of a doubler, while the tripler requires 1.5× the components of a doubler. For the same peak input, the quadrupler delivers a higher output but with **proportionally higher ripple** and worse regulation.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones — Avoid These Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the √2 conversion:** Many students write $V_{out} = 2 \times 230 = 460 \text{ V}$ instead of correctly computing $V_p = V_{rms} \times \sqrt{2}$ first. This single error costs **3 marks** in numerical problems.
>
> 2. **Confusing PIV for D1 and D2:** In half-wave doublers, students often write $PIV_{D1} = V_p$ (incorrect) and $PIV_{D2} = 2V_p$. The correct conservative design value is $PIV = 2V_p$ for **both** diodes.
>
> 3. **Using the wrong ripple frequency:** A full-wave doubler has $f_r = 2f$, NOT $f$. Using $f$ in the ripple formula for a full-wave doubler gives a ripple value **twice as large** as the correct answer.
>
> 4. **Forgetting to state the ripple formula derivation basis:** In the Cockcroft-Walton multiplier, the ripple formula has two forms (per half-cycle vs per full cycle). Always **explicitly state** which assumption is being used to avoid losing method marks.
>
> 5. **Not drawing the circuit diagram with proper polarity markings:** KTU examiners specifically look for **clearly marked diode directions and capacitor polarities** in the circuit diagram. A diagram without polarity marks loses at least **2 marks** in a 7-mark sub-part.
>
> 6. **Mixing up the formula for n-stage CW multiplier:** Some students write $V_{out} = nV_p$ instead of $V_{out} = 2nV_p$. The factor of 2 comes from the fact that **each pair of stages** contributes 2V_p, not V_p.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Voltage Multipliers (KTU Module 1)**

- A **voltage multiplier** produces a DC output that is an integer multiple (2×, 3×, 4×, …, 2n×) of the peak AC input voltage V_p.
- The **half-wave doubler** uses 2 diodes + 2 capacitors, output = 2V_p, ripple frequency = f, PIV per diode = 2V_p.
- The **full-wave doubler (Delon)** uses a center-tapped transformer, 2 diodes + 2 capacitors, output = 2V_p, ripple frequency = 2f, PIV per diode = 2V_p.
- The **voltage tripler** uses 3 diodes + 3 capacitors, output = 3V_p, PIV per diode = 2V_p.
- The **voltage quadrupler** uses 4 diodes + 4 capacitors, output = 4V_p, PIV per diode = 2V_p.
- The **Cockcroft-Walton multiplier** is an n-stage cascade producing $V_{out} = 2nV_p$ with ripple $V_r = \dfrac{I_L}{fC} \cdot \dfrac{n(n+1)}{2}$.
- The **optimum number of stages** for a Cockcroft-Walton multiplier is $n_{opt} = \sqrt{\dfrac{V_p \cdot f \cdot C}{I_L}}$.
- **Ripple voltage** is inversely proportional to the line frequency f and the capacitance C. Doubling either halves the ripple.
- **Regulation worsens** as the number of stages n increases, due to cumulative droop across each stage capacitor.
- Voltage multipliers are used in **high-voltage, low-current** applications: CRT displays, X-ray equipment, laser power supplies, particle accelerators, electrostatic precipitators, and photomultiplier tube (PMT) bias networks.
- The **peak inverse voltage (PIV)** of each diode in cascade multiplier topologies is conservatively rated as **2V_p** in KTU board exam solutions.
- Always **convert RMS to peak** ($V_p = V_{rms} \times \sqrt{2}$) at the start of any numerical problem — this is a **very common** first step that students forget.
- In the **Cockcroft-Walton ripple derivation**, clearly state whether the droop is measured per half-cycle or per full cycle to avoid ambiguity.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transistor Biasing - Concept of DC and AC Load Lines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAET302 - ELECTRONIC DEVICES AND CIRCUITS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Transistor Biasing - Concept of DC and AC Load Lines ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Transistor Biasing** is the systematic process of applying external DC voltages to a Bipolar Junction Transistor (BJT) to establish a stable, predefined **Quiescent Operating Point (Q-point)** on its output characteristics. The Q-point is defined by the DC collector current $I_C$ and collector-emitter voltage $V_{CE}$ in the absence of any input AC signal. The **DC Load Line** is the graphical locus of all possible DC operating points $(I_C, V_{CE})$ on the output characteristics of a transistor, traced by applying Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) to the output (collector-emitter) loop under DC conditions. The **AC Load Line** is the locus of the instantaneous operating point $(i_c, v_{ce})$ of the transistor when a small AC signal is superimposed on the DC bias. It is steeper than the DC load line and passes through the Q-point, governed by the **AC load resistance** $R_{AC} = R_C \parallel R_L$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The student must clearly distinguish between the **DC Load Line** (used for biasing analysis with $R_{DC}$) and the **AC Load Line** (used for small-signal analysis with $R_{AC}$). A common error is using $R_C$ alone for AC analysis — this is incorrect when a load resistor $R_L$ is coupled via a capacitor. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a car driving up a hill with a strong crosswind. The car has a **base speed** (engine setting) — this is analogous to the **DC bias** (it sets the resting position). The crosswind pushing the car represents the **AC signal** (it causes the car to oscillate around its base speed). - If the base speed is too low, the crosswind can push the car backward (signal clipping, distortion). - If the base speed is too high, the car may topple (transistor saturation/damage). - The **Q-point** is the optimal base speed that allows the crosswind to swing the car equally forward and backward without stalling or rolling over. **The DC Load Line** is the entire road the car can travel on (the allowed region of operation). **The AC Load Line** is a smaller, steeper path the car takes as the wind pushes it back and forth. ### 1.3 Why is Biasing Necessary? - To turn the transistor **ON** and place it in the **Active Region**. - To maintain a **stable Q-point** independent of temperature variations, $\beta$ (hFE) variations, and transistor replacement. - To allow **undistorted amplification** of the AC signal (avoiding cutoff and saturation clipping). > [!NOTE] > **Standard Reference Values:** For linear (Class A) amplification, the ideal Q-point is typically placed at the **midpoint** of the DC load line, i.e., $I_C \approx I_{C(sat)}/2$ and $V_{CE} \approx V_{CC}/2$. This gives maximum symmetrical AC swing. ### 1.4 Types of Transistor Biasing Circuits (KTU 2024 Focus) | Biasing Method | Stability | KTU Relevance | |---|---|---| | **Fixed Bias (Base Bias)** | Poor | Often asked as a contrast example | | **Collector-to-Base Bias** | Moderate | Common numerical problem | | **Voltage Divider Bias** | Excellent | **Most important** — high-yield topic | | **Emitter Bias** | Good | Frequently combined with voltage divider | ### 1.5 Stability Factor The **Stability Factor** $S$ quantifies how much the Q-point (specifically $I_C$) drifts with temperature. It is defined as: $$S = \frac{\partial I_C}{\partial I_{CO}} \approx \frac{1 + \beta}{1 + \beta \left( \frac{R_E}{R_E + R_B} \right)}$$ - For **Fixed Bias**: $S = 1 + \beta$ (worst stability) - For **Voltage Divider Bias**: $S \approx 1$ (best stability, as $R_E$ dominates) > [!NOTE] > A **smaller stability factor** means **better thermal stability**. Voltage divider bias achieves this by making the base voltage almost independent of $\beta$. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic #### **A. DC Load Line Derivation Logic** 1. Apply KVL around the **output loop** (Collector → Emitter → Ground → $V_{CC}$). 2. The collector current is governed by the supply voltage $V_{CC}$ and the DC resistance in the path. 3. Identify the two extreme points of the DC load line: - **Saturation Point (B):** $V_{CE} = 0 \Rightarrow I_C = I_{C(sat)}$ - **Cutoff Point (A):** $I_C = 0 \Rightarrow V_{CE} = V_{CC}$ 4. Draw a straight line between A and B on the $I_C$ vs $V_{CE}$ graph. #### **B. Q-Point Establishment Logic** - The Q-point is the **intersection** of the DC load line with the transistor's specific $I_B$ curve (set by the bias network). - It represents the **no-signal** operating condition. #### **C. AC Load Line Logic** 1. When an AC signal $v_i$ is applied, the operating point **oscillates** around the Q-point. 2. The instantaneous collector current and voltage follow the AC load line equation. 3. The **slope** of the AC load line is determined by $R_{AC} = R_C \parallel R_L$. 4. The AC load line is **steeper** than the DC load line (because $R_{AC} < R_C$ when $R_L$ is present). ### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $I_{C(sat)} = \dfrac{V_{CC}}{R_C + R_E}$ | Saturation collector current (with $R_E$ unbypassed) | A | | 2 | $V_{CE(cutoff)} = V_{CC}$ | Cutoff collector-emitter voltage | V | | 3 | $V_{CC} = I_C R_C + V_{CE} + I_E R_E$ | KVL output loop | V | | 4 | $R_{DC} = R_C + R_E$ | DC load resistance (seen by collector) | $\Omega$ | | 5 | $R_{AC} = R_C \parallel R_L = \dfrac{R_C R_L}{R_C + R_L}$ | AC load resistance | $\Omega$ | | 6 | $I_{CQ} = \dfrac{V_{CC} - V_{CEQ}}{R_C + R_E}$ | DC collector current at Q-point | A | | 7 | $V_{CEQ} = V_{CC} - I_{CQ}(R_C + R_E)$ | DC collector-emitter voltage at Q-point | V | | 8 | $i_c(\text{max}) = I_{CQ} + \dfrac{V_{CEQ}}{R_{AC}}$ | Max positive swing of collector current | A | | 9 | $v_{ce}(\text{max}) = V_{CEQ} + I_{CQ} R_{AC}$ | Max positive swing of $V_{CE}$ | V | | 10 | $S = \dfrac{1 + \beta}{1 - \beta \dfrac{\partial I_B}{\partial I_C}}$ | General stability factor | — | | 11 | $V_{BB} = I_B R_B + V_{BE} + I_E R_E$ | KVL input loop (voltage divider approximation) | V | | 12 | $V_B = V_{CC} \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$ | Base voltage (voltage divider bias) | V | | 13 | $I_C \approx I_E = \dfrac{V_B - V_{BE}}{R_E}$ | Emitter current (voltage divider) | A | | 14 | $g_m = \dfrac{I_{CQ}}{V_T}$ | Transconductance at Q-point | S (Siemens) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Rule:** All currents and voltages in the DC load line are **DC quantities (uppercase, $I_C$, $V_{CE}$)**. In the AC load line, they are **instantaneous (lowercase, $i_c$, $v_{ce}$)** with both DC bias and AC signal components. ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Audio Amplifiers (Class A):** DC load line determines quiescent power dissipation; AC load line determines maximum **unclipped output swing**. - **RF Power Amplifiers:** Q-point placement at Class AB reduces crossover distortion — the AC load line intersects the cutoff region partially. - **Op-Amp Output Stages:** Push-pull configurations use AC load lines that differ for positive and negative half-cycles. - **Switching Power Supplies:** BJT operates in **saturation/cutoff only** — Q-point moves along the DC load line between endpoints. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Detailed Derivation 1: DC Load Line Equation **Starting Point:** Apply KVL to the collector-emitter loop of a BJT with emitter resistor $R_E$ (unbypassed for thermal stability). **Step 1: Write the KVL Equation** Starting from $V_{CC}$, going through $R_C$, then collector-emitter junction, then $R_E$ back to ground: $$V_{CC} = I_C R_C + V_{CE} + I_E R_E$$ **Step 2: Apply the BJT Approximation** Since $I_C \approx I_E$ (because $\alpha \approx 1$), we substitute $I_E \approx I_C$: $$V_{CC} = I_C R_C + V_{CE} + I_C R_E$$ **Step 3: Rearrange into the Standard Load Line Form** Grouping the $I_C$ terms: $$V_{CC} = I_C (R_C + R_E) + V_{CE}$$ Solving for $I_C$: $$I_C = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{CE}}{R_C + R_E}$$ This is the **DC load line equation** — a linear relation between $I_C$ and $V_{CE}$ with slope $-1/(R_C + R_E)$. **Step 4: Identify the Endpoints** - **When $V_{CE} = 0$ (Saturation, point B):** $$I_{C(sat)} = \frac{V_{CC}}{R_C + R_E}$$ - **When $I_C = 0$ (Cutoff, point A):** $$V_{CE(cutoff)} = V_{CC}$$ **Step 5: Express as Linear Function** $$I_C = -\frac{1}{R_C + R_E} \cdot V_{CE} + \frac{V_{CC}}{R_C + R_E}$$ This is in the form $y = mx + c$ where $y = I_C$, $x = V_{CE}$, slope $m = -1/(R_C+R_E)$, and y-intercept $c = V_{CC}/(R_C+R_E)$. --- ### 3.2 Detailed Derivation 2: AC Load Line Equation **Starting Point:** When an AC input signal is applied, the transistor's instantaneous collector current $i_C$ and collector-emitter voltage $v_{CE}$ can be decomposed into DC and AC components: $$i_C = I_{CQ} + i_c \quad \text{(where } i_c \text{ is the AC part)}$$ $$v_{CE} = V_{CEQ} + v_{ce} \quad \text{(where } v_{ce} \text{ is the AC part)}$$ **Step 1: AC Equivalent Circuit Analysis** In the AC equivalent circuit, the DC source $V_{CC}$ becomes a short (AC ground). The coupling capacitor $C_C$ becomes a short for the signal frequency. Thus, the AC collector sees $R_C$ in parallel with $R_L$: $$R_{AC} = R_C \parallel R_L = \frac{R_C \cdot R_L}{R_C + R_L}$$ **Step 2: Write the AC KVL Equation** The AC voltage drop across $R_{AC}$ must equal the negative of the AC collector-emitter voltage (signal phase inversion): $$v_{ce} = -i_c \cdot R_{AC}$$ **Step 3: Substitute into the DC + AC Decomposition** We have $v_{CE} = V_{CEQ} + v_{ce}$ and $i_C = I_{CQ} + i_c$. Substituting $v_{ce} = -i_c R_{AC}$: $$v_{CE} = V_{CEQ} - (i_C - I_{CQ}) \cdot R_{AC}$$ **Step 4: Rearrange to Obtain the AC Load Line Equation** $$v_{CE} = -i_C R_{AC} + (V_{CEQ} + I_{CQ} R_{AC})$$ This is again a straight line with: - **Slope** = $-1/R_{AC}$ - **Y-intercept** (where $v_{CE} = 0$): $$i_{C(sat,AC)} = \frac{V_{CEQ} + I_{CQ} R_{AC}}{R_{AC}} = I_{CQ} + \frac{V_{CEQ}}{R_{AC}}$$ - **X-intercept** (where $i_C = 0$): $$v_{CE(cutoff,AC)} = V_{CEQ} + I_{CQ} R_{AC}$$ **Step 5: Verify the Line Passes Through the Q-Point** When $i_C = I_{CQ}$: $$v_{CE} = V_{CEQ} - 0 = V_{CEQ} \quad \checkmark$$ This confirms the AC load line passes through the Q-point $(I_{CQ}, V_{CEQ})$. --- ### 3.3 Detailed Derivation 3: Maximum Symmetrical Swing For maximum **unclipped** (undistorted) output, the Q-point must be centered on the AC load line. The maximum possible swing in each direction is: **Maximum positive swing of $i_c$:** $$i_c(+)_{\text{max}} = I_{CQ} \quad \text{(limited by cutoff)}$$ **Maximum negative swing of $i_c$:** $$i_c(-)_{\text{max}} = I_{CQ} - i_{C(sat,AC)} = -\frac{V_{CEQ}}{R_{AC}}$$ For symmetry: $I_{CQ} = \dfrac{V_{CEQ}}{R_{AC}}$ Solving these two equations: $$I_{CQ} = \frac{V_{CEQ}}{R_{AC}} \quad \text{and} \quad I_{CQ} = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{CEQ}}{R_C + R_E}$$ Setting them equal: $$\frac{V_{CEQ}}{R_{AC}} = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{CEQ}}{R_C + R_E}$$ Cross-multiplying: $$V_{CEQ} (R_C + R_E) = R_{AC} (V_{CC} - V_{CEQ})$$ $$V_{CEQ} (R_C + R_E + R_{AC}) = R_{AC} \cdot V_{CC}$$ $$\boxed{V_{CEQ} = \frac{R_{AC} \cdot V_{CC}}{R_C + R_E + R_{AC}}}$$ This gives the **optimal Q-point** for maximum symmetrical AC swing. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Load Lines | Parameter | DC Load Line | AC Load Line | |---|---|---| | **Source of equation** | DC KVL with $V_{CC}$ | AC equivalent circuit | | **Slope** | $-\dfrac{1}{R_C + R_E}$ | $-\dfrac{1}{R_{AC}}$ | | **Passes through Q-point** | Yes | Yes | | **Used for** | Biasing design, Q-point stability | Small-signal analysis, max swing | | **X-intercept (cutoff)** | $V_{CC}$ | $V_{CEQ} + I_{CQ} R_{AC}$ | | **Y-intercept (saturation)** | $\dfrac{V_{CC}}{R_C + R_E}$ | $I_{CQ} + \dfrac{V_{CEQ}}{R_{AC}}$ | | **Steeper?** | No (less steep) | **Yes (steeper)** | | **When is it valid?** | At DC, no signal | When AC signal is present | | **Tools needed** | $V_{CC}$, $R_C$, $R_E$ | $R_C$, $R_L$, Q-point | --- ### 3.5 Stability Factor Derivation (Voltage Divider Bias) The collector current in a BJT is: $$I_C = \beta I_B + (1 + \beta) I_{CO}$$ Taking the partial derivative with respect to $I_{CO}$ (holding $I_B$ constant) yields the standard form: $$S = \frac{\partial I_C}{\partial I_{CO}} \Big|_{I_B = \text{const}}$$ For the voltage divider bias with $R_E$ unbypassed: $$S = \frac{1 + \beta}{1 + \beta \left( \frac{R_E}{R_E + R_B'} \right)}$$ where $R_B' = R_1 \parallel R_2$ (Thevenin resistance seen at the base). > [!NOTE] > As $R_E$ increases or $R_B'$ decreases, $S \to 1$, indicating **excellent stability**. This is why **voltage divider bias** is the industry standard. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 BJT Common-Emitter Circuit with Voltage Divider Bias ```mermaid graph TB VCC[VCC DC Supply]:::supply R1[R1 Collector Resistor]:::resistor R2[R2 Base Bias Upper]:::resistor R3[R3 Base Bias Lower]:::resistor RE[RE Emitter Resistor]:::resistor RC[RC Collector Resistor]:::resistor RL[RL AC Load Resistor]:::resistor CC1[CC1 Input Coupling]:::capacitor CC2[CC2 Output Coupling]:::capacitor CE[CE Emitter Bypass]:::capacitor V_in[AC Input Signal vi]:::input V_out[AC Output Signal vo]:::output BJT[NPN BJT]:::transistor GND[Ground Reference]:::ground VCC --- R1 R1 --- R2 R2 --- R3 R3 --- GND VCC --- RC RC --- BJT BJT --- RE RE --- GND BJT --- CC2 CC2 --- RL RL --- GND V_in --- CC1 CC1 --- BJT RE --- CE CE --- GND CC2 --- V_out classDef supply fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef resistor fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef capacitor fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef transistor fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef input fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef output fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef ground fill:#8B4513,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF ``` --- ### 4.2 DC Load Line and AC Load Line Visualization (Output Characteristics) ```mermaid graph TB subgraph Y_AXIS[Vertical Axis: IC Collector Current] IC0[IC max]:::yaxis end subgraph X_AXIS[Horizontal Axis: VCE Collector Emitter Voltage] VCE0[VCC]:::xaxis end Q1[Point A Cutoff: IC 0, VCE VCC]:::cutoff Q2[Point B Saturation: IC ICsat, VCE 0]:::saturation Q3[Point Q Q point: ICQ, VCEQ]:::qpoint Q4[Point C AC Saturation]:::acsat Q5[Point D AC Cutoff]:::accutoff DCLINE[DC Load Line Slope minus 1 over RC plus RE]:::dcline ACLINE[AC Load Line Steeper through Q]:::acline Q1 --- DCLINE Q2 --- DCLINE Q3 --- DCLINE Q3 --- ACLINE Q4 --- ACLINE Q5 --- ACLINE classDef yaxis fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef xaxis fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef cutoff fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef saturation fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#FFF classDef qpoint fill:#32CD32,stroke:#000,color:#000 classDef acsat fill:#4169E1,stroke:#000,color:#FFF classDef accutoff fill:#4169E1,stroke:#000,color:#FFF classDef dcline fill:#FF4500,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFF classDef acline fill:#1E90FF,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFF ``` --- ### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: AC Signal Swing on AC Load Line ```mermaid graph LR A[DC Bias Network

via Coupling Capacitor]:::input D[Operating Point Oscillates
