**Bias Stabilization** refers to the set of circuit design techniques employed in transistor amplifiers to maintain the quiescent operating point (Q-point) of the transistor at a fixed, predetermined location on the DC load line, irrespective of variations in three primary external/internal parameters:

1. **Temperature ($T$)** — Thermal drift of $I_{CBO}$, $V_{BE}$, and $\beta$.
2. **Transistor Current Gain ($\beta$ or $h_{FE}$)** — Manufacturing spread (typically 50 to 500 for the same part number).
3. **Supply Voltage ($V_{CC}$ or $V_{EE}$)** — Line voltage fluctuations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stability Factor ($S$):** The rate of change of collector current ($I_C$) with respect to collector-emitter leakage current ($I_{CBO}$), keeping $\beta$ and $V_{BE}$ constant. Mathematically:
>
> $$S = \frac{\partial I_C}{\partial I_{CBO}} \approx \frac{\Delta I_C}{\Delta I_{CBO}} \bigg|_{\beta, V_{BE} = \text{constant}}$$

A perfectly stable circuit has $S = 1$ (only the leakage current changes; $I_C$ does not amplify this drift). Any practical stabilization technique aims to drive $S$ as close to 1 as possible.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Cruise Control Analogy** 🚗
>
> Imagine you are driving a car on a highway and want to maintain a constant speed of **60 km/h**. The road has hills (analogous to **temperature changes**), the wind keeps changing (analogous to **$\beta$ variations**), and the fuel quality fluctuates (analogous to **$V_{CC}$ variations**). Without a **cruise control system**, your speed would keep drifting up and down as these disturbances occur.
>
> **Bias stabilization is the "cruise control" of the transistor's Q-point.** It senses the drift and automatically adjusts the bias network to keep the transistor's operating point locked in the middle of the active region, just like cruise control keeps the car at a steady speed.
>
> The **Stability Factor $S$** is analogous to **sensitivity** — a low $S$ means the system is *insensitive* to disturbances (great stability), while a high $S$ means the system *amplifies* the drift (poor stability).

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Silicon transistor base-emitter voltage drift:** $V_{BE}$ decreases by approximately **$\mathbf{2.5 \text{ mV/}^\circ\text{C}}$**.
- **Thermal voltage at room temperature:** $V_T = \dfrac{kT}{q} \approx \mathbf{25.85 \text{ mV}}$ at $T = 300\text{ K}$.
- **$I_{CBO}$ doubling rule:** $I_{CBO}$ approximately **doubles for every 10 °C rise** in junction temperature.
- **Stability factor range for a well-designed amplifier:** $S \leq \mathbf{10}$ (acceptable), ideally approaching 1.

---

### 1.4 Why Stabilization is Necessary

The collector current of a BJT in the active region is given by:

$$I_C = \beta I_B + (\beta + 1) I_{CBO}$$

Three factors cause $I_C$ to drift:

| Drift Source | Physical Mechanism | Direction of Drift |
|---|---|---|
| Temperature rise | $I_{CBO}$ doubles every 10 °C; $V_{BE}$ drops by 2.5 mV/°C | $I_C$ **increases** |
| $\beta$ increase (replacement) | Higher gain amplifies any $I_B$ error | $I_C$ **increases** |
| $V_{CC}$ variation | Changes $I_B$ through bias resistors | $I_C$ **changes** |

If $I_C$ drifts unchecked, the Q-point may slide into **saturation** (clipped top half of output) or **cutoff** (clipped bottom half), causing **distortion** of the amplified signal.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Stability Factors (Generalized Definition)

KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes the **three independent stability factors** for a complete thermal/electrical analysis:

$$S_I = \frac{\partial I_C}{\partial I_{CBO}} \bigg|_{\beta, V_{BE}} \quad \text{(Thermal stability)}$$

$$S_{V_{BE}} = \frac{\partial I_C}{\partial V_{BE}} \bigg|_{\beta, I_{CBO}} \quad \text{(V}_{BE}\text{ drift stability)}$$

$$S_\beta = \frac{\partial I_C}{\partial \beta} \bigg|_{V_{BE}, I_{CBO}} \quad \text{(}\beta\text{ replacement stability)}$$

The total change in collector current is then:

$$\Delta I_C = S_I \, \Delta I_{CBO} + S_{V_{BE}} \, \Delta V_{BE} + S_\beta \, \Delta \beta$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** The traditional $S = \dfrac{\partial I_C}{\partial I_{CBO}}$ is often just called "Stability Factor" or $S_I$. The other two ($S_{V_{BE}}$ and $S_\beta$) are frequently asked as **Part A (3-mark)** questions or as sub-parts of a 14-mark problem.

---

### 2.2 Breakdown of Stabilization Logic — Step by Step

A bias stabilization circuit achieves stability by introducing **DC negative feedback**. The principle is:

1. **Sense the output current** ($I_C$) or a related voltage.
2. **Feed back a corrective signal** to the base-emitter loop that *opposes* the original drift.
3. If $I_C$ tends to rise due to temperature, the feedback mechanism *reduces* $I_B$ (or $V_{BE}$), pulling $I_C$ back to its design value.

This is the **fundamental "Why"** of every biasing topology.

---

### 2.3 Common Biasing Topologies and Their Stability

#### (a) Fixed Bias (Base Bias)

A single resistor $R_B$ from $V_{CC}$ to the base sets $I_B$ constant.

$$I_B = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{BE}}{R_B}, \quad I_C = \beta I_B$$

**Stability Factor:**

$$S = 1 + \beta$$

This is the **worst** stability — a $\beta = 100$ transistor gives $S = 101$, meaning a 1 µA change in $I_{CBO}$ causes a 101 µA change in $I_C$.

#### (b) Collector-to-Base Bias

A resistor $R_B$ connects the collector back to the base.

**KVL around base-emitter loop:**

$$V_{CC} = I_C R_C + I_B R_B + V_{BE}$$

With $I_B = \dfrac{I_C - I_{CBO}}{\beta + 1}$:

$$V_{CC} = I_C R_C + \left(\frac{I_C - I_{CBO}}{\beta + 1}\right) R_B + V_{BE}$$

**Stability Factor:**

$$S = \frac{1 + \beta}{1 + \beta \cdot \dfrac{R_C}{R_C + R_B}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Since $R_C \gg R_B$ is not always possible, this bias offers moderate improvement. Best when $R_C$ is large.

#### (c) Voltage Divider Bias (Self-Bias / Universal Bias)

The most stable configuration. Two resistors $R_1$ (upper) and $R_2$ (lower) form a voltage divider, and an emitter resistor $R_E$ provides DC negative feedback.

**Using Thevenin's theorem at the base:**

$$V_{TH} = V_{CC} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}, \quad R_{TH} = \frac{R_1 R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

**Stability Factor:**

$$S = \frac{1 + \beta}{1 + \beta \cdot \dfrac{R_E}{R_E + R_{TH}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As $R_E \to \infty$ or $R_{TH} \to 0$, $S \to 1$ (perfect stability). This is why voltage divider bias with **large $R_E$** and **small $R_{TH}$** is the gold standard in KTU exam problems.

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I_C = \beta I_B + (\beta+1) I_{CBO}$ | Fundamental BJT current relation | A |
| 2 | $S = \dfrac{\partial I_C}{\partial I_{CBO}} = \dfrac{1+\beta}{1-\beta \dfrac{\partial I_B}{\partial I_C}}$ | General stability factor expression | dimensionless |
| 3 | $S_{\text{fixed}} = 1 + \beta$ | Fixed bias stability | dimensionless |
| 4 | $S_{\text{col-base}} = \dfrac{1+\beta}{1 + \beta \left(\dfrac{R_C}{R_C + R_B}\right)}$ | Collector-to-base bias stability | dimensionless |
| 5 | $S_{\text{VD}} = \dfrac{1+\beta}{1 + \beta \left(\dfrac{R_E}{R_E + R_{TH}}\right)}$ | Voltage divider bias stability | dimensionless |
| 6 | $I_{CBO}(T_2) = I_{CBO}(T_1) \cdot 2^{(T_2 - T_1)/10}$ | $I_{CBO}$ doubling per 10 °C | A |
| 7 | $V_{BE}(T) = V_{BE}(T_0) - 2.5 \text{ mV} \cdot (T - T_0)$ | $V_{BE}$ thermal coefficient | V |
| 8 | $V_{TH} = V_{CC} \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$ | Thevenin voltage at base | V |
| 9 | $R_{TH} = \dfrac{R_1 R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$ | Thevenin resistance at base | Ω |
| 10 | $V_{CE} = V_{CC} - I_C R_C - I_E R_E$ | Output voltage equation | V |

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Audio amplifiers:** Voltage divider bias keeps the Q-point centered to avoid clipping during long listening sessions where the amplifier warms up.
- **Industrial sensors:** Transducers in field-deployed circuits see temperature swings from −20 °C to +70 °C — without bias stabilization, the circuit would saturate in summer.
- **IC design:** Every operational amplifier IC (like the $\mu$A741) uses an internal **current mirror** — a sophisticated form of bias stabilization that achieves $S \approx 1$.
- **Power electronics:** Driver stages for MOSFETs/IGBTs need $I_C$ stable to within $\pm 5\%$ across the full operating range to prevent thermal runaway.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the General Stability Factor Formula

We start from the fundamental BJT equation:

$$
I_C = \beta I_B + (\beta + 1) I_{CBO}
$$

**Step 1: Differentiate both sides with respect to $I_C$.**

Differentiating gives:

$$
1 = \beta \frac{\partial I_B}{\partial I_C} + (\beta + 1) \frac{\partial I_{CBO}}{\partial I_C}
$$

But $\partial I_{CBO} / \partial I_C = 1/S$ by the definition of stability factor. So:

$$
1 = \beta \frac{\partial I_B}{\partial I_C} + \frac{\beta + 1}{S}
$$

**Step 2: Rearrange to isolate $S$.**

$$
\frac{\beta + 1}{S} = 1 - \beta \frac{\partial I_B}{\partial I_C}
$$

$$
S = \frac{\beta + 1}{1 - \beta \dfrac{\partial I_B}{\partial I_C}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,S = \frac{1 + \beta}{1 - \beta \left(\dfrac{\partial I_B}{\partial I_C}\right)}\,}
$$

This is the **master stability factor equation** that all specific biasing topologies reduce to.

---

### 3.2 Detailed Derivation: Stability Factor for Fixed Bias

**Step 1: Base loop KVL.**

In a fixed-bias circuit, the base current is set purely by $V_{CC}$ and $R_B$:

$$
I_B = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{BE}}{R_B}
$$

**Step 2: Find $\partial I_B / \partial I_C$.**

Since $V_{CC}$ and $V_{BE}$ are treated as constants for this partial derivative, $I_B$ is **independent** of $I_C$:

$$
\frac{\partial I_B}{\partial I_C} = 0
$$

**Step 3: Substitute into the master formula.**

$$
S_{\text{fixed}} = \frac{1 + \beta}{1 - \beta(0)} = \frac{1 + \beta}{1}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,S_{\text{fixed}} = 1 + \beta\,}
$$

For a typical $\beta = 100$ transistor, $S = 101$. This is **very poor stability**.

---

### 3.3 Detailed Derivation: Stability Factor for Voltage Divider Bias

**Step 1: Replace the base network with its Thevenin equivalent.**

The voltage divider $R_1$–$R_2$ seen from the base is replaced by $V_{TH}$ in series with $R_{TH}$.

**Step 2: Write KVL around the base-emitter loop.**

$$
V_{TH} = I_B R_{TH} + V_{BE} + I_E R_E
$$

Substituting $I_E \approx I_C$ (for $\beta \gg 1$):

$$
V_{TH} = I_B R_{TH} + V_{BE} + I_C R_E
$$

**Step 3: Express $I_B$ in terms of $I_C$ and $I_{CBO}$.**

From $I_C = \beta I_B + (\beta+1) I_{CBO}$:

$$
I_B = \frac{I_C - (\beta+1) I_{CBO}}{\beta}
$$

**Step 4: Substitute back into the loop equation.**

$$
V_{TH} = \left(\frac{I_C - (\beta+1) I_{CBO}}{\beta}\right) R_{TH} + V_{BE} + I_C R_E
$$

**Step 5: Differentiate both sides with respect to $I_C$ (keeping $\beta$, $V_{BE}$ constant).**

$$
0 = \frac{1}{\beta} R_{TH} + R_E
$$

Wait — we need $\partial I_B / \partial I_C$ for the master formula, so let's re-derive directly:

From the KVL:

$$
V_{TH} - V_{BE} = I_B R_{TH} + I_C R_E
$$

Differentiating with respect to $I_C$:

$$
0 = R_{TH} \cdot \frac{\partial I_B}{\partial I_C} + R_E
$$

$$
\frac{\partial I_B}{\partial I_C} = -\frac{R_E}{R_{TH}}
$$

**Step 6: Substitute into the master formula.**

$$
S_{VD} = \frac{1 + \beta}{1 - \beta \left(-\dfrac{R_E}{R_{TH}}\right)} = \frac{1 + \beta}{1 + \beta \dfrac{R_E}{R_{TH}}}
$$

Rewriting $R_{TH} = R_E + R_{TH}$ as the denominator combination:

$$
\boxed{\,S_{VD} = \frac{1 + \beta}{1 + \beta \left(\dfrac{R_E}{R_E + R_{TH}}\right)}\,}
$$

**Verification of limiting cases:**

- If $R_E = 0$: $S = (1+\beta)/1 = 1+\beta$ (reduces to fixed bias — correct).
- If $R_{TH} = 0$ (ideal voltage source at base): $S = (1+\beta)/\infty \to 1$ (perfect stability — correct).
- If $\beta \to \infty$: $S \to (R_E + R_{TH})/R_E \approx 1 + R_{TH}/R_E$ (bounded stability — correct).

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A silicon transistor with $\beta = 100$ is used in a voltage divider bias circuit with $R_1 = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 5 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_C = 2 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_E = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$, and $V_{CC} = 12 \text{ V}$. Calculate the stability factor $S$ and the quiescent $I_C$.

**Step 1: Thevenin equivalent at base.**

$$
V_{TH} = 12 \cdot \frac{5}{10+5} = 12 \cdot \frac{5}{15} = 4 \text{ V}
$$

$$
R_{TH} = \frac{10 \cdot 5}{10+5} = \frac{50}{15} = 3.33 \text{ k}\Omega
$$

**Step 2: Stability factor.**

$$
S = \frac{1 + 100}{1 + 100 \cdot \dfrac{1}{1 + 3.33}} = \frac{101}{1 + 100 \cdot 0.2308} = \frac{101}{1 + 23.08} = \frac{101}{24.08}
$$

$$
S \approx 4.19
$$

**Step 3: Quiescent $I_C$.**

Using $V_{BE} = 0.7 \text{ V}$ (silicon), KVL gives:

$$
I_C \approx \frac{V_{TH} - V_{BE}}{R_E + \frac{R_{TH}}{\beta}} = \frac{4 - 0.7}{1 \text{ k}\Omega + \frac{3.33 \text{ k}\Omega}{100}}
$$

$$
I_C \approx \frac{3.3}{1.0333 \text{ k}\Omega} \approx 3.19 \text{ mA}
$$

**Stability check:** With $S = 4.19$, if $I_{CBO}$ changes by 1 µA (e.g., from 1 µA to 2 µA at elevated temperature), $I_C$ changes by only $4.19 \times 1 \mu\text{A} \approx 4.19 \mu\text{A}$ — far less than the 101 µA change in a fixed-bias circuit.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Biasing Methods

| Biasing Type | $S$ Formula | Typical $S$ for $\beta=100$ | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Fixed Bias | $1 + \beta$ | **101** | Simple, few components | **Worst stability** |
| Collector-to-Base | $\dfrac{1+\beta}{1 + \beta \frac{R_C}{R_C+R_B}}$ | ~20–40 | Moderate stability, no $R_E$ | Stability depends on $R_C$ |
| Voltage Divider | $\dfrac{1+\beta}{1 + \beta \frac{R_E}{R_E + R_{TH}}}$ | ~3–10 | **Best stability**, $S \to 1$ | Requires more resistors |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Bias Stabilization Functional Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Power Supply VCC] --> B[Bias Network]
    B --> C[Base Terminal of BJT]
    D[Thermal Disturbance Delta T] --> E[Drift in ICBO and VBE]
    E --> F[Undesired Drift in IC]
    F --> G[Q-Point Shift on Load Line]
    B --> H[DC Negative Feedback Path]
    H --> I[Corrective Signal to Base]
    I --> C
    C --> J[Stable Q-Point Maintained]
    J --> K[Linear Amplification Achieved]
    G -.->|Without Stabilization| L[Saturation or Cutoff Distortion]
    J -.->|With Stabilization| K
```

### 4.2 Voltage Divider Bias Circuit (Mermaid Schematic Representation)

```mermaid
graph TD
    VCC1[VCC = +12V] --- R1node[R1 = 10k]
    R1node --- Bnode[Base]
    VCC1 --- RCnode[RC = 2k]
    RCnode --- Cnode[Collector]
    Bnode --- BJTnode[Transistor NPN]
    Cnode --- BJTnode
    BJTnode --- Enode[Emitter]
    Enode --- REnode[RE = 1k]
    REnode --> GND1[Ground 0V]
    R2node[R2 = 5k] --- Bnode
    R2node --> GND1
```

### 4.3 Stability Factor Comparison Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SB1[Fixed Bias]
        FB[Stability Factor 1+beta]
    end
    subgraph SB2[Collector to Base]
        CB[Stability Factor 1+beta over 1+beta RC over RC+RB]
    end
    subgraph SB3[Voltage Divider]
        VD[Stability Factor 1+beta over 1+beta RE over RE+RTH]
    end
    SB1 --> R1[Result S approx 101 for beta 100]
    SB2 --> R2[Result S approx 20 to 40]
    SB3 --> R3[Result S approx 3 to 10 BEST]
```

### 4.4 Q-Point Drift Visualization (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initial Q-Point at ICQ1 VCEQ1] --> B{Temperature Change}
    B -->|No Stabilization| C[Drift to ICQ2 higher VCEQ2 lower]
    B -->|With Stabilization| D[Drift to ICQ3 only slightly higher]
    C --> E[Possible Saturation Region]
    D --> F[Remains in Active Region]
    E --> G[Output Clipping Distortion]
    F --> H[Clean Linear Amplification]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

Define the term *stability factor* with respect to a transistor biasing circuit. What is its ideal value?

**Model Answer:**

The stability factor $S$ is defined as the rate of change of collector current $I_C$ with respect to the reverse saturation current $I_{CBO}$, with $\beta$ and $V_{BE}$ held constant:

$$S = \frac{\partial I_C}{\partial I_{CBO}} \bigg|_{\beta, V_{BE} = \text{constant}}$$

It measures how sensitively the Q-point drifts due to temperature variations (which primarily affect $I_{CBO}$).

> The **ideal value of $S$ is 1**, meaning the collector current does not amplify the leakage current change at all.

**Marks Distribution (as per KTU pattern):**
- Definition: 2 marks
- Ideal value: 1 mark

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

List the three stability factors used to characterize a transistor bias circuit and state the physical parameter each one represents.

**Model Answer:**

The three stability factors are:

1. **$S_I = \dfrac{\partial I_C}{\partial I_{CBO}}$** — represents the sensitivity of $I_C$ to **temperature-induced leakage current** changes.
2. **$S_{V_{BE}} = \dfrac{\partial I_C}{\partial V_{BE}}$** — represents the sensitivity of $I_C$ to **base-emitter voltage drift** with temperature.
3. **$S_\beta = \dfrac{\partial I_C}{\partial \beta}$** — represents the sensitivity of $I_C$ to **transistor current gain variation** (replacement/spread).

> [!NOTE]
> A well-designed bias circuit must minimize all three simultaneously, but traditional analysis (and most KTU problems) focuses on $S = S_I$ for thermal runaway prevention.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Question]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the stability factor $S$ of a transistor in a **voltage divider bias** configuration. Explain why this configuration provides the best thermal stability among common biasing circuits. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A silicon transistor with $\beta = 120$, $V_{BE} = 0.7$ V is used in a self-bias (voltage divider) circuit with $R_1 = 15 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 5 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_C = 2.2 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_E = 1.2 \text{ k}\Omega$, and $V_{CC} = 15$ V. Calculate:
   (i) The Thevenin equivalent at the base.
   (ii) The stability factor $S$.
   (iii) The quiescent collector current $I_{CQ}$.
   (iv) The quiescent collector-emitter voltage $V_{CEQ}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of $S$ for voltage divider bias:**

*Step 1:* Replace the $R_1$–$R_2$ network by its Thevenin equivalent:

$$
V_{TH} = V_{CC} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}, \quad R_{TH} = \frac{R_1 R_2}{R_1 + R_2}
$$

*Step 2:* Apply KVL to the base-emitter loop:

$$
V_{TH} = I_B R_{TH} + V_{BE} + I_E R_E
$$

*Step 3:* Approximate $I_E \approx I_C$ (valid for $\beta \gg 1$):

$$
V_{TH} - V_{BE} = I_B R_{TH} + I_C R_E
$$

*Step 4:* Differentiate w.r.t. $I_C$ to find $\partial I_B / \partial I_C$:

$$
0 = R_{TH} \cdot \frac{\partial I_B}{\partial I_C} + R_E
\Rightarrow \frac{\partial I_B}{\partial I_C} = -\frac{R_E}{R_{TH}}
$$

*Step 5:* Substitute into the master stability factor equation:

$$
S = \frac{1+\beta}{1 - \beta \left(-\frac{R_E}{R_{TH}}\right)} = \frac{1+\beta}{1 + \beta \frac{R_E}{R_{TH}}} = \frac{1+\beta}{1 + \beta \frac{R_E}{R_E + R_{TH}}}
$$

*Step 6:* The configuration provides best stability because:
- $R_E$ provides **DC negative feedback** — any rise in $I_C$ increases the voltage drop across $R_E$, which reduces $V_{BE}$ and hence $I_B$, pulling $I_C$ back.
- $R_{TH}$ being small makes the base voltage nearly independent of $\beta$ variations, decoupling the Q-point from transistor parameter spread.
- As $R_E \to \infty$ or $R_{TH} \to 0$, $S \to 1$, the theoretical optimum.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Thevenin equivalent:**

$$
V_{TH} = 15 \cdot \frac{5}{15+5} = 15 \cdot \frac{5}{20} = 3.75 \text{ V}
$$

$$
R_{TH} = \frac{15 \cdot 5}{15+5} = \frac{75}{20} = 3.75 \text{ k}\Omega
$$

**(ii) Stability factor:**

$$
S = \frac{1+120}{1 + 120 \cdot \dfrac{1.2}{1.2 + 3.75}} = \frac{121}{1 + 120 \cdot \dfrac{1.2}{4.95}}
$$

$$
= \frac{121}{1 + 120 \cdot 0.2424} = \frac{121}{1 + 29.09} = \frac{121}{30.09} \approx 4.02
$$

**(iii) Quiescent $I_{CQ}$:**

$$
I_{CQ} \approx \frac{V_{TH} - V_{BE}}{R_E + \dfrac{R_{TH}}{\beta}} = \frac{3.75 - 0.7}{1.2 + \dfrac{3.75}{120}}
$$

$$
= \frac{3.05}{1.2 + 0.03125} = \frac{3.05}{1.23125} \approx 2.477 \text{ mA}
$$

**(iv) Quiescent $V_{CEQ}$:**

$$
V_{CEQ} = V_{CC} - I_C R_C - I_E R_E \approx V_{CC} - I_C (R_C + R_E)
$$

$$
V_{CEQ} = 15 - 2.477 \text{ mA} \cdot (2.2 + 1.2) \text{ k}\Omega = 15 - 2.477 \cdot 3.4
$$

$$
V_{CEQ} = 15 - 8.42 = 6.58 \text{ V}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- $V_{TH} = 3.75$ V, $R_{TH} = 3.75$ kΩ
- $S \approx 4.02$
- $I_{CQ} \approx 2.48$ mA
- $V_{CEQ} \approx 6.58$ V

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Alternative Choice]

**(a)** Explain the concept of **DC negative feedback** in the context of transistor bias stabilization. Show how the collector-to-base bias circuit implements this feedback and derive its stability factor. Compare it numerically (for $\beta = 100$, $R_C = 4.7$ kΩ, $R_B = 100$ kΩ) with a fixed-bias circuit using the same $\beta$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The leakage current $I_{CBO}$ of a germanium transistor is 5 µA at 25 °C. Find its value at 75 °C. If this transistor is used in a fixed-bias circuit with $\beta = 50$, by how much will $I_C$ change due to this temperature rise? The change in $I_{CBO}$ is the only drift source. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) DC Negative Feedback & Collector-to-Base Bias:**

**Concept of DC Negative Feedback:**
DC negative feedback is a technique where a portion of the output (DC) quantity is fed back to the input in **opposing polarity** to the original cause of variation. In bias stabilization, the feedback opposes any change in $I_C$ caused by temperature, $\beta$, or $V_{CC}$ drift, thus "locking" the Q-point.

**Collector-to-Base Bias Circuit Operation:**

In a collector-to-base bias circuit, $R_B$ connects the collector back to the base.

Suppose $I_C$ tends to **increase** due to temperature rise:
1. $V_C = V_{CC} - I_C R_C$ **decreases**.
2. The voltage across $R_B$ ($= V_C - V_{BE}$) **decreases**.
3. $I_B = (V_C - V_{BE})/R_B$ **decreases**.
4. The smaller $I_B$ **reduces** $I_C$ back toward its original value.

This self-correcting action is DC negative feedback.

**Derivation of Stability Factor:**

KVL on base-emitter loop:

$$
V_{CC} = I_C R_C + I_B R_B + V_{BE}
$$

Differentiating w.r.t. $I_C$ (with $V_{CC}$, $V_{BE}$ constant):

$$
0 = R_C + R_B \cdot \frac{\partial I_B}{\partial I_C}
\Rightarrow \frac{\partial I_B}{\partial I_C} = -\frac{R_C}{R_B}
$$

Substituting in the master formula:

$$
S = \frac{1+\beta}{1 - \beta \left(-\dfrac{R_C}{R_B}\right)} = \frac{1+\beta}{1 + \beta \dfrac{R_C}{R_B}}
$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $R_B$:

$$
S_{C-B} = \frac{1+\beta}{1 + \beta \dfrac{R_C}{R_C + R_B}}
$$

**Numerical Comparison for $\beta = 100$, $R_C = 4.7$ kΩ, $R_B = 100$ kΩ:**

*Fixed bias:*
$$
S_{\text{fixed}} = 1 + 100 = 101
$$

*Collector-to-base bias:*
$$
S_{C-B} = \frac{1+100}{1 + 100 \cdot \dfrac{4.7}{4.7+100}} = \frac{101}{1 + 100 \cdot 0.0449} = \frac{101}{1 + 4.49} = \frac{101}{5.49} \approx 18.4
$$

**Result:** The collector-to-base bias gives $S \approx 18.4$ vs. $S = 101$ for fixed bias — a **5.5× improvement** in stability.

---

**(b) Temperature Rise and $I_C$ Drift in Fixed Bias:**

**Step 1: Find $I_{CBO}$ at 75 °C using the doubling rule:**

$$
I_{CBO}(T_2) = I_{CBO}(T_1) \cdot 2^{(T_2 - T_1)/10}
$$

$$
I_{CBO}(75°C) = 5 \mu\text{A} \cdot 2^{(75-25)/10} = 5 \mu\text{A} \cdot 2^{5} = 5 \mu\text{A} \cdot 32
$$

$$
I_{CBO}(75°C) = 160 \mu\text{A}
$$

**Step 2: Change in $I_{CBO}$:**

$$
\Delta I_{CBO} = 160 - 5 = 155 \mu\text{A}
$$

**Step 3: Stability factor for fixed bias:**

$$
S = 1 + \beta = 1 + 50 = 51
$$

**Step 4: Change in $I_C$:**

$$
\Delta I_C = S \cdot \Delta I_{CBO} = 51 \cdot 155 \mu\text{A}
$$

$$
\Delta I_C = 7905 \mu\text{A} \approx 7.91 \text{ mA}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- $I_{CBO}$ at 75 °C = **160 µA**
- $\Delta I_C$ in fixed bias = **7.91 mA**

This huge drift in $I_C$ (from, say, 5 mA to nearly 13 mA) would push the transistor deep into saturation, completely distorting the output — illustrating why fixed bias is unsuitable for practical circuits.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign** in $\partial I_B / \partial I_C$: A very common error. The master formula is $S = (1+\beta)/(1 - \beta \cdot \partial I_B / \partial I_C)$. If the negative sign is missed, $S$ comes out **negative** or as a value less than 1 — both physically meaningless for $\beta > 1$. **Always double-check the sign.**
>
> 2. **Using $R_E$ alone in the voltage divider formula** instead of $(R_E + R_{TH})$: The correct denominator form is $R_E / (R_E + R_{TH})$. Using just $R_E$ gives an erroneously small $S$.
>
> 3. **Ignoring the $V_{BE}$ drop in numerical problems**: For silicon, $V_{BE} = 0.7$ V; for germanium, $V_{BE} = 0.3$ V. Forgetting this shifts the Q-point by 0.3–0.7 V.
>
> 4. **Confusing the three stability factors**: $S$ (or $S_I$) is the traditional one. $S_{V_{BE}}$ and $S_\beta$ have different partial derivatives and different formulas. Read the question carefully.
>
> 5. **Units mismatch**: $I_{CBO}$ is typically given in µA or nA; convert to A before substituting in formulas.
>
> 6. **Thevenin resistance calculation**: Students often write $R_{TH} = R_1 + R_2$ (series, wrong) instead of $R_{TH} = R_1 R_2 / (R_1 + R_2)$ (parallel, correct).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> 📋 **KTU 2024 — Bias Stabilization Quick-Fire Revision**

- ⭐ **Stability Factor Definition:** $S = \partial I_C / \partial I_{CBO}$. **Ideal $S = 1$**, worst is $1+\beta$.
- ⭐ **Master Formula:** $S = (1+\beta) / (1 - \beta \cdot \partial I_B / \partial I_C)$ — memorize this; all specific circuits derive from it.
- ⭐ **Fixed Bias:** $S = 1 + \beta$ (worst stability, but simplest).
- ⭐ **Collector-to-Base Bias:** $S = (1+\beta) / (1 + \beta \cdot R_C / (R_C + R_B))$ (moderate stability).
- ⭐ **Voltage Divider (Self) Bias:** $S = (1+\beta) / (1 + \beta \cdot R_E / (R_E + R_{TH}))$ — **best stability**, $S \to 1$ as $R_E \uparrow$ or $R_{TH} \downarrow$.
- ⭐ **Thevenin at base:** $V_{TH} = V_{CC} \cdot R_2 / (R_1 + R_2)$ and $R_{TH} = R_1 R_2 / (R_1 + R_2)$.
- ⭐ **$I_{CBO}$ doubling rule:** Doubles every 10 °C rise in junction temperature.
- ⭐ **$V_{BE}$ drift:** Decreases by 2.5 mV/°C for silicon.
- ⭐ **Three stability factors:** $S_I$ (thermal), $S_{V_{BE}}$ (base-emitter drift), $S_\beta$ (gain spread).
- ⭐ **DC negative feedback:** The mechanism behind all stabilization — opposes drift automatically.
- ⭐ **BJT equation:** $I_C = \beta I_B + (\beta+1) I_{CBO}$ — used in every derivation.
- ⭐ **KVL around base loop** is the starting point for every stability factor derivation.
- ⭐ **Approximation:** $I_E \approx I_C$ is valid for $\beta \gg 1$ and simplifies voltage divider analysis significantly.
- ⭐ **Practical Q-point design rule:** Keep $V_{CEQ} \approx V_{CC}/2$ for maximum symmetric swing; check that the Q-point stays in the active region even at temperature extremes.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Switching Circuits - Astable
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET302 - ELECTRONIC DEVICES AND CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Switching Circuits - Astable

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

An **Astable Multivibrator** is a free-running, regenerative electronic oscillator circuit that possesses **no stable state**. It continuously and automatically switches (toggles) between two **quasi-stable states** without requiring any external triggering pulse. Because its output is a continuous train of rectangular/square waveforms, it is classified as a **non-sinusoidal oscillator** and forms the fundamental building block of pulse-generating circuits.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot:** "An astable multivibrator is a square-wave oscillator in which both states are quasi-stable. The circuit flips between them automatically based on RC time constants, generating a continuous rectangular waveform of frequency determined by $R$ and $C$."

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Tipping Seesaw"

Imagine two children (Child A and Child B) sitting on opposite ends of a seesaw. A "tipping point" occurs when one child becomes heavy enough. Once the heavier child pushes down, a spring-loaded mechanism instantaneously transfers extra weight to the *other* child, making the original child shoot up. The seesaw then flips. The spring slowly drains the transferred weight through a damper until the other child becomes heavy again, and the cycle repeats endlessly.

- **Children** = Two Transistors (Q1 and Q2)
- **Seesaw** = Cross-coupled feedback path
- **Spring + Damper** = RC timing network
- **Tipping Point** = Threshold voltage $\approx 0.7\text{ V}$ (turn-on base-emitter voltage)
- **Continuous flipping** = Continuous square wave generation

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Unlike the **Monostable** (one stable state, needs a trigger) and **Bistable** (two stable states, needs a trigger to flip), the **Astable** has *no* stable state — it is the "always-flopping" multivibrator.

### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- Silicon BJT base-emitter turn-on voltage: $V_{BE(\text{on})} \approx \mathbf{0.7\text{ V}}$
- Saturation collector-emitter voltage: $V_{CE(\text{sat})} \approx \mathbf{0.2\text{ V}}$
- Output frequency range: typically $\mathbf{1\text{ Hz} \text{ to } 1\text{ MHz}}$
- Duty cycle range: variable, default symmetric at **50%**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Circuit Architecture (Collector-Coupled BJT Astable)

The classic astable multivibrator uses **two NPN transistors** with symmetrical cross-coupling:

| Component | Role |
|-----------|------|
| Q1, Q2 | Switching transistors (act as electronic switches) |
| $R_{C1}, R_{C2}$ | Collector load resistors |
| $R_{B1}, R_{B2}$ | Base biasing/timing resistors (drive bases high) |
| $C_1, C_2$ | Cross-coupling capacitors (transfer voltage changes) |
| $V_{CC}$ | DC supply (typically **+5 V to +12 V**) |

The **base of Q1 is coupled via $C_1$ to the collector of Q2**, and the **base of Q2 is coupled via $C_2$ to the collector of Q1** — this is what enables the regenerative switching.

### 2.2 The Two Quasi-Stable States

**State 1 (Q1 = OFF, Q2 = ON):**
- Q2 is saturated → $V_{C2} \approx V_{CE(\text{sat})}$
- Q1 is cut off → $V_{C1} \approx V_{CC}$
- Capacitor $C_2$ (connected between Q1's base and Q2's collector) discharges through $R_{B1}$ from a negative voltage toward $+V_{CC}$
- Q1's base voltage slowly rises toward $V_{CC}$
- This state persists until $V_{B1}$ reaches $V_{BE(\text{on})} \approx 0.7\text{ V}$

**State 2 (Q1 = ON, Q2 = OFF):**
- The roles reverse symmetrically
- $C_1$ discharges through $R_{B2}$ until Q2's base reaches $0.7\text{ V}$
- This state also persists for a finite duration, then flips back

> [!TIP]
> **The "Why" of Automatic Switching:** The capacitor cannot charge/discharge instantaneously. While one transistor is saturated, the *opposite* coupling capacitor is busy slowly recharging through the base resistor of the OFF transistor. Once that base crosses $0.7\text{ V}$, the OFF transistor suddenly turns ON, slamming the other transistor OFF — a regenerative avalanche that completes the flip.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Astable multivibrators are the heartbeat of:
- **Square-wave and pulse generators** in digital logic test benches
- **Clock generation** in simple microcontrollers and timing circuits
- **LED flashers, vehicle turn-signal indicators**
- **Tone generators** in sirens and alarms
- **Time-base generators** in older CROs (Cathode Ray Oscilloscopes)
- **PWM control** in low-frequency power electronics

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Time duration of State 1 ($T_1$) | $T_1 = 0.693 \cdot R_{B1} \cdot C_2$ | seconds | Q1 OFF, Q2 ON |
| 2 | Time duration of State 2 ($T_2$) | $T_2 = 0.693 \cdot R_{B2} \cdot C_1$ | seconds | Q2 OFF, Q1 ON |
| 3 | Total Time Period | $T = T_1 + T_2 = 0.693 \left( R_{B1} C_2 + R_{B2} C_1 \right)$ | seconds | Full cycle |
| 4 | Frequency of Oscillation | $f = \dfrac{1}{T} = \dfrac{1.44}{R_{B1} C_2 + R_{B2} C_1}$ | Hz | Inverse of T |
| 5 | Symmetric Time Period ($R_{B1}=R_{B2}=R$, $C_1=C_2=C$) | $T = 1.386 \cdot R \cdot C$ | seconds | $T = 2 \cdot 0.693 RC$ |
| 6 | Symmetric Frequency | $f = \dfrac{0.722}{R \cdot C}$ | Hz | $f = 1/(1.386 RC)$ |
| 7 | Duty Cycle (asymmetric) | $D = \dfrac{T_1}{T_1 + T_2} \times 100\%$ | percent | Use $\vert$ replaced by $\mid$ if needed |
| 8 | Mark-Space Ratio | $MS = \dfrac{T_1}{T_2}$ | dimensionless | For 50% square wave, MS = 1 |
| 9 | Collector Saturation Voltage | $V_{C(\text{sat})} \approx 0.2\text{ V}$ | volts | Approximation for silicon |
| 10 | Base Switching Threshold | $V_{th} \approx 0.7\text{ V}$ | volts | $V_{BE(\text{on})}$ for silicon |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The constant **0.693** comes from $\ln(2) = 0.6931$. The constant **1.44** is its reciprocal $1/\ln(2)$. Memorize both — questions often supply only the unmarked form and expect you to recognize it.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Output Frequency $f$ of an Astable Multivibrator

We will derive the fundamental time-period equation used in **every KTU astable problem**.

#### Step 1: Define the Operating Conditions

Consider the moment Q1 just turned OFF and Q2 just turned ON (saturated). The key event governing the duration of this state is the discharge of capacitor $C_2$ through resistor $R_{B1}$.

- Q2's collector just dropped from $\approx V_{CC}$ to $V_{CE(\text{sat})}$ — a sudden negative step of magnitude $(V_{CC} - V_{CE(\text{sat})})$.
- Since the voltage across a capacitor cannot change instantaneously, Q1's base voltage also drops by the same amount.
- Before the flip, Q1's base was at $V_{BE(\text{on})} \approx 0.7\text{ V}$.
- Therefore, immediately after the flip, Q1's base is at:

$$V_{B1}(0^+) = 0.7 - (V_{CC} - V_{CE(\text{sat})}) \approx -V_{CC}$$

(taking $V_{CE(\text{sat})}$ and $V_{BE(\text{on})}$ as negligible compared to $V_{CC}$).

#### Step 2: Apply the Capacitor Charging Equation

Capacitor $C_2$ now charges (its left plate) through $R_{B1}$ from the source $+V_{CC}$. The general transient voltage across a charging RC network is:

$$V(t) = V_{\text{final}} - (V_{\text{final}} - V_{\text{initial}}) \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$$

For Q1's base voltage $V_{B1}(t)$:
- $V_{\text{final}} = +V_{CC}$ (asymptotically approaches supply)
- $V_{\text{initial}} = -V_{CC}$ (immediately after the flip)
- Time constant $\tau = R_{B1} \cdot C_2$

Substituting these into the transient equation:

$$V_{B1}(t) = V_{CC} - (V_{CC} - (-V_{CC})) \cdot e^{-t/(R_{B1} C_2)}$$

$$V_{B1}(t) = V_{CC} - 2V_{CC} \cdot e^{-t/(R_{B1} C_2)}$$

#### Step 3: Apply the Switching Threshold Condition

The state ends the instant $V_{B1}(t)$ rises to $V_{BE(\text{on})} = 0.7\text{ V} \approx 0$. Setting $V_{B1}(t_1) = 0$ and solving for $t_1$:

$$0 = V_{CC} - 2V_{CC} \cdot e^{-t_1/(R_{B1} C_2)}$$

$$2V_{CC} \cdot e^{-t_1/(R_{B1} C_2)} = V_{CC}$$

$$e^{-t_1/(R_{B1} C_2)} = \frac{1}{2}$$

Taking the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$-\frac{t_1}{R_{B1} C_2} = \ln\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) = -\ln(2)$$

$$\frac{t_1}{R_{B1} C_2} = \ln(2) = 0.6931$$

Therefore, the duration of the first half-cycle is:

$$t_1 = 0.693 \cdot R_{B1} \cdot C_2$$

#### Step 4: Derive the Total Period

By exact symmetry, when Q1 turns ON and Q2 turns OFF, the analysis is identical with the indices swapped. The duration of the second half-cycle is:

$$t_2 = 0.693 \cdot R_{B2} \cdot C_1$$

The total period of one complete cycle is:

$$T = t_1 + t_2 = 0.693 \cdot R_{B1} C_2 + 0.693 \cdot R_{B2} C_1$$

$$T = 0.693 \left( R_{B1} C_2 + R_{B2} C_1 \right)$$

#### Step 5: Derive the Frequency of Oscillation

The frequency is the reciprocal of the period:

$$f = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{0.693 (R_{B1} C_2 + R_{B2} C_1)}$$

Since $\dfrac{1}{0.693} = 1.4427 \approx 1.44$:

$$\boxed{f = \frac{1.44}{R_{B1} C_2 + R_{B2} C_1}}$$

#### Step 6: Special Case — Symmetric Design

If $R_{B1} = R_{B2} = R$ and $C_1 = C_2 = C$ (the most common textbook case):

$$T = 0.693 \cdot (RC + RC) = 0.693 \cdot 2RC = 1.386 \, RC$$

$$f = \frac{1}{1.386 \, RC} = \frac{0.722}{RC}$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** An astable multivibrator uses $R_{B1} = R_{B2} = 20\text{ k}\Omega$ and $C_1 = C_2 = 0.01\text{ }\mu\text{F}$. Find the frequency and the mark-space ratio.

**Step 1: Calculate the period.**

$$T = 0.693 \cdot (R_{B1} C_2 + R_{B2} C_1)$$

$$T = 0.693 \cdot (20 \times 10^3 \cdot 0.01 \times 10^{-6} + 20 \times 10^3 \cdot 0.01 \times 10^{-6})$$

$$T = 0.693 \cdot (2 \times 10^{-4} + 2 \times 10^{-4}) = 0.693 \cdot 4 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\boxed{T = 2.772 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} = 277.2\text{ }\mu\text{s}}$$

**Step 2: Calculate the frequency.**

$$f = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{2.772 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\boxed{f \approx 3.607\text{ kHz}}$$

**Step 3: Calculate the mark-space ratio.**

Since $R_{B1} = R_{B2}$ and $C_1 = C_2$, the time spent in ON/OFF states is equal:

$$MS = \frac{T_1}{T_2} = \frac{0.693 \cdot R_{B1} C_2}{0.693 \cdot R_{B2} C_1} = \frac{R_{B1} C_2}{R_{B2} C_1} = 1$$

Therefore, the output is a **symmetric square wave** (50% duty cycle).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram represents the **functional signal flow** of the astable multivibrator, showing how the cross-coupling between two transistor stages produces continuous oscillation.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PSU["VCC SUPPLY"]
    R1["RC1 COLLECTOR RESISTOR"]
    R2["RC2 COLLECTOR RESISTOR"]
    RB1["RB1 BASE RESISTOR"]
    RB2["RB2 BASE RESISTOR"]
    C1["COUPLING CAPACITOR C1"]
    C2["COUPLING CAPACITOR C2"]
    Q1["TRANSISTOR Q1"]
    Q2["TRANSISTOR Q2"]
    OUT1["OUTPUT AT COLLECTOR Q1"]
    OUT2["OUTPUT AT COLLECTOR Q2"]
    STATE1["STATE 1: Q1 OFF Q2 ON"]
    STATE2["STATE 2: Q1 ON Q2 OFF"]
    THRESH["RC TIMING EXPIRES"]
    FLIP["REGENERATIVE FLIP"]
    WAVEOUT["RECTANGULAR WAVE OUTPUT"]

    PSU --> R1
    PSU --> R2
    PSU --> RB1
    PSU --> RB2
    R1 --> Q1
    R2 --> Q2
    RB1 --> Q1
    RB2 --> Q2
    Q1 --> C2
    C2 --> RB1
    Q2 --> C1
    C1 --> RB2
    Q1 --> OUT1
    Q2 --> OUT2

    STATE1 --> THRESH
    THRESH --> FLIP
    FLIP --> STATE2
    STATE2 --> THRESH
    THRESH --> FLIP
    FLIP --> STATE1
    OUT1 --> WAVEOUT
    OUT2 --> WAVEOUT
```

### 4.2 Mermaid State Transition Diagram — Oscillation Cycle

This diagram explicitly captures the **two quasi-stable states** and the regenerative transition between them.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> STATE1
    STATE1 : STATE 1 Q1 OFF Q2 ON\nVC1 HIGH NEAR VCC\nVC2 LOW NEAR VCESAT\nC2 CHARGING THROUGH RB1\nDURATION T1 EQUALS 0.693 RB1 C2
    STATE1 --> STATE2 : V BASE Q1 REACHES 0.7V\nC2 DISCHARGE COMPLETES\nCROSS COUPLED REGENERATIVE FLIP
    STATE2 : STATE 2 Q1 ON Q2 OFF\nVC1 LOW NEAR VCESAT\nVC2 HIGH NEAR VCC\nC1 CHARGING THROUGH RB2\nDURATION T2 EQUALS 0.693 RB2 C1
    STATE2 --> STATE1 : V BASE Q2 REACHES 0.7V\nC1 DISCHARGE COMPLETES\nCROSS COUPLED REGENERATIVE FLIP
    STATE1 --> [*] : OPTIONAL POWER OFF
```

### 4.3 Functional Reading Guide for the Diagrams

- **Upper Mermaid chart** shows the *physical/electrical topology*: $V_{CC}$ powers all four resistors; Q1 and Q2 are the active switches; $C_1$ and $C_2$ form the cross-coupling feedback loop.
- **Lower Mermaid chart** shows the *temporal/state behavior*: the system resides in State 1 for a fixed time $T_1 = 0.693 R_{B1} C_2$, then instantly flips to State 2, where it resides for $T_2 = 0.693 R_{B2} C_1$, then flips back — generating the rectangular wave continuously.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024, Model Paper]**

#### **Q1. Define an astable multivibrator. Why is it called "free-running"?**
*(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
An astable multivibrator is a two-stage regenerative oscillator circuit that has **no stable state**. It continuously alternates between two quasi-stable states, producing a rectangular waveform at its output.

It is called **"free-running"** because it does **not require any external trigger pulse** to change states. The transition between states occurs automatically, governed purely by the internal RC time constants of the circuit. Once powered by $V_{CC}$, the circuit oscillates indefinitely without any external stimulus.

---

#### **Q2. Compare the three types of multivibrators in terms of stability of states.**
*(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Multivibrator Type | Stable States | Quasi-Stable States | External Trigger? |
|--------------------|---------------|---------------------|-------------------|
| **Monostable** | 1 | 1 | Required to leave stable state |
| **Bistable** | 2 | 0 | Required to flip states |
| **Astable** | 0 | 2 | Not required — self-oscillating |

> The astable has **zero stable states** and **two quasi-stable states**, making it a continuously oscillating square-wave generator.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Format — Choose EITHER Question A OR Question B.**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam - May 2023]*

**(a)** Draw the circuit diagram of a collector-coupled astable multivibrator using BJT. Explain its operation with the help of waveforms at both collectors and both bases.
*(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Circuit Diagram (textual description for valuation):**
A standard collector-coupled astable multivibrator consists of two NPN transistors Q1 and Q2. The collector of Q1 is connected to $V_{CC}$ through $R_{C1}$ and to the base of Q2 through coupling capacitor $C_1$. Similarly, the collector of Q2 is connected to $V_{CC}$ through $R_{C2}$ and to the base of Q1 through coupling capacitor $C_2$. The bases are returned to $V_{CC}$ through biasing resistors $R_{B1}$ and $R_{B2}$ respectively.

**Operation — Two Quasi-Stable States:**

Let us assume at $t = 0$ that Q1 has just turned OFF and Q2 has just turned ON. This is **State 1**.

**State 1 (Q1 OFF, Q2 ON):**
- $V_{C1} \approx V_{CC}$ (Q1 cut off, no collector current)
- $V_{C2} \approx V_{CE(\text{sat})} \approx 0.2\text{ V}$ (Q2 in saturation)
- $V_{B1}$ is at a negative potential (capacitor $C_2$ couples the drop at Q2's collector to Q1's base)
- $C_2$ slowly charges through $R_{B1}$, and $V_{B1}$ rises exponentially toward $V_{CC}$
- The state persists until $V_{B1}$ reaches $V_{BE(\text{on})} \approx 0.7\text{ V}$

**Transition to State 2:**
- The instant $V_{B1}$ crosses $0.7\text{ V}$, Q1 turns ON
- Q1's collector voltage drops from $V_{CC}$ to $V_{CE(\text{sat})}$
- This drop is coupled through $C_1$ to Q2's base, driving $V_{B2}$ sharply negative
- Q2 turns OFF, its collector rises to $V_{CC}$
- The regenerative feedback completes the flip — **State 2 begins**

**State 2 (Q1 ON, Q2 OFF):**
- The roles are reversed: $V_{C2} \approx V_{CC}$, $V_{C1} \approx V_{CE(\text{sat})}$
- $C_1$ charges through $R_{B2}$, $V_{B2}$ rises
- State persists until $V_{B2} = 0.7\text{ V}$, then flips back to State 1

**Waveform Description:**
- Collector waveforms: $V_{C1}$ and $V_{C2}$ are complementary rectangular waves alternating between $V_{CC}$ and $V_{CE(\text{sat})}$.
- Base waveforms: $V_{B1}$ and $V_{B2}$ are exponential RC charging curves that cross the $0.7\text{ V}$ threshold periodically.
- The base waveforms show a **negative exponential overshoot** at the instant of flip, then slowly rise to $+V_{CC}$.

**(b)** Derive an expression for the time period of an astable multivibrator and hence obtain the frequency of oscillation.
*(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation:**

Assume Q1 is OFF and Q2 is ON. At the moment of transition, Q2's collector drops from $V_{CC}$ to $V_{CE(\text{sat})}$. This negative step of magnitude $(V_{CC} - V_{CE(\text{sat})})$ is coupled to Q1's base through $C_2$.

Immediately before the flip, $V_{B1} \approx V_{BE(\text{on})}$. Therefore, immediately after:

$$V_{B1}(0^+) = V_{BE(\text{on})} - (V_{CC} - V_{CE(\text{sat})}) \approx -V_{CC}$$

Now $C_2$ charges through $R_{B1}$ from $-V_{CC}$ toward $+V_{CC}$. The base voltage follows:

$$V_{B1}(t) = V_{CC} - 2V_{CC} \cdot e^{-t/(R_{B1} C_2)}$$

The state ends when $V_{B1}(t_1) = V_{BE(\text{on})} \approx 0$:

$$0 = V_{CC} - 2V_{CC} \cdot e^{-t_1/(R_{B1} C_2)}$$

$$e^{-t_1/(R_{B1} C_2)} = \frac{1}{2}$$

Taking natural log:

$$\frac{t_1}{R_{B1} C_2} = \ln 2 = 0.693$$

$$t_1 = 0.693 \cdot R_{B1} C_2$$

By symmetry, the duration of State 2 is:

$$t_2 = 0.693 \cdot R_{B2} C_1$$

**Total Period:**

$$T = t_1 + t_2 = 0.693 (R_{B1} C_2 + R_{B2} C_1)$$

**Frequency:**

$$f = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1.44}{R_{B1} C_2 + R_{B2} C_1}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. Confusing the indices: The capacitor connected to Q1's base is $C_2$ (couples from Q2's collector). The capacitor connected to Q2's base is $C_1$. Many students swap $C_1$ and $C_2$ in the formula and lose **2 marks** per occurrence.
> 2. Forgetting the constant **0.693** comes from $\ln(2)$, not from $\pi$ or some other constant.
> 3. Writing $T = RC$ without the multiplier — the half-cycle is $0.693 RC$, the full cycle is $1.386 RC$ for symmetric case.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]*

**(a)** An astable multivibrator has $R_{B1} = R_{B2} = 47\text{ k}\Omega$ and $C_1 = C_2 = 0.1\text{ }\mu\text{F}$. Calculate the frequency of oscillation and the duty cycle. If $V_{CC} = 10\text{ V}$, what is the peak-to-peak amplitude of the output waveform?
*(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $R_{B1} = R_{B2} = 47\text{ k}\Omega = 47 \times 10^3\text{ }\Omega$, $C_1 = C_2 = 0.1\text{ }\mu\text{F} = 0.1 \times 10^{-6}\text{ F} = 10^{-7}\text{ F}$, $V_{CC} = 10\text{ V}$

**Step 1: Calculate the Time Period.**

Using the symmetric formula (since all four components are equal):

$$T = 1.386 \cdot R \cdot C = 1.386 \times 47 \times 10^3 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$T = 1.386 \times 4.7 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\boxed{T = 6.514 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 6.514\text{ ms}}$$

**Step 2: Calculate the Frequency.**

$$f = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{6.514 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$\boxed{f \approx 153.5\text{ Hz}}$$

**Step 3: Calculate the Duty Cycle.**

Since $R_{B1} = R_{B2}$ and $C_1 = C_2$:

$$T_1 = T_2 = \frac{T}{2} = 3.257\text{ ms}$$

$$D = \frac{T_1}{T_1 + T_2} \times 100\% = \frac{3.257}{6.514} \times 100\%$$

$$\boxed{D = 50\%}$$

**Step 4: Calculate the Peak-to-Peak Output Amplitude.**

The collector of the OFF transistor rises to $\approx V_{CC}$, and the collector of the ON transistor drops to $V_{CE(\text{sat})} \approx 0.2\text{ V}$.

$$V_{p-p} = V_{CC} - V_{CE(\text{sat})} = 10 - 0.2$$

$$\boxed{V_{p-p} \approx 9.8\text{ V}}$$

**(b)** Design an astable multivibrator to generate a **1 kHz square wave** with 50% duty cycle using $V_{CC} = 12\text{ V}$. Assume $C_1 = C_2 = 0.01\text{ }\mu\text{F}$.
*(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Write the symmetric period formula.**

$$T = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{1000} = 1\text{ ms} = 10^{-3}\text{ s}$$

For 50% duty cycle, $R_{B1} = R_{B2} = R$ and $C_1 = C_2 = C$:

$$T = 1.386 \, R \, C$$

**Step 2: Solve for $R$.**

$$R = \frac{T}{1.386 \cdot C} = \frac{10^{-3}}{1.386 \times 0.01 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$R = \frac{10^{-3}}{1.386 \times 10^{-8}} = \frac{10^{-3}}{1.386 \times 10^{-8}}$$

$$R = \frac{1}{1.386} \times 10^{5} = 0.7215 \times 10^{5}$$

$$\boxed{R \approx 72.15\text{ k}\Omega}$$

**Step 3: Choose standard resistor value.**

The nearest standard E12/E24 resistor value is $\mathbf{75\text{ k}\Omega}$ (or $68\text{ k}\Omega$).

**Step 4: Choose collector resistors.**

A typical design rule is $R_C \approx \frac{R_B}{10}$ to ensure proper saturation. Thus:

$$R_{C1} = R_{C2} \approx \frac{75\text{ k}\Omega}{10} = 7.5\text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 5: Verify design.**

With $R = 75\text{ k}\Omega$ and $C = 0.01\text{ }\mu\text{F}$:

$$f = \frac{0.722}{RC} = \frac{0.722}{75 \times 10^3 \times 10^{-8}} = \frac{0.722}{7.5 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 962.7\text{ Hz}$$

The small deviation is acceptable given resistor tolerances.

$$\boxed{\text{Final Design: } R_{B1} = R_{B2} = 75\text{ k}\Omega,\ R_{C1} = R_{C2} = 7.5\text{ k}\Omega,\ C_1 = C_2 = 0.01\text{ }\mu\text{F},\ V_{CC} = 12\text{ V}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Design Pitfalls:**
> 1. Many students forget that **$R_C$ is chosen separately from $R_B$** — $R_C$ controls saturation current, $R_B$ controls timing. Mixing them up loses 2–3 marks.
> 2. The 50% duty cycle condition requires $R_{B1} C_2 = R_{B2} C_1$. Either both resistors are equal **OR** the RC products are equal — not just the resistors.
> 3. The formula $1.386 RC$ applies ONLY for the symmetric case. For asymmetric design, use $T = 0.693(R_{B1} C_2 + R_{B2} C_1)$.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Astable = 0 stable states, 2 quasi-stable states, self-oscillating.**
- The two transistors alternate between **cut-off and saturation** — there is no active region operation in steady state.
- **Coupling capacitors** $C_1$ and $C_2$ transfer the sudden voltage changes between collectors and bases.
- **Timing is governed by the OFF transistor's base capacitor charging** through its base resistor toward $V_{CC}$.
- The threshold voltage is $V_{BE(\text{on})} \approx 0.7\text{ V}$ (silicon BJT).
- **Master Formula:** $T = 0.693(R_{B1} C_2 + R_{B2} C_1)$ and $f = 1.44/(R_{B1} C_2 + R_{B2} C_1)$.
- **Symmetric Case:** $T = 1.386 RC$ and $f = 0.722/RC$.
- The constant **0.693** is $\ln 2$; the constant **1.44** is $1/\ln 2$.
- **Index Caution:** The capacitor on Q1's base is $C_2$ (from Q2's collector); the capacitor on Q2's base is $C_1$ (from Q1's collector). Many students swap them.
- **Peak-to-Peak Output** $\approx V_{CC} - V_{CE(\text{sat})}$.
- The duty cycle is $(T_1/T) \times 100\%$. For a square wave, duty cycle is 50%.
- **Collector resistor $R_C$** is independent of timing — it sets saturation current and output amplitude.
- **Base resistor $R_B$** must be large enough to allow capacitor discharge to occur, but small enough to fully saturate the ON transistor.
- Astable circuits find use in **clock generation, LED flashers, PWM, alarm circuits, and time-base generators**.
- **Cross-coupling** is the essence of regenerative feedback — without it, no oscillation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bistable and Monostable Multivibrators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET302 - ELECTRONIC DEVICES AND CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Bistable and Monostable Multivibrators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Multivibrator?

A **multivibrator** is a two-stage regenerative electronic circuit (employing two amplifying devices like BJT transistors cross-coupled by passive components) that is specifically designed to generate **non-sinusoidal waveforms** such as square waves, rectangular pulses, or timed trigger signals. Because the output is heavily clipped and rich in harmonics, the device is called a "multi-vibrator" — it vibrates at many frequencies simultaneously.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Multivibrators are a *core building block* of digital electronics and pulse circuitry. Bistable and Monostable are the two fundamental members of this family. The third member, **Astable**, is covered in a later module.

The three members of the multivibrator family are:

| Type | Stable States | Common Name | Primary Application |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| **Bistable** | 2 | Flip-Flop | Memory, latch, register |
| **Monostable** | 1 | One-Shot | Pulse stretching, timing, debouncing |
| **Astable** | 0 | Free-running | Clock generator, oscillator |

---

### 1.2 Bistable Multivibrator — Definition

A **Bistable Multivibrator (BMV)** is a regenerative circuit possessing **two absolutely stable states**. In one stable state, transistor Q1 is **ON (saturated)** and Q2 is **OFF (cut-off)**; in the other, Q1 is **OFF** and Q2 is **ON**. The circuit remains in whatever state it is pushed into indefinitely, until an external trigger pulse forces it to flip to the other state.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** "A bistable multivibrator is a two-transistor regenerative circuit having two stable output states, used to store one bit of binary information (0 or 1)."

#### 🔑 Intuitive Analogy — The Light Switch
Imagine a **rocker switch** on your wall. It has two positions: UP (lights on) and DOWN (lights off). It will *stay* in whichever position you last left it in. You must actively push it to change state. A bistable multivibrator behaves **exactly** like this rocker switch — it *remembers* its state. This is why it is called a **flip-flop** in digital logic: it "flips" or "flops" between two stored states.

The two stable states are:
- **State 1 (Set):** $V_{C1} = V_{CE(sat)} \approx \mathbf{0.2\ V}$, $V_{C2} \approx +V_{CC}$
- **State 2 (Reset):** $V_{C1} \approx +V_{CC}$, $V_{C2} = V_{CE(sat)} \approx \mathbf{0.2\ V}$

---

### 1.3 Monostable Multivibrator — Definition

A **Monostable Multivibrator (MMV)**, also called a **One-Shot** or **Single-Shot Multivibrator**, has **one stable state and one quasi-stable (temporary) state**. In its natural resting condition, one transistor is ON and the other is OFF. When triggered, the circuit flips to the quasi-stable state for a precisely timed duration $T$, and then automatically returns to its original stable state — producing exactly **one output pulse** per input trigger.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** "A monostable multivibrator is a circuit which, when externally triggered, switches to an unstable state for a predetermined duration determined by an RC time constant, and then returns to its single stable state."

#### 🔑 Intuitive Analogy — The Automatic Doorbell
Think of a **doorbell in a hotel lobby**. You press the button (trigger) once, and it produces a single *"ding…"* that lasts exactly **one second**, then stops — regardless of how long you keep your finger on the button. It generates **one output pulse per trigger**, with a fixed width set by an internal timer (the RC network). It is "monostable" because it has one resting (stable) state and one temporary (quasi-stable) state.

---

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- Saturation voltage of silicon BJT: $V_{CE(sat)} = \mathbf{0.2\ V}$ (standard assumption)
- Base-emitter ON voltage: $V_{BE(sat)} = \mathbf{0.7\ V}$
- Cut-off condition: $V_{BE} < \mathbf{0.5\ V}$ (BJT is OFF)
- Logic levels: $V_{OH} \approx +V_{CC}$, $V_{OL} \approx V_{CE(sat)}$
- Pulse width formula (Monostable): $T = \mathbf{0.69\ RC}$
- Minimum DC supply headroom: $V_{CC} \geq \mathbf{5\ V}$ for reliable switching

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Bistable Multivibrator — Operational Theory

The collector-coupled bistable multivibrator uses **two cross-coupled CE (common-emitter) stages**. The output (collector) of Q1 is fed back to the base of Q2 through a resistor $R_1$ (or $R$ divider), and similarly the output of Q2 is fed to the base of Q1. Positive feedback (collector-to-base coupling) ensures regenerative switching.

#### 2.1.1 Cross-Coupling & Feedback Loop
The two-stage feedback loop provides a **loop gain $\vert A \cdot B \vert \gg 1$** (Regeneration Condition). The product of the voltage gains of the two CE stages (each ≈ 50–200) ensures that any small disturbance is amplified and amplified again, driving the transistors to the extreme states of saturation and cut-off.

#### 2.1.2 Two Stable States — Logic
Let $V_{BB}$ be a small negative (or reverse) bias supplied through $R_1$ to ensure cut-off, and $+V_{CC}$ provide the ON bias. The two stable states:

- **State A:** Q1 → ON (saturated), Q2 → OFF (cut-off). $V_{C1} = V_{CE(sat)}$, $V_{C2} = +V_{CC}$.
- **State B:** Q1 → OFF (cut-off), Q2 → ON (saturated). $V_{C1} = +V_{CC}$, $V_{C2} = V_{CE(sat)}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Regeneration Condition (KTU Favourite):** For reliable bistable action, the loop gain magnitude must satisfy
> $$\vert A_{v1} \cdot A_{v2} \vert \ge 1$$
> where $A_{v1}$ and $A_{v2}$ are the small-signal voltage gains of the two CE stages. In practice, each stage has $\vert A_v \vert \approx \frac{R_C}{r_e + R_E}$ (without $R_E$ bypass, $A_v \approx -g_m R_C$), giving a large loop gain.

#### 2.1.3 Triggering the Bistable
To switch states, an external pulse is applied to one of the bases (or collectors). Two methods:

1. **Unsymmetrical / Asymmetrical Triggering:** A set pulse at the base of Q1 (or collector) forces Q1 ON → Q2 OFF. A reset pulse does the reverse. This requires two separate trigger inputs.
2. **Symmetrical / Steering-Diode Triggering:** A single trigger input is steered by two **steering diodes** to the transistor that is currently OFF. The base of the OFF transistor receives the pulse and switches it ON. This is the most commonly used method in counters and shift registers.

#### 2.1.4 Condition for Saturation
For Q1 to remain saturated when it is meant to be ON, the base current must be sufficient to drive the collector current into saturation:

$$I_{B(sat)} = \frac{I_{C(sat)}}{\beta_{dc, \min}} = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{CE(sat)}}{\beta_{dc, \min} \cdot R_C}$$

$$I_B = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{BE(sat)}}{R_1}$$

The **saturation condition** is:

$$I_B \ge I_{B(sat)} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{R_C}{R_1} \le \beta_{dc, \min}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **design rule** for choosing $R_1$ and $R_C$. For a typical $\beta_{dc} = 50$, we need $R_1 \ge 50 R_C$.

#### 2.1.5 Condition for Cut-off
Q2 must be firmly cut-off, which means its base-emitter junction must be reverse-biased. With a negative supply $V_{BB}$ connected through $R_2$:

$$V_{BE} = \frac{-V_{BB} \cdot R_1}{R_1 + R_2} \le 0\ V$$

The magnitude of this negative voltage must exceed 0.5 V to ensure Q2 stays OFF.

---

### 2.2 Monostable Multivibrator — Operational Theory

The monostable multivibrator is built using **one DC (resistive) coupling and one AC (capacitive) coupling**. The DC-coupled side provides the *stable* state, while the capacitor-coupled side creates the *quasi-stable* state through a timing network.

#### 2.2.1 Stable State (Resting)
In the resting state, Q2 is held **ON** by the base bias through $R_1$ (DC coupling), and Q1 is held **OFF** by the negative base voltage from the charged coupling capacitor $C$. The circuit sits here indefinitely until a trigger pulse arrives.

#### 2.2.2 Quasi-Stable State (Triggered)
When a negative trigger pulse is applied to the base of Q1 (or positive to the base of Q2), Q1 turns ON, causing its collector voltage $V_{C1}$ to drop sharply from $+V_{CC}$ to $V_{CE(sat)}$. This sudden drop is transmitted through the capacitor $C$ to the base of Q2, driving $V_{B2}$ far below 0 V and **cutting Q2 OFF**. The circuit is now in the quasi-stable state.

#### 2.2.3 Recovery & Return to Stable State
With Q1 ON and Q2 OFF, the capacitor $C$ now **recharges** through resistor $R$ from the $+V_{CC}$ supply toward $V_{CC}$. The base of Q2 rises exponentially. The instant $V_{B2}$ reaches $+0.5$ V (or $V_{BE(cut-in)}$), Q2 turns ON, its collector drops, and via regenerative feedback Q1 is forced OFF. The circuit snaps back to the original stable state. One output pulse has been generated.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** In the monostable, the timing capacitor $C$ charges (or discharges) through $R$ during the quasi-stable state. The pulse width is determined **solely** by this RC time constant.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Circuit | Formula | Description | Typical Value / Unit |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Bistable | $\dfrac{R_C}{R_1} \le \beta_{dc,\min}$ | Saturation condition for ON transistor | $\beta_{dc} = 50$ typical |
| 2 | Bistable | $V_{BE(OFF)} = \dfrac{-V_{BB} \cdot R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$ | Negative bias to keep transistor OFF | $V_{BE} < 0.5$ V |
| 3 | Bistable | $A_{v} \approx \dfrac{R_C}{r_e}$ | Voltage gain per CE stage (small signal) | $A_v \approx 50\text{–}200$ |
| 4 | Bistable | $\vert A_{v1} \cdot A_{v2} \vert \ge 1$ | Loop gain condition (regeneration) | Always satisfied in BMV |
| 5 | Monostable | $T = 0.693 \, R C$ | **Pulse width** of the output pulse | seconds (s) |
| 6 | Monostable | $T = R C \ln\!\left(2 - \dfrac{V_{CC}}{V_{CC} - V_{BB}}\right)$ | Generalized pulse width with $V_{BB}$ bias | seconds (s) |
| 7 | Monostable | $V_{B2}(t) = -V_{CC} + (V_{CC} + 0.7) e^{-t/RC}$ | Base voltage waveform during quasi-stable state | Volts (V) |
| 8 | Monostable | $f_{\max} = \dfrac{1}{T}$ | Maximum trigger rate for reliable one-shot | Hertz (Hz) |
| 9 | Both | $t_r \approx 0.35 / f_T$ | Rise time limited by transistor transit frequency | seconds (s) |
| 10 | Both | $V_{CE(sat)} \approx \mathbf{0.2\ V}$ | Standard saturation voltage assumption | Volts (V) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL EXAM TIP:** The factor **0.693 = ln 2** arises because the capacitor charges from $-V_{CC}$ to ground (0 V) — the time taken to reach $V_{BE(cut-in)} \approx 0.5$ V gives $\ln 2$ only when the upper rail is $V_{CC}$ and lower rail is $-V_{CC}$ (symmetric). For asymmetric rails, use the **generalized formula** (Row 6 above).

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Bistable:** Forms the memory cell of every static RAM (SRAM) bit, every flip-flop (D, JK, T, SR), counters, shift registers, and latches. The 6-transistor (6T) SRAM cell in a CPU cache is essentially a pair of cross-coupled CMOS inverters — the CMOS realization of a bistable multivibrator.
- **Monostable:** Used in pulse-stretching (to widen a narrow pulse to a measurable width for a microcontroller input), switch debouncing, missing-pulse detection in industrial control, delay generation in digital timing circuits, and "watchdog" timers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Monostable Pulse Width $T = 0.69 RC$

This is the **most frequently asked derivation** in KTU University Exams for this topic. We will derive it rigorously, step-by-step.

> **Assumption:** Assume identical symmetric supply, $V_{CC}$ on the collector side and the capacitor $C$ initially charges the base of Q2 to approximately $0$ V in the stable state. The trigger pulse causes the collector of Q1 to swing from $+V_{CC}$ to $V_{CE(sat)} \approx 0$ V. This negative step of magnitude $V_{CC}$ is transmitted through the coupling capacitor $C$ to the base of Q2.

**Step 1 — Identify the boundary voltages of the base waveform of Q2.**

At the instant of trigger ($t = 0^+$):
- The collector of Q1 was at $+V_{CC}$ before the trigger.
- After triggering, $V_{C1}$ drops to $V_{CE(sat)} \approx 0$ V.
- Since the voltage across a capacitor cannot change instantaneously, the base of Q2 must drop by the same amount: from its initial value of $V_{BE(sat)} \approx 0.7$ V down to $(0.7 - V_{CC})$.
- Therefore, the **initial voltage** of the capacitor-charging transient is:
$$V_{B2}(0^+) = V_{BE(sat)} - V_{CC} \approx 0.7 - V_{CC} \approx -V_{CC}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the final (asymptotic) voltage of the charging transient.**

After Q2 turns OFF, the base of Q2 is connected to the $+V_{CC}$ supply through the timing resistor $R$ (the only path to the base, since Q1 is now ON and its collector is low). The capacitor charges toward $+V_{CC}$ with time constant $\tau = RC$.

$$V_{B2}(\infty) = +V_{CC}$$

**Step 3 — Write the general charging equation.**

For a first-order RC circuit charging from $V(0)$ toward $V(\infty)$ with time constant $\tau = RC$:

$$V_{B2}(t) = V_{B2}(\infty) - \left[V_{B2}(\infty) - V_{B2}(0^+)\right] e^{-t/RC}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the boundary values.**

$$V_{B2}(t) = V_{CC} - \left[V_{CC} - (-V_{CC})\right] e^{-t/RC}$$

$$V_{B2}(t) = V_{CC} - 2V_{CC}\, e^{-t/RC}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the cut-in condition to find the pulse width $T$.**

Q2 turns back ON when $V_{B2}(t)$ reaches $V_{BE(cut-in)} \approx 0.5$ V (for silicon BJT, often taken as $0$ V for ideal derivation). We set $V_{B2}(T) = 0$ V (idealized boundary):

$$0 = V_{CC} - 2V_{CC}\, e^{-T/RC}$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $T$ algebraically.**

$$2V_{CC}\, e^{-T/RC} = V_{CC}$$

$$e^{-T/RC} = \frac{1}{2}$$

$$-\frac{T}{RC} = \ln\!\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) = -\ln 2$$

$$\boxed{T = RC \ln 2 \approx 0.693\, RC \approx 0.69\, RC}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The factor $\ln 2 = 0.693$ emerges naturally because the capacitor must charge through a voltage swing of $2V_{CC}$ (from $-V_{CC}$ to $+V_{CC}$), and we need it to reach the midpoint (0 V) — exactly **one half-life** of the exponential.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Bistable Saturation Condition

**Step 1 — Collector current in saturation:**

When Q1 is ON, its collector is at $V_{CE(sat)}$. The current through $R_C$ is:

$$I_{C(sat)} = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{CE(sat)}}{R_C} \approx \frac{V_{CC}}{R_C}$$

**Step 2 — Minimum base current to just saturate Q1:**

$$I_{B(\min)} = \frac{I_{C(sat)}}{\beta_{dc,\min}} = \frac{V_{CC}}{\beta_{dc,\min} \cdot R_C}$$

**Step 3 — Actual base current supplied:**

The base of Q1 is fed from the $+V_{CC}$ supply through resistor $R_1$ (since Q2 is OFF, its collector is at $+V_{CC}$ and acts as a voltage source):

$$I_B = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{BE(sat)}}{R_1} \approx \frac{V_{CC}}{R_1}$$

**Step 4 — Saturation condition (force $I_B \ge I_{B(\min)}$):**

$$\frac{V_{CC}}{R_1} \ge \frac{V_{CC}}{\beta_{dc,\min} \cdot R_C}$$

$$\beta_{dc,\min} \cdot R_C \ge R_1$$

$$\boxed{\frac{R_C}{R_1} \le \beta_{dc,\min}}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Bistable Cut-off Condition

**Step 1 — The base of the OFF transistor (say Q2) is connected to two resistors:**
- $R_1$ (from Q1's collector, which is at $V_{CE(sat)} \approx 0$ V since Q1 is ON)
- $R_2$ (from the negative supply $-V_{BB}$)

**Step 2 — Apply superposition (or nodal analysis) at the base of Q2:**

The base voltage is the weighted sum:

$$V_{B2} = \frac{V_{C1} \cdot R_2 + (-V_{BB}) \cdot R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$$

With $V_{C1} = V_{CE(sat)} \approx 0$ V:

$$V_{B2} \approx \frac{-V_{BB} \cdot R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$$

**Step 3 — Cut-off condition (force $V_{B2} \le 0$ V, ideally $< -0.5$ V):**

$$\boxed{\frac{V_{BB} \cdot R_1}{R_1 + R_2} \ge 0.5\ \text{V}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This negative bias is what makes the BMV *bistable* — without $V_{BB}$, any thermal drift could push the OFF transistor weakly into conduction, destroying the stable state.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Bistable vs Monostable

| Parameter | Bistable Multivibrator | Monostable Multivibrator |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Stable states | **2** | **1** |
| Quasi-stable states | 0 | **1** |
| Coupling | Resistive (R-R) for both sides | One R, one C |
| Output waveform | Square wave (on external clock) | Single pulse per trigger |
| Trigger requirement | Continuous clock | Single trigger pulse |
| Pulse width | Set by external clock period | $T = 0.69 RC$ (internal) |
| Triggering methods | Set-Reset / Steering diodes | Base trigger / Collector trigger |
| Regeneration condition | $\vert A_{v1} \cdot A_{v2} \vert \ge 1$ | $\vert A_{v1} \cdot A_{v2} \vert \ge 1$ |
| Use in digital systems | Flip-flop, latch, register | One-shot, timer, delay |
| Power dissipation | Lower (one transistor ON at a time) | Slightly higher during pulse |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note for KTU 2024:** Exact transistor-level schematics are typically drawn in the exam sheet by hand. For a *Mermaid-rendered block architecture*, we map the functional signal flow of the two multivibrators below. This satisfies the KTU 2024 scheme's emphasis on **functional understanding** of the regenerative feedback loop.

### 4.1 Bistable Multivibrator — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeP["VCC Supply Rail"] --> nodeRC1["RC1 Collector Load"]
    nodeRC1 --> nodeQ1C["Collector of Q1 Output VC1"]
    nodeQ1C --> nodeR1["Coupling Resistor R1"]
    nodeR1 --> nodeQ2B["Base of Q2 VB2"]
    nodeQ2B --> nodeQ2["Q2 NPN BJT Stage"]
    nodeQ2 --> nodeRC2["RC2 Collector Load"]
    nodeRC2 --> nodeQ2C["Collector of Q2 Output VC2"]
    nodeQ2C --> nodeR2["Coupling Resistor R2"]
    nodeR2 --> nodeQ1B["Base of Q1 VB1"]
    nodeQ1B --> nodeQ1["Q1 NPN BJT Stage"]
    nodeQ1 --> nodeQ1C
    nodeVBB["Negative Bias VBB"] --> nodeR3["R3"]
    nodeR3 --> nodeQ2B
    nodeVBB --> nodeR4["R4"]
    nodeR4 --> nodeQ1B
    nodeTRIG["External Trigger Pulse"] --> nodeD1["Steering Diode D1"]
    nodeTRIG --> nodeD2["Steering Diode D2"]
    nodeD1 --> nodeQ1B
    nodeD2 --> nodeQ2B
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The cross-coupling from $V_{C1} \to V_{B2}$ and $V_{C2} \to V_{B1}$ creates a **closed regenerative loop**. The negative supply $V_{BB}$ through $R_3$ and $R_4$ ensures the OFF transistor is firmly cut off. Steering diodes $D_1$ and $D_2$ route the trigger to the OFF base.

---

### 4.2 Monostable Multivibrator — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeP["VCC Supply"] --> nodeRC1["RC1 Collector Load"]
    nodeRC1 --> nodeQ1C["Collector of Q1 VC1"]
    nodeQ1C --> nodeC["Timing Capacitor C"]
    nodeC --> nodeQ2B["Base of Q2 VB2 Timing Node"]
    nodeQ2B --> nodeQ2["Q2 NPN BJT Stage"]
    nodeQ2 --> nodeRC2["RC2 Collector Load"]
    nodeRC2 --> nodeQ2C["Collector of Q2 VC2"]
    nodeQ2C --> nodeR1["DC Coupling Resistor R1"]
    nodeR1 --> nodeQ1B["Base of Q1 VB1 DC Coupled"]
    nodeQ1B --> nodeQ1["Q1 NPN BJT Stage"]
    nodeQ1 --> nodeQ1C
    nodeR2["Timing Resistor R from VCC"] --> nodeQ2B
    nodeTRIG["Negative Trigger Pulse"] --> nodeQ1B
    nodeOUT["Output Pulse from VC2 Width T equal 0.69 RC"] -.-> nodeQ2C
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The right loop ($V_{C2} \to R_1 \to V_{B1} \to Q_1$) is **DC-coupled** (resistive) — this establishes the single **stable state**. The left loop ($V_{C1} \to C \to V_{B2} \to Q_2$) is **AC-coupled** (capacitive) — this creates the temporary **quasi-stable state** with time constant $\tau = RC$. Timing resistor $R$ charges the capacitor toward $V_{CC}$ during the quasi-stable state.

---

### 4.3 State Diagram of Bistable Multivibrator

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> StateA: Power ON random
    StateA: STATE A Q1 ON and Q2 OFF
    StateB: STATE B Q1 OFF and Q2 ON
    StateA --> StateB: Set Trigger at Base of Q1
    StateB --> StateA: Reset Trigger at Base of Q2
    StateA --> StateA: Remains Stable
    StateB --> StateB: Remains Stable
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Each state has a **self-loop** ("remains stable") indicating the indefinite retention of the state. The two arrows between the states represent the external trigger events.

---

### 4.4 State Diagram of Monostable Multivibrator

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Stable: Power ON
    Stable: STABLE STATE Q1 OFF and Q2 ON
    QState: QUASI STABLE Q1 ON and Q2 OFF Duration T
    Stable --> QState: External Trigger
    QState --> Stable: Auto return after T equal 0.69 RC
    Stable --> Stable: Idle no trigger
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The circuit can sit in the stable state indefinitely. A single trigger sends it to the quasi-stable state, from which it **automatically returns** after time $T = 0.69 RC$. There is **no second trigger needed** for the return.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> **Mark Distribution Pattern (KTU 2024 Scheme):** University End-Semester Exam questions on this topic carry either **3 marks** (short answer / Part A) or **14 marks** (full descriptive / Part B with internal choice). Internal choice is mandatory for Part B.

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, May 2024 Pattern]**
**Define a bistable multivibrator. Mention its two stable states and one application.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A **bistable multivibrator** is a two-transistor regenerative switching circuit that possesses **two absolutely stable states**. In State 1, transistor Q1 is **ON (saturated)** with $V_{C1} \approx V_{CE(sat)}$ and Q2 is **OFF (cut-off)** with $V_{C2} \approx +V_{CC}$. In State 2, the roles are reversed. The circuit remains in either state indefinitely until an external trigger pulse is applied. *(2 marks)*
>
> **Application:** It is used as a **basic memory cell (flip-flop / latch)** in digital logic circuits, registers, and counters. *(1 mark)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Dec 2023 Pattern]**
**What is a monostable multivibrator? Write its pulse width equation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A **monostable multivibrator** is a two-transistor regenerative circuit with **one stable state and one quasi-stable (temporary) state**. In the resting condition, one transistor is ON and the other is OFF. When triggered, the circuit flips to the quasi-stable state and automatically returns to the original stable state after a fixed duration, producing **one output pulse per input trigger**. *(2 marks)*
>
> **Pulse width equation:**
> $$\boxed{T = 0.693\, RC \approx 0.69\, RC\ \text{seconds}}$$
> where $R$ is the timing resistor and $C$ is the timing capacitor in the AC-coupled feedback path. *(1 mark)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern Note:** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks**, split as **(a) 7 marks** + **(b) 7 marks**. Internal choice means the student answers *either* Question A *or* Question B in full.

---

#### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Model, Module 1, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **collector-coupled bistable multivibrator**. Derive the condition for saturation and the condition for cut-off of the transistors. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain **symmetrical triggering** of a bistable multivibrator using **steering diodes** with a neat circuit diagram. Why is symmetrical triggering preferred in digital counters? *(7 marks)*

##### Model Solution for Q.A(a):

**Circuit Description:** The collector-coupled bistable multivibrator consists of two identical CE stages Q1 and Q2. The collector of Q1 is coupled to the base of Q2 through resistor $R_1$, and the collector of Q2 is coupled to the base of Q1 through resistor $R_2$. A negative supply $-V_{BB}$ is connected through $R_3$ and $R_4$ to the bases of Q1 and Q2 respectively to ensure cut-off. *(2 marks for diagram and description)*

**Operation — State 1 (Q1 ON, Q2 OFF):**

Assume Q1 is ON (saturated). Then $V_{C1} = V_{CE(sat)} \approx 0.2$ V. This low voltage is applied to the base of Q2 through $R_1$. The voltage divider formed by $R_1$ and $R_2$ (with $V_{C1}$ and $-V_{BB}$) makes $V_{B2}$ negative, ensuring Q2 is cut off. With Q2 OFF, $V_{C2} \approx +V_{CC}$. This high voltage is fed back to the base of Q1 through $R_2$, providing sufficient base current to keep Q1 saturated. The circuit is stable in this state. *(2 marks for state analysis)*

**Operation — State 2 (Q1 OFF, Q2 ON):** Symmetric reasoning — Q2 saturates, $V_{C2}$ drops, cutting off Q1, whose collector rises to $+V_{CC}$, feeding back to keep Q2 ON. *(1 mark)*

**Saturation Condition (derivation):**
When Q1 is ON, the base current is $I_B = \frac{V_{CC}}{R_1}$ (since Q2's collector is at $+V_{CC}$ and acts as the source). The collector saturation current is $I_{C(sat)} = \frac{V_{CC}}{R_C}$. For Q1 to saturate:

$$I_B \ge I_{B(\min)} = \frac{I_{C(sat)}}{\beta_{dc,\min}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{R_C}{R_1} \le \beta_{dc,\min}$$

*(1 mark for derivation)*

**Cut-off Condition (derivation):**
When Q1 is ON, $V_{C1} = 0.2$ V. The base of Q2 is connected to $V_{C1} = 0.2$ V through $R_1$ and to $-V_{BB}$ through $R_3$. By superposition:

$$V_{B2} = \frac{V_{C1} R_3 + (-V_{BB}) R_1}{R_1 + R_3} \approx \frac{-V_{BB} R_1}{R_1 + R_3}$$

For Q2 to be cut off, $V_{B2} \le 0$ V (preferably $\le -0.5$ V):

$$\frac{V_{BB} R_1}{R_1 + R_3} \ge 0.5\ \text{V}$$

*(1 mark for derivation)*

##### Model Solution for Q.A(b):

**Symmetrical Triggering with Steering Diodes:** In this method, a **single common trigger input** is fed to the cathodes of two steering diodes $D_1$ and $D_2$, whose anodes are connected to the bases of Q1 and Q2 respectively. A small forward bias $V_{BB}$ (or pull-up resistors to $+V_{CC}$) keeps the diodes normally reverse-biased. *(2 marks for diagram)*

**Operation:** When a trigger pulse of appropriate polarity is applied:
- The diode connected to the **OFF transistor's base** is forward-biased (because the OFF base is more negative) and conducts the trigger to that base, turning it ON.
- The diode connected to the **ON transistor's base** remains reverse-biased, isolating that base.
- Thus, a single trigger pulse always flips the state, regardless of which state the circuit is currently in. *(2 marks)*

**Why Preferred in Counters:** Counters require a sequence of clock pulses to toggle the flip-flop on **every clock edge**. With symmetrical triggering, a single clock line can drive the entire counter chain — no need for separate SET and RESET lines. The circuit naturally steers each clock pulse to the correct base, ensuring reliable toggling. It also reduces the component count and wiring complexity. *(2 marks for explanation)*

**Waveforms:** *(1 mark for drawing the input clock and output waveforms showing toggling on each clock edge)*

---

#### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam – Model, Module 1, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram and waveforms, explain the operation of a **collector-coupled monostable multivibrator**. Clearly mark the stable and quasi-stable states. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Derive the **expression for the pulse width $T$** of a monostable multivibrator. A monostable multivibrator has $R = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$ and $C = 0.1\ \mu\text{F}$. Calculate the pulse width and the maximum trigger frequency for reliable one-shot operation. *(7 marks)*

##### Model Solution for Q.B(a):

**Circuit Description:** The collector-coupled monostable multivibrator uses Q1 and Q2. The collector of Q2 is **resistively coupled** to the base of Q1 through $R_1$ (DC coupling — this defines the stable state). The collector of Q1 is **capacitively coupled** to the base of Q2 through capacitor $C$ (AC coupling — this defines the quasi-stable state).
