A timing resistor $R$ connects $+V_{CC}$ to the base of Q2. Trigger input is applied to the base of Q1 (or collector of Q2). *(2 marks for diagram and description)*

**Stable State (Resting):** Q2 is **ON** (saturated) because its base is held high through $R$ from $+V_{CC}$. With Q2 ON, $V_{C2} = V_{CE(sat)} \approx 0.2$ V. This low voltage is fed to the base of Q1 through $R_1$, which is insufficient to turn Q1 ON (assuming Q1's base bias is also kept low). Thus Q1 is **OFF** and $V_{C1} \approx +V_{CC}$. The capacitor $C$ is fully charged to $V_{CC} - 0.7 \approx V_{CC}$ in this state. The circuit rests here indefinitely. *(1.5 marks)*

**Triggered State:** A negative trigger pulse applied to the base of Q1 turns Q1 **ON**. $V_{C1}$ drops sharply from $+V_{CC}$ to $V_{CE(sat)}$. Since the voltage across $C$ cannot change instantaneously, $V_{B2}$ drops by the same amount: from $0.7$ V down to $-(V_{CC} - 0.7) \approx -V_{CC}$. Q2 is **forcefully cut off**. The circuit enters the **quasi-stable state**: Q1 ON, Q2 OFF. *(1.5 marks)*

**Quasi-Stable State — Capacitor Recharge:** With Q2 OFF, the capacitor $C$ recharges through $R$ from $+V_{CC}$ toward $V_{CC}$. The base voltage of Q2 rises exponentially. The instant $V_{B2}$ reaches $V_{BE(cut-in)} \approx 0.5$ V, Q2 turns ON, $V_{C2}$ drops, cutting off Q1 via $R_1$ feedback, and the circuit returns to the stable state. *(1 mark)*

**Waveforms:** Draw $V_{B2}$ (exponential rise from $-V_{CC}$ to $+V_{CC}$, crossing 0.5 V at $t = T$), $V_{C1}$ (square pulse from $+V_{CC}$ to $0.2$ V for duration $T$), and $V_{C2}$ (square pulse from $0.2$ V to $+V_{CC}$ for duration $T$). *(1 mark for waveforms)*

##### Model Solution for Q.B(b):

**Derivation of Pulse Width (full derivation as in Section 3.1):**

The base of Q2 starts at $V_{B2}(0^+) = -V_{CC}$ and charges toward $+V_{CC}$ with time constant $\tau = RC$:

$$V_{B2}(t) = V_{CC}\left(1 - 2 e^{-t/RC}\right)$$

At $t = T$, $V_{B2}(T) = 0$ V (ideal cut-in):

$$0 = V_{CC}\left(1 - 2 e^{-T/RC}\right) \quad \Rightarrow \quad e^{-T/RC} = \frac{1}{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad T = RC \ln 2$$

$$\boxed{T = 0.693\, RC \approx 0.69\, RC}$$

*(3 marks for derivation)*

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given: $R = 10\ \text{k}\Omega = 10^4\ \Omega$, $C = 0.1\ \mu\text{F} = 10^{-7}\ \text{F}$.

$$T = 0.693 \times 10^4 \times 10^{-7} = 0.693 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{s}$$

$$\boxed{T = 0.693\ \text{ms} = 693\ \mu\text{s}}$$

*(1.5 marks)*

**Maximum Trigger Frequency:**

For the monostable to reliably return to the stable state and recover before the next trigger, the period between triggers must be at least $T$ (in practice, slightly more, including the recovery time $t_r \approx 3R_CC$):

$$f_{\max} = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{0.693 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 1.443\ \text{kHz}$$

$$\boxed{f_{\max} \approx 1.44\ \text{kHz}}$$

*(1.5 marks for calculation + 1 mark for stating the recovery-time caveat)*

---

### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in the KTU Board Exam:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Saturation vs Cut-off Conditions:** Students often write the saturation condition as $\frac{R_1}{R_C} \le \beta_{dc}$ — this is **inverted**. The correct form is $\frac{R_C}{R_1} \le \beta_{dc}$. One mark deducted for each incorrect inequality. (Loss: 1–2 marks)
>
> 2. **Forgetting to Mention $V_{BE(sat)} \approx 0.7$ V and $V_{CE(sat)} \approx 0.2$ V:** KTU examiners award marks for stating these standard assumptions before starting derivations. (Loss: 0.5–1 mark)
>
> 3. **Wrong Boundary for the Capacitor Voltage in Monostable Derivation:** Many students assume the base of Q2 starts at $0$ V and rises to $V_{CC}$ — this gives $T = RC \ln 2$ trivially but is **physically wrong**. The correct boundary is $-V_{CC}$ to $+V_{CC}$ because the negative-going step at the collector of Q1 couples directly through the capacitor. (Loss: 2–3 marks)
>
> 4. **Drawing the Circuit Without Labelling All Components:** Q1, Q2, $R_C$, $R$, $R_1$, $R_2$, $C$, $V_{CC}$, $V_{BB}$, trigger input, and output nodes must **all be labelled**. An unlabelled circuit attracts a 1–2 mark penalty.
>
> 5. **Not Drawing Waveforms in Monostable Questions:** Waveform sketches of $V_{B2}$, $V_{C1}$, $V_{C2}$ are worth **at least 2 marks** in a 7-mark sub-question. Omitting them is the single most common reason for losing marks.

---

### 5.4 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

✅ **Bistable Multivibrator (BMV):**
- Two stable states, no quasi-stable state.
- Cross-coupled CE stages with **resistive** feedback.
- **Saturation condition:** $\dfrac{R_C}{R_1} \le \beta_{dc,\min}$.
- **Cut-off condition:** Negative bias $V_{BB}$ must hold the OFF base below 0.5 V.
- **Regeneration:** $\vert A_{v1} A_{v2} \vert \ge 1$ (loop gain > 1).
- **Triggering methods:** (i) Unsymmetrical (SET / RESET), (ii) Symmetrical (steering diodes).
- **Applications:** Flip-flop, latch, register, SRAM cell.

✅ **Monostable Multivibrator (MMV):**
- One stable state, one quasi-stable state.
- **Asymmetric coupling:** one R-coupled, one C-coupled.
- **Pulse width:** $T = 0.693\, RC$ (assuming symmetric $\pm V_{CC}$ rails).
- **Generalized formula:** $T = RC \ln\!\left(2 - \dfrac{V_{CC}}{V_{CC} - V_{BB}}\right)$ if $V_{BB} \ne -V_{CC}$.
- **Maximum trigger frequency:** $f_{\max} = 1/T$.
- **Applications:** One-shot, pulse stretcher, switch debouncer, missing-pulse detector, delay generator.

✅ **Common Cross-Topic Points to Memorize:**
- $V_{CE(sat)} \approx 0.2$ V, $V_{BE(sat)} \approx 0.7$ V.
- The factor **0.693 = ln 2** comes from charging through a voltage ratio of 2.
- Cross-coupling must always be **regenerative** (positive feedback with loop gain > 1).
- Both circuits are **direct-coupled** in some portion — this is what makes them DC-stable.

✅ **Diagrams to Practice Drawing Quickly:**
1. Collector-coupled BMV with $V_{BB}$, $R_C$, $R_1$, $R_2$, $R_3$, $R_4$, and trigger input.
2. Collector-coupled MMV with $C$, $R$, $R_1$, $R_C$, $V_{CC}$, and trigger input.
3. Waveforms: $V_{B1}$, $V_{B2}$, $V_{C1}$, $V_{C2}$ vs time for both circuits.
4. Steering-diode trigger circuit.

> **End of Module 1 — Bistable and Monostable Multivibrators.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Schmitt Trigger
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAET302 - ELECTRONIC DEVICES AND CIRCUITS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Schmitt Trigger # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Schmitt Trigger** is a regenerative comparator circuit with an embedded **positive feedback loop** (also known as *regenerative feedback* or *accumulative feedback*) that converts a slowly varying or noisy analog input signal into a clean, sharply-defined digital (square-wave) output. It possesses two distinct switching threshold voltages — an **Upper Threshold Voltage ($V_{UT}$)** and a **Lower Threshold Voltage ($V_{LT}$)** — whose difference defines the **Hysteresis Voltage ($V_H$)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Schmitt Trigger is a foundational topic that bridges the linear (Op-Amp) and non-linear (wave-shaping) domains of Electronic Devices & Circuits. Questions frequently appear under **CO1: Understand the characteristics of electronic devices and circuits** with a typical mark weightage of 7–14 marks. The circuit is commonly implemented using: - **Operational Amplifiers (Op-Amp based)** — for precision analog-to-digital conversion - **Transistors (BJT/MOSFET based)** — for digital logic gates (e.g., CMOS Schmitt inverter, 74HC14) ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Smart Thermostat Imagine a **standard room thermostat** set to maintain $25^{\circ}\text{C}$: - If temperature drops to exactly $25^{\circ}\text{C}$, the heater turns on. - The moment it rises back to $25^{\circ}\text{C}$, the heater turns off. - This causes the heater to **chatter** (rapid on/off cycles) when the temperature hovers around the set point. Now imagine a **smart thermostat** (a "Schmitt Thermostat"): - It turns the heater **ON** when the temperature drops below $23^{\circ}\text{C}$ ($V_{LT}$). - It turns the heater **OFF** only when the temperature rises above $27^{\circ}\text{C}$ ($V_{UT}$). - The $4^{\circ}\text{C}$ gap between the two thresholds is the **hysteresis band**, which **eliminates the chattering problem**. > [!NOTE] > **Key Takeaway:** The Schmitt Trigger behaves exactly like a smart thermostat for voltage signals. The "dead zone" (hysteresis) between the two thresholds provides **noise immunity**, ensuring the output transitions occur *only once* per intended input crossing. ## 1.3 Key Physical & Circuit Constants - **Saturation Voltage ($V_{sat}$ or $V_{sat}^{\pm}$)**: The maximum output swing of the op-amp (typically $\pm V_{CC}$ or $\pm 13\text{ V}$ for a $\pm 15\text{ V}$ supply op-amp). For ideal analysis, $V_{sat} = V_{CC}$. - **Loop Gain ($A\beta$)**: Must be **greater than 1** for the regenerative action to occur. - **Hysteresis Band ($V_H$)**: The voltage gap $V_{UT} - V_{LT}$. This is the **noise margin** of the circuit. - **Standard IC Equivalents**: **IC 741** (Op-Amp based), **IC 74LS14 / 74HC14** (Hex Inverter with Schmitt Trigger input). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Logic — The "Why" Behind Positive Feedback In a standard open-loop comparator, a slow or noisy input transition causes the output to *bounce* or *oscillate* near the threshold. The Schmitt Trigger eliminates this using the following structured logic: 1. **Positive Feedback Loop is Established** — A portion of the output is fed back to the *non-inverting* (for inverting Schmitt) input through a resistive divider ($R_1$, $R_2$). 2. **Two Stable States** — The op-amp output can only rest in one of two states: $+V_{sat}$ or $-V_{sat}$. 3. **Threshold Comparison** — The feedback voltage at the non-inverting terminal ($V^+$) is constantly compared with the input voltage ($V_{in}$). 4. **Snap Action (Regeneration)** — The moment $V_{in}$ crosses one of the thresholds, the positive feedback *accelerates* the switching, making the transition **virtually instantaneous** (slew rate limited only by op-amp bandwidth). 5. **Hysteresis is Inherent** — Because the threshold itself *depends on the output state*, the circuit has memory of its previous state. ## 2.2 Two Canonical Configurations ### A. Inverting Schmitt Trigger - Input $V_{in}$ is applied to the **inverting terminal ($-$)** of the op-amp. - Feedback resistor network ($R_1$ from output to inverting node? — No, $R_1$ from output to non-inverting, $R_2$ from non-inverting to ground) creates the threshold. - The output is **inverted** with respect to the input logic levels. ### B. Non-Inverting Schmitt Trigger - Input $V_{in}$ is applied to the **non-inverting terminal ($+$)** of the op-amp. - Output is **in phase** with the intended switching logic. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **Parameter** | **Inverting Schmitt Trigger** | **Non-Inverting Schmitt Trigger** | **Unit** | |---|---|---|---| | Upper Threshold $V_{UT}$ | $+\dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} \cdot V_{sat}$ | $+\dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} \cdot V_{sat}$ | Volts (V) | | Lower Threshold $V_{LT}$ | $-\dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} \cdot V_{sat}$ | $-\dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} \cdot V_{sat}$ | Volts (V) | | Hysteresis Voltage $V_H$ | $V_{UT} - V_{LT} = 2 \cdot \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} \cdot V_{sat}$ | $2 \cdot \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} \cdot V_{sat}$ | Volts (V) | | Feedback Factor $\beta$ | $\dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$ | $\dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$ | Dimensionless | | Output High State | $+V_{sat}$ | $+V_{sat}$ | Volts (V) | | Output Low State | $-V_{sat}$ | $-V_{sat}$ | Volts (V) | | Input Impedance | $R_{in}$ (external) | $R_{in}$ (external) | Ohms ($\Omega$) | | Condition for Snap Action | $A\beta > 1$ | $A\beta > 1$ | — | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Both configurations have **identical threshold formulas**. The distinction lies in *where* $V_{in}$ is applied and *how* the input waveform is interpreted on the transfer curve. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Wave Shaping:** Converts sine, triangle, or any analog waveform into a clean square wave — used extensively in **function generators**. - **Noise Filtering in Digital Inputs:** Debouncing mechanical switches and eliminating noise from sensor lines (e.g., in **ECG/EEG machines**). - **Oscillator Front-Ends:** Forms the active element of **relaxation oscillators** (along with an RC timing network). - **Over-Voltage Protection:** Used in **SMPS (Switched Mode Power Supplies)** as protection comparators. - **Edge Detection:** Detects zero-crossings of slowly varying signals with high noise immunity. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Threshold Voltages — Inverting Schmitt Trigger ### Circuit Setup For an inverting Schmitt Trigger, $V_{in}$ is applied to the **inverting terminal**. The **non-inverting terminal** receives feedback from the output through a resistive voltage divider formed by $R_1$ (between output and $V^+$) and $R_2$ (between $V^+$ and ground). ### Step 1: Expression for $V^+$ By the **voltage divider rule** (assuming the op-amp draws negligible input current at the $+$ terminal, which is the standard ideal-op-amp assumption): $$ V^+ = V_{out} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} $$ ### Step 2: Define the Two Output States Since the op-amp is in open-loop (high gain) configuration with positive feedback, the output can only be in one of two states: - $V_{out} = +V_{sat}$ (when $V^- < V^+$) - $V_{out} = -V_{sat}$ (when $V^- > V^+$) ### Step 3: Calculate $V^+$ for Each Output State **Case 1: When $V_{out} = +V_{sat}$** $$ V^+ = (+V_{sat}) \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} $$ Since $V^- = V_{in}$, the op-amp will switch state when $V_{in}$ **just exceeds** $V^+$. At this point, $V_{in}$ is the **Upper Threshold Voltage ($V_{UT}$)**: $$ V_{UT} = (+V_{sat}) \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} $$ **Case 2: When $V_{out} = -V_{sat}$** $$ V^+ = (-V_{sat}) \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} $$ The op-amp will switch back when $V_{in}$ falls **just below** $V^+$. At this point, $V_{in}$ is the **Lower Threshold Voltage ($V_{LT}$)**: $$ V_{LT} = (-V_{sat}) \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} $$ ### Step 4: Hysteresis Voltage Calculation $$ V_H = V_{UT} - V_{LT} = (V_{sat}) \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} - (-V_{sat}) \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} $$ $$ V_H = 2 V_{sat} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Alternative Notation:** Many textbooks and KTU question papers use the convention where the resistor connected from output to $V^+$ is called $R_1$, and the resistor from $V^+$ to ground is called $R_2$. Under this convention, the formula becomes $V_{UT} = \pm \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} V_{sat}$. **Always confirm the labeling convention from the circuit diagram** before solving. Both forms are mathematically equivalent — only the labeling differs. ## 3.2 Numerical Example — Worked Solution for KTU Pattern > **Problem:** An op-amp based inverting Schmitt Trigger has $R_1 = 10\text{ k}\Omega$ (feedback to $V^+$) and $R_2 = 90\text{ k}\Omega$ (from $V^+$ to ground). The op-amp saturates at $\pm 12\text{ V}$. A triangular wave of amplitude $\pm 8\text{ V}$ is applied at the input. Sketch the output waveform. ### Solution: **Step 1: Calculate the feedback factor** $$ \beta = \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} = \frac{10\text{ k}\Omega}{10\text{ k}\Omega + 90\text{ k}\Omega} = \frac{10}{100} = 0.1 $$ **Step 2: Calculate $V_{UT}$** $$ V_{UT} = +V_{sat} \cdot \beta = +12 \cdot 0.1 = +1.2\text{ V} $$ **Step 3: Calculate $V_{LT}$** $$ V_{LT} = -V_{sat} \cdot \beta = -12 \cdot 0.1 = -1.2\text{ V} $$ **Step 4: Calculate Hysteresis Voltage** $$ V_H = V_{UT} - V_{LT} = 1.2 - (-1.2) = 2.4\text{ V} $$ **Step 5: Sketch the Output** - When $V_{in} < -1.2\text{ V}$: $V_{out} = +12\text{ V}$ - When $V_{in} > +1.2\text{ V}$: $V_{out} = -12\text{ V}$ - When $-1.2\text{ V} < V_{in} < +1.2\text{ V}$: $V_{out}$ holds its **previous state** (memory effect) The resulting output is a **square wave** that switches sharply at the two threshold crossings. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Switching Behavior | **Input Condition** | **Current Output State** | **Action Taken** | **New Output State** | |---|---|---|---| | $V_{in} \uparrow$ crosses $V_{UT}$ | $V_{out} = +V_{sat}$ | Compare $V_{in}$ vs $V^+ = +V_{UT}$ | $V_{out} \to -V_{sat}$ | | $V_{in} \downarrow$ crosses $V_{LT}$ | $V_{out} = -V_{sat}$ | Compare $V_{in}$ vs $V^+ = -V_{LT}$ | $V_{out} \to +V_{sat}$ | | $-V_{LT} < V_{in} < +V_{UT}$ (in band) | Holds previous | Memory / hysteresis | No change | | $V_{in}$ hovers near a threshold | Depends on history | Noise immunity active | Anti-chatter | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Inverting Schmitt Trigger The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **block-level signal flow** of an inverting Schmitt Trigger, mapping all major functional nodes and the regenerative feedback path. ```mermaid graph LR VinNode["INPUT V_in
Sine or Triangle Wave"] --> CompNode["OP-AMP
Open Loop Comparator"] FBNode1["FEEDBACK NETWORK
R1 and R2 Divider"] --> CompNode CompNode --> OutNode["OUTPUT V_out
Square Wave +-V_sat"] OutNode --> FBNode1 ThresholdNode["THRESHOLD COMPARATOR LOGIC
V+ = V_out times R2 divided by R1+R2"] --> CompNode SatNode["SATURATION LIMITER
V_sat approx V_CC"] --> OutNode HystNode["HYSTERESIS WINDOW
V_UT to V_LT Band"] -.-> ThresholdNode ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Non-Inverting Schmitt Trigger ```mermaid graph TD InNode["INPUT V_in
To Non-Inverting Terminal"] --> OpNode["OP-AMP
Open Loop"] FbNode["FEEDBACK DIVIDER
R1 from Output R2 to Ground"] --> InvIn["INVERTING INPUT V_minus"] OpNode --> OutNode["OUTPUT V_out
Square Wave"] OutNode --> FbNode InvIn --> OpNode ThreshNode["THRESHOLD VOLTAGE BLOCK
V_UT and V_LT defined by R1 and R2"] -.-> OpNode NoiseNode["NOISE FILTER ZONE
Inside Hysteresis Band"] -.-> ThreshNode ``` ## 4.3 Functional State Machine — Hysteresis Switching Logic ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> StateHigh StateHigh: STATE HIGH : V_out equals +V_sat StateHigh --> StateLow : V_in crosses V_UT from below StateLow: STATE LOW : V_out equals -V_sat StateLow --> StateHigh : V_in crosses V_LT from above StateHigh --> StateHigh : V_in inside hysteresis band and V_out already high StateLow --> StateLow : V_in inside hysteresis band and V_out already low ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Diagram Interpretation Guide:** The state machine above visually represents the **memory effect** of the Schmitt Trigger. The two self-loop transitions (`StateHigh → StateHigh` and `StateLow → StateLow`) inside the hysteresis band demonstrate why noise near the threshold does *not* cause multiple output transitions. ## 4.4 Transfer Characteristic — Hysteresis Loop (Textual Schematic) Since the transfer curve is a continuous voltage-vs-voltage plot, a Mermaid representation is best rendered as a coordinate-mapped block diagram: ```mermaid graph LR A["Point A : V_in less than V_LT, V_out equals +V_sat"] -->|V_in increasing| B["Point B : V_in equals V_UT, V_out snaps to -V_sat"] B -->|V_in still increasing| C["Point C : V_in greater than V_UT, V_out equals -V_sat"] C -->|V_in decreasing| D["Point D : V_in equals V_LT, V_out snaps to +V_sat"] D -->|V_in still decreasing| A ``` This four-point closed loop (A → B → C → D → A) is the **hysteresis loop** of the inverting Schmitt Trigger's transfer characteristic. The horizontal segments A→B and C→D represent the *snap-action transitions*, and the vertical segments B→C and D→A represent the *hold states*. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]** ### Question 1 **Define a Schmitt Trigger. What is the role of positive feedback in its operation?** **Model Answer:** A Schmitt Trigger is a comparator circuit with **hysteresis**, implemented using an op-amp with **positive feedback**. It produces a square wave output for a slowly varying analog input. **Role of Positive Feedback:** 1. It forces the op-amp output into one of two stable states ($+V_{sat}$ or $-V_{sat}$). 2. It creates two distinct switching thresholds ($V_{UT}$ and $V_{LT}$), resulting in a hysteresis band. 3. It provides **regenerative action** — once switching begins, the feedback *accelerates* the transition, producing sharp, snap-action edges. 4. It ensures **noise immunity** by preventing multiple output transitions due to input noise near the threshold. (3 marks — *1 mark definition, 2 marks for role*) ### Question 2 **Define Hysteresis Voltage. How is it related to the noise immunity of a Schmitt Trigger?** **Model Answer:** The **Hysteresis Voltage ($V_H$)** is defined as the difference between the upper and lower threshold voltages: $$ V_H = V_{UT} - V_{LT} $$ **Relation to Noise Immunity:** A larger hysteresis voltage means the input signal must traverse a *wider* voltage range to cause an output transition. Any noise or ripple whose peak-to-peak amplitude is **less than $V_H$** will be ignored by the circuit. Thus, **$V_H$ is directly proportional to the noise immunity** of the Schmitt Trigger. (3 marks — *1 mark formula, 2 marks explanation*) --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, ESE Pattern]** ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of an **op-amp based inverting Schmitt Trigger**. Derive the expressions for the **Upper Threshold Voltage** and **Lower Threshold Voltage**. (7 marks) **(b)** An inverting Schmitt Trigger uses resistors $R_1 = 1\text{ k}\Omega$ and $R_2 = 9\text{ k}\Omega$. The op-amp is powered by $\pm 15\text{ V}$ and saturates at $\pm 13.5\text{ V}$. Calculate $V_{UT}$, $V_{LT}$, and the hysteresis voltage. If a $1\text{ kHz}$ triangular wave of peak amplitude $5\text{ V}$ is applied, determine the **duty cycle** of the output square wave. (7 marks) --- ### **Model Solution for Question A:** #### Part (a) Solution — 7 Marks **Circuit Description (3 marks):** The inverting Schmitt Trigger consists of: - An op-amp in **open-loop** configuration (no external feedback capacitor or resistor in the inverting branch). - $V_{in}$ applied to the **inverting terminal** through a series resistor $R_{in}$ (typically $10\text{ k}\Omega$ to limit input current). - A **resistive voltage divider** comprising $R_1$ (from output to non-inverting terminal $V^+$) and $R_2$ (from $V^+$ to ground). - Output $V_{out}$ is taken from the op-amp output terminal. **Operation (2 marks):** When $V_{in}$ is very negative (i.e., $V_{in} < V^+$), the op-amp output saturates to $+V_{sat}$. Conversely, when $V_{in} > V^+$, the output saturates to $-V_{sat}$. **Derivation (2 marks):** The voltage at the non-inverting terminal is given by the voltage divider rule: $$ V^+ = V_{out} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} $$ When $V_{out} = +V_{sat}$: $$ V_{UT} = +V_{sat} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} $$ When $V_{out} = -V_{sat}$: $$ V_{LT} = -V_{sat} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} $$ #### Part (b) Solution — 7 Marks **Step 1: Calculate $\beta$ (1 mark)** $$ \beta = \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} = \frac{1\text{ k}\Omega}{1\text{ k}\Omega + 9\text{ k}\Omega} = 0.1 $$ **Step 2: Calculate $V_{UT}$ (1 mark)** $$ V_{UT} = +V_{sat} \cdot \beta = +13.5 \times 0.1 = +1.35\text{ V} $$ **Step 3: Calculate $V_{LT}$ (1 mark)** $$ V_{LT} = -V_{sat} \cdot \beta = -13.5 \times 0.1 = -1.35\text{ V} $$ **Step 4: Calculate Hysteresis Voltage (1 mark)** $$ V_H = V_{UT} - V_{LT} = 1.35 - (-1.35) = 2.7\text{ V} $$ **Step 5: Calculate Duty Cycle (3 marks)** For a triangular wave of peak amplitude $5\text{ V}$ (i.e., rises from $-5\text{ V}$ to $+5\text{ V}$ and falls back), we need to find the **time duration** the output remains high vs low. Let $T$ be the period of the triangular wave ($T = 1/f = 1/1000 = 1\text{ ms}$). The triangular wave is symmetric. The time for the wave to rise from $-5\text{ V}$ to $+5\text{ V}$ is $T/2 = 0.5\text{ ms}$. The slope of the rising edge is: $$ \text{Slope} = \frac{10\text{ V}}{0.5\text{ ms}} = 20\text{ V/ms} $$ **Time for $V_{in}$ to rise from $V_{LT} = -1.35\text{ V}$ to $V_{UT} = +1.35\text{ V}$:** $$ t_1 = \frac{V_{UT} - V_{LT}}{\text{Slope}} = \frac{2.7\text{ V}}{20\text{ V/ms}} = 0.135\text{ ms} $$ During this $t_1$, the output is **LOW** ($V_{out} = -V_{sat}$). **Time for $V_{in}$ to fall from $V_{UT} = +1.35\text{ V}$ back to $V_{LT} = -1.35\text{ V}$:** By symmetry of the triangular wave, this is also $t_1 = 0.135\text{ ms}$. During this $t_1$, the output is **HIGH** ($V_{out} = +V_{sat}$). **Total Period of Output:** Since the input is symmetric and the thresholds are symmetric: $$ T_{out} = 2 \times t_1 = 2 \times 0.135 = 0.27\text{ ms} $$ Wait — correction. The output is a square wave with period equal to the input triangular wave period (since one full input cycle produces one full output cycle). Let us recompute. The output is **LOW** for the entire duration the input is *above* $V_{UT}$ during the rising phase AND the falling phase. Specifically: - During the **rising** edge of the triangle: $V_{out} = \text{LOW}$ from when $V_{in}$ crosses $V_{UT}$ down to when it crosses $V_{LT}$ during the *falling* edge. - Actually, let us reason carefully: When $V_{in}$ is in the rising portion of the triangle: - $V_{in} < V_{UT}$: $V_{out} = \text{HIGH}$ (until $V_{in}$ just crosses $V_{UT}$) - $V_{in} = V_{UT}$: $V_{out}$ snaps to LOW - $V_{in} > V_{UT}$ (during rising): $V_{out} = \text{LOW}$ When $V_{in}$ is in the falling portion of the triangle: - $V_{in} > V_{LT}$ (and falling): $V_{out} = \text{LOW}$ (continues to be LOW) - $V_{in} = V_{LT}$: $V_{out}$ snaps to HIGH - $V_{in} < V_{LT}$: $V_{out} = \text{HIGH}$ So during one full period of the triangular input: - $V_{out} = \text{HIGH}$ from the moment $V_{in}$ falls below $V_{LT}$ until $V_{in}$ rises back to $V_{UT}$. This is $T - t_1$ in duration? Let us re-examine. Time during one period $T = 1\text{ ms}$: - $V_{out} = \text{LOW}$: from the moment $V_{in}$ crosses $V_{UT}$ (on the rising edge) until the moment $V_{in}$ crosses $V_{LT}$ (on the falling edge). - $V_{out} = \text{HIGH}$: the remaining time. Time $V_{out}$ is LOW = (time $V_{in}$ spends above $V_{UT}$ during rise) + (time $V_{in}$ spends above $V_{LT}$ during fall) By symmetry of the triangle wave around zero: - Time $V_{in}$ is above $V_{UT} = +1.35\text{ V}$ during rise: from $V_{in} = +1.35$ to $V_{in} = +5$. Duration $= \frac{5 - 1.35}{20} = 0.1825\text{ ms}$. - Time $V_{in}$ is above $V_{LT} = -1.35\text{ V}$ during fall: from $V_{in} = +5$ to $V_{in} = -1.35$. Duration $= \frac{5 - (-1.35)}{20} = 0.3175\text{ ms}$. Total time LOW $= 0.1825 + 0.3175 = 0.5\text{ ms}$. Time HIGH $= T - 0.5 = 0.5\text{ ms}$. **Duty Cycle (percentage of time HIGH):** $$ \text{Duty Cycle} = \frac{T_{\text{HIGH}}}{T} \times 100\% = \frac{0.5}{1.0} \times 100\% = 50\% $$ > This makes intuitive sense: **for a symmetric triangular input and symmetric thresholds, the output is a 50% duty cycle square wave**, regardless of the absolute values of the thresholds (as long as the triangle is symmetric about zero). --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **(a)** Explain the operation of a **non-inverting Schmitt Trigger** with a circuit diagram. Derive the expressions for $V_{UT}$ and $V_{LT}$. Compare it with the inverting configuration. (7 marks) **(b)** Design an inverting Schmitt Trigger with $V_{UT} = +2\text{ V}$ and $V_{LT} = -2\text{ V}$, assuming $V_{sat} = \pm 10\text{ V}$. Choose appropriate resistor values. Also calculate the noise margin if the input signal has a peak-to-peak noise of $0.5\text{ V}$. (7 marks) #### **Model Solution for Question B:** #### Part (a) Solution — 7 Marks **Circuit (2 marks):** In a non-inverting Schmitt Trigger, $V_{in}$ is applied to the **non-inverting terminal ($+$)** of the op-amp. The inverting terminal ($-$) is connected to the junction of $R_1$ and $R_2$ (where $R_1$ goes to output, $R_2$ goes to ground). **Derivation of $V^-$ (2 marks):** $$ V^- = V_{out} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} $$ When $V_{out} = +V_{sat}$: $$ V_{UT} = +V_{sat} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} $$ When $V_{out} = -V_{sat}$: $$ V_{LT} = -V_{sat} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} $$ **Comparison Table (3 marks):** | **Feature** | **Inverting Schmitt** | **Non-Inverting Schmitt** | |---|---|---| | Input applied to | Inverting ($-$) terminal | Non-Inverting ($+$) terminal | | Feedback to | Non-inverting ($+$) terminal | Inverting ($-$) terminal | | Output transition direction | Output LOW when $V_{in}$ HIGH | Output HIGH when $V_{in}$ HIGH | | Threshold formula | Identical | Identical | | Use case | Square wave generation | Zero-crossing detector with hysteresis | #### Part (b) Solution — 7 Marks **Step 1: Calculate feedback factor (2 marks)** From the given data: $V_{UT} = +2\text{ V}$, $V_{sat} = +10\text{ V}$. $$ \beta = \frac{V_{UT}}{V_{sat}} = \frac{2}{10} = 0.2 $$ **Step 2: Choose $R_2$ and calculate $R_1$ (2 marks)** Choose $R_2 = 10\text{ k}\Omega$ (standard value). $$ \beta = \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} = 0.2 $$ $$ R_1 = 0.2 \cdot (R_1 + 10\text{ k}\Omega) \Rightarrow 0.8 R_1 = 2\text{ k}\Omega \Rightarrow R_1 = 2.5\text{ k}\Omega $$ (Use standard $2.4\text{ k}\Omega$ or $2.7\text{ k}\Omega$ from E12 series.) **Step 3: Calculate Hysteresis Voltage (1 mark)** $$ V_H = V_{UT} - V_{LT} = 2 - (-2) = 4\text{ V} $$ **Step 4: Calculate Noise Margin (2 marks)** Noise margin is the *unused* hysteresis available beyond the noise: $$ V_{\text{noise margin}} = V_H - V_{\text{noise, peak-to-peak}} = 4 - 0.5 = 3.5\text{ V} $$ Since $V_{\text{noise margin}} > 0$, the design is **robust against the given noise**. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Resistor Labeling Confusion:** The most common mistake (≈30% of scripts) is mixing up the convention. If the question gives the circuit with $R_1$ from output to ground and $R_2$ to $V^+$, the formula inverts. **Always redraw the circuit and label from your own voltage divider analysis.** > 2. **Sign of $V_{LT}$:** Students frequently write $V_{LT} = +\beta V_{sat}$ instead of $-\beta V_{sat}$. The negative sign is critical. > 3. **Threshold vs. Saturation:** $V_{UT}$ is **always less than** $V_{sat}$. If you obtain $V_{UT} > V_{sat}$, your $\beta > 1$, which is **physically impossible** for a passive divider — recheck your resistor values. > 4. **Duty Cycle Logic:** For a symmetric triangular input, the duty cycle is **always 50%** when thresholds are symmetric about zero. Do not over-complicate this. > 5. **Forgetting Units:** $V_H$ is in Volts, $\beta$ is dimensionless. Always carry and write units explicitly — KTU examiners award a half-mark for unit consistency in long answers. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Schmitt Trigger (Module 1)** - ⭐ **Schmitt Trigger = Comparator + Positive Feedback + Hysteresis** — the holy trinity definition. - ⭐ The **hysteresis band** $V_H$ is the **key design parameter** — it directly controls noise immunity. - ⭐ For an op-amp based Schmitt, the formula $V_{UT} = +\beta V_{sat}$ and $V_{LT} = -\beta V_{sat}$ applies to **both** inverting and non-inverting variants. - ⭐ The output is a **square wave** whose frequency is determined by the input signal's *slope* and the threshold values — not by the Schmitt circuit itself. - ⭐ The **positive feedback fraction $\beta = \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$** must always be **less than 1** (physical constraint). - ⭐ For regeneration (snap action), the **loop gain $A\beta > 1$** — for an ideal op-amp, $A \to \infty$, so this is automatically satisfied. - ⭐ **Inverting Schmitt:** Output switches to LOW when $V_{in}$ crosses $V_{UT}$ (rising), and to HIGH when $V_{in}$ crosses $V_{LT}$ (falling). - ⭐ **Non-Inverting Schmitt:** Output switches to HIGH when $V_{in}$ crosses $V_{UT}$ (rising), and to LOW when $V_{in}$ crosses $V_{LT}$ (falling). - ⭐ **Two stable states:** The output can *never* settle at any voltage between $-V_{sat}$ and $+V_{sat}$ in the steady state. - ⭐ **Memory effect:** Once switched, the output *holds* its state until the input crosses the *opposite* threshold — this is the source of hysteresis. - ⭐ **Real-world ICs to remember:** Op-Amp based = **IC 741**; Transistor/CMOS based = **74LS14, 74HC14, CD40106**. - ⭐ **Applications to memorize:** Square wave generator, sine-to-square converter, relaxation oscillator front-end, switch debouncer, zero-crossing detector, line receiver, SMPS over-voltage protection. - ⭐ **Common KTU exam trap:** If the question asks "draw the transfer characteristic" — always draw the **closed hysteresis loop**, not a single line. Marks are specifically awarded for showing the loop direction (arrows).

topics.title: BJT Amplifiers - RC Coupled Amplifier - Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET302 - ELECTRONIC DEVICES AND CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** BJT Amplifiers - RC Coupled Amplifier - Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **RC Coupled Amplifier** is a multi-stage small-signal voltage amplifier in which the output of one Common Emitter (CE) BJT amplifier stage is connected to the input of the next stage through a **resistor-capacitor (RC) coupling network**. The coupling capacitor blocks the DC bias of one stage from interfering with the DC bias of the next stage, while passing the time-varying AC signal from stage to stage. The word "design" refers to the analytical procedure of selecting component values ($R_C$, $R_E$, $R_1$, $R_2$, $C_{in}$, $C_C$, $C_E$) such that the amplifier meets a prescribed **mid-band voltage gain ($A_v$)**, **input impedance ($Z_{in}$)**, **output impedance ($Z_{out}$)**, and a desired **lower cutoff frequency ($f_L$)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The "RC Coupled Amplifier – Design" topic in Module 2 explicitly expects the student to derive expressions for **voltage gain**, **input resistance**, and **output resistance** of a single CE stage, and then apply the **lower cutoff frequency** design equations to size the coupling and bypass capacitors.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a chain of buckets (BJT stages) passing water (AC signal) down a line. Each bucket has its own water level (DC operating point). If we directly connect the buckets, the water from one will spill into the next, ruining its level. So we install a **small pipe with a one-way valve** (the coupling capacitor $C_C$) between buckets. This valve lets water flow in only one direction (AC) but stops the water from pooling backward (DC). Around each bucket we place a **drainpipe** (the emitter resistor $R_E$ with bypass capacitor $C_E$) to control how fast the bucket fills/empties — this controls the **gain** and the **low-frequency response**.

- **Coupling Capacitor $C_C$:** Sets the low-frequency cutoff $f_L$ by forming an RC high-pass filter with the input resistance of the next stage.
- **Emitter Bypass Capacitor $C_E$:** Sets the low-frequency cutoff by forming a high-pass filter with $R_E$.
- **Standard Design Metric:** A well-designed RC coupled amplifier has a flat mid-band response, and $f_L$ is typically chosen in the range of **20 Hz to 200 Hz** for audio applications.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants Used:**
> - Thermal voltage $V_T \approx \mathbf{26\ mV}$ at room temperature ($T = 300\ K$).
> - Boltzmann's constant $k = \mathbf{1.38 \times 10^{-23}\ J/K}$.
> - Electronic charge $q = \mathbf{1.6 \times 10^{-19}\ C}$.

---

### 1.3 Why "RC Coupled"?

The name itself is the circuit topology:
- **R** = Collector resistor $R_C$ (and bias resistors $R_1$, $R_2$).
- **C** = Coupling capacitor $C_C$, input capacitor $C_{in}$, emitter bypass capacitor $C_E$.
- The signal is **coupled** (transferred) from one stage to the next through a capacitor.

Because capacitors block DC and pass AC, each stage can have its **own independent DC bias**, while the AC signal cascades freely.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle (Stage-by-Stage Logic)

The CE amplifier stage performs three simultaneous jobs:
1. **DC Biasing:** Sets the quiescent operating point $(I_{CQ}, V_{CEQ})$ via the voltage divider $R_1$, $R_2$ and emitter resistor $R_E$.
2. **Small-Signal Amplification:** The transistor's transconductance $g_m$ converts the small base-emitter voltage $v_{be}$ into a proportional collector current $i_c = g_m v_{be}$.
3. **Load Conversion:** The collector resistor $R_C$ converts this current into an output voltage $v_c = -i_c R_C$, giving voltage gain.

The **mid-band gain** is computed assuming all coupling/bypass capacitors are **short circuits** at the signal frequency.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Small-Signal Analysis (CE Stage with $R_E$ unbypassed and bypassed)

**Step 1 — Transconductance:** Depends only on the DC collector current.

$$g_m = \dfrac{I_{CQ}}{V_T}$$

**Step 2 — Input Resistance of Transistor (base-emitter loop):**

$$r_\pi = \dfrac{\beta}{g_m} = \dfrac{\beta V_T}{I_{CQ}}$$

**Step 3 — Voltage Gain (with $R_E$ fully bypassed by $C_E$):**

$$A_v = -g_m (R_C \parallel R_L) = -\dfrac{I_{CQ}}{V_T}(R_C \parallel R_L)$$

**Step 4 — Voltage Gain (with $R_E$ unbypassed):**

$$A_v = -\dfrac{\beta (R_C \parallel R_L)}{r_\pi + (\beta + 1)R_E} \approx -\dfrac{g_m (R_C \parallel R_L)}{1 + g_m R_E}$$

**Step 5 — Input Resistance of the Stage:**

$$R_{in(stage)} = R_1 \parallel R_2 \parallel r_\pi$$

If $R_E$ is unbypassed:

$$R_{in(stage)} = R_1 \parallel R_2 \parallel \left[r_\pi + (\beta + 1)R_E\right]$$

**Step 6 — Output Resistance of the Stage:**

$$R_{out(stage)} \approx R_C \quad (\text{neglecting } r_o)$$

### 2.3 Lower Cutoff Frequency Design

Each coupling/bypass capacitor, combined with the surrounding resistance, forms a **first-order high-pass filter**. The lower cutoff frequency is the frequency at which the gain drops to $1/\sqrt{2}$ (i.e., $-3\ dB$).

**For input coupling capacitor $C_{in}$:**

$$f_{L1} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi (R_S + R_{in(stage)}) C_{in}}$$

**For inter-stage coupling capacitor $C_C$:**

$$f_{L2} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi (R_{out(prior)} + R_{in(next)}) C_C}$$

**For emitter bypass capacitor $C_E$:**

$$f_{LE} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \left(R_E \parallel \dfrac{r_\pi + R_{th}}{\beta + 1}\right) C_E}$$

where $R_{th} = R_S \parallel R_1 \parallel R_2$ is the Thevenin resistance seen from the base.

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Rule (KTU Standard):** For a multi-stage amplifier, the overall lower cutoff is given by:
> $$f_L = \sqrt{f_{L1}^2 + f_{L2}^2 + f_{L3}^2 + \dots}$$
> If one capacitor dominates (highest $f_{Li}$), the **dominant pole approximation** gives $f_L \approx f_{L,\max}$.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Sheet)

| **Parameter** | **Formula** | **Condition / Unit** |
|---|---|---|
| Transconductance | $g_m = I_{CQ} / V_T$ | $V_T = 26\ mV$ at 300 K; $g_m$ in Siemens (S) |
| Base-emitter resistance | $r_\pi = \beta / g_m = \beta V_T / I_{CQ}$ | Ohms ($\Omega$) |
| Mid-band voltage gain (bypassed) | $A_v = -g_m (R_C \parallel R_L)$ | Dimensionless |
| Mid-band voltage gain (unbypassed) | $A_v = -g_m(R_C \parallel R_L)/(1 + g_m R_E)$ | Dimensionless |
| Input resistance (bypassed) | $R_{in} = R_1 \parallel R_2 \parallel r_\pi$ | Ohms ($\Omega$) |
| Output resistance | $R_{out} \approx R_C$ | Ohms ($\Omega$) |
| Input capacitor cutoff | $f_{L,in} = 1 / [2\pi (R_S + R_{in}) C_{in}]$ | Hz |
| Coupling capacitor cutoff | $f_{L,C} = 1 / [2\pi (R_{out,prev} + R_{in,next}) C_C]$ | Hz |
| Emitter capacitor cutoff | $f_{L,E} = 1 / [2\pi R_E' C_E]$ | Hz, where $R_E' = R_E \parallel (r_\pi + R_{th})/(\beta+1)$ |
| Overall lower cutoff (dominant) | $f_L \approx \max(f_{L,in}, f_{L,C}, f_{L,E})$ | Hz |
| dB Gain | $A_v(dB) = 20 \log_{10} \vert A_v \vert$ | dB |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** RC coupled amplifiers are the **backbone of audio amplifiers**, public-address systems, radio receivers, and pre-amplifiers in instrumentation. The design procedure taught here is the same procedure used in IC op-amp internal stages and in audio codec ICs.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Voltage Gain of a CE Stage with Bypassed $R_E$

Starting from the small-signal hybrid-$\pi$ equivalent circuit of the BJT:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_{be} &= i_b \cdot r_\pi \\
i_c &= g_m \cdot v_{be} = g_m \cdot r_\pi \cdot i_b = \beta \cdot i_b \\
v_o &= -i_c \cdot (R_C \parallel R_L) \\
v_{in} &= i_b \cdot r_\pi
\end{aligned}
$$

Dividing $v_o$ by $v_{in}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_v &= \dfrac{v_o}{v_{in}} = \dfrac{-i_c (R_C \parallel R_L)}{i_b \cdot r_\pi} = \dfrac{-\beta \cdot i_b (R_C \parallel R_L)}{i_b \cdot r_\pi} = \dfrac{-\beta (R_C \parallel R_L)}{r_\pi}
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting $\beta = g_m \cdot r_\pi$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_v &= \dfrac{-g_m \cdot r_\pi \cdot (R_C \parallel R_L)}{r_\pi} = -g_m (R_C \parallel R_L)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final boxed result:**

$$\boxed{A_v = -g_m (R_C \parallel R_L) = -\dfrac{I_{CQ}}{V_T}(R_C \parallel R_L)}$$

The negative sign indicates a **180° phase inversion** between input and output — a defining feature of the CE configuration.

### 3.2 Derivation: Lower Cutoff Frequency Due to the Emitter Bypass Capacitor $C_E$

When $C_E$ is present, it forms a low-impedance short across $R_E$ at high frequencies. As frequency decreases, $C_E$'s impedance $\frac{1}{j\omega C_E}$ increases, making $R_E$ progressively unbypassed, which **reduces the gain**.

**Step 1 — Effective emitter impedance:**
$$
Z_E = R_E \parallel \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_E} = \dfrac{R_E}{1 + j\omega R_E C_E}
$$

**Step 2 — Gain as a function of frequency (with partial bypass):**
$$
A_v(\omega) = -\dfrac{g_m (R_C \parallel R_L)}{1 + g_m Z_E}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $Z_E$:**
$$
A_v(\omega) = -\dfrac{g_m (R_C \parallel R_L)}{1 + \dfrac{g_m R_E}{1 + j\omega R_E C_E}}
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply numerator and denominator by $(1 + j\omega R_E C_E)$:**
$$
A_v(\omega) = -\dfrac{g_m (R_C \parallel R_L)(1 + j\omega R_E C_E)}{1 + g_m R_E + j\omega R_E C_E}
$$

**Step 5 — Mid-band gain (when $\omega \to \infty$, capacitor shorts $R_E$):**
$$
A_{v,mid} = -g_m (R_C \parallel R_L)
$$

**Step 6 — Normalized gain:**
$$
\dfrac{A_v(\omega)}{A_{v,mid}} = \dfrac{1 + j\omega R_E C_E}{1 + g_m R_E + j\omega R_E C_E} = \dfrac{1 + j\omega R_E C_E}{(1 + g_m R_E)(1 + j\omega \tau_E C_E)}
$$

**Step 7 — At very low frequency ($\omega \to 0$), the gain reduces by a factor of $(1 + g_m R_E)$:**
$$
\dfrac{A_v(0)}{A_{v,mid}} = \dfrac{1}{1 + g_m R_E}
$$

**Step 8 — The $-3\ dB$ cutoff frequency** occurs when the imaginary part of the denominator equals the real part. Solving:

$$
\omega_{LE} = \dfrac{1 + g_m R_E}{R_E C_E}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{f_{LE} = \dfrac{1 + g_m R_E}{2\pi R_E C_E} \approx \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_E' C_E}}
$$

where the equivalent emitter resistance is:

$$
R_E' = R_E \parallel \dfrac{r_\pi + R_{th}}{\beta + 1}
$$

### 3.3 Exhaustive Worked Design Example

**Design Problem:** Design a single-stage RC coupled CE amplifier using a silicon BJT ($\beta = 100$, $V_{BE} = 0.7\ V$) with:
- Supply voltage $V_{CC} = 12\ V$
- Quiescent collector current $I_{CQ} = 2\ mA$
- Quiescent collector-emitter voltage $V_{CEQ} = 6\ V$
- Load resistance $R_L = 4.7\ k\Omega$
- Lower cutoff frequency $f_L = 50\ Hz$
- Source resistance $R_S = 600\ \Omega$

**Step 1 — Compute $g_m$:**
$$
g_m = \dfrac{I_{CQ}}{V_T} = \dfrac{2 \times 10^{-3}}{26 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0769\ S \approx 76.92\ mS
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $r_\pi$:**
$$
r_\pi = \dfrac{\beta}{g_m} = \dfrac{100}{0.0769} = 1300\ \Omega \approx 1.3\ k\Omega
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $R_C$ from DC bias equation:**

Using KVL on the output loop:
$$
V_{CC} = I_{CQ} R_C + V_{CEQ} + I_{EQ} R_E
$$

Assume $I_{EQ} \approx I_{CQ} = 2\ mA$ and design with $R_E = R_C / 10$ for stability. Let $R_E = 470\ \Omega$:
$$
V_{CEQ} = V_{CC} - I_{CQ}(R_C + R_E)
$$
$$
6 = 12 - (2 \times 10^{-3})(R_C + 470)
$$
$$
R_C + 470 = \dfrac{12 - 6}{2 \times 10^{-3}} = 3000\ \Omega
$$
$$
R_C = 3000 - 470 = 2530\ \Omega \approx \mathbf{2.4\ k\Omega} \text{ (standard value)}
$$

**Step 4 — Voltage gain:**
$$
A_v = -g_m (R_C \parallel R_L) = -0.0769 \times (2400 \parallel 4700)
$$
$$
R_C \parallel R_L = \dfrac{2400 \times 4700}{2400 + 4700} = \dfrac{11{,}280{,}000}{7100} = 1588.7\ \Omega
$$
$$
A_v = -0.0769 \times 1588.7 = \mathbf{-122.2} \quad (\text{i.e., } 41.7\ dB)
$$

**Step 5 — Bias resistors $R_1$ and $R_2$:**

Choose $R_{th} = R_1 \parallel R_2 = 10 \cdot r_\pi = 13\ k\Omega$ for bias stability.

Base voltage:
$$
V_B = V_{BE} + I_{EQ} R_E = 0.7 + (2 \times 10^{-3})(470) = 0.7 + 0.94 = 1.64\ V
$$

Using voltage divider:
$$
V_B = V_{CC} \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}
$$

Choose $R_2 = 6.8\ k\Omega$ (standard). Then:
$$
1.64 = 12 \cdot \dfrac{6800}{R_1 + 6800}
$$
$$
R_1 + 6800 = \dfrac{12 \times 6800}{1.64} = 49{,}756\ \Omega
$$
$$
R_1 = 49{,}756 - 6800 = 42{,}956\ \Omega \approx \mathbf{43\ k\Omega}
$$

Verify: $R_1 \parallel R_2 = (43{,}000 \times 6800)/(43{,}000 + 6800) = 292{,}400{,}000 / 49{,}800 = 5872\ \Omega \approx 5.87\ k\Omega$

> Note: This is less than $10 \cdot r_\pi$ but acceptable; for tighter design, scale $R_2$ upward.

**Step 6 — Input Resistance of the Stage:**
$$
R_{in(stage)} = R_1 \parallel R_2 \parallel r_\pi = 5872 \parallel 1300 = \mathbf{1066\ \Omega}
$$

**Step 7 — Coupling Capacitor $C_{in}$ for $f_{L1} = 50\ Hz$:**
$$
C_{in} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi (R_S + R_{in(stage)}) f_{L1}} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi (600 + 1066)(50)}
$$
$$
C_{in} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \times 1666 \times 50} = \dfrac{1}{523{,}392} = 1.91\ \mu F \approx \mathbf{2.2\ \mu F}
$$

**Step 8 — Output Coupling Capacitor $C_C$ for $f_{L2} = 50\ Hz$:**
$$
R_{out(stage)} = R_C = 2400\ \Omega
$$
Assuming next stage has the same $R_{in} = 1066\ \Omega$:
$$
C_C = \dfrac{1}{2\pi (2400 + 1066)(50)} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \times 3466 \times 50} = \dfrac{1}{1{,}088{,}800} = 0.918\ \mu F \approx \mathbf{1\ \mu F}
$$

**Step 9 — Emitter Bypass Capacitor $C_E$ for $f_{LE} = 50\ Hz$:**

First compute $R_E'$:
$$
R_{th} = R_S \parallel R_1 \parallel R_2 = 600 \parallel 5872 = 544.5\ \Omega
$$
$$
R_E' = 470 \parallel \dfrac{1300 + 544.5}{101} = 470 \parallel \dfrac{1844.5}{101} = 470 \parallel 18.26
$$
$$
R_E' = \dfrac{470 \times 18.26}{470 + 18.26} = \dfrac{8582}{488.26} = 17.58\ \Omega
$$
$$
C_E = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_E' f_L} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \times 17.58 \times 50} = \dfrac{1}{5522} = 181\ \mu F \approx \mathbf{220\ \mu F}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Design Values Summary:**
> $R_C = 2.4\ k\Omega$, $R_E = 470\ \Omega$, $R_1 = 43\ k\Omega$, $R_2 = 6.8\ k\Omega$, $C_{in} = 2.2\ \mu F$, $C_C = 1\ \mu F$, $C_E = 220\ \mu F$, $A_v \approx -122$ (41.7 dB), $R_{in} \approx 1.07\ k\Omega$, $R_{out} \approx 2.4\ k\Omega$, $f_L \approx 50\ Hz$.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Two-Stage RC Coupled Amplifier)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA[Vin AC Source] --> csaA{Rs Source Resistance}
    csaA --> capInA[/Cin Coupling Capacitor/]
    capInA --> biasNetA[/R1 R2 Voltage Divider Bias/]
    biasNetA --> bjtA[/BJT Q1 Common Emitter Stage 1/]
    bjtA --> rcA[/RC Collector Resistor/]
    bjtA --> reBranchA[/RE Emitter Resistor with CE Bypass/]
    rcA --> capCcA[/CC Interstage Coupling Capacitor/]
    capCcA --> biasNetB[/R3 R4 Voltage Divider Bias Stage 2/]
    biasNetB --> bjtB[/BJT Q2 Common Emitter Stage 2/]
    bjtB --> rcB[/RC2 Collector Resistor Stage 2/]
    bjtB --> reBranchB[/RE2 Emitter Resistor with CE2 Bypass/]
    rcB --> capOutA[/Cout Output Coupling Capacitor/]
    capOutA --> loadA[RL Load Resistor]
    loadA --> voutA[Vout AC Output]

    classDef active fill:#FFD580,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef passive fill:#E0E0FF,stroke:#4040A0,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef source fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class bjtA,bjtB active
    class srcA,csaA,capInA,capOutA,capCcA,loadA,voutA source
    class biasNetA,biasNetB,rcA,rcB,reBranchA,reBranchB passive
```

### 4.2 Functional Flow of Signal Through Each Capacitor (Cutoff Frequency Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    sigStart[Input Signal Vi] --> sA[Source Rs]
    sA --> hp1{High Pass Filter 1 Cin with Rs plus Rin}
    hp1 --> biasP[DC Bias Point Set by R1 R2 RE]
    biasP --> ceAmp[CE Transistor Amplification gm vbe]
    ceAmp --> hp2{High Pass Filter 2 CC with Rout plus Rin Next}
    hp2 --> ceAmp2[CE Transistor Amplification Stage 2]
    ceAmp2 --> hp3{High Pass Filter 3 CE with RE equivalent}
    hp3 --> outTerm[Output Vout across RL]

    hp1 -. fL1 .-> fcalc[/Compute fL1 = 1 over 2pi Rs plus Rin times Cin/]
    hp2 -. fL2 .-> fcalc2[/Compute fL2 = 1 over 2pi Rout plus RinNext times CC/]
    hp3 -. fL3 .-> fcalc3[/Compute fL3 = 1 over 2pi REp times CE/]
    fcalc --> overall[/Overall fL = dominant pole approximation/]
    fcalc2 --> overall
    fcalc3 --> overall

    classDef sig fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef amp fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef cap fill:#B3E5FC,stroke:#0277BD,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef res fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:1px,color:#000

    class sigStart,sA,outTerm sig
    class ceAmp,ceAmp2 amp
    class hp1,hp2,hp3,fcalc,fcalc2,fcalc3,overall cap
    class biasP res
```

### 4.3 Signal Path Interpretation

- **Stage 1 (Q1):** Performs the first voltage amplification with gain $A_{v1} \approx -g_{m1}(R_C \parallel R_{in2})$.
- **Inter-stage Coupling ($C_C$):** Blocks DC bias of Stage 1 from disturbing Stage 2's bias; passes AC forward.
- **Stage 2 (Q2):** Performs second amplification with gain $A_{v2} \approx -g_{m2}(R_C \parallel R_L)$.
- **Overall Gain (cascade):** $A_{v,total} = A_{v1} \cdot A_{v2}$ (in linear scale) or $A_{v,total}(dB) = A_{v1}(dB) + A_{v2}(dB)$.
