## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
What is an RC coupled amplifier? Why is it the most widely used configuration in audio amplifiers?

**Model Answer:**
An RC coupled amplifier is a multi-stage amplifier in which the output of one stage is connected to the input of the next through a coupling capacitor and the bias networks are resistor-based. The coupling capacitor blocks the DC operating point of one stage from interfering with the next stage while freely passing the AC signal.

It is widely used because:
1. It provides excellent **frequency response in the audio range (20 Hz – 20 kHz)**.
2. It offers **high gain per stage** (≈ 40–50 dB).
3. It is **cheap and compact**, using only resistors and capacitors (no expensive transformers or inductors).
4. It provides **DC isolation between stages**, allowing independent biasing.

(3 marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
List the **three high-pass RC networks** in a single-stage RC coupled CE amplifier and state the formula for each cutoff frequency.

**Model Answer:**
1. **Input coupling capacitor $C_{in}$** with $R_S + R_{in(stage)}$:
   $f_{L1} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi (R_S + R_{in(stage)}) C_{in}}$

2. **Inter-stage (output) coupling capacitor $C_C$** with $R_{out} + R_{in(next)}$:
   $f_{L2} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi (R_{out} + R_{in(next)}) C_C}$

3. **Emitter bypass capacitor $C_E$** with equivalent emitter resistance $R_E'$:
   $f_{LE} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_E' C_E}$

The overall lower cutoff is dominated by the **highest** of these three frequencies: $f_L = \max(f_{L1}, f_{L2}, f_{LE})$.

(3 marks)

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**(a)** Derive the expression for the **voltage gain** of a single-stage CE amplifier with a **fully bypassed emitter resistor**. State the assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a CE amplifier, the following parameters are given: $V_{CC} = 15\ V$, $I_{CQ} = 1\ mA$, $V_{CEQ} = 7.5\ V$, $\beta = 120$, $R_L = 4.7\ k\Omega$. Calculate $g_m$, $r_\pi$, $R_C$, and the mid-band voltage gain $A_v$. **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution to Q.A(a):

**Step 1 — Assumptions:**
- Coupling and bypass capacitors are short circuits at the signal (mid-band) frequency.
- Transistor output resistance $r_o$ is neglected.
- Small-signal, linear operation (BJT in active region).

**Step 2 — Hybrid-π model equations:**
$$
i_b = \dfrac{v_{be}}{r_\pi}, \quad i_c = g_m v_{be} = \beta i_b
$$

**Step 3 — Output voltage:**
$$
v_o = -i_c \cdot (R_C \parallel R_L)
$$

**Step 4 — Input voltage:**
$$
v_{in} = v_{be} = i_b r_\pi
$$

**Step 5 — Voltage Gain:**
$$
A_v = \dfrac{v_o}{v_{in}} = \dfrac{-i_c (R_C \parallel R_L)}{i_b r_\pi} = \dfrac{-\beta (R_C \parallel R_L)}{r_\pi} = -g_m (R_C \parallel R_L)
$$

$$\boxed{A_v = -\dfrac{I_{CQ}}{V_T}(R_C \parallel R_L)}$$

The negative sign indicates 180° phase reversal. **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution to Q.A(b):

**Given:** $V_{CC} = 15\ V$, $I_{CQ} = 1\ mA$, $V_{CEQ} = 7.5\ V$, $\beta = 120$, $R_L = 4.7\ k\Omega$.

**Step 1 — Transconductance:**
$$
g_m = \dfrac{I_{CQ}}{V_T} = \dfrac{1 \times 10^{-3}}{26 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.03846\ S = 38.46\ mS
$$

**Step 2 — Base-emitter resistance:**
$$
r_\pi = \dfrac{\beta}{g_m} = \dfrac{120}{0.03846} = 3120\ \Omega = 3.12\ k\Omega
$$

**Step 3 — DC equation for $R_C$ (assume $R_E$ small/negligible for simplicity, or that $V_{CEQ}$ already accounts for it):**
$$
V_{CC} = I_{CQ} R_C + V_{CEQ}
$$
$$
R_C = \dfrac{V_{CC} - V_{CEQ}}{I_{CQ}} = \dfrac{15 - 7.5}{1 \times 10^{-3}} = 7500\ \Omega = 7.5\ k\Omega
$$

**Step 4 — Voltage Gain:**
$$
R_C \parallel R_L = \dfrac{7500 \times 4700}{7500 + 4700} = \dfrac{35{,}250{,}000}{12{,}200} = 2889\ \Omega
$$
$$
A_v = -g_m (R_C \parallel R_L) = -0.03846 \times 2889 = -111.1
$$

$$\boxed{g_m = 38.46\ mS,\ r_\pi = 3.12\ k\Omega,\ R_C = 7.5\ k\Omega,\ A_v = -111.1\ \ (\approx 40.9\ dB)}$$

**(7 marks)**

---

#### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**(a)** With the help of a **small-signal equivalent circuit**, derive the expression for the **lower cutoff frequency** due to the **emitter bypass capacitor** $C_E$ in a CE amplifier. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design the **values of $C_{in}$, $C_C$, and $C_E$** for an RC coupled amplifier with the following specifications: source resistance $R_S = 1\ k\Omega$, input resistance of stage $R_{in} = 2\ k\Omega$, output resistance $R_{out} = 3\ k\Omega$, equivalent emitter resistance $R_E' = 25\ \Omega$, desired lower cutoff $f_L = 100\ Hz$. **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution to Q.B(a):

**Step 1 — Equivalent impedance of the emitter branch:**
$$
Z_E = R_E \parallel \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_E} = \dfrac{R_E}{1 + j\omega R_E C_E}
$$

**Step 2 — Voltage gain with partially bypassed $R_E$:**
$$
A_v(\omega) = -\dfrac{g_m (R_C \parallel R_L)}{1 + g_m Z_E} = -\dfrac{g_m (R_C \parallel R_L)(1 + j\omega R_E C_E)}{1 + g_m R_E + j\omega R_E C_E}
$$

**Step 3 — Mid-band gain ($\omega \to \infty$):**
$$
A_{v,mid} = -g_m (R_C \parallel R_L)
$$

**Step 4 — At the cutoff frequency $\omega_{LE}$, the magnitude is $\frac{A_{v,mid}}{\sqrt{2}}$.** Setting the denominator magnitude equal to $\sqrt{2}$ times its real part:

$$
\sqrt{(1 + g_m R_E)^2 + (\omega_{LE} R_E C_E)^2} = \sqrt{2}(1 + g_m R_E)
$$

**Step 5 — Solving for $\omega_{LE}$:**
$$
(\omega_{LE} R_E C_E)^2 = (1 + g_m R_E)^2
$$
$$
\omega_{LE} = \dfrac{1 + g_m R_E}{R_E C_E}
$$

$$
\boxed{f_{LE} = \dfrac{1 + g_m R_E}{2\pi R_E C_E} \approx \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_E' C_E}}
$$

where $R_E' = R_E \parallel \dfrac{r_\pi + R_{th}}{\beta + 1}$. **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution to Q.B(b):

**Given:** $R_S = 1\ k\Omega$, $R_{in} = 2\ k\Omega$, $R_{out} = 3\ k\Omega$, $R_E' = 25\ \Omega$, $f_L = 100\ Hz$.

**Step 1 — Input coupling capacitor $C_{in}$:**
$$
C_{in} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi (R_S + R_{in}) f_L} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi (1000 + 2000)(100)} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \times 3000 \times 100}
$$
$$
C_{in} = \dfrac{1}{1{,}884{,}956} = 0.5305\ \mu F \approx \mathbf{0.56\ \mu F} \text{ (standard)}
$$

**Step 2 — Inter-stage coupling capacitor $C_C$ (assume next stage input = $R_{in} = 2\ k\Omega$):**
$$
C_C = \dfrac{1}{2\pi (R_{out} + R_{in,next}) f_L} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi (3000 + 2000)(100)} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \times 5000 \times 100}
$$
$$
C_C = \dfrac{1}{3{,}141{,}593} = 0.318\ \mu F \approx \mathbf{0.33\ \mu F} \text{ (standard)}
$$

**Step 3 — Emitter bypass capacitor $C_E$:**
$$
C_E = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_E' f_L} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \times 25 \times 100} = \dfrac{1}{15{,}708} = 63.66\ \mu F \approx \mathbf{68\ \mu F} \text{ (standard)}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Marks lost** if students forget to **sum source and input resistance** in $C_{in}$ formula (a very common mistake is using only $R_S$ or only $R_{in}$).
> 2. **Marks lost** if students **swap $C_{in}$ and $C_C$ equations** (input vs. inter-stage).
> 3. **Marks lost** if $V_T = 26\ mV$ is **not used at 300 K** or if $g_m$ is **incorrectly calculated** as $1/r_\pi$ instead of $I_C/V_T$.
> 4. **Marks lost** for forgetting the **dominant-pole logic**: the largest of the three $f_L$ values determines the overall $f_L$, not the sum or average.
> 5. **Marks lost** if the **negative sign of $A_v$** is dropped (the 180° phase inversion of CE stage is examinable).
> 6. **Marks lost** if the **standard capacitor values** (using E12 series: 0.33, 0.47, 1.0, 2.2, 4.7, 10, 22, 47, 100, 220, 470 μF) are not chosen properly.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ RC Coupled Amplifier = **CE stage** + **resistor biasing** + **capacitor coupling**. The "RC" refers to the Resistor-Capacitor coupling network.
- ✅ Mid-band voltage gain (bypassed $R_E$): $\mathbf{A_v = -g_m(R_C \parallel R_L)}$, where $\mathbf{g_m = I_{CQ}/V_T}$.
- ✅ Mid-band voltage gain (unbypassed $R_E$): $\mathbf{A_v = -g_m(R_C \parallel R_L)/(1 + g_m R_E)}$.
- ✅ Input resistance: $\mathbf{R_{in} = R_1 \parallel R_2 \parallel r_\pi}$.
- ✅ Output resistance: $\mathbf{R_{out} \approx R_C}$.
- ✅ **Three high-pass filters** set the lower cutoff frequency: input cap $C_{in}$, coupling cap $C_C$, emitter bypass cap $C_E$.
- ✅ Overall $f_L$ is governed by the **dominant pole** (highest $f_{Li}$).
- ✅ To minimize $f_L$ (extend low-frequency response), use **larger capacitors**.
- ✅ Emitter bypass capacitor $C_E$ is typically the **largest** (often > 100 μF) because $R_E'$ is small.
- ✅ $V_T = 26\ mV$ at room temperature; $g_m$ scales linearly with $I_{CQ}$.
- ✅ Higher $I_{CQ}$ → higher $g_m$ → higher gain, **but** lower input resistance and higher power consumption (trade-off).
- ✅ The CE stage is **inverting** (180° phase shift); a two-stage RC coupled amplifier therefore produces **0°** (or 360°) net phase.
- ✅ Stability rule of thumb: choose $R_{th} = R_1 \parallel R_2 \approx 10 \cdot r_\pi$ for stable bias against $\beta$ variations.
- ✅ Standard design order: **Bias point → $g_m$, $r_\pi$ → $R_C$, $R_E$ → Gain check → $C_{in}$, $C_C$, $C_E$ from $f_L$ spec.**
- ✅ Multi-stage gain: $A_{v,total} = A_{v1} \cdot A_{v2}$ (linear), or sum of dB values.
- ✅ Practical limitation: at very high frequencies, **internal transistor capacitances** ($C_\pi$, $C_\mu$, Miller effect) limit the upper cutoff $f_H$.
- ✅ KTU exam favorite: always expect a **derivation of $A_v$** and a **numerical capacitor sizing** question together as a 14-mark Part B question.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Voltage Gain and Frequency Response
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET302 - ELECTRONIC DEVICES AND CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Voltage Gain and Frequency Response

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Voltage Gain — Formal Definition

In the context of small-signal amplifier analysis (KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 2 of *Electronic Devices and Circuits*), the **voltage gain ($A_v$)** of a single-stage amplifier is formally defined as the ratio of the output alternating voltage to the input alternating voltage, measured under small-signal sinusoidal excitation.

$$A_v = \frac{v_o}{v_i}$$

In decibel (dB) form, which is the standard metric used for Bode-plot representation:

$$A_v(\text{dB}) = 20 \log_{10}\left|\frac{v_o}{v_i}\right| \; \text{dB}$$

For a *cascaded* multi-stage amplifier containing $n$ stages, the total voltage gain expressed in dB is the **algebraic sum** of the individual stage gains:

$$A_{v,\text{total}}(\text{dB}) = A_{v1}(\text{dB}) + A_{v2}(\text{dB}) + \cdots + A_{vn}(\text{dB}) = 20 \log_{10}\left|\prod_{k=1}^{n} A_{vk}\right|$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Definition (CO2, Understand Level)**
> Voltage gain is a *dimensionless transfer function* evaluated under small-signal conditions where the active device (BJT/FET) operates in the **active region / saturation region**, ensuring linear amplification.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Bench Metrics (must memorise)**
> - **Mid-band gain ($A_{v,\text{mid}}$)**: Flat, frequency-independent region. Occurs roughly between **20 Hz and 20 kHz** for audio amplifiers, and may extend to **MHz range** for RF front-end amplifiers.
> - **Lower cutoff frequency ($f_L$)**: Frequency at which gain falls to **$1/\sqrt{2}$** (≈ 0.707) of mid-band value, i.e., –3 dB point.
> - **Upper cutoff frequency ($f_H$)**: Frequency at which gain again drops to –3 dB of mid-band value.
> - **Bandwidth (BW)** = $f_H - f_L$.

## 1.2 Frequency Response — Formal Definition

**Frequency response** is the quantitative description of an amplifier's voltage gain magnitude (and phase) as a function of the input signal frequency, typically visualised as a **Bode magnitude plot** (gain in dB vs. frequency on a log scale).

$$A_v(f) = \vert A_v(f) \vert \, e^{j\phi(f)}$$

The frequency response of any practical amplifier is divided into three distinct regions:

| Region | Frequency Range | Dominant Reactive Elements | Behaviour |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Low-frequency region** | $f < f_L$ | Coupling capacitors $C_{in}$, $C_{out}$ and emitter/source bypass capacitor $C_E$ | Gain **rolls off at +20 dB/decade** per capacitor |
| **Mid-band region** | $f_L \le f \le f_H$ | All capacitors treated as **AC short circuits** | Gain is **flat and maximum** ($A_{v,\text{mid}}$) |
| **High-frequency region** | $f > f_H$ | Internal junction / parasitic capacitances ($C_{be}$, $C_{bc}$, $C_{gs}$, $C_{gd}$, $C_{ds}$) | Gain **rolls off at –20 dB/decade** |

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Stethoscope**
> Imagine a stethoscope used by a doctor. It amplifies sounds in the **mid-frequency range** (human voice and heartbeats ~20 Hz – 2 kHz) very well, but **rejects** extremely low rumbles (building vibrations) and extremely high frequencies (electronic hiss). The stethoscope has a *band-pass* characteristic.
>
> An electronic amplifier behaves **identically**: it passes a *band* of frequencies and attenuates those outside the band. The voltage gain is essentially **constant (flat)** in the band and *rolls off* outside it.

Another intuitive analogy:

> **Analogy — The Water Tap with Filter**
> Think of a water tap with a sand filter. The filter lets a "middle range" of water flow easily (mid-band), but blocks very slow seepage (low frequencies — analogous to coupling capacitor reactance $X_C = 1/2\pi f C$ being very large at low $f$, blocking DC and slow signals) and very rapid bursts (high frequencies — analogous to internal capacitances short-circuiting the high-frequency signal to ground).

## 1.4 Why Frequency Response Matters in Engineering

- **Communication systems**: Each channel requires a specific bandwidth (e.g., AM broadcast = 10 kHz, FM = 200 kHz, Wi-Fi = 20–40 MHz).
- **Audio amplifiers**: Must faithfully reproduce 20 Hz – 20 kHz.
- **Oscilloscope probes & instrumentation**: Wideband flat response is essential.
- **Medical electronics (ECG/EEG)**: Specific low-frequency response needed (0.05 Hz – 150 Hz for ECG).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constants Used in This Module**
> - $1/\sqrt{2} \approx 0.7071$ (magnitude at –3 dB point)
> - $-3 \text{ dB} = 20 \log_{10}(1/\sqrt{2})$
> - $\log_{10} 2 \approx 0.3010$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Three Reactive Regimes — Why Each Capacitor Dominates Where It Does

The single-stage BJT Common-Emitter (CE) amplifier is the canonical example used in KTU Module 2. The equivalent circuit contains three classes of capacitors, each dominant in a different frequency range:

### (A) Low-Frequency Dominant Capacitors
- **Input coupling capacitor** $C_{in}$ (between source and base).
- **Output coupling capacitor** $C_{out}$ (between collector and load).
- **Emitter bypass capacitor** $C_E$ (across $R_E$ to provide AC ground at emitter).

At low $f$, the capacitive reactance $X_C = 1/(2\pi f C)$ becomes **very large**, so the capacitor behaves like an *open circuit*. This:
- Blocks signal transfer from source to amplifier input (via $C_{in}$),
- Blocks signal transfer from collector to load (via $C_{out}$),
- Removes the AC ground at emitter, causing **local degenerative feedback** (via $C_E$).

### (B) Mid-Band Region
- All coupling/bypass capacitors act as **AC short circuits** ($X_C \to 0$).
- All internal junction capacitances are treated as **AC open circuits** (their reactance is still very high at these frequencies).
- Hence the amplifier reduces to a **purely resistive small-signal model** — gain is flat.

### (C) High-Frequency Dominant Capacitors
- **Base–emitter diffusion capacitance** $C_{be}$ (or $C_\pi$),
- **Base–collector depletion capacitance** $C_{bc}$ (or $C_\mu$),
- For FET: $C_{gs}$, $C_{gd}$, $C_{ds}$.

At high $f$, these small capacitances (typically a few pF) develop low reactance, providing **bypass paths** that shunt the signal away from the load, reducing gain.

## 2.2 Miller's Theorem — The High-Frequency Lifeline

When $C_{bc}$ (BJT) or $C_{gd}$ (FET) bridges a high-gain inverting node (collector to base), its effective input-side capacitance is **amplified by the gain**. This is **Miller's Theorem**.

For a general impedance $Z$ connected between input node (voltage $v_i$) and output node (voltage $v_o = K v_i$, where $K$ is the voltage gain from that node to output):

$$Z_{\text{in}} = \frac{Z}{1-K}, \qquad Z_{\text{out}} = \frac{Z}{1-1/K} = \frac{Z \cdot K}{K-1}$$

Applied to $C_{bc}$ in a CE stage with mid-band gain $A_v = -g_m R_C'$ (negative sign ⇒ inverting), the **Miller capacitance** is:

$$C_{M} = C_{bc}(1 + g_m R_C')$$

This single (large) capacitance combines with the source resistance $R_S$ and $r_\pi$ to form a low-pass RC network that **determines $f_H$**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

### Mid-Band Voltage Gain Formulae

| Configuration | Device | Voltage Gain $A_{v,\text{mid}}$ | Phase |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Common-Emitter (CE) | BJT | $A_v = -g_m (R_C \parallel R_L)$ | 180° (inverting) |
| Common-Source (CS) | FET | $A_v = -g_m (R_D \parallel R_L)$ | 180° (inverting) |
| Common-Collector / Emitter Follower (CC) | BJT | $A_v = \dfrac{g_m R_E'}{1 + g_m R_E'}$ | 0° (non-inverting) |
| Common-Drain / Source Follower (CD) | FET | $A_v = \dfrac{g_m R_S'}{1 + g_m R_S'}$ | 0° (non-inverting) |
| Common-Base (CB) | BJT | $A_v = g_m (R_C \parallel R_L)$ | 0° (non-inverting) |

> Notation: $R_E' = R_E \parallel R_L$, $\;R_S' = R_S \parallel R_L$, $\;R_C' = R_C \parallel R_L$, $\;R_D' = R_D \parallel R_L$.

### Frequency Cutoff Formulae

| Cutoff | BJT CE | FET CS | Roll-off Slope |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $f_{L,\text{in}}$ (input coupling) | $\dfrac{1}{2\pi (R_S + r_\pi) C_{in}}$ | $\dfrac{1}{2\pi (R_S + R_G \parallel R_{in}) C_{in}}$ | +20 dB/dec |
| $f_{L,\text{out}}$ (output coupling) | $\dfrac{1}{2\pi (R_C + R_L) C_{out}}$ | $\dfrac{1}{2\pi (R_D + R_L) C_{out}}$ | +20 dB/dec |
| $f_{L,E}$ (emitter bypass) | $\dfrac{1}{2\pi (R_E \parallel \frac{r_\pi + R_S'\text{-source}}{1+\beta}) C_E}$ | N/A (uses $R_S$ instead) | +20 dB/dec |
| $f_H$ (high-frequency) | $\dfrac{1}{2\pi R_{\text{th}} C_{M,\text{tot}}}$ where $C_{M,\text{tot}} = C_{be} + C_{bc}(1+g_m R_C')$ | $\dfrac{1}{2\pi R_{\text{th}}' (C_{gs} + C_{gd}(1+g_m R_D'))}$ | –20 dB/dec |
| Bandwidth | $BW = f_H - f_L \approx f_H$ (when $f_H \gg f_L$) | Same | — |

### Decibel Conversions (Frequently Tested)

| Ratio (magnitude) | dB Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| 1 | 0 dB |
| $\sqrt{2}$ | +3 dB |
| $1/\sqrt{2}$ | –3 dB |
| 2 | +6.02 dB |
| 10 | +20 dB |
| 100 | +40 dB |
| 0.1 | –20 dB |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility of Frequency Response**
> - Designers use Bode plots to design **compensation networks** (e.g., Miller compensation in op-amps).
> - **Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBW)** is a *figure of merit* for wideband amplifiers: $\text{GBW} = \vert A_{v,\text{mid}} \vert \cdot f_H \approx$ constant.
> - In multi-stage amplifiers, the **dominant-pole approximation** is used: $f_H \approx 1.1 \sqrt{\sum f_{Hi}^2}$ when poles are non-interacting.

## 2.4 Step-by-Step Logical Flow of Amplifier Analysis (KTU Exam Strategy)

1. **Draw the small-signal equivalent** (hybrid-π for BJT, small-signal model for FET).
2. **Identify mid-band region** — short all coupling/bypass caps, open all junction caps.
3. **Compute $A_{v,\text{mid}}$** using resistive divider and $g_m r_\pi$ relations.
4. **For low-frequency response**: Re-insert one coupling/bypass cap at a time; find the Thevenin resistance seen by it; compute $f_L = 1/(2\pi R_{\text{th}} C)$.
5. **For high-frequency response**: Use Miller's theorem to reflect $C_{bc}$ or $C_{gd}$ to input; compute Thevenin resistance; $f_H = 1/(2\pi R_{\text{th,eq}} C_{\text{eq}})$.
6. **Sketch the Bode plot** with asymptotes at ±20 dB/decade and –3 dB breakpoints.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exhaustive Derivation Mandate**: Every algebraic step is shown explicitly. No steps are skipped.

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Mid-Band Voltage Gain of BJT Common-Emitter (CE) Amplifier

### Given
- Small-signal hybrid-π model: $r_\pi$ between base and emitter, dependent current source $g_m v_{be}$ from collector to emitter.
- Collector load $R_C' = R_C \parallel R_L$.
- Bypass capacitor $C_E$ is a short at mid-band → emitter is at AC ground.
- Input coupling cap $C_{in}$ is a short at mid-band → $v_i$ appears directly at the base.

### Step 1: Express the input loop

The input voltage $v_i$ drops across $r_\pi$ (since emitter is at AC ground):

$$v_i = v_{be} = i_b \cdot r_\pi$$

### Step 2: Relate collector current to base current

In the hybrid-π model:

$$i_c = g_m v_{be} = g_m \cdot i_b \cdot r_\pi$$

Using the BJT small-signal identity $g_m r_\pi = \beta$:

$$i_c = \beta \, i_b$$

### Step 3: Express output voltage

The collector current $i_c$ flows through $R_C'$ (by passive sign convention, with $v_o$ defined as positive at the collector):

$$v_o = -i_c \cdot R_C'$$

> Note: The negative sign arises because an **increase** in $i_c$ causes the collector voltage to **decrease** (inverting amplifier).

### Step 4: Form the ratio

$$A_v = \frac{v_o}{v_i} = \frac{-i_c R_C'}{i_b r_\pi} = \frac{-g_m v_{be} R_C'}{v_{be}} = -g_m R_C'$$

Substituting $R_C' = R_C \parallel R_L$:

$$\boxed{A_{v,\text{mid}} = -g_m (R_C \parallel R_L)}$$

The negative sign indicates a **180° phase shift** between input and output — a defining feature of the CE configuration.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Lower Cutoff Frequency due to Output Coupling Capacitor $C_{out}$

### Circuit at Low Frequency
At low $f$, $C_{out}$ is no longer a short. The output side consists of a series combination: collector node → $C_{out}$ → load $R_L$. Looking from $C_{out}$'s terminals, we see $R_C$ (to AC ground, since $V_{CC}$ is AC ground) on one side and $R_L$ (to AC ground) on the other.

### Step 1: Thevenin resistance seen by $C_{out}$

The two resistances are in series through the capacitor:

$$R_{\text{th,out}} = R_C + R_L$$

### Step 2: The transfer function

The output node sees a high-pass RC network:

$$V_o = V_c \cdot \frac{R_L}{R_C + R_L + 1/(j\omega C_{out})}$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{V_o}{V_c} = \frac{R_L}{R_C + R_L} \cdot \frac{1}{1 + \frac{1}{j\omega C_{out}(R_C + R_L)}}$$

### Step 3: Identify the cutoff

The corner frequency of the high-pass section is at $\omega = 1/[C_{out}(R_C + R_L)]$:

$$\boxed{f_{L,\text{out}} = \frac{1}{2\pi (R_C + R_L) C_{out}}}$$

This is the frequency at which $\vert V_o/V_c \vert$ falls to $1/\sqrt{2}$ of its mid-band value $R_L/(R_C + R_L)$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Lower Cutoff Frequency due to Emitter Bypass Capacitor $C_E$

This is the most-tested derivation in KTU Module 2 — it requires careful Thevenin reduction.

### Circuit Setup
$C_E$ is in parallel with $R_E$, connected from the emitter to ground. To find the resistance seen by $C_E$, we **"freeze" all independent sources** ($v_i = 0$, so the base is grounded through source resistance $R_S$). Then we look into the terminals of $C_E$ (emitter to ground).

### Step 1: Apply $v_i = 0$
With the input shorted, looking from the emitter, we see $R_E$ in parallel with the resistance looking into the emitter of the BJT.

### Step 2: Resistance looking into the emitter

With base AC-grounded (via $R_S$ to ground since $v_i = 0$ becomes ground), the resistance looking into the emitter terminal of a BJT is:

$$R_{e,\text{in}} = \frac{r_\pi + R_S}{1 + \beta}$$

> Derivation: With base grounded through $R_S$, a test voltage $v_x$ injected at the emitter produces a current $i_x = i_b + i_c = i_b(1+\beta)$ where $i_b = v_x/(r_\pi + R_S)$. Hence $R_{e,\text{in}} = v_x/i_x = (r_\pi + R_S)/(1+\beta)$.

### Step 3: Total resistance seen by $C_E$

$R_E$ is in parallel with $R_{e,\text{in}}$:

$$R_{\text{th},E} = R_E \parallel \frac{r_\pi + R_S}{1 + \beta} = R_E \parallel \left(\frac{r_\pi + R_S}{1 + \beta}\right)$$

### Step 4: Cutoff frequency

$$\boxed{f_{L,E} = \frac{1}{2\pi \, C_E \left[R_E \,\middle\Vert\, \dfrac{r_\pi + R_S}{1 + \beta}\right]}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is $f_{L,E}$ usually the dominant low-frequency pole?**
> The term $(r_\pi + R_S)/(1+\beta)$ is typically **much smaller** than $R_E$ (often by a factor of 50–200). So the parallel combination is dominated by the small term, giving a **larger** time constant and a **higher** cutoff frequency contribution. This makes $C_E$ the most critical low-frequency capacitor.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Upper Cutoff Frequency using Miller's Theorem (BJT CE)

### Step 1: Apply Miller's Theorem to $C_{bc}$

$C_{bc}$ bridges the base (node 1) and collector (node 2). The voltage gain from base to collector is the mid-band gain $A_v = -g_m R_C'$. Substituting into Miller's formula with $K = A_v$:

$$C_{M,\text{in}} = C_{bc}(1 - A_v) = C_{bc}(1 - (-g_m R_C')) = C_{bc}(1 + g_m R_C')$$

$$C_{M,\text{out}} = C_{bc}\left(1 - \frac{1}{A_v}\right) \approx C_{bc} \quad (\text{since } \vert A_v \vert \gg 1)$$

### Step 2: Total input-side capacitance

$$C_{\text{in,tot}} = C_{be} + C_{M,\text{in}} = C_{be} + C_{bc}(1 + g_m R_C')$$

### Step 3: Thevenin resistance at the input

Looking into the base from $C_{be} + C_{M,\text{in}}$, the source $R_S$ is in parallel with $r_\pi$ (since $V_{CC}$ is AC ground but $r_\pi$ is between base and emitter-ground):

$$R_{\text{th,in}} = R_S \parallel r_\pi$$

### Step 4: Upper cutoff frequency

The high-frequency response is dominated by this input RC low-pass section:

$$\boxed{f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi (R_S \parallel r_\pi)\left[C_{be} + C_{bc}(1 + g_m R_C')\right]}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation**
> The Miller effect can multiply a small $C_{bc} \approx 2$ pF into an effective input capacitance of several hundred pF when $g_m R_C' \approx 100$. This is the **primary limiting factor** on high-frequency response of CE amplifiers and explains why CE stages have **lower $f_H$** than CB or CC stages.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Bandwidth and Gain-Bandwidth Product

The total bandwidth is:

$$BW = f_H - f_L$$

In most practical amplifiers, $f_H \gg f_L$ (often by 3–4 orders of magnitude), so:

$$BW \approx f_H$$

The **Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBW)**, a key KTU-exam metric:

$$\text{GBW} = \vert A_{v,\text{mid}} \vert \cdot f_H \approx g_m R_C' \cdot \frac{1}{2\pi (R_S \parallel r_\pi) [C_{be} + C_{bc}(1 + g_m R_C')]}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip**: For the CB stage, the Miller effect is **absent** (gain ≈ +1, non-inverting, $A_v$ not large negative), so $f_H$ is much higher. The CB stage is therefore used in **RF and high-frequency** front-ends. The CC (emitter follower) also has high $f_H$ because its voltage gain is ≈ 1.

---

## 3.6 Numerical Worked Example (Typical KTU 2-Mark Sub-Part)

**Problem**: A BJT CE amplifier has $g_m = 50$ mA/V, $r_\pi = 1$ k$\Omega$, $R_C = 4.7$ k$\Omega$, $R_L = 10$ k$\Omega$, $C_{in} = 1$ $\mu$F, $C_{out} = 1$ $\mu$F, $C_E = 100$ $\mu$F, $R_S = 600$ $\Omega$, $C_{be} = 30$ pF, $C_{bc} = 4$ pF. Find $A_{v,\text{mid}}$, $f_L$, and $f_H$.

**Solution**:

Step 1 — Mid-band load:
$$R_C' = R_C \parallel R_L = \frac{4.7 \times 10}{4.7 + 10} = 3.20 \text{ k}\Omega$$

Step 2 — Mid-band gain:
$$A_{v,\text{mid}} = -g_m R_C' = -(0.05)(3200) = -160$$

Step 3 — Input-side low-frequency cutoff:
$$f_{L,\text{in}} = \frac{1}{2\pi (R_S + r_\pi) C_{in}} = \frac{1}{2\pi (600 + 1000)(1 \times 10^{-6})} \approx 99.5 \text{ Hz}$$

Step 4 — Output-side low-frequency cutoff:
$$f_{L,\text{out}} = \frac{1}{2\pi (R_C + R_L) C_{out}} = \frac{1}{2\pi (4700 + 10000)(1 \times 10^{-6})} \approx 10.8 \text{ Hz}$$

Step 5 — Emitter bypass cutoff:
$$R_{\text{th},E} = R_E \parallel \frac{r_\pi + R_S}{1+\beta} \approx \frac{r_\pi + R_S}{1+\beta} = \frac{1600}{101} \approx 15.84 \text{ }\Omega \text{ (assuming } R_E \gg 15.84 \text{ }\Omega\text{)}$$

$$f_{L,E} = \frac{1}{2\pi (15.84)(100 \times 10^{-6})} \approx 100.5 \text{ Hz}$$

Step 6 — Dominant low-frequency pole (largest of the three):
$$f_L \approx 100.5 \text{ Hz}$$

Step 7 — High-frequency cutoff using Miller:
$$C_{M,\text{in}} = 4(1 + 0.05 \times 3200) = 4 \times 161 = 644 \text{ pF}$$
$$C_{\text{in,tot}} = 30 + 644 = 674 \text{ pF}$$
$$R_{\text{th,in}} = R_S \parallel r_\pi = \frac{600 \times 1000}{1600} = 375 \text{ }\Omega$$
$$f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi (375)(674 \times 10^{-12})} \approx 630 \text{ kHz}$$

Step 8 — Bandwidth:
$$BW \approx f_H \approx 630 \text{ kHz}$$

Step 9 — Gain-Bandwidth Product:
$$\text{GBW} = 160 \times 630 \times 10^3 = 1.008 \times 10^8 \text{ Hz} \approx 100.8 \text{ MHz}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block-Level Frequency Response Architecture

This Mermaid block diagram represents the **functional decomposition** of an amplifier's voltage gain transfer function into its three frequency-region contributions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Signal Source vin] --> B1[Input Coupling Capacitor Cin Block]
    B1 --> B2[BJT or FET Active Device Hybrid Pi Model]
    B2 --> B3[Emitter or Source Bypass Capacitor CE Block]
    B3 --> B4[Output Coupling Capacitor Cout Block]
    B4 --> C[Load Resistor RL Output vo]

    D[High Frequency Internal Capacitances] -.Miller Reflected.-> B2

    subgraph REGION_LF[LOW FREQUENCY REGION f less than fL]
        B1
        B3
        B4
    end

    subgraph REGION_MID[MID BAND REGION fL to fH]
        B2
    end

    subgraph REGION_HF[HIGH FREQUENCY REGION f greater than fH]
        D
    end

    E[Mid Band Gain Av mid equals negative gm times RC prime] --- B2
    F[Lower Cutoff fL from Cin CE Cout] --- REGION_LF
    G[Upper Cutoff fH from Miller Theorem] --- REGION_HF
    H[Bandwidth BW equals fH minus fL] --- REGION_MID
```

## 4.2 Bode Magnitude Plot — Typical BJT CE Amplifier

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph AXES[LOG LOG AXES]
        AX1[Frequency axis f in Hz logarithmic scale]
        AX2[Gain axis Av in dB linear dB]
    end

    P1[f less than fL] -->|Positive 20 dB per decade slope| P2[f equals fL minus 3 dB point]
    P2 -->|Flat asymptote at Av mid| P3[f between fL and fH mid band]
    P3 -->|Flat at Av mid| P4[f equals fH minus 3 dB point]
    P4 -->|Negative 20 dB per decade slope| P5[f greater than fH]

    P2 -.Marker.-> M1[3 dB below Av mid at fL]
    P4 -.Marker.-> M2[3 dB below Av mid at fH]
    P3 -.Marker.-> M3[Constant gain Av mid equals 20 log gm RC prime]
```

## 4.3 Flowchart — Step-by-Step Frequency Response Analysis Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start: Given Amplifier with Component Values] --> S1[Draw Small Signal Equivalent]
    S1 --> S2[Identify Three Capacitor Types Coupling Bypass Internal]
    S2 --> S3[Compute Mid Band Gain by Shorting Coupling Caps and Opening Internal Caps]
    S3 --> S4[For Low Frequency Compute fL for Each Capacitor]
    S4 --> S5[Select Dominant fL as Maximum of All fL values]
    S5 --> S6[For High Frequency Apply Miller Theorem to Cbc or Cgd]
    S6 --> S7[Compute Total Input Capacitance Cin tot]
    S7 --> S8[Compute Thevenin Resistance R th in]
    S8 --> S9[Compute fH equals 1 over 2 pi R th in C in tot]
    S9 --> S10[Bandwidth BW equals fH minus fL]
    S10 --> S11[Sketch Bode Plot with Asymptotes at 20 dB per decade]
    S11 --> S12[End: Report Av mid fL fH BW and GBW]
```

## 4.4 Functional Comparison: CE vs CB vs CC Frequency Behaviour

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Single Stage BJT Amplifier Configurations] --> B0[Common Emitter CE]
    A0 --> C0[Common Base CB]
    A0 --> D0[Common Collector CC Emitter Follower]

    B0 --> B1[Av mid: Large negative value approx negative gm RC prime]
    B0 --> B2[fH: Low due to Miller effect on Cbc]
    B0 --> B3[Use: General purpose voltage amplifier audio stages]

    C0 --> C1[Av mid: Positive approx gm RC prime]
    C0 --> C2[fH: Very high no Miller multiplication]
    C0 --> C3[Use: High frequency RF front end low impedance input]

    D0 --> D1[Av mid: Less than 1 non inverting approx 1]
    D0 --> D2[fH: High small Miller effect]
    D0 --> D3[Use: Buffer impedance transformer output stage]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define voltage gain of an amplifier. Why is it expressed in decibels (dB)?**

**Model Answer**:
Voltage gain $A_v$ is the ratio of output signal voltage to input signal voltage, $A_v = v_o/v_i$. It is dimensionless. It is expressed in decibels because:
1. The human ear perceives loudness **logarithmically**; dB matches this perception.
2. For cascaded multi-stage amplifiers, the overall gain in dB is the **sum** of individual stage dB gains, simplifying calculation.
3. Bode plots use dB on the y-axis, enabling easy visualisation of the wide gain range (from 0.1 to 100,000) on a single graph.
4. The –3 dB points precisely identify the cutoff frequencies.

$$A_v(\text{dB}) = 20 \log_{10}\left|\frac{v_o}{v_i}\right|$$

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is meant by the –3 dB frequency of an amplifier? How is bandwidth related to it?**

**Model Answer**:
The –3 dB frequency is the frequency at which the magnitude of the voltage gain falls to $1/\sqrt{2}$ (≈ 0.7071) of its mid-band maximum value. Equivalently, in logarithmic terms, the gain has dropped by 3 dB from its mid-band value. There are two such frequencies: $f_L$ (lower) and $f_H$ (upper).

**Bandwidth (BW)** is defined as the range of frequencies over which the amplifier provides useful (near-mid-band) gain, bounded by the –3 dB points:

$$BW = f_H - f_L \approx f_H \quad (\text{since } f_H \gg f_L)$$

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU ESE Pattern**: Part B questions carry 14 marks each, with sub-parts typically split as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Students answer ONE of the two alternatives.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the lower cutoff frequency of a Common-Emitter amplifier due to the **emitter bypass capacitor** $C_E$. Explain why this capacitor usually determines the dominant low-frequency pole. (7 marks)

**(b)** A BJT CE amplifier has the following parameters: $g_m = 40$ mA/V, $r_\pi = 1.25$ k$\Omega$, $\beta = 50$, $R_S = 1$ k$\Omega$, $R_C = 5$ k$\Omega$, $R_L = 10$ k$\Omega$, $R_E = 2$ k$\Omega$, $C_{in} = C_{out} = 1$ $\mu$F, $C_E = 100$ $\mu$F, $C_{be} = 25$ pF, $C_{bc} = 5$ pF.
Calculate: (i) Mid-band voltage gain $A_{v,\text{mid}}$, (ii) All three lower cutoff frequencies and the dominant $f_L$, (iii) Upper cutoff frequency $f_H$ using Miller's theorem, (iv) Bandwidth and GBW. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation for $f_{L,E}$**: See Derivation 3 in Section 3.3 above.

- Set $v_i = 0$, base becomes AC ground through $R_S$.
- Resistance looking into emitter: $R_{e,\text{in}} = (r_\pi + R_S)/(1 + \beta)$.
- $R_E$ is in parallel with $R_{e,\text{in}}$.
- $\boxed{f_{L,E} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \, C_E \left[R_E \,\middle\Vert\, \dfrac{r_\pi + R_S}{1 + \beta}\right]}}$

**Why dominant?**: The term $(r_\pi + R_S)/(1 + \beta)$ is very small (e.g., for $r_\pi = 1$ k$\Omega$, $R_S = 1$ k$\Omega$, $\beta = 50$ → 40 $\Omega$). This dominates the parallel combination, **reducing** the effective resistance and **increasing** the time constant $R_{\text{th}} C_E$, hence pushing $f_{L,E}$ higher than the other two coupling-capacitor cutoffs. Physically: removing $C_E$ introduces strong local negative feedback (degeneration), which suppresses low-frequency gain severely.

**(b) Numerical Solution**:

(i) Mid-band gain:
$$R_C' = R_C \parallel R_L = \frac{5 \times 10}{15} = 3.33 \text{ k}\Omega$$
$$A_{v,\text{mid}} = -g_m R_C' = -(0.04)(3333) = -133.3$$

(ii) Lower cutoffs:
$$f_{L,\text{in}} = \frac{1}{2\pi (R_S + r_\pi)C_{in}} = \frac{1}{2\pi (1000 + 1250)(10^{-6})} = 70.7 \text{ Hz}$$
$$f_{L,\text{out}} = \frac{1}{2\pi (R_C + R_L)C_{out}} = \frac{1}{2\pi (5000 + 10000)(10^{-6})} = 10.6 \text{ Hz}$$
$$R_{\text{th},E} = 2000 \,\big\Vert\, \frac{1250 + 1000}{51} = 2000 \,\big\Vert\, 44.1 = 43.2 \text{ }\Omega$$
$$f_{L,E} = \frac{1}{2\pi (43.2)(100 \times 10^{-6})} = 36.8 \text{ Hz}$$

Dominant $f_L$ = **70.7 Hz** (largest).

(iii) Upper cutoff using Miller:
$$C_{M} = 5(1 + 0.04 \times 3333) = 5 \times 134.3 = 671.7 \text{ pF}$$
$$C_{\text{in,tot}} = 25 + 671.7 = 696.7 \text{ pF}$$
$$R_{\text{th,in}} = R_S \parallel r_\pi = \frac{1000 \times 1250}{2250} = 555.6 \text{ }\Omega$$
$$f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi (555.6)(696.7 \times 10^{-12})} = 410.5 \text{ kHz}$$

(iv) Bandwidth and GBW:
$$BW = f_H - f_L = 410500 - 70.7 \approx 410.4 \text{ kHz}$$
$$\text{GBW} = |A_{v,\text{mid}}| \times f_H = 133.3 \times 410.5 \text{ kHz} \approx 54.7 \text{ MHz}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 — Alternative]

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **frequency response** of a single-stage RC-coupled BJT CE amplifier. Clearly indicate the three regions of operation, the –3 dB points, and the slopes. (7 marks)

**(b)** State and prove **Miller's theorem**. Apply it to find the upper cutoff frequency of a CE amplifier whose internal capacitances are $C_{be}$ and $C_{bc}$. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**(a) Frequency Response Sketch and Explanation** (see Mermaid Bode plot in Section 4.2):

The frequency response is plotted as $|A_v|$ in dB vs $\log f$. It has three regions:

- **Low-frequency region** ($f < f_L$): Coupling and bypass capacitors dominate. Gain rises with +20 dB/decade slope per capacitor.
- **Mid-band region** ($f_L \le f \le f_H$): All capacitors are inactive (coupling caps = short, junction caps = open). Gain is **flat** at $|A_{v,\text{mid}}| = g_m(R_C \parallel R_L)$.
- **High-frequency region** ($f > f_H$): Internal capacitances $C_{be}$, $C_{bc}$ dominate via Miller effect. Gain falls with –20 dB/decade slope.

The –3 dB points at $f_L$ and $f_H$ define the bandwidth $BW = f_H - f_L$.

**(b) Miller's Theorem** (see Derivation 4 in Section 3.4):

**Statement**: If an impedance $Z$ is connected between two nodes where voltages are $v_1$ and $v_2$ with ratio $K = v_2/v_1$, then $Z$ can be replaced by:
- $Z_1 = Z/(1 - K)$ from node 1 to ground,
- $Z_2 = Z \cdot K/(K - 1)$ from node 2 to ground,

without changing the node voltages or currents drawn from the rest of the circuit.

**Proof**: Current through $Z$ is $I = (v_1 - v_2)/Z = v_1(1 - K)/Z = v_1/Z_1$. Hence $Z_1 = Z/(1 - K)$. Similarly from node 2: $I = (v_2 - v_1)/Z = v_2(1 - 1/K)/Z = v_2/Z_2$. Hence $Z_2 = Z \cdot K/(K-1)$.

**Application to CE**: With $K = A_v = -g_m R_C'$, for $C_{bc}$: $C_{M,\text{in}} = C_{bc}(1 - A_v) = C_{bc}(1 + g_m R_C')$. Total input capacitance = $C_{be} + C_{M,\text{in}}$. Upper cutoff:
$$f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi (R_S \parallel r_\pi)\left[C_{be} + C_{bc}(1 + g_m R_C')\right]}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU Valuation**
> 1. **Sign of $A_v$**: Forgetting the negative sign in CE gain ($A_v = -g_m R_C'$) — losing 1 mark.
> 2. **Mixing $R_C$ and $R_C \parallel R_L$**: Always use $R_C' = R_C \parallel R_L$ for the load, *not* just $R_C$.
> 3. **Forgetting Miller multiplication**: Writing $f_H = 1/[2\pi(R_S \parallel r_\pi)(C_{be} + C_{bc})]$ — this **misses the $(1 + g_m R_C')$ factor** and gives a hugely over-estimated $f_H$. Loses 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Emitter bypass formula**: Writing $R_E$ alone in the denominator instead of $R_E \parallel [(r_\pi + R_S)/(1+\beta)]$ — common error, loses 2 marks.
> 5. **Bandwidth definition**: Writing BW = $f_H$ without justifying "$f_H \gg f_L$" — KTU examiners deduct 1 mark for missing the justification.
> 6. **Unit consistency**: Mixing Hz and kHz, or pF with $\mu$F without conversion. Always use SI base units in formulas.
> 7. **Bode plot slope**: Drawing +40 dB/dec in low-frequency region when only one capacitor is dominant at a time — slopes add only when poles coincide, otherwise use +20 dB/dec per pole.

---

## 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final High-Density Revision Block**

### Must-Memorise Formulae
- [ ] $A_{v,\text{mid}}(\text{CE}) = -g_m (R_C \parallel R_L)$
- [ ] $A_{v,\text{mid}}(\text{CS}) = -g_m (R_D \parallel R_L)$
- [ ] $A_{v,\text{mid}}(\text{CC}) \approx 1$, $\;A_{v,\text{mid}}(\text{CB}) = +g_m (R_C \parallel R_L)$
- [ ] $A_v(\text{dB}) = 20 \log_{10}|v_o/v_i|$
- [ ] $f_{L,\text{out}} = 1/[2\pi(R_C + R_L)C_{out}]$
- [ ] $f_{L,E} = 1/[2\pi C_E \cdot (R_E \parallel (r_\pi + R_S)/(1+\beta))]$
- [ ] $C_{M} = C_{bc}(1 + g_m R_C')$ — **Miller multiplication**
- [ ] $f_H = 1/[2\pi (R_S \parallel r_\pi) (C_{be} + C_{M})]$
- [ ] $BW = f_H - f_L \approx f_H$
- [ ] $\text{GBW} = |A_{v,\text{mid}}| \cdot f_H$

### Key Conceptual Points
- [ ] Mid-band: coupling caps = short, junction caps = open.
- [ ] Low-frequency cutoff: caused by coupling and bypass capacitors.
- [ ] High-frequency cutoff: caused by internal junction capacitances amplified by Miller effect.
- [ ] Bode plot slope: **+20 dB/dec** in low-frequency region per pole, **flat** in mid-band, **–20 dB/dec** in high-frequency region per pole.
- [ ] The **–3 dB** point corresponds to gain = $1/\sqrt{2}$ of mid-band value.
- [ ] **CE has lowest $f_H$** (Miller-heavy); **CB has highest $f_H$** (no Miller).
- [ ] Emitter bypass capacitor is the **dominant** low-frequency pole in most CE designs.
- [ ] GBW is approximately **constant** for a given transistor — to increase bandwidth, reduce gain.

### Common Exam Traps
- [ ] "Mid-band" means NOT 0 Hz — DC is blocked by coupling caps.
- [ ] The phase of CE is **180°**, not 0°.
- [ ] For FETs, $C_E$ is replaced by $C_S$ at the source; the analysis uses $1/g_m$ instead of $(r_\pi + R_S)/(1+\beta)$.
- [ ] Always state units explicitly in the final answer.

### Past-Year Question Pattern Frequency
- [ ] 3-mark: Definition of voltage gain, –3 dB frequency, bandwidth, Miller's theorem statement — **guaranteed every year**.
- [ ] 7-mark: Derivation of $f_{L,E}$ or $f_H$ using Miller — **rotates every exam**.
- [ ] 7-mark: Numerical problem computing $A_{v,\text{mid}}$, $f_L$, $f_H$, BW, GBW — **always asked in some form**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Small Signal Analysis of CE Configuration - Small Signal Hybrid-pi Model for Mid and Low Frequency (Gain, Input and Output Impedance)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET302 - ELECTRONIC DEVICES AND CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Small Signal Analysis of CE Configuration - Small Signal Hybrid-pi Model for Mid and Low Frequency (Gain, Input and Output Impedance)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Common Emitter (CE) Configuration** is the most widely used transistor amplifier topology in analog electronics, wherein the emitter terminal is common to both the input and output loops. **Small Signal Analysis** refers to the linearized AC analysis of the transistor biased in the active region, where the input signal amplitude is small enough (typically $\leq 10$ mV) that the non-linear transistor characteristics can be approximated by a linear equivalent circuit.

The **Hybrid-π Model** (developed by L.J. Giacoletto in 1954) is a two-port small-signal model of the BJT that represents the transistor as a voltage-controlled current source in parallel with a resistance, derived directly from the physics of the device. Unlike the h-parameter model (which is defined at specific terminals), the hybrid-π model is tied to the **internal physical node** between the base and emitter — making it accurate over a wide range of frequencies and the preferred model for KTU 2024 Scheme analysis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The hybrid-π model is the *primary* small-signal model mandated for CE configuration analysis. Students must derive gain, input impedance, and output impedance using this model — not the h-parameter model — for Module 2.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Valve" Intuition

Imagine a transistor as a **water valve** at the end of a garden hose:

- The **base** is the *small control handle* — you only need a tiny turn of the handle to control a large flow.
- The **collector** is the *main water pipe* carrying a huge current.
- The **emitter** is the *output pipe* where the regulated water exits.

In small-signal operation, you **wiggle the handle slightly** (input AC signal), and a **proportional large wiggling** happens in the main water flow. The hybrid-π model simply quantifies this proportionality:

- $g_m$ = *how sensitive the main flow is to the handle movement* (transconductance, in A/V).
- $r_\pi$ = *friction the handle has to overcome* (base-emitter input resistance, in Ω).
- $r_o$ = *slight leakage in the main pipe* that depends on water pressure (output resistance from Early effect).

> [!NOTE]
> **Thermal Voltage Constant:** At room temperature ($T = 300$ K), the thermal voltage $V_T = \dfrac{kT}{q} = \mathbf{25.85 \text{ mV}}$. This is the foundational constant from which $g_m$ and $r_\pi$ are derived.

## 1.3 Why Mid and Low Frequency?

The hybrid-π model itself is the **intrinsic transistor model** valid from DC up to the transition frequency $f_T$. When external **coupling capacitors ($C_{in}$, $C_{out}$)** and **bypass capacitor ($C_E$)** are added:

| Frequency Range | Dominant Reactive Elements | Analysis Method |
|---|---|---|
| **Mid-Frequency** | All coupling/bypass capacitors are *short circuits*; internal $C_\pi$, $C_\mu$ are *open circuits* | Pure resistive hybrid-π model used |
| **Low Frequency** | Coupling/bypass capacitors create *high-pass poles* | Hybrid-π model + capacitor impedances |
| **High Frequency** | Internal $C_\pi$, $C_\mu$ matter (Miller effect) | Hybrid-π + Miller's Theorem |

> [!NOTE]
> At **low frequencies**, the hybrid-π resistive network still drives the analysis, but the external capacitors ($C_{in}$, $C_E$, $C_{out}$) add series/parallel reactive impedances that **degrade the gain at low frequencies**. The lower cutoff frequency $f_L$ is set by these capacitors.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Hybrid-π Model — Component-by-Component Breakdown

The hybrid-π model replaces the BJT with the following linear elements connected between the **internal base node B'** (not the external base B) and emitter, and between collector and emitter:

**Resistive Elements:**
- **$r_\pi$** — Resistance between internal base B' and emitter E (input resistance looking into the base).
- **$r_o$** — Output resistance between collector C and emitter E (Early effect resistance).
- **$r_x$ (or $r_b$)** — Base spreading resistance between external base B and internal base B' (usually $\leq 100$ Ω, often neglected in basic KTU problems).
- **$g_m v_{b'e}$** — Voltage-controlled current source from collector to emitter (the amplification action).

**Capacitive Elements (for completeness, used in high-frequency analysis):**
- **$C_\pi$** — Base-emitter diffusion + junction capacitance.
- **$C_\mu$** — Base-collector junction capacitance (Miller capacitance source).

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why is $g_m$ the key parameter?** Because it directly quantifies the *amplification action* — a small change in base-emitter voltage $v_{b'e}$ produces a collector current $g_m \cdot v_{b'e}$.
- **Why include $r_o$?** The Early effect makes $I_C$ slightly dependent on $V_{CE}$. Without $r_o$, the model predicts infinite output impedance, which is unrealistic.
- **Why neglect $r_x$ in basic analysis?** For $I_C \geq 1$ mA, $r_\pi$ is in the kΩ range, dwarfing $r_x$. KTU problems usually specify "neglect base spreading resistance."

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Thermal Voltage | $V_T = \dfrac{kT}{q}$ | $\approx 25.85$ mV at 300 K | V |
| Transconductance | $g_m = \dfrac{I_C}{V_T}$ | Slope of $I_C$ vs $V_{BE}$ | A/V (S) |
| Base-Emitter Resistance | $r_\pi = \dfrac{\beta}{g_m} = \dfrac{\beta V_T}{I_C}$ | Input resistance at base | Ω |
| Output Resistance | $r_o = \dfrac{V_A + V_{CE}}{I_C} \approx \dfrac{V_A}{I_C}$ | Early effect resistance | Ω |
| Relationship | $g_m \cdot r_\pi = \beta$ | Fundamental identity | dimensionless |
| Voltage Gain (CE, no $R_E$) | $A_v = -g_m (R_C \parallel r_o \parallel R_L)$ | Mid-band gain | V/V |
| Voltage Gain (CE with bypassed $R_E$) | $A_v = -g_m (R_C \parallel r_o \parallel R_L)$ | Same as above | V/V |
| Voltage Gain (CE with unbypassed $R_E$) | $A_v = -\dfrac{g_m (R_C \parallel r_o \parallel R_L)}{1 + g_m R_E}$ | Reduced by feedback | V/V |
| Input Impedance (looking into base) | $Z_{in(base)} = r_\pi + (\beta + 1) R_E$ | With unbypassed $R_E$ | Ω |
| Input Impedance (CE amplifier) | $Z_{in} = R_1 \parallel R_2 \parallel Z_{in(base)}$ | With bias network | Ω |
| Output Impedance | $Z_{out} = R_C \parallel r_o$ | Looking into collector | Ω |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorization Priority for KTU:** The three formulas $\boxed{g_m = I_C/V_T}$, $\boxed{r_\pi = \beta/g_m}$, and $\boxed{A_v = -g_m R_C'}$ appear in nearly every Part B question. Commit them to memory first.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The hybrid-π model is the **workhorse model in SPICE simulators** (the simulator at the heart of virtually every analog IC design tool, including Cadence, LTspice, and Synopsys). When a chip designer in Intel, Texas Instruments, or Analog Devices simulates a circuit, they are using a hybrid-π model internally. Understanding it manually — as KTU requires — gives you the intuition to debug, optimize, and design analog circuits used in:

- Audio amplifiers (op-amp input stages)
- RF front-ends (radios, smartphones)
- Sensor signal conditioning circuits
- Operational transconductance amplifiers (OTAs)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Transconductance $g_m$ from Device Physics

Starting from the Ebers-Moll equation for collector current in the active region:

$$I_C = I_S \, e^{V_{BE}/V_T}$$

Taking the derivative with respect to $V_{BE}$ (which gives the small-signal transconductance):

$$g_m = \frac{\partial I_C}{\partial V_{BE}} = \frac{I_S}{V_T} \, e^{V_{BE}/V_T}$$

Since $I_C = I_S \, e^{V_{BE}/V_T}$, we substitute back:

$$g_m = \frac{I_C}{V_T}$$

This is the **master equation** of small-signal BJT analysis.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Base-Emitter Resistance $r_\pi$

By definition, the small-signal input resistance looking into the base is:

$$r_\pi = \frac{v_{b'e}}{i_b}$$

The collector current is $i_c = g_m \, v_{b'e}$ and the base current is $i_b = i_c / \beta$ (since $i_c = \beta i_b$ in the active region).

Therefore:

$$i_b = \frac{g_m \, v_{b'e}}{\beta}$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{v_{b'e}}{i_b} = \frac{\beta}{g_m}$$

$$r_\pi = \frac{\beta}{g_m} = \frac{\beta V_T}{I_C}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Voltage Gain $A_v$ for CE (Mid-Frequency, Bypassed $R_E$)

**Step 1:** Draw the small-signal equivalent circuit. The input $v_i = v_{b'e}$ appears across $r_\pi$. The current source $g_m v_{b'e}$ flows from collector to emitter.

**Step 2:** Apply KCL at the collector node. The total load at the collector is $R_C \parallel r_o \parallel R_L$. Let $R_C' = R_C \parallel r_o \parallel R_L$.

**Step 3:** The output voltage is:

$$v_o = -g_m v_{b'e} \cdot R_C'$$

The negative sign indicates a **180° phase inversion** (hallmark of CE amplifier).

**Step 4:** Since $v_i = v_{b'e}$ (the source drives the base directly in mid-band, capacitors are shorts):

$$A_v = \frac{v_o}{v_i} = -g_m R_C'$$

$$A_v = -g_m (R_C \parallel r_o \parallel R_L)$$

**Step 5:** If $r_o \gg R_C$ (typical for $I_C < 10$ mA), the approximation $A_v \approx -g_m R_C$ is valid.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Voltage Gain with Unbypassed Emitter Resistor $R_E$

**Step 1:** With $R_E$ unbypassed, the input loop equation is:

$$v_i = v_{b'e} + v_{R_E} = v_{b'e} + i_e R_E$$

**Step 2:** The emitter current $i_e = i_c + i_b = (\beta + 1) i_b \approx \beta i_b$ for $\beta \gg 1$.

**Step 3:** Also, $i_c = g_m v_{b'e}$, so $v_{b'e} = i_c / g_m = \beta i_b / g_m$.

**Step 4:** Substituting:

$$v_i = \frac{\beta i_b}{g_m} + (\beta + 1) i_b R_E$$

$$v_i = i_b \left[\frac{\beta}{g_m} + (\beta + 1) R_E\right]$$

For $\beta \gg 1$:

$$v_i \approx i_b \left[\frac{1}{g_m} + R_E\right] \cdot \beta$$

**Step 5:** The output voltage remains $v_o = -g_m v_{b'e} R_C' = -\beta i_b R_C'$.

**Step 6:** The voltage gain is:

$$A_v = \frac{v_o}{v_i} = \frac{-\beta i_b R_C'}{i_b \left[\frac{\beta}{g_m} + (\beta + 1) R_E\right]}$$

For $\beta \gg 1$:

$$A_v \approx \frac{-g_m R_C'}{1 + g_m R_E}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Insight:** The unbypassed $R_E$ introduces **series-series negative feedback**. It reduces the gain by a factor of $(1 + g_m R_E)$ but improves linearity, bandwidth, and thermal stability — a classic engineering trade-off.

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Input Impedance $Z_{in}$ (Looking into the Base)

**Step 1:** Looking into the base terminal (internal node B'), the impedance is $r_\pi$ in parallel with the feedback path through $R_E$.

**Step 2:** Apply a test voltage $v_x$ at the base; the resulting base current is $i_b$. The voltage across $r_\pi$ is:

$$v_{b'e} = v_x - i_e R_E = v_x - (\beta + 1) i_b R_E$$

**Step 3:** The current through $r_\pi$ is:

$$i_{r_\pi} = \frac{v_{b'e}}{r_\pi} = \frac{v_x - (\beta + 1) i_b R_E}{r_\pi}$$

**Step 4:** KCL at the base: $i_b = i_{r_\pi}$ (assuming $r_x$ is neglected), so:

$$i_b \cdot r_\pi = v_x - (\beta + 1) i_b R_E$$

$$v_x = i_b \left[r_\pi + (\beta + 1) R_E\right]$$

**Step 5:** Therefore:

$$Z_{in(base)} = \frac{v_x}{i_b} = r_\pi + (\beta + 1) R_E$$

**Step 6:** The total amplifier input impedance (including bias network $R_1 \parallel R_2$):

$$Z_{in} = R_1 \parallel R_2 \parallel \left[r_\pi + (\beta + 1) R_E\right]$$

## 3.6 Derivation 6: Output Impedance $Z_{out}$

**Step 1:** Set the input source $v_i = 0$. This means $v_{b'e} = 0$, so the controlled source $g_m v_{b'e} = 0$ (open circuit).

**Step 2:** Looking back into the collector, we see $r_o$ from collector to emitter, in parallel with $R_C$ (which is connected to ground/AC ground via $V_{CC}$).

**Step 3:**

$$Z_{out} = R_C \parallel r_o$$

If $r_o \gg R_C$:

$$Z_{out} \approx R_C$$

## 3.7 Low-Frequency Considerations

At low frequencies, the **coupling capacitor $C_{in}$** (between source and base) introduces a series impedance $\dfrac{1}{j\omega C_{in}}$, and the **bypass capacitor $C_E$** (across $R_E$) becomes an open circuit (no longer shorting $R_E$).

The lower cutoff frequency is determined by the dominant pole:

$$f_L \approx \frac{1}{2\pi (R_S + r_\pi) C_{in}} \quad \text{(input RC)}$$

$$f_L \approx \frac{1}{2\pi (R_E \parallel \frac{r_\pi + R_S'}{\beta + 1}) C_E} \quad \text{(bypass RC)}$$

The **overall lower cutoff** is the **sum** of individual $f_L$ contributions (or computed by the short-circuit time-constant method):

$$f_L \approx f_{L1} + f_{L2} + f_{L3}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **At low frequencies, the hybrid-π model is still used for the transistor itself**, but now the external capacitors are no longer assumed to be short circuits. This is the key distinction between mid-band and low-frequency analysis.

## 3.8 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A CE amplifier has $I_C = 2$ mA, $\beta = 100$, $V_A = 100$ V, $R_C = 4.7$ kΩ, $R_L = 10$ kΩ, $R_1 = 33$ kΩ, $R_2 = 10$ kΩ, $R_E = 1$ kΩ (bypassed by $C_E$), $R_S = 600$ Ω. Find $g_m$, $r_\pi$, $r_o$, $Z_{in}$, $Z_{out}$, and mid-band $A_v$.

**Solution:**

$$g_m = \frac{I_C}{V_T} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-3}}{25.85 \times 10^{-3}} = 77.37 \text{ mA/V}$$

$$r_\pi = \frac{\beta}{g_m} = \frac{100}{77.37 \times 10^{-3}} = 1292.5 \text{ Ω} \approx 1.29 \text{ kΩ}$$

$$r_o = \frac{V_A}{I_C} = \frac{100}{2 \times 10^{-3}} = 50 \text{ kΩ}$$

$$R_C' = R_C \parallel r_o \parallel R_L = 4.7\text{k} \parallel 50\text{k} \parallel 10\text{k} \approx 3.07 \text{ kΩ}$$

$$A_v = -g_m R_C' = -(77.37 \times 10^{-3})(3070) = -237.5$$

$$Z_{in(base)} = r_\pi = 1.29 \text{ kΩ (since } R_E \text{ is bypassed)}$$

$$Z_{in} = R_1 \parallel R_2 \parallel r_\pi = 33\text{k} \parallel 10\text{k} \parallel 1.29\text{k} \approx 1.13 \text{ kΩ}$$

$$Z_{out} = R_C \parallel r_o = 4.7\text{k} \parallel 50\text{k} \approx 4.30 \text{ kΩ}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Hybrid-π Model for CE

```mermaid
graph TD
    srcA[Input Source v_s] --> resA[Source Resistance R_S]
    resA --> capA[Coupling Capacitor C_in]
    capA --> nodeB[Base Terminal B]
    nodeB --> resB[Bias Network R_1 parallel R_2]
    resB --> gndA[Ground AC Reference]
    nodeB --> resC[Base Spreading r_x optional]
    resC --> nodeBp[Internal Base Node B prime]
    nodeBp --> resPi[Input Resistance r_pi]
    resPi --> nodeE[Emitter Node E]
    nodeE --> resE[Emitter Resistor R_E]
    resE --> gndB[Ground]
    nodeE --> capE[Bypass Capacitor C_E]
    capE --> gndB
    nodeBp --> vcs[Controlled Current Source g_m times v_b prime e]
    vcs --> nodeC[Collector Node C]
    nodeC --> resRo[Output Resistance r_o]
    resRo --> nodeE
    nodeC --> resRc[Collector Resistor R_C]
    resRc --> vcc[V CC AC Ground]
    nodeC --> capOut[Coupling Capacitor C_out]
    capOut --> nodeOut[Output Node v_out]
    nodeOut --> resRl[Load Resistor R_L]
    resRl --> gndC[Ground]
```

## 4.2 Flowchart: Mid-Frequency vs Low-Frequency Analysis Decision

```mermaid
graph TD
    startA[Start CE Amplifier Analysis] --> q1{Is frequency in mid-band?}
    q1 -- Yes --> midA[All coupling and bypass capacitors are short circuits]
    midA --> midB[Internal capacitances C_pi and C_mu are open circuits]
    midB --> midC[Apply pure resistive hybrid-pi model]
    midC --> midD[Compute A_v, Z_in, Z_out using resistive formulas]
    q1 -- No --> q2{Is frequency in low-band?}
    q2 -- Yes --> lowA[C_in C_out C_E have finite impedances]
    lowA --> lowB[Hybrid-pi model still valid for transistor]
    lowB --> lowC[Add capacitor impedances in series and parallel]
    lowC --> lowD[Compute lower cutoff frequency f_L using RC time constants]
    q2 -- No --> q3{Is frequency in high-band?}
    q3 -- Yes --> highA[Apply Miller Theorem to C_mu]
    highA --> highB[Compute Miller capacitance C_M equals C_mu times 1 plus A_v]
    highB --> highC[Compute upper cutoff frequency f_H]
```

## 4.3 Signal Flow: Input-to-Output Path in CE

```mermaid
graph LR
    inA[Input Signal v_s] --> proc1[Step 1 Drop across R_S and C_in]
    proc1 --> proc2[Step 2 Appears as v_be across r_pi]
    proc2 --> proc3[Step 3 Transconductance converts to current g_m times v_be]
    proc3 --> proc4[Step 4 Current flows through R_C prime producing v_o]
    proc4 --> proc5[Step 5 Output coupled via C_out to load]
    proc5 --> outA[Output v_o with 180 degree phase shift]
    proc5 --> fb1[Feedback path v_o through r_o and r_pi]
    fb1 --> proc2
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Draw the small-signal hybrid-π model of a BJT in CE configuration and label all parameters.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The hybrid-π model consists of:
1. **$r_\pi$** between internal base B' and emitter E — represents input resistance. Value: $r_\pi = \beta/g_m$.
2. **$g_m v_{b'e}$** current source from collector to emitter — represents amplification. Value: $g_m = I_C/V_T$.
3. **$r_o$** between collector and emitter — represents Early effect. Value: $r_o = V_A/I_C$.
4. **$r_x$** (optional) between external base B and internal base B' — base spreading resistance.

(1 mark for each correctly identified element with its formula, 3 marks total.)

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Why is the hybrid-π model preferred over the h-parameter model for CE configuration analysis at high frequencies?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. The h-parameter model is defined at **external terminals** and is valid only at low frequencies where the parameters are essentially real (1 mark).
2. The hybrid-π model uses an **internal node B'** which physically represents the base region, allowing the model to remain accurate when **junction capacitances** ($C_\pi$, $C_\mu$) are included (1 mark).
3. Therefore, the hybrid-π model is naturally extendable to high-frequency analysis via **Miller's Theorem**, making it the universal small-signal model (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2]`

**For a CE amplifier with the following specifications:**
- $I_C = 1.5$ mA, $\beta = 120$, $V_A = 90$ V
- $R_C = 5.6$ kΩ, $R_E = 1.2$ kΩ, $R_L = 8.2$ kΩ
- $R_1 = 39$ kΩ, $R_2 = 12$ kΩ, $R_S = 1$ kΩ
- $C_E$ is fully bypassed (mid-frequency analysis)

**Solve the following:**

**(a)** Draw the small-signal hybrid-π equivalent circuit and calculate $g_m$, $r_\pi$, and $r_o$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Find the mid-band voltage gain $A_v = v_o/v_s$, input impedance $Z_{in}$, and output impedance $Z_{out}$. Also state the phase relationship between input and output. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Step-by-step:**

**Step 1:** Calculate $g_m$:

$$g_m = \frac{I_C}{V_T} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^{-3}}{25.85 \times 10^{-3}} = 58.03 \text{ mA/V}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate $r_\pi$:

$$r_\pi = \frac{\beta}{g_m} = \frac{120}{58.03 \times 10^{-3}} = 2067.9 \text{ Ω} \approx 2.07 \text{ kΩ}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate $r_o$:

$$r_o = \frac{V_A}{I_C} = \frac{90}{1.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 60 \text{ kΩ}$$

**Step 4:** The hybrid-π model for CE has $r_\pi$ between B' and E, $g_m v_{b'e}$ from C to E, $r_o$ from C to E, and the external $R_C$, $R_L$, $R_1 \parallel R_2$, $R_E$ (bypassed) connected to the model.

(Marks distribution: 1 mark for diagram, 2 marks each for the three parameter calculations.)

**Part (b) — Step-by-step:**

**Step 1:** Effective load at collector:

$$R_C' = R_C \parallel r_o \parallel R_L = \frac{1}{\frac{1}{5600} + \frac{1}{60000} + \frac{1}{8200}}$$

$$R_C' = \frac{1}{0.0001786 + 0.0000167 + 0.0001220} = \frac{1}{0.0003172} = 3152 \text{ Ω} \approx 3.15 \text{ kΩ}$$

**Step 2:** Mid-band voltage gain (from base to collector):

$$A_{v(base)} = -g_m R_C' = -(58.03 \times 10^{-3})(3152) = -182.9$$

**Step 3:** Input impedance at the base:

$$Z_{in(base)} = r_\pi = 2.07 \text{ kΩ (since } R_E \text{ is bypassed)}$$

**Step 4:** Total input impedance including bias network:

$$R_B = R_1 \parallel R_2 = \frac{39 \times 12}{39 + 12} = \frac{468}{51} = 9.18 \text{ kΩ}$$

$$Z_{in} = R_B \parallel r_\pi = \frac{9180 \times 2070}{9180 + 2070} = \frac{19,002,600}{11,250} = 1689 \text{ Ω} \approx 1.69 \text{ kΩ}$$

**Step 5:** Overall gain from source $v_s$ to output $v_o$ (voltage divider at input):

$$\frac{v_{be}}{v_s} = \frac{Z_{in}}{Z_{in} + R_S} = \frac{1689}{1689 + 1000} = 0.628$$

$$A_v = \frac{v_o}{v_s} = A_{v(base)} \times \frac{v_{be}}{v_s} = -182.9 \times 0.628 = -114.9$$

**Step 6:** Output impedance:

$$Z_{out} = R_C \parallel r_o = \frac{5600 \times 60000}{5600 + 60000} = \frac{336,000,000}{65,600} = 5122 \text{ Ω} \approx 5.12 \text{ kΩ}$$

**Step 7:** Phase relationship: The output is **180° out of phase** with the input (due to the negative sign in $A_v$).

(Marks distribution: 2 marks for $R_C'$ and $A_{v(base)}$, 2 marks for $Z_{in}$ including source divider, 2 marks for $Z_{out}$ and phase, 1 mark for final answer with units.)

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]`

**A CE amplifier has an unbypassed emitter resistor. Given:**
- $I_C = 2$ mA, $\beta = 100$, $V_A = 100$ V
- $R_C = 3.3$ kΩ, $R_E = 500$ Ω, $R_L = 6.8$ kΩ
- $R_1 = 15$ kΩ, $R_2 = 5$ kΩ, $R_S = 600$ Ω
- $C_E$ is **removed** (unbypassed $R_E$)

**Solve the following:**

**(a)** Calculate $g_m$, $r_\pi$, $r_o$, and derive the input impedance looking into the base, $Z_{b} = r_\pi + (\beta+1)R_E$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Determine the mid-band voltage gain $A_v = v_o/v_s$, output impedance $Z_{out}$, and comment on how removing $C_E$ affects gain and bandwidth. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Step-by-step:**

**Step 1:** Calculate $g_m$:

$$g_m = \frac{I_C}{V_T} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-3}}{25.85 \times 10^{-3}} = 77.37 \text{ mA/V}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate $r_\pi$:

$$r_\pi = \frac{\beta}{g_m} = \frac{100}{77.37 \times 10^{-3}} = 1292.5 \text{ Ω} \approx 1.29 \text{ kΩ}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate $r_o$:

$$r_o = \frac{V_A}{I_C} = \frac{100}{2 \times 10^{-3}} = 50 \text{ kΩ}$$

**Step 4:** Derive $Z_b$. Apply test voltage $v_x$ at base. Emitter current $i_e = (\beta+1)i_b$. Voltage at emitter is $i_e R_E = (\beta+1)i_b R_E$.

$$v_x = i_b r_\pi + (\beta+1) i_b R_E = i_b [r_\pi + (\beta+1) R_E]$$

$$Z_b = \frac{v_x}{i_b} = r_\pi + (\beta+1) R_E = 1292.5 + 101 \times 500 = 1292.5 + 50{,}500 = 51{,}792.5 \text{ Ω} \approx 51.8 \text{ kΩ}$$

(Marks distribution: 1 mark each for $g_m$, $r_\pi$, $r_o$; 4 marks for derivation of $Z_b$.)

**Part (b) — Step-by-step:**

**Step 1:** Effective AC load:

$$R_C' = R_C \parallel r_o \parallel R_L = 3.3\text{k} \parallel 50\text{k} \parallel 6.8\text{k}$$

$$\frac{1}{R_C'} = \frac{1}{3300} + \frac{1}{50000} + \frac{1}{6800} = 0.0003030 + 0.0000200 + 0.0001471 = 0.0004701$$

$$R_C' = 2127 \text{ Ω} \approx 2.13 \text{ kΩ}$$

**Step 2:** Voltage gain from base to output:

$$A_{v(base)} = \frac{v_o}{v_{be}} = \frac{-g_m R_C'}{1 + g_m R_E} = \frac{-(77.37 \times 10^{-3})(2127)}{1 + (77.37 \times 10^{-3})(500)}$$

$$= \frac{-164.6}{1 + 38.685} = \frac{-164.6}{39.685} = -4.15$$

**Step 3:** Total input impedance:

$$R_B = R_1 \parallel R_2 = \frac{15 \times 5}{15 + 5} = 3.75 \text{ kΩ}$$

$$Z_{in} = R_B \parallel Z_b = \frac{3750 \times 51{,}792.5}{3750 + 51{,}792.5} = \frac{194,221,875}{55,542.5} = 3497 \text{ Ω} \approx 3.5 \text{ kΩ}$$

**Step 4:** Voltage divider at input:

$$\frac{v_{be}}{v_s} = \frac{Z_{in}}{Z_{in} + R_S} = \frac{3497}{3497 + 600} = 0.854$$

**Step 5:** Overall gain:

$$A_v = \frac{v_o}{v_s} = A_{v(base)} \times 0.854 = -4.15 \times 0.854 = -3.54$$

**Step 6:** Output impedance:

$$Z_{out} = R_C \parallel r_o = \frac{3300 \times 50{,}000}{3300 + 50{,}000} = 3094 \text{ Ω} \approx 3.09 \text{ kΩ}$$

**Step 7:** **Comment on removing $C_E$:** The gain drops from approximately $-g_m R_C' \approx -164.6$ (with bypass) to $-3.54$ (without bypass) — a reduction of factor $\approx 46.5$, which equals $1 + g_m R_E$. However, this trade-off brings:
- **Increased input impedance** (3.5 kΩ vs 1.13 kΩ with bypass) — better matching to high-impedance sources.
- **Improved bandwidth** (Miller effect reduced by factor $(1 + g_m R_E)$).
- **Better linearity and thermal stability** (negative feedback).

(Marks distribution: 2 marks for $A_{v(base)}$, 2 marks for $Z_{in}$ and source divider, 2 marks for $Z_{out}$ and commentary, 1 mark for final answer.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to include $r_o$ in $R_C'$:** KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly tests whether you include Early effect. Omitting $r_o$ costs 1-2 marks.
> 2. **Using $V_T = 26$ mV approximately is fine, but be consistent.** Don't mix 25 mV and 26 mV in the same problem.
> 3. **Forgetting the input voltage divider:** When the source has $R_S$, you must compute $A_v = v_o/v_s$ (not just $v_o/v_{be}$). The divider $Z_{in}/(Z_{in} + R_S)$ is often missed.
> 4. **Sign error in phase:** CE amplifier inverts (180° phase shift). Writing $|A_v|$ without the negative sign costs a mark.
> 5. **Confusing $Z_{in(base)}$ and total $Z_{in}$:** Looking into the **base** gives $r_\pi + (\beta+1)R_E$ (with unbypassed $R_E$). The **amplifier input impedance** also includes the bias network in parallel.
> 6. **For mid-frequency analysis, do NOT include** $C_\pi$, $C_\mu$ — these are high-frequency elements. The mid-band model is purely resistive.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2, Hybrid-π CE Analysis**

- [x] **Hybrid-π model** is the **physical, internal-node** small-signal model; preferred over h-parameters for CE analysis.
- [x] **Three primary parameters:** $g_m = I_C/V_T$ (transconductance), $r_\pi = \beta/g_m$ (input resistance), $r_o = V_A/I_C$ (output resistance).
- [x] **Thermal voltage** $V_T = 25.85$ mV at 300 K is a constant — always used in $g_m$ formula.
- [x] **Key identity:** $g_m \cdot r_\pi = \beta$.
- [x] **Mid-band voltage gain (bypassed $R_E$):** $A_v = -g_m(R_C \parallel r_o \parallel R_L)$.
- [x] **Mid-band voltage gain (unbypassed $R_E$):** $A_v = -g_m R_C' / (1 + g_m R_E)$.
- [x] **CE amplifier inverts** the signal (180° phase shift) — hallmark feature.
- [x] **Input impedance (no $R_E$):** $Z_{in} = R_1 \parallel R_2 \parallel r_\pi$.
- [x] **Input impedance (with $R_E$):** $Z_{in} = R_1 \parallel R_2 \parallel [r_\pi + (\beta+1)R_E]$.
- [x] **Output impedance:** $Z_{out} = R_C \parallel r_o \approx R_C$ when $r_o \gg R_C$.
- [x] **Low-frequency behavior:** Coupling and bypass capacitors create **high-pass filter** action; lower cutoff $f_L$ is determined by RC time constants involving $C_{in}$, $C_{out}$, $C_E$.
- [x] **Bypass capacitor $C_E$ short-circuits $R_E$ at mid-band**, maximizing gain but reducing stability.
- [x] **Removing $C_E$** trades gain for **bandwidth, linearity, and stability** (negative feedback).
- [x] **High-frequency analysis** requires Miller's Theorem on $C_\mu$ (covered in higher modules, not mid-band).
- [x] **Units check:** $g_m$ in A/V (or S), $r_\pi$ in Ω, $r_o$ in Ω, $V_T$ in V, $I_C$ in A.

> [!TIP]
> **One-line memory trick:** *"In CE, $g_m$ is the **g**ain **m**aker, $r_\pi$ is the **in**put, and $r_o$ is the **out**put — remember the trio!"*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: High Frequency Equivalent Circuits of BJT
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET302 - ELECTRONIC DEVICES AND CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** High Frequency Equivalent Circuits of BJT

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **High Frequency Equivalent Circuit of BJT:** A small-signal linear model of the Bipolar Junction Transistor that explicitly includes the **internal junction capacitances** (depletion-region capacitances of the reverse-biased junctions and the diffusion capacitance of the forward-biased junction), which dominate the device behavior when the operating signal frequency approaches the transistor's cutoff limits.

At **low frequencies** (typically $< 1$ MHz for general-purpose BJTs), the BJT is modeled by the simple **hybrid-parameter (h-parameter) model** containing only resistors ($h_{ie}, h_{re}, h_{fe}, h_{oe}$), because the junction capacitances present a very high reactance and are effectively open-circuited.

At **high frequencies** (tens to hundreds of MHz), the internal **depletion-layer capacitance** $C_\mu$ (also called $C_b$ or $C_{bc}$) between base and collector, and the **diffusion capacitance** $C_\pi$ (also called $C_b$ or $C_{be}$) between base and emitter, can no longer be ignored. These capacitances introduce **frequency-dependent phase shifts** and **gain roll-off**, demanding a more accurate model — the **Hybrid-π Model** developed by L.J. Giacoletto (1964).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 2):** Students must master the **Hybrid-π model**, the relationship between low-frequency $\beta$ and high-frequency parameters, the **short-circuit current gain** $A_i$, the **β-cutoff frequency** $f_\beta$, the **α-cutoff frequency** $f_\alpha$, and the **transition frequency (gain-bandwidth product)** $f_T$.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are **pouring water through a funnel** 🪔.

- At **low water pressure (low frequency)**, water flows out smoothly in proportion to what you pour in — the funnel itself is invisible. This is the low-frequency $\beta$ model: input current simply equals output current scaled by $\beta$.
- At **high water pressure (high frequency)**, the **funnel walls begin to vibrate and bulge**, absorbing some of the water energy. The walls also have a small amount of "give" (elasticity) — this is the **junction capacitance**. The more pressure you apply, the more energy the walls soak up, and less water makes it through.
- Eventually, at extremely high pressure, the funnel can no longer keep up, and the water output drops to nearly nothing. The frequency at which the output current gain drops to **1 (unity)** is called the **transition frequency $f_T$** — the absolute speed limit of that transistor.

**The Hybrid-π model is the "vibrating funnel" picture in circuit form:** it adds two capacitors ($C_\pi$ and $C_\mu$) that "siphon off" signal current at high frequencies.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | Typical Magnitude (small-signal BJT) | Unit |
|----------|--------|--------------------------------------|------|
| Thermal voltage | $V_T$ | **≈ 26 mV** at 300 K | V |
| Transconductance | $g_m$ | $\approx 1$ to 500 mA/V | A/V (S) |
| Base-emitter diffusion capacitance | $C_\pi$ | **10 pF – 1000 pF** | F |
| Base-collector depletion capacitance | $C_\mu$ | **0.5 pF – 10 pF** | F |
| Transition frequency | $f_T$ | **100 MHz – 10 GHz** | Hz |
| Low-frequency current gain | $\beta_0 \; (h_{fe})$ | **50 – 500** | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $C_\mu \ll C_\pi$?** The base-collector junction is **reverse-biased** (depletion capacitance is small), while the base-emitter junction is **forward-biased** (diffusion capacitance is large). This asymmetry is the very reason high-frequency response is dominated by $C_\pi$ but $C_\mu$ cannot be ignored due to the **Miller effect**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Hybrid-π Model — Component-by-Component Breakdown

The Hybrid-π equivalent circuit is a **two-port, small-signal, linear, lumped-parameter** model valid for the **active region** of operation. It captures both the **resistive (low-frequency)** and **capacitive (high-frequency)** behavior in a single topology.

### 2.1.1 Model Elements — Physical Origin

- **$r_\pi$ (Base-emitter dynamic resistance):** Represents the **slope of the base-emitter V-I characteristic** at the operating Q-point.
  $$r_\pi = \frac{\beta_0}{g_m} = \frac{V_T}{I_B}$$
  where $\beta_0$ is the low-frequency common-emitter current gain.

- **$g_m$ (Transconductance):** A **voltage-controlled current source** $g_m v_{be}$ modeling how the collector current responds to small changes in $v_{BE}$.
  $$g_m = \frac{I_C}{V_T} = \frac{\alpha_0}{r_e}$$
  where $r_e = V_T / I_E \approx V_T / I_C$ is the intrinsic emitter resistance.

- **$r_\mu$ (Base-collector resistance):** A very large resistance ($\approx \beta_0 r_o$) representing the **feedback** from collector to base. Usually **neglected** in simplified analysis ($\to \infty$).

- **$r_o$ (Output resistance):** Models the **finite output impedance** of the transistor due to the Early effect. Often neglected in first-order analysis ($\to \infty$).

- **$r_b$ (Base spreading resistance):** The **ohmic resistance** of the base semiconductor material between the external base terminal and the active base region. Typically **10–100 Ω**. Important at very high frequencies.

- **$C_\pi$ (Base-emitter capacitance):** Sum of the **diffusion capacitance** $C_D$ and the **emitter-base depletion capacitance** $C_{je}$.
  $$C_\pi = C_D + C_{je} = g_m \tau_F + C_{je}$$
  where $\tau_F$ is the **forward transit time** (≈ 0.1 – 10 ns for modern BJTs).

- **$C_\mu$ (Base-collector capacitance):** The **depletion-layer capacitance** of the reverse-biased collector-base junction. Strongly dependent on the reverse-bias voltage $V_{CB}$.

### 2.1.2 Simplified Hybrid-π Model (Most Common Form for KTU)

For textbook analysis, we assume $r_\mu \to \infty$, $r_o \to \infty$, and $r_b \to 0$ (or include $r_b$ only for very-high-frequency problems). The result is a clean, three-terminal model with three reactive elements: $r_\pi$, $C_\pi$, $C_\mu$.

---

## 2.2 Frequency Response of Common-Emitter Current Gain

When a **short-circuit load** ($R_L = 0$) is placed at the output, the **output current** is simply the current through the dependent source. The **short-circuit current gain** is:

$$A_{is} = \frac{i_c}{i_b}$$

Because $C_\mu$ and $r_\pi$ form a **frequency-dependent voltage divider** across $v_{be}$, the gain rolls off with frequency.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The single-pole roll-off assumption** gives the standard first-order result used throughout KTU problems. Real BJTs have multiple poles, but for the 2024 syllabus, the **single-pole approximation** is sufficient.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $g_m = I_C / V_T$ | Transconductance | A/V (S) |
| 2 | $r_\pi = \beta_0 / g_m$ | Base-emitter input resistance | Ω |
| 3 | $r_e = V_T / I_E \approx V_T / I_C$ | Intrinsic emitter resistance | Ω |
| 4 | $g_m = \alpha_0 / r_e$ | Transconductance-emitter link | A/V |
| 5 | $A_{is} = \dfrac{\beta_0}{1 + j(f/f_\beta)}$ | Short-circuit CE current gain | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\vert A_{is} \vert = \dfrac{\beta_0}{\sqrt{1 + (f/f_\beta)^2}}$ | Magnitude of $A_{is}$ | dimensionless |
| 7 | $f_\beta = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \, r_\pi (C_\pi + C_\mu)}$ | β-cutoff (-3 dB) frequency | Hz |
| 8 | $f_T = \beta_0 \, f_\beta$ | Transition frequency (gain-BW product) | Hz |
| 9 | $f_T = \dfrac{g_m}{2\pi (C_\pi + C_\mu)}$ | $f_T$ in terms of device capacitances | Hz |
| 10 | $f_\alpha \approx f_T$ | α-cutoff frequency (for $\alpha_0 \approx 1$) | Hz |
| 11 | $C_M = C_\mu (1 + g_m R_L)$ | Miller capacitance (input side) | F |
| 12 | $A_v \approx -g_m R_L$ | Midband CE voltage gain | dimensionless |
| 13 | $C_{in} = C_\pi + C_M$ | Total input capacitance | F |
| 14 | $f_H \approx \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_{sig} C_{in}}$ | Upper -3 dB frequency | Hz |
| 15 | $\tau_F$ = forward transit time | Carrier transit base time | s |
| 16 | $C_\pi = g_m \tau_F + C_{je}$ | $C_\pi$ decomposition | F |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The Hybrid-π model is the **workhorse of RF and microwave amplifier design**, broadband amplifier compensation, video amplifier design, oscilloscope front-ends, and high-speed digital logic (TTL, ECL). The transition frequency $f_T$ is the **Figure of Merit** quoted on every BJT datasheet.
