topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET302 - ELECTRONIC DEVICES AND CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Miller Effect

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 2 - BJT/FET High Frequency Response)**
> The **Miller Effect** describes the multiplication of the effective input capacitance of an inverting voltage amplifier due to the amplification factor and the feedback capacitance (Miller Capacitance, $C_M$) connected between the input and output terminals. This phenomenon drastically limits the high-frequency response of multistage amplifiers.

---

## 🧠 1.1 What is the Miller Effect? (The Plain-English Analogy)

Imagine you are pushing a child on a **swing** (this swing is the *output node*). You push the child, and the swing goes up. However, the child has a **rubber band tied to your hand** (the rubber band is the *feedback capacitance*). 

- When the swing goes forward, it **pulls your hand forward** with a multiplied force.
- To *you* (the *input node*), it feels as though the swing is **heavier than it actually is**. 
- This "apparent heaviness" at the input is exactly what happens in an amplifier with a feedback capacitor. The tiny feedback capacitor, when combined with the voltage gain, **appears as a much larger capacitance at the input**.

In an electronic amplifier, a small capacitance $C_f$ between input and output is **multiplied by the voltage gain** at the input, becoming a much larger effective input capacitance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exam:** The Miller Effect is the primary reason why the **dominant pole** in a BJT/FET amplifier shifts to lower frequencies, degrading the high-frequency response. Without understanding Miller's Theorem, high-frequency amplifier design is impossible.

---

## 🧮 1.2 Formal Mathematical Definition

Consider an inverting voltage amplifier with voltage gain $A_v$ and an impedance $Z_f$ connected between input ($V_{in}$) and output ($V_{out}$). The Miller Effect states that:

$$C_{in(Miller)} = C_f \cdot (1 - A_v) \approx -A_v \cdot C_f \quad \text{(for \vert A_v \vert >> 1)}$$

For a typical inverting common-emitter amplifier, $A_v$ is **negative**, making $C_{in(Miller)}$ very large and positive.

| Symbol | Meaning | Standard Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $C_M$ | Miller Capacitance (apparent input capacitance) | Farads (F) |
| $C_f$ | Physical feedback/parasitic capacitance | Farads (F) |
| $A_v$ | Mid-band voltage gain of the amplifier | Dimensionless |

---

## 🎯 1.3 Why Does the Miller Effect Matter?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Reality Check:** 
> The Miller Effect is a **parasitic phenomenon** that engineers must actively combat. It is responsible for:
> 1. **Bandwidth Reduction** in amplifiers
> 2. **Reduced slew rate** in operational amplifiers
> 3. **Dominant pole limitation** in multistage designs
> 4. **Design challenges** in RF and high-speed digital circuits (e.g., cascode amplifiers are used to eliminate it!)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 🔬 2.1 Miller's Theorem (The Mathematical Foundation)

Miller's Theorem is the bedrock concept that explains the Miller Effect. It states:

> **"A floating impedance $Z_f$ connected between input and output of an inverting amplifier can be replaced by two grounded impedances: one at the input ($Z_1$) and one at the output ($Z_2$)."**

### 2.1.1 Statement of Miller's Theorem

Let the input node voltage be $V_1$ and the output node voltage be $V_2$. The voltage gain is:
$$A_v = \frac{V_2}{V_1}$$

The current flowing through the floating impedance $Z_f$ from input to output is:
$$I_1 = \frac{V_1 - V_2}{Z_f} = \frac{V_1 - A_v V_1}{Z_f} = V_1 \cdot \frac{1 - A_v}{Z_f}$$

By **Ohm's Law**, the input impedance seen looking into $Z_f$ from node 1 is:
$$Z_1 = \frac{V_1}{I_1} = \frac{Z_f}{1 - A_v}$$

Similarly, the output impedance seen looking into $Z_f$ from node 2 is:
$$Z_2 = \frac{Z_f}{1 - \frac{1}{A_v}} = \frac{Z_f \cdot A_v}{A_v - 1}$$

---

## 📊 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **This table is the ONLY thing you need for solving 90% of Miller Effect problems in KTU University Exams.**

| **Formula** | **Expression** | **Physical Meaning** | **Condition** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Miller's Theorem (Input) | $Z_1 = \dfrac{Z_f}{1 - A_v}$ | Equivalent input impedance | General case |
| Miller's Theorem (Output) | $Z_2 = \dfrac{Z_f \cdot A_v}{A_v - 1}$ | Equivalent output impedance | General case |
| Miller Capacitance | $C_M = C_f \cdot (1 - A_v)$ | Effective input capacitance | Inverting amplifier |
| Simplified Miller Capacitance | $C_M \approx C_f \cdot \vert A_v \vert$ | Approximation for $\vert A_v \vert \gg 1$ | High-gain amplifiers |
| Dominant Pole Frequency | $f_H = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_{th} (C_{in} + C_{M})}$ | Upper cutoff frequency | After Miller multiplication |
| Input RC Time Constant | $\tau_{in} = R_S \cdot (C_{in} + C_M)$ | High-frequency time constant | Using open-circuit TC method |

---

## 🏗️ 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where is the Miller Effect observed in industry-grade designs?**
> - **BJT Common-Emitter (CE) Amplifiers**: The base-collector junction capacitance ($C_\mu$ or $C_{bc}$) is the primary Miller capacitance. This is the **dominant pole contributor** in CE stages.
> - **FET Common-Source (CS) Amplifiers**: The gate-drain capacitance ($C_{gd}$ or $C_{rss}$) creates the Miller Effect.
> - **Operational Amplifiers**: Internal compensation capacitors exploit the Miller Effect to achieve dominant pole compensation.
> - **Cascode Amplifier**: Specifically designed to **eliminate the Miller Effect** by shielding the input device from voltage swings at the output.
> - **Wideband Amplifiers**: Use techniques like **neutralization** (adding a cancelling capacitor) to negate Miller multiplication.

---

## 🧠 2.4 The Open-Circuit Time Constant (OCTC) Method

In KTU 2024 Scheme, the high-frequency response is analyzed using the **Miller Approximation / OCTC method**. The steps are:

1. Identify all capacitances (intrinsic + external).
2. Replace the dependent source with its internal resistance to find Thevenin resistance at each capacitor node.
3. **Multiply the input-side feedback capacitance** by $(1 - A_v)$ to get $C_M$.
4. Sum all parallel capacitances at the input to get total input capacitance: $C_{T} = C_{in} + C_M$.
5. Calculate upper cutoff: $f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{th} C_T}$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 📐 3.1 Detailed Derivation of Miller's Theorem

### Step 1: Define the Network

Consider a two-port network with:
- Input node at voltage $V_1$
- Output node at voltage $V_2$  
- A floating impedance $Z_f$ connecting input to output
- A voltage amplifier with gain $A_v = \frac{V_2}{V_1}$

### Step 2: Express the Current Through $Z_f$

The current flowing from input to output through $Z_f$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_1 &= \frac{V_1 - V_2}{Z_f} \\
    &= \frac{V_1 - A_v V_1}{Z_f} \\
    &= V_1 \cdot \frac{1 - A_v}{Z_f}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** We substituted $V_2 = A_v V_1$ because the output voltage is related to the input voltage through the amplifier gain.

### Step 3: Derive Input Equivalent Impedance

The impedance seen at the input (between node 1 and ground) looking only into $Z_f$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_1 &= \frac{V_1}{I_1} = \frac{V_1}{V_1 \cdot \frac{1 - A_v}{Z_f}} \\
    &= \frac{Z_f}{1 - A_v}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** This is the **Miller's Theorem** for the input side.

### Step 4: Derive Output Equivalent Impedance

By symmetry, the current flowing *out* of the output node through $Z_f$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_2 &= \frac{V_2 - V_1}{Z_f} = \frac{A_v V_1 - V_1}{Z_f} \\
    &= V_1 \cdot \frac{A_v - 1}{Z_f} = \frac{V_2}{A_v} \cdot \frac{A_v - 1}{Z_f} \\
    &= V_2 \cdot \frac{A_v - 1}{A_v \cdot Z_f}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, the output-side equivalent impedance is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_2 &= \frac{V_2}{I_2} = \frac{V_2}{V_2 \cdot \frac{A_v - 1}{A_v \cdot Z_f}} \\
    &= \frac{Z_f \cdot A_v}{A_v - 1}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** The output side sees a scaled version of $Z_f$, scaled by the gain factor.

---

## 📐 3.2 Derivation of Miller Capacitance

### Step 1: Apply Miller's Theorem to a Capacitor

If $Z_f$ is a pure capacitor with impedance $Z_f = \frac{1}{j\omega C_f}$, then the input-side equivalent impedance is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_1 &= \frac{Z_f}{1 - A_v} = \frac{1}{j\omega C_f (1 - A_v)} = \frac{1}{j\omega C_{M,input}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Identify Miller Capacitance

By comparing the form $\frac{1}{j\omega C}$, the input-side equivalent capacitance is:

$$
\boxed{C_{M,input} = C_f \cdot (1 - A_v)}
$$

### Step 3: Specialization for Inverting Amplifier

For a common-emitter or common-source stage, $A_v$ is **negative**. Let $A_v = -|A_v|$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{M,input} &= C_f \cdot (1 - (-|A_v|)) \\
            &= C_f \cdot (1 + |A_v|) \\
            &\approx C_f \cdot |A_v| \quad \text{(for } |A_v| \gg 1\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** This is why a tiny $C_\mu$ (e.g., 5 pF) with a gain of -100 appears as a **505 pF** capacitance at the input!

---

## 📐 3.3 Worked Example: Finding the Upper Cutoff Frequency

**Problem:** A common-emitter amplifier has the following parameters:
- Source resistance $R_S = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$
- Base-emitter capacitance $C_\pi = 10 \text{ pF}$
- Base-collector capacitance $C_\mu = 2 \text{ pF}$
- Mid-band voltage gain $A_v = -100$

**Step 1: Calculate Miller Capacitance**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_M &= C_\mu \cdot (1 - A_v) = 2 \text{ pF} \cdot (1 - (-100)) \\
    &= 2 \text{ pF} \cdot 101 = 202 \text{ pF}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate Total Input Capacitance**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{T} &= C_\pi + C_M = 10 \text{ pF} + 202 \text{ pF} = 212 \text{ pF}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate Upper Cutoff Frequency**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_H &= \frac{1}{2\pi R_S C_T} = \frac{1}{2\pi \cdot (1 \times 10^3) \cdot (212 \times 10^{-12})} \\
    &= \frac{1}{2\pi \cdot 2.12 \times 10^{-7}} \\
    &= \frac{1}{1.332 \times 10^{-6}} \\
    &= 750.6 \text{ kHz}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation:** Notice how the original 2 pF capacitor became effectively 202 pF — a 101× multiplication! If the amplifier had a gain of -1000, the Miller capacitance would be ~2000 pF, and the bandwidth would collapse to ~80 kHz. **This is why the Miller Effect is the bandwidth killer in amplifiers!**

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 🗺️ 4.1 Miller Effect Functional Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the operational flow of how a small feedback capacitance gets transformed into a large effective input capacitance through the Miller Effect.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Voltage Source V_in] --> B[Source Resistance R_S]
    B --> C[Input Node of Amplifier]
    C --> D[Intrinsic Input Capacitance C_in]
    C --> E[Gain Stage with Voltage Gain A_v]
    E --> F[Output Node V_out = A_v * V_in]
    C --> G[Feedback Capacitance C_f between Input and Output]
    F --> G
    G -->|Miller Theorem Applied| H[Equivalent Input Capacitance C_M = C_f * 1 - A_v]
    D --> I[Total Input Capacitance C_T = C_in + C_M]
    H --> I
    I --> J[RC Low-Pass Filter Formation]
    B --> J
    J --> K[Upper Cutoff Frequency f_H = 1 divided by 2 pi R_S C_T]
    K --> L[Bandwidth Limitation at High Frequencies]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** Follow the arrows from top (input source) to bottom (bandwidth limitation). The key transformation happens at node G, where the small feedback capacitance $C_f$ becomes a multiplied $C_M$ at the input due to the voltage gain $A_v$.

---

## 🔄 4.2 Miller's Theorem Transformation Diagram

This second diagram explicitly shows how the **floating impedance $Z_f$** gets split into **two grounded impedances** using Miller's Theorem.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BEFORE[Before Miller Theorem]
        B1[Input Node V1] -->|Z_f| B2[Output Node V2]
    end
    subgraph AFTER[After Miller Theorem]
        A1[Input Node V1] -->|Z1 = Z_f divided by 1 - A_v| A3[Ground]
        A2[Output Node V2] -->|Z2 = Z_f A_v divided by A_v - 1| A4[Ground]
    end
    BEFORE ==> AFTER
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "apply Miller's Theorem," you must show BOTH transformations — the input-side equivalent AND the output-side equivalent. Marks are split between these two derivations.

---

## 📊 4.3 High-Frequency Response Comparison: With vs Without Miller Effect

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Amplifier Stage] --> Q{Miller Capacitance Exists?}
    Q -->|Yes - CE/CS Stage| R[C_in = C_pi + C_mu * 1 - A_v]
    Q -->|No - Cascode or Buffer| S[C_in = C_pi only]
    R --> T[Bandwidth f_H = 1 over 2 pi R C_in = LOW]
    S --> U[Bandwidth f_H = 1 over 2 pi R C_in = HIGH]
    T --> V[Bandwidth Limited Design]
    U --> W[Wideband Design]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📝 PART A: 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: Define Miller's Theorem. Why is it useful in amplifier analysis?**

**Model Answer:**
Miller's Theorem states that a floating impedance $Z_f$ connected between the input and output of an inverting voltage amplifier with gain $A_v$ can be replaced by two equivalent impedances to ground: 
- $Z_1 = \frac{Z_f}{1 - A_v}$ at the input 
- $Z_2 = \frac{Z_f \cdot A_v}{A_v - 1}$ at the output.

**Usefulness:** It allows the input and output circuits to be analyzed independently, simplifying the calculation of high-frequency response and the upper cutoff frequency $f_H$ of multistage amplifiers.

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: What is the Miller Effect? How does it affect the high-frequency response of a BJT common-emitter amplifier?**

**Model Answer:**
The Miller Effect is the phenomenon where a capacitance $C_\mu$ between the input (base) and output (collector) of an inverting amplifier appears as a much larger capacitance $C_M = C_\mu(1 - A_v)$ at the input. 

**Effect on high-frequency response:** The effective input capacitance increases dramatically, reducing the upper cutoff frequency $f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi R_S C_T}$ where $C_T = C_\pi + C_M$. This severely limits the bandwidth of the amplifier.

---

## 📝 PART B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - 2024 Pattern]

**Q: (a)** Derive Miller's Theorem for a floating impedance connected between the input and output of an inverting amplifier. **(7 marks)**

**Q: (b)** For a common-emitter amplifier with mid-band gain $A_v = -120$, source resistance $R_S = 2 \text{ k}\Omega$, $C_\pi = 15 \text{ pF}$, and $C_\mu = 3 \text{ pF}$, calculate the Miller capacitance, total input capacitance, and the upper cutoff frequency using Miller's approximation. **(7 marks)**

---

#### 📖 Model Solution for Question A(a): Derivation of Miller's Theorem

**Step 1:** Let the input node be at potential $V_1$ and the output node at potential $V_2$. The voltage gain is $A_v = \frac{V_2}{V_1}$.

**Step 2:** The current flowing through the floating impedance $Z_f$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_1 &= \frac{V_1 - V_2}{Z_f} = \frac{V_1 - A_v V_1}{Z_f} = V_1 \left(\frac{1 - A_v}{Z_f}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** The input-side equivalent impedance is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_1 &= \frac{V_1}{I_1} = \frac{Z_f}{1 - A_v}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Similarly, the current flowing from the output side is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_2 &= \frac{V_2 - V_1}{Z_f} = V_2 \left(\frac{A_v - 1}{A_v \cdot Z_f}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** The output-side equivalent impedance is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_2 &= \frac{V_2}{I_2} = \frac{Z_f \cdot A_v}{A_v - 1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The floating impedance $Z_f$ is replaced by $Z_1$ at the input and $Z_2$ at the output, both referenced to ground. **(7 marks awarded for complete derivation)**

---

#### 📖 Model Solution for Question A(b): Numerical Calculation

**Given:** $A_v = -120$, $R_S = 2 \text{ k}\Omega$, $C_\pi = 15 \text{ pF}$, $C_\mu = 3 \text{ pF}$

**Step 1: Calculate Miller Capacitance**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_M &= C_\mu (1 - A_v) = 3 \text{ pF} \cdot (1 - (-120)) \\
    &= 3 \text{ pF} \cdot 121 = 363 \text{ pF}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate Total Input Capacitance**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_T &= C_\pi + C_M = 15 \text{ pF} + 363 \text{ pF} = 378 \text{ pF}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate Upper Cutoff Frequency**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_H &= \frac{1}{2\pi R_S C_T} = \frac{1}{2\pi \cdot (2 \times 10^3) \cdot (378 \times 10^{-12})} \\
    &= \frac{1}{4.7496 \times 10^{-6}} \\
    &= 210.5 \text{ kHz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answers:** $C_M = 363 \text{ pF}$, $C_T = 378 \text{ pF}$, $f_H \approx 210.5 \text{ kHz}$ **(7 marks for full calculation with units)**

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]

**Q: (a)** Explain the concept of the Miller Effect with reference to a common-emitter amplifier. Derive the expression for the Miller capacitance. **(7 marks)**

**Q: (b)** A common-source FET amplifier has $g_m = 5 \text{ mA/V}$, $R_D = 4 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_S = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$, $C_{gs} = 8 \text{ pF}$, and $C_{gd} = 2 \text{ pF}$. Calculate (i) the mid-band gain, (ii) the Miller capacitance, and (iii) the upper cutoff frequency. **(7 marks)**

---

#### 📖 Model Solution for Question B(a): Conceptual Explanation

**Conceptual Explanation:** In a common-emitter BJT amplifier, the base-collector junction capacitance $C_\mu$ appears as a feedback element between input (base) and output (collector). Since the output voltage is the input voltage multiplied by the gain $A_v$ (with phase inversion), the small physical capacitor $C_\mu$ behaves as a much larger capacitor at the input.

**Derivation of Miller Capacitance:**

The impedance of $C_\mu$ is $Z_f = \frac{1}{j\omega C_\mu}$. By Miller's Theorem, the equivalent input impedance is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_1 &= \frac{Z_f}{1 - A_v} = \frac{1}{j\omega C_\mu (1 - A_v)} = \frac{1}{j\omega C_M}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, the **Miller Capacitance** is:

$$
\boxed{C_M = C_\mu (1 - A_v)}
$$

For $A_v$ negative (inverting CE stage), $C_M \approx C_\mu \cdot \vert A_v \vert$ when $\vert A_v \vert \gg 1$. **(7 marks for explanation + derivation)**

---

#### 📖 Model Solution for Question B(b): FET Amplifier Numerical

**Given:** $g_m = 5 \text{ mA/V}$, $R_D = 4 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_S = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$, $C_{gs} = 8 \text{ pF}$, $C_{gd} = 2 \text{ pF}$

**Step 1: Mid-band Voltage Gain**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_v &= -g_m R_D = -(5 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot (4 \times 10^3) = -20
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Miller Capacitance**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_M &= C_{gd} (1 - A_v) = 2 \text{ pF} \cdot (1 - (-20)) \\
    &= 2 \text{ pF} \cdot 21 = 42 \text{ pF}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Total Input Capacitance**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_T &= C_{gs} + C_M = 8 \text{ pF} + 42 \text{ pF} = 50 \text{ pF}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Upper Cutoff Frequency**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_H &= \frac{1}{2\pi R_S C_T} = \frac{1}{2\pi \cdot (1 \times 10^3) \cdot (50 \times 10^{-12})} \\
    &= \frac{1}{3.1416 \times 10^{-7}} \\
    &= 3.183 \text{ MHz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answers:** (i) $A_v = -20$, (ii) $C_M = 42 \text{ pF}$, (iii) $f_H \approx 3.18 \text{ MHz}$ **(7 marks for complete numerical solution)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign Error in Miller Capacitance:** Students often write $C_M = C_f(1 + A_v)$ instead of $C_M = C_f(1 - A_v)$. Always remember the formula has a **MINUS** sign.
> 2. **Forgetting to Add $C_\pi$:** The total input capacitance is $C_T = C_\pi + C_M$, not just $C_M$. Missing $C_\pi$ costs 1-2 marks.
> 3. **Units Mistake:** Capacitance must be in **Farads** (not pF) when using $f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$. Convert: $1 \text{ pF} = 10^{-12} \text{ F}$. This is the **#1 calculation error** in KTU scripts.
> 4. **Not Specifying "Inverting Amplifier":** Miller's Theorem applies only to **inverting amplifiers** with $A_v < 0$. For non-inverting stages, the formula changes.
> 5. **Ignoring $R_L$ in Gain:** In actual CE amplifiers, the gain is $A_v = -g_m(R_C \vert\vert R_L)$. Many students forget to include $R_L$ in parallel with $R_C$.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **⚡ Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2 - Miller Effect:**

- ✅ **Miller's Theorem** replaces a floating impedance $Z_f$ with two grounded impedances: $Z_1 = \frac{Z_f}{1-A_v}$ and $Z_2 = \frac{Z_f A_v}{A_v-1}$.
- ✅ **Miller Capacitance Formula:** $C_M = C_f(1 - A_v)$. For inverting amplifiers with large $\vert A_v \vert$, $C_M \approx C_f \cdot \vert A_v \vert$.
- ✅ **Total Input Capacitance:** $C_T = C_\pi + C_M$ (BJT) or $C_T = C_{gs} + C_M$ (FET).
- ✅ **Upper Cutoff Frequency:** $f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{th} C_T}$, where $R_{th}$ is the Thevenin resistance at the input node.
- ✅ **The Miller Effect INCREASES input capacitance**, REDUCING bandwidth — this is the **dominant pole** in CE/CS amplifiers.
- ✅ **Cascode amplifier** is the standard solution to **eliminate the Miller Effect** by shielding the input transistor.
- ✅ **Miller's Theorem is valid ONLY for inverting amplifiers** ($A_v < 0$). For non-inverting stages, the multiplication factor becomes $(1 + A_v)$.
- ✅ **Source resistance $R_S$** is the primary Thevenin resistance at the input; the upper cutoff is inversely proportional to $R_S$.
- ✅ **Practical implication:** A 2 pF feedback capacitor with $A_v = -100$ looks like **202 pF** at the input — a **101× multiplication**.
- ✅ **KTU exam weightage:** Miller Effect + High-Frequency Response is a guaranteed 14-mark question in Part B of Module 2.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analysis of High Frequency Response of CE Amplifier
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAET302 - ELECTRONIC DEVICES AND CIRCUITS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Analysis of High Frequency Response of CE Amplifier # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **High Frequency Response of CE Amplifier:** It is the analysis of the gain–frequency behaviour of a Common Emitter (CE) amplifier in the region where the **internal (parasitic) capacitances** of the BJT — namely the base–emitter diffusion capacitance $C_{b'e}$ and the base–collector depletion capacitance $C_{b'c}$ — dominate and cause the mid-band gain to roll off. The frequency at which the gain falls to $1/\sqrt{2}$ (or $-3\text{ dB}$) of its mid-band value is called the **upper cutoff frequency, $f_H$**. In the high-frequency region, the coupling and bypass capacitors behave as **short circuits** (since $X_C = \frac{1}{2\pi f C}$ becomes very small), so they can be removed from the equivalent circuit. However, the transistor's internal capacitances — which were ignorable at low and mid frequencies — now act as **low-impedance shunts** and become the dominant frequency-determining elements. > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The analysis uses the **hybrid-$\pi$ model** of the BJT together with **Miller's Theorem** to reduce the feedback capacitance $C_{b'c}$ into an equivalent input-side Miller capacitance. The result is a single-pole low-pass response from which $f_H$ is obtained. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy Think of the CE amplifier as a **water pipeline system**: - The **mid-band signal** is a smooth, steady flow of water — it passes through easily because the pipe is wide open. - As you try to pump water faster and faster (i.e., increase the **signal frequency**), the **internal "valves and springs"** inside the transistor (the capacitances $C_{b'e}$ and $C_{b'c}$) start resisting the sudden changes. - The diffusion capacitance $C_{b'e}$ acts like a **shock absorber** at the input — it doesn't want the base voltage to change rapidly. - The depletion capacitance $C_{b'c}$ acts like a **leaky valve** that lets some signal "feed back" from output to input — this is the **Miller effect**, and it dramatically multiplies the apparent size of $C_{b'c}$. The moment the input signal oscillates too fast for these "springs" to follow, the gain begins to **droop**. The frequency at which the gain has dropped by **30 %** (i.e., $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707$) is the **upper 3-dB frequency, $f_H$**. ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics > [!NOTE] > - The standard unit of capacitance in such analyses is the **picofarad (pF)** or **femtofarad (fF)**, since internal BJT capacitances are extremely small. > - The standard cutoff metric is **$-3\text{ dB}$**, corresponding to a power ratio of $\frac{1}{2}$ or voltage ratio of $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$. > - The **transit frequency / gain–bandwidth product, $f_T$**, is treated as a **device constant** for a given BJT. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Why Is High-Frequency Analysis Necessary? At **low frequencies**, the reactance of $C_{b'e}$ and $C_{b'c}$ is very high (open circuit), so the amplifier behaves linearly with constant mid-band gain $A_{mid}$. At **high frequencies**, the reactance $\frac{1}{2\pi f C}$ becomes small: $$ X_{C_{b'e}} = \frac{1}{2\pi f \, C_{b'e}} \quad , \quad X_{C_{b'c}} = \frac{1}{2\pi f \, C_{b'c}} $$ These small reactances **shunt the signal currents** away from the load, reducing the gain. Thus the gain becomes **frequency-dependent** and rolls off above the upper cutoff $f_H$. ## 2.2 The High-Frequency Hybrid-$\pi$ Model of the CE Amplifier At high frequencies, the coupling and emitter-bypass capacitors are replaced by short circuits. The transistor is represented by its **hybrid-$\pi$ model** with two internal capacitances: - $r_{b'e}$ : base–emitter dynamic resistance - $r_{b'b}$ : base-spreading resistance (often lumped with source resistance $R_S$) - $g_m \, v_{b'e}$ : voltage-controlled current source - $C_{b'e}$ : base–emitter diffusion capacitance (order of pF to nF) - $C_{b'c}$ : base–collector depletion capacitance (order of pF) The small-signal CE amplifier circuit (with biasing resistors $R_1, R_2$, collector resistor $R_C$, emitter resistor $R_E$ bypassed by $C_E$, and source $V_S$ with source resistance $R_S$) becomes — at high frequencies — the **hybrid-$\pi$ equivalent circuit**. ## 2.3 Miller's Theorem > [!IMPORTANT] > **Miller's Theorem** states that an impedance $Z$ connected between input node and output node of an amplifier (with voltage gain $K = \frac{V_o}{V_i}$) can be replaced by: > - An impedance $Z_1 = \frac{Z}{1-K}$ from input to ground > - An impedance $Z_2 = \frac{Z}{1 - \frac{1}{K}}$ from output to ground Applied to the feedback capacitance $C_{b'c}$ (impedance $Z = \frac{1}{s C_{b'c}}$) with gain $K = -g_m R_L'$: $$ Z_1 = \frac{Z}{1 - K} = \frac{1}{s C_{b'c}(1 + g_m R_L')} \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_M = C_{b'c}(1 + g_m R_L') $$ This is the **Miller capacitance** — a huge multiplication of $C_{b'c}$ that dominates the input side. > [!NOTE] > The output-side Miller capacitance $C_{b'c}\left(1 + \frac{1}{g_m R_L'}\right) \approx C_{b'c}$ is usually small and often neglected in finding $f_H$. ## 2.4 Step-by-Step Analytical Logic **Step 1:** Identify the source resistance seen by the input node of the hybrid-$\pi$ circuit. $$ R_S' = R_S \parallel R_B \parallel r_{b'e} $$ where $R_B = R_1 \parallel R_2$ is the Thevenin resistance of the bias network. **Step 2:** Apply **Miller's Theorem** to reflect $C_{b'c}$ to the input side. $$ C_M = C_{b'c}(1 + g_m R_L') $$ **Step 3:** Combine all input-side capacitances in parallel. $$ C_{in} = C_{b'e} + C_M = C_{b'e} + C_{b'c}(1 + g_m R_L') $$ **Step 4:** Compute the input time constant. $$ \tau_S = R_S' \cdot C_{in} $$ **Step 5:** The upper 3-dB frequency is the inverse of this time constant. $$ f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi \, \tau_S} = \frac{1}{2\pi \, R_S' \, \left[C_{b'e} + C_{b'c}(1 + g_m R_L')\right]} $$ **Step 6:** For finding the dominant pole, neglect the small output-side time constant, or combine both if high accuracy is required (often called the **Miller approximation** vs the **exact two-pole analysis**). ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes | |---|----------|---------|---------------| | 1 | Input-side source resistance | $R_S' = R_S \parallel R_B \parallel r_{b'e}$ | $\Omega$ | | 2 | Effective load at collector | $R_L' = R_C \parallel R_L$ | $\Omega$ | | 3 | Base–emitter dynamic resistance | $r_{b'e} = (1 + \beta) \frac{V_T}{I_E} = \frac{\beta}{g_m}$ | $\Omega$ | | 4 | Transconductance | $g_m = \frac{I_C}{V_T} = \frac{\beta}{r_{b'e}}$ | S (Siemens) or A/V | | 5 | Miller Capacitance | $C_M = C_{b'c}\,(1 + g_m R_L')$ | F (farads) | | 6 | Total input capacitance | $C_{in} = C_{b'e} + C_{b'c}(1 + g_m R_L')$ | F | | 7 | Input time constant | $\tau_S = R_S' \cdot C_{in}$ | seconds | | 8 | Upper 3-dB frequency (Miller approx.) | $f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi R_S' [C_{b'e} + C_{b'c}(1 + g_m R_L')]}$ | Hz | | 9 | $\beta$-cutoff frequency | $f_\beta = \frac{1}{2\pi r_{b'e}(C_{b'e} + C_{b'c})}$ | Hz | | 10 | Transistor current gain with $\beta$ roll-off | $\beta(s) = \frac{\beta_0}{1 + s/f_\beta}$ (in Hz: $\beta(f) = \frac{\beta_0}{\sqrt{1 + (f/f_\beta)^2}}$) | dimensionless | | 11 | Short-circuit CE current gain | $A_{is}(s) = \frac{\beta_0}{1 + s \beta_0 (C_{b'e} + C_{b'c}) / g_m}$ | dimensionless | | 12 | Transit / Gain-Bandwidth frequency | $f_T = \beta_0 \, f_\beta = \frac{g_m}{2\pi (C_{b'e} + C_{b'c})}$ | Hz — **device constant** | | 13 | Current gain with load $R_L$ | $A_i(s) = \frac{-\beta_0 R_C / (R_C + R_L)}{1 + s \, r_{b'e} [C_{b'e} + C_{b'c}(1 + g_m R_L')]}$ | dimensionless | | 14 | 3-dB frequency with load $R_L$ (using $R_S'$) | $f_H \approx \frac{1}{2\pi R_S' [C_{b'e} + C_{b'c}(1 + g_m R_L')]}$ | Hz | | 15 | Voltage gain magnitude (high-freq) | $\vert A_v(f) \vert = \frac{A_{mid}}{\sqrt{1 + (f/f_H)^2}}$ | dimensionless | | 16 | Gain-Bandwidth Product | $GBP = \vert A_{mid} \vert \cdot f_H = \text{constant} = f_T$ (approx.) | Hz | ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **RF & Microwave Design:** $f_H$ determines the usable bandwidth of tuned RF amplifiers, broadband video amplifiers, and oscilloscope front-ends. - **High-Speed Digital Logic:** The CE switching speed is limited by $f_T$; this is critical in designing ECL and high-speed TTL gates. - **Op-Amp Internal Stages:** Every op-amp contains a CE (or its FET equivalent CS) high-gain stage whose $f_H$ sets the op-amp's dominant pole and hence its GBW. - **Communication Systems:** Video amplifiers in TV/oscilloscopes and IF amplifiers in superheterodyne receivers depend critically on bandwidth-extension techniques (e.g., cascode, neutralisation) that increase $f_H$. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Short-Circuit Current Gain $A_{is}(s)$ > [!NOTE] > We start from the **short-circuit** hybrid-$\pi$ model — i.e., the output is shorted ($R_L' = 0$). This is the standard way to find the intrinsic high-frequency $\beta$ roll-off of the BJT. **Starting point:** Apply KCL at the output node with $R_L' = 0$. The output current $I_o$ flows through the short. $$ I_i = \frac{V_{b'e}}{r_{b'e}} + s C_{b'e} V_{b'e} + s C_{b'c}(V_{b'e} - V_o) $$ $$ I_o = g_m V_{b'e} + s C_{b'c}(V_o - V_{b'e}) $$ **Step (a) — Use output short circuit $V_o = 0$:** With $V_o = 0$: $$ I_i = V_{b'e}\left[\frac{1}{r_{b'e}} + s(C_{b'e} + C_{b'c})\right] $$ $$ I_o = (g_m - s C_{b'c}) \, V_{b'e} \approx g_m V_{b'e} \quad \text{(since } s C_{b'c} \ll g_m \text{ at low/mid freqs)} $$ **Step (b) — Form the current gain:** $$ A_{is}(s) = \frac{I_o}{I_i} = \frac{g_m V_{b'e}}{V_{b'e}\left[\dfrac{1}{r_{b'e}} + s(C_{b'e} + C_{b'c})\right]} $$ **Step (c) — Simplify using $g_m r_{b'e} = \beta_0$:** $$ A_{is}(s) = \frac{g_m}{\dfrac{1}{r_{b'e}} + s(C_{b'e} + C_{b'c})} $$ Dividing numerator and denominator by $\frac{1}{r_{b'e}}$: $$ A_{is}(s) = \frac{g_m r_{b'e}}{1 + s \, r_{b'e}(C_{b'e} + C_{b'c})} $$ $$ \boxed{A_{is}(s) = \frac{\beta_0}{1 + s \, r_{b'e}(C_{b'e} + C_{b'c})}} $$ **Step (d) — Identify the 3-dB cutoff (the $\beta$-cutoff frequency):** $$ f_\beta = \frac{1}{2\pi \, r_{b'e}(C_{b'e} + C_{b'c})} $$ **Step (e) — The transit frequency $f_T$ (gain–bandwidth product):** At $f = f_T$, $\vert A_{is} \vert = 1$. Setting $\vert A_{is}(j2\pi f_T) \vert = 1$: $$ \frac{\beta_0}{\sqrt{1 + (f_T / f_\beta)^2}} = 1 $$ For $f_T \gg f_\beta$, $\sqrt{1 + (f_T/f_\beta)^2} \approx f_T / f_\beta$: $$ \boxed{f_T = \beta_0 \, f_\beta = \frac{g_m}{2\pi(C_{b'e} + C_{b'c})}} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > $f_T$ is a **constant of the transistor** at a given bias point. It is the single most important figure of merit for high-frequency transistors. --- ## 3.2 Derivation of $f_H$ for the CE Amplifier with Load (Miller Approximation) > [!NOTE] > Now we keep $R_L' = R_C \parallel R_L$ finite. We apply **Miller's theorem** to fold $C_{b'c}$ into the input side. **Step (a) — Voltage gain of the CE stage (approximate, mid-band):** From the simplified small-signal model with $R_L'$ as the collector load: $$ A_v = \frac{V_o}{V_{b'e}} = -g_m R_L' $$ **Step (b) — Apply Miller's theorem to $C_{b'c}$:** A capacitance $C_{b'c}$ between input ($V_{b'e}$) and output ($V_o$) with $V_o / V_{b'e} = -g_m R_L' = K$: $$ C_{M1} = C_{b'c}(1 - K) = C_{b'c}(1 + g_m R_L') \quad \text{(input side)} $$ $$ C_{M2} = C_{b'c}\left(1 - \frac{1}{K}\right) \approx C_{b'c} \quad \text{(output side, for } \vert K \vert \gg 1 \text{)} $$ **Step (c) — Total input capacitance:** $$ C_{in} = C_{b'e} + C_{M1} = C_{b'e} + C_{b'c}(1 + g_m R_L') $$ **Step (d) — Input-loop Thévenin resistance:** Looking into the base node from the source side (Thevenin equivalent): $$ R_S' = R_S \parallel R_B \parallel r_{b'e} $$ **Step (e) — Input time constant and upper cutoff:** $$ \tau_S = R_S' \, C_{in} $$ $$ \boxed{f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi \, R_S' [C_{b'e} + C_{b'c}(1 + g_m R_L')]}} $$ This is the **single-pole Miller approximation** — the form most used in KTU problems. --- ## 3.3 Derivation of the Current Gain $A_i(s)$ with Load For completeness, the current gain from source current $I_i$ to load current $I_L$ in the CE amplifier with load $R_L$ is: $$ A_i(s) = \frac{I_L}{I_i} = \frac{-\beta_0 \, R_C / (R_C + R_L)}{1 + s \, r_{b'e}\left[C_{b'e} + C_{b'c}\left(1 + g_m (R_C \parallel R_L)\right)\right]} $$ **Derivation sketch:** 1. Output current $I_o$ divides between $R_C$ and $R_L$ in the ratio $I_L = I_o \cdot \frac{R_C}{R_C + R_L}$. 2. With $R_L' = R_C \parallel R_L$, the Miller capacitance becomes $C_{b'c}(1 + g_m R_L')$. 3. The denominator pole is then at $s = -\frac{1}{r_{b'e}[C_{b'e} + C_{b'c}(1 + g_m R_L')]}$. **3-dB frequency with load:** $$ f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi \, r_{b'e}\left[C_{b'e} + C_{b'c}(1 + g_m R_L')\right]} $$ (Here, $r_{b'e}$ alone appears if the source resistance is included in $r_{b'e}$ or if $R_S = 0$. In the general KTU problem, the source $R_S$ is also included in the parallel combination, giving the full formula from §3.2.) --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Quick Comparison | Configuration | Dominant Capacitance | Cutoff Frequency | |---|---|---| | Short-circuited output ($R_L = 0$) | $C_{b'e} + C_{b'c}$ | $f_\beta = \frac{1}{2\pi r_{b'e}(C_{b'e} + C_{b'c})}$ | | With load $R_L$ (Miller approx.) | $C_{b'e} + C_{b'c}(1 + g_m R_L')$ | $f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi R_S' C_{in}}$ | | Cascode amplifier | Approx. $C_{b'e}$ only | $f_H$ increases $\sim 5\times$ to $10\times$ | --- ## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style) **Given:** A CE amplifier has $R_S = 1\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_B = 10\text{ k}\Omega$, $r_{b'e} = 1\text{ k}\Omega$, $g_m = 50\text{ mA/V}$, $C_{b'e} = 100\text{ pF}$, $C_{b'c} = 5\text{ pF}$, $R_C = 2\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_L = 2\text{ k}\Omega$. **Step 1:** Compute $R_L'$: $$ R_L' = R_C \parallel R_L = \frac{2 \times 2}{2 + 2} = 1\text{ k}\Omega $$ **Step 2:** Compute Miller capacitance: $$ C_M = C_{b'c}(1 + g_m R_L') = 5 \times 10^{-12}\,(1 + 0.05 \times 1000) = 5\text{ pF} \times 51 = 255\text{ pF} $$ **Step 3:** Total input capacitance: $$ C_{in} = C_{b'e} + C_M = 100 + 255 = 355\text{ pF} $$ **Step 4:** Input resistance: $$ R_S' = R_S \parallel R_B \parallel r_{b'e} = 1\text{ k} \parallel 10\text{ k} \parallel 1\text{ k} \approx 0.498\text{ k}\Omega \approx 498\,\Omega $$ **Step 5:** Upper cutoff frequency: $$ f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 498 \times 355 \times 10^{-12}} \approx 0.9\text{ MHz} $$ **Step 6:** Gain–bandwidth product: $$ f_T = \frac{g_m}{2\pi(C_{b'e} + C_{b'c})} = \frac{0.05}{2\pi \times 105 \times 10^{-12}} \approx 75.8\text{ MHz} $$ Mid-band voltage gain: $A_{mid} = -g_m R_L' = -0.05 \times 1000 = -50$. GBP $\approx 50 \times 0.9\text{ MHz} = 45\text{ MHz}$ — somewhat less than $f_T$ due to Miller multiplication. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 High-Frequency Hybrid-$\pi$ Equivalent Circuit of CE Amplifier (Block-Level Functional Architecture) > [!NOTE] > Since the physical circuit contains many discrete components, the Mermaid diagram below maps the **signal-flow architecture** of the high-frequency model — showing how the signal path interacts with the capacitances that determine $f_H$. ```mermaid flowchart LR Src[V_S with R_S] --> Node1[Base Node A] RB[R_B = R_1 || R_2] --> Node1 Node1 --> rbe[r_b'e] rbe --> Node2[Emitter Node E - AC GND] Node1 --> Cbe[C_b'e] Cbe --> Node2 Node1 --> Cbc[C_b'c - feedback path] Cbc --> Node3[Output Node C] Node3 --> RLprime[R_L' = R_C || R_L] Node3 --> Cbc2[C_b'c to ground - Miller output side] Node2 --> gm[g_m * V_b'e Current Source] gm --> Node3 Node3 --> Vout[V_out] classDef cap fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc8800,color:#000; classDef res fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003d99,color:#000; classDef node fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000; classDef src fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000; class Cbe,Cbc,Cbc2 cap; class rbe,RB,RLprime res; class Node1,Node2,Node3 node; class Src,Vout src; ``` ## 4.2 Miller-Approximation Simplified Circuit (One-Pole Equivalent) ```mermaid flowchart LR Vsrc[V_S] --> Rs[R_S] Rs --> InNode[Input Node] RB[R_B] --> InNode InNode --> rbePrime[r_b'e] InNode --> Cin[C_in = C_b'e + C_M] Cin --> Gnd1[Ground] rbePrime --> Gnd1 InNode --> gmCtrl[V_b'e controls g_m source] gmCtrl --> Iout[Current Source g_m * V_b'e] Iout --> OutNode[Output Node] OutNode --> RLprime[R_L' = R_C || R_L] OutNode --> Vout2[V_out] RLprime --> Gnd2[Ground] classDef cap fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc8800,color:#000; classDef res fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003d99,color:#000; classDef ctrl fill:#e2d4f0,stroke:#5b2c83,color:#000; class Cin cap; class Rs,RB,rbePrime,RLprime res; class gmCtrl,Iout ctrl; ``` ## 4.3 Frequency-Response Plot (Block-Level Description) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Low["LOW FREQUENCY REGION"] LF["Gain rises at +20 dB/decade
due to coupling & bypass capacitors"] end subgraph Mid["MID-BAND REGION"] MF["Gain = A_mid (flat, constant)
All caps ignored"] end subgraph High["HIGH FREQUENCY REGION"] HF["Gain falls at -20 dB/decade
due to C_b'e and Miller C_M"] end FL[f_L] --> FMid[Mid-band region: 0.707 A_mid boundaries] --> FH[f_H] Low --> FL Mid --> FMid High --> FH LF --> MF MF --> HF classDef region fill:#d1ecf1,stroke:#0c5460,color:#000; classDef point fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000; class Low,Mid,High region; class FL,FMid,FH point; ``` ## 4.4 Procedure Flowchart for Solving KTU $f_H$ Problems ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Start: Given R_S, R_B, r_b'e, g_m, C_b'e, C_b'c, R_C, R_L] --> B[Compute R_L' = R_C || R_L] B --> C[Compute g_m R_L'] C --> D[Compute C_M = C_b'c * 1 + g_m R_L'] D --> E[Compute C_in = C_b'e + C_M] E --> F[Compute R_S' = R_S || R_B || r_b'e] F --> G[Compute f_H = 1 / 2*pi*R_S'*C_in] G --> H[Compare with f_T = g_m / 2*pi * C_b'e + C_b'c] H --> I[If needed: compute GBP = A_mid * f_H] classDef step fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000; classDef calc fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085,color:#000; classDef result fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000; class A,B result; class C,D,E,F,G,H,I calc; class A,B step; ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Define Miller capacitance. Show how the Miller effect modifies the effective input capacitance of a CE amplifier.** **Model Answer:** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Miller Capacitance:** When an impedance $Z$ is connected between the input and output nodes of an inverting amplifier with voltage gain $K = V_o / V_i$, Miller's theorem states that the impedance can be replaced by an equivalent impedance $Z_1 = \dfrac{Z}{1-K}$ referred to the input. For the CE amplifier, $C_{b'c}$ appears between base and collector. The mid-band voltage gain from base to collector is $A_v = -g_m R_L'$. Hence $K = -g_m R_L'$ and: $$ C_{M} = C_{b'c}(1 - K) = C_{b'c}(1 + g_m R_L') $$ The total input capacitance becomes $C_{in} = C_{b'e} + C_M$, where $C_M$ is **drastically larger** than $C_{b'c}$ because of the gain multiplication. This severely limits the high-frequency response. --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] **What is the transit frequency $f_T$ of a BJT? Why is it considered a figure of merit?** **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > **Transit Frequency $f_T$:** It is the frequency at which the **short-circuit common-emitter current gain** $\vert A_{is} \vert$ of the BJT drops to **unity** (i.e., $0\text{ dB}$). It is also called the **gain–bandwidth product**. $$ f_T = \beta_0 \, f_\beta = \frac{g_m}{2\pi (C_{b'e} + C_{b'c})} $$ **Figure of merit** because: 1. It is **independent of the external circuit** — purely a property of the transistor at a given bias current. 2. It sets the **upper limit on useful frequency** for any amplifier built using the device. 3. The **bandwidth of any CE stage** is bounded by $f_T$, with the actual $f_H$ being lower due to the Miller multiplication. 4. Designers compare transistors by quoting $f_T$ values (e.g., 100 MHz general-purpose, 5 GHz RF transistors). --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - **Do NOT omit the Miller multiplication** $(1 + g_m R_L')$ — this is the most common error and costs 4–5 marks. > - Students frequently confuse $f_\beta$ with $f_H$ — remember, $f_\beta$ is the **intrinsic** BJT cutoff (output short-circuited), while $f_H$ is the **amplifier** cutoff (with load and source). > - Always include **all three** resistances in $R_S' = R_S \parallel R_B \parallel r_{b'e}$. Forgetting $R_B$ is a frequent mistake. > - State the **assumptions** clearly: coupling and bypass capacitors are short circuits, $r_{b'b}$ is neglected, output-side Miller capacitance is neglected. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024] **(a)** Derive the expression for the **upper 3-dB frequency $f_H$** of a CE amplifier using the hybrid-$\pi$ model and Miller's theorem. **(7 marks)** **(b)** For a CE amplifier, the following data is given: - $R_S = 600\,\Omega$, $R_1 = 10\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 5\text{ k}\Omega$ - $r_{b'e} = 1.1\text{ k}\Omega$, $g_m = 50\text{ mA/V}$ - $C_{b'e} = 100\text{ pF}$, $C_{b'c} = 5\text{ pF}$ - $R_C = 2.2\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_L = 4.7\text{ k}\Omega$ Calculate: **(i)** the Miller capacitance, **(ii)** the total input capacitance, **(iii)** the input-loop resistance, and **(iv)** the upper cutoff frequency $f_H$. Also comment on the **bandwidth limitation** due to the Miller effect. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for Question A: **Part (a) — Derivation of $f_H$:** Refer to the derivation in §3.2. State the steps: **Step 1:** Replace the CE amplifier by its high-frequency hybrid-$\pi$ equivalent. The coupling and emitter-bypass capacitors become short circuits. **Step 2:** Identify the voltage gain $A_v = -g_m R_L'$ where $R_L' = R_C \parallel R_L$. **Step 3:** Apply Miller's theorem to $C_{b'c}$: $$ C_M = C_{b'c}(1 + g_m R_L') $$ **Step 4:** Total input capacitance: $$ C_{in} = C_{b'e} + C_M $$ **Step 5:** Thevenin resistance at the input: $$ R_S' = R_S \parallel R_B \parallel r_{b'e}, \quad R_B = R_1 \parallel R_2 $$ **Step 6:** The pole frequency: $$ f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi R_S' [C_{b'e} + C_{b'c}(1 + g_m R_L')]} $$ **Part (b) — Numerical Solution:** **(i) Miller Capacitance:** First compute $R_L' = R_C \parallel R_L = \dfrac{2.2 \times 4.7}{2.2 + 4.7}\text{ k}\Omega = 1.497\text{ k}\Omega \approx 1.5\text{ k}\Omega$ $$ g_m R_L' = 0.05 \times 1497 = 74.85 $$ $$ C_M = 5 \text{ pF} \times (1 + 74.85) = 5 \times 75.85 = 379.25 \text{ pF} $$ **(ii) Total input capacitance:** $$ C_{in} = C_{b'e} + C_M = 100 + 379.25 = 479.25 \text{ pF} $$ **(iii) Input-loop resistance:** $R_B = R_1 \parallel R_2 = \dfrac{10 \times 5}{10 + 5} = 3.33\text{ k}\Omega$ $$ R_S' = R_S \parallel R_B \parallel r_{b'e} = \frac{1}{\frac{1}{0.6} + \frac{1}{3.33} + \frac{1}{1.1}}\text{ k}\Omega = \frac{1}{1.667 + 0.300 + 0.909}\text{ k}\Omega $$ $$ R_S' = \frac{1}{2.876}\text{ k}\Omega = 347.7\,\Omega $$ **(iv) Upper cutoff frequency:** $$ f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 347.7 \times 479.25 \times 10^{-12}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 1.666 \times 10^{-7}} $$ $$ f_H \approx \frac{1}{1.047 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 0.955 \text{ MHz} \approx 955\text{ kHz} $$ **Comment on Miller effect:** If the Miller multiplication were ignored, $C_{in}$ would be only $105$ pF, giving $f_H \approx 4.4$ MHz. The Miller effect has therefore reduced the bandwidth by a factor of $\sim 4.6$ — illustrating how strongly the feedback capacitance limits high-frequency response. Techniques to mitigate this include: - **Cascode amplifier** (eliminates Miller effect by buffering the collector) - **Neutralisation** (cancels $C_{b'c}$ via a feedback capacitor) - Using a transistor with smaller $C_{b'c}$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Alternative] **(a)** Starting from the hybrid-$\pi$ model of a BJT in **short-circuit** configuration, derive the expression for the short-circuit CE current gain $A_{is}(s)$ and hence obtain the **transit frequency $f_T$**. Show that $f_T$ is independent of the quiescent point current $I_C$ to a first approximation. **(7 marks)** **(b)** For a BJT at $I_C = 5\text{ mA}$ and $V_{CE} = 10\text{ V}$, the following parameters are given: $\beta_0 = 100$, $C_{b'e} = 50\text{ pF}$, $C_{b'c} = 3\text{ pF}$, $V_T = 25\text{ mV}$. Compute **(i)** $g_m$, **(ii)** $r_{b'e}$, **(iii)** $f_\beta$, and **(iv)** $f_T$. If the same transistor is used in a CE amplifier with $R_S = 100\,\Omega$ and $R_L' = 2\text{ k}\Omega$, find the approximate $f_H$ using the Miller formula. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution for Question B: **Part (a) — Derivation of $A_{is}(s)$ and $f_T$:** Refer to §3.1. The derivation gives: $$ A_{is}(s) = \frac{\beta_0}{1 + s\, r_{b'e}(C_{b'e} + C_{b'c})} $$ The 3-dB frequency is: $$ f_\beta = \frac{1}{2\pi\, r_{b'e}(C_{b'e} + C_{b'c})} $$ Hence: $$ f_T = \beta_0 f_\beta = \frac{g_m}{2\pi(C_{b'e} + C_{b'c})} $$ **Independence of $I_C$:** Substituting $g_m = I_C / V_T$: $$ f_T = \frac{I_C / V_T}{2\pi (C_{b'e} + C_{b'c})} $$ Now $C_{b'e}$ is the diffusion capacitance, which scales with $I_C$ as $C_{b'e} \propto I_C$ (since it depends on the stored base charge $Q_B \propto I_C$). Thus $g_m / C_{b'e}$ is **approximately constant** with $I_C$, making $f_T$ relatively independent of quiescent current. The depletion capacitance $C_{b'c}$ is bias-independent and contributes only a small fixed term. **Part (b) — Numerical Computation:** **(i) Transconductance:** $$ g_m = \frac{I_C}{V_T} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{25 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.2 \text{ A/V} = 200\text{ mA/V} $$ **(ii) Base–emitter dynamic resistance:** $$ r_{b'e} = \frac{\beta_0}{g_m} = \frac{100}{0.2} = 500\,\Omega $$ **(iii) $\beta$-cutoff frequency:** $$ f_\beta = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 500 \times (50 + 3) \times 10^{-12}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 500 \times 53 \times 10^{-12}} $$ $$ f_\beta = \frac{1}{1.665 \times 10^{-7}} \approx 6.0\text{ MHz} $$ **(iv) Transit frequency:** $$ f_T = \beta_0 \cdot f_\beta = 100 \times 6\text{ MHz} = 600\text{ MHz} $$ (Alternatively, $f_T = \dfrac{g_m}{2\pi(C_{b'e} + C_{b'c})} = \dfrac{0.2}{2\pi \times 53 \times 10^{-12}} = 600\text{ MHz}$ — consistent.) **$f_H$ in CE amplifier with load:** $$ C_M = C_{b'c}(1 + g_m R_L') = 3 \text{ pF} \times (1 + 0.2 \times 2000) = 3 \text{ pF} \times 401 = 1203 \text{ pF} $$ $$ C_{in} = 50 + 1203 = 1253\text{ pF} $$ $$ R_S' = R_S \parallel r_{b'e} = \frac{100 \times 500}{100 + 500} = 83.3\,\Omega $$ (assuming $R_B \gg r_{b'e}$) $$ f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 83.3 \times 1253 \times 10^{-12}} = \frac{1}{6.56 \times 10^{-7}} \approx 1.52\text{ MHz} $$ **Comment:** The Miller effect has reduced the amplifier's $f_H$ from $6\text{ MHz}$ (intrinsic $\beta$ cutoff) to $1.52\text{ MHz}$ — a factor of $\sim 4$ reduction. The transistor could in principle work up to $600\text{ MHz}$, but the CE topology limits us to a few MHz. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — High-Frequency Response of CE Amplifier** - **Why high-frequency analysis?** Internal transistor capacitances $C_{b'e}$ and $C_{b'c}$ cause gain to roll off above $f_H$. - **Hybrid-$\pi$ model** is the standard small-signal high-frequency model. It contains $r_{b'e}$, controlled source $g_m V_{b'e}$, and two capacitances $C_{b'e}$, $C_{b'c}$. - **Coupling and bypass capacitors** become short circuits in the high-frequency region — they are removed from the equivalent circuit. - **Miller's Theorem:** Replaces the bridge capacitance $C_{b'c}$ by an equivalent input-side capacitance $C_M = C_{b'c}(1 + g_m R_L')$. The output-side Miller capacitance is usually neglected. - **Miller effect** is the dominant bandwidth-limiting mechanism in CE amplifiers — it can multiply $C_{b'c}$ by 50 to 400 times. - **Upper 3-dB frequency (single-pole Miller approximation):** $$ f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi R_S' [C_{b'e} + C_{b'c}(1 + g_m R_L')]}, \quad R_S' = R_S \parallel R_B \parallel r_{b'e} $$ - **$\beta$-cutoff frequency** (intrinsic BJT, output shorted): $$ f_\beta = \frac{1}{2\pi r_{b'e}(C_{b'e} + C_{b'c})} $$ - **Transit frequency $f_T$** = $\beta_0 f_\beta$ = $g_m / [2\pi(C_{b'e} + C_{b'c})]$ — the **device figure of merit**, approximately constant over a wide range of $I_C$. - **Gain–Bandwidth Product (GBP):** For the CE amplifier, $\vert A_{mid} \vert \cdot f_H$ is approximately constant and bounded by $f_T$. - **Voltage gain magnitude at high frequency:** $$ \vert A_v(f) \vert = \frac{\vert A_{mid} \vert}{\sqrt{1 + (f/f_H)^2}}, \quad f \ge f_H $$ - **Bandwidth extension techniques:** cascode amplifier, neutralisation, choosing low-$C_{b'c}$ RF transistors, and using FETs (where Miller effect is much weaker). - **Typical KTU pitfall:** forgetting to include $R_B$ in $R_S'$; omitting the $(1 + g_m R_L')$ factor; confusing $f_\beta$ with $f_H$. - **Useful identities to memorise:** - $g_m r_{b'e} = \beta_0$ - $r_{b'e} = (1 + \beta) V_T / I_E \approx \beta V_T / I_C$ - $g_m = I_C / V_T$ ($V_T \approx 25$ mV at room temperature) - **Final sanity check:** The computed $f_H$ should always be **less than $f_T$** of the transistor. If your $f_H > f_T$, re-check the calculation.

topics.title: Multistage Amplifiers - Cascade and Cascode Amplifiers - Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET302 - ELECTRONIC DEVICES AND CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Multistage Amplifiers - Cascade and Cascode Amplifiers - Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Multistage Amplifier — The Foundation

A **Multistage Amplifier** is an electronic amplifier system constructed by connecting two or more single-stage amplifier configurations in a series (cascade) chain so that the output of one stage acts as the input to the next. The fundamental purpose is to achieve a **larger overall voltage gain**, **greater bandwidth control**, and **better impedance matching** than any single transistor stage can provide on its own.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Directive (PCAET302 — Module 2):**
> A single Common-Emitter (CE) stage provides moderate gain but suffers from **Miller multiplication** of the base–collector capacitance $C_{bc}$, which drastically shrinks the high-frequency response. Multistage topologies, particularly the **Cascade** and **Cascode**, are introduced to overcome this limitation.

---

## 1.2 Cascade Amplifier — Definition

A **Cascade Amplifier** is a multistage amplifier in which two (or more) amplifier stages are connected **directly in series**, where the **output of the first stage is coupled to the input of the second stage** through a coupling network (RC coupling, direct coupling, or transformer coupling).

Each stage operates as an **independent amplifier**, and the **overall voltage gain** is the product of the individual stage gains (assuming isolation between stages via buffer coupling).

$$A_{V(\text{Total})} = A_{V1} \times A_{V2} \times A_{V3} \times \dots \times A_{Vn}$$

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Whisper Chain"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine a classroom where a teacher whispers a message to Student 1, Student 1 whispers it (louder) to Student 2, and Student 2 whispers it (even louder) to Student 3 at the back of the room. Each student "amplifies" the message they receive. However, every student also adds a tiny bit of "noise" and "delay." A **cascade amplifier** works exactly like this whisper chain — each transistor stage multiplies the signal amplitude, but the chain bandwidth and phase shift are determined by the slowest stage.

For a **two-stage CE–CE cascade**, the effective AC load on the first stage becomes:

$$R_{L1(\text{eff})} = R_{C1} \,\|\, R_{B2} \,\|\, r_{\pi 2}$$

where $r_{\pi 2}$ is the input resistance of the second CE stage.

---

## 1.3 Cascode Amplifier — Definition

A **Cascode Amplifier** is a *specialized* two-stage amplifier topology consisting of a **Common-Emitter (CE) stage directly stacked on top of a Common-Base (CB) stage**, sharing a single bias current. The CE stage provides the voltage gain, while the CB stage acts as a **current buffer** that dramatically suppresses the Miller effect.

The cascode is essentially a **"CE + CB sandwich"** with the emitters of the CB tied to the collector of the CE through a shared bias point.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Baton Relay"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Picture a relay race. Runner 1 (the CE stage) builds up tremendous speed (voltage gain), but the baton is heavy. Runner 2 (the CB stage) accepts the baton almost instantly — without any effort to "lift" it (no Miller multiplication). The CB stage offers **extremely low input impedance** at the collector of the CE, which is the secret to its high bandwidth. The runners don't work in series independently; they work as a **single unified team** sharing the same current.

The cascode amplifier retains the **high input resistance** of a CE stage and the **high output resistance** of a CB stage, but the Miller capacitance is reduced by a factor of approximately $(1 - A_{V2}) \approx (1 + g_m R_C)$, which is enormous.

---

## 1.4 Why Two Topologies? — The Engineering Trade-off

| Parameter | Cascade (CE-CE) | Cascode (CE-CB) |
|---|---|---|
| **Gain per stage** | High | Comparable to single CE |
| **Bandwidth** | Reduced by Miller effect | **Greatly extended** |
| **Input Impedance** | Moderate ($r_\pi$) | Moderate ($r_\pi$ of CE) |
| **Output Impedance** | Moderate ($R_C$) | High ($r_o$ of CB) |
| **Stages counted** | Truly two independent stages | One stage electrically (CE) |
| **Bias current** | Two independent bias networks | **Shared single bias** |
| **Noise performance** | Good | Excellent at high frequencies |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Focus Point:** The examiner expects you to explicitly state that the cascode is **electrically a single-stage amplifier** (one $V_{CC}$ path) but **topologically two transistors**. This distinction is frequently tested.

---

## 1.5 Standard Physical Constants Used in This Module

- **Thermal Voltage:** $V_T \approx \mathbf{26 \text{ mV}}$ at $T = 300 \text{ K}$
- **Boltzmann Constant:** $k = \mathbf{1.38 \times 10^{-23} \text{ J/K}}$
- **Electronic Charge:** $q = \mathbf{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}}$
- **Intrinsic Silicon carrier concentration:** $n_i \approx \mathbf{1.5 \times 10^{10} \text{ cm}^{-3}}$ at 300 K
- **Standard Bandwidth reference:** $f_T$ (transition frequency) of BJT — used for Cascode HF analysis.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Two-Stage RC-Coupled Cascade Amplifier — Architecture

A **two-stage RC-coupled cascade** uses two CE stages linked by a **DC-blocking capacitor** $C_C$ (coupling capacitor) and a bias network. The coupling capacitor:

- Blocks the DC collector voltage of stage 1 from reaching the base of stage 2.
- Passes the AC signal forward with negligible attenuation at mid-band frequencies.

At **mid-band frequencies**, all coupling and bypass capacitors behave as **short circuits**, and all internal capacitances ($C_{bc}$, $C_{be}$) behave as **open circuits**. This simplifies the small-signal analysis dramatically.

### Step-by-Step Operating Logic

1. **Input signal** $v_s$ is applied to the base of $Q_1$ via the input coupling capacitor.
2. **Stage 1 (CE):** $Q_1$ amplifies the input. Its collector current $i_{c1} = g_{m1} \cdot v_{be1}$ produces an AC voltage across $R_{C1} \| R_{B2} \| r_{\pi 2}$.
3. **Coupling network:** $C_C$ passes this AC voltage to the base of $Q_2$ while isolating DC bias.
4. **Stage 2 (CE):** $Q_2$ further amplifies the signal and delivers it to the load $R_L$ through the output coupling capacitor.
5. **Output signal** $v_o$ is the final amplified version of $v_s$.

### The Critical "Loading Effect" Insight

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is cascade gain *not* simply $A_{V1} \times A_{V2}$ without consideration?**
> Because the input resistance of stage 2 ($R_{in2} = R_{B2} \| r_{\pi 2}$) **loads down** the output of stage 1. The **effective load** seen by $Q_1$ is $R_{C1} \| R_{in2}$, NOT just $R_{C1}$. This loading reduces $A_{V1}$ below its unloaded value.

So the rigorous gain expression is:

$$A_{V1} = -g_{m1}\,(R_{C1} \,\|\, R_{B2} \,\|\, r_{\pi 2})$$

$$A_{V2} = -g_{m2}\,(R_{C2} \,\|\, R_L)$$

$$\boxed{A_{V(\text{Total})} = A_{V1} \times A_{V2} = (+g_{m1} g_{m2}) \cdot (R_{C1} \| R_{B2} \| r_{\pi 2}) \cdot (R_{C2} \| R_L)}$$

The negative signs cancel because **two inversions in series yield a non-inverted output** relative to the input.

---

## 2.2 Cascode Amplifier — Architecture & Theory

The cascode amplifier consists of:

- **$Q_1$ (Bottom transistor — CE configuration):** Input applied to base, emitter grounded (or bypassed), collector connected to emitter of $Q_2$.
- **$Q_2$ (Top transistor — CB configuration):** Base is **AC grounded** (held at a fixed DC bias), collector is the output.

Both transistors share the **same DC collector current** $I_C$ because they are in series:

$$I_{C1} = I_{C2} = I_C \quad \Rightarrow \quad g_{m1} = g_{m2} = g_m = \frac{I_C}{V_T}$$

### Why Cascode Provides High Bandwidth — The Miller Suppression

In a single CE stage, the gain from base to collector is $A_{V1} = -g_m R_C$. The Miller effect multiplies $C_{bc}$ by $(1 - A_{V1}) \approx (1 + g_m R_C)$, which is large.

In the cascode:

- The CE stage's collector is connected to the emitter of the CB stage, which has a **very low input impedance** $r_{e2} = 1/g_{m2} \approx 1/g_m$.
- The voltage gain from the base of $Q_1$ to the collector of $Q_1$ is now:

$$A_{V(\text{CE in cascode})} = -g_{m1} \cdot (r_{e2}) = -g_m \cdot \frac{1}{g_m} = -1$$

- Miller multiplication becomes $(1 - (-1)) = 2$ — a **drastic reduction** from the typical $(1 + g_m R_C)$ factor.
- The dominant pole frequency is extended by roughly a factor of $g_m R_C$.

### Cascode Voltage Gain

The overall cascode voltage gain (assuming identical $Q_1$, $Q_2$ and neglecting $r_o$):

$$\boxed{A_{V(\text{cascode})} = -g_m \cdot (R_C \| R_L) \approx -g_m R_C}$$

This is **numerically identical** to a single CE stage, but the bandwidth is **vastly superior**.

### Cascode Input Resistance

$$R_{in(\text{cascode})} = R_B \| r_{\pi 1} \approx r_{\pi 1} = \frac{\beta}{g_m} = \frac{\beta V_T}{I_C}$$

### Cascode Output Resistance

$$R_{out(\text{cascode})} = R_C \| r_{o2} \cdot (1 + g_{m2} \cdot r_{\pi 1} \| \text{something}) \approx R_C \| r_{o2}$$

This is **much higher** than a single CE stage's output, making the cascode an excellent **transimpedance driver**.

---

## 2.3 Frequency Response of Multistage Amplifiers

When $n$ identical stages are cascaded, each with the same upper 3-dB frequency $f_{H1}$ and lower 3-dB frequency $f_{L1}$, the **overall 3-dB frequencies** shift:

### Upper 3-dB Frequency (Bandwidth Shrinkage)

$$\boxed{f_H(\text{overall}) = f_{H1} \cdot \sqrt{2^{1/n} - 1}}$$

| Number of Stages ($n$) | Bandwidth Shrinkage Factor $\sqrt{2^{1/n}-1}$ |
|---|---|
| 1 | **1.000** |
| 2 | **0.644** |
| 3 | **0.510** |
| 4 | **0.435** |

### Lower 3-dB Frequency (Extension)

$$f_L(\text{overall}) = \frac{f_{L1}}{\sqrt{2^{1/n} - 1}}$$

### Rise Time Degradation

$$\boxed{t_r = \sqrt{t_{r1}^2 + t_{r2}^2 + \dots + t_{rn}^2}}$$

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $A_{V(\text{Total})} = A_{V1} \times A_{V2} \times \dots \times A_{Vn}$ | Cascade overall voltage gain (in linear scale) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $A_{V(\text{dB})} = 20 \log_{10} \vert A_V \vert$ | Conversion from linear to decibel | dB |
| 3 | $A_{V(\text{dB,total})} = \sum A_{Vi(\text{dB})}$ | dB gains add in cascade | dB |
| 4 | $A_{V1(\text{eff})} = -g_{m1}(R_{C1} \vert\mid R_{B2} \vert\mid r_{\pi 2})$ | Stage 1 effective gain under loading | V/V |
| 5 | $r_{\pi} = \beta V_T / I_C$ | BJT small-signal base-emitter resistance | $\Omega$ |
| 6 | $g_m = I_C / V_T$ | Transconductance of BJT | A/V (or S) |
| 7 | $r_e = V_T / I_E = \alpha / g_m$ | Emitter resistance of BJT | $\Omega$ |
| 8 | $A_{V(\text{cascode})} = -g_m (R_C \vert\mid R_L)$ | Cascode mid-band voltage gain | V/V |
| 9 | $R_{in(\text{cascode})} \approx r_{\pi 1}$ | Cascode input resistance | $\Omega$ |
| 10 | $R_{out(\text{cascode})} \approx R_C \vert\mid r_{o2}$ | Cascode output resistance | $\Omega$ |
| 11 | $f_H = f_{H1} \sqrt{2^{1/n} - 1}$ | Multistage upper cutoff shrinkage | Hz |
| 12 | $f_L = f_{L1} / \sqrt{2^{1/n} - 1}$ | Multistage lower cutoff extension | Hz |
| 13 | $t_r = \sqrt{t_{r1}^2 + t_{r2}^2 + \dots + t_{rn}^2}$ | Cumulative rise time (independent stages) | seconds |
| 14 | $C_{M(\text{eff})} = C_{bc}(1 - A_V)$ | Miller-multiplied feedback capacitance | F |
| 15 | $C_{M(\text{cascode,eff})} \approx 2 C_{bc}$ | Reduced Miller capacitance in cascode | F |
| 16 | $f_T = g_m / (2\pi C_{be}) \cdot \text{correction}$ | Transition frequency (unity current gain) | Hz |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note on KTU Exam Hall:** When the question says "design a two-stage amplifier for a gain of 40 dB," the student must first **convert dB to linear** ($A_V = 10^{40/20} = 100$), then **split the gain** between the two stages (often 10 each, since $10 \times 10 = 100$). Choosing unequal splits (e.g., 50 and 2) is possible but **never optimal** because the bandwidth is maximized when the gain is distributed **geometrically** as $A_{V1} = A_{V2} = \sqrt{A_{V,\text{total}}}$.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cascade amplifiers** are the workhorses of **audio pre-amplifiers**, **op-amp input stages**, and **IF amplifier chains** in radio receivers.
- **Cascode amplifiers** are the heart of **RF front-ends**, **television tuners**, **wideband oscilloscope front-ends**, and the **input stage of virtually every op-amp** (e.g., the classic $\mu$A741 input differential pair is a differential cascode).
- The cascode is also used inside **current mirrors** and **active loads** in integrated circuits to boost output impedance without sacrificing bandwidth.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Overall Voltage Gain of a Two-Stage CE-CE Cascade

### Given:

- Stage 1: BJT $Q_1$ with $g_{m1}$, $R_{C1}$, $R_{B1}$ (base bias).
- Stage 2: BJT $Q_2$ with $g_{m2}$, $R_{C2}$, $R_{B2}$ (base bias), load $R_L$.
- Inter-stage coupling is AC short at mid-band.

### Step 1 — Apply KVL at the input of stage 1

The base of $Q_1$ sees the source signal $v_s$ divided by the source resistance and the input resistance of stage 1. Assuming an ideal voltage source ($R_s = 0$):

$$v_{be1} = v_s$$

### Step 2 — Write the small-signal collector current of $Q_1$

For a CE stage, the small-signal collector current is governed by transconductance:

$$i_{c1} = g_{m1} \cdot v_{be1} = g_{m1} \cdot v_s$$

### Step 3 — Compute the AC output voltage of stage 1

The collector of $Q_1$ is connected to $R_{C1}$ in parallel with the input resistance of stage 2. At mid-band, the input resistance of stage 2 is:

$$R_{in2} = R_{B2} \,\|\, r_{\pi 2} = \frac{R_{B2} \cdot r_{\pi 2}}{R_{B2} + r_{\pi 2}}$$

So the **effective load on $Q_1$** is:

$$R_{L1}' = R_{C1} \,\|\, R_{in2} = \frac{R_{C1} \cdot R_{in2}}{R_{C1} + R_{in2}}$$

The output voltage of stage 1 (at the collector of $Q_1$) is therefore:

$$v_{o1} = -i_{c1} \cdot R_{L1}' = -g_{m1} \cdot v_s \cdot R_{L1}'$$

$$v_{o1} = -g_{m1} \cdot v_s \cdot (R_{C1} \| R_{B2} \| r_{\pi 2})$$

### Step 4 — Compute the small-signal base voltage of $Q_2$

The coupling capacitor $C_C$ passes the AC voltage without attenuation at mid-band. Therefore:

$$v_{be2} = v_{o1} = -g_{m1} \cdot v_s \cdot (R_{C1} \| R_{B2} \| r_{\pi 2})$$

### Step 5 — Compute the small-signal collector current of $Q_2$

$$i_{c2} = g_{m2} \cdot v_{be2} = -g_{m1} \cdot g_{m2} \cdot v_s \cdot (R_{C1} \| R_{B2} \| r_{\pi 2})$$

### Step 6 — Compute the final output voltage of stage 2

The collector of $Q_2$ drives $R_{C2}$ in parallel with the external load $R_L$:

$$R_{L2}' = R_{C2} \,\|\, R_L = \frac{R_{C2} \cdot R_L}{R_{C2} + R_L}$$

The final output voltage is:

$$v_o = -i_{c2} \cdot R_{L2}' = g_{m1} \cdot g_{m2} \cdot v_s \cdot (R_{C1} \| R_{B2} \| r_{\pi 2}) \cdot (R_{C2} \| R_L)$$

### Step 7 — Final overall gain expression

Dividing $v_o$ by $v_s$:

$$\boxed{A_{V(\text{Total})} = \frac{v_o}{v_s} = +g_{m1} g_{m2} \cdot (R_{C1} \| R_{B2} \| r_{\pi 2}) \cdot (R_{C2} \| R_L)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **positive sign** confirms that the two CE inversions cancel — the output is **in phase** with the input in a two-stage CE-CE cascade.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Cascode Mid-Band Voltage Gain

### Given:

- $Q_1$ in CE, $Q_2$ in CB, sharing $I_C$, with $R_C$ at the collector of $Q_2$ and load $R_L$.

### Step 1 — Input signal at base of $Q_1$

$$v_{be1} = v_s \quad \text{(assuming ideal source)}$$

### Step 2 — Collector current of $Q_1$

$$i_{c1} = g_{m1} \cdot v_{be1} = g_m \cdot v_s$$

### Step 3 — Voltage at the collector of $Q_1$ (= emitter of $Q_2$)

This node sees the **input resistance of the CB stage** ($Q_2$) looking into its emitter:

$$R_{in(\text{emitter of CB})} = r_{e2} = \frac{\alpha_2}{g_{m2}} \approx \frac{1}{g_m}$$

The AC voltage at the collector of $Q_1$:

$$v_{c1} = -i_{c1} \cdot r_{e2} = -g_m v_s \cdot \frac{1}{g_m} = -v_s$$

So the CE stage in cascode has a **voltage gain of $-1$** (non-inverting buffer at low frequencies).

### Step 4 — Current flowing into the collector of $Q_2$

Since $Q_2$ is in CB, its collector current approximately equals its emitter current (with $\alpha \approx 1$):

$$i_{c2} \approx i_{e2} = -i_{c1} = -g_m v_s$$

(Here the negative sign is conventional based on current direction; magnitude flows out of the collector node.)

### Step 5 — Output voltage at the collector of $Q_2$

$$v_o = -i_{c2} \cdot (R_C \| R_L) = -(-g_m v_s) \cdot (R_C \| R_L)$$

$$\boxed{v_o = -g_m (R_C \| R_L) \cdot v_s \quad \Rightarrow \quad A_{V(\text{cascode})} = -g_m (R_C \| R_L)}$$

This is **numerically identical to a single CE stage**, but the bandwidth is dramatically wider.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Bandwidth Shrinkage in $n$ Identical Cascade Stages

### Given:

- Each stage has identical upper 3-dB frequency $f_{H1}$.
- Magnitude response of each stage: $\vert A_{V1}(f) \vert = \frac{A_{V0}}{\sqrt{1 + (f/f_{H1})^2}}$

### Step 1 — Overall response of $n$ stages

$$\vert A_{V(\text{total})}(f) \vert = \left[\frac{A_{V0}}{\sqrt{1 + (f/f_{H1})^2}}\right]^n = \frac{A_{V0}^n}{\left[1 + (f/f_{H1})^2\right]^{n/2}}$$

### Step 2 — Define overall cutoff frequency $f_H$

At $f = f_H$, the overall gain drops to $1/\sqrt{2}$ of its mid-band value:

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{1}{\left[1 + (f_H/f_{H1})^2\right]^{n/2}}$$

Raising both sides to the power $-2/n$:

$$2^{1/n} = 1 + \left(\frac{f_H}{f_{H1}}\right)^2$$

### Step 3 — Solve for $f_H$

$$\left(\frac{f_H}{f_{H1}}\right)^2 = 2^{1/n} - 1$$

$$\boxed{f_H = f_{H1} \cdot \sqrt{2^{1/n} - 1}}$$

### Step 4 — Verification for $n = 2$

$$f_H = f_{H1} \cdot \sqrt{2^{1/2} - 1} = f_{H1} \cdot \sqrt{1.414 - 1} = f_{H1} \cdot \sqrt{0.414} = f_{H1} \cdot 0.6439$$

This matches the standard **0.644 factor** for two-stage bandwidth shrinkage.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Design Procedure for a Two-Stage BJT Amplifier

### Problem Statement

> Design a two-stage BJT amplifier to achieve $A_{V(\text{total})} \geq 100$ (40 dB), $R_{in} \geq 2 \text{ k}\Omega$, and a load $R_L = 5 \text{ k}\Omega$. Use $V_{CC} = 12 \text{ V}$, $\beta = 100$, $V_{BE} = 0.7 \text{ V}$, and standard $V_T = 26 \text{ mV}$.

### Step 1 — Split the gain optimally

For maximum bandwidth, use **equal gain split**:

$$A_{V1} = A_{V2} = \sqrt{100} = 10$$

### Step 2 — Choose $I_C$ for sufficient $g_m$

Pick $I_C = 2 \text{ mA}$ for both transistors:

$$g_m = \frac{I_C}{V_T} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-3}}{26 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0769 \text{ A/V} \approx 77 \text{ mA/V}$$

### Step 3 — Determine $R_{in}$ requirement

$$r_\pi = \frac{\beta}{g_m} = \frac{100}{0.0769} = 1300 \, \Omega \approx 1.3 \text{ k}\Omega$$

This is below the 2 k$\Omega$ requirement, so $R_B$ (the bias divider) must be sized to bring the parallel combination above 2 k$\Omega$. Choose:

$$R_{B1} \| R_{B2} = 5 \text{ k}\Omega \quad \text{(typical bias divider)}$$

$$R_{in} = R_{B1} \| R_{B2} \| r_\pi \approx 5\text{k} \| 1.3\text{k} \approx 1.03 \text{ k}\Omega$$

For $R_{in} \geq 2 \text{ k}\Omega$, increase the bias divider or reduce $I_C$ to increase $r_\pi$. Try $I_C = 1 \text{ mA}$:

$$g_m = 1/0.026 = 0.0385 \text{ A/V}, \quad r_\pi = 100/0.0385 = 2.6 \text{ k}\Omega$$

Now with $R_{B1} \| R_{B2} = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$:

$$R_{in} = 10\text{k} \| 2.6\text{k} = 2.06 \text{ k}\Omega \quad \checkmark$$

### Step 4 — Determine collector resistor for required gain

For stage 2, the gain is $A_{V2} = -g_m (R_{C2} \| R_L) = -10$:

$$R_{C2} \| 5\text{k} = \frac{10}{g_m} = \frac{10}{0.0385} = 260 \, \Omega$$

Solving for $R_{C2}$:

$$R_{C2} = \frac{260 \times 5000}{5000 - 260} = 274 \, \Omega$$

This is impractically small. Reduce $I_C$ further or use a higher $R_L$ design.

### Step 5 — Recompute with $I_C = 0.5 \text{ mA}$

$$g_m = 0.5/26 = 0.0192 \text{ A/V}, \quad r_\pi = 100/0.0192 = 5.2 \text{ k}\Omega$$

For $A_{V2} = 10$:

$$R_{C2} \| 5\text{k} = 10/0.0192 = 520 \, \Omega$$

$$R_{C2} = \frac{520 \times 5000}{5000 - 520} = 580 \, \Omega$$

Use $R_{C2} = 560 \, \Omega$ (standard value). Check: $A_{V2} = 0.0192 \times (560 \| 5000) = 0.0192 \times 504 = 9.68 \approx 10 \quad \checkmark$

For stage 1, the effective load is $R_{C1} \| R_{B2,\text{total}} \| r_{\pi 2}$. Assuming $R_{B,\text{total}} = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$ and $r_{\pi 2} = 5.2 \text{ k}\Omega$:

$$R_{B2,\text{total}} \| r_{\pi 2} = 10\text{k} \| 5.2\text{k} = 3.42 \text{ k}\Omega$$

For $A_{V1} = 10$:

$$R_{C1} \| 3.42\text{k} = 10/0.0192 = 520 \, \Omega$$

$$R_{C1} = \frac{520 \times 3420}{3420 - 520} = 612 \, \Omega$$

Use $R_{C1} = 620 \, \Omega$ (standard value).

### Step 6 — Design the bias network for both stages

Use the standard **voltage-divider bias** with $R_{B1} + R_{B2} \leq 0.1 \cdot \beta \cdot R_C$ for bias stability. Choose:

For each stage: $R_{B1} = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_{B2} = 4.7 \text{ k}\Omega$ to set $V_{BE,\text{off}} = 0.7 \text{ V}$ approximately.

Solve KVL: $V_{BB} = V_{CC} \cdot R_{B2}/(R_{B1}+R_{B2}) = 12 \cdot 4.7/14.7 = 3.84 \text{ V}$

Then $V_E = V_{BB} - V_{BE} = 3.84 - 0.7 = 3.14 \text{ V}$. The emitter resistor:

$$R_E = V_E / I_E = 3.14 / 0.5\text{ mA} = 6.28 \text{ k}\Omega \quad \rightarrow \text{use } 6.2 \text{ k}\Omega$$

### Final Design Summary

| Component | Stage 1 | Stage 2 |
|---|---|---|
| $I_C$ | 0.5 mA | 0.5 mA |
| $g_m$ | 19.2 mA/V | 19.2 mA/V |
| $r_\pi$ | 5.2 k$\Omega$ | 5.2 k$\Omega$ |
| $R_C$ | 620 $\Omega$ | 560 $\Omega$ |
| $R_E$ | 6.2 k$\Omega$ | 6.2 k$\Omega$ |
| $R_{B1}$ | 10 k$\Omega$ | 10 k$\Omega$ |
| $R_{B2}$ | 4.7 k$\Omega$ | 4.7 k$\Omega$ |
| $C_C$ (input/inter/output) | 10 $\mu$F | 10 $\mu$F |
| $C_E$ (bypass) | 100 $\mu$F | 100 $\mu$F |

$$\boxed{A_{V(\text{Total})} \approx 10 \times 10 = 100 \quad (40 \text{ dB}) \quad \checkmark}$$
