# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Q: Define a multistage amplifier. Why is it necessary to cascade multiple amplifier stages in a practical amplifier design?**

**Model Answer:**

A multistage amplifier is a combination of two or more single-stage amplifier configurations connected in cascade (series) so that the output of one stage drives the input of the next. The overall voltage gain is the product of the individual stage gains: $A_{V,\text{total}} = A_{V1} \times A_{V2} \times \dots \times A_{Vn}$.

Cascading is necessary because:
1. **Gain limitation of a single stage:** A single CE stage provides a gain of typically 50–200, but many applications (e.g., audio amplifiers, op-amp open-loop designs) require gains of $10^4$ to $10^6$.
2. **Impedance matching:** Input and output impedances can be optimized independently at each stage.
3. **Bandwidth shaping:** Different stages can be designed for different frequency responses.
4. **DC stability:** Stages can be AC-coupled to avoid DC drift accumulation.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Q: Compare the cascade and cascode amplifier topologies with respect to bandwidth and gain.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Cascade (CE-CE) | Cascode (CE-CB) |
|---|---|---|
| Overall voltage gain | $A_{V1} \times A_{V2}$ (multiplicative) | $\approx g_m R_C$ (single-stage equivalent) |
| Bandwidth | Suffers bandwidth shrinkage by factor 0.644 for two stages | **Extended** by factor $\approx g_m R_C$ due to Miller suppression |
| Miller effect | Severe (CB at $Q_2$ collector) | **Suppressed** (gain of CE in cascode $\approx -1$) |
| DC bias | Two independent bias networks | **Shared single bias** current |
| Output resistance | Moderate ($R_{C2}$) | High ($\approx r_{o2}$) |

**Conclusion:** Cascade gives high gain, cascode gives wide bandwidth. For wideband high-gain applications, **multiple cascode stages** are cascaded.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]`

**Q (a): Draw the AC equivalent circuit of a two-stage CE-CE RC-coupled amplifier and derive the expression for the overall voltage gain. State the loading effect of the second stage on the first.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Draw the AC equivalent circuit:**

At mid-band, all coupling and bypass capacitors are short circuits, and all internal capacitances are open. The resulting AC equivalent has:
- $Q_1$ replaced by its hybrid-π model ($r_{\pi 1}$, $g_{m1} v_{be1}$, $r_{o1}$ neglected for simplicity).
- $Q_2$ similarly replaced.
- $R_{B1} \| R_{B2}$ in parallel with $r_{\pi 1}$ at input.
- $R_{C1}$ in parallel with $R_{B3} \| R_{B4} \| r_{\pi 2}$ at the collector of $Q_1$.

**Step 2 — Apply small-signal analysis:**

Let $v_s$ be the source. Assuming $R_s = 0$, then $v_{be1} = v_s$.

$$i_{c1} = g_{m1} v_s$$

$$v_{o1} = -i_{c1} \cdot (R_{C1} \| R_{B,\text{stage2}} \| r_{\pi 2})$$

$$v_{be2} = v_{o1}$$

$$i_{c2} = g_{m2} \cdot v_{be2} = -g_{m1} g_{m2} v_s \cdot (R_{C1} \| R_{B,\text{stage2}} \| r_{\pi 2})$$

$$v_o = -i_{c2} \cdot (R_{C2} \| R_L)$$

**Step 3 — Overall voltage gain:**

$$A_{V,\text{total}} = \frac{v_o}{v_s} = g_{m1} g_{m2} \cdot (R_{C1} \| R_{B,\text{stage2}} \| r_{\pi 2}) \cdot (R_{C2} \| R_L)$$

**Step 4 — Loading effect:**

The input resistance of stage 2 ($R_{B,\text{stage2}} \| r_{\pi 2}$) appears in parallel with $R_{C1}$, **reducing the effective load** of stage 1. This is the **loading effect**. If stage 2 had infinite input impedance, $A_{V1}$ would be $-g_{m1} R_{C1}$; with loading, it falls to $-g_{m1} (R_{C1} \| R_{in2})$.

---

**Q (b): For a two-stage cascade with each stage having $f_{H1} = 20 \text{ kHz}$, compute the overall upper 3-dB frequency. If the desired overall bandwidth is 50 kHz, what should be the individual stage bandwidth? Comment on the bandwidth shrinkage. (Assume identical non-interacting stages.)** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Overall $f_H$ for two identical stages:**

$$f_H = f_{H1} \cdot \sqrt{2^{1/2} - 1} = 20 \times \sqrt{0.4142} = 20 \times 0.6436 = 12.87 \text{ kHz}$$

**Step 2 — Required individual $f_{H1}$ for $f_H = 50 \text{ kHz}$:**

$$f_{H1} = \frac{f_H}{\sqrt{2^{1/n} - 1}} = \frac{50}{0.6436} = 77.7 \text{ kHz}$$

**Step 3 — Comments on bandwidth shrinkage:**

- The overall bandwidth **shrinks** by a factor of $\sqrt{2^{1/n} - 1}$ when $n$ identical stages are cascaded.
- For $n=2$, shrinkage factor is **0.644**; for $n=3$, it is **0.510**; for $n=4$, it is **0.435**.
- To maintain a desired overall bandwidth, each individual stage must be designed with a **wider** bandwidth — by the reciprocal shrinkage factor.
- This is the **bandwidth-gain trade-off** in multistage amplifiers: you cannot increase gain indefinitely without sacrificing bandwidth.

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]`

**Q (a): With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a cascode amplifier. Derive the expression for its mid-band voltage gain and show how the Miller effect is reduced compared to a common-emitter stage.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Circuit description:**

The cascode consists of $Q_1$ (CE) stacked on $Q_2$ (CB). The base of $Q_2$ is held at a fixed DC bias (typically $V_{CC}/2$) and is AC-grounded via a bypass capacitor. The input signal enters at the base of $Q_1$. The output is taken at the collector of $Q_2$, which has the load $R_C$ (or active load).

**Step 2 — Small-signal analysis:**

The input resistance of the CB stage ($Q_2$) looking into its emitter is $r_{e2} = 1/g_{m2} = 1/g_m$. The collector of $Q_1$ sees this low impedance. The gain from base of $Q_1$ to collector of $Q_1$ is:

$$A_{V1,\text{cascode}} = -g_{m1} \cdot r_{e2} = -g_m \cdot \frac{1}{g_m} = -1$$

So the Miller multiplication factor becomes:

$$C_{M,\text{eff}} = C_{bc}(1 - A_{V1}) = C_{bc}(1 - (-1)) = 2 C_{bc}$$

**Step 3 — Output of the cascode:**

The current through $Q_2$ is approximately $i_{c2} \approx -i_{c1} = -g_m v_{be1}$. The output voltage at the collector of $Q_2$:

$$v_o = -i_{c2} \cdot (R_C \| R_L) = -g_m (R_C \| R_L) \cdot v_{be1}$$

**Step 4 — Cascode voltage gain:**

$$\boxed{A_{V,\text{cascode}} = -g_m (R_C \| R_L)}$$

**Step 5 — Miller effect comparison:**

| Topology | Miller Multiplication | Effective $C_{bc}$ |
|---|---|---|
| Single CE ($A_V \approx -g_m R_C$) | $(1 + g_m R_C) \approx 100$ | $100 C_{bc}$ |
| Cascode ($A_V \approx -1$) | $(1 - (-1)) = 2$ | $2 C_{bc}$ |

The cascode reduces the Miller capacitance by a factor of **$(g_m R_C)/2 \approx 50$**, dramatically extending the upper 3-dB frequency.

---

**Q (b): Design a two-stage BJT cascade amplifier to deliver a mid-band voltage gain of 60 dB into a 4 k$\Omega$ load. The available supply is $V_{CC} = 15 \text{ V}$. Assume identical stages with $\beta = 100$, $V_{BE} = 0.7 \text{ V}$, and $V_T = 26 \text{ mV}$. Compute $g_m$, $r_\pi$, $R_C$, and $R_E$ for each stage.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert dB to linear:**

$$A_{V,\text{total}} = 10^{60/20} = 10^3 = 1000$$

**Step 2 — Split gain equally for maximum bandwidth:**

$$A_{V1} = A_{V2} = \sqrt{1000} = 31.62$$

**Step 3 — Choose $I_C$ and compute $g_m$ and $r_\pi$:**

Choose $I_C = 1 \text{ mA}$ for moderate power consumption.

$$g_m = \frac{I_C}{V_T} = \frac{1 \text{ mA}}{26 \text{ mV}} = 38.46 \text{ mA/V}$$

$$r_\pi = \frac{\beta}{g_m} = \frac{100}{0.03846} = 2600 \, \Omega = 2.6 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 4 — Determine $R_C$ for stage 2:**

For $A_{V2} = -31.62$:

$$g_m \cdot (R_{C2} \| R_L) = 31.62$$

$$R_{C2} \| 4\text{k} = \frac{31.62}{0.03846} = 822 \, \Omega$$

$$R_{C2} = \frac{822 \times 4000}{4000 - 822} = 1034 \, \Omega$$

Use $R_{C2} = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$ (standard value). Recompute: $A_{V2} = 0.03846 \times (1000 \| 4000) = 0.03846 \times 800 = 30.77$. Acceptable.

**Step 5 — Determine $R_C$ for stage 1:**

Assume bias divider of stage 2 has $R_{B,\text{total}} = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$:

$$R_{B,\text{total}} \| r_{\pi 2} = 10\text{k} \| 2.6\text{k} = 2.07 \text{ k}\Omega$$

For $A_{V1} = -31.62$:

$$R_{C1} \| 2.07\text{k} = \frac{31.62}{0.03846} = 822 \, \Omega$$

$$R_{C1} = \frac{822 \times 2070}{2070 - 822} = 1361 \, \Omega$$

Use $R_{C1} = 1.3 \text{ k}\Omega$ (standard value).

**Step 6 — Determine $R_E$ for bias stability:**

Choose $V_E = 0.1 \cdot V_{CC} = 1.5 \text{ V}$ (rule of thumb):

$$R_E = \frac{V_E}{I_E} \approx \frac{1.5}{1 \text{ mA}} = 1.5 \text{ k}\Omega$$

Use $R_E = 1.5 \text{ k}\Omega$ for both stages with bypass capacitor $C_E$.

**Final Design Table:**

| Component | Stage 1 | Stage 2 |
|---|---|---|
| $I_C$ | 1 mA | 1 mA |
| $g_m$ | 38.46 mA/V | 38.46 mA/V |
| $r_\pi$ | 2.6 k$\Omega$ | 2.6 k$\Omega$ |
| $R_C$ | 1.3 k$\Omega$ | 1 k$\Omega$ |
| $R_E$ | 1.5 k$\Omega$ | 1.5 k$\Omega$ |
| $R_{B1}$ | 10 k$\Omega$ | 10 k$\Omega$ |
| $R_{B2}$ | 4.7 k$\Omega$ | 4.7 k$\Omega$ |
| $A_{V,\text{stage}}$ | 31.6 | 30.8 |
| $A_{V,\text{total}}$ | $\approx 974$ | $\approx 59.8 \text{ dB} \checkmark$ |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes in KTU Exam Papers (causing 2–5 mark loss):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the loading effect:** Students compute $A_{V1} = -g_{m1} R_{C1}$ without including $R_{in2}$ in parallel with $R_{C1}$. **Always include $R_{in2} = R_{B2} \| r_{\pi 2}$** in the effective load of stage 1.
>
> 2. **Sign errors in cascade gain:** Two CE stages give a **positive** overall gain (non-inverting). One CE stage gives a **negative** gain. Mix these up and lose 2 marks.
>
> 3. **Cascode gain confusion:** Students sometimes think cascode gives "double the gain of CE." It does **NOT**. The cascode has approximately the **same gain as a single CE stage**, but with much **wider bandwidth**.
>
> 4. **Bandwidth shrinkage factor:** Using $f_H = f_{H1}/2$ for two stages is **WRONG**. The correct factor is $0.644$, not $0.5$. This is a frequently tested numerical question.
>
> 5. **dB conversion mistake:** Converting voltage gain to dB using $10 \log_{10}$ instead of $20 \log_{10}$ is a common error. Remember: dB for voltage/power uses $20 \log_{10}(V_{\text{ratio}})$.
>
> 6. **Missing bypass capacitor:** In the AC equivalent, $R_E$ must be **shorted** by $C_E$ to get the full gain. If you forget this, the gain expression must include the unbypassed $R_E$ factor $(1 + g_m R_E)$ in the denominator.
>
> 7. **Confusing $r_\pi$ and $r_e$:** $r_\pi = \beta / g_m$ is the **base resistance**. $r_e = 1/g_m$ is the **emitter resistance**. They differ by a factor of $\beta$. Mixing them in formulas leads to incorrect answers.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam:**
>
> - [ ] A **multistage amplifier** combines multiple transistor stages to achieve higher gain, better impedance matching, and controlled bandwidth.
> - [ ] In a **cascade**, the overall gain is the **product** of individual gains: $A_{V,\text{total}} = \prod A_{Vi}$.
> - [ ] In a **cascade**, dB gains **add**: $A_{V,\text{dB,total}} = \sum A_{Vi,\text{dB}}$.
> - [ ] The **loading effect** means $R_{in}$ of the next stage **reduces** the effective load of the previous stage. Always include $R_{in2}$ in $R_{L1}'$.
> - [ ] A **two-stage CE-CE cascade** produces a **non-inverted** output (two phase inversions cancel).
> - [ ] The **cascode amplifier** is a CE stage stacked on a CB stage, sharing a single bias current. It is **electrically one stage** but **topologically two transistors**.
> - [ ] **Cascode voltage gain** $\approx -g_m (R_C \| R_L)$ — same magnitude as a single CE stage.
> - [ ] **Cascode bandwidth** is much wider because the Miller multiplication factor is reduced from $(1 + g_m R_C)$ to just **2**.
> - [ ] **Bandwidth shrinkage** for $n$ identical stages: $f_H = f_{H1} \sqrt{2^{1/n} - 1}$.
> - [ ] For $n = 2$: shrinkage factor = **0.644**. For $n = 3$: **0.510**.
> - [ ] **Rise time accumulation:** $t_r = \sqrt{t_{r1}^2 + t_{r2}^2 + \dots + t_{rn}^2}$.
> - [ ] **$g_m = I_C / V_T$** at $V_T = 26 \text{ mV}$.
> - [ ] **$r_\pi = \beta V_T / I_C = \beta / g_m$**.
> - [ ] **$r_e = \alpha / g_m \approx 1/g_m$**.
> - [ ] **AC equivalent at mid-band:** all coupling caps are shorts, all internal capacitances are opens.
> - [ ] **AC equivalent at low frequency:** coupling and bypass caps contribute poles at $f_L = 1/(2\pi R C)$.
> - [ ] **AC equivalent at high frequency:** $C_{bc}$ and $C_{be}$ contribute poles, with $C_{bc}$ multiplied by $(1 - A_V)$ (Miller effect).
> - [ ] **Design strategy:** For a target gain $A_{V,\text{total}}$, split equally between stages: $A_{V1} = A_{V2} = \sqrt{A_{V,\text{total}}}$ for maximum bandwidth.
> - [ ] **Bias design:** Use $V_E = 0.1 V_{CC}$ as a starting point for $R_E$, and $R_{B1}/R_{B2}$ to set $V_{BB} = V_{BE} + V_E$.
> - [ ] **Cascode vs cascade summary:** Cascade → high gain; Cascode → wide bandwidth. Combine them: cascade of cascodes gives **both** high gain and wide bandwidth — the basis of modern RF IC design.

---

*End of Module 2 Notes — Multistage Amplifiers (Cascade and Cascode Design)*
*Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme — PCAET302 Electronic Devices and Circuits*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effect on Gain and Bandwidth
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET302 - ELECTRONIC DEVICES AND CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Effect on Gain and Bandwidth

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Effect on Gain and Bandwidth"?

In the context of single-stage **BJT/FET RC-coupled amplifiers**, the term *"Effect on Gain and Bandwidth"* refers to the systematic study of how various capacitive elements in the amplifier circuit — namely the **input/output coupling capacitors**, the **emitter/source bypass capacitor**, and the **internal transistor capacitances** ($C_{be}$, $C_{bc}$, $C_{gs}$, $C_{gd}$) — modify the **voltage gain** ($A_v$) and the **bandwidth** ($BW = f_H - f_L$) of the amplifier as a function of the operating signal frequency $f$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCAET302 - Module 2):**
> *"Effect on Gain and Bandwidth"* is the analytical study of the frequency response of a single-stage transistor amplifier, which determines how the **mid-band gain** ($A_{mid}$) is maintained within a flat region bounded by a **lower cutoff frequency** ($f_L$) and an **upper cutoff frequency** ($f_H$), and how the **bandwidth** ($BW$) and **gain-bandwidth product** ($GBW = A_{mid} \cdot BW$) characterize the amplifier's frequency-dependent performance.

---

## 1.2 The Three Distinct Frequency Regions of an Amplifier

The voltage gain magnitude $\vert A_v \vert$ of an RC-coupled transistor amplifier is **not constant** across all frequencies. It exhibits a characteristic **band-pass response** with three distinct regions:

| Region | Frequency Range | Controlling Elements | Gain Behavior |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Low-Frequency Region** | $f < f_L$ | Coupling Capacitors ($C_{in}$, $C_{out}$), Bypass Capacitor ($C_E$) | Gain **decreases** as $f$ decreases |
| **Mid-Band Region** | $f_L \le f \le f_H$ | All capacitors treated as ideal (short at high f, open at low f) | Gain is **maximum and constant** ($= A_{mid}$) |
| **High-Frequency Region** | $f > f_H$ | Internal Transistor Capacitances ($C_{be}$, $C_{bc}$ or $C_\pi$, $C_\mu$) | Gain **decreases** as $f$ increases |

> [!NOTE]
> **Where $f_L$ and $f_H$ Come From:**
> - $f_L$ is the **lower cutoff frequency** where gain falls to $\frac{A_{mid}}{\sqrt{2}}$ (i.e., **−3 dB** point) on the low-frequency side.
> - $f_H$ is the **upper cutoff frequency** where gain again falls to $\frac{A_{mid}}{\sqrt{2}}$ on the high-frequency side.
> - **Bandwidth** is defined as $BW = f_H - f_L \approx f_H$ (since $f_H \gg f_L$ in most practical amplifiers).

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy: The Audio Speaker System

Imagine a **car audio amplifier** connected to a **speaker**:

- **Coupling Capacitors** are like the **door to a concert hall**. They let the music signal (AC) into the amplifier, but block any DC bias from the previous stage. At very low frequencies (deep bass that the door cannot pass easily), the music is **muffled** → **low-frequency gain drops**.

- **Bypass Capacitor** is like a **shortcut path for the bass notes** around the emitter resistor. At low frequencies, if the bypass capacitor is "too small," the bass frequencies get "stuck" in the emitter resistor, causing **loss of low-frequency gain**.

- **Internal Transistor Capacitances** are like the **inertia of the speaker cone**. At very high frequencies (treble), the speaker cone cannot move fast enough, so the high notes are **damped** → **high-frequency gain drops**.

The "sweet spot" in the middle, where the music sounds **full and balanced**, is the **mid-band region** where the gain is at its peak value. The **bandwidth** of the audio amplifier is essentially the range of frequencies the speaker can faithfully reproduce.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineer's Mnemonic — "CLOW-HIGH":**
> - **C**oupling & bypass **C**apacitors → **LOW** frequency response
> - **HIGH** frequency response → **H**ybrid-π internal capacitances ($C_\pi$, $C_\mu$)

---

## 1.4 The Critical Trade-off: Gain vs. Bandwidth

One of the most important and counter-intuitive results in amplifier design is the **Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBW)**:

$$GBW = A_{mid} \times BW = A_{mid} \times f_H \approx \text{constant (for a given transistor & bias)}$$

This means that **you cannot increase the mid-band gain without sacrificing bandwidth**, and vice-versa. Doubling the gain approximately halves the bandwidth. This is a fundamental limitation imposed by the physics of the transistor.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Misconception:** "Adding more gain stages increases both gain AND bandwidth." This is **FALSE** for the upper cutoff. Cascading $n$ identical stages multiplies the gain by $A^n$ but **reduces the upper cutoff** to approximately $\frac{f_H}{\sqrt{2^{1/n} - 1}}$. The overall bandwidth **shrinks** with cascading.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Hybrid-π Equivalent Circuit — Foundation for High-Frequency Analysis

To study the effect on gain and bandwidth, we replace the **h-parameter model** (valid only at low/mid frequencies) with the more accurate **Hybrid-π model** which includes the internal capacitances:

**Key Hybrid-π Parameters:**
- $r_\pi$ = Base-emitter dynamic resistance = $\frac{\beta}{g_m}$
- $g_m$ = Transconductance = $\frac{I_C}{V_T}$ (where $V_T \approx 26 \text{ mV}$ at room temperature)
- $r_o$ = Output resistance (due to Early effect, often neglected)
- $C_\pi$ = Base-emitter diffusion + junction capacitance
- $C_\mu$ = Base-collector junction capacitance (Miller capacitance)
- $r_b$ = Base spreading resistance

The voltage gain expression in the **mid-band** (where all capacitors are effectively short or open) for a **CE amplifier** with emitter fully bypassed is:

$$A_{v,mid} = -g_m (R_C \parallel R_L) = -g_m R_{L}^{'}$$

where $R_{L}^{'} = R_C \parallel R_L$ is the **effective AC load**.

---

## 2.2 Why Coupling and Bypass Capacitors Reduce Low-Frequency Gain

In the **low-frequency region**, the reactance of the coupling and bypass capacitors becomes significant (they are no longer "AC shorts"). Each capacitor, in conjunction with the surrounding resistance, forms an **RC high-pass filter** with a cutoff frequency:

$$f_L = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{eq} C_{eq}}$$

The three major contributors to the **overall lower cutoff** $f_{L,total}$ are:

1. **Input Coupling Capacitor** $C_{in}$:
   $$f_{L1} = \frac{1}{2\pi (R_S + R_{in}) C_{in}}$$
   where $R_{in} = R_B \parallel \text{Input Impedance of Transistor}$.

2. **Output Coupling Capacitor** $C_{out}$:
   $$f_{L2} = \frac{1}{2\pi (R_C + R_L) C_{out}}$$

3. **Emitter Bypass Capacitor** $C_E$ (often the **dominant** contributor):
   $$f_{LE} = \frac{1}{2\pi (R_E \parallel r_e) C_E}$$
   where $r_e = \frac{1}{g_m} = \frac{V_T}{I_E}$.

**Combining the three** (the dominant pole approximation, valid when poles are widely separated):

$$f_L \approx f_{L,dominant} = \max(f_{L1}, f_{L2}, f_{LE})$$

A more accurate combined formula:

$$f_L \approx \sqrt{f_{L1}^2 + f_{L2}^2 + f_{LE}^2}$$

---

## 2.3 Why Internal Capacitances Reduce High-Frequency Gain

In the **high-frequency region**, the internal capacitances $C_\pi$ and $C_\mu$ provide **shunt paths** to ground, which **bypass the signal** away from the output. Each capacitance forms an **RC low-pass filter**.

The key insight is **Miller's Theorem**: $C_\mu$ appears between input and output (gain $A_v$ from input to output). By Miller's theorem, the effective input capacitance becomes:

$$C_{M,in} = C_\mu (1 - A_v) = C_\mu (1 + g_m R_{L}^{'}) \approx C_\mu \cdot g_m R_{L}^{'}$$

This **dramatically multiplies** $C_\mu$ and is the **primary reason** the upper cutoff $f_H$ is so much lower than what the transistor's intrinsic $f_T$ would suggest.

The upper cutoff frequency of a CE amplifier (neglecting $r_o$, $r_b$, and $r_\mu$):

$$f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{eq,th} \cdot C_{eq,th}}$$

where:
- $C_{eq,th} = C_\pi + C_{M,in} = C_\pi + C_\mu (1 + g_m R_{L}^{'})$
- $R_{eq,th} = (R_S \parallel R_B \parallel r_\pi) \parallel r_b \approx R_S \parallel r_\pi$ (typically)

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table — these formulas are repeatedly tested in KTU exams.**

| # | Parameter / Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Mid-band Voltage Gain (CE, bypassed) | $A_{v,mid} = -g_m R_{L}^{'}$ | Dimensionless |
| 2 | Mid-band Voltage Gain (CE, unbypassed) | $A_{v,mid} = -\frac{g_m R_{L}^{'}}{1 + g_m R_E}$ | $R_E$ is un-bypassed emitter resistance |
| 3 | Transconductance | $g_m = \frac{I_C}{V_T}$ | Siemens (S); $V_T \approx 26$ mV at 300 K |
| 4 | Base-Emitter Dynamic Resistance | $r_\pi = \frac{\beta}{g_m} = \frac{\beta V_T}{I_C}$ | Ohms ($\Omega$) |
| 5 | Lower cutoff from $C_{in}$ | $f_{L1} = \frac{1}{2\pi (R_S + R_{in}) C_{in}}$ | Hz |
| 6 | Lower cutoff from $C_{out}$ | $f_{L2} = \frac{1}{2\pi (R_C + R_L) C_{out}}$ | Hz |
| 7 | Lower cutoff from $C_E$ (bypass) | $f_{LE} = \frac{1}{2\pi (R_E \parallel r_e) C_E}$ | Hz; usually dominant |
| 8 | Overall lower cutoff (dominant pole) | $f_L \approx \max(f_{L1}, f_{L2}, f_{LE})$ | Hz |
| 9 | Miller Input Capacitance | $C_{M,in} = C_\mu (1 + g_m R_{L}^{'})$ | Farads |
| 10 | Equivalent High-Frequency Capacitance | $C_{th} = C_\pi + C_\mu (1 + g_m R_{L}^{'})$ | Farads |
| 11 | Upper cutoff frequency | $f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{th} C_{th}}$ | Hz |
| 12 | Bandwidth | $BW = f_H - f_L \approx f_H$ | Hz |
| 13 | Gain-Bandwidth Product (CE) | $GBW = \vert A_{v,mid} \vert \cdot f_H = \frac{g_m R_{L}^{'}}{2\pi R_{th} C_{th}}$ | Hz |
| 14 | Transistor Transition Frequency | $f_T = g_m / [2\pi (C_\pi + C_\mu)]$ | Hz (intrinsic GBW of BJT) |
| 15 | Effective GBW with loading | $GBW = \frac{f_T}{1 + (R_C \parallel R_L)(C_\mu \cdot 2\pi f_T) / g_m}$ | Hz — practical form |
| 16 | Emitter Follower Gain (CC) | $A_v = \frac{g_m R_E^{'}}{1 + g_m R_E^{'}} \approx 1$ | $\approx 1$ (voltage follower) |
| 17 | Overall Gain (dB) at any $f$ | $A_{v,dB} = 20 \log_{10} \vert A_v \vert$ | Decibels (dB) |
| 18 | Decibel Definition for Power | $dB = 10 \log_{10}(P_2/P_1)$ | For power ratio |
| 19 | Decibel Definition for Voltage | $dB = 20 \log_{10}(V_2/V_1)$ | For voltage ratio (assuming same $R$) |
| 20 | Emitter AC resistance | $r_e = \frac{V_T}{I_E} = \frac{26 \text{ mV}}{I_E}$ | $\Omega$ |

---

## 2.5 Step-by-Step Logic of the Frequency Response

The amplifier's frequency response is governed by the following chain of physical effects:

1. **At very low frequencies** ($f \to 0$): The coupling capacitors ($C_{in}$, $C_{out}$) behave as **open circuits**, completely blocking the signal from reaching the next stage. The bypass capacitor $C_E$ also becomes an open circuit, making the unbypassed $R_E$ appear in the signal path, which **drastically reduces** the gain by a factor of $(1 + g_m R_E)$.

2. **As $f$ increases** past the lowest $f_L$: Each of the three capacitors starts to "wake up" and act more like a short circuit. The gain begins to **rise** at **+20 dB/decade** for each capacitor that becomes effective.

3. **In the mid-band** ($f_L \ll f \ll f_H$): All coupling/bypass capacitors are effective shorts, and the internal capacitances are still open. The gain is **flat** at the maximum value $A_{v,mid}$.

4. **As $f$ increases** past $f_H$: The internal capacitances $C_\pi$ and $C_\mu$ start to conduct significant current, **shunting** the signal to ground at the input. The gain begins to **fall** at **−20 dB/decade** (for a single dominant pole).

5. **At very high frequencies** ($f \to \infty$): The internal capacitances become effective shorts, and the gain approaches **zero**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Decade Rule:** A single-pole RC network produces a gain roll-off of **−20 dB/decade** (equivalently, **−6 dB/octave**). The −3 dB cutoff is where the gain has dropped to $1/\sqrt{2} \approx 0.707$ of its mid-band value.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Lower Cutoff Frequency due to Emitter Bypass Capacitor (CE Amplifier)

**Given:** Common-Emitter (CE) amplifier with emitter resistor $R_E$ and bypass capacitor $C_E$.

**Circuit at low frequency (small-signal hybrid-π with $C_E$ in parallel with $R_E$):**

The impedance of the emitter branch is:
$$Z_E = R_E \parallel \frac{1}{j\omega C_E} = \frac{R_E \cdot \frac{1}{j\omega C_E}}{R_E + \frac{1}{j\omega C_E}} = \frac{R_E}{1 + j\omega R_E C_E}$$

The voltage gain at low frequency with this complex emitter impedance:

$$A_v(\omega) = -\frac{g_m R_{L}^{'}}{1 + g_m Z_E} = -\frac{g_m R_{L}^{'}}{1 + g_m \cdot \frac{R_E}{1 + j\omega R_E C_E}}$$

Multiply numerator and denominator by $(1 + j\omega R_E C_E)$:

$$A_v(\omega) = -\frac{g_m R_{L}^{'} (1 + j\omega R_E C_E)}{(1 + j\omega R_E C_E) + g_m R_E}$$

$$A_v(\omega) = -\frac{g_m R_{L}^{'} (1 + j\omega R_E C_E)}{1 + g_m R_E + j\omega R_E C_E}$$

$$\text{Factor } (1 + g_m R_E) \text{ from denominator:}$$

$$A_v(\omega) = -\frac{g_m R_{L}^{'}}{1 + g_m R_E} \cdot \frac{1 + j\omega R_E C_E}{1 + j\omega \frac{R_E C_E}{1 + g_m R_E}}$$

The **mid-band gain** (unbypassed) is:
$$A_{v,mid} = -\frac{g_m R_{L}^{'}}{1 + g_m R_E}$$

The **upper frequency factor** in the denominator gives the pole at:
$$\omega_{LE} = \frac{1 + g_m R_E}{R_E C_E} = \frac{1}{(R_E \parallel r_e) C_E}$$

Converting to Hz:
$$f_{LE} = \frac{1}{2\pi (R_E \parallel r_e) C_E}$$

**Reasoning:** The bypass capacitor $C_E$ does NOT see the full $R_E$; it sees $R_E$ in parallel with the small emitter resistance $r_e$ (looking from the emitter into the emitter-base junction, the impedance is approximately $r_e = 1/g_m$). Since $r_e \ll R_E$, the parallel combination is approximately $r_e$, which is **small**, making $f_{LE}$ **high** and hence the **dominant** lower cutoff in most practical designs.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Upper Cutoff Frequency using Miller's Theorem

**Given:** CE amplifier with hybrid-π model. The internal feedback capacitance $C_\mu$ is between collector (output) and base (input).

**Step 1 — Identify the gain across $C_\mu$:**

The voltage gain from base to collector in mid-band is $A_v = -g_m R_{L}^{'}$. Hence, the voltage across $C_\mu$ is:
$$V_\mu = V_{be} - V_{ce} = V_{be}(1 - A_v) = V_{be}(1 + g_m R_{L}^{'})$$

**Step 2 — Apply Miller's Theorem:**

Miller's theorem states that an impedance $Z$ between input and output (where voltage gain is $A_v$) can be replaced by:
- Input-side: $Z_{in,M} = \frac{Z}{1 - A_v}$
- Output-side: $Z_{out,M} = \frac{Z \cdot A_v}{A_v - 1}$

For a capacitor $C_\mu$ with impedance $Z_\mu = \frac{1}{j\omega C_\mu}$:

Input-side Miller impedance:
$$Z_{\mu, in} = \frac{1/(j\omega C_\mu)}{1 - A_v} = \frac{1}{j\omega C_\mu (1 - A_v)}$$

Since $A_v$ is negative, $(1 - A_v) = (1 + g_m R_{L}^{'})$. This is equivalent to a **larger capacitance**:
$$C_{\mu, in} = C_\mu (1 - A_v) = C_\mu (1 + g_m R_{L}^{'})$$

**Step 3 — Identify the input node total capacitance:**

At the input node (base of transistor), the total capacitance to AC ground is:
$$C_{in,total} = C_\pi + C_{\mu, in} = C_\pi + C_\mu (1 + g_m R_{L}^{'})$$

**Step 4 — Identify the input-side driving resistance:**

Thevenin equivalent resistance seen by $C_{in,total}$:
$$R_{th} = R_S \parallel R_B \parallel r_\pi$$

(In most problems, $R_B \gg r_\pi$ and $R_S \ll r_\pi$, so $R_{th} \approx R_S \parallel r_\pi$.)

**Step 5 — Form the RC low-pass filter:**

The input node is now a simple RC low-pass network. The −3 dB frequency is:

$$f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{th} C_{in,total}} = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{th} [C_\pi + C_\mu (1 + g_m R_{L}^{'})]}$$

**Step 6 — Compute the Gain-Bandwidth Product:**

$$GBW = \vert A_{v,mid} \vert \cdot f_H = g_m R_{L}^{'} \cdot \frac{1}{2\pi R_{th} [C_\pi + C_\mu (1 + g_m R_{L}^{'})]}$$

For $C_\mu (1 + g_m R_{L}^{'}) \gg C_\pi$ (Miller capacitance dominates — the usual case):

$$GBW \approx \frac{g_m R_{L}^{'}}{2\pi R_{th} C_\mu g_m R_{L}^{'}} = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{th} C_\mu}$$

**Important Result:** The GBW is **approximately independent of $g_m R_{L}^{'}$** (the mid-band gain). Doubling the gain (by doubling $R_{L}^{'}$) approximately **halves $f_H$**, keeping the product constant.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Combined Lower Cutoff Frequency

When all three lower-cutoff contributions ($f_{L1}, f_{L2}, f_{LE}$) are present, the overall gain in the low-frequency region is:

$$\frac{A_v(f)}{A_{v,mid}} = \frac{jf/f_{L1}}{1 + jf/f_{L1}} \cdot \frac{jf/f_{L2}}{1 + jf/f_{L2}} \cdot \frac{jf/f_{LE}}{1 + jf/f_{LE}}$$

The overall lower cutoff $f_L$ (where this ratio equals $1/\sqrt{2}$) is given by:

$$f_L \approx \sqrt{f_{L1}^2 + f_{L2}^2 + f_{LE}^2}$$

This formula is **valid only when the poles are widely separated**. When they are close, the true $f_L$ is somewhat higher than this estimate.

**Reasoning (textual):** The corner frequency of a series of cascaded high-pass filters is determined by the geometric mean of the individual poles (in the widely-separated case). The dominant pole — the one with the **highest $f_{Lk}$** — sets the approximate cutoff, because the gain has already started to fall significantly by the time the other poles become active.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU 14-Mark Style Problem)

**Problem Statement:** A CE amplifier has the following parameters:
- $R_S = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_B = 100 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_C = 4.7 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_L = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$
- $R_E = 1.2 \text{ k}\Omega$ (with bypass $C_E$)
- $C_{in} = 1 \text{ \mu F}$, $C_{out} = 1 \text{ \mu F}$, $C_E = 100 \text{ \mu F}$
- $\beta = 100$, $I_C = 1 \text{ mA}$, $V_T = 26 \text{ mV}$
- Hybrid-π: $C_\pi = 30 \text{ pF}$, $C_\mu = 5 \text{ pF}$

**Calculate:** (a) Mid-band voltage gain $A_{v,mid}$, (b) Lower cutoff $f_L$, (c) Upper cutoff $f_H$, (d) Bandwidth and GBW.

**Step 1 — Calculate small-signal parameters:**

$$g_m = \frac{I_C}{V_T} = \frac{1 \text{ mA}}{26 \text{ mV}} = 0.0385 \text{ S} = 38.5 \text{ mS}$$

$$r_\pi = \frac{\beta}{g_m} = \frac{100}{0.0385} = 2.6 \text{ k}\Omega$$

$$r_e = \frac{1}{g_m} = \frac{1}{0.0385} = 26 \text{ }\Omega$$

**Step 2 — Effective load:**

$$R_{L}^{'} = R_C \parallel R_L = \frac{4.7 \times 10}{4.7 + 10} = 3.2 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 3 — Mid-band gain (bypassed):**

$$A_{v,mid} = -g_m R_{L}^{'} = -(0.0385)(3200) = -123.2$$

$$\approx -123$$

**Step 4 — Lower cutoff contributions:**

$f_{L1}$ (input coupling):
$$R_{in} = R_B \parallel r_\pi = \frac{100 \times 2.6}{100 + 2.6} \approx 2.53 \text{ k}\Omega$$
$$f_{L1} = \frac{1}{2\pi (R_S + R_{in}) C_{in}} = \frac{1}{2\pi (1000 + 2530)(1 \times 10^{-6})}$$
$$f_{L1} = \frac{1}{2\pi (3530)(10^{-6})} = \frac{1}{2.218 \times 10^{-2}} \approx 45.1 \text{ Hz}$$

$f_{L2}$ (output coupling):
$$f_{L2} = \frac{1}{2\pi (R_C + R_L) C_{out}} = \frac{1}{2\pi (4700 + 10000)(10^{-6})}$$
$$f_{L2} = \frac{1}{2\pi (14700)(10^{-6})} = \frac{1}{9.236 \times 10^{-2}} \approx 10.8 \text{ Hz}$$

$f_{LE}$ (emitter bypass):
$$R_E \parallel r_e = \frac{1200 \times 26}{1200 + 26} \approx 25.5 \text{ }\Omega$$
$$f_{LE} = \frac{1}{2\pi (25.5)(100 \times 10^{-6})} = \frac{1}{2\pi (2.55 \times 10^{-3})}$$
$$f_{LE} = \frac{1}{0.01602} \approx 62.4 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 5 — Combined lower cutoff (dominant pole):**

$$f_L \approx \max(45.1, 10.8, 62.4) = 62.4 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 6 — Miller input capacitance:**

$$C_{M,in} = C_\mu (1 + g_m R_{L}^{'}) = 5 \text{ pF} \times (1 + 123.2) = 5 \times 124.2 = 621 \text{ pF}$$

**Step 7 — Total input capacitance:**

$$C_{th} = C_\pi + C_{M,in} = 30 + 621 = 651 \text{ pF}$$

**Step 8 — Thevenin resistance at input:**

$$R_{th} = R_S \parallel R_B \parallel r_\pi \approx R_S \parallel r_\pi = \frac{1 \times 2.6}{1 + 2.6} = 0.722 \text{ k}\Omega = 722 \text{ }\Omega$$

**Step 9 — Upper cutoff frequency:**

$$f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{th} C_{th}} = \frac{1}{2\pi (722)(651 \times 10^{-12})}$$
$$f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi (4.70 \times 10^{-7})} = \frac{1}{2.954 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 338.5 \text{ kHz}$$

**Step 10 — Bandwidth and GBW:**

$$BW = f_H - f_L \approx 338.5 \text{ kHz} - 62.4 \text{ Hz} \approx 338.4 \text{ kHz}$$

$$GBW = \vert A_{v,mid} \vert \times BW = 123 \times 338.5 \text{ kHz} \approx 41.6 \text{ MHz}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Verification:** Note that $f_H \approx 338.5$ kHz, but the transistor's intrinsic $f_T = g_m / [2\pi(C_\pi + C_\mu)] = 0.0385 / [2\pi(35 \times 10^{-12})] \approx 175$ MHz. Our $f_H$ is much lower than $f_T$ — this is the **Miller effect** in action. The Miller multiplication of $C_\mu$ has reduced the useful bandwidth by a factor of about **500×**.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define the bandwidth of an amplifier. How is it related to the gain-bandwidth product?**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **bandwidth (BW)** of an amplifier is defined as the range of frequencies over which the amplifier's gain is within **−3 dB** of its maximum (mid-band) value. Mathematically:
$$BW = f_H - f_L \approx f_H$$
where $f_H$ is the upper cutoff frequency and $f_L$ is the lower cutoff frequency.

The **Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBW)** is defined as the product of the mid-band voltage gain magnitude and the bandwidth:
$$GBW = \vert A_{v,mid} \vert \times BW \approx \text{constant}$$
For a given transistor at a given bias, GBW is essentially a **fixed value**. Hence, increasing the gain decreases the bandwidth, and vice versa. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Q2. State Miller's theorem. Why is it important in the analysis of high-frequency response of a CE amplifier?**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Miller's Theorem** states that an impedance $Z$ connected between two nodes with a voltage gain $A_v$ between them can be replaced by two shunt impedances:
- $Z_1 = \frac{Z}{1 - A_v}$ (at the input node)
- $Z_2 = \frac{Z \cdot A_v}{A_v - 1}$ (at the output node)

For the CE amplifier, the feedback capacitance $C_\mu$ between base and collector is subjected to a large negative gain $A_v = -g_m R_{L}^{'}$. The Miller theorem converts $C_\mu$ into a **much larger input capacitance**:
$$C_{M,in} = C_\mu (1 + g_m R_{L}^{'})$$
This **multiplied capacitance** drastically reduces the upper cutoff frequency $f_H$. Without Miller's theorem, the bandwidth analysis of the CE amplifier would be intractable. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks): Effect of Coupling and Bypass Capacitors on Low-Frequency Response**

**[KTU University Exam - June 2024 — Set A]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the lower cutoff frequency of a CE amplifier due to the **emitter bypass capacitor** $C_E$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A CE amplifier has $R_S = 600 \text{ }\Omega$, $R_B = 80 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_C = 5 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_L = 8 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_E = 1.5 \text{ k}\Omega$, $C_{in} = 0.5 \text{ \mu F}$, $C_{out} = 1 \text{ \mu F}$, $C_E = 80 \text{ \mu F}$, $\beta = 120$, $I_C = 1.5 \text{ mA}$, $V_T = 26 \text{ mV}$. Calculate: (i) Mid-band gain, (ii) Individual lower cutoff frequencies $f_{L1}, f_{L2}, f_{LE}$, (iii) Overall $f_L$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

Consider the CE amplifier at low frequency. The bypass capacitor $C_E$ is in parallel with $R_E$. The impedance of this parallel combination is:

$$Z_E = R_E \parallel \frac{1}{j\omega C_E} = \frac{R_E}{1 + j\omega R_E C_E}$$

The voltage gain with this complex emitter impedance:

$$A_v(\omega) = -\frac{g_m R_{L}^{'}}{1 + g_m Z_E} = -\frac{g_m R_{L}^{'} (1 + j\omega R_E C_E)}{1 + g_m R_E + j\omega R_E C_E}$$

$$\Rightarrow A_v(\omega) = -\frac{g_m R_{L}^{'}}{1 + g_m R_E} \cdot \frac{1 + j\omega R_E C_E}{1 + j\omega \frac{R_E C_E}{1 + g_m R_E}}$$

The mid-band gain (unbypassed) is $A_{v,mid} = -\frac{g_m R_{L}^{'}}{1 + g_m R_E}$. The pole is at:

$$\omega_{LE} = \frac{1 + g_m R_E}{R_E C_E} = \frac{1}{(R_E \parallel r_e) C_E}$$

$$\boxed{f_{LE} = \frac{1}{2\pi (R_E \parallel r_e) C_E}} \quad \text{(3 marks for derivation)}$$

**Physical Interpretation (1 mark):** $C_E$ sees the parallel combination of $R_E$ and $r_e$ (the resistance looking into the emitter). Since $r_e = 1/g_m$ is small, $R_E \parallel r_e \approx r_e$, making $f_{LE}$ high and **dominant** in determining $f_L$.

**Effect on Gain (3 marks):** At low frequencies ($f \to 0$), $C_E$ acts as an open circuit. The full $R_E$ appears unbypassed, reducing the gain by a factor of $(1 + g_m R_E)$. As $f$ increases past $f_{LE}$, $C_E$ becomes a short, bypassing $R_E$, and the gain rises by **+20 dB/decade** to reach the bypassed mid-band value.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Small-signal parameters (1 mark):**
$$g_m = \frac{I_C}{V_T} = \frac{1.5 \text{ mA}}{26 \text{ mV}} = 0.0577 \text{ S}$$
$$r_\pi = \frac{\beta}{g_m} = \frac{120}{0.0577} = 2.08 \text{ k}\Omega$$
$$r_e = \frac{1}{g_m} = 17.3 \text{ }\Omega$$

**Step 2 — Effective load (0.5 marks):**
$$R_{L}^{'} = R_C \parallel R_L = \frac{5 \times 8}{5 + 8} = 3.08 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 3 — Mid-band gain (0.5 marks):**
$$A_{v,mid} = -g_m R_{L}^{'} = -(0.0577)(3080) = -177.7 \approx -178$$

**Step 4 — Lower cutoff $f_{L1}$ (input coupling) (1 mark):**
$$R_{in} = R_B \parallel r_\pi = \frac{80 \times 2.08}{82.08} = 2.03 \text{ k}\Omega$$
$$f_{L1} = \frac{1}{2\pi (R_S + R_{in}) C_{in}} = \frac{1}{2\pi (600 + 2030)(0.5 \times 10^{-6})}$$
$$f_{L1} = \frac{1}{2\pi (2630)(0.5 \times 10^{-6})} = \frac{1}{8.26 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 121 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 5 — Lower cutoff $f_{L2}$ (output coupling) (1 mark):**
$$f_{L2} = \frac{1}{2\pi (R_C + R_L) C_{out}} = \frac{1}{2\pi (5000 + 8000)(10^{-6})}$$
$$f_{L2} = \frac{1}{2\pi (13000)(10^{-6})} = \frac{1}{0.0817} \approx 12.2 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 6 — Lower cutoff $f_{LE}$ (emitter bypass) (1 mark):**
$$R_E \parallel r_e = \frac{1500 \times 17.3}{1500 + 17.3} = 17.1 \text{ }\Omega$$
$$f_{LE} = \frac{1}{2\pi (17.1)(80 \times 10^{-6})} = \frac{1}{2\pi (1.37 \times 10^{-3})}$$
$$f_{LE} = \frac{1}{8.59 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 116.4 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 7 — Overall $f_L$ (2 marks):**
Using the dominant pole approximation:
$$f_L \approx \max(f_{L1}, f_{L2}, f_{LE}) = \max(121, 12.2, 116.4) = 121 \text{ Hz}$$

More accurate combined formula:
$$f_L \approx \sqrt{f_{L1}^2 + f_{L2}^2 + f_{LE}^2} = \sqrt{121^2 + 12.2^2 + 116.4^2}$$
$$= \sqrt{14641 + 149 + 13549} = \sqrt{28339} \approx 168.4 \text{ Hz}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting $R_S$ in $f_{L1}$:** Many students use only $R_{in}$ instead of $(R_S + R_{in})$. This causes a **1–2 mark penalty**.
> 2. **Using $R_E$ alone in $f_{LE}$:** The correct effective resistance is $R_E \parallel r_e$, not $R_E$. Using $R_E$ alone gives a cutoff that is **~80× too low**, indicating a conceptual misunderstanding worth **2 marks**.
> 3. **Mixing up the bypass formula:** $f_{LE}$ uses $R_E \parallel r_e$ in the denominator, NOT $R_E \parallel R_C$.
> 4. **Forgetting the mid-band gain sign:** $A_{v,mid}$ is **negative** for CE configuration (180° phase inversion). The magnitude is positive, but the sign carries information about phase.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): Effect of Internal Capacitances and Miller's Theorem on High-Frequency Response**

**[KTU University Exam - June 2024 — Set B]**

**(a)** Using Miller's theorem, derive the expression for the upper cutoff frequency $f_H$ of a CE amplifier in terms of $g_m, R_{L}^{'}, C_\pi, C_\mu, R_S, r_\pi, R_B$. Clearly state the dominant-pole approximation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a CE amplifier, $g_m = 50 \text{ mS}$, $R_{L}^{'} = 2 \text{ k}\Omega$, $C_\pi = 50 \text{ pF}$, $C_\mu = 4 \text{ pF}$, $R_S = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$, $r_\pi = 2 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_B = 50 \text{ k}\Omega$. Calculate: (i) $A_{v,mid}$, (ii) Miller input capacitance, (iii) $f_H$, (iv) Gain-bandwidth product. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** The voltage gain in mid-band of a CE amplifier (with emitter fully bypassed) is:
$$A_v = -g_m R_{L}^{'}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Apply Miller's theorem to $C_\mu$ (between input node at $V_b$ and output node at $V_c$ with gain $A_v$):
$$C_{\mu, in} = C_\mu (1 - A_v) = C_\mu (1 + g_m R_{L}^{'})$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Total input capacitance to AC ground at the base:
$$C_{in,total} = C_\pi + C_{\mu, in} = C_\pi + C_\mu (1 + g_m R_{L}^{'})$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Thevenin resistance at the base:
$$R_{th} = R_S \parallel R_B \parallel r_\pi$$

**Step 5 (2 marks):** The input node forms an RC low-pass filter. Upper cutoff:
$$\boxed{f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{th} [C_\pi + C_\mu (1 + g_m R_{L}^{'})]}}$$

**Dominant Pole Approximation (1 mark):** When $C_\mu (1 + g_m R_{L}^{'}) \gg C_\pi$ (usually true for high-gain stages), the formula simplifies to:
$$f_H \approx \frac{1}{2\pi R_{th} C_\mu (1 + g_m R_{L}^{'})}$$

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Mid-band gain (1 mark):**
$$A_{v,mid} = -g_m R_{L}^{'} = -(0.050)(2000) = -100$$

**(ii) Miller input capacitance (2 marks):**
$$C_{\mu, in} = C_\mu (1 + g_m R_{L}^{'}) = 4 \text{ pF} \times (1 + 100) = 4 \times 101 = 404 \text{ pF}$$

**(iii) Upper cutoff frequency (2 marks):**
Total capacitance:
$$C_{th} = C_\pi + C_{\mu, in} = 50 + 404 = 454 \text{ pF}$$

Thevenin resistance:
$$R_{th} = R_S \parallel R_B \parallel r_\pi = 1000 \parallel 50000 \parallel 2000$$
$$= 1000 \parallel 1923 = \frac{1000 \times 1923}{2923} \approx 658 \text{ }\Omega$$

$$f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi (658)(454 \times 10^{-12})} = \frac{1}{2\pi (2.99 \times 10^{-7})}$$
$$f_H = \frac{1}{1.876 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 533 \text{ kHz}$$

**(iv) Gain-Bandwidth Product (2 marks):**
$$GBW = \vert A_{v,mid} \vert \times f_H = 100 \times 533 \text{ kHz} = 53.3 \text{ MHz}$$

**Cross-verification using simplified formula:**
$$GBW \approx \frac{1}{2\pi R_{th} C_\mu} = \frac{1}{2\pi (658)(4 \times 10^{-12})} = \frac{1}{1.65 \times 10^{-8}} \approx 60.5 \text{ MHz}$$

(The two values are close but not identical because we retained $C_\pi$ in the full calculation. The full calculation is more accurate.)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $(1 - A_v)$ sign in Miller's theorem:** Since $A_v$ is negative, students often write $(1 + A_v)$ instead of $(1 - A_v)$, which gives a **wrong, much smaller Miller capacitance**. This is the most common mistake.
> 2. **Using $R_S$ alone for $R_{th}$:** The Thevenin resistance includes $R_B \parallel r_\pi$ as well. Skipping these is a **2-mark penalty**.
> 3. **Unit mismatch in capacitor value:** Using $C_\mu = 4$ pF without converting to Farads ($4 \times 10^{-12}$ F) gives a wildly wrong answer.
> 4. **Stopping at $C_{\mu, in}$ and forgetting to add $C_\pi$:** $C_{th}$ is the **sum** of $C_\pi$ and the Miller-multiplied $C_{\mu, in}$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU PCAET302 Module 2**

- [x] The voltage gain of an RC-coupled amplifier is **band-pass** in nature: constant in the mid-band, falling at low and high frequencies.
- [x] **Low-frequency response** is governed by **external** capacitors: $C_{in}, C_{out}, C_E$. They form high-pass RC filters.
- [x] **High-frequency response** is governed by **internal** transistor capacitances: $C_\pi$ and $C_\mu$ (or $C_{be}$ and $C_{bc}$). They form low-pass RC filters.
- [x] **Miller's Theorem** converts the feedback capacitor $C_\mu$ into a much larger input capacitance: $C_{M,in} = C_\mu (1 + g_m R_{L}^{'})$. This is the **primary bottleneck** for high-frequency response.
- [x] The **emitter bypass capacitor** $C_E$ sees $R_E \parallel r_e$, NOT just $R_E$. This makes $f_{LE}$ the **dominant** lower cutoff in most practical designs.
- [x] **Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBW)** is approximately **constant** for a given transistor at a given bias. Gain and bandwidth can be **traded** but not independently increased.
- [x] **Cascading $n$ identical stages** with each having bandwidth $BW_1$ gives an overall bandwidth of $BW_{overall} = BW_1 \sqrt{2^{1/n} - 1}$, which is **less** than $BW_1$. This is the **bandwidth shrinkage** effect.
- [x] The **transition frequency** $f_T$ is the intrinsic GBW of the BJT: $f_T = g_m / [2\pi(C_\pi + C_\mu)]$. It is the frequency at which the **short-circuit** current gain drops to unity.
- [x] The **Miller effect** does **not** exist in the **common-base (CB)** configuration because its voltage gain is approximately **unity**, so the Miller multiplication factor is approximately 1. CB amplifiers therefore have **much higher bandwidth** than CE amplifiers.
- [x] The **emitter follower (CC)** has voltage gain $\approx 1$ and is used as an **impedance buffer** (high input impedance, low output impedance) with **wide bandwidth**.
- [x] To extend low-frequency response, increase $C_{in}, C_{out}, C_E$ (and use **direct coupling** to eliminate them entirely in DC amplifiers).
- [x] To extend high-frequency response, use transistors with **smaller** $C_\mu$ (or use **cascode** configuration which reduces the Miller multiplication).
- [x] In decibel calculations: $\text{dB} = 20 \log_{10} \vert A_v \vert$. A **−3 dB** point corresponds to gain $A_v = A_{mid}/\sqrt{2} \approx 0.707 A_{mid}$.
- [x] The **Bode plot** slope: each pole contributes **−20 dB/decade** (or **−6 dB/octave**) roll-off.
- [x] **Remember the 26 mV thermal voltage** $V_T$ at room temperature (300 K). It's used in $g_m = I_C/V_T$.
- [x] **Differential amplifiers**, used in op-amps, also suffer from Miller effect — but op-amp internal compensation uses the Miller technique **intentionally** to create a dominant pole for stability.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: MOSFETs - MOSFET as an Amplifier
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET302 - ELECTRONIC DEVICES AND CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** MOSFETs - MOSFET as an Amplifier

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

A **MOSFET (Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor Field-Effect Transistor)** operating as an **amplifier** is a small-signal electronic configuration in which a time-varying (AC) input signal applied to the gate terminal controls a much larger output signal at the drain, while the MOSFET remains biased in the **saturation (active) region**. The amplification mechanism is governed by the transconductance parameter **$g_m$**, which represents the sensitivity of the drain current to changes in the gate-to-source voltage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A MOSFET amplifier is a linear circuit in which the MOSFET is DC-biased in the saturation region, and a small AC input signal $v_{gs}$ superimposed on the DC bias produces an amplified AC output voltage at the drain, characterized by the small-signal parameters $g_m$, $r_o$, and the associated voltage gain $A_v$.

The MOSFET used as a small-signal amplifier is generally analyzed using one of three canonical single-stage configurations:
- **Common-Source (CS) Amplifier** — high voltage gain, inverting output
- **Common-Drain (CD) / Source Follower** — unity voltage gain, low output impedance
- **Common-Gate (CG) Amplifier** — non-inverting, high-frequency, low input impedance

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Water Faucet with a Pressure Sensor:**
> Imagine a water tap (the drain-source channel) whose flow rate depends on how hard you push a small lever (the gate voltage). Even a tiny push on the lever causes a large change in water flow. Now, attach that water flow to a paddle wheel connected to a heavy gate (a load resistor). The small push on the lever creates a large swing in the paddle's position (the output voltage). This is precisely how a MOSFET amplifies: a small input voltage at the gate modulates a larger output voltage across the drain load.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Thermal Voltage:** $V_T = \dfrac{kT}{q} \approx \mathbf{25\ mV}$ at $T = 300\ K$
- **Process Transconductance Parameter:** $k'_n = \mu_n C_{ox}$ (units: **A/V²**)
- **Transconductance:** $g_m$ (units: **A/V** or **S** = **mho**)
- **Output Resistance:** $r_o$ (units: **Ω**)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU 2024 examination purposes, students must commit to memory the standard small-signal parameters of the MOSFET in saturation: $g_m$, $r_o$, and the resulting voltage gain expressions for CS, CD, and CG configurations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why a MOSFET Can Amplify — The Underlying Physics

A MOSFET operates in the **saturation (active) region** when $V_{DS} \geq V_{GS} - V_{th}$ (for an n-channel enhancement MOSFET). In this region, the drain current $I_D$ depends quadratically on the gate-to-source voltage:

$$I_D = \frac{1}{2} k'_n \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{th})^2$$

The key observation is that $I_D$ is a **strong nonlinear function** of $V_{GS}$. By applying a small AC signal $v_{gs}$ on top of a DC bias $V_{GS}$, the drain current becomes time-varying, and this varying current flowing through a load resistor $R_D$ produces a much larger time-varying output voltage $v_{ds}$.

## 2.2 Conditions for Linear Amplification

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For **distortion-free linear amplification**, the following must hold:
> 1. The MOSFET must be biased in the **saturation region** with a stable Q-point.
> 2. The input signal amplitude $v_{gs}$ must be **small** ($v_{gs} \ll 2(V_{GS} - V_{th})$) so that the AC variation stays within the linear region of the $i_D$–$v_{GS}$ characteristic.
> 3. The Q-point must be **temperature-stable** and **independent of MOSFET parameter variations**.

## 2.3 The Small-Signal Model of the MOSFET

When the input signal is small, the MOSFET can be replaced by its **small-signal equivalent circuit**, which linearizes the nonlinear device behavior. The two-port model consists of:

- A **voltage-controlled current source** $g_m v_{gs}$ between drain and source
- An **output resistance** $r_o$ between drain and source (accounting for channel-length modulation)
- **Infinite input resistance** at the gate (since the gate is insulated by the oxide)

## 2.4 Biasing the MOSFET Amplifier

A MOSFET amplifier requires a stable DC bias point. The four classical biasing methods are:

1. **Fixed-bias (gate bias)** — Simplest, but Q-point is highly sensitive to device parameters.
2. **Self-bias (source-bias)** — Uses a source resistor $R_S$ to provide negative feedback; widely used.
3. **Voltage-divider bias** — Most common and stable configuration; uses $R_1$ and $R_2$.
4. **Source-bias with drain-feedback** — Uses a gate resistor connected to the drain.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Drain Current (Saturation) | $I_D = \frac{1}{2} k'_n \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{th})^2$ | A | Quadratic in $V_{GS}$ |
| 2 | Drain Current (with channel-length modulation) | $I_D = \frac{1}{2} k'_n \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{th})^2 (1 + \lambda V_{DS})$ | A | $\lambda$ is modulation parameter |
| 3 | Transconductance $g_m$ | $g_m = k'_n \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{th})$ | A/V or S | Most-used small-signal parameter |
| 4 | $g_m$ in terms of $I_D$ | $g_m = \sqrt{2 k'_n \frac{W}{L} I_D}$ | A/V | Useful when $I_D$ is known |
| 5 | $g_m$ in terms of overdrive voltage $V_{OV}$ | $g_m = \dfrac{2 I_D}{V_{OV}}$ | A/V | $V_{OV} = V_{GS} - V_{th}$ |
| 6 | Output Resistance $r_o$ | $r_o = \dfrac{1}{\lambda I_D}$ | Ω | Models channel-length modulation |
| 7 | Intrinsic Voltage Gain | $A_{v0} = g_m r_o$ | dimensionless | Gain of CS stage with $R_D \to \infty$ |
| 8 | CS Voltage Gain (with $R_D$, ignoring $r_o$) | $A_v = -g_m R_D$ | dimensionless | Inverting amplifier |
| 9 | CS Voltage Gain (with $R_D \parallel r_o$) | $A_v = -g_m (R_D \parallel r_o)$ | dimensionless | More accurate |
| 10 | CS Voltage Gain (with source resistor $R_S$ unbypassed) | $A_v = -\dfrac{g_m (R_D \parallel r_o)}{1 + g_m R_S}$ | dimensionless | Reduced gain, improved linearity |
| 11 | Input Resistance (CS, gate-driven) | $R_{in} = R_1 \parallel R_2$ | Ω | Set by bias network |
| 12 | Output Resistance (CS) | $R_{out} = R_D \parallel r_o$ | Ω | Looking into drain |
| 13 | CD (Source Follower) Voltage Gain | $A_v = \dfrac{g_m (R_S \parallel r_o)}{1 + g_m (R_S \parallel r_o)}$ | dimensionless | $\approx 1$, non-inverting |
| 14 | CD Output Resistance | $R_{out} = R_S \parallel \dfrac{1}{g_m} \parallel r_o$ | Ω | Very low |
| 15 | CG Voltage Gain (with $R_D \parallel r_o$) | $A_v = g_m (R_D \parallel r_o)$ | dimensionless | Non-inverting |
| 16 | CG Input Resistance | $R_{in} = R_S \parallel \dfrac{1}{g_m}$ | Ω | Very low |
| 17 | Coupling Capacitor Condition | $X_C = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi f C} \ll R_{in}$ | Ω | For low-frequency cutoff |
| 18 | Lower Cutoff Frequency (CS) | $f_L = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi R_{eq} C}$ | Hz | One pole per coupling/bypass cap |

## 2.6 Real-World Utility of MOSFET Amplifiers

> [!NOTE]
> MOSFET amplifiers are the **fundamental building blocks** of nearly all modern integrated circuits. They form the core of:
> - **Operational amplifiers** (op-amps) — millions of MOSFETs on one chip
> - **RF front-ends** in mobile phones and Wi-Fi transceivers
> - **Audio amplifiers** in consumer electronics
> - **Switched-capacitor circuits** in analog-to-digital converters (ADCs)
> - **Power amplifiers** in motor drivers and class-D audio systems
> - **Sensor interface circuits** — MOSFETs' high input impedance makes them ideal for amplifying weak signals from piezoelectric, biochemical, or MEMS sensors
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Transconductance $g_m$ from First Principles

**Starting point:** The drain current of a MOSFET in saturation (ignoring channel-length modulation):

$$I_D = \frac{1}{2} k'_n \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{th})^2$$

**Step 1:** Define the small-signal transconductance as the partial derivative of $I_D$ with respect to $V_{GS}$, evaluated at the Q-point:

$$g_m \equiv \left. \frac{\partial I_D}{\partial V_{GS}} \right|_{Q\text{-point}}$$

**Step 2:** Differentiate $I_D$ with respect to $V_{GS}$:

$$\frac{\partial I_D}{\partial V_{GS}} = \frac{1}{2} k'_n \frac{W}{L} \cdot 2 (V_{GS} - V_{th}) \cdot 1$$

**Step 3:** Simplify:

$$g_m = k'_n \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{th})$$

**Step 4:** Introduce the overdrive voltage $V_{OV} = V_{GS} - V_{th}$ for compactness:

$$g_m = k'_n \frac{W}{L} \cdot V_{OV}$$

**Step 5:** Express $g_m$ in terms of the DC bias current $I_D$. Starting from the saturation equation:

$$I_D = \frac{1}{2} k'_n \frac{W}{L} V_{OV}^2$$

Solving for $k'_n \frac{W}{L} V_{OV}$:

$$k'_n \frac{W}{L} V_{OV} = \frac{2 I_D}{V_{OV}}$$

Therefore:

$$g_m = \frac{2 I_D}{V_{OV}}$$

**Step 6:** Alternative form by eliminating $V_{OV}$:

$$V_{OV} = \sqrt{\frac{2 I_D}{k'_n \frac{W}{L}}}$$

Substituting:

$$g_m = \sqrt{2 k'_n \frac{W}{L} I_D}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three equivalent expressions for $g_m$:**
> 1. $g_m = k'_n \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{th})$ — uses overdrive voltage
> 2. $g_m = \dfrac{2 I_D}{V_{OV}}$ — uses DC current
> 3. $g_m = \sqrt{2 k'_n \frac{W}{L} I_D}$ — uses only $I_D$ and process parameters

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Voltage Gain of the Common-Source (CS) Amplifier

**Circuit:** A MOSFET in CS configuration with drain resistor $R_D$ and source grounded (bypassed). Input applied at gate, output taken at drain.

**Step 1:** Apply KCL at the drain node in the small-signal model. The small-signal drain current is:

$$i_d = g_m v_{gs}$$

**Step 2:** The drain voltage (output) with respect to ground:

$$v_{ds} = -i_d \cdot R_D = -g_m v_{gs} R_D$$

The negative sign arises because an **increase** in $v_{gs}$ causes an **increase** in drain current, which causes a **larger voltage drop** across $R_D$, making the drain voltage **decrease**.

**Step 3:** Define the voltage gain:

$$A_v = \frac{v_{ds}}{v_{gs}}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute:

$$\boxed{A_v = -g_m R_D}$$

**Step 5:** If channel-length modulation is included, the effective load at the drain is $R_D \parallel r_o$:

$$\boxed{A_v = -g_m (R_D \parallel r_o)}$$

**Step 6:** If a source resistor $R_S$ is present and **not bypassed**, the gain is reduced due to local feedback. The effective transconductance becomes:

$$G_m = \frac{g_m}{1 + g_m R_S}$$

And the gain becomes:

$$\boxed{A_v = -\frac{g_m (R_D \parallel r_o)}{1 + g_m R_S}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Voltage Gain of the Common-Drain (Source Follower) Amplifier

**Circuit:** Drain tied to $V_{DD}$ (AC ground), output taken at the source.

**Step 1:** In the small-signal model, the drain is at AC ground. Applying KCL at the source node:

$$g_m v_{gs} + \frac{v_s}{r_o} + \frac{v_s}{R_S} = 0$$

**Step 2:** Since $v_{gs} = v_g - v_s$ and $v_g = v_{in}$ (the input AC voltage at the gate):

$$g_m (v_{in} - v_s) + \frac{v_s}{r_o} + \frac{v_s}{R_S} = 0$$

**Step 3:** Expand and collect $v_s$ terms:

$$g_m v_{in} - g_m v_s + \frac{v_s}{r_o} + \frac{v_s}{R_S} = 0$$

**Step 4:** Group $v_s$ on the right-hand side:

$$g_m v_{in} = v_s \left( g_m - \frac{1}{r_o} - \frac{1}{R_S} \right)$$

**Step 5:** Solve for $v_s / v_{in}$:

$$A_v = \frac{v_s}{v_{in}} = \frac{g_m}{g_m + \frac{1}{r_o} + \frac{1}{R_S}}$$

**Step 6:** Define $R'_S = R_S \parallel r_o$. Then $\frac{1}{r_o} + \frac{1}{R_S} = \frac{1}{R'_S}$:

$$\boxed{A_v = \frac{g_m R'_S}{1 + g_m R'_S}}$$

**Step 7:** When $g_m R'_S \gg 1$:

$$A_v \approx 1$$

This confirms the name **"source follower"** — the output voltage at the source **follows** the input voltage at the gate (with a gain of nearly unity and **no phase inversion**).

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Voltage Gain of the Common-Gate (CG) Amplifier

**Circuit:** Gate at AC ground, input applied at the source, output at the drain.

**Step 1:** In the small-signal model, since the gate is at AC ground:

$$v_{gs} = -v_s = -v_{in}$$

(The source voltage is the negative of the gate-to-source voltage because the gate is grounded.)

**Step 2:** The drain current is:

$$i_d = g_m v_{gs} = -g_m v_{in}$$

**Step 3:** The output voltage at the drain is:

$$v_{out} = -i_d \cdot (R_D \parallel r_o) = -(-g_m v_{in})(R_D \parallel r_o)$$

**Step 4:** Simplify:

$$\boxed{A_v = g_m (R_D \parallel r_o)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The CG amplifier is **non-inverting** (positive gain), unlike the CS amplifier. It also has a **very low input resistance**, making it suitable as a current buffer or for high-frequency applications.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix of the Three Configurations

| Parameter | Common-Source (CS) | Common-Drain (CD) | Common-Gate (CG) |
|-----------|-------------------|-------------------|------------------|
| Voltage Gain $\vert A_v \vert$ | High ($g_m R_D$) | $\approx 1$ (Unity) | High ($g_m R_D$) |
| Phase | Inverting (180°) | Non-inverting (0°) | Non-inverting (0°) |
| Input Resistance $R_{in}$ | Very High (gate) | Very High (gate) | Very Low ($1/g_m$) |
| Output Resistance $R_{out}$ | Moderate to High | Very Low ($1/g_m$) | Moderate to High |
| Current Gain | High | High | $\approx 1$ |
| Power Gain | High | Moderate | Moderate |
| Typical Use | Voltage amplifier | Buffer / impedance matching | High-frequency, current buffer |
| High-Frequency Response | Limited by $C_{gd}$ (Miller) | Better than CS | Best (no Miller effect) |

---

## 3.6 Numerical Example: CS Amplifier Design Verification

**Given:** An n-channel MOSFET with $k'_n (W/L) = 1\ mA/V^2$, $V_{th} = 1\ V$, $\lambda = 0.02\ V^{-1}$, $R_D = 10\ k\Omega$, $V_{GS} = 2\ V$.

**Step 1:** Compute the overdrive voltage:

$$V_{OV} = V_{GS} - V_{th} = 2 - 1 = 1\ V$$

**Step 2:** Compute the DC drain current:

$$I_D = \frac{1}{2} k'_n \frac{W}{L} V_{OV}^2 = \frac{1}{2} (1\ mA/V^2)(1\ V)^2 = 0.5\ mA$$

**Step 3:** Compute $g_m$:

$$g_m = k'_n \frac{W}{L} \cdot V_{OV} = (1\ mA/V^2)(1\ V) = 1\ mA/V = 1\ mS$$

**Step 4:** Compute $r_o$:

$$r_o = \frac{1}{\lambda I_D} = \frac{1}{(0.02)(0.5\ mA)} = \frac{1}{0.01\ mA} = 100\ k\Omega$$

**Step 5:** Compute the voltage gain (ignoring $r_o$):

$$A_v = -g_m R_D = -(1\ mS)(10\ k\Omega) = -10\ V/V$$

**Step 6:** Compute the voltage gain (including $r_o$):

$$A_v = -g_m (R_D \parallel r_o) = -(1\ mS)(10\ k\Omega \parallel 100\ k\Omega) = -(1\ mS)(9.09\ k\Omega) = -9.09\ V/V$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 MOSFET Small-Signal Equivalent Model

This diagram shows the low-frequency small-signal model of the MOSFET, which is the foundation of all amplifier analysis.

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeG["GATE g"]
    nodeD["DRAIN d"]
    nodeS["SOURCE s"]
    nodeVccs["DEPENDENT CURRENT SOURCE gm vgs"]
    nodeRo["OUTPUT RESISTANCE ro"]
    nodeCtl["CONTROL VOLTAGE vgs"]

    nodeG --- nodeCtl
    nodeCtl --- nodeVccs
    nodeVccs --- nodeD
    nodeVccs --- nodeRo
    nodeRo --- nodeS
    nodeG --- nodeS

    style nodeG fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeD fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeS fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeVccs fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeRo fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeCtl fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Common-Source Amplifier Architecture

This diagram illustrates the functional signal flow of a CS amplifier from input source through the MOSFET to the output.

```mermaid
graph TB
    nodeIn["INPUT SIGNAL vin"]
    nodeC1["COUPLING CAPACITOR C1"]
    nodeGate["GATE OF MOSFET"]
    nodeMos["MOSFET in SATURATION"]
    nodeDrain["DRAIN NODE"]
    nodeRD["DRAIN RESISTOR RD"]
    nodeVdd["VDD SUPPLY"]
    nodeC2["COUPLING CAPACITOR C2"]
    nodeOut["OUTPUT SIGNAL vout"]
    nodeCs["BYPASS CAPACITOR CS"]
    nodeSrc["SOURCE TO GROUND"]

    nodeIn --> nodeC1
    nodeC1 --> nodeGate
    nodeGate --> nodeMos
    nodeMos --> nodeDrain
    nodeDrain --> nodeRD
    nodeRD --> nodeVdd
    nodeDrain --> nodeC2
    nodeC2 --> nodeOut
    nodeMos --> nodeCs
    nodeCs --> nodeSrc

    style nodeIn fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeGate fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeMos fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeDrain fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeOut fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeVdd fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Comparison of CS, CD, and CG Amplifiers

```mermaid
graph TB
    nodeCs["COMMON SOURCE CS"]
    nodeCd["COMMON DRAIN CD SOURCE FOLLOWER"]
    nodeCg["COMMON GATE CG"]
    nodeInCs["INPUT AT GATE"]
    nodeInCd["INPUT AT GATE"]
    nodeInCg["INPUT AT SOURCE"]
    nodeOutCs["OUTPUT AT DRAIN INVERTING"]
    nodeOutCd["OUTPUT AT SOURCE UNITY GAIN"]
    nodeOutCg["OUTPUT AT DRAIN NON INVERTING"]
    nodeUseCs["VOLTAGE AMPLIFIER HIGH GAIN"]
    nodeUseCd["BUFFER IMPEDANCE MATCHING"]
    nodeUseCg["HIGH FREQUENCY CURRENT BUFFER"]

    nodeCs --> nodeInCs
    nodeCs --> nodeOutCs
    nodeCs --> nodeUseCs
    nodeCd --> nodeInCd
    nodeCd --> nodeOutCd
    nodeCd --> nodeUseCd
    nodeCg --> nodeInCg
    nodeCg --> nodeOutCg
    nodeCg --> nodeUseCg

    style nodeCs fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeCd fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeCg fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeOutCs fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeOutCd fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeOutCg fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.4 Signal Flow Through a CS Amplifier

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA["DC BIAS VGS"]
    nodeB["AC INPUT vgs SMALL"]
    nodeC["SUPERIMPOSED vGS = VGS + vgs"]
    nodeD["DRAIN CURRENT id = gm vgs"]
    nodeE["OUTPUT VOLTAGE vds = minus gm vgs RD"]
    nodeF["AMPLIFIED vout LARGER"]

    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF

    style nodeA fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeB fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeC fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeD fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeE fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeF fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
```
