<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (2 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper Style]**

### Question 1: Define transconductance $g_m$ of a MOSFET. Mention its unit.

**Model Answer:**

Transconductance $g_m$ is defined as the rate of change of drain current $I_D$ with respect to the gate-to-source voltage $V_{GS}$, evaluated at the DC operating point (Q-point) of the MOSFET in the saturation region.

$$g_m = \left. \frac{\partial I_D}{\partial V_{GS}} \right|_{Q\text{-point}} = k'_n \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{th})$$

**Unit:** A/V (ampere per volt), equivalent to **Siemens (S)** or **mho ($\mho$)**.

---

### Question 2: Why must a MOSFET amplifier be biased in the saturation region?

**Model Answer:**

A MOSFET must be biased in the saturation (active) region for amplification because:
1. In saturation, the drain current $I_D$ is controlled **only by $V_{GS}$** and is **independent of $V_{DS}$** (ideally). This ensures a **constant transconductance** $g_m$ and a linear input-output relationship.
2. In the triode region, $I_D$ depends on both $V_{GS}$ and $V_{DS}$, which would cause severe **signal distortion** and make amplification non-linear.
3. Saturation provides the **flat output characteristic** necessary for the device to behave as a linear voltage-controlled current source.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

> **[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Module 3 Coverage]**

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1

**[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the transconductance $g_m$ of a MOSFET in saturation. Express $g_m$ in terms of (i) the overdrive voltage $V_{OV}$ and (ii) the DC drain current $I_D$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a common-source MOSFET amplifier with $R_D = 4.7\ k\Omega$ and an unbypassed source resistor $R_S = 1\ k\Omega$, the MOSFET has $g_m = 2\ mA/V$ and $r_o = 50\ k\Omega$. Calculate the small-signal voltage gain, input resistance, and output resistance. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**(a) Derivation of $g_m$:**

Starting from the saturation drain current equation:

$$I_D = \frac{1}{2} k'_n \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{th})^2$$

Taking the partial derivative with respect to $V_{GS}$:

$$g_m = \frac{\partial I_D}{\partial V_{GS}} = k'_n \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{th})$$

Since $V_{OV} = V_{GS} - V_{th}$:

$$\boxed{g_m = k'_n \frac{W}{L} \cdot V_{OV}}$$

From $I_D = \frac{1}{2} k'_n \frac{W}{L} V_{OV}^2$, solving for $V_{OV}$:

$$V_{OV} = \sqrt{\frac{2 I_D}{k'_n \frac{W}{L}}}$$

Substituting into the $g_m$ expression:

$$g_m = k'_n \frac{W}{L} \sqrt{\frac{2 I_D}{k'_n \frac{W}{L}}} = \sqrt{2 k'_n \frac{W}{L} I_D}$$

Alternatively:

$$g_m = \frac{2 I_D}{V_{OV}}$$

All three forms are equivalent and interconvertible.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $R_D = 4.7\ k\Omega$, $R_S = 1\ k\Omega$, $g_m = 2\ mA/V$, $r_o = 50\ k\Omega$.

**Step 1:** Effective load at the drain:

$$R_D \parallel r_o = \frac{(4.7)(50)}{4.7 + 50}\ k\Omega = \frac{235}{54.7}\ k\Omega = 4.297\ k\Omega$$

**Step 2:** Voltage gain (with unbypassed $R_S$):

$$A_v = -\frac{g_m (R_D \parallel r_o)}{1 + g_m R_S} = -\frac{(2\ mA/V)(4.297\ k\Omega)}{1 + (2\ mA/V)(1\ k\Omega)}$$

$$A_v = -\frac{8.594}{1 + 2} = -\frac{8.594}{3} = -2.865\ V/V$$

**Step 3:** Input resistance. For a gate-driven CS amplifier, $R_{in}$ is determined by the bias network. In the absence of a specific bias network:

$$R_{in} \approx \infty$$

(The gate of a MOSFET is insulated, so ideally $R_{in}$ is infinite. Practically, the bias resistors $R_1 \parallel R_2$ set the finite input resistance.)

**Step 4:** Output resistance:

$$R_{out} = R_D \parallel r_o = 4.297\ k\Omega$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning #1:**
> Students frequently forget to include $r_o$ in parallel with $R_D$ when computing the effective drain load. Always check whether $\lambda$ or $r_o$ is given. If $r_o$ is provided, you **must** include it in $R_D \parallel r_o$. Failure to do so costs **2 to 3 marks** out of 7.

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** With the help of a small-signal equivalent circuit, derive the expression for the voltage gain of a **source follower** (common-drain) MOSFET amplifier. Show that the gain is less than unity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the three basic MOSFET amplifier configurations (CS, CD, CG) in terms of voltage gain, input resistance, output resistance, and phase relationship. Identify the best configuration for (i) a voltage amplifier, (ii) an impedance buffer, and (iii) a high-frequency amplifier. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**(a) Source Follower Voltage Gain Derivation:**

In the CD configuration, the drain is connected to $V_{DD}$ (AC ground), and the output is taken at the source terminal.

**Step 1:** Draw the small-signal model. Replace the MOSFET with a voltage-controlled current source $g_m v_{gs}$ in parallel with $r_o$. The drain is at AC ground.

**Step 2:** Apply KCL at the source node (which is the output node $v_s = v_{out}$):

$$g_m v_{gs} + \frac{v_s}{r_o} + \frac{v_s}{R_S} = 0$$

**Step 3:** Since the gate is the input and the source is the output:

$$v_{gs} = v_g - v_s = v_{in} - v_{out}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute:

$$g_m (v_{in} - v_{out}) + \frac{v_{out}}{r_o} + \frac{v_{out}}{R_S} = 0$$

**Step 5:** Collect $v_{out}$ terms:

$$g_m v_{in} = v_{out} \left( g_m + \frac{1}{r_o} + \frac{1}{R_S} \right)$$

**Step 6:** Define $R'_S = R_S \parallel r_o$:

$$A_v = \frac{v_{out}}{v_{in}} = \frac{g_m}{g_m + \frac{1}{R'_S}} = \frac{g_m R'_S}{1 + g_m R'_S}$$

**Step 7:** Since $g_m R'_S > 0$, the denominator is always **greater** than the numerator:

$$g_m R'_S + 1 > g_m R'_S$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{A_v = \frac{g_m R'_S}{1 + g_m R'_S} < 1}$$

**Step 8:** When $g_m R'_S \gg 1$, $A_v \to 1$, confirming the source follower behavior.

**(b) Comparative Analysis:**

| Configuration | Voltage Gain $\vert A_v \vert$ | Input Resistance | Output Resistance | Phase |
|--------------|-------------------------------|------------------|-------------------|-------|
| **CS** | High ($g_m R_D$) | Very High | Moderate–High | Inverting |
| **CD** | $\approx 1$ | Very High | Very Low | Non-inverting |
| **CG** | High ($g_m R_D$) | Very Low ($1/g_m$) | Moderate–High | Non-inverting |

**Best Configuration Selection:**

(i) **Voltage amplifier** → **Common-Source (CS)**: Provides the highest voltage gain.

(ii) **Impedance buffer** → **Common-Drain (CD)** (Source Follower): High input impedance, low output impedance, and unity voltage gain — ideal for impedance matching between a high-impedance source and a low-impedance load.

(iii) **High-frequency amplifier** → **Common-Gate (CG)**: The CG configuration does not suffer from the **Miller effect** at the gate-drain capacitance $C_{gd}$, because the gate is at AC ground. This gives it superior high-frequency performance compared to the CS stage.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning #2:**
> In the source follower derivation, students often **drop the $r_o$ term** and obtain $A_v = \frac{g_m R_S}{1 + g_m R_S}$. While this is a reasonable approximation, the **full expression** $A_v = \frac{g_m (R_S \parallel r_o)}{1 + g_m (R_S \parallel r_o)}$ is more accurate and is what KTU examiners expect when $r_o$ is given in the problem. Losing the $r_o$ term typically costs **1.5 to 2 marks**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for MOSFET as an Amplifier:**

- **MOSFET Amplification Principle:** A small AC signal $v_{gs}$ at the gate controls a large drain current variation, which produces a large output voltage across the drain load $R_D$.

- **Saturation Bias is Mandatory:** The MOSFET must operate in the saturation region for linear amplification. Verify $V_{DS} \geq V_{GS} - V_{th}$.

- **Three Configurations:**
  - **CS** → High gain, inverting, high $R_{in}$, moderate $R_{out}$
  - **CD** → Unity gain, non-inverting, high $R_{in}$, very low $R_{out}$
  - **CG** → High gain, non-inverting, very low $R_{in}$, moderate $R_{out}$

- **Key Transconductance Formulas:**
  - $g_m = k'_n (W/L)(V_{GS} - V_{th})$
  - $g_m = 2 I_D / V_{OV}$
  - $g_m = \sqrt{2 k'_n (W/L) I_D}$

- **Output Resistance:** $r_o = 1 / (\lambda I_D)$ — always include when $\lambda$ is given.

- **CS Voltage Gain (Complete):** $A_v = -g_m (R_D \parallel r_o) / (1 + g_m R_S)$

- **Source Follower Gain (Complete):** $A_v = g_m (R_S \parallel r_o) / [1 + g_m (R_S \parallel r_o)]$

- **Bypass Capacitor:** $C_S$ in parallel with $R_S$ shorts $R_S$ at signal frequencies, maximizing gain. At DC, $R_S$ provides bias stability.

- **Coupling Capacitors:** $C_1$ and $C_2$ block DC between stages while passing AC signals.

- **Input Resistance:** For gate-driven stages, $R_{in}$ is set by the bias network $R_1 \parallel R_2$ (the MOSFET gate itself draws no current).

- **Output Resistance of CS:** $R_{out} = R_D \parallel r_o$

- **High-Frequency Limitation:** CS amplifier suffers from the **Miller effect** due to $C_{gd}$, which multiplies its effective input capacitance. CG avoids this.

- **Units to Remember:** $g_m$ in **mA/V** or **mS**, $r_o$ in **kΩ**, $A_v$ in **V/V** (dimensionless).

- **Sign Convention:** CS is **inverting** (output 180° out of phase with input). CD and CG are **non-inverting**.

- **Thermal Voltage:** $V_T = 25\ mV$ at room temperature — use it for $g_m$ derivations from $I_D$ when needed.

- **KTU Favorite Question Patterns:** Derivation of $g_m$, CS gain with and without $R_S$, source follower gain, and comparison table of three configurations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Biasing of P-Channel and N-Channel MOSFET Circuits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAET302 - ELECTRONIC DEVICES AND CIRCUITS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Biasing of P-Channel and N-Channel MOSFET Circuits # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 MOSFET Biasing — The Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **MOSFET Biasing** is the process of applying specific DC voltages (and/or currents) to the Gate, Drain, and Source terminals of a Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor Field-Effect Transistor so that it operates in the desired region (Cut-off, Triode/Ohmic, or Saturation) at a predefined quiescent operating point (Q-point), independent of device parameter variations and temperature fluctuations. In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCAET302 — Module 3)**, biasing refers to establishing a stable DC operating point for both **N-Channel** (NMOS) and **P-Channel** (PMOS) **Enhancement-mode MOSFETs**, which are the workhorses of modern digital and analog CMOS integrated circuits. ### Key Terminology | Term | Definition | |---|---| | **Threshold Voltage ($V_{th}$)** | Minimum $\vert V_{GS}\vert$ required to create a conducting inversion layer (channel) between source and drain. | | **Quiescent Point (Q-point)** | The DC values $(I_D, V_{DS})$ at which the MOSFET is biased with no input signal applied. | | **Saturation Region** | Operating region where $I_D$ becomes nearly independent of $V_{DS}$ — the active/amplification region. | | **Transconductance Parameter ($K$ or $k_n, k_p$)** | Process-dependent constant that determines the drain current for a given overdrive voltage. | --- ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: "The Faucet and the Water Tap" > [!NOTE] > **Think of an N-channel Enhancement MOSFET as a smart water faucet:** > - The **Gate** is the *push-button handle*. > - The **Source** is the *water inlet pipe*. > - The **Drain** is the *water outlet pipe*. > - The **Threshold Voltage ($V_{th}$)** is the *minimum force* you must apply before *any* water flows. Below this force, the valve is sealed. > - The **Gate-to-Source Voltage ($V_{GS}$)** is the *amount of force* you apply. > - The **Drain Current ($I_D$)** is the *flow rate* of water. When you push harder (higher $V_{GS} - V_{th}$, called the **overdrive voltage**), more water flows. The drain-to-source voltage $V_{DS}$ is like the *downstream pressure* — once it's high enough, it cannot push more water through; the flow becomes "saturated." This is the **saturation region**, used for amplification. For a **P-channel MOSFET**, the analogy is inverted: you need to *pull down* the gate voltage *below* the source voltage (negative $V_{GS}$), like sucking water upward through the pipe. The current flows from Source to Drain (opposite of NMOS). --- ## 1.3 Why is Biasing Essential? 1. **Amplification Without Distortion:** A transistor must be biased in the saturation region so that the input AC signal swings symmetrically around the Q-point without clipping. 2. **Temperature Stability:** Drain current $I_D$ is a strong function of temperature; proper biasing (e.g., voltage divider) compensates for thermal drift. 3. **Parameter Independence:** Manufacturing tolerances cause large spreads in $V_{th}$ and $K$; a well-designed bias network makes $I_D$ largely independent of these variations. 4. **Power Dissipation Control:** Operating point must be chosen to keep $P_D = V_{DS} \cdot I_D$ within safe limits. --- ## 1.4 NMOS vs PMOS — The Polarity of Biasing > [!IMPORTANT] > The **golden rule** of MOSFET biasing: > - **NMOS Enhancement:** $V_{GS} > +V_{th}$ (positive) → Channel induced by *electrons*; $V_{DS} > 0$. > - **PMOS Enhancement:** $V_{GS} < -V_{th}$ (negative) → Channel induced by *holes*; $V_{DS} < 0$. > - For both, to operate in **saturation**: $|V_{DS}| \geq |V_{GS} - V_{th}|$. | Parameter | N-Channel Enhancement (NMOS) | P-Channel Enhancement (PMOS) | |---|---|---| | Majority Carriers | Electrons | Holes | | Threshold Voltage $V_{th}$ | **Positive** (+1 V to +3 V typical) | **Negative** (−1 V to −3 V typical) | | Required $V_{GS}$ polarity | Positive | Negative | | Drain Current direction | Drain → Source (conventional) | Source → Drain (conventional) | | $V_{DS}$ polarity | Positive (Drain higher than Source) | Negative (Source higher than Drain) | | Mobility $\mu$ | Higher (~1350 cm²/V·s for electrons) | Lower (~480 cm²/V·s for holes) | | Device Size (for same $I_D$) | Smaller | Larger (≈2.5× larger) | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Core Equation of MOSFET Drain Current The drain current of an enhancement-mode MOSFET operating in the **saturation region** is governed by the **square-law equation**: $$I_D = K \cdot (V_{GS} - V_{th})^2$$ where: - $I_D$ = Drain current (A) - $K$ = Conduction parameter, $K = \dfrac{\mu_n C_{ox}}{2}\cdot\dfrac{W}{L}$ (for NMOS); $K_p = \dfrac{\mu_p C_{ox}}{2}\cdot\dfrac{W}{L}$ (for PMOS) - $V_{GS}$ = Gate-to-Source voltage (V) - $V_{th}$ = Threshold voltage (V) - $W/L$ = Aspect ratio of the channel The term $(V_{GS} - V_{th})$ is called the **effective voltage** or **overdrive voltage** $V_{OV}$. --- ## 2.2 Biasing Techniques for Enhancement MOSFETs ### 2.2.1 Fixed Bias (Gate Bias) **Structure:** A single DC supply $V_{GG}$ drives the gate through $R_G$, with $R_D$ at the drain. **Operating Logic (NMOS):** - Gate draws no DC current → $V_{GS} = V_{GG}$ (constant). - $I_D = K(V_{GG} - V_{th})^2$ (independent of $R_D$). - $V_{DS} = V_{DD} - I_D R_D$. **Advantages:** Simple, easy to design. **Disadvantages:** Highly dependent on $V_{th}$ variations; not thermally stable. > [!WARNING] > KTU Examiners frequently deduct marks for not explicitly stating the **direction of current flow** when analyzing PMOS fixed-bias circuits. Always draw the current arrow from Source to Drain for PMOS! ### 2.2.2 Voltage Divider Bias (Most Common, KTU High-Yield) **Structure:** Gate voltage set by resistor divider $R_1, R_2$. Source resistor $R_S$ provides DC and thermal stability. **Step-by-Step Analysis (NMOS):** 1. The gate is at voltage $V_G$ given by the unloaded divider (since gate current ≈ 0): $$V_G = V_{DD} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$ 2. The source voltage is $V_S = I_D R_S$. 3. Therefore, $V_{GS} = V_G - V_S = V_G - I_D R_S$. 4. Applying the saturation current equation: $$I_D = K(V_{GS} - V_{th})^2 = K(V_G - I_D R_S - V_{th})^2$$ 5. Apply KVL on the output loop: $$V_{DS} = V_{DD} - I_D(R_D + R_S)$$ > [!NOTE] > **PMOS Voltage Divider:** All voltage polarities are inverted. $V_{GG}$ is negative (or tapped from below ground). KVL must be written as $V_{SS} - I_D R_S - V_{DS} - I_D R_D = 0$, and $V_{SG} = |V_S| - |V_G|$. ### 2.2.3 Drain-Feedback Bias - The gate resistor $R_G$ is connected to the drain instead of $V_{DD}$. - This provides **negative feedback**: if $I_D$ tries to increase, $V_{DS}$ drops, $V_{GS}$ drops, $I_D$ is pulled back down. - Excellent $V_{th}$ compensation. ### 2.2.4 Source Self-Bias (for Depletion-Mode MOSFETs) - $R_G$ connects gate to ground; $R_S$ between source and ground. - $V_{GS} = -I_D R_S$ (negative for N-channel depletion). - No $V_{GG}$ required. --- ## 2.3 The KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master this table — it covers 90% of KTU Module-3 numerical problems.** | # | Quantity | N-Channel (NMOS) | P-Channel (PMOS) | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Threshold Voltage $V_{th}$ | **+ve** (e.g., +1.5 V) | **−ve** (e.g., −1.5 V) | Volts (V) | | 2 | Drain Current (Saturation) | $I_D = K_n(V_{GS} - V_{th})^2$ | $I_D = K_p(V_{SG} + V_{th,p})^2$ | Amperes (A) | | 3 | Drain Current (Triode) | $I_D = K_n\left[2(V_{GS}-V_{th})V_{DS} - V_{DS}^2\right]$ | $I_D = K_p\left[2(V_{SG}+V_{th})V_{SD} - V_{SD}^2\right]$ | Amperes (A) | | 4 | Conduction Parameter $K$ | $K_n = \frac{\mu_n C_{ox}}{2}\frac{W}{L}$ | $K_p = \frac{\mu_p C_{ox}}{2}\frac{W}{L}$ | A/V² | | 5 | Saturation Condition | $V_{DS} \geq V_{GS} - V_{th}$ | $V_{SD} \geq V_{SG} + V_{th,p}$ | — | | 6 | Transconductance $g_m$ | $g_m = 2K_n(V_{GS} - V_{th}) = 2\sqrt{K_n I_D}$ | $g_m = 2K_p(V_{SG} + V_{th,p}) = 2\sqrt{K_p I_D}$ | Siemens (S) or A/V | | 7 | Output Resistance $r_o$ | $r_o = \dfrac{1}{\lambda I_D}$ | $r_o = \dfrac{1}{\lambda I_D}$ | Ohms ($\Omega$) | | 8 | Voltage Divider $V_G$ | $V_G = V_{DD} \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1+R_2}$ | $V_G = -V_{SS} \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1+R_2}$ (ref. ground) | Volts (V) | | 9 | KVL on Output Loop | $V_{DD} = I_D R_D + V_{DS} + I_D R_S$ | $V_{SS} = I_D R_S + V_{SD} + I_D R_D$ | — | | 10 | Power Dissipation | $P_D = V_{DS} \cdot I_D$ | $P_D = V_{SD} \cdot I_D$ | Watts (W) | --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **CMOS Inverters in Microprocessors:** Billions of NMOS/PMOS pairs (PCET logic gates) are biased in complementary fashion — the hallmark of digital IC design. - **Analog Amplifier Stages (Common-Source):** Voltage-divider biased NMOS in saturation acts as the gain stage in op-amp input stages. - **Current Mirrors:** PMOS/NMOS matched pairs biased in saturation deliver precise reference currents in analog ICs. - **Power Electronics:** Large-area power MOSFETs in switching regulators use similar biasing for efficient low-loss operation. - **Sensor Front-Ends:** JFET/MOSFET input stages biased at low $I_D$ provide extremely high input impedance for biomedical and instrumentation amplifiers. --- ## 2.5 The Small-Signal Model (Connected to Biasing) Once the Q-point is established, the AC small-signal parameters are computed **at** that Q-point: $$g_m = \frac{\partial I_D}{\partial V_{GS}}\bigg\vert_{Q} = 2K(V_{GS,Q} - V_{th}) = 2\sqrt{K \cdot I_{D,Q}}$$ For a common-source amplifier, the voltage gain is: $$A_v = -g_m (R_D \Vert r_o)$$ > [!NOTE] > Notice that $g_m$ depends *directly* on the bias current $I_D$. This is why **biasing design is the first step** in any analog amplifier design — it determines the gain, bandwidth, and noise performance. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Drain Current Equation in Saturation We begin with the gradual channel approximation, where the inversion charge density at a point $y$ along the channel is: $$Q_I(y) = -C_{ox}\,[V_{GS} - V_{th} - V(y)]$$ where $V(y)$ is the channel potential at position $y$ measured from the source. The drain current (constant along the channel by KCL) is: $$I_D = -\mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L}\left[(V_{GS} - V_{th})V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^2}{2}\right]$$ **Step 1 — Identify the boundary condition for saturation:** As $V_{DS}$ increases, the inversion charge at the drain end $(y = L)$ shrinks: $$Q_I(L) = -C_{ox}\,[V_{GS} - V_{th} - V_{DS}]$$ **Step 2 — Set $Q_I(L) = 0$ (pinch-off condition):** $$V_{GS} - V_{th} - V_{DS} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_{DS,sat} = V_{GS} - V_{th}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute $V_{DS} = V_{DS,sat}$ into the drain current equation:** $$I_D = \mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L}\left[(V_{GS} - V_{th})^2 - \frac{(V_{GS} - V_{th})^2}{2}\right]$$ $$I_D = \mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L}\cdot \frac{(V_{GS} - V_{th})^2}{2}$$ **Step 4 — Define the conduction parameter $K$:** $$K \equiv \frac{\mu_n C_{ox}}{2}\cdot \frac{W}{L}$$ **Step 5 — Final compact saturation equation:** $$\boxed{\,I_D = K\,(V_{GS} - V_{th})^2\,}$$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Q-Point of Voltage-Divider Biased NMOS **Given Circuit:** $V_{DD}$, $R_1$, $R_2$, $R_D$, $R_S$, MOSFET with parameters $K$, $V_{th}$. **Step 1 — Find Gate Voltage (no gate current):** $$V_G = V_{DD}\,\frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$ This is the textbook unloaded divider formula, valid because $I_G \approx 0$ in a MOSFET. **Step 2 — Express $V_{GS}$ in terms of $I_D$:** By KVL on the gate-source-source loop: $$V_{GS} = V_G - V_S = V_G - I_D R_S$$ **Step 3 — Substitute into the saturation current equation:** $$I_D = K\,(V_G - I_D R_S - V_{th})^2$$ **Step 4 — Take the square root to convert quadratic into linear form:** $$\sqrt{\frac{I_D}{K}} = V_G - I_D R_S - V_{th}$$ **Step 5 — Solve for $I_D$ explicitly (linear equation in $\sqrt{I_D}$):** Let $x = \sqrt{I_D}$, then $I_D = x^2$: $$x = V_G - V_{th} - R_S x^2$$ $$R_S x^2 + x - (V_G - V_{th}) = 0$$ Applying the quadratic formula: $$x = \frac{-1 + \sqrt{1 + 4R_S(V_G - V_{th})}}{2R_S}$$ $$\boxed{\,I_D = \left[\frac{-1 + \sqrt{1 + 4R_S(V_G - V_{th})}}{2R_S}\right]^2\,}$$ **Step 6 — Compute $V_{DS}$ using KVL on the output loop:** $$V_{DS} = V_{DD} - I_D R_D - I_D R_S = V_{DD} - I_D(R_D + R_S)$$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — PMOS Q-Point for a Source-Bias Circuit **Given:** A P-channel enhancement MOSFET in a source-bias configuration with $-V_{SS}$ supply, source resistor $R_S$, drain resistor $R_D$, gate tied to ground through $R_G$. **Step 1 — Gate current is zero, so $V_G = 0$ (ground).** **Step 2 — Source voltage is determined by current flowing out of the source (conventional current for PMOS):** $$V_S = -V_{SS} + I_D R_S$$ (The source is *above* ground; the supply is negative, current $I_D$ flows from source to drain.) **Step 3 — Compute $V_{SG}$ (Source-to-Gate voltage):** $$V_{SG} = V_S - V_G = -V_{SS} + I_D R_S - 0 = -V_{SS} + I_D R_S$$ **Step 4 — PMOS saturation equation (note the signs):** $$I_D = K_p\,(V_{SG} + V_{th,p})^2$$ where $V_{th,p}$ is negative. **Step 5 — Substitute and solve for $I_D$ (analogous quadratic):** $$I_D = K_p\,(-V_{SS} + I_D R_S + V_{th,p})^2$$ Let $V_{SG0} = -V_{SS} + V_{th,p}$: $$I_D = K_p\,(V_{SG0} + I_D R_S)^2$$ This yields a quadratic in $I_D$: $$K_p R_S^2 I_D^2 + (2K_p R_S V_{SG0} - 1)I_D + K_p V_{SG0}^2 = 0$$ **Step 6 — Compute $V_{SD}$ using KVL:** $$V_{SD} = V_{SS} - I_D R_S - I_D R_D = -V_{SS} - I_D(R_D + R_S) - 2V_{SS}\quad \text{(corrected)}$$ KVL properly written: starting from ground, going up through $-V_{SS}$ supply, through $R_S$, to source, to drain, through $R_D$, to ground: $$V_{SD} = V_{SS} - I_D R_S - I_D R_D \quad \text{(magnitude)}$$ --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Small-Signal $g_m$ from Q-Point Starting from the saturation equation: $$I_D = K\,(V_{GS} - V_{th})^2$$ Differentiate with respect to $V_{GS}$: $$g_m = \frac{\partial I_D}{\partial V_{GS}} = 2K(V_{GS} - V_{th})$$ Since at the Q-point, $(V_{GS} - V_{th}) = \sqrt{I_D / K}$: $$g_m = 2K \cdot \sqrt{\frac{I_D}{K}} = 2\sqrt{K \cdot I_D}$$ This dual form is extremely useful in KTU problems where $I_D$ is given but $V_{GS}$ is not. --- ## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) **Problem:** An N-channel enhancement MOSFET in a voltage-divider bias circuit has the following parameters: $V_{DD} = 12$ V, $R_1 = 1$ M$\Omega$, $R_2 = 500$ k$\Omega$, $R_D = 2$ k$\Omega$, $R_S = 1$ k$\Omega$, $K = 0.5$ mA/V², $V_{th} = 1.5$ V. **Find:** The Q-point $(I_D, V_{DS})$ and verify saturation. **Solution:** Step 1: Gate voltage: $$V_G = 12 \cdot \frac{500\,k}{1\,M + 500\,k} = 12 \cdot \frac{1}{3} = 4 \text{ V}$$ Step 2: KVL gives $V_{GS} = V_G - I_D R_S = 4 - I_D(1\,k)$. Step 3: Apply saturation equation: $$I_D = 0.5\,m \cdot (4 - I_D \cdot 1 - 1.5)^2 = 0.5\,m \cdot (2.5 - I_D)^2$$ Step 4: Let $x = I_D$ (in mA), then: $$x = 0.5\,(2.5 - x)^2$$ $$x = 0.5\,(6.25 - 5x + x^2)$$ $$x = 3.125 - 2.5x + 0.5x^2$$ $$0.5x^2 - 3.5x + 3.125 = 0$$ $$x^2 - 7x + 6.25 = 0$$ Step 5: Quadratic formula: $$x = \frac{7 \pm \sqrt{49 - 25}}{2} = \frac{7 \pm \sqrt{24}}{2} = \frac{7 \pm 4.899}{2}$$ Two solutions: $x_1 = 5.95$ mA, $x_2 = 1.05$ mA. The physically valid solution is the **smaller** one (the larger would push the MOSFET out of saturation or below 0 V $V_{DS}$). $$\boxed{\,I_{D,Q} = 1.05 \text{ mA}\,}$$ Step 6: Compute $V_{DS}$: $$V_{DS} = 12 - I_D(2k + 1k) = 12 - 1.05 \times 3 = 12 - 3.15 = 8.85 \text{ V}$$ Step 7: Verify saturation. Compute $V_{GS} = 4 - 1.05 = 2.95$ V. Then $V_{DS,sat} = V_{GS} - V_{th} = 2.95 - 1.5 = 1.45$ V. Since $V_{DS} = 8.85 \text{ V} \gg 1.45 \text{ V}$, **the device is firmly in saturation.** ✓ # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram: MOSFET Biasing Analysis Flow This flow shows the systematic procedure a KTU student should follow when solving a biasing problem. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Identify MOSFET Type
N-channel or P-channel] --> B[Determine Biasing Configuration
Fixed / Voltage Divider / Drain-Feedback] B --> C[Find V_G using unloaded divider
V_G = V_DD times R2 by R1 plus R2] C --> D[Express V_GS or V_SG in terms of I_D
using KVL on gate-source loop] D --> E[Apply square-law equation
I_D = K times V_GS minus V_th squared] E --> F[Convert to linear form
take square root, solve quadratic] F --> G{Q1: I_D positive
and less than V_DD by R_D?} G -- No --> H[Reject solution, use other root] G -- Yes --> I[Compute V_DS using KVL
on output loop] I --> J{Q2: V_DS greater than
V_GS minus V_th?} J -- No --> K[Device in Triode region
Use triode equation] J -- Yes --> L[Q-point established
Compute g_m, r_o, gain] L --> M[Final Answer: I_DQ, V_DSQ, g_m] ``` --- ## 4.2 Block Diagram: NMOS vs PMOS Biasing Polarity Comparison ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph NMOS_BIAS [N-Channel Biasing] N1[V_DD Positive Supply] --> N2[Gate at V_G greater than V_th] N2 --> N3[Current flows Drain to Source] N3 --> N4[V_DS positive] end subgraph PMOS_BIAS [P-Channel Biasing] P1[V_SS Negative Supply] --> P2[Gate at V_G less than V_th] P2 --> P3[Current flows Source to Drain] P3 --> P4[V_SD positive or V_DS negative] end NMOS_BIAS --- COMP[Polarity Comparison Summary] --- PMOS_BIAS ``` --- ## 4.3 Block Diagram: Voltage-Divider Bias Circuit Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A[V_DD Supply] --> B[R_1 Resistor] B --> C[Gate Node V_G] A --> D[R_D Resistor] D --> E[Drain Node] E --> F[MOSFET Channel] F --> G[Source Node V_S] G --> H[R_S Resistor] H --> I[Ground] C -. Gate-Drain Capacitance
C_gd .-> E C -. Gate-Source Capacitance
C_gs .-> G J[R_2 Resistor] --> C J --> I K[Input AC Signal v_in] --> C E --> L[Output v_out to next stage] ``` --- ## 4.4 Decision Table: Region of Operation ```mermaid flowchart TD S[Given V_GS and V_DS] --> Q1{V_GS greater than V_th
for NMOS?} Q1 -- No --> R1[Cutoff Region
I_D = 0] Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{V_DS less than
V_GS minus V_th?} Q2 -- Yes --> R2[Triode / Ohmic Region
I_D = K bracket 2V_OV V_DS minus V_DS squared] Q2 -- No --> R3[Saturation Region
I_D = K times V_OV squared] ``` --- ## 4.5 Schematic Symbol Reference (Textual Representation) | Device | Symbol Description | Arrow Direction | |---|---|---| | **NMOS Enhancement** | Broken line between Drain and Source; **arrow on source points INWARD** (toward channel) | Source arrow: into the device | | **PMOS Enhancement** | Broken line between Drain and Source; **arrow on source points OUTWARD** (away from channel) | Source arrow: out of the device | | **NMOS Depletion** | Solid line between Drain and Source (channel exists at $V_{GS} = 0$) | Source arrow: into the device | | **PMOS Depletion** | Solid line between Drain and Source | Source arrow: out of the device | # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 > **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]** > **Define the threshold voltage of a MOSFET. How does it differ for N-channel and P-channel enhancement devices?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - (1 mark) **Threshold voltage** $V_{th}$ is the minimum gate-to-source voltage magnitude required to create a conducting inversion layer (channel) between source and drain. - (1 mark) For **N-channel enhancement MOSFETs**, $V_{th}$ is **positive** (typically +0.5 V to +3 V), and conduction requires $V_{GS} > +V_{th}$. - (1 mark) For **P-channel enhancement MOSFETs**, $V_{th}$ is **negative** (typically −0.5 V to −3 V), and conduction requires $V_{GS} < -V_{th}$ (equivalently $V_{SG} > |V_{th}|$). --- ### Question 2 > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]** > **List any three biasing techniques used for enhancement MOSFETs. State one advantage and one limitation of voltage-divider biasing.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - (2 marks) Three biasing techniques: 1. **Fixed bias (gate bias)** — simple but parameter-dependent. 2. **Voltage divider bias** — most stable, uses $R_1, R_2, R_S$. 3. **Drain-feedback bias** — provides automatic $V_{th}$ compensation. - (1 mark) **Voltage divider advantage:** excellent thermal and $V_{th}$ stability. **Limitation:** requires more components and design calculation; performance sensitive to resistor tolerances. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:** > - Forgetting to **check the saturation condition** ($V_{DS} \geq V_{GS} - V_{th}$) after finding $I_D$ — **loss of 2 marks**. > - Using the **wrong sign convention** for PMOS biasing (treating $V_{th}$ as positive) — **loss of 3-4 marks**. > - Skipping units in the final answer — **loss of 1 mark** per KTU strict marking scheme. > - Selecting the **physically invalid root** of the quadratic (the larger one) without checking — **loss of 2 marks**. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for drain current $I_D$ in saturation for an N-channel enhancement MOSFET. State and explain the condition for the device to operate in the saturation region. **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Set up the gradual channel approximation:** The mobile charge density at a point $y$ along the channel is: $$Q_I(y) = -C_{ox}[V_{GS} - V_{th} - V(y)]$$ **Step 2 — Write drain current from drift transport:** $$I_D = \mu_n C_{ox}\frac{W}{L}\left[(V_{GS} - V_{th})V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^2}{2}\right]$$ **Step 3 — Derive the pinch-off condition:** At the drain end ($y = L$), the channel charge vanishes when $V(y=L) = V_{GS} - V_{th}$. This defines $V_{DS,sat}$: $$V_{DS,sat} = V_{GS} - V_{th}$$ **Step 4 — Substitute back to get the saturation equation:** $$I_D = \frac{\mu_n C_{ox}}{2}\cdot\frac{W}{L}\cdot(V_{GS} - V_{th})^2 = K(V_{GS} - V_{th})^2$$ **Step 5 — State the saturation condition explicitly:** $$\boxed{\,V_{DS} \geq V_{GS} - V_{th}\,}$$ The device is in saturation when the drain-to-source voltage exceeds the overdrive voltage; beyond this, the channel is "pinched off" near the drain, and $I_D$ becomes (ideally) independent of $V_{DS}$. **(b) [7 Marks]** For an N-channel enhancement MOSFET in voltage-divider bias with $V_{DD} = 15$ V, $R_1 = 2.2$ M$\Omega$, $R_2 = 1$ M$\Omega$, $R_D = 4.7$ k$\Omega$, $R_S = 1$ k$\Omega$, $K = 0.25$ mA/V², and $V_{th} = 2$ V, determine the Q-point $(I_{D,Q}, V_{DS,Q})$ and verify saturation. **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Compute gate voltage:** $$V_G = V_{DD}\cdot\frac{R_2}{R_1+R_2} = 15\cdot\frac{1\,M}{2.2\,M + 1\,M} = 15 \cdot \frac{1}{3.2} = 4.6875 \text{ V}$$ **Step 2 — Express $V_{GS}$ in terms of $I_D$:** $$V_{GS} = V_G - I_D R_S = 4.6875 - I_D(1\,k)$$ **Step 3 — Substitute into the square-law equation:** $$I_D = 0.25\,m \cdot (4.6875 - I_D - 2)^2 = 0.25\,m\cdot(2.6875 - I_D)^2$$ **Step 4 — Take square root and solve linearly:** $$\sqrt{\frac{I_D}{0.25\,m}} = 2.6875 - I_D$$ Let $x = \sqrt{I_D/(0.25\,m)}$, so $I_D = 0.25\,m \cdot x^2$: $$x = 2.6875 - 0.25\,m \cdot x^2$$ **Step 5 — Solve the quadratic in $x$:** $$0.25\,x^2 + x - 2.6875 = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; x^2 + 4x - 10.75 = 0$$ $$x = \frac{-4 + \sqrt{16 + 43}}{2} = \frac{-4 + \sqrt{59}}{2} = \frac{-4 + 7.681}{2} = 1.841$$ (We discard the negative root: $x = -5.841$.) **Step 6 — Recover $I_D$:** $$I_{D,Q} = 0.25\,m \cdot (1.841)^2 = 0.25\,m \cdot 3.389 = 0.847 \text{ mA}$$ **Step 7 — Compute $V_{DS}$:** $$V_{DS} = V_{DD} - I_D(R_D + R_S) = 15 - 0.847\,m \cdot (4.7\,k + 1\,k) = 15 - 0.847 \times 5.7 = 15 - 4.83 = 10.17 \text{ V}$$ **Step 8 — Verify saturation:** - $V_{GS} = 4.6875 - 0.847 = 3.84$ V - $V_{GS} - V_{th} = 3.84 - 2 = 1.84$ V - Since $V_{DS} = 10.17 \text{ V} \gg 1.84 \text{ V}$, **saturation is verified.** ✓ $$\boxed{\,\text{Q-point: } I_{D,Q} = 0.847 \text{ mA}, \quad V_{DS,Q} = 10.17 \text{ V}\,}$$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** **(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of neat circuit diagrams, explain the operation of **fixed bias**, **voltage-divider bias**, and **drain-feedback bias** for N-channel enhancement MOSFETs. Compare their stability factors. **Model Solution:** **1. Fixed Bias:** - Circuit: $V_{GG}$ connected to gate via $R_G$; drain at $V_{DD}$ via $R_D$; source grounded. - $V_{GS} = V_{GG}$ (constant, since $I_G = 0$). - $I_D = K(V_{GG} - V_{th})^2$ → **highly sensitive** to $V_{th}$ variations. - Stability factor: $S = \dfrac{\partial I_D}{\partial V_{th}}$ is very large. **2. Voltage-Divider Bias:** - Circuit: $R_1, R_2$ set $V_G$; $R_S$ at source for negative DC feedback. - $V_{GS} = V_G - I_D R_S$. - If $I_D$ rises (due to temperature or $V_{th}$ drop), $V_S$ rises, $V_{GS}$ falls, $I_D$ is pulled back. - **Excellent stability**; widely used in practical designs. **3. Drain-Feedback Bias:** - Circuit: $R_G$ connects gate to drain instead of $V_{DD}$. - If $I_D$ increases, $V_D$ drops, $V_{GS} = V_D - I_D R_S$ drops, $I_D$ reduces → automatic compensation. - **Best $V_{th}$ compensation** among the three. **Comparison Table:** | Parameter | Fixed Bias | Voltage Divider | Drain Feedback | |---|---|---|---| | Stability against $V_{th}$ variation | Poor | Good | Excellent | | Number of components | 2 (R_D, R_G) | 4 (R_1, R_2, R_D, R_S) | 3 (R_D, R_S, R_G) | | Design complexity | Low | Medium | Medium | | Use case | Switches, simple stages | Amplifier stages | Amplifier stages | **(b) [7 Marks]** A P-channel enhancement MOSFET has parameters $K_p = 0.4$ mA/V² and $V_{th,p} = -1.8$ V. It is biased in a common-source configuration with $V_{SG} = 4$ V. Calculate the drain current, the transconductance $g_m$, and the output resistance $r_o$ (assuming $\lambda = 0.02$ V⁻¹). **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Apply the PMOS saturation equation:** $$I_D = K_p(V_{SG} + V_{th,p})^2$$ $$I_D = 0.4\,m \cdot (4 + (-1.8))^2 = 0.4\,m \cdot (2.2)^2 = 0.4\,m \cdot 4.84 = 1.936 \text{ mA}$$ **Step 2 — Compute $g_m$:** $$g_m = 2K_p(V_{SG} + V_{th,p}) = 2 \cdot 0.4\,m \cdot 2.2 = 1.76 \text{ mA/V} = 1.76 \text{ mS}$$ (Alternatively: $g_m = 2\sqrt{K_p I_D} = 2\sqrt{0.4\,m \cdot 1.936\,m} = 2\sqrt{0.7744\,m^2} = 2 \cdot 0.88\,m = 1.76$ mS ✓) **Step 3 — Compute $r_o$:** $$r_o = \frac{1}{\lambda I_D} = \frac{1}{0.02 \cdot 1.936\,m} = \frac{1}{38.72\,\mu} = 25.83 \text{ k}\Omega$$ $$\boxed{\,I_D = 1.936 \text{ mA}, \quad g_m = 1.76 \text{ mS}, \quad r_o = 25.83 \text{ k}\Omega\,}$$ --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Module 3 — Rapid Revision Checklist** - [ ] **Saturation current equation:** $I_D = K(V_{GS} - V_{th})^2$ — memorize and apply without hesitation. - [ ] **Saturation condition:** $V_{DS} \geq V_{GS} - V_{th}$ (NMOS) or $V_{SD} \geq V_{SG} + V_{th,p}$ (PMOS). - [ ] **PMOS polarity inversion:** All voltages are mirrored; current flows Source → Drain; $V_{th}$ is negative; $V_{SG}$ replaces $V_{GS}$. - [ ] **Voltage divider bias is the gold standard** for analog amplifier stages due to thermal stability. - [ ] **Conduction parameter $K$** has units of A/V²; subscripts $n$ for NMOS, $p$ for PMOS. - [ ] **Two-equation, two-unknown strategy:** Combine KVL output loop with the square-law equation; solve via substitution or quadratic formula. - [ ] **Always verify saturation** after finding $I_D$ and $V_{DS}$ — this is a 2-mark KTU valuation guarantee. - [ ] **$g_m$ is bias-dependent:** $g_m = 2K(V_{GS} - V_{th}) = 2\sqrt{K I_D}$ — both forms are tested in KTU. - [ ] **Output resistance $r_o = 1/(\lambda I_D)$** — represents channel-length modulation; use only when the problem explicitly gives $\lambda$. - [ ] **Quadratic roots:** always select the smaller, physically valid $I_D$ that satisfies $V_{DS} > 0$ and saturation. - [ ] **Fixed bias = unstable; Drain feedback = best $V_{th}$ compensation; Source self-bias = only for depletion-mode devices.** - [ ] **Practical rule of thumb for design:** choose $V_{DS,Q} \approx V_{DD}/2$ for maximum symmetric AC swing. - [ ] **Current direction arrow:** Always draw the conventional current arrow from Drain to Source for NMOS, and Source to Drain for PMOS in KTU circuit diagrams. - [ ] **FET input impedance is essentially infinite** at DC — never assume gate current flows in biasing calculations. - [ ] **Q-point notation:** $I_{D,Q}$ or $I_{DQ}$ and $V_{DS,Q}$ or $V_{DSQ}$ are both acceptable in KTU answer scripts.

topics.title: Small Signal Equivalent Circuit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET302 - ELECTRONIC DEVICES AND CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Small Signal Equivalent Circuit

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Small Signal Equivalent Circuit** is a linear, AC-only circuit model of a non-linear electronic device (such as a BJT or FET) in which the device is replaced by its small-signal parameters under the assumption that the signal variations around the quiescent (Q) operating point are infinitesimally small (typically $v_{be} \ll V_T$ for BJT, where $V_T \approx \mathbf{26~mV}$ at room temperature).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** A small signal equivalent circuit is obtained by replacing the active device with its linear incremental parameters ($h_{ie}, h_{fe}, h_{re}, h_{oe}$ for BJT; $g_m, r_d$ for FET) derived by linearizing the device V-I characteristics at the DC bias point.

The two principal small signal models used in the KTU syllabus are:

1. **Hybrid (h) Parameter Model** — Most common for BJT amplifier analysis.
2. **r-Parameter (T) Model** — Used for deriving input/output resistances.
3. **Simplified Hybrid Model** — Approximation where $h_{re} \approx 0$ and $h_{oe} \approx 0$ (low frequency, moderate gain cases).
4. **FET Small Signal Model** — Voltage-controlled current source (VCCS) with $g_m$ and $r_d$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a non-linear device like a transistor as a **sloped hill** whose shape depends on the DC bias point (the Q-point). When a tiny rock (the AC signal) rolls down this hill, the hill looks essentially **flat** to the rock — its motion is predictable and linear.

- The **DC bias** sets the location on the hill (Q-point).
- The **AC signal** is so small that the hill appears flat (linear).
- The **small signal equivalent circuit** is the "flat hill approximation" — a linear model that predicts how the rock will roll.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 module-3 specifically requires deriving voltage gain $A_v$, current gain $A_i$, input resistance $R_i$, and output resistance $R_o$ for CE, CB, and CC configurations using both the **h-parameter model** and the **simplified model**.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics / Physical Constants

- Thermal Voltage: $V_T = \mathbf{26~mV}$ (at $T = 300~K$)
- Boltzmann Constant: $k = \mathbf{1.38 \times 10^{-23}~J/K}$
- Electron Charge: $q = \mathbf{1.6 \times 10^{-19}~C}$
- Common value: $\beta$ (current gain) ranges from **50 to 500** in standard silicon BJTs.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Do We Need a Small Signal Model?

A BJT is inherently a **non-linear** device (its $i_C$-$v_{BE}$ relation is exponential). However, for small AC signals superposed on a DC operating point, the device behaves **approximately linearly**. We linearize using Taylor series expansion around the Q-point, retaining only first-order terms — this yields the small-signal model.

## 2.2 The h-Parameter Model (Two-Port Network)

The BJT as a two-port network with input port (B-E) and output port (C-E):

$$v_1 = h_i \cdot i_1 + h_r \cdot v_2$$

$$i_2 = h_f \cdot i_1 + h_o \cdot v_2$$

| h-Parameter | Meaning | Common Emitter (CE) Notation |
|:-----------:|:-------:|:----------------------------:|
| $h_i$ | Input impedance (output shorted) | $h_{ie}$ |
| $h_r$ | Reverse voltage ratio (input open) | $h_{re}$ |
| $h_f$ | Forward current gain (output shorted) | $h_{fe} = \beta$ |
| $h_o$ | Output admittance (input open) | $h_{oe}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Typical values:** $h_{ie} = 1\text{–}5~k\Omega$, $h_{re} \approx 2\text{–}5 \times 10^{-4}$, $h_{fe} = 50\text{–}500$, $h_{oe} \approx 10\text{–}50~\mu S$.

## 2.3 Approximations Leading to the Simplified Hybrid Model

For most practical amplifier analysis (and KTU problems), the following approximations are valid:

- $h_{re} \approx 0$ (negligible feedback)
- $h_{oe} \approx 0$ (negligible output admittance, i.e., $r_o \to \infty$)
- $h_{ie} \approx r_\pi$ (intrinsic base-emitter resistance)
- $h_{fe} = \beta$

## 2.4 The r-Parameter (T) Model

| Parameter | Definition | Relation |
|:---------:|:----------:|:--------:|
| $r_e$ | Emitter junction dynamic resistance | $r_e = V_T / I_E \approx 26~mV / I_E$ |
| $r_b$ | Base spreading resistance | $r_b = h_{ie} - (1+\beta)r_e$ |
| $r_c$ | Collector dynamic resistance | $r_c \approx 1/h_{oe}$ |
| $\alpha$ | Common-base current gain | $\alpha = \beta/(1+\beta)$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Relationship:** $r_\pi = (1+\beta)r_e = \beta/g_m$, where $g_m = I_C / V_T$ is the transconductance.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Unit |
|:--------:|:------:|:-------:|:----:|
| Transconductance | $g_m$ | $I_C / V_T$ | Siemens (A/V) |
| Base-emitter resistance | $r_\pi$ | $\beta / g_m = V_T / I_B$ | $\Omega$ |
| Emitter resistance | $r_e$ | $V_T / I_E$ | $\Omega$ |
| CE Voltage Gain (simplified) | $A_v$ | $-g_m R_C = -\beta R_C / r_\pi$ | dimensionless |
| CE Current Gain | $A_i$ | $-\beta$ (approx) | dimensionless |
| CE Input Resistance | $R_i$ | $r_\pi$ (or $h_{ie}$) | $\Omega$ |
| CE Output Resistance | $R_o$ | $R_C$ (or $1/h_{oe}$) | $\Omega$ |
| CB Voltage Gain | $A_v$ | $+g_m R_C = R_C / r_e$ | dimensionless |
| CB Current Gain | $A_i$ | $\approx 1$ (slightly less) | dimensionless |
| CB Input Resistance | $R_i$ | $r_e$ (very low) | $\Omega$ |
| CC Voltage Gain | $A_v$ | $\approx 1$ (follower) | dimensionless |
| CC Current Gain | $A_i$ | $\beta + 1$ | dimensionless |
| CC Output Resistance | $R_o$ | $(R_S + r_\pi) / (1+\beta)$ | $\Omega$ |
| FET Common-Source $A_v$ | $A_v$ | $-g_m R_D$ | dimensionless |
| FET Source Follower $A_v$ | $A_v$ | $g_m R_S / (1 + g_m R_S)$ | dimensionless |
| FET Common-Gate $A_v$ | $A_v$ | $g_m R_D$ | dimensionless |

> [!WARNING]
> **Negative sign in CE gain:** The minus sign indicates a **180° phase inversion** between input and output — a hallmark feature of the Common Emitter amplifier. CB and CC do NOT invert.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **CE Amplifier:** Audio pre-amplifiers, IF stages in radios, voltage amplifiers where high gain with phase inversion is acceptable.
- **CB Amplifier:** High-frequency RF amplifiers, impedance matching from low-impedance sources (antennas, coaxial cables).
- **CC Amplifier (Emitter Follower):** Buffer stages, impedance matching to low-impedance loads (speakers, transmission lines).
- **FET Common Source:** High input impedance pre-amplifiers, audio equipment.
- **FET Source Follower:** Analog IC output stages, sample-and-hold circuits.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: CE Amplifier Using h-Parameter Model (Full)

Consider a CE amplifier with source resistance $R_S$ and load resistance $R_L$, output resistance $1/h_{oe}$ and collector resistor $R_C$ in parallel with $R_L$.

**Equivalent circuit equations** at the input loop (KVL):

$$V_s = I_b R_S + V_{be}$$

$$V_{be} = h_{ie} I_b + h_{re} V_{ce}$$

At the output node (KCL):

$$I_c = h_{fe} I_b + h_{oe} V_{ce}$$

$$V_{ce} = -I_c \cdot (R_C \parallel R_L \parallel 1/h_{oe}) = -I_c \cdot R_L'$$

where $R_L' = R_C \parallel R_L$.

### Step 1: Current Gain $A_i$

$$A_i = \frac{I_L}{I_b} = -\frac{I_c}{I_b} = -\frac{h_{fe}}{1 + h_{oe} R_L'}$$

**Explanation:** From the output KCL, the load current $I_L = -I_c$ since the dependent source drives current into the parallel combination, and the voltage $V_{ce}$ develops across it. We factor $I_c = h_{fe} I_b + h_{oe} V_{ce}$ and substitute $V_{ce} = -I_c R_L'$.

### Step 2: Input Resistance $R_i$

$$R_i = \frac{V_{be}}{I_b} = h_{ie} + h_{re} \cdot A_i \cdot R_L'$$

**Explanation:** Substituting $V_{ce} = -I_c R_L' = A_i I_b R_L'$ into the input KVL equation yields the term $h_{re} A_i R_L'$.

### Step 3: Voltage Gain $A_v$ (from base to collector)

$$A_v = \frac{V_{ce}}{V_{be}} = \frac{-h_{fe} R_L'}{h_{ie} + (h_{ie} h_{oe} - h_{re} h_{fe}) R_L'}$$

**Explanation:** Direct algebraic manipulation. The denominator correction term $(h_{ie} h_{oe} - h_{re} h_{fe})$ is small in practice.

### Step 4: Overall Voltage Gain $A_{vs}$ (from source to load)

$$A_{vs} = A_v \cdot \frac{R_i}{R_i + R_S}$$

**Explanation:** Voltage divider at the input — the base sees only a fraction of the source voltage.

### Step 5: Output Resistance $R_o$

$$R_o = \frac{1}{h_{oe}} \cdot \frac{1 + R_S (h_{ie}^{-1} - h_{re} h_{fe}/h_{ie})}{1 + R_S / h_{ie}}$$

For the simplified model ($h_{re}=0, h_{oe}=0$):

$$R_o \approx R_C$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: CE Amplifier Using Simplified Hybrid Model

Drop $h_{re}$ and $h_{oe}$. The circuit becomes a current-controlled current source $\beta I_b$ in parallel with $R_C \parallel R_L$.

$$A_v = \frac{V_o}{V_{be}} = \frac{-\beta I_b (R_C \parallel R_L)}{I_b \cdot r_\pi} = -g_m (R_C \parallel R_L)$$

$$A_i = -\beta$$

$$R_i = r_\pi$$

$$R_o = R_C$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Common Base (CB) Amplifier

The CB input is at the emitter; output is at the collector.

**Input loop KVL:**

$$V_s = I_e R_S + V_{eb} = I_e R_S + I_e r_e + (-\alpha I_e R_L' \cdot \text{terms})$$

**Output loop:** $V_o = -\alpha I_e R_L'$ (with appropriate signs)

### Step 1: Current Gain

$$A_i = \frac{I_L}{I_e} = \frac{\alpha}{1 + h_{ob} R_L'} \approx \alpha \approx 1$$

### Step 2: Input Resistance

$$R_i = r_e + (1-\alpha) R_L' \approx r_e$$

For the simplified case (ignoring feedback): $R_i = r_e = V_T / I_E$.

### Step 3: Voltage Gain

$$A_v = \frac{V_o}{V_{eb}} = \frac{\alpha R_L'}{r_e} \approx \frac{R_L'}{r_e} = g_m R_L'$$

**Positive sign** — no phase inversion.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Common Collector (CC) / Emitter Follower

The output is taken across the emitter resistor $R_E$.

### Step 1: Input Resistance

$$R_i = h_{ie} + (1+h_{fe}) R_E \approx r_\pi + (1+\beta) R_E \approx (1+\beta) R_E$$

(when $R_E$ dominates)

### Step 2: Voltage Gain

$$A_v = \frac{(1+h_{fe}) R_E}{h_{ie} + (1+h_{fe}) R_E} = \frac{\beta R_E}{r_\pi + (\beta+1) R_E} \approx 1$$

### Step 3: Output Resistance

$$R_o = \frac{R_S + h_{ie}}{1 + h_{fe}} \approx \frac{R_S + r_\pi}{\beta+1}$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation: FET Common Source Amplifier

The FET is replaced by a **voltage-controlled current source** $g_m V_{gs}$ in parallel with $r_d$ (often $\to \infty$).

### Step 1: Voltage Gain

$$A_v = -g_m (R_D \parallel r_d \parallel R_L) \approx -g_m R_D$$

**Explanation:** $V_{gs} = V_{in}$, $I_d = g_m V_{gs}$, $V_o = -I_d \cdot R_D$.

### Step 2: Input Resistance

$$R_i = R_G \quad \text{(gate is essentially open circuit)}$$

### Step 3: Output Resistance

$$R_o = R_D \parallel r_d \approx R_D$$

---

## 3.6 Numerical Example (KTU-Style Problem)

**Given:** CE amplifier with $\beta = 100$, $I_C = 1~mA$, $R_C = 4.7~k\Omega$, $R_L = 10~k\Omega$, $R_S = 1~k\Omega$, $V_T = 26~mV$. Calculate $A_v, A_i, R_i, R_o$.

### Solution

**Step 1:** Calculate $g_m$

$$g_m = \frac{I_C}{V_T} = \frac{1 \times 10^{-3}}{26 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.03846~S \approx 38.46~mS$$

**Step 2:** Calculate $r_\pi$

$$r_\pi = \frac{\beta}{g_m} = \frac{100}{0.03846} = 2600~\Omega = 2.6~k\Omega$$

**Step 3:** Effective load $R_L'$

$$R_L' = R_C \parallel R_L = \frac{4.7 \times 10}{4.7 + 10} = \frac{47}{14.7} = 3.197~k\Omega$$

**Step 4:** Voltage gain $A_v$

$$A_v = -g_m R_L' = -0.03846 \times 3197 = -122.96$$

**Step 5:** Current gain $A_i$

$$A_i = -\beta = -100$$

**Step 6:** Input resistance $R_i = r_\pi = 2.6~k\Omega$

**Step 7:** Output resistance $R_o = R_C = 4.7~k\Omega$

**Step 8:** Overall voltage gain from source

$$A_{vs} = A_v \cdot \frac{R_i}{R_i + R_S} = -122.96 \times \frac{2.6}{2.6 + 1} = -122.96 \times 0.722 = -88.78$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define the term small signal equivalent circuit. State the conditions under which a transistor can be replaced by its small signal model.**

**Model Answer:**

A small signal equivalent circuit is a linearized AC model of a non-linear device (BJT/FET) in which the device is replaced by its small-signal parameters (such as $h_{ie}, h_{fe}, h_{re}, h_{oe}$ for BJT) derived by linearizing the device V-I characteristics at the DC operating (Q) point.

**Conditions:**
- The AC signal amplitude must be small enough that the device operates over a linear region of its characteristic.
- For a BJT: $v_{be} \ll V_T$ (thermal voltage, **26 mV** at room temperature).
- The Q-point must be fixed by proper DC biasing and remain stable during signal variation.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**List the four h-parameters of a transistor in CE configuration and state the significance of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| h-Parameter | Significance |
|:-----------:|:------------:|
| $h_{ie}$ | Input impedance with output AC short-circuited (units: $\Omega$) |
| $h_{re}$ | Reverse voltage feedback ratio with input AC open-circuited (dimensionless) |
| $h_{fe}$ | Forward short-circuit current gain (dimensionless) |
| $h_{oe}$ | Output admittance with input AC open-circuited (units: Siemens) |

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 - Model Question Paper]**

**Question:** 
(a) Draw the h-parameter equivalent circuit of a CE amplifier and derive expressions for current gain $A_i$, input resistance $R_i$, and voltage gain $A_v$ in terms of h-parameters and load resistance $R_L$. **(7 marks)**

(b) For a CE amplifier with $\beta = 120$, $I_C = 1.5~mA$, $R_C = 4.7~k\Omega$, $R_L = 6.8~k\Omega$, $V_T = 26~mV$, calculate $A_v, A_i, R_i, R_o$ using the simplified hybrid model. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution

#### Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)

The CE h-parameter equivalent circuit consists of:
- Input port: $h_{ie}$ in series with a voltage source $h_{re} V_{ce}$ (dependent).
- Output port: Current source $h_{fe} I_b$ in parallel with $1/h_{oe}$ and load $R_L$.

**Applying KVL at input loop:**

$$V_{be} = h_{ie} I_b + h_{re} V_{ce}$$

**Applying KCL at output node:**

$$I_c = h_{fe} I_b + h_{oe} V_{ce}$$

With $V_{ce} = -I_c R_L'$ where $R_L' = R_C \parallel R_L$:

**Current Gain:**

$$I_c = \frac{h_{fe} I_b}{1 + h_{oe} R_L'}$$

$$A_i = \frac{I_L}{I_b} = -I_c/I_b = \frac{-h_{fe}}{1 + h_{oe} R_L'}$$

**Input Resistance:**

$$R_i = \frac{V_{be}}{I_b} = h_{ie} + h_{re} A_i R_L' = h_{ie} - \frac{h_{re} h_{fe} R_L'}{1 + h_{oe} R_L'}$$

**Voltage Gain (base to collector):**

$$A_v = \frac{V_o}{V_{be}} = \frac{A_i R_L'}{R_i} = \frac{-h_{fe} R_L'}{h_{ie} + (h_{ie} h_{oe} - h_{re} h_{fe}) R_L'}$$

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given:** $\beta = 120$, $I_C = 1.5~mA$, $R_C = 4.7~k\Omega$, $R_L = 6.8~k\Omega$, $V_T = 26~mV$

**Step 1: Calculate $g_m$**

$$g_m = \frac{I_C}{V_T} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^{-3}}{26 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0577~S = 57.7~mS$$

**Step 2: Calculate $r_\pi$**

$$r_\pi = \frac{\beta}{g_m} = \frac{120}{0.0577} = 2080~\Omega \approx 2.08~k\Omega$$

**Step 3: Effective load $R_L'$**

$$R_L' = \frac{R_C \cdot R_L}{R_C + R_L} = \frac{4.7 \times 6.8}{4.7 + 6.8} = \frac{31.96}{11.5} = 2.779~k\Omega$$

**Step 4: Voltage gain**

$$A_v = -g_m R_L' = -0.0577 \times 2779 = -160.34$$

**Step 5: Current gain**

$$A_i = -\beta = -120$$

**Step 6: Input resistance**

$$R_i = r_\pi = 2.08~k\Omega$$

**Step 7: Output resistance**

$$R_o = R_C = 4.7~k\Omega$$

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam - June 2023]**

**Question:**
(a) Draw the small signal equivalent circuit of a Common Collector (emitter follower) amplifier using h-parameters. Derive expressions for voltage gain $A_v$, input resistance $R_i$, and output resistance $R_o$. **(7 marks)**

(b) Compare the small signal parameters of CE, CB, and CC configurations. State two applications of each. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution

#### Part (a) — CC Amplifier Derivation (7 marks)

In the CC configuration, the output is taken across the emitter resistor $R_E$. The input loop contains $R_S$ and $h_{ie}$; the output loop contains $R_E$ in series with $(1+h_{fe})r_e$ feedback terms.

**Voltage Gain:**

Output voltage $V_o$ is the voltage across $R_E$:

$$V_o = (1 + h_{fe}) I_b R_E \cdot (\text{after current division})$$

$$A_v = \frac{V_o}{V_i} = \frac{(1+h_{fe}) R_E}{h_{ie} + (1+h_{fe}) R_E} = \frac{(\beta+1) R_E}{r_\pi + (\beta+1) R_E} \approx 1$$

**Input Resistance:**

$$R_i = h_{ie} + (1+h_{fe}) R_E \approx (\beta+1) R_E \quad \text{(large)}$$

**Output Resistance:**

$$R_o = \frac{R_S + h_{ie}}{1+h_{fe}} \approx \frac{R_S + r_\pi}{\beta+1} \quad \text{(very small)}$$

#### Part (b) — Comparison Table (7 marks)

| Parameter | CE | CB | CC |
|:---------:|:--:|:--:|:--:|
| Voltage Gain $A_v$ | High (negative) | High (positive) | $\approx 1$ (positive) |
| Current Gain $A_i$ | High ($\beta$) | $\approx 1$ | High ($\beta+1$) |
| Input Resistance $R_i$ | Medium ($r_\pi$) | Very low ($r_e$) | Very high |
| Output Resistance $R_o$ | Medium ($R_C$) | High ($R_C$) | Very low |
| Phase Inversion | Yes (180°) | No (0°) | No (0°) |

**Applications:**
- **CE:** Audio pre-amplifiers, RF IF stages, general purpose voltage amplifiers.
- **CB:** High-frequency RF amplifiers, impedance matching from low-impedance antennas.
- **CC (Emitter Follower):** Buffer amplifier, impedance matching to low-impedance loads, power amplifier driver stage.

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign** in CE voltage gain — always include $-g_m R_C$ to indicate phase inversion. **(Lose 1 mark)**
> 2. **Using $I_C$ instead of $I_E$** in the $r_e$ formula — always use emitter current $I_E \approx I_C$ for small errors, or $I_E = I_C + I_B$ for precise calculation.
> 3. **Confusing $r_\pi$ and $r_e$** — remember $r_\pi = (1+\beta) r_e$. Mixing them up leads to grossly wrong $A_v$ values.
> 4. **Not simplifying load** — for CE/CB, $R_L$ is in parallel with $R_C$ in AC analysis. Forgetting this gives wrong gain.
> 5. **Wrong units** — $g_m$ is in Siemens (or A/V), $V_T$ is in volts, $I_C$ is in amperes. KTU examiners deduct marks for inconsistent units.
> 6. **Skipping steps in derivation** — the 7-mark derivation questions require showing every step. Boxed final answers without intermediate steps lose partial credit.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ Small signal model is valid only when AC signal amplitude is small ($v_{be} \ll V_T$).
- ✅ Thermal voltage $V_T = 26~mV$ at room temperature ($300~K$).
- ✅ h-parameter model: $h_{ie}$ (input R), $h_{re}$ (reverse V gain), $h_{fe}$ (forward I gain = $\beta$), $h_{oe}$ (output admittance).
- ✅ Simplified model: $h_{re} \approx 0$, $h_{oe} \approx 0$ — most commonly used in KTU problems.
- ✅ **CE:** $A_v = -g_m R_L'$, $A_i = -\beta$, $R_i = r_\pi$, $R_o = R_C$, **180° phase inversion**.
- ✅ **CB:** $A_v = +g_m R_L'$, $A_i \approx 1$, $R_i = r_e$ (very low), $R_o = R_C$, **no phase inversion**.
- ✅ **CC (Emitter Follower):** $A_v \approx 1$, $A_i = \beta+1$, $R_i \approx \beta R_E$ (high), $R_o \approx R_S/\beta$ (low), **no phase inversion**.
- ✅ **FET CS:** $A_v = -g_m R_D$, $R_i = R_G$ (very high).
- ✅ **FET CD (Source Follower):** $A_v = g_m R_S/(1 + g_m R_S) \approx 1$.
- ✅ **FET CG:** $A_v = g_m R_D$, $R_i = 1/g_m$ (low).
- ✅ Master formulas: $g_m = I_C/V_T$, $r_\pi = \beta/g_m = V_T/I_B$, $r_e = V_T/I_E$, $r_\pi = (1+\beta)r_e$.
- ✅ Always compute $R_L' = R_C \parallel R_L$ for AC analysis.
- ✅ Phase inversion is the trademark of CE configuration; CB and CC do not invert.
- ✅ For Part B derivations, always show KVL/KCL steps explicitly — KTU examiners award step marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Small Signal Voltage Gain
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET302 - ELECTRONIC DEVICES AND CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Small Signal Voltage Gain

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Small Signal Voltage Gain — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Small Signal Voltage Gain ($A_v$)** is defined as the ratio of the small-signal **output voltage** delivered to a load to the **small-signal input voltage** applied at the input terminals of an amplifier, under the assumption that the transistor operates strictly in the **linear (active) region** and the signal excursions are small enough that the device parameters ($h_{ie}$, $h_{fe}$, etc.) can be treated as **constants** independent of the instantaneous signal value.

Mathematically:

$$A_v \equiv \left. \frac{v_{out}}{v_{in}} \right|_{i.c. \, \text{conditions fixed}}$$

For a loaded amplifier, it is the **overall voltage gain**:

$$A_{v(s)} = A_{v(\text{no-load})} \cdot \frac{R_L}{R_L + R_{out}}$$

where $R_L$ is the load resistance and $R_{out}$ is the amplifier's output resistance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Small Signal"?** Because the BJT is inherently a **non-linear device** (the Ebers–Moll equations are exponential). To permit linear (superposition-based) analysis, we restrict the input to a tiny perturbation (typically $\leq 10\,\text{mV}$ for a silicon BJT) around the quiescent (Q) operating point so that the small-signal parameters remain essentially constant during the AC cycle.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Volume Knob on a Mic

Imagine a microphone connected to a public-address (PA) amplifier feeding a loudspeaker. The microphone produces a **weak whisper** (a few millivolts), the amplifier magnifies it, and the loudspeaker produces a **loud voice** (volts). 

The **voltage gain** is simply the ratio:
- *Output loudness* (volts at speaker) ÷ *Input whisper* (volts at mic)
- It tells you **how many times** the amplifier "stretches" the input signal in voltage amplitude.

The "small signal" qualifier means the input whisper is so soft that the amplifier behaves like a **predictable, linear stretcher** — it does not get "yelled at" (i.e., not pushed into saturation or cutoff), so the output remains a faithful, scaled-up replica of the input.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics (Highlighted)

- **$A_v$** : Voltage gain (dimensionless, often expressed in **dB** as $20 \log_{10} \vert A_v \vert$)
- **$h_{fe}$** : Forward short-circuit current gain (common-emitter), typically **50 to 300**
- **$h_{ie}$** : Input resistance (CE, with output shorted), typically **1 kΩ to 4 kΩ**
- **$g_m$** : Transconductance, **$I_C / V_T$** with $V_T \approx 26\,\text{mV}$ at **300 K**
- **$r_\pi$** : Base–emitter dynamic resistance = **$h_{ie}$** (in T-model, equal to $V_T / I_B$)
- **$r_e$** : Emitter dynamic resistance = **$V_T / I_E$ ≈ 26 mV / $I_E$**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Conditions for Validity

For a **small-signal** analysis to be mathematically valid, the following conditions must be satisfied at the quiescent point (Q-point):

1. **BJT is biased in the active (forward-active) region**: $V_{CE} > V_{CE(\text{sat})}$ and $V_{BE} \approx 0.7\,\text{V}$.
2. **Signal swing is small**: $v_{be}(t) \ll V_T$ (thermal voltage ≈ 26 mV at room temperature).
3. **Coupling/bypass capacitors are AC short circuits** at the signal frequency.
4. **DC supply rails ($V_{CC}$, $V_{EE}$) are AC grounds** because of their low internal impedance at signal frequencies.
5. **Hybrid parameters ($h_{ie}$, $h_{fe}$, $h_{re}$, $h_{oe}$) are constants** evaluated at the Q-point.

> [!NOTE]
> These five conditions allow us to replace the non-linear BJT with a **linear small-signal equivalent circuit** (h-parameter model, T-model, or hybrid-π model) and use **superposition** and **Ohm's law** freely.

## 2.2 The CE Small-Signal h-Model Recap

For a Common-Emitter (CE) configuration, the simplified small-signal h-parameter model uses two key parameters:

- $h_{ie}$ : input resistance with output shorted
- $h_{fe}$ : forward current gain with output shorted
- $h_{oe}$, $h_{re}$ are usually neglected (approximated as zero) for hand analysis.

| Element | Physical Meaning | Typical Value @ $I_C$ = 1 mA |
|---|---|---|
| $h_{ie}$ | Base–emitter dynamic resistance | **1 kΩ to 4 kΩ** |
| $h_{fe} = \beta_{ac}$ | Small-signal CE current gain | **50 to 300** |
| $g_m = I_C / V_T$ | Transconductance | **38.5 mA/V** |
| $r_\pi = \beta / g_m$ | Base–emitter resistance in π-model | **1.3 kΩ to 5.2 kΩ** |
| $r_e = \alpha / g_m \approx 1/g_m$ | Emitter dynamic resistance | **26 Ω** |

## 2.3 The KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the **exact voltage-gain expressions** you must memorize for KTU ESE. Master these in all three configurations.

| Configuration | No-Load Voltage Gain $A_v$ | With $R_S$ & $R_L$ : $A_{vs}$ | Input Resistance $R_{in}$ | Output Resistance $R_{out}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **CE** (Common Emitter) | $A_v = -h_{fe} \dfrac{R_C}{h_{ie}}$ | $A_{vs} = A_v \cdot \dfrac{R_{in}}{R_{in}+R_S} \cdot \dfrac{R_L}{R_L+R_C}$ | $R_{in} \approx h_{ie} \parallel R_B$ | $R_{out} \approx R_C$ |
| **CB** (Common Base) | $A_v = \dfrac{h_{fe} R_C}{h_{ie}}$ (positive, ≈ unity when $R_C$ small) | $A_{vs} = A_v \cdot \dfrac{R_{in}}{R_{in}+R_S} \cdot \dfrac{R_L}{R_L+R_C}$ | $R_{in} = \dfrac{h_{ie}}{1+h_{fe}} \approx r_e$ (very small) | $R_{out} \approx R_C$ |
| **CC** (Emitter Follower) | $A_v = \dfrac{(1+h_{fe}) R_E}{h_{ie} + (1+h_{fe}) R_E}$ (positive, < 1) | $A_{vs} \approx 1$ (voltage follower) | $R_{in} = h_{ie} + (1+h_{fe})R_E$ (large) | $R_{out} = \dfrac{h_{ie} + R_S}{1+h_{fe}}$ (small) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign convention:** The **CE** amplifier produces a **180° phase inversion** between input and output, hence the **negative** sign on $A_v$. The **CB** and **CC** configurations are **non-inverting**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **CE amplifiers** are the workhorse of audio pre-amplifiers, RF front-ends, and op-amp gain stages because they provide **high voltage gain** with moderate input impedance.
- **CB amplifiers** are used at very high frequencies (VHF/UHF) and in **low-noise applications** (e.g., the input stage of a TV tuner or satellite LNB) because of their excellent high-frequency response.
- **CC amplifiers (emitter followers)** are used as **buffers** between a high-impedance source and a low-impedance load — for example, driving a low-impedance speaker from a high-impedance op-amp output.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Voltage Gain of the CE Amplifier (h-Model, No Source Resistance, No Load)

### Step 1: Draw the AC equivalent circuit

Replace the BJT with its h-parameter small-signal model. Replace $V_{CC}$ with an AC ground, short-circuit all coupling and emitter-bypass capacitors, and replace $R_B$ and $R_C$ with their AC-grounded branches. The resulting circuit has:

- An input loop containing $v_{in}$, $h_{ie}$, and the dependent current source $h_{fe}\,i_b$.
- An output loop containing $R_C$ and the same dependent current source.

### Step 2: Apply KVL to the input (base–emitter) loop

The current entering the base is $i_b$. The voltage across $h_{ie}$ is exactly $v_{in}$ (the emitter is grounded in a CE).

$$v_{in} = i_b \cdot h_{ie}$$

### Step 3: Apply KCL at the output (collector) node

The collector current is $i_c = h_{fe} \cdot i_b$ flowing **from collector to emitter** inside the model. With $R_C$ connected from collector to AC ground, this current develops a voltage:

$$v_{out} = -\,i_c \cdot R_C = -\,(h_{fe}\,i_b) \cdot R_C$$

The **negative sign** appears because conventional current flowing from collector to ground (through $R_C$) leaves the collector node **negative** with respect to ground (passive sign convention).

### Step 4: Take the ratio $v_{out}/v_{in}$

$$A_v = \frac{v_{out}}{v_{in}} = \frac{-\,h_{fe}\,i_b\,R_C}{i_b\,h_{ie}}$$

The $i_b$ cancels cleanly:

$$A_v = -\,h_{fe}\,\frac{R_C}{h_{ie}}$$

This is the **fundamental CE voltage-gain expression** required by the KTU syllabus.

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The magnitude $\vert A_v \vert$ is proportional to the load $R_C$ and to the current gain $h_{fe}$, and inversely proportional to the input resistance $h_{ie}$. To obtain a large gain, one needs a high $\beta$ transistor biased at a reasonable $I_C$ (so $h_{ie}$ is moderate) and a large collector resistor.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — CE Voltage Gain Including Source Resistance $R_S$ and Load $R_L$

### Step 1: Recognize voltage division at the input

When a source resistance $R_S$ is present, the voltage reaching the base of the transistor is reduced by the divider formed by $R_S$ and the input resistance of the amplifier $R_{in} \approx h_{ie}$ (assuming $R_B \gg h_{ie}$):

$$v_{in} = v_S \cdot \frac{h_{ie}}{R_S + h_{ie}}$$

### Step 2: Recognize voltage division at the output

The amplified current $i_c = h_{fe}\,i_b$ now flows through the **parallel combination** of $R_C$ and $R_L$:

$$v_{out} = -\,h_{fe}\,i_b \cdot (R_C \parallel R_L)$$

### Step 3: Form the overall gain

$$A_{vs} = \frac{v_{out}}{v_S} = \frac{v_{out}}{v_{in}} \cdot \frac{v_{in}}{v_S}$$

$$A_{vs} = \left( -h_{fe}\,\frac{R_C \parallel R_L}{h_{ie}} \right) \cdot \left( \frac{h_{ie}}{R_S + h_{ie}} \right)$$

$$A_{vs} = -\,h_{fe}\,\frac{R_C \parallel R_L}{R_S + h_{ie}}$$

This compact formula captures **both** the source-loading and load-loading effects in a single expression.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — CB Amplifier Voltage Gain

### Step 1: AC equivalent with input at emitter

In a CB stage, the **base is AC grounded**, the **input is applied to the emitter**, and the output is taken at the collector. Let $i_e$ be the AC emitter current and $i_c$ the AC collector current.

### Step 2: Input loop

The small-signal emitter current is driven by the input voltage $v_{in}$ acting across the dynamic emitter resistance $r_e$:

$$v_{in} = i_e \cdot r_e = i_e \cdot \frac{h_{ie}}{1+h_{fe}}$$

### Step 3: Output loop

The collector current is related to the emitter current by $i_c = \alpha\,i_e$, where $\alpha = h_{fe}/(1+h_{fe})$. The output voltage developed across $R_C$ is:

$$v_{out} = -\,i_c\,R_C = -\,\frac{h_{fe}}{1+h_{fe}}\,i_e\,R_C$$

### Step 4: Take the ratio

$$A_v = \frac{v_{out}}{v_{in}} = -\,\frac{h_{fe}}{1+h_{fe}} \cdot R_C \cdot \frac{1+h_{fe}}{h_{ie}} = \frac{h_{fe}\,R_C}{h_{ie}}$$

The $1+h_{fe}$ factors cancel, leaving the **positive** (non-inverting) gain:

$$A_v^{CB} = +\,\frac{h_{fe}\,R_C}{h_{ie}} \quad (\text{same magnitude as CE, but no phase inversion})$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Emitter Follower (CC) Voltage Gain

### Step 1: AC equivalent

The collector is AC grounded; the load $R_E$ appears between emitter and ground. The input voltage is applied at the base.

### Step 2: KVL from base to ground

Going from base → through $h_{ie}$ → through the dependent source to emitter → through $R_E$ to ground:

$$v_{in} = i_b\,h_{ie} + i_e\,R_E$$

Since $i_e = (1+h_{fe})\,i_b$:

$$v_{in} = i_b\,h_{ie} + (1+h_{fe})\,i_b\,R_E = i_b\left[h_{ie} + (1+h_{fe})R_E\right]$$

### Step 3: Output voltage

The output is the voltage across $R_E$, which is also the emitter voltage:

$$v_{out} = i_e\,R_E = (1+h_{fe})\,i_b\,R_E$$

### Step 4: Take the ratio

$$A_v^{CC} = \frac{(1+h_{fe})\,i_b\,R_E}{i_b\left[h_{ie} + (1+h_{fe})R_E\right]}$$

$$A_v^{CC} = \frac{(1+h_{fe})\,R_E}{h_{ie} + (1+h_{fe})\,R_E}$$

This value is always **positive** and **less than unity** (≈ 1 when $R_E$ is large), justifying the name "follower".

---

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

**Given:** A CE amplifier has $h_{fe} = 100$, $h_{ie} = 1.5\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_C = 4.7\,\text{k}\Omega$, source resistance $R_S = 600\,\Omega$, load $R_L = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$. Find the overall small-signal voltage gain $A_{vs}$ and convert to dB.

**Solution:**

$$R_C \parallel R_L = \frac{4.7 \times 10}{4.7 + 10}\,\text{k}\Omega = \frac{47}{14.7}\,\text{k}\Omega \approx 3.197\,\text{k}\Omega$$

$$A_{vs} = -h_{fe}\,\frac{R_C \parallel R_L}{R_S + h_{ie}} = -100 \times \frac{3197}{600 + 1500} = -100 \times \frac{3197}{2100}$$

$$A_{vs} = -100 \times 1.5224 \approx -152.24$$

In dB:

$$A_{vs}(\text{dB}) = 20 \log_{10}(152.24) = 20 \times 2.1826 \approx 43.65\,\text{dB}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The negative sign is **purely a phase indicator** (180° inversion). For decibel magnitude, we use the absolute value.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Generic Small-Signal Amplifier

The following Mermaid diagram represents the **signal-flow architecture** of a generic small-signal voltage amplifier, with the four canonical gain stages and impedance interactions explicitly mapped.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    vS["vS Source Voltage"] --> RS["RS Source Resistance"]
    RS --> INNODE["Input Node"]
    INNODE --> RIN["Rin Input Resistance"]
    INNODE --> AMP["BJT Small-Signal Amplifier Core"]
    RIN --> GND1["AC Ground"]
    AMP --> ROUT["Rout Output Resistance"]
    ROUT --> OUTNODE["Output Node"]
    OUTNODE --> RL["RL Load Resistance"]
    OUTNODE --> VOUT["vout Output Voltage"]
    RL --> GND2["AC Ground"]
    AMP -. depends on .-> HFE["hfe Current Gain"]
    AMP -. depends on .-> HIE["hie Input Resistance"]
    RIN -.||.- HIE
```

## 4.2 Configuration Comparison Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CE["CE Common Emitter"]
        CE1["Input at Base"] --> CE2["Output at Collector"]
        CE3["Emitter AC Grounded"]
        CE2 --> CER["Av = - hfe RC / hie"]
        CER --> CEPROP["HIGH Gain, 180 phase, Med R_in"]
    end
    subgraph CB["CB Common Base"]
        CB1["Input at Emitter"] --> CB2["Output at Collector"]
        CB3["Base AC Grounded"]
        CB2 --> CBR["Av = + hfe RC / hie"]
        CBR --> CBPROP["Unity-ish, 0 phase, LOW R_in"]
    end
    subgraph CC["CC Emitter Follower"]
        CC1["Input at Base"] --> CC2["Output at Emitter"]
        CC3["Collector AC Grounded"]
        CC2 --> CCR["Av = 1+hfe RE / hie + 1+hfe RE"]
        CCR --> CCPROP["Less than 1, 0 phase, HIGH R_in"]
    end
```

## 4.3 CE Amplifier — Small-Signal h-Model Equivalent

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BASE["Base Node"] --> HIE["hie"]
    HIE --> EMIT["Emitter Grounded"]
    BASE --> CTRL["Dependent Current Source hfe times ib"]
    CTRL --> COLL["Collector Node"]
    COLL --> RC["RC"]
    RC --> GND["AC Ground"]
    COLL --> OUT["vout Terminal"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This schematic is the **textbook equivalent circuit** on which all CE derivations in Section 3 are based. The dependent source has a value of $h_{fe}\,i_b$ and is oriented from collector to emitter (conventional current direction).

## 4.4 Source-and-Load Loading Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VSRC["vS"] --> RSRC["RS"] --> NODEIN["v_in to BJT"]
    NODEIN --> HIE["hie"] --> GNDA["GND"]
    BJT["Amplifier: Av_ideal = -hfe RC / hie"] --> ROUT["RC or Rout"]
    ROUT --> NODELOAD["v_out to load"]
    NODELOAD --> RL["RL"] --> GNDB["GND"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define small-signal voltage gain of a BJT amplifier. Why is the qualifier "small-signal" essential?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Small-signal voltage gain $A_v$ is the ratio of output signal voltage to input signal voltage of an amplifier operating in the linear region. (1 mark)

$$A_v = \frac{v_{out}}{v_{in}}$$

The qualifier "small-signal" is essential because the BJT is a non-linear device (exponential $i_C$–$v_{BE}$ relation). For linear analysis using constant h-parameters, the input signal amplitude must be small (typically $< 10$ mV) so that the operating point remains essentially stationary. (2 marks)

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Compare the voltage gain and phase relationship of CE, CB, and CC BJT configurations.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Configuration | Voltage Gain Magnitude | Phase | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| CE | Large ($\vert A_v \vert = h_{fe} R_C / h_{ie}$) | 180° inversion | General voltage amplifier |
| CB | Moderate ($\approx A_v^{CE}$ magnitude) | 0° (non-inverting) | High-frequency, low-noise |
| CC | < 1 (≈ 0.99 follower) | 0° (non-inverting) | Buffer / impedance matching |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### `Question A (14 Marks)` — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**For a CE amplifier using h-parameters, derive the expression for:**
**(a)** The small-signal voltage gain with source resistance $R_S$ and load $R_L$ included. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** The expressions for input and output resistance. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Step 1:** Replace the BJT with its small-signal h-model and apply AC short-circuit equivalents. The base sees $R_S$ in series with the input resistance; the collector sees $R_C \parallel R_L$. (1 mark)

**Step 2:** Input KVL gives $v_{in} = i_b h_{ie}$, hence $i_b = v_{in}/h_{ie}$. (1 mark)

**Step 3:** Output current $i_c = h_{fe} i_b$ flows through $R_C \parallel R_L$, so $v_{out} = -h_{fe} i_b (R_C \parallel R_L)$. (1 mark)

**Step 4:** Voltage division at the input: $v_{in} = v_S \cdot h_{ie}/(R_S + h_{ie})$ (assuming $R_B \gg h_{ie}$). (1 mark)

**Step 5:** Form the overall ratio:

$$A_{vs} = \frac{v_{out}}{v_S} = -h_{fe} \cdot \frac{R_C \parallel R_L}{R_S + h_{ie}} \tag{1.5 marks}$$

**Step 6:** State the validity conditions: active region operation, $v_{be} \ll V_T$, all h-parameters constant. (0.5 mark)

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Input resistance** — Looking into the base, the BJT contributes $h_{ie}$; this is in parallel with the bias resistors $R_1 \parallel R_2$:

$$R_{in} = h_{ie} \parallel R_B = \frac{h_{ie} \cdot R_B}{h_{ie} + R_B} \tag{3 marks}$$

**Output resistance** — Looking back into the collector, the dependent source becomes open-circuited (for ideal $h_{oe} = 0$). The output sees only the collector resistor:

$$R_{out} = R_C \tag{2 marks}$$

**Effective source loading** — The amplifier's input resistance appears as a load to the source, so the source delivers $v_{in} = v_S \cdot R_{in}/(R_S + R_{in})$. (2 marks)

---

### `Question B (14 Marks)` — `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the small-signal voltage gain of an **emitter follower (CC configuration)** using the h-model. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** A CE amplifier has $h_{ie} = 2\,\text{k}\Omega$, $h_{fe} = 120$, $R_C = 5\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_S = 1\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_L = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$. Calculate the overall voltage gain $A_{vs}$ in linear and dB form. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Step 1:** In CC configuration, collector is AC grounded and output is at emitter across $R_E$. (1 mark)

**Step 2:** Apply KVL from base to ground through the model: $v_{in} = i_b h_{ie} + (1+h_{fe}) i_b R_E$. (2 marks)

**Step 3:** Output voltage: $v_{out} = (1+h_{fe}) i_b R_E$. (1 mark)

**Step 4:** Form the ratio:

$$A_v^{CC} = \frac{(1+h_{fe}) R_E}{h_{ie} + (1+h_{fe}) R_E} \tag{2 marks}$$

**Step 5:** Show that $A_v^{CC} \to 1$ as $R_E \to \infty$ (voltage follower property) and that the gain is always positive (non-inverting). (1 mark)

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Step 1:** Compute the effective AC load:

$$R_C \parallel R_L = \frac{5 \times 10}{5 + 10} = \frac{50}{15} = 3.333\,\text{k}\Omega \tag{2 marks}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the overall-gain formula:

$$A_{vs} = -h_{fe} \cdot \frac{R_C \parallel R_L}{R_S + h_{ie}} = -120 \cdot \frac{3.333}{1 + 2} \tag{2 marks}$$

$$A_{vs} = -120 \cdot \frac{3.333}{3} = -120 \cdot 1.111 = -133.33 \tag{1 mark}$$

**Step 3:** Convert to decibels:

$$A_{vs}(\text{dB}) = 20 \log_{10}(133.33) = 20 \times 2.1248 \approx 42.5\,\text{dB} \tag{2 marks}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Sign convention forgotten** — Students often drop the negative sign in $A_v = -h_{fe} R_C/h_{ie}$. The negative sign is **mandatory** because it encodes the 180° phase inversion of the CE stage.
> 2. **Unit mismatch in $R_C \parallel R_L$** — Mixing kΩ and Ω in the same expression is a common arithmetic slip. Convert all resistances to the same unit (typically kΩ) **before** substitution.
> 3. **dB conversion errors** — Use $20 \log_{10}$ for **voltage** gain, NOT $10 \log_{10}$ (which is for power). Using the wrong factor costs 2–3 marks instantly.
> 4. **Confusing $R_{in}$ of BJT with $h_{ie}$** — When bias resistors $R_1$ and $R_2$ are present, $R_{in} = h_{ie} \parallel (R_1 \parallel R_2)$, **not** $h_{ie}$ alone.
> 5. **Forgetting $V_T = 26$ mV at 300 K** — When the question specifies a different temperature, you must update $V_T = kT/q$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Small-signal voltage gain** $A_v$ is valid **only** when the BJT is in the active region and the signal amplitude is small ($v_{be} \ll V_T \approx 26$ mV).
- **CE amplifier**: $A_v = -h_{fe} R_C / h_{ie}$ — **inverting**, **high gain**, moderate $R_{in}$.
- **CB amplifier**: $A_v = +h_{fe} R_C / h_{ie}$ — **non-inverting**, very **low $R_{in}$**, ideal for HF.
