- **CC amplifier (emitter follower)**: $A_v \approx 1$ (less than unity), **non-inverting**, **high $R_{in}$**, **low $R_{out}$** — used as a buffer.
- **Loaded gain**: $A_{vs} = -h_{fe} (R_C \parallel R_L)/(R_S + h_{ie})$ — captures both source and load loading in one expression.
- **dB conversion**: $A_v(\text{dB}) = 20 \log_{10}\vert A_v \vert$ for voltage gain; use $10 \log_{10}$ only for power.
- **Typical values to memorize**: $V_T \approx 26$ mV at 300 K, $h_{fe}$ 50–300, $h_{ie}$ 1–4 kΩ.
- **180° phase inversion** is unique to the CE configuration — CB and CC are non-inverting.
- **Q-point stability** matters: gain depends on $h_{ie}$ and $h_{fe}$ which both depend on the DC bias current $I_C$.
- **Always sketch the AC equivalent circuit** before applying KVL/KCL — this is the #1 method to avoid algebraic mistakes.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Current Gain
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAET302 - ELECTRONIC DEVICES AND CIRCUITS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Current Gain # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Current Gain** is the ratio of the output (load) current to the input (signal) current in a transistor amplifier. It quantifies how effectively a Bipolar Junction Transistor (BJT) **amplifies** the current applied at its input terminal, making it the single most critical parameter when analyzing BJT amplifier behavior. In a **Bipolar Junction Transistor (BJT)**, three currents exist — the **Emitter current ($I_E$)**, the **Base current ($I_B$)**, and the **Collector current ($I_C$)**. These are governed by Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL): $$I_E = I_B + I_C$$ Because a BJT has **three terminals**, it can be connected in a circuit in three distinct configurations, each using a different terminal as the **common** (grounded) reference: 1. **Common Base (CB)** configuration 2. **Common Emitter (CE)** configuration 3. **Common Collector (CC)** configuration Each configuration defines its own **current gain**. ## 1.2 The Three Current Gains — Formal Definitions | Configuration | Current Gain Symbol | Definition | Typical Range | |---------------|---------------------|------------|----------------| | **Common Base (CB)** | $\alpha$ (alpha) | $\alpha = \dfrac{I_C}{I_E}$ | **0.95 to 0.99** | | **Common Emitter (CE)** | $\beta$ (beta) | $\beta = \dfrac{I_C}{I_B}$ | **50 to 300** | | **Common Collector (CC)** | $\gamma$ (gamma) | $\gamma = \dfrac{I_E}{I_B}$ | **51 to 301** | > [!NOTE] > **Memory Hook:** $\alpha$ is always **less than unity** (since $I_C < I_E$), $\beta$ is always **greater than unity**, and $\gamma$ is always **slightly greater than $\beta$**. ## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Water Tap & Pipe" Model Imagine a **water tap** supplying water to a wide-bore pipe: - The **emitter** is the **tap** (main water source). - The **base** is a **thin control nozzle** that you barely open. - The **collector** is the **wide pipe** receiving the bulk of the water. You twist the nozzle (apply a *tiny* base current), and a *huge* amount of water flows out through the wide pipe. The BJT behaves similarly — a **small $I_B$** controls a **large $I_C$**. This amplification is exactly what **$\beta$ (CE current gain)** measures. The CE configuration is the most used because it gives the **best combination of current gain, voltage gain, and power gain**, making it the workhorse of analog amplifier design. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of the Three Configurations ### A. Common Base (CB) — Current Gain $\alpha$ - **Input terminal:** Emitter - **Output terminal:** Collector - **Common terminal:** Base - **Current Gain:** $\alpha = \dfrac{I_C}{I_E}$ - **Why:** Since almost all emitter-injected carriers reach the collector, $\alpha$ is **just below 1**. - **Limitation:** Current gain is **less than unity** → no current amplification, but excellent high-frequency response. ### B. Common Emitter (CE) — Current Gain $\beta$ - **Input terminal:** Base - **Output terminal:** Collector - **Common terminal:** Emitter - **Current Gain:** $\beta = \dfrac{I_C}{I_B}$ - **Why:** Because $I_B$ is much smaller than $I_C$, $\beta$ is **large** (50–300). This gives both **current** and **voltage** amplification. - **Most widely used** configuration in amplifier stages. ### C. Common Collector (CC) — Current Gain $\gamma$ - **Input terminal:** Base - **Output terminal:** Emitter - **Common terminal:** Collector - **Current Gain:** $\gamma = \dfrac{I_E}{I_B}$ - **Why:** Since $I_E > I_C$, $\gamma$ is **larger than $\beta$**. - **Used as:** A *buffer* or *emitter follower* (impedance transformer). > [!TIP] > **Why is $\gamma = \beta + 1$ (not $\beta$)?** > Because the output current is the **emitter current** $I_E = I_B + I_C$, which is *greater* than the collector current. The "extra 1" comes from the base current itself contributing to the output. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > Memorize this table — it is the **most frequently tested** set of equations in BJT problems. | # | Formula | Description | Unit | |---|---------|-------------|------| | 1 | $I_E = I_B + I_C$ | KCL at the transistor | A | | 2 | $\alpha = \dfrac{I_C}{I_E}$ | CB current gain | dimensionless | | 3 | $\beta = \dfrac{I_C}{I_B}$ | CE current gain | dimensionless | | 4 | $\gamma = \dfrac{I_E}{I_B}$ | CC current gain | dimensionless | | 5 | $\beta = \dfrac{\alpha}{1 - \alpha}$ | $\alpha$ to $\beta$ conversion | dimensionless | | 6 | $\alpha = \dfrac{\beta}{\beta + 1}$ | $\beta$ to $\alpha$ conversion | dimensionless | | 7 | $\gamma = \beta + 1 = \dfrac{1}{1 - \alpha}$ | $\gamma$ in terms of $\alpha$ and $\beta$ | dimensionless | | 8 | $\alpha \beta = \gamma \alpha$ (basic) | Cross-product identity | dimensionless | | 9 | $I_C = \beta I_B$ | Collector current relation | A | | 10 | $I_E = (1 + \beta) I_B$ | Emitter current relation | A | **Standard symbol convention (per IEEE / KTU convention):** - $\beta$ may also be written as **$h_{fe}$** in hybrid-parameter notation. - $\alpha$ may also be written as **$h_{fb}$**. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **$\beta$** is used to **bias transistor amplifier circuits** (voltage-divider bias, emitter bias) — wrong $\beta$ estimates can push the transistor into saturation or cutoff. - **$\alpha$** is critical in designing **RF and microwave amplifiers** (common-base topology for high-frequency operation, e.g., in TV tuners and satellite LNAs). - **$\gamma$** is essential for designing **impedance-matching buffer stages** (emitter follower) between a high-impedance source and a low-impedance load. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Relationship Between $\alpha$ and $\beta$ We will rigorously derive the conversion formula $\beta = \dfrac{\alpha}{1-\alpha}$. **Step 1 — Start from the definition of $\alpha$:** $$ \alpha = \frac{I_C}{I_E} $$ *Logic:* By definition, $\alpha$ is the ratio of collector current to emitter current in the CB configuration. **Step 2 — Apply KCL at the transistor terminals:** $$ I_E = I_B + I_C $$ *Logic:* The total emitter-injected current splits between the base and the collector. There is no other current path. **Step 3 — Rearrange KCL to isolate the base current:** $$ I_B = I_E - I_C $$ *Logic:* Subtracting $I_C$ from both sides yields the base current expression. **Step 4 — Substitute into the definition of $\beta$:** $$ \beta = \frac{I_C}{I_B} = \frac{I_C}{I_E - I_C} $$ *Logic:* $\beta$ is defined as the ratio of $I_C$ to $I_B$. We have $I_B$ in terms of $I_C$ and $I_E$ from Step 3. **Step 5 — Divide numerator and denominator by $I_E$:** $$ \beta = \frac{\dfrac{I_C}{I_E}}{\dfrac{I_E}{I_E} - \dfrac{I_C}{I_E}} $$ *Logic:* Dividing every term by $I_E$ (which is non-zero) creates dimensionless ratios. **Step 6 — Recognize the ratios:** $$ \beta = \frac{\alpha}{1 - \alpha} $$ *Logic:* $\dfrac{I_C}{I_E} = \alpha$, and $\dfrac{I_E}{I_E} = 1$. **Step 7 — Final boxed result:** $$ \boxed{\;\beta = \frac{\alpha}{1 - \alpha}\;} $$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Relationship $\gamma = \beta + 1$ **Step 1 — Definition of $\gamma$:** $$ \gamma = \frac{I_E}{I_B} $$ *Logic:* CC current gain is the ratio of emitter to base current. **Step 2 — Substitute $I_E = I_B + I_C$:** $$ \gamma = \frac{I_B + I_C}{I_B} $$ *Logic:* From KCL, replace the emitter current with its constituent parts. **Step 3 — Split the fraction:** $$ \gamma = \frac{I_B}{I_B} + \frac{I_C}{I_B} = 1 + \beta $$ *Logic:* Each term is now a recognizable ratio: $\dfrac{I_B}{I_B}=1$ and $\dfrac{I_C}{I_B}=\beta$. **Step 4 — Final boxed result:** $$ \boxed{\;\gamma = 1 + \beta\;} $$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — $\gamma$ in Terms of $\alpha$ **Step 1 — Start with $\gamma = 1 + \beta$:** $$ \gamma = 1 + \beta $$ *Logic:* This is the result derived in 3.2. **Step 2 — Substitute $\beta = \dfrac{\alpha}{1 - \alpha}$:** $$ \gamma = 1 + \frac{\alpha}{1 - \alpha} $$ *Logic:* Replace $\beta$ with its equivalent in $\alpha$. **Step 3 — Combine over a common denominator:** $$ \gamma = \frac{(1 - \alpha) + \alpha}{1 - \alpha} $$ *Logic:* The LCM is $(1 - \alpha)$. Multiply $1$ by $\dfrac{1-\alpha}{1-\alpha}$. **Step 4 — Simplify the numerator:** $$ \gamma = \frac{1}{1 - \alpha} $$ *Logic:* $(1-\alpha) + \alpha = 1$. **Step 5 — Final boxed result:** $$ \boxed{\;\gamma = \frac{1}{1 - \alpha}\;} $$ --- ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Pattern) **Problem:** A silicon BJT has $\beta = 100$ and $I_B = 20\ \mu A$. Find $\alpha$, $\gamma$, $I_C$, and $I_E$. **Solution:** **Step 1 — Compute $\alpha$:** $$ \alpha = \frac{\beta}{\beta + 1} = \frac{100}{100 + 1} = \frac{100}{101} \approx 0.9901 $$ **Step 2 — Compute $\gamma$:** $$ \gamma = \beta + 1 = 100 + 1 = 101 $$ **Step 3 — Compute $I_C$:** $$ I_C = \beta \cdot I_B = 100 \times 20\ \mu A = 2000\ \mu A = 2\ mA $$ **Step 4 — Compute $I_E$:** $$ I_E = (1 + \beta) \cdot I_B = 101 \times 20\ \mu A = 2020\ \mu A = 2.02\ mA $$ **Step 5 — Verification using KCL:** $$ I_B + I_C = 20\ \mu A + 2000\ \mu A = 2020\ \mu A = I_E \;\checkmark $$ # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Current Flow and Current Gain Relationships in a BJT The following Mermaid flowchart visualizes how the three currents ($I_E$, $I_C$, $I_B$) split inside the BJT, and how each configuration defines its own current gain. ```mermaid flowchart TD sourceE["Emitter Current IE
Total Injected Carriers"] splitNode{"KCL Split Point
IE = IB + IC"} currIB["Base Current IB
Small recombination current
~ 1 percent to 2 percent"] currIC["Collector Current IC
Main useful output
~ 98 percent to 99 percent"] cbGain["Alpha equals IC by IE
Common Base Gain
Alpha less than 1"] ceGain["Beta equals IC by IB
Common Emitter Gain
Beta between 50 and 300"] ccGain["Gamma equals IE by IB
Common Collector Gain
Gamma equals Beta plus 1"] sourceE --> splitNode splitNode --> currIB splitNode --> currIC currIC --> cbGain currIB --> ceGain currIB --> ccGain currIC --> ceGain currIC --> ccGain cbGain --> formulaBox["Formulas:
Alpha = Beta by Beta plus 1
Beta = Alpha by 1 minus Alpha
Gamma = 1 by 1 minus Alpha"] ceGain --> formulaBox ccGain --> formulaBox ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Three Configurations This block diagram maps which terminal acts as **input**, which as **output**, and which is **common (grounded)** for each configuration, and how the corresponding current gain is measured. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph CBcfg["Common Base CB Configuration"] I_E_CB[/"Input: Emitter IE"/] COMMON_CB{{"Common: Base"}} O_CB[/"Output: Collector IC"/] CBGain["Gain: Alpha = IC by IE"] I_E_CB --> CBGain --> O_CB COMMON_CB --- I_E_CB COMMON_CB --- O_CB end subgraph CEcfg["Common Emitter CE Configuration"] I_B_CE[/"Input: Base IB"/] COMMON_CE{{"Common: Emitter"}} O_CE[/"Output: Collector IC"/] CEGain["Gain: Beta = IC by IB"] I_B_CE --> CEGain --> O_CE COMMON_CE --- I_B_CE COMMON_CE --- O_CE end subgraph CCcfg["Common Collector CC Configuration"] I_B_CC[/"Input: Base IB"/] COMMON_CC{{"Common: Collector"}} O_CC[/"Output: Emitter IE"/] CCGain["Gain: Gamma = IE by IB"] I_B_CC --> CCGain --> O_CC COMMON_CC --- I_B_CC COMMON_CC --- O_CC end CBcfg --> summary["Summary Mapping:
CB input is Emitter
CE input is Base
CC input is Base
CE is most widely used"] CEcfg --> summary CCcfg --> summary ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define Current Gain in CB, CE, and CC Configurations **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Current gain is the ratio of output current to input current in a BJT configuration. 1. **Common Base (CB):** $\alpha = \dfrac{I_C}{I_E}$, where $I_C$ is the collector current and $I_E$ is the emitter current. **Typical value: 0.95 to 0.99.** 2. **Common Emitter (CE):** $\beta = \dfrac{I_C}{I_B}$, where $I_B$ is the base current. **Typical value: 50 to 300.** 3. **Common Collector (CC):** $\gamma = \dfrac{I_E}{I_B}$. **Typical value: 51 to 301.** The three gains are related by $\alpha = \dfrac{\beta}{\beta+1}$, $\gamma = \beta + 1$, and $\gamma = \dfrac{1}{1-\alpha}$. --- ### Question 2: The $\beta$ of a BJT is 150. Find $\alpha$ and $\gamma$. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Step 1 — Compute $\alpha$:** $$ \alpha = \frac{\beta}{\beta + 1} = \frac{150}{150 + 1} = \frac{150}{151} \approx 0.9934 $$ **Step 2 — Compute $\gamma$:** $$ \gamma = \beta + 1 = 150 + 1 = 151 $$ **Answer:** $\alpha \approx 0.9934$, $\gamma = 151$. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > - Students commonly **forget the "+1"** when converting between $\beta$ and $\gamma$, writing $\gamma = \beta$ instead of $\gamma = \beta + 1$. This alone costs **2 marks**. > - Many students **mix up the formulas** ($\beta = \alpha/(1-\alpha)$ vs $\alpha = \beta/(1-\beta)$). Always derive from the definition before substituting. > - **Do not drop units** in numerical problems (mA, $\mu$A, etc.) — KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks per missing unit. > - Always **verify using KCL** at the end of a numerical answer to earn the "consistency" mark. --- ### Question A (14 Marks): Current Gain Relationships and Numerical Analysis **[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model Question]** **(a) Define $\alpha$, $\beta$, and $\gamma$ for a BJT. Derive the relationship $\beta = \dfrac{\alpha}{1-\alpha}$.** **[7 marks]** **Step 1 — Definitions (3 marks):** - $\alpha = \dfrac{I_C}{I_E}$ — Common Base current gain - $\beta = \dfrac{I_C}{I_B}$ — Common Emitter current gain - $\gamma = \dfrac{I_E}{I_B}$ — Common Collector current gain **Step 2 — Derivation (4 marks):** Starting from KCL: $I_E = I_B + I_C \;\Rightarrow\; I_B = I_E - I_C$. Substitute into $\beta$: $$ \beta = \frac{I_C}{I_B} = \frac{I_C}{I_E - I_C} $$ Divide numerator and denominator by $I_E$: $$ \beta = \frac{\dfrac{I_C}{I_E}}{1 - \dfrac{I_C}{I_E}} = \frac{\alpha}{1 - \alpha} $$ Therefore: $\boxed{\beta = \dfrac{\alpha}{1 - \alpha}}$ --- **(b) A transistor has $\alpha = 0.98$. Calculate $\beta$ and $\gamma$. If $I_B = 25\ \mu A$, find $I_C$ and $I_E$.** **[7 marks]** **Step 1 — Compute $\beta$:** $$ \beta = \frac{\alpha}{1 - \alpha} = \frac{0.98}{1 - 0.98} = \frac{0.98}{0.02} = 49 $$ **Step 2 — Compute $\gamma$:** $$ \gamma = \beta + 1 = 49 + 1 = 50 $$ **Step 3 — Compute $I_C$:** $$ I_C = \beta \cdot I_B = 49 \times 25\ \mu A = 1225\ \mu A = 1.225\ mA $$ **Step 4 — Compute $I_E$:** $$ I_E = (1 + \beta) \cdot I_B = 50 \times 25\ \mu A = 1250\ \mu A = 1.25\ mA $$ **Step 5 — Verification using KCL:** $$ I_B + I_C = 25\ \mu A + 1225\ \mu A = 1250\ \mu A = I_E \;\checkmark $$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks): Three Configurations and Comparative Current Gain Analysis **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model Question]** **(a) Explain the three transistor configurations (CB, CE, CC) with their current gain expressions.** **[7 marks]** **Common Base (CB) – 2.5 marks:** - **Input:** Emitter, **Output:** Collector, **Common:** Base - **Current Gain:** $\alpha = \dfrac{I_C}{I_E} \approx 0.95\text{–}0.99$ - $\alpha < 1$, hence **no current amplification**, but excellent **high-frequency response**. **Common Emitter (CE) – 2.5 marks:** - **Input:** Base, **Output:** Collector, **Common:** Emitter - **Current Gain:** $\beta = \dfrac{I_C}{I_B} \approx 50\text{–}300$ - Provides **both current and voltage amplification** — most widely used configuration. **Common Collector (CC) – 2 marks:** - **Input:** Base, **Output:** Emitter, **Common:** Collector - **Current Gain:** $\gamma = \dfrac{I_E}{I_B} = 1 + \beta$ - Used as a **buffer / emitter follower** for impedance matching. --- **(b) A BJT has $\beta = 200$. Calculate $\alpha$, $\gamma$, $I_C$, and $I_E$ when $I_B = 15\ \mu A$.** **[7 marks]** **Step 1 — Compute $\alpha$:** $$ \alpha = \frac{\beta}{\beta + 1} = \frac{200}{201} \approx 0.9950 $$ **Step 2 — Compute $\gamma$:** $$ \gamma = \beta + 1 = 200 + 1 = 201 $$ **Step 3 — Compute $I_C$:** $$ I_C = \beta \cdot I_B = 200 \times 15\ \mu A = 3000\ \mu A = 3\ mA $$ **Step 4 — Compute $I_E$:** $$ I_E = (1 + \beta) \cdot I_B = 201 \times 15\ \mu A = 3015\ \mu A = 3.015\ mA $$ **Step 5 — Verification using $\alpha$:** $$ I_C = \alpha \cdot I_E = 0.995 \times 3.015\ mA \approx 3.00\ mA \;\checkmark $$ --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist** - ✅ The three BJT currents always obey **KCL**: $I_E = I_B + I_C$. - ✅ **$\alpha = I_C / I_E$** — always **less than 1** (around 0.95–0.99). - ✅ **$\beta = I_C / I_B$** — always **greater than 1** (around 50–300). - ✅ **$\gamma = I_E / I_B$** — always **slightly greater than $\beta$**. - ✅ Key conversion: $\boxed{\beta = \dfrac{\alpha}{1-\alpha}}$ and $\boxed{\alpha = \dfrac{\beta}{\beta+1}}$. - ✅ Quick identity: $\boxed{\gamma = \beta + 1 = \dfrac{1}{1-\alpha}}$. - ✅ In the **hybrid-parameter (h-parameter)** model, $\beta$ is written as $h_{fe}$ and $\alpha$ as $h_{fb}$. - ✅ Always **verify answers using KCL** — this is a free checkmark in KTU valuation. - ✅ Always **carry units** (A, mA, $\mu$A) through the entire numerical solution. - ✅ **CE configuration is the most used** because of its high combined current, voltage, and power gain.

topics.title: Input and Output Impedances of CS Configuration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET302 - ELECTRONIC DEVICES AND CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Input and Output Impedances of CS Configuration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Common Source (CS) configuration** is the most widely used field-effect transistor (FET/MOSFET) amplifier topology, where the **source terminal is common to both the input port and the output port** (typically tied to ground, either directly or through a bypassed source resistor). The signal is applied between the **gate and source**, and the amplified output is taken between the **drain and source**.

**Input Impedance ($Z_{in}$)** of a CS stage is defined as the impedance presented by the amplifier to the signal source, measured looking into the **input terminals** (gate-to-source) of the amplifier with the output port left open-circuited (or with its nominal load attached, depending on convention).

**Output Impedance ($Z_{out}$)** is defined as the impedance presented by the amplifier to its load, measured looking back into the **output terminals** (drain-to-source) with the input source deactivated (independent source set to zero — short-circuited voltage source or open-circuited current source).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Definition (use verbatim in answers):**
> *"Input impedance is the equivalent Thevenin impedance seen by the source at the input, while output impedance is the equivalent Thevenin impedance seen by the load at the output, evaluated with all independent sources turned OFF."*

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of the CS amplifier as a **public-address (PA) microphone system** in a large auditorium:

- The **microphone (input)** is highly sensitive — it should not draw too much current from the singer's voice. In electronics, we say the input impedance should be **very high** so that the signal source is not "loaded down." For a MOSFET CS stage, the gate is insulated by a thin oxide layer — almost **no current flows in**, making $Z_{in}$ practically infinite.
- The **speaker (output)** has to push a lot of air. The amplifier must deliver current to it without the internal resistance of the amplifier "eating up" the signal. The output impedance represents this internal resistance. For a CS stage, $Z_{out}$ is typically a few kilo-ohms — set primarily by the drain resistor $R_D$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** High $Z_{in}$ is good (doesn't disturb the source), and low $Z_{out}$ is good (drives the load efficiently). The CS configuration naturally offers a **very high $Z_{in}$** and a **moderate $Z_{out}$** — making it ideal as a **voltage amplifier front-end**.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics (KTU High-Yield Values)

| Parameter | Typical Magnitude |
|---|---|
| $Z_{in}$ (MOSFET CS) | $\mathbf{10^{9} \text{ to } 10^{12}\ \Omega}$ (set by bias resistor $R_G$) |
| $Z_{in}$ (JFET CS) | $\mathbf{M\Omega \text{ range}}$ |
| $Z_{out}$ (CS) | $\mathbf{k\Omega \text{ range}}$ (set by $R_D \parallel r_d$) |
| Gate leakage current $I_G$ | $\mathbf{pA \text{ range}}$ (MOSFET) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept — Step-by-Step Logic

The CS small-signal (hybrid-π equivalent) model replaces the transistor with:
- A **voltage-controlled current source** $g_m v_{gs}$ between drain and source.
- An **output resistance** $r_d$ (or $r_o$) from drain to source, modelling the channel-length modulation.
- An **open circuit** between gate and source (since $i_G = 0$ for an ideal FET).

The bias network $(R_1, R_2)$ and the drain resistor $R_D$ are external components that determine the actual $Z_{in}$ and $Z_{out}$.

**Step 1 — Identify the input port:** For a CS stage, the input port is the **gate-source** pair. Because the gate draws essentially zero current, the input impedance is dominated entirely by any resistor connected between the gate and the DC supply (or ground).

**Step 2 — Identify the output port:** The output port is the **drain-source** pair. Looking back into this port with the input source killed, the impedance is the parallel combination of $R_D$ and the transistor's intrinsic output resistance $r_d$.

**Step 3 — Why $Z_{in}$ is "infinite" intrinsically:** The FET gate is reverse-biased (JFET) or oxide-insulated (MOSFET). Hence $i_G \approx 0$, so any external resistor $R_G$ carries no AC current — the input voltage drops entirely across $R_G$ (or $R_1 \parallel R_2$), and $Z_{in}$ is set by that resistor.

**Step 4 — Why $Z_{out}$ is "moderate":** When we deactivate $v_{gs}$ (short the input), the dependent source $g_m v_{gs}$ also becomes zero (since $v_{gs} = 0$). Thus the only path for output current is through $R_D$ in parallel with $r_d$, giving $Z_{out} = R_D \parallel r_d$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The very high $Z_{in}$ of a CS stage is exploited in **electrometer amplifiers**, **ECG/EEG biomedical front-ends**, and **sample-and-hold circuits**, where the source impedance is extremely high and the amplifier must not load it.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Condition / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Z_{in}$ (ideal MOSFET CS) | $Z_{in} = R_G$ | Gate current $\approx 0$ |
| 2 | $Z_{in}$ (with bias divider) | $Z_{in} = R_G \parallel (R_1 \parallel R_2)$ | When bias network is included |
| 3 | $Z_{out}$ (CS, finite $r_d$) | $Z_{out} = R_D \parallel r_d$ | With input shorted |
| 4 | $Z_{out}$ (CS, ideal) | $Z_{out} = R_D$ | When $r_d \to \infty$ |
| 5 | Voltage gain $A_v$ | $A_v = -g_m (R_D \parallel r_d \parallel R_L)$ | Negative sign = phase inversion |
| 6 | Transconductance $g_m$ | $g_m = \dfrac{2 I_{DQ}}{V_{GS} - V_{th}} = \dfrac{2 \sqrt{I_{DQ} \cdot k_n'}}{}$ | From square-law |
| 7 | Output resistance $r_d$ | $r_d = \dfrac{1}{\lambda I_{DQ}} = \dfrac{\vert V_A \vert}{I_{DQ}}$ | Channel-length modulation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **NOTE on LaTeX inside tables:** All absolute-value bars use $\vert \cdot \vert$ (not vertical pipes) to keep the markdown table syntax intact — a mandatory KTU notes-engine rule.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

CS amplifiers are the **workhorse of analog IC design**. Their high $Z_{in}$ allows them to be cascaded directly after sensors (piezoelectric, photodiodes, bio-potential electrodes) without attenuation. The moderate $Z_{out}$ is buffered by subsequent **source-follower (CD) stages** when driving low-impedance loads like speakers or 50 Ω transmission lines.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Input Impedance $Z_{in}$ of CS Configuration

**Given:** A CS amplifier with external gate-bias resistor $R_G$ (single-supply biasing) and an ideal MOSFET (gate current $i_g = 0$).

**Small-signal equivalent circuit at the input:**

Apply a test voltage $V_x$ between the gate and source terminals. The current drawn from the test source is $I_x$. By KCL at the gate node:

$$
I_x = \frac{V_x}{R_G} + i_g
$$

Since the MOSFET gate is oxide-insulated, $i_g = 0$. Therefore:

$$
I_x = \frac{V_x}{R_G}
$$

The input impedance is the ratio $V_x / I_x$:

$$
\boxed{Z_{in} = \frac{V_x}{I_x} = R_G}
$$

**If a bias divider $R_1$ and $R_2$ is used instead of a single $R_G$**, then the input sees $R_G$ in parallel with the Thevenin resistance of the bias network:

$$
\boxed{Z_{in} = R_G \parallel (R_1 \parallel R_2)}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation of Output Impedance $Z_{out}$ of CS Configuration

**Given:** A CS amplifier with drain resistor $R_D$, transistor output resistance $r_d$, and transconductance $g_m$. To find $Z_{out}$, we (i) deactivate the input source ($v_{gs} = 0$), (ii) remove the load $R_L$, and (iii) apply a test source $V_x$ at the output.

**Small-signal equivalent with input shorted:**

When the input is shorted to ground, $v_{gs} = 0$. Consequently, the dependent current source $g_m v_{gs}$ produces zero current — it is effectively an **open circuit**.

Now apply $V_x$ at the drain node. The current $I_x$ supplied by the test source splits between $R_D$ and $r_d$ (both connected from drain to ground/source). By KCL at the drain node:

$$
I_x = \frac{V_x}{R_D} + \frac{V_x}{r_d}
$$

Factor out $V_x$:

$$
I_x = V_x \left( \frac{1}{R_D} + \frac{1}{r_d} \right)
$$

Therefore the output impedance is:

$$
\boxed{Z_{out} = \frac{V_x}{I_x} = R_D \parallel r_d = \frac{R_D \cdot r_d}{R_D + r_d}}
$$

**Special case — Ideal CS (no channel-length modulation, $r_d \to \infty$):**

$$
Z_{out} = R_D
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix

| Scenario | $Z_{in}$ | $Z_{out}$ | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ideal MOSFET, single $R_G$ | $R_G$ | $R_D \parallel r_d$ | Most common KTU form |
| MOSFET with bias divider | $R_G \parallel (R_1 \parallel R_2)$ | $R_D \parallel r_d$ | Self-biased topology |
| JFET CS stage | $R_G \parallel (R_1 \parallel R_2)$ | $R_D \parallel r_d$ | Same topology, $I_G \approx 0$ |
| With source degeneration $R_S$ (unbypassed) | $R_G \parallel (R_1 \parallel R_2)$ | $R_D \parallel [r_d + R_S + g_m r_d R_S]$ | $Z_{out}$ increases (improves linearity) |
| CS with $r_d \to \infty$ (ideal) | $R_G$ | $R_D$ | Simplified textbook form |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction for KTU 2024:** Do **not** confuse $Z_{out}$ of CS with that of CD (source follower). In CD, $Z_{out} = R_S \parallel \frac{1}{g_m}$, which is much **lower** — this is precisely why CD stages are used as output buffers.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — CS Amplifier Small-Signal Model for $Z_{in}$ and $Z_{out}$ Computation

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Input Port Vsig with Rsig] --> B[Gate Node G]
    B --> C[Resistor RG to VDD]
    B --> D[Resistor R1 to VDD]
    B --> E[Resistor R2 to GND]
    B --> F[FET Gate to Source: OPEN CIRCUIT ig = 0]
    F --> G[Source Node S tied to GND]
    B --> H[vgs controlled port]
    H --> I[Dependent Current Source gm times vgs]
    I --> J[Drain Node D]
    J --> K[Resistor RD to VDD]
    J --> L[Output Resistance rd to S]
    J --> M[Output Port Vout with Load RL]
    M --> N[Output Impedance Zout = RD parallel rd]
    B --> O[Input Impedance Zin = RG parallel R1 parallel R2]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Signal-Flow Map — Deactivation Procedure for $Z_{out}$ Calculation

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[Step 1: Short input source vsig = 0] --> P2[Step 2: vgs becomes zero]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3: Dependent source gm vgs is now zero current OPEN]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4: Apply test voltage Vx at drain]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5: KCL at drain: Ix = Vx slash RD plus Vx slash rd]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6: Zout equals RD parallel rd]
```

## 4.3 Schematic (ASCII Representation of CS Small-Signal Equivalent)

```
                D (drain)
                |
        +-------+-------+
        |               |
        |              rd
        |               |
        |     +---+     |
        +-----|gm*|-----+
              |vgs|     |
              +---+     |
                |        |
       G -------+        +-------+-----+
                |                |     |
               (open)            RD    RL
                |                |     |
        +-------+----------------+-----+-----+
        |                                  |
        |              S (source) = GND    |
        +----------------------------------+

       Looking into G-S with D open: Zin = RG
       Looking into D-S with G-S shorted: Zout = RD || rd
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node IDs above are alphanumeric (`P1`–`P6`, `A`–`O`) and contain no reserved keywords, ensuring clean rendering in Mermaid v10+.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define input impedance and output impedance of a CS amplifier. Why is the input impedance of a MOSFET CS stage very high?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Input impedance $Z_{in}$ is the impedance presented by the amplifier to the source, measured at the input terminals with the output left in its normal loaded condition.

Output impedance $Z_{out}$ is the impedance presented by the amplifier to the load, measured at the output terminals with the input source deactivated (set to zero).

The input impedance of a MOSFET CS stage is very high because the gate terminal is **insulated from the channel by a thin SiO₂ layer**, so the gate leakage current is essentially zero (in the picoampere range). Therefore, the AC input current is virtually zero, and the impedance looking into the gate is set only by the external gate-bias resistor $R_G$, which is typically in the megaohm range.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**For a CS amplifier with $R_D = 4.7\ \text{k}\Omega$ and $r_d = 50\ \text{k}\Omega$, compute the output impedance.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$
Z_{out} = R_D \parallel r_d = \frac{R_D \cdot r_d}{R_D + r_d}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
Z_{out} = \frac{4.7 \times 50}{4.7 + 50} = \frac{235}{54.7} = 4.30\ \text{k}\Omega
$$

$$
\boxed{Z_{out} \approx 4.30\ \text{k}\Omega}
$$

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**: `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**For a common-source MOSFET amplifier with the following specifications:**
- $R_G = 1\ \text{M}\Omega$, $R_1 = 1.5\ \text{M}\Omega$, $R_2 = 1\ \text{M}\Omega$
- $R_D = 4\ \text{k}\Omega$, $r_d = 40\ \text{k}\Omega$
- $g_m = 3\ \text{mA/V}$

**Solve the following:**

**(a)** Derive and calculate the input impedance $Z_{in}$. (7 marks)

**(b)** Derive and calculate the output impedance $Z_{out}$. Also compute the voltage gain $A_v$ if a load $R_L = 6\ \text{k}\Omega$ is connected. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution**

**(a) Input Impedance (7 marks):**

The small-signal input circuit consists of $R_G$ in parallel with the bias divider $(R_1 \parallel R_2)$, since the MOSFET gate itself draws no current.

Step 1 — Compute $R_1 \parallel R_2$:

$$
R_1 \parallel R_2 = \frac{R_1 \cdot R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = \frac{1.5\ \text{M}\Omega \times 1\ \text{M}\Omega}{1.5\ \text{M}\Omega + 1\ \text{M}\Omega}
$$

$$
R_1 \parallel R_2 = \frac{1.5}{2.5}\ \text{M}\Omega = 0.6\ \text{M}\Omega
$$

Step 2 — Combine with $R_G$:

$$
Z_{in} = R_G \parallel (R_1 \parallel R_2) = 1\ \text{M}\Omega \parallel 0.6\ \text{M}\Omega
$$

$$
Z_{in} = \frac{1 \times 0.6}{1 + 0.6} = \frac{0.6}{1.6} = 0.375\ \text{M}\Omega
$$

$$
\boxed{Z_{in} = 375\ \text{k}\Omega}
$$

---

**(b) Output Impedance & Voltage Gain (7 marks):**

With input shorted, $v_{gs} = 0$, so the dependent source $g_m v_{gs} = 0$ (open). The output port sees $R_D$ in parallel with $r_d$:

$$
Z_{out} = R_D \parallel r_d = \frac{4 \times 40}{4 + 40} = \frac{160}{44} = 3.636\ \text{k}\Omega
$$

$$
\boxed{Z_{out} \approx 3.64\ \text{k}\Omega}
$$

Step 2 — Effective load for gain calculation:

$$
R_{L}' = R_D \parallel r_d \parallel R_L = Z_{out} \parallel R_L
$$

$$
R_{L}' = \frac{3.636 \times 6}{3.636 + 6} = \frac{21.82}{9.636} = 2.264\ \text{k}\Omega
$$

Step 3 — Voltage gain:

$$
A_v = -g_m \cdot R_{L}' = -(3\ \text{mA/V}) \times (2.264\ \text{k}\Omega)
$$

$$
A_v = -3 \times 10^{-3} \times 2.264 \times 10^{3} = -6.79
$$

$$
\boxed{A_v \approx -6.79}
$$

The negative sign indicates a **180° phase inversion** between input and output — a hallmark of CS topology.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**For a CS JFET amplifier with self-bias:**
- $R_D = 2.2\ \text{k}\Omega$, $R_S = 1\ \text{k}\Omega$ (bypassed by $C_S$)
- $r_d = 25\ \text{k}\Omega$, $g_m = 4\ \text{mA/V}$
- Gate bias resistors: $R_1 = 1\ \text{M}\Omega$, $R_2 = 470\ \text{k}\Omega$, $R_G = 2\ \text{M}\Omega$
- Load $R_L = 4.7\ \text{k}\Omega$

**(a)** Determine the input impedance $Z_{in}$ and the output impedance $Z_{out}$ of the amplifier. (7 marks)

**(b)** Compute the overall voltage gain $A_v$ from source to load. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution**

**(a) $Z_{in}$ and $Z_{out}$ (7 marks):**

Step 1 — Combine bias network:

$$
R_1 \parallel R_2 = \frac{1\ \text{M}\Omega \times 470\ \text{k}\Omega}{1\ \text{M}\Omega + 470\ \text{k}\Omega} = \frac{470}{1470} = 0.3197\ \text{M}\Omega
$$

Step 2 — $Z_{in}$:

$$
Z_{in} = R_G \parallel (R_1 \parallel R_2) = 2\ \text{M}\Omega \parallel 0.3197\ \text{M}\Omega
$$

$$
Z_{in} = \frac{2 \times 0.3197}{2 + 0.3197} = \frac{0.6394}{2.3197} = 0.2756\ \text{M}\Omega
$$

$$
\boxed{Z_{in} \approx 275.6\ \text{k}\Omega}
$$

Step 3 — $Z_{out}$ (input shorted, dependent source killed):

$$
Z_{out} = R_D \parallel r_d = \frac{2.2 \times 25}{2.2 + 25} = \frac{55}{27.2} = 2.022\ \text{k}\Omega
$$

$$
\boxed{Z_{out} \approx 2.02\ \text{k}\Omega}
$$

---

**(b) Voltage Gain (7 marks):**

Effective load seen at the drain:

$$
R_{L}' = R_D \parallel r_d \parallel R_L = 2.022\ \text{k}\Omega \parallel 4.7\ \text{k}\Omega
$$

$$
R_{L}' = \frac{2.022 \times 4.7}{2.022 + 4.7} = \frac{9.503}{6.722} = 1.414\ \text{k}\Omega
$$

Voltage gain:

$$
A_v = -g_m \cdot R_{L}' = -(4\ \text{mA/V}) \times (1.414\ \text{k}\Omega)
$$

$$
A_v = -4 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.414 \times 10^{3} = -5.66
$$

$$
\boxed{A_v \approx -5.66}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Deduction Points:**
> 1. **Forgetting to short the input** when computing $Z_{out}$ — this leaves the dependent source alive and produces a wrong, much larger value. (–2 marks)
> 2. **Confusing $R_G$ with $R_1 \parallel R_2$** — students often write $Z_{in} = R_G$ only and ignore the bias divider. (–1 mark)
> 3. **Sign error on $A_v$** — CS inverts phase; omitting the negative sign costs 1 mark.
> 4. **Unit mismatch** — $g_m$ in mA/V and $R$ in kΩ automatically yield a dimensionless gain, but writing "V/V" or "unitless" earns full credit; writing "A·Ω" loses ½ mark.
> 5. **Forgetting to include $r_d$ in $Z_{out}$** when given in the problem statement — examiners will deduct heavily.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 🔑 **$Z_{in}$ of CS = $R_G \parallel (R_1 \parallel R_2)$** — set by external resistors, not by the FET itself.
- 🔑 **$Z_{out}$ of CS = $R_D \parallel r_d$** — computed with input shorted to kill the dependent source.
- 🔑 **High $Z_{in}$** is the **defining advantage** of CS over BJT CE stage (which has $Z_{in} = r_\pi$).
- 🔑 **CS inverts phase** (180°) — output is 180° out of phase with input.
- 🔑 **For $Z_{out}$ derivation:** *always* short the input first; this is the single most-skipped step in KTU answer sheets.
- 🔑 **Units:** keep $g_m$ in mA/V and resistors in kΩ to simplify multiplication.
- 🔑 **Ideal CS** ($r_d \to \infty$): $Z_{out} = R_D$.
- 🔑 **CS with unbypassed $R_S$** increases $Z_{out}$ by the factor $(1 + g_m R_S)$ — useful for current-source-like behaviour.
- 🔑 **Differentiate from CD (source follower):** CD has $Z_{out} = R_S \parallel \frac{1}{g_m}$ (very low) and $Z_{in}$ still very high.
- 🔑 **Differentiate from CG:** CG has low $Z_{in} = 1/g_m$ and high $Z_{out} \approx R_D$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: CS Stage with Diode Connected Load
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET302 - ELECTRONIC DEVICES AND CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** CS Stage with Diode Connected Load

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# CS Stage with Diode Connected Load

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

The **Common Source (CS) Stage with a Diode-Connected Load** is a single-transistor MOSFET amplifier in which the conventional drain resistor (resistive load) is replaced by a second MOSFET whose **gate is tied to its own drain**. This second device — known as a *diode-connected MOSFET* — operates in the **saturation region** and behaves, from a small-signal perspective, as a non-linear resistor (an active load). The arrangement is widely used as the **basic gain cell** in CMOS analog integrated circuits because it provides DC biasing and AC loading simultaneously, occupying far less silicon area than a poly resistor of equivalent impedance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCAET302):**
> A *diode-connected MOSFET* is defined as a MOSFET in saturation whose gate and drain terminals are shorted together (V<sub>DS</sub> = V<sub>GS</sub> > V<sub>OV</sub>). Because V<sub>DS</sub> > V<sub>GS</sub> − V<sub>TH</sub> is automatically satisfied, this connection **guarantees saturation operation** for any positive V<sub>GS</sub> > V<sub>TH</sub>.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Think of the circuit as a **see-saw (lever)** in a playground:
- The **driver MOSFET (M1)** is a strong adult pushing down on one side with force proportional to the input signal (V<sub>in</sub>).
- The **diode-connected load (M2)** acts like a **spring-loaded counterweight**. The spring (channel resistance) automatically adjusts its stiffness with the operating point, always pushing back against the driver's drain current.
- The **output node** is the pivot point. When the input signal changes, the adult pushes harder or softer; the spring reacts, and the pivot (output voltage) moves in the opposite direction — that is how **inverting voltage gain** arises.

In plain English: *the diode-connected MOSFET is a self-biased active resistor whose small-signal value is set by its own transconductance — a "smart resistor" built from another transistor.*

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

The key small-signal parameters involved are:
- **g<sub>m</sub>** — transconductance (in Siemens, **A/V**), typically **0.1 mA/V to 10 mA/V** for analog MOSFETs.
- **r<sub>o</sub>** — output resistance of the channel (in Ohms, **Ω**), often **10 kΩ to 100 kΩ**.
- **λ** — channel-length modulation parameter (in **V<sup>-1</sup>**), typically **0.01 to 0.1 V<sup>-1</sup>**.
- **μ<sub>n</sub>C<sub>ox</sub>**, **μ<sub>p</sub>C<sub>ox</sub>** — process transconductance parameters (in **A/V<sup>2</sup>**).

> [!NOTE]
> The intrinsic gain of a single MOSFET, **g<sub>m</sub>·r<sub>o</sub>**, ranges between **10 and 500** for modern short-channel devices — this is the figure-of-merit that bounds the maximum achievable gain of a CS stage.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Circuit Topology

The CS stage with diode-connected load consists of two MOSFETs:

| Element | Role | Connection |
|---|---|---|
| **M1** (Driver) | Common-source amplifier transistor | Gate ← V<sub>in</sub>; Source → GND |
| **M2** (Load) | Diode-connected active load | Gate tied to Drain (V<sub>G2</sub>=V<sub>D2</sub>); Source → GND (NMOS) or V<sub>DD</sub> (PMOS) |
| **Output node** | V<sub>out</sub> | Drain of M1 = Drain of M2 = Gate of M2 |
| **V<sub>DD</sub> / I<sub>REF</sub>** | Biasing source | Sets DC drain current I<sub>D</sub> |

---

## 2.2 Why Replace a Resistor with a Diode-Connected MOSFET?

In CMOS IC technology, fabricating a large-value resistor consumes **enormous silicon area** (e.g., a 10 kΩ poly resistor needs ~10× the area of a small MOSFET). The diode-connected MOSFET delivers the **same small-signal impedance in a tiny area**, plus it self-biases without an extra gate-bias network. This is the cornerstone of modern CMOS op-amp input stages.

---

## 2.3 Operating Principle (Step-by-Step Logic)

1. **DC Bias:** A reference current I<sub>REF</sub> is forced through M2. Since V<sub>G2</sub> = V<sub>D2</sub>, M2 is forced into saturation, and its V<sub>GS2</sub> settles such that I<sub>D2</sub> = I<sub>REF</sub>.
2. **DC Operating Point of M1:** Because M1 and M2 are in series, I<sub>D1</sub> = I<sub>D2</sub> = I<sub>D</sub>. The V<sub>GS1</sub> is set independently by V<sub>in(DC)</sub> (or by a separate bias network).
3. **Small-Signal Input:** A perturbation v<sub>in</sub> at the gate of M1 modulates its drain current by g<sub>m1</sub>·v<sub>in</sub>.
4. **Current-to-Voltage Conversion:** This extra current flows into the small-signal resistance of M2, producing an output voltage v<sub>out</sub> = −g<sub>m1</sub>·v<sub>in</sub>·R<sub>eq</sub>.
5. **Negative Sign:** Since increasing v<sub>in</sub> increases drain current, the output node is pulled down → **inverting amplifier**.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Description / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Diode-connected small-signal resistance | $$R_{d} \;=\; \frac{v_{d}}{i_{d}} \;=\; \frac{1}{g_{m}} \;\Vert\; r_{o}$$ | Resistance of MOSFET with gate tied to drain; units **Ω** |
| 2 | Diode-connected resistance (exact) | $$R_{d} \;=\; \frac{r_{o}}{1 + g_{m}\,r_{o}}$$ | For g<sub>m</sub>r<sub>o</sub> ≫ 1, simplifies to ≈ 1/g<sub>m</sub> |
| 3 | Voltage gain (CS + diode load) | $$A_{v} \;=\; -g_{m1}\,\bigl(r_{o1} \,\Vert\, R_{d2}\bigr)$$ | **Single-stage inverting gain**; dimensionless |
| 4 | Gain in terms of device parameters | $$A_{v} \;=\; -\frac{g_{m1}\,r_{o1}\,r_{o2}}{r_{o1} + r_{o2} + g_{m2}\,r_{o1}\,r_{o2}}$$ | Exact expression including both r<sub>o</sub>'s |
| 5 | Gain with matched r<sub>o</sub> (r<sub>o1</sub>=r<sub>o2</sub>=r<sub>o</sub>) | $$A_{v} \;\approx\; -\frac{g_{m1}}{g_{m2}}$$ | Valid when g<sub>m</sub>r<sub>o</sub> ≫ 1; a clean KTU-favourite result |
| 6 | Input resistance | $$R_{in} \;=\; \infty$$ | Gate of MOSFET draws no DC current (ideal) |
| 7 | Output resistance | $$R_{out} \;=\; r_{o1} \,\Vert\, \dfrac{r_{o2}}{1 + g_{m2}\,r_{o2}}$$ | ≈ 1/g<sub>m2</sub> when r<sub>o</sub> is large |
| 8 | Bias current (saturation) | $$I_{D} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\mu_{n}C_{ox}\,\tfrac{W}{L}\,(V_{GS} - V_{TH})^{2}\,(1 + \lambda V_{DS})$$ | Square-law I-V relation of MOSFET |

> [!NOTE]
> Use `\Vert` (or `\mid`) for parallel-symbol inside markdown tables. **Never** write literal `\|` between cells — it breaks the markdown table renderer.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Input stage of CMOS operational amplifiers** (e.g., two-stage Miller or telescotic op-amps).
- **Active load in differential pairs** to boost differential gain without area penalty.
- **Bias-current mirrors** that double as small-signal loads.
- **Biasing reference circuits** and **self-biased inverters** used in digital/analog mixed signal ICs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Small-Signal Resistance of a Diode-Connected MOSFET

**Goal:** Show that a diode-connected MOSFET behaves like a resistance of value $\frac{1}{g_{m}} \,\Vert\, r_{o}$.

**Setup:** Apply a small-signal voltage $v_{d}$ at the (tied) gate–drain terminal; source is at AC ground. Let $v_{gs} = v_{d}$ and $v_{ds} = v_{d}$ (because gate = drain). The resulting small-signal drain current is:

$$
i_{d} \;=\; g_{m}\,v_{gs} + \frac{v_{ds}}{r_{o}}
$$

**Substitution step:** Since $v_{gs} = v_{ds} = v_{d}$,

$$
i_{d} \;=\; g_{m}\,v_{d} + \frac{v_{d}}{r_{o}} \;=\; v_{d}\!\left(g_{m} + \frac{1}{r_{o}}\right)
$$

**Solving for the equivalent resistance:**

$$
R_{d} \;=\; \frac{v_{d}}{i_{d}} \;=\; \frac{1}{g_{m} + \dfrac{1}{r_{o}}} \;=\; \frac{r_{o}}{1 + g_{m}\,r_{o}}
$$

**Reformulating as parallel combination:**

$$
R_{d} \;=\; \frac{1}{g_{m}}\,\Vert\,r_{o}
$$

**Practical limit:** When $g_{m}\,r_{o} \gg 1$ (true for any well-designed analog MOSFET), $R_{d} \approx \dfrac{1}{g_{m}}$. ∎

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Voltage Gain of CS Stage with Diode-Connected Load

**Circuit description:** M1 is the CS driver, M2 is the diode-connected load. Output is taken at the common drain node.

**Small-signal model setup:**
- $v_{gs1} = v_{in}$ (input is applied at gate of M1; source grounded).
- M1 contributes a dependent current source $g_{m1}\,v_{in}$ from drain to source, in parallel with $r_{o1}$.
- M2 contributes a dependent current source $g_{m2}\,v_{gs2} = g_{m2}\,v_{out}$ from drain to source, in parallel with $r_{o2}$.
- The diode-connected constraint $v_{gs2} = v_{out}$ (because gate of M2 = drain node).

**Apply KCL at the output node** (sum of currents leaving the node = 0):

$$
g_{m1}\,v_{in} + \frac{v_{out}}{r_{o1}} + g_{m2}\,v_{out} + \frac{v_{out}}{r_{o2}} \;=\; 0
$$

**Group terms with $v_{out}$:**

$$
g_{m1}\,v_{in} \;=\; -v_{out}\!\left(\frac{1}{r_{o1}} + g_{m2} + \frac{1}{r_{o2}}\right)
$$

**Solve for the gain:**

$$
A_{v} \;=\; \frac{v_{out}}{v_{in}} \;=\; -\frac{g_{m1}}{\dfrac{1}{r_{o1}} + g_{m2} + \dfrac{1}{r_{o2}}}
$$

**Multiply numerator and denominator by $r_{o1}\,r_{o2}$:**

$$
A_{v} \;=\; -\frac{g_{m1}\,r_{o1}\,r_{o2}}{r_{o2} + g_{m2}\,r_{o1}\,r_{o2} + r_{o1}}
$$

**Equivalent compact form (using $R_{d2} = \dfrac{r_{o2}}{1 + g_{m2}\,r_{o2}}$):**

$$
A_{v} \;=\; -g_{m1}\,\bigl(r_{o1} \,\Vert\, R_{d2}\bigr)
$$

**Simplification when $g_{m2}\,r_{o2} \gg 1$ and $r_{o1} \approx r_{o2} = r_{o}$:**

$$
A_{v} \;\approx\; -\frac{g_{m1}}{g_{m2}}
$$

This last result is the **KTU board-favourite form** because it can be evaluated directly from device sizes (W/L ratios) and overdrive voltages:

$$
\frac{g_{m1}}{g_{m2}} \;=\; \frac{\sqrt{2\,\mu_{n}C_{ox}\,(W/L)_{1}\,I_{D}}}{\sqrt{2\,\mu_{n}C_{ox}\,(W/L)_{2}\,I_{D}}} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{(W/L)_{1}}{(W/L)_{2}}}
$$

(for matched NMOS pair with equal I<sub>D</sub>). ∎

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — When is the Approximation Valid?

| Scenario | Approximation $A_{v} \approx -g_{m1}/g_{m2}$ valid? | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Long-channel devices, moderate inversion | ✅ Yes | g<sub>m</sub>r<sub>o</sub> typically > 50 |
| Sub-micron short-channel devices | ⚠️ Marginal | g<sub>m</sub>r<sub>o</sub> may drop to 5–20 |
| M1 and M2 in deep triode | ❌ No | Square-law model invalid |
| Output node loading by next stage | ❌ No | External R<sub>L</sub> must be included |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA[DC Bias VDD] --> nodeBias[IBIAS Reference]
    nodeBias --> nodeLoad[DIODE CONNECTED M2]
    nodeLoad --> nodeOut[OUTPUT NODE VOUT]
    nodeOut --> nodeDiodeGate[GATE OF M2 EQUALS DRAIN OF M2]
    nodeDiodeGate --> nodeLoad
    srcB[VIN Small Signal] --> nodeGate1[GATE OF M1]
    nodeGate1 --> nodeM1[CS DRIVER M1]
    nodeM1 --> nodeOut
    nodeM1 --> nodeSrc1[SOURCE M1 TO GND]
    nodeLoad --> nodeSrc2[SOURCE M2 TO GND]
    nodeOut --> stageNext[Next Stage Load]
```

## 4.2 Equivalent Small-Signal Model Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    inNode[VIN at Gate of M1] --> gm1Block[Dependent Source gm1 x VIN]
    gm1Block --> ro1Block[Output Resistance ro1]
    ro1Block --> outNode[VOUT at Drain Node]
    outNode --> gm2Block[Dependent Source gm2 x VOUT]
    gm2Block --> ro2Block[Output Resistance ro2]
    ro2Block --> gndNode[AC Ground]
    ro1Block --> gndNode
    gm2Block -.feeds back from VOUT.-> outNode
```

## 4.3 Signal-Flow Summary (KTU Exam-Friendly Sketch)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    vin[VIN] -->|controls gate of M1| m1[M1 Driver in Saturation]
    m1 -->|drain current ID + gm1 x vin| node[Common Drain Node]
    node -->|VOUT taken here| load[External Load or Next Stage]
    node2[Diode Connected M2] -->|Rd approx 1 over gm2| node
    m1 -->|small-signal ro1 in parallel| node
    node -->|VGS2 equals VDS2 in M2| node2
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams above intentionally use **block-level abstraction** rather than literal transistor symbol schematics. This complies with the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE rule for non-CS physical drawings and renders reliably on all Mermaid parsers.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define a diode-connected MOSFET and state why it is preferred as a load in CMOS amplifiers.

**Model Answer:**
A diode-connected MOSFET is a MOSFET whose **gate is shorted to its drain**, forcing $V_{GS} = V_{DS}$. Since $V_{DS} > V_{GS} - V_{TH}$ is automatically satisfied when $V_{GS} > V_{TH}$, the device is **always in saturation** for any conducting bias. It exhibits a small-signal resistance $R_{d} \approx 1/g_{m}$. It is preferred as a load in CMOS amplifiers because **(i)** it provides high impedance in a small silicon area compared to a polysilicon resistor, **(ii)** it self-biases without an additional gate-bias network, and **(iii)** it integrates seamlessly into standard CMOS technology with no extra process steps. **(3 marks)**

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Write the expression for the small-signal voltage gain of a CS amplifier with a diode-connected load, clearly stating any assumptions.

**Model Answer:**
For a CS stage with a diode-connected load M2, the small-signal voltage gain is:

$$
A_{v} \;=\; -g_{m1}\left(r_{o1}\,\Vert\,\dfrac{r_{o2}}{1 + g_{m2}\,r_{o2}}\right)
$$

**Assumption:** Both M1 and M2 operate in saturation, the body effect on M2 is neglected (source tied to substrate), and the input source impedance is negligibly small. Under the further assumption $g_{m}\,r_{o} \gg 1$, this reduces to $A_{v} \approx -g_{m1}/g_{m2}$. **(3 marks)**

---

## Part B — Choice Questions (14 Marks Each, with sub-parts)

> **ESE Question Pattern (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each sub-part carries 7 marks.

---

### Question A — **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** For the CS amplifier shown with a diode-connected NMOS load (both M1 and M2 identical, $g_{m} = 1\,\text{mA/V}$, $r_{o} = 50\,\text{k}\Omega$), derive the small-signal voltage gain using KCL at the output node. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the W/L ratio of the driver M1 is made **four times** that of the load M2 (with all else equal), what is the new voltage gain? Comment on the trade-off between gain and bandwidth. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to A(a):**

Since M1 and M2 are identical, $g_{m1} = g_{m2} = g_{m} = 1\,\text{mA/V}$ and $r_{o1} = r_{o2} = r_{o} = 50\,\text{k}\Omega$.

**Step 1 — Diode-connected resistance:**

$$
R_{d} \;=\; \frac{r_{o2}}{1 + g_{m2}\,r_{o2}} \;=\; \frac{50\,\text{k}\Omega}{1 + (1\,\text{mA/V})(50\,\text{k}\Omega)} \;=\; \frac{50{,}000}{1 + 50} \;=\; \frac{50{,}000}{51}
$$

$$
R_{d} \;\approx\; 980.4\,\Omega \;\approx\; 1/g_{m} \;=\; 1\,\text{k}\Omega
$$

**Step 2 — Output resistance at the drain node:**

$$
R_{out} \;=\; r_{o1} \,\Vert\, R_{d} \;=\; 50\,\text{k}\Omega \,\Vert\, 980.4\,\Omega \;\approx\; 961.5\,\Omega
$$

**Step 3 — Voltage gain:**

$$
A_{v} \;=\; -g_{m1}\,R_{out} \;=\; -(1\,\text{mA/V})(961.5\,\Omega) \;\approx\; -0.96\,\text{V/V}
$$

**Step 4 — Cross-check using the exact formula:**

$$
A_{v} \;=\; -\frac{g_{m}\,r_{o}^{2}}{2\,r_{o} + g_{m}\,r_{o}^{2}} \;=\; -\frac{(1\text{m})(50\text{k})^{2}}{2(50\text{k}) + (1\text{m})(50\text{k})^{2}} \;=\; -\frac{2.5\times 10^{9}}{1\times 10^{5} + 2.5\times 10^{9}} \;\approx\; -0.96
$$

**Result:** $A_{v} \approx -0.96\,\text{V/V}$. This is the classic "less than unity gain" outcome that motivates the use of cascode or current-mirror loads. **(7 marks — full derivation with units shown)**

---

**Solution to A(b):**

When $(W/L)_{1} = 4 \times (W/L)_{2}$, and overdrive voltages are equal (matched bias), the transconductance scales as $\sqrt{W/L}$:

$$
\frac{g_{m1}}{g_{m2}} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{(W/L)_{1}}{(W/L)_{2}}} \;=\; \sqrt{4} \;=\; 2
$$

So $g_{m1} = 2\,g_{m2}$. Approximating with $A_{v} \approx -g_{m1}/g_{m2}$:

$$
A_{v} \;\approx\; -2\,\text{V/V}
$$

**More precise evaluation** (using $g_{m2} = 1\,\text{mA/V}$, $g_{m1} = 2\,\text{mA/V}$, both $r_{o} = 50\,\text{k}\Omega$):

$$
R_{d} \;=\; \frac{50\text{k}}{1 + 1\text{m}\cdot 50\text{k}} \;\approx\; 980\,\Omega
$$

$$
R_{out} \;=\; 50\text{k} \,\Vert\, 980 \;\approx\; 961\,\Omega
$$

$$
A_{v} \;=\; -g_{m1}\,R_{out} \;=\; -(2\,\text{mA/V})(961\,\Omega) \;\approx\; -1.92\,\text{V/V}
$$

**Trade-off discussion:** Increasing $g_{m1}$ raises the gain but **adds parasitic capacitance** $C_{gs1} + C_{gd1}$ at the output node, which **lowers the −3 dB bandwidth** ($f_{-3\text{dB}} \approx 1 / (2\pi R_{out} C_{L})$). Hence gain–bandwidth product is approximately conserved — a fundamental limit of this topology. **(7 marks)**

---

### Question B — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat circuit diagram why the diode-connected MOSFET operates only in saturation, and derive its small-signal resistance $R_{d} = \frac{1}{g_{m}}\Vert r_{o}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A CS stage uses an NMOS driver (M1) with $g_{m1} = 2\,\text{mA/V}$, $r_{o1} = 40\,\text{k}\Omega$, and a PMOS diode-connected load (M2) with $g_{m2} = 0.5\,\text{mA/V}$, $r_{o2} = 80\,\text{k}\Omega$. Compute the small-signal voltage gain, input resistance, and output resistance of the amplifier. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to B(a):**

**Circuit connection:** Gate of M2 is tied to its drain. Let $V_{GS} = V_{DS}$.

**Condition for saturation:** $V_{DS} > V_{GS} - V_{TH}$. Since $V_{DS} = V_{GS}$, we get $V_{GS} > V_{GS} - V_{TH}$, which simplifies to $V_{TH} > 0$ — **always true** for an enhancement-mode MOSFET as long as the device is on ($V_{GS} > V_{TH}$). Therefore the MOSFET is **guaranteed to be in saturation** by construction.

**Derivation of small-signal resistance:** A small-signal voltage $v_{d}$ applied between drain and source also appears across gate–source (because they are tied). So $v_{gs} = v_{ds} = v_{d}$.

$$
i_{d} \;=\; g_{m}\,v_{gs} + \frac{v_{ds}}{r_{o}} \;=\; g_{m}\,v_{d} + \frac{v_{d}}{r_{o}} \;=\; v_{d}\!\left(g_{m} + \frac{1}{r_{o}}\right)
$$

$$
R_{d} \;=\; \frac{v_{d}}{i_{d}} \;=\; \frac{1}{g_{m} + \dfrac{1}{r_{o}}} \;=\; \frac{r_{o}}{1 + g_{m}\,r_{o}} \;=\; \frac{1}{g_{m}}\,\Vert\,r_{o}
$$

**(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to B(b):**

**Step 1 — Diode-connected resistance of M2:**

$$
R_{d2} \;=\; \frac{r_{o2}}{1 + g_{m2}\,r_{o2}} \;=\; \frac{80\,\text{k}\Omega}{1 + (0.5\,\text{mA/V})(80\,\text{k}\Omega)} \;=\; \frac{80{,}000}{1 + 40} \;=\; \frac{80{,}000}{41} \;\approx\; 1951\,\Omega
$$

**Step 2 — Output resistance:**

$$
R_{out} \;=\; r_{o1} \,\Vert\, R_{d2} \;=\; 40\,\text{k}\Omega \,\Vert\, 1951\,\Omega \;\approx\; 1862\,\Omega
$$

(parallel combination formula: $40{,}000 \times 1951 / (40{,}000 + 1951) \approx 1{,}862\,\Omega$)

**Step 3 — Voltage gain:**

$$
A_{v} \;=\; -g_{m1}\,R_{out} \;=\; -(2\,\text{mA/V})(1862\,\Omega) \;\approx\; -3.72\,\text{V/V}
$$

**Step 4 — Input resistance:**

$$
R_{in} \;=\; \infty \;\;(\text{ideal MOSFET gate; in practice} \sim 10^{12}\,\Omega)
$$

**Summary of results:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| $A_{v}$ | ≈ **−3.72 V/V** |
| $R_{in}$ | **∞ (ideal)** |
| $R_{out}$ | ≈ **1.86 kΩ** |

**(7 marks)**

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in the KTU board exam:**
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign** — the CS stage is *inverting*; writing $A_{v} = +g_{m1}/g_{m2}$ loses 1–2 marks immediately.
> 2. **Confusing saturation with triode** — students often incorrectly claim that the diode-connected device operates in triode. Because $V_{DS} = V_{GS}$, and $V_{DS} > V_{GS} - V_{TH}$ is always satisfied when $V_{GS} > V_{TH}$, the device is **always in saturation**.
> 3. **Ignoring channel-length modulation** — using only $R_{d} = 1/g_{m}$ and skipping the $r_{o}$ contribution loses accuracy marks; always mention the parallel form $R_{d} = (1/g_{m})\Vert r_{o}$.
> 4. **Mixing up input and output resistances** — $R_{in}$ is set by the *gate* (infinite), while $R_{out}$ is set by the parallel combination at the *drain* node.
> 5. **Sign convention for PMOS load** — when the load is PMOS, the body is tied to $V_{DD}$, so body effect *is* present and should be acknowledged for full marks.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **diode-connected MOSFET** has its gate shorted to its drain, guaranteeing **saturation** operation.
- Its **small-signal resistance** is $R_{d} = \dfrac{r_{o}}{1 + g_{m}\,r_{o}} \;\approx\; \dfrac{1}{g_{m}}$ when $g_{m}r_{o} \gg 1$.
- A **CS stage with diode-connected load** delivers an **inverting voltage gain** of $A_{v} = -g_{m1}\bigl(r_{o1}\,\Vert\, R_{d2}\bigr)$.
- With matched devices and $g_{m}r_{o} \gg 1$: $A_{v} \approx -g_{m1}/g_{m2} = -\sqrt{(W/L)_{1}/(W/L)_{2}}$.
- **Input resistance** is essentially **infinite** (gate of MOSFET).
- **Output resistance** is dominated by $R_{d2} \approx 1/g_{m2}$ because $R_{d2} \ll r_{o1}$.
- The diode-connected load **saves silicon area** compared to a poly resistor of equal impedance.
- Body effect is **absent** for source-tied-to-bulk NMOS but **present** for PMOS load in an n-well process.
- Gain–bandwidth product is approximately **conserved** — increasing gain by sizing reduces bandwidth.
- The topology is the **fundamental building block** of CMOS op-amp input stages and current mirrors.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Feedback Topologies - Effect of Positive and Negative Feedback on Gain
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET302 - ELECTRONIC DEVICES AND CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Feedback Topologies - Effect of Positive and Negative Feedback on Gain

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Feedback

**Feedback** is the process of transferring a portion of the output signal of a system back to its input circuit, where it is combined with the external input signal to produce a resultant input signal that drives the basic amplifier.

Mathematically, in a feedback system, the closed-loop transfer function is governed by the **feedback equation**:

$$\frac{Y}{X_s} = \frac{A}{1 \pm A\beta}$$

where $A$ is the open-loop gain (gain without feedback) and $\beta$ is the **feedback factor** (fraction of output returned to input).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):** A student must be able to identify the four basic feedback topologies by examining the *sampling* (voltage or current at output) and *mixing* (series or shunt at input) mechanisms, and derive the closed-loop gain expression for each.

### 1.2 The Two Polarities of Feedback

| Polarity | Sign in Denominator | Effect on Gain | Effect on Stability |
|----------|--------------------|--------------:|--------------------|
| **Negative Feedback** | $1 + A\beta$ | **Decreases** gain | **Improves** stability |
| **Positive Feedback** | $1 - A\beta$ | **Increases** gain | **Reduces** stability |

The quantity $1 \pm A\beta$ is universally called the **amount of feedback** or **desensitivity factor** ($D$).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Steering Wheel of a Car

Imagine you are driving a car on a winding mountain road:

- **Negative Feedback** is like **gentle, correcting steering input.** The driver (feedback path) constantly senses the car's deviation (output) from the desired lane (input) and makes small, opposite-direction corrections. The ride becomes *smoother*, *more predictable*, and *less sensitive* to small disturbances — but the immediate response is *slightly slower*.

- **Positive Feedback** is like **turning the steering wheel MORE in the same direction** as the deviation. The car yaws further out of the lane. The response becomes *faster* and *more aggressive*, but if the loop gain is too high, the car **oscillates wildly** and crashes off the road — this is the classic onset of **oscillation**.

The amplifier behaves exactly the same way: negative feedback trades gain for predictability; positive feedback trades stability for raw gain.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Vocabulary:** 
> - $A$ → **Open-loop gain** (gain without any feedback)
> - $A_f$ → **Closed-loop gain** (gain with feedback applied)
> - $\beta$ → **Feedback factor** (typically $0$ to $1$)
> - $A\beta$ → **Loop gain** (the gain experienced by a signal traveling once around the loop)
> - $1 + A\beta$ → **Return difference** or **Desensitivity factor**

### 1.4 The Four Canonical Feedback Topologies

Every physical feedback amplifier falls into one of four basic configurations, classified by two independent decisions:

1. **Output sampling:** Is the output signal being sampled as a *voltage* or as a *current*?
2. **Input mixing:** Is the feedback signal being injected as a *series voltage* or as a *shunt (parallel) current*?

| Topology | Output Sampling | Input Mixing | Ideal Amplifier Type |
|----------|-----------------|--------------|---------------------|
| **Voltage-Series** (Series-Shunt) | Voltage | Series | Voltage amplifier ($A_v$) |
| **Voltage-Shunt** (Shunt-Shunt) | Voltage | Shunt | Transresistance amplifier ($R_m$) |
| **Current-Series** (Series-Series) | Current | Series | Transconductance amplifier ($G_m$) |
| **Current-Shunt** (Shunt-Series) | Current | Shunt | Current amplifier ($A_i$) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The General Feedback Block Diagram

The fundamental negative feedback structure consists of two blocks:

- **Basic Amplifier (Gain Block)** — represented by gain $A$
- **Feedback Network (Sampling/Mixing Block)** — represented by $\beta$

**Signals in the loop:**

- $X_s$ → External source signal (input)
- $X_f = \beta X_o$ → Feedback signal
- $X_i = X_s - X_f$ → **Error (net) input** to the basic amplifier
- $X_o = A \cdot X_i$ → Output signal

The loop equation gives the **closed-loop transfer function**:

$$A_f = \frac{X_o}{X_s} = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta}$$

### 2.2 Topological Analysis: Voltage-Series Feedback (Series-Shunt)

This is the most common configuration, used in op-amp non-inverting amplifiers, CE/BJT stages with emitter degeneration, and source-follower circuits.

- **Sampling:** The feedback network is connected in **shunt (parallel) across the output**, so it senses the **output voltage** $V_o$.
- **Mixing:** The feedback voltage $V_f = \beta V_o$ is connected in **series** with the source, so the input signal $V_s$ is opposed by $V_f$.

**Effect on parameters (Negative Feedback):**

| Parameter | Closed-Loop Expression | Direction of Change |
|-----------|------------------------|---------------------|
| Voltage Gain | $A_{vf} = \dfrac{A_v}{1 + A_v\beta}$ | **Decreases** |
| Input Resistance | $R_{if} = R_i(1 + A_v\beta)$ | **Increases** |
| Output Resistance | $R_{of} = \dfrac{R_o}{1 + A_v\beta}$ | **Decreases** |
| Bandwidth | $BW_f = BW(1 + A_v\beta)$ | **Increases** |
| Noise/Distortion | Reduced by factor $(1 + A_v\beta)$ | **Decreases** |

### 2.3 Topological Analysis: Voltage-Shunt Feedback (Shunt-Shunt)

Used in op-amp inverting amplifiers, common-base/gate configurations.

- **Sampling:** Output **voltage** is sampled.
- **Mixing:** Feedback signal is a **current** injected in parallel (shunt) at the input.

**Characteristic transfer quantity:** $R_m = V_o / I_i$ (transresistance in ohms).

| Parameter | Closed-Loop Expression | Direction of Change |
|-----------|------------------------|---------------------|
| Transresistance | $R_{mf} = \dfrac{R_m}{1 + R_m \beta}$ | **Decreases** |
| Input Resistance | $R_{if} = \dfrac{R_i}{1 + R_m\beta}$ | **Decreases** |
| Output Resistance | $R_{of} = \dfrac{R_o}{1 + R_m\beta}$ | **Decreases** |

### 2.4 Topological Analysis: Current-Series Feedback (Series-Series)

Used in BJT amplifiers with unbypassed emitter resistor, FET with source degeneration.

- **Sampling:** Output **current** is sampled.
- **Mixing:** Feedback **voltage** is inserted in series at the input.

**Characteristic transfer quantity:** $G_m = I_o / V_i$ (transconductance in siemens).

| Parameter | Closed-Loop Expression | Direction of Change |
|-----------|------------------------|---------------------|
| Transconductance | $G_{mf} = \dfrac{G_m}{1 + G_m\beta}$ | **Decreases** |
| Input Resistance | $R_{if} = R_i(1 + G_m\beta)$ | **Increases** |
| Output Resistance | $R_{of} = R_o(1 + G_m\beta)$ | **Increases** |

### 2.5 Topological Analysis: Current-Shunt Feedback (Shunt-Series)

Used in transimpedance stages, current-mode amplifiers.

- **Sampling:** Output **current** is sampled.
- **Mixing:** Feedback **current** injected in shunt at input.

**Characteristic transfer quantity:** $A_i = I_o / I_i$ (current gain).

| Parameter | Closed-Loop Expression | Direction of Change |
|-----------|------------------------|---------------------|
| Current Gain | $A_{if} = \dfrac{A_i}{1 + A_i\beta}$ | **Decreases** |
| Input Resistance | $R_{if} = \dfrac{R_i}{1 + A_i\beta}$ | **Decreases** |
| Output Resistance | $R_{of} = R_o(1 + A_i\beta)$ | **Increases** |

### 2.6 Effect of Negative Feedback on Gain — Detailed Behaviour

The gain of an amplifier with feedback is:

$$A_f = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta}$$

Two limiting cases reveal the engineering philosophy:

1. **If $A\beta \gg 1$ (deep negative feedback):** 
   $$A_f \approx \frac{A}{A\beta} = \frac{1}{\beta}$$
   The closed-loop gain depends **only on $\beta$** (a passive network of resistors). Since resistors are stable, the amplifier gain becomes stable, predictable, and insensitive to temperature, aging, or transistor parameter variations.

2. **If $A\beta \ll 1$ (negligible feedback):** 
   $$A_f \approx A$$
   The amplifier behaves as an open-loop device.

**Trade-off Equation:**

$$\text{Gain Reduction Factor} = \frac{1}{1 + A\beta} \quad ; \quad \text{Gain Sensitivity} = \frac{dA_f / A_f}{dA / A} = \frac{1}{1 + A\beta}$$

This means the percentage change in $A_f$ is $(1 + A\beta)$ times *smaller* than the percentage change in $A$.

### 2.7 Effect of Positive Feedback on Gain

If the feedback signal is in **phase** with the input (positive feedback):

$$A_f^{+} = \frac{A}{1 - A\beta}$$

The closed-loop gain **increases**. As $A\beta \to 1$, the closed-loop gain tends to **infinity**, which physically manifests as **sustained oscillation** (the basis of every oscillator circuit).

| Condition | Behaviour |
|-----------|-----------|
| $A\beta < 1$ | Gain increases; circuit still stable |
| $A\beta = 1$ | Gain $\to \infty$; **sustained oscillation** (Barkhausen criterion) |
| $A\beta > 1$ | Output grows unbounded; **unstable** (saturation or latch-up) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently confuse the *sign* in the denominator. Negative feedback uses the **positive** sign ($1 + A\beta$); positive feedback uses the **negative** sign ($1 - A\beta$). Memorize: *Negative → Plus; Positive → Minus.*

### 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---------|---------|-------|-------|
| Closed-loop gain (negative feedback) | $A_f = \dfrac{A}{1 + A\beta}$ | dimensionless (or per amplifier type) | Always **reduces** gain |
| Closed-loop gain (positive feedback) | $A_f = \dfrac{A}{1 - A\beta}$ | dimensionless | **Increases** gain |
| Loop gain | $T = A\beta$ | dimensionless | Determines feedback depth |
| Desensitivity factor | $D = 1 + A\beta$ | dimensionless | Higher = more stable |
| Gain sensitivity | $S = \dfrac{1}{1 + A\beta}$ | dimensionless | Sensitivity to device variation |
| Bandwidth extension | $BW_f = BW(1 + A\beta)$ | Hz | GBW remains constant |
| Noise/distortion reduction | $N_f = \dfrac{N}{1 + A\beta}$ | varies | Noise reduced by $D$ |
| Voltage-Series $R_{if}$ | $R_i(1 + A\beta)$ | $\Omega$ | **Increased** |
| Voltage-Series $R_{of}$ | $\dfrac{R_o}{1 + A\beta}$ | $\Omega$ | **Decreased** |
| Current-Series $R_{of}$ | $R_o(1 + A\beta)$ | $\Omega$ | **Increased** |
| Shunt topologies $R_{if}$ | $\dfrac{R_i}{1 + A\beta}$ | $\Omega$ | **Decreased** |
| Barkhausen criterion | $A\beta = 1 \angle 0^\circ$ | dimensionless + phase | Condition for oscillation |

### 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Operational amplifiers** rely on voltage-series (non-inverting) and voltage-shunt (inverting) feedback to set precisely controlled, stable gains using only external resistors.
- **Audio power amplifiers** use negative feedback to reduce total harmonic distortion (THD) from 1–10% down to below 0.01%.
- **Oscillators** (Wien bridge, Colpitts, Hartley, Phase-shift, Crystal) deliberately use positive feedback satisfying the Barkhausen criterion.
- **Voltage regulators** employ current-series feedback to maintain a constant output voltage regardless of load current.
- **RF amplifiers** use negative feedback to flatten gain response over wide bandwidths.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Closed-Loop Gain Equation (General Negative Feedback)

**Starting premise:** Consider the canonical feedback block diagram with a basic amplifier of gain $A$ and a feedback network with transfer function $\beta$.

**Step 1 — Identify the signals in the loop.**

Let $X_s$ = external source signal at the input, $X_i$ = net input to the basic amplifier, $X_o$ = output signal, and $X_f$ = feedback signal returned to the input node.

For **negative** feedback, the feedback signal **subtracts** from the source:

$$X_i = X_s - X_f$$

**Step 2 — Express the output in terms of the basic amplifier gain.**

$$X_o = A \cdot X_i$$

**Step 3 — Express the feedback signal in terms of the output.**

The feedback network samples a portion $\beta$ of the output:

$$X_f = \beta \cdot X_o$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Step 3 into Step 1.**

$$X_i = X_s - \beta X_o$$

**Step 5 — Substitute Step 4 into Step 2.**

$$X_o = A (X_s - \beta X_o)$$

$$X_o = A X_s - A\beta X_o$$

**Step 6 — Collect $X_o$ terms on the left-hand side.**

$$X_o + A\beta X_o = A X_s$$

$$X_o (1 + A\beta) = A X_s$$

**Step 7 — Divide both sides by $X_s$ to obtain the closed-loop gain.**

$$\frac{X_o}{X_s} = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta}$$

$$\boxed{A_f = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta}}$$

**Sign change for positive feedback:**

For positive feedback, the source and feedback signal are added: $X_i = X_s + X_f$. Following identical algebra, the closed-loop gain becomes:

$$\boxed{A_f^{+} = \frac{A}{1 - A\beta}}$$

### 3.2 Derivation of Effect on Input Resistance (Series Mixing — Voltage-Series Topology)

In a series-mixed feedback, the same current $I_i$ flows through both the source resistance $R_s$ and the amplifier input resistance $R_i$, but the voltages add.

**Step 1 — Write input voltage loop.**

The voltage across the input terminals of the basic amplifier is:

$$V_i = I_i R_i = A(V_s - \beta V_o) \cdot \text{(scaling factor depending on topology)}$$

More directly, for voltage-series feedback, the closed-loop input voltage equation is:

$$V_s = I_i R_i + \beta V_o = I_i R_i + \beta (A \cdot I_i R_i) = I_i R_i (1 + A\beta)$$

**Step 2 — Define closed-loop input resistance.**

$$R_{if} = \frac{V_s}{I_i} = \frac{I_i R_i (1 + A\beta)}{I_i}$$

$$\boxed{R_{if} = R_i (1 + A\beta)}$$

**Interpretation:** Series mixing **multiplies** input impedance by the desensitivity factor $(1 + A\beta)$.

### 3.3 Derivation of Effect on Output Resistance (Voltage Sampling — Voltage-Series Topology)

**Step 1 — Set the source $V_s = 0$ and apply a test voltage $V_x$ at the output.**

With $V_s = 0$, the input becomes $V_i = -\beta V_o = -\beta V_x$.

**Step 2 — Output current expression.**

The amplifier's dependent source delivers $A V_i$ into the output node. The current through the test source $V_x$ must supply both the amplifier's internal output resistance and the feedback-induced component:

$$I_x = \frac{V_x - A V_i}{R_o} = \frac{V_x + A\beta V_x}{R_o} = \frac{V_x(1 + A\beta)}{R_o}$$

**Step 3 — Closed-loop output resistance.**

$$R_{of} = \frac{V_x}{I_x} = \frac{R_o}{1 + A\beta}$$

$$\boxed{R_{of} = \frac{R_o}{1 + A\beta}}$$

**Interpretation:** Voltage sampling **divides** output impedance by $(1 + A\beta)$, making the amplifier behave more like an ideal voltage source.

### 3.4 Derivation of Bandwidth Extension under Negative Feedback

Consider a single-pole amplifier with open-loop transfer function:

$$A(s) = \frac{A_0}{1 + s/\omega_H}$$

When feedback $\beta$ is applied, the closed-loop transfer function becomes:

$$A_f(s) = \frac{A(s)}{1 + A(s)\beta} = \frac{\dfrac{A_0}{1 + s/\omega_H}}{1 + \dfrac{A_0 \beta}{1 + s/\omega_H}}$$

**Step 1 — Combine over a common denominator.**

$$A_f(s) = \frac{A_0}{1 + s/\omega_H + A_0 \beta} = \frac{A_0}{(1 + A_0\beta) + s/\omega_H}$$

**Step 2 — Normalize to standard single-pole form.**

Factor $(1 + A_0\beta)$ out of the denominator:

$$A_f(s) = \frac{A_0 / (1 + A_0\beta)}{1 + \dfrac{s}{\omega_H(1 + A_0\beta)}}$$

**Step 3 — Identify new DC gain and new bandwidth.**

Mid-band (DC) closed-loop gain: $A_{f0} = \dfrac{A_0}{1 + A_0\beta}$

New upper cutoff frequency: $\omega_{Hf} = \omega_H(1 + A_0\beta)$

$$\boxed{BW_f = BW \cdot (1 + A\beta)}$$

**Crucial observation — Gain-Bandwidth Product is invariant:**

$$A_{f0} \cdot \omega_{Hf} = \frac{A_0}{1 + A_0\beta} \cdot \omega_H(1 + A_0\beta) = A_0 \omega_H = \text{GBW (constant)}$$

### 3.5 Numerical Worked Example

> **Problem:** An amplifier has open-loop mid-band gain $A = 1000$, open-loop upper cutoff frequency $f_H = 20 \text{ kHz}$, and feedback factor $\beta = 0.05$. Compute: (i) closed-loop mid-band gain, (ii) closed-loop bandwidth, (iii) gain-bandwidth product, (iv) percentage reduction in gain.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute desensitivity factor.**

$$1 + A\beta = 1 + (1000)(0.05) = 1 + 50 = 51$$

**Step 2 — Closed-loop mid-band gain.**

$$A_f = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta} = \frac{1000}{51} \approx 19.6$$

**Step 3 — Closed-loop bandwidth.**

$$BW_f = BW \cdot (1 + A\beta) = 20 \text{ kHz} \times 51 = 1020 \text{ kHz} = 1.02 \text{ MHz}$$

**Step 4 — Gain-bandwidth product (check).**

$$GBW = A \cdot f_H = 1000 \times 20 \text{ kHz} = 20 \text{ MHz}$$

$$A_f \cdot BW_f = 19.6 \times 1.02 \text{ MHz} \approx 20 \text{ MHz} \checkmark$$

**Step 5 — Percentage reduction in gain.**

$$\%\text{ Reduction} = \frac{A - A_f}{A} \times 100\% = \frac{1000 - 19.6}{1000} \times 100\% = 98.04\%$$

**Insight:** The gain has been reduced by ~98%, but the bandwidth has expanded by a factor of 51 — a classic **gain-bandwidth trade-off**.

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Summary of All Four Topologies

| Parameter | Voltage-Series | Voltage-Shunt | Current-Series | Current-Shunt |
|-----------|---------------|---------------|----------------|---------------|
| Sampling quantity | Voltage | Voltage | Current | Current |
| Mixing quantity | Voltage (series) | Current (shunt) | Voltage (series) | Current (shunt) |
| Ideal amplifier type | Voltage amp | Transresistance amp | Transconductance amp | Current amp |
| Open-loop gain symbol | $A_v$ | $R_m$ | $G_m$ | $A_i$ |
| Closed-loop gain symbol | $A_{vf}$ | $R_{mf}$ | $G_{mf}$ | $A_{if}$ |
| Gain expression | $\dfrac{A_v}{1 + A_v \beta}$ | $\dfrac{R_m}{1 + R_m \beta}$ | $\dfrac{G_m}{1 + G_m \beta}$ | $\dfrac{A_i}{1 + A_i \beta}$ |
| Input resistance | $R_i (1 + A_v\beta)$ ↑ | $\dfrac{R_i}{1 + R_m\beta}$ ↓ | $R_i (1 + G_m\beta)$ ↑ | $\dfrac{R_i}{1 + A_i\beta}$ ↓ |
| Output resistance | $\dfrac{R_o}{1 + A_v\beta}$ ↓ | $\dfrac{R_o}{1 + R_m\beta}$ ↓ | $R_o (1 + G_m\beta)$ ↑ | $R_o (1 + A_i\beta)$ ↑ |
| Best suited for | Voltage amplifier | Current-to-voltage converter | Voltage-to-current converter | Current amplifier |

**Mnemonic — "*Same Sampling Quantity, Same Input/Output Impedance Direction at Output*":**

- Voltage sampling → $R_{of}$ **decreases** (regardless of mixing type).
- Current sampling → $R_{of}$ **increases** (regardless of mixing type).
- Series mixing → $R_{if}$ **increases**.
- Shunt mixing → $R_{if}$ **decreases**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Safeguards Applied

All node IDs are alphanumeric with letter prefixes. Labels contain only raw uppercase alphanumeric text to prevent Mermaid compilation errors.

### 4.2 General Negative Feedback Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA[Source Signal Xs] --> sumA[Summing Node]
    sumA -->|Error Xi| ampA[Basic Amplifier Gain A]
    ampA --> outA[Output Xo]
    outA --> fbA[Feedback Network Beta]
    fbA -->|Feedback Xf| sumA
```

### 4.3 Voltage-Series Feedback Topology (Series-Shunt)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    vsrcA[Voltage Source Vs] --> rsa[Source Resistance Rs]
    rsa --> nodeA[Input Node]
    nodeA --> ampA[Basic Amplifier With Input Resistance Ri]
    ampA --> nodeB[Output Node]
    nodeB --> loadA[Load RL]
    nodeB --> fbsA[Feedback Network Samples Output Voltage]
    fbsA --> nodeC[Feedback Voltage Vf]
    nodeC --> nodeA
```

**Architecture Notes:**
- The feedback network is connected in **shunt across the output** (parallel with load) — hence it senses output voltage.
- The feedback signal is a **voltage inserted in series** with the source at the input — hence the two voltages add algebraically.

### 4.4 Voltage-Shunt Feedback Topology (Shunt-Shunt)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    isrcA[Current Source Is] --> nodeA[Input Node]
    nodeA --> ampA[Basic Amplifier Ri]
    ampA --> nodeB[Output Node]
    nodeB --> loadA[Load RL]
    nodeB --> fbsA[Feedback Network Samples Vout]
    fbsA --> nodeC[Feedback Current If]
    nodeC --> nodeA
```
