
**Architecture Notes:**
- Feedback network is in **shunt across the output**.
- Feedback signal is a **current injected at the input node** (Kirchhoff's Current Law applies).

### 4.5 Current-Series Feedback Topology (Series-Series)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    vsrcA[Voltage Source Vs] --> rsa[Source Resistance Rs]
    rsa --> nodeA[Input Loop]
    nodeA --> ampA[Basic Amplifier]
    ampA --> nodeB[Output Loop]
    nodeB --> loadA[Load RL]
    nodeB --> fbsA[Feedback Network Samples Output Current]
    fbsA --> nodeC[Feedback Voltage Vf]
    nodeC --> nodeA
```

**Architecture Notes:**
- Feedback network is in **series with the load** — it senses the current flowing through the load.
- Feedback signal is a **voltage inserted in series** with the source.

### 4.6 Current-Shunt Feedback Topology (Shunt-Series)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    isrcA[Current Source Is] --> nodeA[Input Node]
    nodeA --> ampA[Basic Amplifier]
    ampA --> nodeB[Output Loop]
    nodeB --> loadA[Load RL]
    nodeB --> fbsA[Feedback Network Samples Output Current]
    fbsA --> nodeC[Feedback Current If]
    nodeC --> nodeA
```

**Architecture Notes:**
- Feedback network is in **series with the load** at the output.
- Feedback signal is a **current injected at the input node**.

### 4.7 Effect-of-Feedback Master Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start Identify Topology] --> q1A{Output Sampling Type}
    q1A -->|Voltage| q2A{Input Mixing Type}
    q1A -->|Current| q3A{Input Mixing Type}
    q2A -->|Series| r1A[Voltage Series Topology Rin Up Rout Down]
    q2A -->|Shunt| r2A[Voltage Shunt Topology Rin Down Rout Down]
    q3A -->|Series| r3A[Current Series Topology Rin Up Rout Up]
    q3A -->|Shunt| r4A[Current Shunt Topology Rin Down Rout Up]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define negative feedback. Mention any **three** effects of negative feedback on the performance of an amplifier.

**Model Answer:**

**Negative Feedback** is the process of feeding back a portion of the output signal to the input in such a way that the feedback signal is **out of phase** (180° anti-phase) with the externally applied input signal, thereby *opposing* the input and reducing the effective input to the amplifier.

**Three effects of negative feedback:**

1. **Gain Reduction:** The closed-loop gain decreases from $A$ to $A/(1 + A\beta)$.
2. **Bandwidth Improvement:** The bandwidth increases by the factor $(1 + A\beta)$.
3. **Distortion and Noise Reduction:** Harmonic distortion and internal noise voltages are reduced by the factor $(1 + A\beta)$.

*(Additional effects accepted: input/output impedance modification, improved gain stability against temperature and component variations.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Differentiate between **positive feedback** and **negative feedback** in an amplifier.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Positive Feedback | Negative Feedback |
|-----------|-------------------|-------------------|
| Phase of feedback signal | In phase with input | 180° out of phase with input |
| Denominator sign | $1 - A\beta$ | $1 + A\beta$ |
| Effect on gain | **Increases** gain | **Decreases** gain |
| Stability | **Reduces** stability; may cause oscillation | **Improves** stability |
| Bandwidth | **Reduces** bandwidth | **Increases** bandwidth |
| Distortion | **Increases** distortion | **Reduces** distortion |
| Application | Oscillators (Wien bridge, Colpitts) | Amplifiers, regulators, filters |

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Instruction (KTU 2024 Scheme):** *Answer any ONE full question from the choice provided. Each full question carries 14 marks and is divided into sub-parts.*

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, derive the expression for the closed-loop gain of a **voltage-series feedback amplifier**. Show how negative feedback modifies the input and output impedances.

**(b) [7 Marks)** An amplifier without feedback gives an output of 50 V with an input of 1 V. When feedback is applied with $\beta = 0.05$, find: (i) closed-loop gain, (ii) output voltage, (iii) feedback voltage, and (iv) feedback factor if $A_f$ is to be reduced by a factor of 20.

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

The voltage-series feedback block diagram consists of a basic voltage amplifier with gain $A_v$ and a feedback network that samples the output voltage and returns a proportional voltage in series with the input source.

**Derivation:**

The error (net) input to the basic amplifier is:

$$V_i = V_s - V_f$$

The amplifier output is:

$$V_o = A_v V_i = A_v (V_s - V_f)$$

The feedback network senses $V_o$ and produces:

$$V_f = \beta V_o$$

Substituting:

$$V_o = A_v V_s - A_v \beta V_o \implies V_o(1 + A_v\beta) = A_v V_s$$

$$\boxed{A_{vf} = \frac{V_o}{V_s} = \frac{A_v}{1 + A_v\beta}}$$

**Input Impedance:**

$$R_{if} = R_i (1 + A_v\beta) \quad \text{(increased)}$$

**Output Impedance:**

$$R_{of} = \frac{R_o}{1 + A_v\beta} \quad \text{(decreased)}$$

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Given:** $V_o = 50$ V (open loop), $V_s = 1$ V (open loop), $\beta = 0.05$.

**(i) Open-loop gain:**

$$A_v = \frac{V_o}{V_s} = \frac{50}{1} = 50$$

**(ii) Desensitivity factor:**

$$1 + A_v\beta = 1 + (50)(0.05) = 1 + 2.5 = 3.5$$

**(iii) Closed-loop gain:**

$$A_{vf} = \frac{A_v}{1 + A_v\beta} = \frac{50}{3.5} \approx 14.29$$

**(iv) Output voltage with feedback:**

$$V_{of} = A_{vf} \cdot V_s = 14.29 \times 1 = 14.29 \text{ V}$$

**(v) Feedback voltage:**

$$V_f = \beta V_{of} = 0.05 \times 14.29 \approx 0.714 \text{ V}$$

**(vi) For gain reduction by factor 20:**

Desired closed-loop gain: $A_{vf}' = \dfrac{A_v}{20} = \dfrac{50}{20} = 2.5$

$$A_{vf}' = \frac{A_v}{1 + A_v \beta'} \implies 2.5 = \frac{50}{1 + 50\beta'}$$

$$1 + 50\beta' = 20 \implies \beta' = \frac{19}{50} = 0.38$$

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** List the **four basic feedback topologies** with neat block diagrams. For each topology, state the type of ideal amplifier and the effect of negative feedback on input and output impedances.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An amplifier has open-loop gain $A = 200$, bandwidth $BW = 50$ kHz, and feedback factor $\beta = 0.04$. Calculate the closed-loop gain, closed-loop bandwidth, and verify the gain-bandwidth product remains constant. Comment on the percentage reduction in gain.

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

The **four basic feedback topologies** are:

**1. Voltage-Series Feedback (Series-Shunt)**
- Output Sampling: **Voltage**
- Input Mixing: **Series (Voltage)**
- Ideal Amplifier: **Voltage Amplifier** ($A_v$)
- $R_{if} = R_i(1 + A_v\beta)$ → **Increases**
- $R_{of} = R_o/(1 + A_v\beta)$ → **Decreases**

**2. Voltage-Shunt Feedback (Shunt-Shunt)**
- Output Sampling: **Voltage**
- Input Mixing: **Shunt (Current)**
- Ideal Amplifier: **Transresistance Amplifier** ($R_m$ in $\Omega$)
- $R_{if} = R_i/(1 + R_m\beta)$ → **Decreases**
- $R_{of} = R_o/(1 + R_m\beta)$ → **Decreases**

**3. Current-Series Feedback (Series-Series)**
- Output Sampling: **Current**
- Input Mixing: **Series (Voltage)**
- Ideal Amplifier: **Transconductance Amplifier** ($G_m$ in S)
- $R_{if} = R_i(1 + G_m\beta)$ → **Increases**
- $R_{of} = R_o(1 + G_m\beta)$ → **Increases**

**4. Current-Shunt Feedback (Shunt-Series)**
- Output Sampling: **Current**
- Input Mixing: **Shunt (Current)**
- Ideal Amplifier: **Current Amplifier** ($A_i$)
- $R_{if} = R_i/(1 + A_i\beta)$ → **Decreases**
- $R_{of} = R_o(1 + A_i\beta)$ → **Increases**

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Given:** $A = 200$, $BW = 50$ kHz, $\beta = 0.04$.

**(i) Desensitivity factor:**

$$1 + A\beta = 1 + (200)(0.04) = 1 + 8 = 9$$

**(ii) Closed-loop gain:**

$$A_f = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta} = \frac{200}{9} \approx 22.22$$

**(iii) Closed-loop bandwidth:**

$$BW_f = BW \cdot (1 + A\beta) = 50 \text{ kHz} \times 9 = 450 \text{ kHz}$$

**(iv) Gain-bandwidth product verification:**

$$\text{Open-loop GBW} = A \cdot BW = 200 \times 50 \text{ kHz} = 10{,}000 \text{ kHz} = 10 \text{ MHz}$$

$$\text{Closed-loop GBW} = A_f \cdot BW_f = 22.22 \times 450 \text{ kHz} \approx 10 \text{ MHz} \checkmark$$

**(v) Percentage reduction in gain:**

$$\%\text{ Reduction} = \frac{A - A_f}{A} \times 100\% = \frac{200 - 22.22}{200} \times 100\% = 88.89\%$$

**Comment:** The gain has reduced by approximately 88.9%, but the bandwidth has expanded by a factor of 9. The gain-bandwidth product is conserved at 10 MHz, confirming the fundamental trade-off in negative feedback amplifier design.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign error in denominator:** Negative feedback → $1 + A\beta$; Positive feedback → $1 - A\beta$. Marks deducted for swapping.
> 2. **Topology identification mistakes:** Students often confuse *mixing* at input with *sampling* at output. Always state *both* clearly in your answer.
> 3. **Impedance direction errors:** Voltage sampling **decreases** output impedance; current sampling **increases** output impedance. Series mixing **increases** input impedance; shunt mixing **decreases** input impedance.
> 4. **Forgetting units:** Transresistance is in $\Omega$; transconductance is in Siemens (S). Voltage and current gains are dimensionless.
> 5. **Calculation mistakes:** Always compute $1 + A\beta$ first; it is the master desensitivity factor used in nearly every sub-expression.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Feedback equation (negative):** $A_f = A / (1 + A\beta)$ — reduces gain, improves stability.
- **Feedback equation (positive):** $A_f^{+} = A / (1 - A\beta)$ — increases gain, may cause oscillation.
- **Barkhausen criterion for oscillation:** Magnitude condition $|A\beta| = 1$ AND phase condition $\angle A\beta = 0^\circ$ (or multiples of $2\pi$).
- **Gain-Bandwidth invariance:** $A \cdot BW = A_f \cdot BW_f = \text{constant}$.
- **Sensitivity to device parameters:** Reduced by factor $(1 + A\beta)$.
- **Four topologies in one line:** V-S (V-amp), V-P (Trans-R), C-S (Trans-G), C-P (I-amp) — where V=Voltage, C=Current, S=Series, P=Parallel (Shunt).
- **Output impedance rule:** Voltage-sampled → $R_o$ divided by $(1 + A\beta)$; Current-sampled → $R_o$ multiplied by $(1 + A\beta)$.
- **Input impedance rule:** Series-mixed → $R_i$ multiplied by $(1 + A\beta)$; Shunt-mixed → $R_i$ divided by $(1 + A\beta)$.
- **Noise/distortion:** Both reduced by $(1 + A\beta)$.
- **Deep negative feedback limit:** When $A\beta \gg 1$, $A_f \to 1/\beta$ — gain becomes a function of only the passive feedback network.
- **Sign convention:** Negative feedback adds a **plus** in the denominator; positive feedback uses a **minus**. Memorize this with the phrase "Negative → Plus, Positive → Minus."
- **Practical design guideline:** Aim for $A\beta \approx 10$ to $100$ for a good balance between gain stability and adequate signal magnitude.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Frequency Response and Distortion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET302 - ELECTRONIC DEVICES AND CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Frequency Response and Distortion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Frequency Response — The Formal Definition

**Frequency Response** of an electronic amplifier is defined as the variation of the amplifier's output (gain magnitude and phase) as a function of the input signal frequency, while keeping the input signal amplitude constant. It is a measure of how faithfully an amplifier reproduces signals across a continuous band of frequencies.

Mathematically, the frequency response of an amplifier is expressed as:

$$\mathbf{A_v(j\omega) = \frac{V_{out}(j\omega)}{V_{in}(j\omega)}}$$

This complex ratio has two components:
- **Magnitude Response:** $\vert A_v(\omega) \vert = \dfrac{V_{out}}{V_{in}}$
- **Phase Response:** $\phi(\omega) = \angle A_v(j\omega)$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** An ideal amplifier must have a *flat* magnitude response and a *linear* phase response across the entire operating band. Any deviation from this ideal behavior is termed **distortion**.

### 1.2 Frequency Response Regions — Conceptual Analogy

Imagine you are playing a song through a speaker system. The song contains a low-frequency bass drum, mid-frequency vocals, and high-frequency cymbals. A **perfect amplifier** would reproduce all three at the *same relative loudness and timing*. In reality:

- **Low frequencies** (bass) get attenuated if the coupling capacitor is too small → bass sounds weak.
- **High frequencies** (treble) get attenuated due to internal device capacitances → cymbals sound muffled.
- **Mid frequencies** (vocals) pass through perfectly → voice is clear.

This selective attenuation across frequency is what we call the **frequency response** of the amplifier. The "sweet spot" where all frequencies pass equally well is called the **mid-band region**, bounded by two **cutoff frequencies** $f_1$ and $f_2$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Definition (KTU Board Standard):**
> - **Lower Cutoff Frequency ($f_1$ or $f_L$):** Frequency at which the gain drops to **0.707** (or $-3$ dB) of the mid-band gain.
> - **Upper Cutoff Frequency ($f_2$ or $f_H$):** Frequency at which the gain again drops to **0.707** (or $-3$ dB) of the mid-band gain.
> - **Bandwidth (BW):** $BW = f_2 - f_1$

### 1.3 Distortion — The Formal Definition

**Distortion** is any unwanted change in the shape, amplitude, or timing of a signal as it passes through an amplifier. A signal is said to be *undistorted* if the output is an exact scaled replica of the input (possibly with a constant time delay).

For an output $v_o(t)$ to be a faithful reproduction of input $v_i(t)$:

$$v_o(t) = A_v \cdot v_i(t - t_d)$$

Where:
- $A_v$ = constant voltage gain (frequency-independent)
- $t_d$ = constant time delay (frequency-independent)

Any departure from this ideal equation constitutes **distortion**.

### 1.4 The Three Primary Types of Distortion

| S.No | Type of Distortion | Cause | Effect on Signal |
|------|--------------------|-------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Amplitude (Non-linear) Distortion** | Non-linearity of active device (BJT/FET) transfer characteristic | New frequency components (harmonics) are generated |
| 2 | **Frequency (Linear) Distortion** | Gain variation with frequency ($A_v \neq$ constant) | Different frequencies amplified unequally |
| 3 | **Phase (Delay) Distortion** | Non-linear phase response (phase shift not proportional to $\omega$) | Time delay differs for different frequencies |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Exams:**
> - **Non-linear (Amplitude) distortion** → Creates NEW frequencies (harmonics of input).
> - **Linear (Frequency/Phase) distortion** → Does NOT create new frequencies, only changes relative amplitude/phase of existing ones.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Decibel (dB) Notation — The Language of Frequency Response

The human ear responds logarithmically to sound intensity. Similarly, the gain of amplifiers varies over an enormous range (from fractions to millions). Engineers use a logarithmic scale called the **decibel (dB)** to compress this wide range into manageable numbers.

**Voltage Gain in dB:**

$$A_v \text{(dB)} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}}\right)$$

**Power Gain in dB:**

$$A_P \text{(dB)} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Aid:** Notice the factor of **2** difference — voltage uses $20 \log$ because power is proportional to $V^2$, and $\log(V^2) = 2\log(V)$.

### 2.2 Cutoff Frequency and the Significance of $-3$ dB

The **cutoff frequency** $f_c$ is the frequency at which the output power drops to half the maximum value, OR equivalently, the voltage drops to $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707$ of its maximum.

$$0.707 \text{ in dB} = 20 \log(0.707) = -3 \text{ dB}$$

For a simple RC low-pass filter, the cutoff frequency is given by:

$$f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$$

The gain at cutoff frequency:
$$\vert A_v \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (f/f_c)^2}} \xrightarrow{f = f_c} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$$

### 2.3 Slope of Gain Roll-Off

Beyond the cutoff frequency, gain falls at a constant rate per decade of frequency:

- **$-20$ dB/decade** (or **$-6$ dB/octave**) — for each pole (one capacitor)
- **$-40$ dB/decade** (or **$-12$ dB/octave**) — for two poles
- **$-60$ dB/decade** (or **$-18$ dB/octave**) — for three poles

> [!NOTE]
> **Decade vs. Octave:**
> - **Decade:** Frequency changes by factor of 10 (e.g., 100 Hz to 1000 Hz)
> - **Octave:** Frequency changes by factor of 2 (e.g., 100 Hz to 200 Hz)
> - Relation: $20 \text{ dB/decade} = 6 \text{ dB/octave}$

### 2.4 Rise Time and Bandwidth Relationship

**Rise Time ($t_r$)** is defined as the time required for the output to rise from **10% to 90%** of its final value in response to a step input.

For a first-order RC-coupled amplifier:

$$t_r \cdot BW = \frac{0.35}{1} = 0.35$$

Where BW is in **Hz** and $t_r$ is in **seconds**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favorite:** This relationship shows an inverse trade-off — wider bandwidth means faster rise time (better high-frequency response).

### 2.5 Amplitude (Non-linear) Distortion — Quantitative Analysis

When a sinusoidal signal is applied to a non-linear device, the output contains the fundamental frequency plus integer multiples called **harmonics**.

**Total Harmonic Distortion (THD):**

$$\text{THD} = \frac{\sqrt{V_2^2 + V_3^2 + V_4^2 + \cdots + V_n^2}}{V_1} \times 100\%$$

Where:
- $V_1$ = RMS voltage of fundamental frequency
- $V_2, V_3, \ldots, V_n$ = RMS voltages of 2nd, 3rd, ..., nth harmonics

### 2.6 Frequency and Phase Distortion — Quantitative Analysis

**Frequency Distortion** occurs when the gain magnitude $\vert A_v(\omega) \vert$ varies with frequency within the operating band. A complex signal containing multiple frequency components will have its components amplified unequally.

**Phase Distortion** occurs when the phase shift $\phi(\omega)$ is NOT a linear function of $\omega$. For a phase-linear system:

$$\phi(\omega) = -\omega \cdot t_d \quad (\text{linear with frequency})$$

For a delay-distortionless system, the **group delay** must be constant:

$$t_d = -\frac{d\phi(\omega)}{d\omega} = \text{constant}$$

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula | Expression | Application | Units |
|---------|-----------|-------------|-------|
| Voltage Gain in dB | $A_v(dB) = 20 \log_{10}(V_{out}/V_{in})$ | Converting gain to logarithmic scale | dB |
| Power Gain in dB | $A_P(dB) = 10 \log_{10}(P_{out}/P_{in})$ | Power amplifier analysis | dB |
| Cutoff Frequency | $f_c = 1/(2\pi RC)$ | RC circuit cutoff | Hz |
| Bandwidth | $BW = f_2 - f_1$ | Amplifier operating range | Hz |
| Mid-band Gain | $A_{v(mid)} = $ constant | Flat region gain | V/V |
| Gain at $f_c$ | $\vert A_v \vert = A_{v(mid)}/\sqrt{2}$ | At cutoff frequencies | V/V |
| Rise Time-BW | $t_r \cdot BW = 0.35$ | Step response analysis | s·Hz |
| THD | $\sqrt{V_2^2+V_3^2+\cdots}/V_1 \times 100$ | Non-linear distortion measure | % |
| Roll-off (single pole) | $-20$ dB/decade or $-6$ dB/octave | High/low frequency attenuation | dB/decade |
| Group Delay | $t_d = -d\phi/d\omega$ | Phase linearity check | seconds |

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Audio Amplifiers:** Require flat response from 20 Hz to 20 kHz (human hearing range).
2. **Video Amplifiers:** Need bandwidth of several MHz with linear phase to avoid color smear.
3. **RF Communication Systems:** Selective bandpass response to isolate specific channels.
4. **Oscilloscope Probes:** Compensation networks adjust frequency response for accurate waveform display.
5. **Medical Equipment (ECG/EEG):** Faithful reproduction of biological signals demands low distortion across the signal band.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Cutoff Frequency for an RC Low-Pass Filter

Consider a simple RC low-pass network as shown:

```
   Vin ----[R]----+---- Vout
                  [C]
                   |
                  GND
```

The transfer function using the voltage divider rule:

$$H(j\omega) = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \frac{(1/j\omega C)}{R + (1/j\omega C)}$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $j\omega C$:

$$H(j\omega) = \frac{1}{1 + j\omega RC}$$

**Step 1:** Identify the magnitude response.

$$\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the cutoff condition: $\vert H(j\omega_c) \vert = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega_c RC)^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$$

**Step 3:** Square both sides.

$$\frac{1}{1 + (\omega_c RC)^2} = \frac{1}{2}$$

**Step 4:** Cross-multiply.

$$1 + (\omega_c RC)^2 = 2$$

**Step 5:** Solve for $\omega_c$.

$$(\omega_c RC)^2 = 1 \implies \omega_c RC = 1$$

$$\boxed{\omega_c = \frac{1}{RC} \implies f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}}$$

**Step 6:** Phase response derivation.

The phase angle of the transfer function is:

$$\phi(\omega) = -\tan^{-1}\left(\frac{\omega RC}{1}\right) = -\tan^{-1}(\omega RC)$$

At cutoff: $\phi(f_c) = -\tan^{-1}(1) = -45°$

### 3.2 Derivation of Gain Roll-Off Slope ($-20$ dB/decade)

Consider the magnitude response at frequencies $f \gg f_c$:

$$\vert H(j\omega) \vert \approx \frac{1}{\omega RC} = \frac{1}{2\pi f RC}$$

In dB:

$$A_v(dB) = 20 \log\left(\frac{1}{2\pi f RC}\right) = -20 \log(2\pi RC) - 20 \log(f)$$

**Step 1:** Find the change in gain when frequency increases by a factor of 10 (one decade).

$$\Delta A_v = -20 \log(10f) - (-20\log f) = -20 \log(10) = -20 \text{ dB}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Slope} = -20 \text{ dB/decade}}$$

**Step 2:** Find the change in gain when frequency doubles (one octave).

$$\Delta A_v = -20 \log(2f) - (-20 \log f) = -20 \log(2) = -20 \times 0.301 \approx -6 \text{ dB}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Slope} = -6 \text{ dB/octave}}$$

### 3.3 Derivation of Rise Time-Bandwidth Relationship

For an RC low-pass filter subjected to a step input, the output voltage is:

$$v_{out}(t) = V_{final}\left(1 - e^{-t/RC}\right)$$

**Step 1:** At $t = t_1$ (10% of $V_{final}$):

$$0.1 V_{final} = V_{final}\left(1 - e^{-t_1/RC}\right)$$

$$e^{-t_1/RC} = 0.9 \implies t_1 = -RC \ln(0.9) = 0.1053 \, RC$$

**Step 2:** At $t = t_2$ (90% of $V_{final}$):

$$0.9 V_{final} = V_{final}\left(1 - e^{-t_2/RC}\right)$$

$$e^{-t_2/RC} = 0.1 \implies t_2 = -RC \ln(0.1) = 2.3026 \, RC$$

**Step 3:** Rise time calculation.

$$t_r = t_2 - t_1 = (2.3026 - 0.1053) RC = 2.197 \, RC \approx 2.2 \, RC$$

**Step 4:** Substitute the bandwidth relation $RC = 1/(2\pi f_c)$:

$$t_r = 2.2 \times \frac{1}{2\pi f_c} = \frac{0.35}{f_c} = \frac{0.35}{BW}$$

$$\boxed{t_r \cdot BW = 0.35}$$

### 3.4 Derivation of Total Harmonic Distortion (THD)

Suppose a non-linear amplifier produces output voltage components:

$$v_o(t) = V_0 + V_1 \sin(\omega t) + V_2 \sin(2\omega t) + V_3 \sin(3\omega t) + \cdots$$

**Step 1:** The fundamental RMS voltage.

$$V_1(\text{rms}) = \frac{V_1}{\sqrt{2}}$$

**Step 2:** The total RMS voltage of all harmonics.

$$V_h(\text{rms}) = \sqrt{\left(\frac{V_2}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{V_3}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^2 + \cdots}$$

$$V_h(\text{rms}) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\sqrt{V_2^2 + V_3^2 + \cdots}$$

**Step 3:** THD definition.

$$\text{THD} = \frac{V_h(\text{rms})}{V_1(\text{rms})} = \frac{\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\sqrt{V_2^2 + V_3^2 + \cdots}}{\dfrac{V_1}{\sqrt{2}}}$$

$$\boxed{\text{THD} = \frac{\sqrt{V_2^2 + V_3^2 + V_4^2 + \cdots + V_n^2}}{V_1} \times 100\%}$$

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Multi-Pole Amplifier

**Problem:** A two-stage amplifier has two poles at $f_{p1} = 1$ kHz and $f_{p2} = 100$ kHz. The mid-band gain is $A_{v(mid)} = 1000$.

**Find:** (a) The overall bandwidth, (b) Gain at $f = 50$ kHz in dB, (c) Total roll-off slope per decade.

**Solution:**

**(a)** For cascaded stages, the overall cutoff is approximately where the combined gain falls to $-3$ dB. The dominant (lower) pole primarily determines $f_1$:

$$f_1 \approx f_{p1} = 1 \text{ kHz}$$

The upper cutoff is approximately:

$$f_2 \approx f_{p2} = 100 \text{ kHz}$$

$$\boxed{BW = f_2 - f_1 \approx 99 \text{ kHz} \approx 100 \text{ kHz}}$$

**(b)** Since $f = 50$ kHz is between $f_1$ and $f_2$, only the high-frequency pole affects the gain:

$$\vert A_v \vert = \frac{A_{v(mid)}}{\sqrt{1 + (f/f_2)^2}} = \frac{1000}{\sqrt{1 + (50/100)^2}}$$

$$\vert A_v \vert = \frac{1000}{\sqrt{1 + 0.25}} = \frac{1000}{\sqrt{1.25}} = \frac{1000}{1.118} \approx 894.4$$

In dB:

$$A_v(dB) = 20 \log(894.4) = 20 \times 2.9516 \approx 59.03 \text{ dB}$$

**(c)** Past $f_2 = 100$ kHz, the slope becomes $-40$ dB/decade (since both poles contribute):

$$\boxed{\text{Slope beyond } f_2 = -40 \text{ dB/decade}}$$

### 3.6 Analytical Summary Table — Identifying Distortion Type

| Observation in Output | Likely Distortion Type | Verification Method |
|----------------------|------------------------|---------------------|
| Output has same shape as input but reduced bass | Frequency Distortion | Check gain at low frequencies |
| Output has same shape as input but delayed differently per freq. | Phase Distortion | Plot phase vs. frequency |
| Output contains new frequencies not in input | Amplitude (Non-linear) Distortion | FFT/spectrum analyzer |
| Clipping at peaks of waveform | Amplitude Distortion (saturation) | Reduce input amplitude |
| Symmetric flattening (both peaks clipped) | Amplitude Distortion (hard clipping) | Check Q-point |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Frequency Response Regions of an Amplifier

This Mermaid flowchart illustrates the three operating regions of an amplifier and the dominant capacitive elements responsible for gain roll-off in each.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Signal v_in] --> B[Amplifier Frequency Response]
    B --> C{Low Frequency Region}
    B --> D{Mid Frequency Region}
    B --> E{High Frequency Region}

    C --> C1[Coupling Capacitor C_c]
    C --> C2[Emitter Bypass Capacitor C_E]
    C --> C3[Gain ROLLS OFF]
    C1 --> C3
    C2 --> C3

    D --> D1[All Capacitors ACT as Short Circuits]
    D --> D2[Gain A_v is FLAT and Maximum]
    D --> D3[No Reactive Elements Affect Performance]
    D1 --> D2
    D2 --> D3

    E --> E1[Junction Capacitance C_be of BJT]
    E --> E2[Miller Effect Multiplies C_bc]
    E --> E3[Wiring and Stray Capacitances]
    E --> E4[Gain ROLLS OFF]
    E1 --> E4
    E2 --> E4
    E3 --> E4

    C3 --> F[Cutoff f_1 at -3 dB]
    D3 --> G[Mid-band Gain A_v_mid]
    E4 --> H[Cutoff f_2 at -3 dB]

    F --> I[Bandwidth BW = f_2 minus f_1]
    H --> I
    G --> I
```

### 4.2 Classification Diagram — Types of Distortion

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    J[Signal Distortion in Amplifiers] --> K[Amplitude Distortion]
    J --> L[Frequency Distortion]
    J --> M[Phase Distortion]

    K --> K1[Cause: Non-linear transfer characteristic]
    K --> K2[Creates NEW frequencies]
    K --> K3[Harmonics generated]
    K --> K4[Quantified by THD percent]
    K1 --> K5
    K2 --> K5
    K3 --> K5
    K4 --> K5
    K5[Effect: Signal shape altered]

    L --> L1[Cause: Gain varies with frequency]
    L --> L2[No new frequencies created]
    L --> L3[Complex waveform altered]
    L --> L4[Audio sounds unnatural]
    L1 --> L6
    L2 --> L6
    L3 --> L6
    L4 --> L6
    L6[Effect: Tonal imbalance]

    M --> M1[Cause: Phase not linear with omega]
    M --> M2[Different delays per frequency]
    M --> M3[Wave shape changed in time]
    M --> M4[Transient smearing in video]
    M1 --> M7
    M2 --> M7
    M3 --> M7
    M4 --> M7
    M7[Effect: Timing errors]
```

### 4.3 Bode Plot Construction — Gain vs. Frequency

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Frequency Axis: Logarithmic f] --> P2[Gain Axis: Linear in dB]
    P2 --> P3[Mid-band region: FLAT line at A_v_mid dB]
    P3 --> P4[At f_1: gain drops 3 dB]
    P4 --> P5[Below f_1: SLOPE rises at plus 20 dB per decade]
    P3 --> P6[At f_2: gain drops 3 dB]
    P6 --> P7[Above f_2: SLOPE falls at minus 20 dB per decade]

    P5 --> P8[Asymptotic Bode Plot Shape]
    P7 --> P8
    P8 --> P9[Actual Response: Smooth Curve through Corner Frequencies]
    P9 --> P10[Cutoff Points: f_1 and f_2 at minus 3 dB from mid-band]
```

### 4.4 RC Low-Pass Filter — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q1[Input Source V_in at frequency f] --> Q2[Series Resistance R in ohms]
    Q2 --> Q3[Output Node V_out]
    Q3 --> Q4[Shunt Capacitance C in farads]
    Q4 --> Q5[Ground Reference]

    Q1 --> R1[Frequencies MUCH LESS than f_c]
    R1 --> R2[Capacitor acts OPEN circuit]
    R2 --> R3[Output equals Input - FULL PASS]

    Q1 --> S1[Frequencies MUCH GREATER than f_c]
    S1 --> S2[Capacitor acts SHORT circuit]
    S2 --> S3[Output drops to ZERO - FULL BLOCK]

    Q1 --> T1[Frequencies EQUAL to f_c]
    T1 --> T2[Output equals 0.707 times Input]
    T2 --> T3[Cutoff Frequency Point]
```

### 4.5 Decision Flowchart — Diagnosing Distortion Type from Symptoms

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    U1[Observe Amplifier Output] --> U2{New frequencies present in spectrum?}
    U2 -- YES --> U3[Amplitude or Non-linear Distortion]
    U3 --> U4[Reduce input signal amplitude]
    U4 --> U5{Distortion persists?}
    U5 -- YES --> U6[Check Q-point biasing of transistor]
    U5 -- NO --> U7[Distortion due to overdrive - acceptable]

    U2 -- NO --> U8{Gain varies with frequency?}
    U8 -- YES --> U9[Frequency or Linear Distortion]
    U9 --> U10[Check coupling and bypass capacitors]
    U10 --> U11[Measure cutoff frequencies f_1 and f_2]

    U8 -- NO --> U12{Phase shift varies non-linearly?}
    U12 -- YES --> U13[Phase or Delay Distortion]
    U13 --> U14[Check for improper load or compensation]
    U12 -- NO --> U15[Amplifier operating correctly - no distortion]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

Define frequency response of an amplifier. What do you understand by the terms lower cutoff frequency, upper cutoff frequency, and bandwidth?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **frequency response** of an amplifier is the variation of its gain (magnitude and phase) with the input signal frequency, while maintaining constant input amplitude. It is typically represented as a plot of gain (in dB) versus frequency (on logarithmic scale), known as a **Bode plot**.

- **Lower cutoff frequency ($f_1$):** The frequency at which the gain falls to **0.707** times (or **$-3$ dB** below) the mid-band gain, due to coupling and bypass capacitors.
- **Upper cutoff frequency ($f_2$):** The frequency at which the gain again falls to **0.707** times (or **$-3$ dB** below) the mid-band gain, due to internal device capacitances.
- **Bandwidth (BW):** The range of useful frequencies for amplification, given by $BW = f_2 - f_1$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

List and briefly explain the different types of distortion that can occur in an amplifier.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The three primary types of distortion in amplifiers are:

1. **Amplitude (Non-linear) Distortion:** Caused by the non-linear transfer characteristic of the active device. It generates new frequency components called **harmonics** in the output that were not present in the input. Quantified by **Total Harmonic Distortion (THD)**.

2. **Frequency (Linear) Distortion:** Occurs when the gain of the amplifier is not constant across the operating frequency range. Different frequency components of a complex signal (e.g., music) are amplified by different amounts, altering the tonal balance without creating new frequencies.

3. **Phase (Delay) Distortion:** Occurs when the phase shift introduced by the amplifier is not a linear function of frequency. This causes different frequency components to experience different time delays, distorting the waveform shape, particularly critical in video and pulse signals.

---

### PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### **QUESTION A — Choice Option 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2023]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the cutoff frequency of an RC low-pass filter. Show that the gain rolls off at $-20$ dB per decade above the cutoff frequency.

**(7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Transfer Function:**

For an RC low-pass filter with resistance $R$ and capacitance $C$, the voltage divider gives:

$$H(j\omega) = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \frac{1/j\omega C}{R + 1/j\omega C} = \frac{1}{1 + j\omega RC}$$

**Step 2 — Magnitude Response:**

$$\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}}$$

**Step 3 — Applying Cutoff Condition:**

At cutoff frequency, $\vert H(j\omega_c) \vert = 1/\sqrt{2}$:

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega_c RC)^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$$

Squaring: $1 + (\omega_c RC)^2 = 2$, so $\omega_c RC = 1$

$$\boxed{f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}}$$

**Step 4 — Roll-Off Derivation:**

For $f \gg f_c$, $\omega RC \gg 1$, so:

$$\vert H(j\omega) \vert \approx \frac{1}{\omega RC}$$

In dB:

$$A_v(dB) = 20 \log\left(\frac{1}{\omega RC}\right) = -20\log(2\pi RC) - 20\log(f)$$

When $f$ increases by a factor of 10:

$$\Delta A_v = -20\log(10f) + 20\log(f) = -20 \log(10) = -20 \text{ dB}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Slope} = -20 \text{ dB/decade}}$$

**(b)** An amplifier has a mid-band gain of 200 and cutoff frequencies $f_1 = 50$ Hz and $f_2 = 20$ kHz. Calculate:

(i) The bandwidth of the amplifier.

(ii) The voltage gain in dB at $f = 100$ Hz.

(iii) The gain (in dB) at $f = 50$ kHz.

**(7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**(i) Bandwidth:**

$$BW = f_2 - f_1 = 20{,}000 - 50 = 19{,}950 \text{ Hz} \approx 19.95 \text{ kHz}$$

**(ii) Gain in dB at $f = 100$ Hz:**

Since $100$ Hz $>$ $f_1 = 50$ Hz, we are above the lower cutoff, in the mid-band region.

$$A_v = 200 \implies A_v(dB) = 20 \log(200) = 20 \times 2.301 = 46.02 \text{ dB}$$

**(iii) Gain at $f = 50$ kHz:**

Since $50$ kHz $>$ $f_2 = 20$ kHz, we are above the upper cutoff. Gain rolls off at $-20$ dB/decade.

Number of decades above $f_2$:

$$n = \log\left(\frac{50{,}000}{20{,}000}\right) = \log(2.5) = 0.3979 \text{ decades}$$

Gain drop:

$$\Delta A_v = -20 \times 0.3979 = -7.96 \text{ dB}$$

Final gain:

$$A_v(50\text{ kHz}) = 46.02 - 7.96 = 38.06 \text{ dB}$$

$$\boxed{A_v(50 \text{ kHz}) \approx 38.06 \text{ dB}}$$

---

### **QUESTION B — Choice Option 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** What is Total Harmonic Distortion (THD)? Derive its expression. An amplifier output contains fundamental of 10 V (rms) and second and third harmonics of 2 V and 1 V (rms) respectively. Calculate the THD.

**(7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition of THD:**

**Total Harmonic Distortion (THD)** is a quantitative measure of the degree of non-linear (amplitude) distortion in an amplifier. It is defined as the ratio of the RMS voltage of all harmonic components to the RMS voltage of the fundamental, expressed as a percentage.

**Step 2 — Derivation:**

For a non-linear amplifier output containing harmonics:

$$v_o(t) = V_1 \sin(\omega t) + V_2 \sin(2\omega t) + V_3 \sin(3\omega t) + \cdots$$

RMS voltage of fundamental: $V_1^{rms} = V_1/\sqrt{2}$

RMS voltage of all harmonics:

$$V_h^{rms} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{V_2}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{V_3}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^2 + \cdots} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\sqrt{V_2^2 + V_3^2 + \cdots}$$

By definition:

$$\text{THD} = \frac{V_h^{rms}}{V_1^{rms}} \times 100\% = \frac{\sqrt{V_2^2 + V_3^2 + \cdots}}{V_1} \times 100\%$$

**Step 3 — Numerical Calculation:**

Given: $V_1 = 10$ V, $V_2 = 2$ V, $V_3 = 1$ V

$$\text{THD} = \frac{\sqrt{(2)^2 + (1)^2}}{10} \times 100\% = \frac{\sqrt{4 + 1}}{10} \times 100\%$$

$$\text{THD} = \frac{\sqrt{5}}{10} \times 100\% = \frac{2.236}{10} \times 100\%$$

$$\boxed{\text{THD} = 22.36\%}$$

**(b)** Explain with diagrams the difference between amplitude distortion, frequency distortion, and phase distortion. State one method to minimize each type.

**(7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Amplitude (Non-linear) Distortion:**

- **Cause:** Non-linear transfer characteristic of the active device (BJT/FET).
- **Effect:** Output waveform is "flattened" or "sharpened" at peaks. New harmonic frequencies appear in the output spectrum.
- **Diagram Description:** Input sine wave produces output with flattened peaks (clipping) — the symmetry is broken.
- **Minimization:** Operate the transistor in the linear region of its transfer curve by proper Q-point selection. Use negative feedback to linearize the overall response.

**Frequency (Linear) Distortion:**

- **Cause:** Gain varies with frequency due to frequency-dependent reactive elements (capacitors, inductors).
- **Effect:** Different frequency components of a complex waveform are amplified unequally. Audio output sounds "muffled" or "tinny."
- **Diagram Description:** Input contains equal-amplitude fundamental and harmonics; output shows the same frequencies but with differing amplitudes.
- **Minimization:** Use appropriate coupling and bypass capacitor values to extend low-frequency response. Use cascaded stages with staggered cutoff frequencies to widen bandwidth.

**Phase (Delay) Distortion:**

- **Cause:** Phase shift is not proportional to frequency (non-linear phase response).
- **Effect:** Different frequencies arrive at the output with different time delays, distorting the waveform shape and smearing transients.
- **Diagram Description:** Input pulse has sharp edges; output pulse has rounded edges due to differential delay between frequency components.
- **Minimization:** Design amplifier with all-pole Butterworth or Bessel response. Use phase compensation networks (e.g., Miller capacitor compensation in op-amps).

---

### **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing dB Formulas:** Students often write $10\log(P)$ for voltage gain. **Correct:** Voltage gain uses **$20\log(V_o/V_i)$** because power $\propto V^2$.
>
> 2. **Sign Error on Roll-Off:** The slope is **$-20$ dB/decade**, NOT $+20$. The gain *falls* above $f_2$, not rises.
>
> 3. **Wrong THD Formula:** Some students add harmonic voltages instead of taking the **square root of sum of squares**. Always use $\sqrt{V_2^2 + V_3^2 + \cdots}$.
>
> 4. **Confusing "Frequency" and "Amplitude" Distortion:** Frequency distortion is **linear** (no new frequencies). Amplitude distortion is **non-linear** (creates harmonics). This distinction is asked almost every semester.
>
> 5. **Forgetting $-3$ dB Reference:** At cutoff, gain drops to **$0.707$** or **$-3$ dB**, not $0.5$ or $-6$ dB.
>
> 6. **Rise Time-BW Formula:** Many students write $t_r \cdot BW = 0.35$ without units — it requires $t_r$ in **seconds** and BW in **Hz**.
>
> 7. **Decade vs. Octave Confusion:** $20$ dB/decade $= 6$ dB/octave. Mixing these up in problems leads to wrong answers.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Frequency Response and Distortion**

- **Frequency Response** = variation of gain (magnitude + phase) with frequency.

- **Three Regions:** Low-frequency, **Mid-band (flat gain)**, High-frequency.

- **Cutoff Frequencies:** $f_1$ (lower), $f_2$ (upper) — defined at **$-3$ dB** point.

- **Bandwidth:** $BW = f_2 - f_1$.

- **Voltage Gain in dB:** $A_v(dB) = 20\log(V_o/V_i)$.

- **Power Gain in dB:** $A_P(dB) = 10\log(P_o/P_i)$.

- **RC Cutoff Frequency:** $f_c = 1/(2\pi RC)$.

- **Roll-Off Rate:** $-20$ dB/decade per pole.

- **Rise Time-Bandwidth Product:** $t_r \cdot BW = 0.35$.

- **THD Formula:** $\sqrt{V_2^2 + V_3^2 + \cdots}/V_1 \times 100\%$.

- **Three Distortion Types:**
  - *Amplitude (Non-linear):* Creates harmonics.
  - *Frequency (Linear):* Gain varies with frequency.
  - *Phase (Delay):* Non-linear phase shift.

- **Linear vs. Non-linear Distortion:** Linear distortion changes amplitude/phase of existing frequencies; non-linear distortion creates new frequencies.

- **Q-point Selection:** Proper biasing minimizes amplitude distortion.

- **Negative Feedback:** Reduces all three types of distortion.

- **Miller Effect:** Multiplies $C_{bc}$ in CE amplifier, lowering upper cutoff frequency.

- **Butterworth Response:** Maximally flat magnitude response in passband.

- **Bode Plot:** Semi-log plot (frequency on log axis, gain in dB on linear axis).

- **Group Delay:** Must be constant for phase-distortionless system.

- **Phase at Cutoff:** $\pm 45°$ for single-pole RC filter.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Feedback Topologies and Its Effect on Input and Output Impedance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET302 - ELECTRONIC DEVICES AND CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Feedback Topologies and Its Effect on Input and Output Impedance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Feedback in Amplifiers

In the context of **Electronic Devices and Circuits (PCAET302)**, **feedback** refers to the process of returning a portion of the output signal of an amplifier back to its input terminal, where it is combined with the external input signal. This returned signal is called the **feedback signal**.

A **feedback topology** defines the specific method by which the output is *sampled* (voltage sampling or current sampling) and the manner in which the feedback signal is *mixed* (series mixing or shunt/parallel mixing) with the input source. The four canonical topologies are:

1. **Voltage-Series Feedback** (also called *Series-Shunt* or *Voltage-Series*)
2. **Voltage-Shunt Feedback** (also called *Shunt-Shunt* or *Voltage-Shunt*)
3. **Current-Series Feedback** (also called *Series-Series* or *Current-Series*)
4. **Current-Shunt Feedback** (also called *Shunt-Series* or *Current-Shunt*)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** A student must be able to identify the feedback topology, draw the block diagram, and **quantitatively determine the change in input and output impedance** for each of the four configurations. This is a guaranteed Part-B (14-mark) and Part-A (3-mark) question theme.

### 1.2 The Fundamental Closed-Loop Gain Equation

The general closed-loop gain of a feedback amplifier is given by the classical **Black's Feedback Equation**:

$$A_{f} = \dfrac{A}{1 + A\beta}$$

where:
- $A$ = **Open-loop gain** of the basic amplifier
- $\beta$ = **Feedback factor** (the fraction of output returned to input)
- $A\beta$ = **Loop gain** (a dimensionless scalar)
- $(1 + A\beta)$ = **Desensitivity factor** or **Return Difference**
- $A_{f}$ = **Closed-loop gain** (gain with feedback applied)

> [!NOTE]
> The quantity $(1 + A\beta)$ is the *single most important number* in feedback theory. It governs **gain stability, bandwidth extension, linearity improvement, noise reduction, AND impedance transformation** at both the input and output ports.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Steering Wheel

Imagine driving a car. The steering wheel is your input, and the road is your output. Without feedback, if a gust of wind pushes the car, it veers off course — and you, the driver, have no way of knowing until it's too late.

Now imagine adding a **feedback camera system** that shows you a small display of the road position. You constantly *sample* the output (where the car is heading), and you *mix* that information with your steering input to make small corrections. The result: **the car resists disturbances**.

In an electronic amplifier, the **sampling network** at the output "sees" the actual output (voltage or current), and the **mixing network** at the input combines this feedback with the original signal. The amplifier becomes:
- **Less sensitive** to component variations (e.g., temperature drift of $\beta$ of a BJT)
- **More predictable** — the closed-loop gain depends mainly on the passive feedback network, not on the active device parameters
- **More controllable** at its input and output ports (impedance changes)

### 1.4 Sampling and Mixing — The Two Fundamental Choices

| Mechanism | At the **Output Port** | At the **Input Port** |
|---|---|---|
| **Voltage / Shunt connection** | Voltage Sampling (parallel with load) | Shunt / Parallel Mixing (KCL at the node) |
| **Current / Series connection** | Current Sampling (series with load) | Series Mixing (KVL around the loop) |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid for KTU Exams:** 
> - **"Voltage sampling puts a voltmeter across the load."** → Reduces output impedance.
> - **"Current sampling puts an ammeter in series with the load."** → Increases output impedance.
> - **"Series mixing puts a voltage source in series with input."** → Increases input impedance.
> - **"Shunt mixing puts a current source in parallel with input."** → Decreases input impedance.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two-Port Block Diagram of a Feedback Amplifier

A general feedback amplifier can be represented by a **two-port network** with:
- A **basic amplifier (A)** with input port and output port
- A **feedback network (β)** that samples the output
- A **mixing junction (Σ)** that combines input and feedback at the input

> [!NOTE]
> In the *ideal* feedback model, the feedback network is **unilateral** (signal flows only from output to input) and **does not load** the basic amplifier. In the *practical* model, loading effects must be accounted for. KTU 2024 typically tests the ideal model unless specified.

### 2.2 The Closed-Loop Gain in Each Topology

Each feedback topology is associated with a different **"type of gain"** because the dimensions of the input and output quantities change:

| Topology | Sampling | Mixing | Ideal Source Type | Basic Gain $A$ | Feedback Gain $A_f$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Voltage-Series** | Voltage | Series | $V_{in}, V_{out}$ | Voltage Gain $A_V$ | $A_{Vf} = \dfrac{A_V}{1 + A_V \beta}$ |
| **Voltage-Shunt** | Voltage | Shunt | $I_{in}, V_{out}$ | Transresistance $A_R$ | $A_{Rf} = \dfrac{A_R}{1 + A_R \beta}$ |
| **Current-Series** | Current | Series | $V_{in}, I_{out}$ | Transconductance $A_G$ | $A_{Gf} = \dfrac{A_G}{1 + A_G \beta}$ |
| **Current-Shunt** | Current | Shunt | $I_{in}, I_{out}$ | Current Gain $A_I$ | $A_{If} = \dfrac{A_I}{1 + A_I \beta}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The dimensionless loop gain $A\beta$ must always be a **pure number**. Therefore, the units of $A$ and $1/\beta$ must match. For example, in voltage-shunt feedback, $A$ is in **ohms (Ω)** and $\beta$ is in **siemens (S = 1/Ω)**, making $A\beta$ dimensionless.

### 2.3 The KTU High-Yield Impedance Transformation Formulas

This is the **most important table** for the Module-3 examination. Memorize it completely.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Effect of Negative Feedback on Impedance:**
> - **Output port** depends on **sampling** (Voltage vs Current)
> - **Input port** depends on **mixing** (Series vs Shunt)
> - The factor is **always** $(1 + A\beta)$.

| Feedback Topology | Output Impedance $Z_{of}$ | Input Impedance $Z_{if}$ |
|---|---|---|
| **Voltage-Series** | $\dfrac{Z_o}{1 + A\beta}$ | $Z_i (1 + A\beta)$ |
| **Voltage-Shunt** | $\dfrac{Z_o}{1 + A\beta}$ | $\dfrac{Z_i}{1 + A\beta}$ |
| **Current-Series** | $Z_o (1 + A\beta)$ | $Z_i (1 + A\beta)$ |
| **Current-Shunt** | $Z_o (1 + A\beta)$ | $\dfrac{Z_i}{1 + A\beta}$ |

where $Z_i$ and $Z_o$ are the **open-loop** (without feedback) input and output impedances of the basic amplifier.

### 2.4 Logical Reasoning — Why These Results Hold

The underlying logic is remarkably simple:

**At the Input Port (Mixing Type):**

- **Series Mixing (KVL):** The feedback signal appears as a voltage in **opposition** to the source. The input current $I_i$ sees a *larger effective driving voltage* ($V_s = V_i + V_f$). Since the same current now requires a larger applied voltage, $Z_{if} = V_s/I_i > Z_i$. Hence $Z_{if} = Z_i(1+A\beta)$.

- **Shunt Mixing (KCL):** The feedback signal appears as a current at the input node. The source current $I_s$ is split between $I_i$ (entering amplifier) and $I_f$ (entering feedback network). For the same input voltage $V_i$, a *larger source current* is required. Hence $Z_{if} = V_i/I_s < Z_i$, and $Z_{if} = Z_i/(1+A\beta)$.

**At the Output Port (Sampling Type):**

- **Voltage Sampling:** The output is a near-ideal voltage source. The amplifier's Thevenin resistance is *reduced* because the feedback action corrects any deviation in $V_o$ by adjusting the input. Hence $Z_{of} = Z_o/(1+A\beta)$.

- **Current Sampling:** The output is a near-ideal current source. The amplifier's Norton resistance is *increased* because the feedback action corrects any deviation in $I_o$. Hence $Z_{of} = Z_o(1+A\beta)$.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Voltage-Series Feedback** is used in **non-inverting operational amplifiers** → high $Z_{in}$, low $Z_{out}$ — the ideal voltage amplifier.
- **Voltage-Shunt Feedback** is used in **inverting operational amplifiers** → low $Z_{in}$, low $Z_{out}$ — the ideal transresistance amplifier.
- **Current-Series Feedback** is used in **transistor stages with emitter degeneration** → high $Z_{in}$, high $Z_{out}$ — used in transconductance stages.
- **Current-Shunt Feedback** is used in **current-mirror biasing and active loads** → low $Z_{in}$, high $Z_{out}$ — used in current amplifiers.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The four derivations below are **exhaustive** and proceed step-by-step. Each algebraic transition is explicitly explained. These derivations are the **backbone of any 14-mark derivation question** in the KTU University Exam.

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Voltage-Series Feedback (Input Impedance $Z_{if}$)

Consider the small-signal model of the basic amplifier with open-loop input impedance $Z_i$. The series mixing gives:

**Step 1 — Apply KVL around the input loop:**

The source voltage $V_s$ drives the input. The feedback voltage $V_f$ opposes $V_s$ (for negative feedback):

$$V_s = V_i + V_f$$

**Step 2 — Express the feedback voltage in terms of output voltage:**

By definition of the voltage-sampling feedback network:

$$V_f = \beta \cdot V_o$$

**Step 3 — Express the output voltage in terms of input voltage:**

For the basic (open-loop) voltage amplifier:

$$V_o = A_V \cdot V_i$$

**Step 4 — Substitute to obtain the relationship between $V_s$ and $V_i$:**

$$V_s = V_i + \beta V_o = V_i + \beta A_V V_i = V_i \left(1 + A_V \beta\right)$$

**Step 5 — Compute the closed-loop input impedance:**

The input current $I_i$ is the same (since the mixing is in series — KVL). By Ohm's law for the open-loop input:

$$I_i = \dfrac{V_i}{Z_i}$$

Therefore:

$$Z_{if} = \dfrac{V_s}{I_i} = \dfrac{V_i(1 + A_V \beta)}{V_i / Z_i}$$

$$\boxed{Z_{if} = Z_i \left(1 + A_V \beta\right)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result interpretation:** The input impedance is **multiplied** by the desensitivity factor. Series mixing **always increases** $Z_{in}$ by a factor of $(1+A\beta)$.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Voltage-Shunt Feedback (Input Impedance $Z_{if}$)

For shunt (parallel) mixing, KCL applies at the input node.

**Step 1 — Apply KCL at the input node:**

The source current $I_s$ splits between the amplifier input current $I_i$ and the feedback current $I_f$:

$$I_s = I_i + I_f$$

**Step 2 — Express the feedback current in terms of output voltage:**

The feedback network samples the output voltage $V_o$ and produces a current into the input node:

$$I_f = \beta \cdot V_o$$

(Note: $\beta$ here has units of siemens, S.)

**Step 3 — Express the output voltage in terms of input current:**

For a transresistance amplifier, $V_o = A_R \cdot I_i$, where $A_R$ has units of ohms.

**Step 4 — Substitute and simplify:**

$$I_s = I_i + \beta A_R I_i = I_i \left(1 + A_R \beta\right)$$

**Step 5 — Compute the closed-loop input impedance:**

The input voltage $V_i$ is the same (parallel connection). With $V_i = I_i \cdot Z_i$:

$$Z_{if} = \dfrac{V_i}{I_s} = \dfrac{I_i Z_i}{I_i(1 + A_R \beta)}$$

$$\boxed{Z_{if} = \dfrac{Z_i}{1 + A_R \beta}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result interpretation:** Shunt mixing **always decreases** $Z_{in}$ by a factor of $(1+A\beta)$. The amplifier behaves as an ideal current-sensing input port.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Voltage Sampling (Output Impedance $Z_{of}$)

The output port of the basic amplifier is modeled as a Thevenin source $A \cdot V_i$ in series with $Z_o$, with the load $Z_L$ connected across the output.

**Step 1 — Write the open-loop output equation:**

The output voltage with the basic amplifier alone:

$$V_o = A \cdot V_i - I_o \cdot Z_o$$

where $I_o$ is the current delivered to the load.

**Step 2 — Include the feedback action (series mixing):**

With series mixing, $V_i = V_s - V_f = V_s - \beta V_o$. Substituting:

$$V_o = A(V_s - \beta V_o) - I_o Z_o$$

**Step 3 — Collect the $V_o$ terms:**

$$V_o + A\beta V_o = A V_s - I_o Z_o$$

$$V_o (1 + A\beta) = A V_s - I_o Z_o$$

$$V_o = \dfrac{A V_s}{1 + A\beta} - \dfrac{I_o Z_o}{1 + A\beta}$$

**Step 4 — Identify the new Thevenin equivalent:**

This is in the standard Thevenin form $V_o = V_{th} - I_o Z_{of}$, where:

$$Z_{of} = \dfrac{Z_o}{1 + A\beta}$$

$$\boxed{Z_{of} = \dfrac{Z_o}{1 + A\beta}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result interpretation:** Voltage sampling **always decreases** $Z_{out}$ by a factor of $(1+A\beta)$. The amplifier approaches an **ideal voltage source** as $A\beta \to \infty$.

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Current Sampling (Output Impedance $Z_{of}$)

The output port is now modeled as a Norton source $A \cdot V_i$ in parallel with $Z_o$ (or equivalently, a current source $I_{sc}$ and parallel $Z_o$). The feedback samples the output current $I_o$.

**Step 1 — Write the open-loop Norton output equation:**

The short-circuit current at the output (or the Norton current source) is $A \cdot V_i$ (in current sampling, the basic amplifier's "gain" $A$ has units of siemens, A/V). The Norton form gives:

$$I_o = A V_i - \dfrac{V_o}{Z_o}$$

**Step 2 — Include the feedback action (current sampling, series mixing at input):**

For current-series topology, $V_i = V_s - \beta I_o$ (feedback voltage opposes source voltage). Substituting:

$$I_o = A(V_s - \beta I_o) - \dfrac{V_o}{Z_o}$$

**Step 3 — Collect the $I_o$ terms:**

$$I_o + A\beta I_o = A V_s - \dfrac{V_o}{Z_o}$$

$$I_o (1 + A\beta) = A V_s - \dfrac{V_o}{Z_o}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $I_o$ in terms of $V_o$ (with $V_s = 0$ to find $Z_{of}$):**

Setting $V_s = 0$ (the source is deactivated to find the output impedance):

$$I_o (1 + A\beta) = -\dfrac{V_o}{Z_o}$$

$$\dfrac{V_o}{I_o} = -Z_o (1 + A\beta)$$

The negative sign indicates the assumed direction; taking the magnitude:

$$\boxed{Z_{of} = Z_o (1 + A\beta)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result interpretation:** Current sampling **always increases** $Z_{out}$ by a factor of $(1+A\beta)$. The amplifier approaches an **ideal current source** as $A\beta \to \infty$.

### 3.5 Consolidated Analytical Matrix

For the final quick-reference, the **universal impedance transformation rule** is summarized as:

$$Z_{if} = Z_i \cdot (1+A\beta)^{\pm 1}, \qquad Z_{of} = Z_o \cdot (1+A\beta)^{\pm 1}$$

where the exponent is **$+1$** if feedback *increases* impedance (series mixing at input, current sampling at output) and **$-1$** if feedback *decreases* impedance (shunt mixing at input, voltage sampling at output).

| Topology | Mixing | Sampling | $Z_{if}$ Sign | $Z_{of}$ Sign |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Voltage-Series | Series ↑ | Voltage ↓ | $\times$ | $\div$ |
| Voltage-Shunt | Shunt ↓ | Voltage ↓ | $\div$ | $\div$ |
| Current-Series | Series ↑ | Current ↑ | $\times$ | $\times$ |
| Current-Shunt | Shunt ↓ | Current ↑ | $\div$ | $\times$ |

### 4.3 Signal Flow Graph Representation (Mason's Gain Formula View)

The signal-flow graph makes the loop-gain $A\beta$ appear explicitly as a closed loop. This is the preferred representation for deriving closed-loop gain using **Mason's rule**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Nin[NIN Input Node] -->|forward path = A| Nout[NOUT Output Node]
    Nout -->|feedback path = Beta| Nin
    Nin((NIN)) --- Nout((NOUT))

    classDef nodeStyle fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000,stroke-width:2px
    class Nin,Nout nodeStyle
```

In this graph:
- **Forward path gain** $P_1 = A$
- **Single loop gain** $L_1 = A\beta$
- **Closed-loop transfer function** $T = \dfrac{P_1}{1 - L_1} = \dfrac{A}{1 - A\beta}$

For **negative** feedback, $\beta$ is taken as a negative quantity (since the feedback signal opposes the input), so $1 - A\beta = 1 + A\beta$, recovering **Black's equation** $A_f = A/(1+A\beta)$.

### 4.4 Impedance Transformation Flow

The following architecture flow summarizes the **decision tree** a student should follow in the exam when identifying which impedance-transformation formula to apply.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Start: Identify Topology]) --> Q1{Output Sampling}
    Q1 -->|Voltage across load| Volt[Voltage Sampling]
    Q1 -->|Current through load| Cur[Current Sampling]

    Volt --> Q2V{Input Mixing}
    Q2V -->|Series KVL| VSm[Voltage Series]
    Q2V -->|Shunt KCL| VSh[Voltage Shunt]

    Cur --> Q2C{Input Mixing}
    Q2C -->|Series KVL| CSm[Current Series]
    Q2C -->|Shunt KCL| CSh[Current Shunt]

    VSm --> RV1[ZIF Increases ZI times 1+ABeta]
    VSm --> RO1[ZOF Decreases ZO over 1+ABeta]

    VSh --> RV2[ZIF Decreases ZI over 1+ABeta]
    VSh --> RO2[ZOF Decreases ZO over 1+ABeta]

    CSm --> RV3[ZIF Increases ZI times 1+ABeta]
    CSm --> RO3[ZOF Increases ZO times 1+ABeta]

    CSh --> RV4[ZIF Decreases ZI over 1+ABeta]
    CSh --> RO4[ZOF Increases ZO times 1+ABeta]

    classDef startnode fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
    classDef result fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,color:#000

    class Start startnode

```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Paper]**

**Define negative feedback. List the four types of feedback topologies and state how each one affects the input and output impedance of an amplifier.**

**Model Answer:**

**Negative feedback** is the process of returning a portion of the output signal of an amplifier to its input in **anti-phase** (180° out of phase) with the applied input signal, so as to reduce the overall gain but improve other characteristics such as stability, bandwidth, and linearity.

The four basic feedback topologies and their impedance effects are:

| Topology | $Z_{if}$ | $Z_{of}$ |
|---|---|---|
| Voltage-Series | $Z_i(1 + A\beta)$ | $Z_o / (1 + A\beta)$ |
| Voltage-Shunt | $Z_i / (1 + A\beta)$ | $Z_o / (1 + A\beta)$ |
| Current-Series | $Z_i(1 + A\beta)$ | $Z_o(1 + A\beta)$ |
| Current-Shunt | $Z_i / (1 + A\beta)$ | $Z_o(1 + A\beta)$ |

The general rule: **Series mixing at input → $Z_{in}$ increases; Shunt mixing at input → $Z_{in}$ decreases; Voltage sampling at output → $Z_{out}$ decreases; Current sampling at output → $Z_{out}$ increases**, with the factor being the desensitivity factor $(1+A\beta)$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary Exam]**

**What is the "desensitivity factor" in a feedback amplifier? Why is it called so? What is its role in impedance transformation?**

**Model Answer:**

The **desensitivity factor** is the quantity $(1 + A\beta)$, where $A$ is the open-loop gain and $\beta$ is the feedback factor.

It is called "desensitivity" because it represents the factor by which the amplifier's sensitivity to variations in its internal parameters (e.g., transistor $\beta$, temperature, supply voltage) is **reduced** in the closed-loop configuration. The fractional change in closed-loop gain is:

$$\dfrac{dA_f}{A_f} = \dfrac{1}{1 + A\beta} \cdot \dfrac{dA}{A}$$

In impedance transformation, the same factor $(1+A\beta)$ governs both the input and output impedance changes:
- **Input impedance** is multiplied by $(1+A\beta)$ for series mixing, and divided by $(1+A\beta)$ for shunt mixing.
- **Output impedance** is multiplied by $(1+A\beta)$ for current sampling, and divided by $(1+A\beta)$ for voltage sampling.

Thus, $(1+A\beta)$ is the **universal scaling factor** that quantifies how strongly feedback modifies the port impedances.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, the student must answer **either Question A or Question B**. Each question is split into **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks** sub-parts.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Voltage-Series Feedback Analysis

**[a] (7 Marks):** A non-inverting amplifier has open-loop voltage gain $A = 10^4$, feedback factor $\beta = 0.01$, input impedance $Z_i = 10\text{ k}\Omega$, and output impedance $Z_o = 100\text{ }\Omega$. Find the closed-loop voltage gain $A_{Vf}$, the closed-loop input impedance $Z_{if}$, and the closed-loop output impedance $Z_{of}$.

**[b] (7 Marks):** Derive the expression for closed-loop input impedance $Z_{if}$ and closed-loop output impedance $Z_{of}$ for a **voltage-series feedback amplifier**, clearly stating the assumptions made.

---

**Model Solution to A(a):**

**Step 1 — Compute the desensitivity factor:**

$$1 + A\beta = 1 + (10^4)(0.01) = 1 + 100 = 101$$

**Step 2 — Closed-loop voltage gain:**

$$A_{Vf} = \dfrac{A}{1 + A\beta} = \dfrac{10^4}{101} \approx 99.01$$

This is close to $1/\beta = 100$, confirming that with high loop gain, $A_{Vf} \approx 1/\beta$.

**Step 3 — Closed-loop input impedance (series mixing → increases):**

$$Z_{if} = Z_i (1 + A\beta) = (10 \text{ k}\Omega)(101) = 1010 \text{ k}\Omega = 1.01 \text{ M}\Omega$$

**Step 4 — Closed-loop output impedance (voltage sampling → decreases):**

$$Z_{of} = \dfrac{Z_o}{1 + A\beta} = \dfrac{100\text{ }\Omega}{101} \approx 0.990\text{ }\Omega$$

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight:** The output impedance dropped from $100\text{ }\Omega$ to under $1\text{ }\Omega$, making this amplifier behave as a near-ideal voltage source — exactly what is required to drive a low-impedance speaker load without signal loss.

---

**Model Solution to A(b):**

**Assumptions:**
1. The feedback network is **unilateral** and **does not load** the basic amplifier.
2. The feedback signal is **subtracted** from the source (negative feedback).
3. The amplifier operates in the **linear region**.

**Derivation of $Z_{if}$ (Input Impedance):**

Applying KVL at the input loop (series mixing):

$$V_s = V_i + V_f$$

For voltage sampling, $V_f = \beta V_o$, and for the basic amplifier, $V_o = A V_i$. Substituting:

$$V_s = V_i + \beta (A V_i) = V_i (1 + A\beta)$$

Since the input current is unchanged ($I_i = V_i / Z_i$):

$$Z_{if} = \dfrac{V_s}{I_i} = \dfrac{V_i (1 + A\beta)}{V_i / Z_i} = Z_i (1 + A\beta)$$

$$\boxed{Z_{if} = Z_i (1 + A\beta)}$$

**Derivation of $Z_{of}$ (Output Impedance):**

The output of the basic amplifier is a Thevenin equivalent: $V_{th} = A V_i$ in series with $Z_o$. With series mixing, $V_i = V_s - \beta V_o$. The output equation becomes:

$$V_o = A(V_s - \beta V_o) - I_o Z_o$$

$$V_o(1 + A\beta) = A V_s - I_o Z_o$$

$$V_o = \dfrac{A V_s}{1 + A\beta} - I_o \cdot \dfrac{Z_o}{1 + A\beta}$$

Comparing with $V_o = V_{th}' - I_o Z_{of}$:

$$\boxed{Z_{of} = \dfrac{Z_o}{1 + A\beta}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Current-Shunt Feedback Analysis

**[a] (7 Marks):** A current-shunt feedback amplifier has open-loop current gain $A_I = 500$, feedback factor $\beta = 0.05$, input impedance $Z_i = 2\text{ k}\Omega$, and output impedance $Z_o = 20\text{ k}\Omega$. Calculate the closed-loop current gain $A_{If}$, the closed-loop input impedance $Z_{if}$, and the closed-loop output impedance $Z_{of}$.

**[b] (7 Marks):** Explain the architecture of a **current-shunt feedback amplifier** with a neat block diagram, and derive the expressions for closed-loop input and output impedance.

---

**Model Solution to B(a):**

**Step 1 — Desensitivity factor:**

$$1 + A_I \beta = 1 + (500)(0.05) = 1 + 25 = 26$$

**Step 2 — Closed-loop current gain:**

$$A_{If} = \dfrac{A_I}{1 + A_I \beta} = \dfrac{500}{26} \approx 19.23$$

**Step 3 — Closed-loop input impedance (shunt mixing → decreases):**

$$Z_{if} = \dfrac{Z_i}{1 + A_I \beta} = \dfrac{2 \text{ k}\Omega}{26} \approx 76.92 \text{ }\Omega$$

**Step 4 — Closed-loop output impedance (current sampling → increases):**

$$Z_{of} = Z_o (1 + A_I \beta) = (20 \text{ k}\Omega)(26) = 520 \text{ k}\Omega$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** The output impedance jumped from $20\text{ k}\Omega$ to $520\text{ k}\Omega$. This makes the current-shunt feedback amplifier behave as a **near-ideal current source** — perfect for driving a high-impedance load like an LED string or a current-mode transducer.

---

**Model Solution to B(b):**

**Architecture of a Current-Shunt Feedback Amplifier:**

In this topology, the output **current** is sampled by placing the feedback network in **series** with the load. The sampled current produces a current that is fed back to the input node, where it is **summed (shunt)** with the input source current.

**Derivation of $Z_{if}$ (Shunt Mixing):**

Apply KCL at the input node:

$$I_s = I_i + I_f$$

For current sampling, $I_f = \beta I_o$, and for the basic amplifier, $I_o = A_I I_i$. Substituting:

$$I_s = I_i + \beta (A_I I_i) = I_i (1 + A_I \beta)$$

The input voltage $V_i = I_i Z_i$, so:

$$Z_{if} = \dfrac{V_i}{I_s} = \dfrac{I_i Z_i}{I_i(1 + A_I \beta)}$$

$$\boxed{Z_{if} = \dfrac{Z_i}{1 + A_I \beta}}$$

**Derivation of $Z_{of}$ (Current Sampling):**

The output of the basic amplifier is a Norton equivalent: current source $A_I I_i$ in parallel with $Z_o$. With shunt mixing at the input, $I_i = I_s - I_f = I_s - \beta I_o$. The output Norton equation is:

$$I_o = A_I I_i - \dfrac{V_o}{Z_o} = A_I (I_s - \beta I_o) - \dfrac{V_o}{Z_o}$$

$$I_o (1 + A_I \beta) = A_I I_s - \dfrac{V_o}{Z_o}$$

$$I_o = \dfrac{A_I I_s}{1 + A_I \beta} - \dfrac{V_o}{Z_o(1 + A_I \beta)}$$

Comparing with $I_o = I_{th}' - V_o / Z_{of}'$:

$$\boxed{Z_{of} = Z_o (1 + A_I \beta)}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 
> 1. **Mixing up the "type of gain"**: In voltage-shunt feedback, $A$ is a transresistance (in **ohms**), not a voltage gain. Students often blindly plug in $A_V$ from a voltage amplifier's spec sheet and get dimensionally wrong answers. **Always check the units.**
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the sign of feedback**: The desensitivity factor is $(1 + A\beta)$ for **negative** feedback. If the problem says *positive* feedback (oscillator condition), the formula becomes $A/(1 - A\beta)$ and the impedance formulas change signs/behavior.
> 
> 3. **Confusing sampling with mixing**: "Series" at the *input* is a mixing term, but "Series" at the *output* is a sampling term. Read the topology name carefully: "Voltage-Series" means **Voltage-sampling at output + Series-mixing at input**.
> 
> 4. **Forgetting to compute the closed-loop gain first**: Many numerical problems give you $A$ and $\beta$ and ask for $Z_{if}, Z_{of}$. You must compute $(1+A\beta)$ **before** applying the impedance formula.
> 
> 5. **Mixing up $Z_o$ with $Z_L$**: $Z_o$ is the amplifier's *output* impedance (open-loop); $Z_L$ is the *load*. They are different quantities. The closed-loop output impedance formula $Z_{of} = Z_o/(1+A\beta)$ does **not** include $Z_L$.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- **Black's Equation** (the master equation): $A_f = \dfrac{A}{1 + A\beta}$
- **Desensitivity Factor** = $(1 + A\beta)$ — governs **everything** in negative feedback.
- **Four Topologies** to memorize: Voltage-Series, Voltage-Shunt, Current-Series, Current-Shunt.
- **Sampling rule**: Voltage sampling $\Rightarrow Z_{out} \downarrow$ (divide by $1+A\beta$). Current sampling $\Rightarrow Z_{out} \uparrow$ (multiply by $1+A\beta$).
- **Mixing rule**: Series mixing $\Rightarrow Z_{in} \uparrow$ (multiply by $1+A\beta$). Shunt mixing $\Rightarrow Z_{in} \downarrow$ (divide by $1+A\beta$).
- **Type of gain for each topology**: Voltage-Series → $A_V$; Voltage-Shunt → $A_R$ (Ω); Current-Series → $A_G$ (S); Current-Shunt → $A_I$.
- **$A\beta$ is always dimensionless** — the units of $A$ and $1/\beta$ must match.
- **Negative feedback** sacrifices gain for stability; $(1+A\beta)$ is the "price" paid in gain and the "reward" in linearity, bandwidth, and impedance control.
- **Two-port ideal models** assume **unilateral feedback network with no loading**; in practical circuits, loading must be absorbed into the basic amplifier $A$ block.
- **KTU-specific tip**: For a 14-mark derivation, always **state assumptions**, **draw the block diagram**, **label the sampling and mixing nodes explicitly**, and **show every algebraic step**. A direct jump from question to final answer will cost at least 3–4 marks.
- **Common exam traps**: positive feedback (use $1 - A\beta$); ideal vs. practical feedback network loading; $Z_i, Z_o$ vs. $Z_{if}, Z_{of}$ confusion; dimensional mismatch in $A\beta$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Feedback Amplifier Circuits Using BJT in Each Feedback Topologies (Analysis of Only Voltage Series Feedback Circuit is Required)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET302 - ELECTRONIC DEVICES AND CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Feedback Amplifier Circuits Using BJT in Each Feedback Topologies (Analysis of Only Voltage Series Feedback Circuit is Required)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Feedback Amplifier?

A **Feedback Amplifier** is an amplifier in which a portion of the output signal is returned (fed back) to the input and combined with the input signal. This returned signal may either *aid* (positive feedback) or *oppose* (negative feedback) the original input signal. In analog electronic circuit design, **negative feedback** is overwhelmingly used because it dramatically improves amplifier performance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> *A feedback amplifier is a two-port network consisting of a basic amplifier (A) with gain A and a feedback network (β) which samples the output and returns a fraction β of it to the input. The closed-loop transfer function is Af = A / (1 + Aβ), where (1 + Aβ) is called the **return difference** or **desensitivity factor**.*

## 1.2 The Four Feedback Topologies

Feedback amplifiers are classified based on **two independent criteria**:

| Criterion | Option A | Option B |
|---|---|---|
| **Quantity Sampled at Output** | **Voltage** (Shunt connection at output) | **Current** (Series connection at output) |
| **Quantity Mixed at Input** | **Voltage** (Series connection at input) | **Current** (Shunt connection at input) |

This gives the four canonical topologies:

1. **Voltage-Series Feedback** (also called *Series-Shunt* or *Voltage Amplifier*) — *Focus of this module*
2. **Voltage-Shunt Feedback** (also called *Shunt-Shunt* or *Transresistance Amplifier*)
3. **Current-Series Feedback** (also called *Series-Series* or *Transconductance Amplifier*)
4. **Current-Shunt Feedback** (also called *Shunt-Series* or *Current Amplifier*)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mandate:** Only **Voltage-Series Feedback** requires detailed quantitative analysis. The other three topologies need only block-diagram level understanding and identification.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The Cruise Control of Cars 🚗

Think of a **car's cruise control system** as a perfect analogy for negative feedback:

- **Desired Speed (Reference Input):** You set 80 km/h.
- **Actual Speed (Output):** The car is currently at 75 km/h.
- **Error Detector (Mixer):** The system finds the *difference* between desired and actual speed.
- **Controller (Basic Amplifier):** Applies more accelerator.
- **Sensor (Feedback Network):** Measures the wheel speed and sends it back.

If the car climbs a hill and slows to 70 km/h, the feedback system **automatically increases throttle** to bring it back to 80 km/h. The amplifier does exactly the same: any drift in output (due to temperature, transistor β variation, or noise) is **corrected automatically** by the feedback loop. The result is a **stable, predictable, and accurate** system — but slightly *slower* (reduced gain) in exchange for the huge stability benefits.

## 1.4 Why Do We Use Negative Feedback? (The 5 Golden Benefits)

1. **Gain Desensitization** — Closed-loop gain depends *only* on passive feedback components, not on the active device.
2. **Input Impedance Modification** — Series mixing **increases** Rif; shunt mixing **decreases** Rif.
3. **Output Impedance Modification** — Voltage sampling **decreases** Rof; current sampling **increases** Rof.
4. **Bandwidth Extension** — Bandwidth increases by the factor (1 + Aβ).
5. **Reduction of Nonlinear Distortion and Noise** — Reduced by the factor (1 + Aβ).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Block Diagram of a Generic Feedback Amplifier

The fundamental signal-flow representation of a feedback amplifier consists of two blocks and a summing junction:

```
        Vi        +→ ( A ) → Vo
                  ↑ −
                  |
                  β
                  |
                  +←——————————————+
```

**Where:**
- **A** = Open-loop gain of the basic amplifier (without feedback)
- **β** = Feedback factor (the fraction of output fed back to input)
- **Vs** = Source signal
- **Vi** = Net input to the basic amplifier (error signal)
- **Vf** = Feedback signal = β·Vo
- **Vo** = Output signal

## 2.2 The Fundamental Closed-Loop Gain Equation

The basic operating principle of negative feedback:

> **Step 1:** The feedback network samples the output and produces: $V_f = \beta \cdot V_o$

> **Step 2:** The mixer (summing junction) computes the error signal: $V_i = V_s - V_f$

> **Step 3:** The basic amplifier amplifies the error: $V_o = A \cdot V_i$

> **Step 4:** Substituting (2) into (3): $V_o = A(V_s - \beta V_o)$

> **Step 5:** Solving for Vo/Vs: This gives the **closed-loop gain**.

## 2.3 Identification of Voltage-Series Feedback Topology

A **Voltage-Series Feedback** amplifier is identified by these two physical features:

| Connection Point | Method | What it Does |
|---|---|---|
| **At the Output** | Output **voltage** is sampled by connecting the feedback network in **parallel (shunt)** with the load | Stabilizes the output **voltage** (acts as ideal voltage source) |
| **At the Input** | Feedback **voltage** is injected in **series** (KVL loop) with the source | Acts as a voltage-controlled voltage source (VCVS) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical Identification Rule:** If you can disconnect the load RL and the output voltage remains essentially unchanged → it's **voltage sampling**. If disconnecting the source still allows feedback to flow into a current-summing node → it's **shunt mixing**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) — Voltage-Series Feedback

| # | Parameter | Formula | Effect of Negative Feedback |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Open-loop gain | $A = \dfrac{V_o}{V_i}$ | — |
| 2 | Feedback factor | $\beta = \dfrac{V_f}{V_o}$ | Dimensionless |
| 3 | Loop gain | $A\beta$ | Dimensionless |
| 4 | Return difference | $(1 + A\beta)$ | $\gg 1$ for deep feedback |
| 5 | Closed-loop gain | $A_f = \dfrac{A}{1 + A\beta}$ | **Decreases** |
| 6 | Input resistance (series mixing) | $R_{if} = R_i(1 + A\beta)$ | **Increases** |
| 7 | Output resistance (voltage sampling) | $R_{of} = \dfrac{R_o}{1 + A\beta}$ | **Decreases** |
| 8 | Bandwidth (B.W.) | $(BW)_f = (BW)(1 + A\beta)$ | **Increases** |
| 9 | Gain-Bandwidth Product | $GBP_f = GBP$ (invariant) | **Constant** |
| 10 | Nonlinear distortion | $D_f = \dfrac{D}{1 + A\beta}$ | **Decreases** |
| 11 | Noise figure | $NF_f = 1 + \dfrac{NF - 1}{1 + A\beta}$ | **Decreases** |
| 12 | Voltage gain in dB | $A_f(\text{dB}) = 20 \log_{10}\vert A_f \vert$ | Used in problems |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Voltage-series feedback is the *most common* topology in audio pre-amplifiers, op-amp input stages, and instrumentation amplifiers because it presents a **high input impedance** (does not load the source) and a **low output impedance** (drives the load stiffly). It is, in effect, an ideal **Voltage-Controlled Voltage Source (VCVS)**.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Audio Power Amplifiers** — Emitter resistor RE in a CE stage provides series mixing.
- **Operational Amplifiers** — The 741 op-amp uses voltage-series feedback at the input differential pair.
- **Instrumentation Amplifiers** — Use voltage-series feedback for high CMRR.
- **Voltage Regulators** — Series-pass transistors use voltage-series feedback to regulate output.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Closed-Loop Gain $A_f = \dfrac{A}{1 + A\beta}$

This is the *single most important* derivation in this module. Every other feedback equation is a logical extension of this.

**Given:**

$$V_i = V_s - V_f$$

$$V_f = \beta \cdot V_o$$

$$V_o = A \cdot V_i$$

**Step 1:** Substitute $V_f = \beta V_o$ into the mixer equation:

$$V_i = V_s - \beta V_o$$

**Step 2:** Substitute this $V_i$ into the amplifier equation $V_o = A \cdot V_i$:

$$V_o = A \cdot (V_s - \beta V_o)$$

**Step 3:** Distribute the multiplication on the right-hand side:

$$V_o = A \cdot V_s - A \cdot \beta \cdot V_o$$

**Step 4:** Move the term $A\beta V_o$ to the left-hand side to group all $V_o$ terms:

$$V_o + A \cdot \beta \cdot V_o = A \cdot V_s$$

**Step 5:** Factor out $V_o$ on the left side:

$$V_o \cdot (1 + A\beta) = A \cdot V_s$$

**Step 6:** Solve for the ratio $V_o / V_s$, which is the closed-loop gain $A_f$:

$$A_f = \frac{V_o}{V_s} = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The factor $(1 + A\beta)$ is called the **amount of feedback** in dB. It governs *all* performance changes. If $A\beta \gg 1$ (deep feedback), then $A_f \approx 1/\beta$, meaning the closed-loop gain is determined entirely by the **passive** feedback network — independent of transistor $\beta$, temperature, and supply variations.

## 3.2 Derivation of Input Resistance with Series Mixing ($R_{if}$)

**Conceptual Setup:** In voltage-series feedback, the feedback voltage $V_f$ appears in **series** with the source $V_s$, in the input loop (KVL).

**Step 1:** Define the open-loop input resistance as seen by $V_i$:

$$R_i = \frac{V_i}{I_i}$$

**Step 2:** With feedback, the input loop equation is (KVL around the source-feedback loop):

$$V_s = I_i \cdot R_i + V_f = I_i \cdot R_i + \beta \cdot V_o$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $V_o = A \cdot V_i = A \cdot (I_i R_i)$ into the equation:

$$V_s = I_i \cdot R_i + \beta \cdot A \cdot I_i \cdot R_i$$

**Step 4:** Factor out $I_i \cdot R_i$:

$$V_s = I_i \cdot R_i \cdot (1 + A\beta)$$

**Step 5:** Define the closed-loop input resistance $R_{if} = V_s / I_i$:

$$R_{if} = \frac{V_s}{I_i} = R_i \cdot (1 + A\beta)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
