> **Physical Interpretation:** The input current $I_i$ required from the source is reduced by the factor $(1 + A\beta)$, so the *effective* input impedance is **boosted**. This is a huge benefit when amplifying weak signals from high-impedance sources like crystal microphones or piezoelectric sensors.

## 3.3 Derivation of Output Resistance with Voltage Sampling ($R_{of}$)

**Conceptual Setup:** Voltage sampling means the amplifier behaves like an ideal voltage source — the output resistance must be **low**.

**Step 1:** Set the source $V_s = 0$ (independent source deactivated, but the dependent source inside A remains active).

**Step 2:** Apply a test voltage $V_t$ at the output terminals and measure the resulting test current $I_t$:

$$R_{of} = \frac{V_t}{I_t}$$

**Step 3:** The output current $I_t$ has two components:
- Current through the open-loop output resistance $R_o$
- Current delivered by the amplifier's dependent source

**Step 4:** The voltage at the amplifier input (due to feedback) is:

$$V_i = -\beta \cdot V_t \quad \text{(because } V_s = 0 \text{ and } V_f = \beta V_t \text{, so } V_i = 0 - \beta V_t)$$

**Step 5:** The amplifier's dependent source produces: $A \cdot V_i = -A\beta V_t$

**Step 6:** Apply KCL at the output node:

$$I_t = \frac{V_t}{R_o} - \frac{A\beta V_t}{R_o} = \frac{V_t (1 + A\beta)}{R_o}$$

> Wait — for voltage sampling with a *dependent voltage source* A·Vi, the standard approach is slightly different. The Thevenin equivalent of the output is:

**Step 7:** The open-circuit output voltage (with feedback) when driven by $V_s$ is:

$$V_{o,oc} = A_f \cdot V_s = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta} \cdot V_s$$

**Step 8:** The Thevenin output resistance (looking back with $V_s = 0$) is:

$$R_{of} = \frac{R_o}{1 + A\beta}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Interpretation:** A low $R_{of}$ means the output voltage is **stiff** — it does not sag when the load current increases. This makes the voltage-series feedback amplifier behave like an **ideal voltage source** with $R_o \to 0$.

## 3.4 Derivation of Bandwidth Extension

The open-loop gain of a single-stage amplifier falls as:

$$A(j\omega) = \frac{A_{mid}}{1 + j(f/f_H)}$$

**Step 1:** The magnitude at any frequency is: $\vert A(jf) \vert = \dfrac{A_{mid}}{\sqrt{1 + (f/f_H)^2}}$

**Step 2:** With negative feedback, the closed-loop gain is:

$$A_f(jf) = \frac{A(jf)}{1 + A(jf)\beta}$$

**Step 3:** Substituting $A(jf)$:

$$A_f(jf) = \frac{A_{mid}/(1 + jf/f_H)}{1 + \beta \cdot A_{mid}/(1 + jf/f_H)}$$

**Step 4:** Multiply numerator and denominator by $(1 + jf/f_H)$:

$$A_f(jf) = \frac{A_{mid}}{1 + jf/f_H + \beta A_{mid}} = \frac{A_{mid}/(1 + A_{mid}\beta)}{1 + jf/[f_H(1 + A_{mid}\beta)]}$$

**Step 5:** Recognize the standard form $A_f / (1 + jf/f_{Hf})$:

$$A_{f,mid} = \frac{A_{mid}}{1 + A_{mid}\beta}, \quad f_{Hf} = f_H \cdot (1 + A_{mid}\beta)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBP) Invariance:**
> $GBP_f = A_{f,mid} \cdot f_{Hf} = \dfrac{A_{mid}}{1 + A_{mid}\beta} \cdot f_H(1 + A_{mid}\beta) = A_{mid} \cdot f_H = GBP$
> This is a **fundamental trade-off** — you exchange gain for bandwidth while keeping GBP constant.

## 3.5 BJT Implementation of Voltage-Series Feedback Amplifier

The most common BJT realization of voltage-series feedback is the **CE amplifier with unbypassed emitter resistor** combined with a **collector-side voltage divider**:

```
        Rc
    +Vcc ┌──┬──┐
          │  │  │
          R1 │  │
          │  │  │
          ├──┤  │
          │  │  │
          B  C  │    RL
   Vs ──Rs──┤   ├──┐
              E  │   │
              │  │   │
              Re ├───┘
              │  │ (unbypassed)
              ├──┘
              │
             GND
```

**How it works:**

- The collector output $V_o$ is taken across the parallel combination of $R_C \Vert R_L$.
- The voltage divider $(R_1, R_2)$ is **not** present in this simple form. Instead, the emitter resistor $R_E$ itself acts as the **series-mixing element** because the emitter voltage follows: $V_e = I_e \cdot R_E \approx I_c \cdot R_E$.
- The feedback voltage is $V_f = V_e$, which appears in series with $V_{be}$: $V_{be} = V_s - V_f$.

**Hybrid-π small-signal analysis gives the closed-loop voltage gain:**

$$A_f = \frac{-g_m R_C'}{1 + g_m R_E} \approx \frac{-R_C'}{R_E} \quad \text{when } g_m R_E \gg 1$$

**Input resistance:**

$$R_{if} = r_\pi + (1 + \beta_{ac}) R_E \approx \beta_{ac} R_E$$

**Output resistance:**

$$R_{of} \approx R_C$$

The voltage divider version (true voltage sampling) is built as:

$$V_f = V_o \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}, \quad \beta = \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Generic Feedback Amplifier Block Diagram

This is the foundational block diagram for *all* four feedback topologies.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcNode[Vs Source Signal] --> sumMixer{{"Summing Junction: Vs - Vf"}}
    sumMixer --> |Vi error signal| basicAmp[/"Basic Amplifier Gain A"/]
    basicAmp --> outLoad[/"Vo Output to Load"/]
    outLoad --> |sampled output| fbNet[/"Feedback Network Beta"/]
    fbNet --> |Vf feedback signal| sumMixer
```

## 4.2 Voltage-Series Feedback Block Diagram (Detailed)

In voltage-series feedback, the **mixer is a KVL loop** and the **sampler is a parallel (shunt) connection at the output**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sourceNode[Vs] -->|series loop| inputLoop((Input KVL Loop))
    inputLoop -->|Vi| ampBlock[/"A: Basic CE Amplifier"/]
    ampBlock -->|Vo| outputNode[Output Node]
    outputNode -->|parallel sample| fbDivider[/"Voltage Divider: R1, R2"/]
    fbDivider -->|Vf| inputLoop
    outputNode --> loadRl[RL]

    style inputLoop fill:#FFE5B4
    style fbDivider fill:#B4D8FF
```

## 4.3 BJT Voltage-Series Feedback Amplifier — Functional Architecture

The BJT-level circuit showing how emitter resistor RE provides series mixing.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    vccNode[+Vcc Supply] --> rcResistor[Rc Collector Resistor]
    rcResistor --> collectorNode[Collector of BJT]
    collectorNode --> outTerminal[Vo Output Terminal]
    outTerminal --> loadBranch[RL Load]
    loadBranch --> gndNode1[Ground]

    biasR1[R1 Upper Bias] --> baseNode[Base of BJT]
    biasR2[R2 Lower Bias] --> gndNode2[Ground]
    baseNode --> couplingCin[Coupling Capacitor Cin]
    couplingCin --> inputSig[Vs Input Signal Source]

    emitterNode[Emitter of BJT] --> reResistor[RE Emitter Resistor]
    reResistor --> gndNode3[Ground]

    outputNode[Vo from Collector] -.feedback path.-> emitterNode
```

**Key observations from the diagram:**

- The **input loop** is a KVL loop: $V_s = V_{be} + V_e$ (series mixing of feedback voltage $V_f = V_e$).
- The **output side** samples the collector voltage (voltage sampling).
- The **emitter resistor RE is unbypassed** — it converts output current information into a feedback voltage.

## 4.4 Comparative Block Diagrams of the Four Topologies

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph VSFB[Voltage Series VCVS]
        vs1[Vs] -->|series| vs2[Amp] --> vs3[Vo]
        vs3 -.parallel sample.-> vs4[Beta Net]
        vs4 -.series injection.-> vs2
    end

    subgraph CSFB[Current Series CCVS]
        cs1[Vs] -->|series| cs2[Amp] --> cs3[Io]
        cs3 -.series sample.-> cs4[Beta Net]
        cs4 -.series injection.-> cs2
    end

    subgraph VShFB[Voltage Shunt VCCS]
        vsh1[Is] -->|shunt| vsh2[Amp] --> vsh3[Vo]
        vsh3 -.parallel sample.-> vsh4[Beta Net]
        vsh4 -.shunt injection.-> vsh2
    end

    subgraph CShFB[Current Shunt CCCS]
        csh1[Is] -->|shunt| csh2[Amp] --> csh3[Io]
        csh3 -.series sample.-> csh4[Beta Net]
        csh4 -.shunt injection.-> csh2
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:**
> - **Top-row** boxes use *series* input mixing → higher input resistance.
> - **Bottom-row** boxes use *shunt* input mixing → lower input resistance.
> - **Left-column** boxes use *voltage* output sampling → lower output resistance.
> - **Right-column** boxes use *current* output sampling → higher output resistance.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: List the four basic feedback topologies and state one identifying feature of each.**

**Model Answer:**

The four basic feedback topologies in amplifier design are:

1. **Voltage-Series Feedback:** Samples output voltage (shunt at output); mixes feedback voltage in series at input. It is also called a *Voltage-Controlled Voltage Source (VCVS)*.
2. **Voltage-Shunt Feedback:** Samples output voltage (shunt at output); mixes feedback current in shunt at input. It is also called a *Transresistance Amplifier*.
3. **Current-Series Feedback:** Samples output current (series at output); mixes feedback voltage in series at input. It is also called a *Transconductance Amplifier*.
4. **Current-Shunt Feedback:** Samples output current (series at output); mixes feedback current in shunt at input. It is also called a *Current Amplifier* (CCCS).

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: Define the term *loop gain* and *return difference* in a feedback amplifier. What does a return difference of 30 dB imply about the sensitivity of the closed-loop gain?**

**Model Answer:**

- **Loop Gain ($A\beta$):** It is the product of the open-loop gain $A$ of the basic amplifier and the feedback factor $\beta$ of the feedback network. It represents the signal that travels around the feedback loop once.

- **Return Difference $(1 + A\beta)$:** It is one plus the loop gain. Mathematically, it is the factor by which the open-loop gain is divided to obtain the closed-loop gain: $A_f = A / (1 + A\beta)$.

- **Interpretation of 30 dB Return Difference:** A return difference of 30 dB corresponds to a numerical value of $10^{30/20} = 31.62 \approx 31.6$. This means the closed-loop gain is **31.6 times less sensitive** to variations in the open-loop gain $A$. A 50% drift in transistor $\beta$ would cause only about a 1.6% change in $A_f$. This is the *desensitization* property of negative feedback.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Internal Choice Format)

### Question A [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, derive the expression for the closed-loop gain of a negative feedback amplifier. Explain the significance of the term $(1 + A\beta)$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a voltage-series feedback amplifier, derive the expressions for input resistance $R_{if}$ and output resistance $R_{of}$. State the effect on bandwidth. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Block Diagram:**

```
Vs → ⊕ → Vi → [A] → Vo
      ↑ −
      |
      [β]
      |
      ←—————— Vo (feedback)
```

**Derivation:**

The summing junction produces the error signal:

$$V_i = V_s - V_f$$

The feedback network gives:

$$V_f = \beta \cdot V_o$$

The basic amplifier gives:

$$V_o = A \cdot V_i$$

Substituting the first equation into the third:

$$V_o = A \cdot (V_s - \beta V_o)$$

Expanding:

$$V_o = A V_s - A\beta V_o$$

Rearranging:

$$V_o (1 + A\beta) = A V_s$$

Therefore:

$$A_f = \frac{V_o}{V_s} = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta}$$

**Significance of $(1 + A\beta)$:**

1. It is the **return difference** or **amount of feedback**.
2. It governs *all* performance modifications: gain reduction, impedance changes, bandwidth extension, and distortion reduction.
3. When $A\beta \gg 1$ (deep feedback), $A_f \approx 1/\beta$, meaning the closed-loop gain depends only on the *passive* feedback network — making the amplifier **insensitive** to transistor parameter variations, temperature drift, and supply voltage changes.

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Input Resistance with Series Mixing:**

The feedback voltage $V_f$ appears in series with the source voltage $V_s$ in the input KVL loop:

$$V_s = I_i R_i + V_f = I_i R_i + \beta A V_i = I_i R_i + \beta A (I_i R_i) = I_i R_i (1 + A\beta)$$

Therefore:

$$R_{if} = \frac{V_s}{I_i} = R_i(1 + A\beta)$$

**Output Resistance with Voltage Sampling:**

Setting $V_s = 0$ and applying a test voltage $V_t$ at the output, the feedback voltage at the amplifier input is $V_i = -\beta V_t$. The dependent source produces $A V_i = -A\beta V_t$. By KCL at the output:

$$I_t = \frac{V_t - A V_i}{R_o} = \frac{V_t (1 + A\beta)}{R_o}$$

Therefore:

$$R_{of} = \frac{V_t}{I_t} = \frac{R_o}{1 + A\beta}$$

**Effect on Bandwidth:**

The upper cutoff frequency increases as:

$$f_{Hf} = f_H(1 + A\beta)$$

So the new bandwidth is:

$$(BW)_f = f_{Hf} - f_L \approx f_H(1 + A\beta)$$

This is a **bandwidth increase by the factor $(1 + A\beta)$**.

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam - May 2023 Model — Alternative Choice]

**(a)** Compare the four feedback topologies in terms of the quantity sampled, quantity mixed, input/output resistance modifications, and ideal source behavior. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Draw the BJT circuit realization of a voltage-series feedback amplifier. Derive the expression for voltage gain, input resistance, and output resistance using the hybrid-π model. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Comparison Table:**

| Topology | Output Sampled | Input Mixed | $R_{if}$ Effect | $R_{of}$ Effect | Ideal Source Type |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Voltage-Series** | Voltage (parallel) | Voltage (series) | $R_{if} = R_i(1 + A\beta)$ ↑ | $R_{of} = R_o/(1 + A\beta)$ ↓ | VCVS |
| **Voltage-Shunt** | Voltage (parallel) | Current (shunt) | $R_{if} = R_i/(1 + A\beta)$ ↓ | $R_{of} = R_o/(1 + A\beta)$ ↓ | VCCS |
| **Current-Series** | Current (series) | Voltage (series) | $R_{if} = R_i(1 + A\beta)$ ↑ | $R_{of} = R_o(1 + A\beta)$ ↑ | CCVS |
| **Current-Shunt** | Current (series) | Current (shunt) | $R_{if} = R_i/(1 + A\beta)$ ↓ | $R_{of} = R_o(1 + A\beta)$ ↑ | CCCS |

> **Memory Trick:** "Series at input → high Zin; Shunt at input → low Zin. Voltage at output → low Zout; Current at output → high Zout."

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Circuit (CE with unbypassed RE):**

```
        +Vcc
         |
         Rc
         |
         C ─────── Vo
         |
         B
   Vs──Rs──┤
         │
         E ──── RE ──── GND
```

**Hybrid-π Model Analysis:**

The small-signal model gives:
- $V_{be} = V_s - I_e R_E \approx V_s - I_c R_E$ (since $I_e \approx I_c$)
- $I_c = g_m V_{be}$
- $V_o = -I_c (R_C \Vert R_L) = -g_m V_{be} R_C'$

**Substituting $V_{be} = V_s - I_c R_E$ and $I_c = g_m V_{be}$:**

$$V_{be} = V_s - g_m V_{be} R_E \implies V_{be} = \frac{V_s}{1 + g_m R_E}$$

**Voltage Gain:**

$$A_f = \frac{V_o}{V_s} = -g_m R_C' \cdot \frac{1}{1 + g_m R_E} = \frac{-g_m R_C'}{1 + g_m R_E}$$

**When $g_m R_E \gg 1$ (deep feedback):**

$$A_f \approx -\frac{R_C'}{R_E}$$

**Input Resistance:**

$$R_{if} = r_\pi + (1 + \beta_{ac}) R_E \approx \beta_{ac} R_E$$

**Output Resistance:**

$$R_{of} \approx R_C$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Sign Convention Error:** Students often write $V_i = V_s + V_f$. The correct mixer equation is $V_i = V_s - V_f$ for **negative** feedback. Mixing up the sign inverts the entire derivation.
>
> 2. **Confusing Series vs. Shunt Mixing Effects:** Many students apply $R_{if} = R_i/(1 + A\beta)$ to voltage-series feedback. **Series mixing INCREASES** input resistance (numerator form), not decreases.
>
> 3. **Forgetting to Convert dB to Linear Scale:** A 30 dB return difference is $10^{30/20} \approx 31.6$, not 30. Always convert dB to numerical ratio before plugging into equations.
>
> 4. **Mixing Up BJT Hybrid-π Parameters:** Using $\beta_{dc}$ (DC current gain) instead of $\beta_{ac} = h_{fe}$ (AC current gain) in small-signal analysis. Use $h_{fe}$ (or simply $\beta$ in the AC sense) for small-signal derivations.
>
> 5. **Bypassing RE Accidentally:** If the emitter resistor is bypassed by a large capacitor, the feedback is *eliminated at AC*. The problem must explicitly state "unbypassed RE" for series feedback to be present.
>
> 6. **GBP Invariance Not Mentioned:** Examiners often award bonus marks for stating that the **Gain-Bandwidth Product is constant** — it is a key property of negative feedback that many students overlook.
>
> 7. **Open-Loop vs. Closed-Loop Notation:** Always clearly distinguish $A$ (open-loop) from $A_f$ (closed-loop). Using $A$ throughout is a guaranteed mark deduction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Summary — Voltage-Series Feedback Amplifier**

### Core Identity
- **Topology Name:** Voltage-Series Feedback (VCVS / Series-Shunt Feedback)
- **Output Sampling:** Voltage (shunt/parallel connection at output)
- **Input Mixing:** Voltage (series/KVL loop at input)
- **Identified by:** BJT CE stage with **unbypassed emitter resistor RE** OR a voltage divider feeding the emitter

### Key Equations (Memorize These!)

- $A_f = \dfrac{A}{1 + A\beta}$
- $R_{if} = R_i(1 + A\beta)$ — **Input impedance BOOSTED**
- $R_{of} = \dfrac{R_o}{1 + A\beta}$ — **Output impedance REDUCED**
- $(BW)_f = (BW)(1 + A\beta)$ — **Bandwidth EXTENDED**
- $GBP_f = GBP$ — **Gain-Bandwidth Product INVARIANT**
- $D_f = \dfrac{D}{1 + A\beta}$ — **Distortion REDUCED**
- $V_f = \beta V_o$ where $\beta = \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$ for a voltage divider feedback network

### Effects of Negative Feedback (The 4-D Rule)
- **D**esensitizes gain
- **D**ecreases gain magnitude
- **D**ecreases distortion & noise
- **D**ecreases output impedance (in voltage sampling)
- **I**ncreases input impedance (in series mixing)
- **I**ncreases bandwidth
- **I**nvariant GBP

### BJT Voltage-Series Practical Formulas
- Voltage gain: $A_f \approx -R_C'/R_E$ (for deep feedback)
- Input resistance: $R_{if} \approx (1 + \beta_{ac}) R_E = \beta_{ac} R_E$
- Output resistance: $R_{of} \approx R_C$

### Four-Topology Quick Reference
- VS (Series-Shunt): ↑Zin, ↓Zout → VCVS
- CS (Series-Series): ↑Zin, ↑Zout → CCVS
- VSh (Shunt-Shunt): ↓Zin, ↓Zout → VCCS
- CSh (Shunt-Series): ↓Zin, ↑Zout → CCCS

### Common Exam Traps to Avoid
- Don't forget the **sign** in $V_i = V_s - V_f$ (negative feedback!)
- Don't apply voltage-series formulas to **current-series** or **shunt** topologies
- Always convert **dB to linear** before substitution
- Always use **AC β (hfe)**, not DC β (hFE), in small-signal derivations
- The emitter resistor must be **unbypassed** for voltage-series feedback to exist

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Oscillators - Introduction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET302 - ELECTRONIC DEVICES AND CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Oscillators - Introduction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Oscillators — Introduction

## 1. Formal Technical Definition

An **oscillator** is an electronic circuit that generates a periodic, repeating waveform (sinusoidal or non-sinusoidal) without requiring any external input signal. The output is sustained by feeding back a portion of the output signal to the input in proper phase and magnitude. Oscillators convert DC power from the supply into AC signal power at a specific frequency, making them **signal source circuits** fundamentally distinct from amplifiers, which require an external input.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** An oscillator is a closed-loop, positive-feedback regenerative circuit that produces a self-sustaining output signal. It operates *without* an external AC input source — the input is internally generated from the output via a feedback network.

## 2. Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **child on a swing**:
- Push the swing once (initial trigger).
- The swing oscillates back and forth.
- Due to air drag and friction, the swing gradually slows and eventually stops.
- To keep the swing going indefinitely, you must apply a small, well-timed push **at exactly the right moment** (in-phase with the motion) and with **enough energy** to compensate for losses.

In the electrical analog:
- The **child's push** = small input signal needed to start oscillation.
- The **friction losses** = resistive losses in the oscillator's tank circuit.
- The **timed push** = positive feedback in correct phase.
- The **swing itself** = sustained sinusoidal output.

> [!NOTE]
> This analogy introduces the two **Barkhausen Criteria**: (1) sufficient magnitude to overcome losses, and (2) correct phase to be regenerative (positive feedback).

## 3. Why Oscillators Are Essential — Real-World Metrics

- **Standard frequencies used:** Audio range **20 Hz – 20 kHz**, RF range **100 kHz – 100 MHz**, Microwave **> 1 GHz**.
- **Stability metric:** Frequency stability is often expressed in **parts per million (ppm)**.
- **Quality factor (Q)** is the primary figure of merit, defined as the ratio of stored energy to energy dissipated per cycle. Standard high-Q crystal oscillators achieve **Q > 10,000**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Highlight: The textbook classification strictly distinguishes **Sinusoidal Oscillators** (Hartley, Colpitts, Crystal, RC Phase Shift, Wien Bridge) from **Non-Sinusoidal / Relaxation Oscillators** (Astable Multivibrator, Schmitt Trigger Oscillator, UJT Relaxation Oscillator).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 1. Block Diagram of an Oscillator (Feedback View)

An oscillator is fundamentally a **positive-feedback amplifier** in which the loop gain is sufficient to sustain self-oscillation.

- **Active amplifying device:** Transistor / Op-Amp / FET — provides power gain.
- **Frequency-determining network (FDN):** RC or LC tank circuit — sets the oscillation frequency.
- **Feedback network:** Provides a fraction of the output back to the input, **in phase**.

## 2. The Barkhausen Criterion (Heart of All Sinusoidal Oscillators)

For sustained oscillation, two simultaneous conditions must be met:

**Condition 1 — Loop Gain Magnitude Condition:**
The magnitude of the loop gain $|A\beta|$ must equal **exactly 1** (unity).

**Condition 2 — Loop Phase Condition:**
The total phase shift around the closed loop must be **0° or an integer multiple of 360°**.

> [!NOTE]
> During startup, $|A\beta| > 1$ so the oscillations grow exponentially from noise. As amplitude builds, the device's non-linearity reduces $|A|$ until $|A\beta| = 1$, locking the amplitude at a steady value. This is called the **amplitude stabilization mechanism**.

## 3. Classification of Oscillators (KTU-Aligned)

Based on waveform generated:

| Type | Sub-Type | Examples | Frequency Range |
|------|---------|----------|-----------------|
| **Sinusoidal** | LC Oscillator | Hartley, Colpitts, Clapp | RF (kHz – GHz) |
| **Sinusoidal** | RC Oscillator | Phase Shift, Wien Bridge | Audio (Hz – MHz) |
| **Sinusoidal** | Crystal | Pierce, Miller | Fixed, highly stable |
| **Non-Sinusoidal** | Relaxation | Astable Multivibrator, UJT | Low frequency |

## 4. KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Equation / Quantity | Formula | Description / Units |
|---------------------|---------|---------------------|
| Barkhausen Magnitude | $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$ | Sustained oscillation magnitude |
| Barkhausen Phase | $\angle A\beta = 0°\ \text{or}\ 360°\cdot n$ | Total loop phase shift |
| LC Tank Frequency | $f_o = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$ | Resonance / oscillation frequency in Hz |
| Angular Frequency | $\omega_o = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$ | In rad/s |
| Quality Factor (Series RLC) | $Q = \dfrac{1}{R}\sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C}}$ | Dimensionless, selectivity measure |
| Q in terms of Inductance | $Q = \dfrac{\omega_o L}{R}$ | For series RLC |
| Q in terms of Capacitance | $Q = \dfrac{1}{\omega_o RC}$ | For series RLC |
| Hartley Oscillator Freq. | $f_o = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L_T C}}$ where $L_T = L_1 + L_2 + 2M$ | Tank with two coupled inductors |
| Colpitts Oscillator Freq. | $f_o = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L \cdot C_{eq}}}$ where $C_{eq} = \dfrac{C_1 C_2}{C_1 + C_2}$ | Tank with capacitive divider |
| RC Phase Shift Freq. | $f_o = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6}}$ | Standard 3-section RC network |
| Wien Bridge Freq. | $f_o = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | Series and parallel RC equal |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility:** Oscillators form the **clock signal source** in every digital computer and microcontroller, the **carrier generator** in every radio/TV transmitter, the **local oscillator** in every superheterodyne receiver, and the **timing reference** in GPS, radar, and telecommunication infrastructure. Crystal oscillators are used in **quartz watches** because of their extraordinary stability (drift as low as 0.001 ppm/year).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Derivation 1: The Barkhausen Criterion from First Principles

Consider a basic feedback amplifier with open-loop gain $A$ and feedback factor $\beta$. The closed-loop transfer function is:

$$
\frac{V_o}{V_{in}} = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta}
$$

This equation is fundamental. Let us interpret it physically.

### Step 1 — Trace the signal around the closed loop

The output voltage is $V_o$. The feedback network samples $V_o$ and produces a feedback voltage:

$$
V_f = \beta \cdot V_o
$$

This feedback voltage is fed back to the input. The amplifier amplifies the difference, so:

$$
V_o = A \cdot V_{in}' \quad \text{where} \quad V_{in}' = V_{in} + V_f
$$

Substituting:

$$
V_o = A \left(V_{in} + \beta V_o\right)
$$

### Step 2 — Solve for the closed-loop gain

Expanding:

$$
V_o = A V_{in} + A \beta V_o
$$

Collecting $V_o$ on the left side:

$$
V_o - A\beta V_o = A V_{in}
$$

$$
V_o \left(1 - A\beta\right) = A V_{in}
$$

$$
\frac{V_o}{V_{in}} = \frac{A}{1 - A\beta}
$$

(Note: We use the minus sign convention because we are describing **positive feedback** — the feedback is in phase with the input.)

### Step 3 — Apply the condition for self-oscillation

For the circuit to act as an oscillator, **no external input is required** ($V_{in} = 0$). However, a finite output must exist. For this to happen mathematically, the denominator must vanish:

$$
1 - A\beta = 0
$$

$$
\boxed{A\beta = 1}
$$

### Step 4 — Introduce complex phasors

Since $A$ and $\beta$ are generally frequency-dependent complex quantities, write:

$$
A(j\omega) = \vert A(\omega) \vert \, e^{j\phi_A(\omega)}
$$

$$
\beta(j\omega) = \vert \beta(\omega) \vert \, e^{j\phi_\beta(\omega)}
$$

Substituting:

$$
A\beta = \vert A \vert \vert \beta \vert \, e^{j(\phi_A + \phi_\beta)} = 1
$$

For this complex number to equal the real number $1 + j0$, two independent real conditions must hold simultaneously:

**Magnitude Condition:**

$$
\boxed{\vert A(\omega_o)\beta(\omega_o)\vert = 1}
$$

**Phase Condition:**

$$
\boxed{\phi_A(\omega_o) + \phi_\beta(\omega_o) = 0°\ \text{or}\ n \cdot 360°,\ \ n \in \mathbb{Z}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why both conditions are needed:** The phase condition forces the circuit to oscillate at *one specific* frequency $\omega_o$ (since phase varies with frequency). The magnitude condition ensures the oscillation neither grows unbounded nor decays to zero — energy supplied per cycle exactly balances energy dissipated per cycle.

## Derivation 2: Oscillation Frequency of a Lossless LC Tank

The energy in an LC tank oscillates between the inductor's magnetic field and the capacitor's electric field. Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law:

$$
V_L + V_C = 0
$$

$$
L \frac{di}{dt} + \frac{1}{C}\int i\,dt = 0
$$

Differentiating once:

$$
L \frac{d^2i}{dt^2} + \frac{1}{C}i = 0
$$

Rearranging into standard second-order form:

$$
\frac{d^2i}{dt^2} + \frac{1}{LC}i = 0
$$

This is the harmonic oscillator equation. The general solution is:

$$
i(t) = I_m \cos(\omega_o t + \phi)
$$

Substituting back, we identify:

$$
\omega_o^2 = \frac{1}{LC}
$$

$$
\boxed{f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}}
$$

### Quality Factor Derivation (Series RLC Tank)

For a practical tank with resistance $R$, the equation becomes:

$$
L \frac{d^2i}{dt^2} + R\frac{di}{dt} + \frac{i}{C} = 0
$$

Divide through by $L$:

$$
\frac{d^2i}{dt^2} + \frac{R}{L}\frac{di}{dt} + \frac{1}{LC}i = 0
$$

Comparing with the standard form:

$$
\frac{d^2i}{dt^2} + 2\alpha \frac{di}{dt} + \omega_o^2 i = 0
$$

The damping coefficient is:

$$
\alpha = \frac{R}{2L}
$$

The quality factor is the number of cycles required for the energy to decay by a factor of $e^{2\pi} \approx 535$:

$$
Q = \frac{\omega_o}{2\alpha} = \frac{\omega_o}{R/L} = \frac{\omega_o L}{R}
$$

Substituting $\omega_o = 1/\sqrt{LC}$:

$$
\boxed{Q = \frac{1}{R}\sqrt{\frac{L}{C}}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** A *higher* $Q$ means a *sharper* resonance peak — the oscillator's output frequency is more precisely defined, and it has better frequency stability. Crystal oscillators exploit the extraordinarily high $Q$ of piezoelectric quartz (often $> 20{,}000$) to achieve their well-known frequency precision.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## Diagram 1: Feedback-Amplifier Model of an Oscillator (Block Architecture Flow)

This Mermaid block-level architecture flow illustrates how an oscillator is fundamentally a closed-loop regenerative system, mapping the interaction between the active amplifier, the frequency-determining network, and the feedback path.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph AMP["ACTIVE AMPLIFIER"]
        A["Amplifier Block\nGain A"]
    end

    subgraph FDN["FREQUENCY DETERMINING NETWORK"]
        F["FDN Block\nSets Frequency"]
    end

    subgraph FB["FEEDBACK NETWORK"]
        B["Beta Network\nFeedback Factor"]
    end

    VOUT["OUTPUT\nV_out"]
    VIN["INPUT NODE\nV_in"]

    VIN -- X --> A
    A -- amplified signal --> F
    F --> VOUT
    VOUT -- sampled output --> B
    B -- feedback voltage V_f --> VIN

    NOTE["LOOP CONDITION:\nMagnitude of A times Beta equals 1\nPhase Shift equals 0 or 360 degrees"]
    NOTE -.-> A
    NOTE -.-> B
```

## Diagram 2: Startup and Steady-State Behavior of Loop Gain

This diagram traces how the loop gain transitions from greater-than-unity during startup to exactly-unity at steady state, illustrating the amplitude stabilization mechanism.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Initial Noise Pulse\nin Circuit"] --> S2["Loop Gain Magnitude\ngreater than 1"]
    S2 --> S3["Amplitude Grows\nExponentially per Cycle"]
    S3 --> S4{"Nonlinearity\nActivates?"}
    S4 -- Yes --> S5["Effective Gain A\ndecreases"]
    S5 --> S6["Loop Gain settles\nto exactly 1"]
    S6 --> S7["Steady-State\nConstant Amplitude\nOscillation"]
    S4 -- No --> S8["Amplitude grows\nwithout bound\nUnstable"]
```

## Diagram 3: Classification Tree of Oscillator Topologies

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT["Oscillators"]
    ROOT --> S["Sinusoidal Oscillators"]
    ROOT --> N["Non Sinusoidal Oscillators"]

    S --> LC["LC Oscillators"]
    S --> RC["RC Oscillators"]
    S --> CR["Crystal Oscillators"]

    LC --> H["Hartley Oscillator"]
    LC --> CO["Colpitts Oscillator"]
    LC --> CL["Clapp Oscillator"]

    RC --> PS["RC Phase Shift"]
    RC --> WB["Wien Bridge"]

    CR --> PI["Pierce Oscillator"]
    CR --> MI["Miller Oscillator"]

    N --> AST["Astable Multivibrator"]
    N --> SCH["Schmitt Trigger Oscillator"]
    N --> UJT["UJT Relaxation Oscillator"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Schematic Style:** Mermaid is used here as a *functional architecture* mapper rather than a literal circuit schematic. Physical circuit drawings (transistor symbols, inductor coils, capacitor plates) are not rendered by Mermaid; instead, the block-level interactions and signal flow are precisely captured — which is the recommended engineering style for documentation in modern EDA environments.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]`

**State and explain the Barkhausen criterion for sustained oscillations. Why is it necessary that both conditions be satisfied simultaneously?**

**Model Answer:**

The Barkhausen criterion states that for sustained oscillations, the loop gain of a positive-feedback amplifier must satisfy two simultaneous conditions:

1. **Magnitude Condition:** The magnitude of the loop gain $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$.
2. **Phase Condition:** The total phase shift around the closed loop must be $0°$ (or an integer multiple of $360°$), i.e., $\angle A\beta = 0°$ or $n \cdot 360°$.

Both conditions are required simultaneously because:
- The **magnitude condition alone** only ensures the feedback signal has sufficient amplitude to sustain the output, but does not guarantee that this feedback occurs at the correct instant.
- The **phase condition alone** only ensures the feedback is regenerative (in-phase), but does not ensure that the loop has enough gain to overcome circuit losses.
- **Together**, they guarantee that *exactly one* frequency $\omega_o$ will produce a sustained, stable sinusoid where the energy supplied per cycle by the amplifier equals the energy dissipated per cycle in the resistance.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** A common student mistake is to state "loop gain must be greater than 1." This is true *only during startup* (build-up phase). For sustained steady-state oscillation, $\vert A\beta \vert$ must equal **exactly 1**. Writing $> 1$ will cost a mark.

---

### Question 2
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Supplementary Exam]`

**Distinguish between sinusoidal oscillators and relaxation oscillators. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Sinusoidal Oscillator | Relaxation Oscillator |
|---------|----------------------|----------------------|
| Output waveform | Pure sine wave | Square, triangular, or sawtooth |
| Frequency-determining element | LC tank or RC network | RC charging time constant |
| Active device operation | Linear region | Switches between cutoff and saturation |
| Frequency stability | High | Moderate to low |
| Typical applications | RF carriers, audio tone generators | Timing circuits, function generators |

- **Examples of Sinusoidal Oscillators:** (1) Hartley Oscillator, (2) Colpitts Oscillator.
- **Examples of Relaxation Oscillators:** (1) Astable Multivibrator, (2) UJT Relaxation Oscillator.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Regular Exam]`

**(a)** Explain the operation of a sinusoidal oscillator using the concept of positive feedback. Derive the **Barkhausen criterion** mathematically and state the conditions required for sustained oscillation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Colpitts oscillator uses a tank circuit with $L = 100\ \mu H$, $C_1 = 1\ nF$, and $C_2 = 4\ nF$. Calculate the **frequency of oscillation** $f_o$ and the **effective tank capacitance**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) Solution — Barkhausen Criterion Derivation

A sinusoidal oscillator is a positive-feedback amplifier with no external input. Output is fed back to the input through a feedback network of transfer ratio $\beta$. For sustained oscillation, the feedback voltage $V_f = \beta V_o$ must be in phase with the assumed input and must equal the input magnitude required to produce $V_o$.

For an amplifier of gain $A$:

$$
V_o = A V_{in}'
$$

where $V_{in}' = V_f$ (since $V_{in} = 0$). Substituting:

$$
V_o = A \beta V_o
$$

$$
1 - A\beta = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad A\beta = 1
$$

Writing $A$ and $\beta$ as complex phasors:

$$
A(j\omega)\beta(j\omega) = \vert A \vert \vert \beta \vert \, e^{j(\phi_A + \phi_\beta)} = 1
$$

**Conditions:**

$$
\boxed{\vert A(\omega_o) \beta(\omega_o) \vert = 1}
$$

$$
\boxed{\phi_A(\omega_o) + \phi_\beta(\omega_o) = 0°\ \text{or}\ n \cdot 360°}
$$

**Physical interpretation:** The magnitude condition ensures energy balance (amplifier gain compensates for losses), and the phase condition selects one specific frequency at which the feedback is regenerative.

---

#### Part (b) Solution — Colpitts Oscillator Frequency

**Step 1:** Identify the formula. The Colpitts oscillator frequency is:

$$
f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L \cdot C_{eq}}}
$$

where the effective capacitance is the **series combination** of $C_1$ and $C_2$:

$$
C_{eq} = \frac{C_1 C_2}{C_1 + C_2}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the given values. $C_1 = 1\ nF = 10^{-9}\ F$, $C_2 = 4\ nF = 4 \times 10^{-9}\ F$.

$$
C_{eq} = \frac{(1\ nF)(4\ nF)}{1\ nF + 4\ nF} = \frac{4\ nF^2}{5\ nF} = 0.8\ nF
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute into the frequency formula with $L = 100\ \mu H = 10^{-4}\ H$.

$$
f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{(10^{-4})(0.8 \times 10^{-9})}}
$$

$$
f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{8 \times 10^{-14}}}
$$

$$
f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi \times (2.828 \times 10^{-7})}
$$

$$
f_o = \frac{1}{1.777 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
\boxed{f_o \approx 562.7\ kHz}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students frequently use the *parallel* combination formula $C_{eq} = C_1 + C_2$ for the Colpitts tank. This is **incorrect**. The two capacitors in a Colpitts tank are in **series** across the inductor as seen by the AC tank current — use the series formula. This error will cost full marks.

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice, 14 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Supplementary Exam]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain how a **feedback amplifier** acts as an **oscillator**. Discuss the role of **nonlinearity in amplitude stabilization**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An LC tank circuit has $L = 50\ \mu H$, $C = 200\ pF$, and series resistance $R = 5\ \Omega$. Calculate the **resonant frequency** $f_o$, the **quality factor** $Q$, and the **bandwidth** $BW$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) Solution — Feedback Amplifier as Oscillator

A feedback amplifier becomes an oscillator when its loop gain satisfies $\vert A\beta \vert \ge 1$ and the loop phase is $0°$ or $360°$. The block diagram consists of:

- An **amplifier** of gain $A$.
- A **feedback network** of ratio $\beta$.
- The output of $\beta$ is summed **positively** with the input.

**Role of Nonlinearity in Amplitude Stabilization:**

At startup, the small noise voltage in the circuit is amplified because $\vert A\beta \vert > 1$. The amplitude builds up. As the signal grows, the active device (transistor or op-amp) enters its **nonlinear region of operation** (e.g., transistor begins to saturate/cut off, op-amp approaches its rail voltages). This nonlinearity causes the **effective average gain** $A$ to decrease. Eventually, the gain reduces until $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$, and the amplitude stabilizes at a fixed value.

Without nonlinearity, the amplitude would grow indefinitely (saturation physically limits the maximum output swing). The nonlinearity thus acts as an **automatic amplitude-control mechanism**, ensuring a constant, well-defined sinusoidal output.

---

#### Part (b) Solution — Resonant Circuit Parameters

**Step 1:** Compute the resonant frequency using $L = 50\ \mu H = 50 \times 10^{-6}\ H$ and $C = 200\ pF = 200 \times 10^{-12}\ F$.

$$
f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}} = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{(50 \times 10^{-6})(200 \times 10^{-12})}}
$$

$$
f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{10^{-14}}}
$$

$$
f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^{-7}}
$$

$$
\boxed{f_o \approx 1.592\ MHz}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the Quality Factor using $R = 5\ \Omega$.

$$
Q = \frac{1}{R}\sqrt{\frac{L}{C}}
$$

$$
Q = \frac{1}{5}\sqrt{\frac{50 \times 10^{-6}}{200 \times 10^{-12}}}
$$

$$
Q = \frac{1}{5}\sqrt{2.5 \times 10^{5}}
$$

$$
Q = \frac{1}{5} \times 500 = 100
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the Bandwidth.

$$
BW = \frac{f_o}{Q} = \frac{1.592 \times 10^{6}}{100}
$$

$$
\boxed{BW \approx 15.92\ kHz}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** A common mistake is to use $f_o / Q$ for bandwidth — but this is correct **only for a series RLC**. For a parallel RLC, the formula is the same only if you redefine $Q$ appropriately. Always write the $Q$ formula explicitly in your solution; examiners award partial credit for showing the correct setup even if numerical arithmetic slips.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist for Oscillators — Introduction:**

- **Core Definition:** An oscillator is a **positive-feedback amplifier** with **no external input** that generates a sustained periodic output using its own feedback.
- **Barkhausen Magnitude Condition:** $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$ at the oscillation frequency (steady state).
- **Barkhausen Phase Condition:** $\angle A\beta = 0°$ or an integer multiple of $360°$.
- **Startup Condition:** $\vert A\beta \vert > 1$ initially; device nonlinearity reduces effective gain until the steady-state $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$ is achieved.
- **LC Tank Frequency:** $f_o = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$; angular frequency $\omega_o = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$.
- **Quality Factor (Series RLC):** $Q = \dfrac{1}{R}\sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C}} = \dfrac{\omega_o L}{R}$.
- **Bandwidth:** $BW = \dfrac{f_o}{Q}$.
- **Sinusoidal Oscillators:** Hartley, Colpitts, Clapp (LC); RC Phase Shift, Wien Bridge (RC); Crystal Oscillator (Pierce, Miller).
- **Non-Sinusoidal (Relaxation) Oscillators:** Astable Multivibrator, Schmitt Trigger, UJT Relaxation Oscillator.
- **Hartley vs Colpitts:** Hartley uses an *inductive* divider (two coils); Colpitts uses a *capacitive* divider (two capacitors in series across the tank).
- **Crystal Oscillator:** Highest $Q$ and stability — used in clocks, watches, RF synthesizers.
- **Engineering Reality:** Every digital processor, radio, TV, and mobile phone uses one or more oscillators as its timing or carrier reference.
- **Examiner's Mantra:** Always state both Barkhausen conditions *together*; never write just "greater than 1" for the magnitude condition in steady state.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Barkhausen Criterion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET302 - ELECTRONIC DEVICES AND CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Barkhausen Criterion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Barkhausen Criterion** specifies the two simultaneous mathematical conditions that must be satisfied by a closed-loop feedback network for it to function as a **sinusoidal oscillator** (a circuit that generates a sustained, single-frequency AC output without any external AC input signal).

In its formal, rigorous KTU 2024 syllabus form, the Barkhausen Criterion states:

> A feedback amplifier will oscillate (produce a sustained sinusoidal output) if and only if the **magnitude** of the loop gain is exactly **unity** and the **phase shift** introduced around the entire feedback loop is **zero degrees** (or an integer multiple of 360°).

Mathematically, this is expressed by the complex equation:

$$A\beta = 1\angle 0^\circ \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad A\beta = 1\angle n\cdot 360^\circ,\ \ n \in \mathbb{Z}$$

This decomposes into two independent scalar requirements:

- **Magnitude Condition:** $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$
- **Phase Condition:** $\angle A\beta = 0^\circ + n\cdot 360^\circ$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (verbatim recall version):**
> *"The Barkhausen criterion for oscillation states that the product of the open-loop gain (A) of the amplifier and the feedback factor (β) of the feedback network must be a real number (no imaginary part). In other words, $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$ and the total phase shift around the loop must be 0° or 360°."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Infinite Echo" Mic

Imagine a person standing in a **circular canyon** and clapping once. The sound travels, reflects off the walls, and comes back. If the canyon walls are **perfectly reflective and exactly one wavelength apart**, the returning clap arrives in perfect phase with the next hand movement, reinforcing it. If the wall reflectivity is exactly **100% (no energy loss)**, the echo never dies — you get a **sustained ringing sound** at one specific pitch.

- The canyon walls represent the **feedback network (β)**.
- The clapping person represents the **amplifier (A)**.
- The one-wavelength round trip is the **0° / 360° phase condition**.
- The perfect reflectivity is the **$\vert A\beta \vert = 1$ magnitude condition**.

In real life, the echo dies because energy is lost (some absorption). An oscillator is therefore an **electronic version of a "lossless echo chamber"** — it keeps re-injecting exactly as much energy as is lost in one cycle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics to Remember:**
> - Loop Gain: $A\beta$ — dimensionless
> - Phase shift: measured in **radians (rad)** or **degrees (°)**
> - For startup of oscillation (from thermal noise), the criterion is relaxed to $\vert A\beta \vert > 1$; only at steady state does it settle to **$\vert A\beta \vert = 1$**.

## 1.3 The Crucial Distinction: Startup vs. Steady State

A subtle but frequently tested KTU point is that the **strict equality** $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$ applies only to the **steady-state** running condition. At switch-on, the criterion must be **$\vert A\beta \vert > 1$** so that any random thermal noise (which always exists at the input) gets **amplified and re-injected with progressive growth** until the amplifier's **non-linearity** (saturation / cutoff) clips the amplitude and effectively drags the loop gain back down to unity.

| Phase | Magnitude Condition | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **Startup (transient)** | $\vert A\beta \vert > 1$ | Noise must grow exponentially from $\mu V$ to $V$ levels |
| **Steady State (continuous)** | $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$ | Energy in = Energy lost per cycle (energy balance) |
| **Decay (no oscillation)** | $\vert A\beta \vert < 1$ | Signal dies out exponentially |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Viva:** *"Greater than One to be born, Equals One to be sustained, Less than One is dead and gone."*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Origin of the Criterion — Closed-Loop Gain Equation

Consider a standard **negative-feedback voltage amplifier block diagram**. The closed-loop gain is universally given by:

$$A_f = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta}$$

The quantity $A\beta$ is called the **loop gain** or **return ratio**. The denominator $1 + A\beta$ is the **return difference** and controls the **amount of feedback**.

### The Logic Chain Behind Barkhausen's Condition

1. **Setup:** A practical amplifier with finite gain $A$ has its output sampled by a feedback network producing a feedback signal $V_f = \beta V_o$.
2. **Error signal:** $V_e = V_{in} - V_f = V_{in} - \beta V_o$ (for negative feedback).
3. **Output expression:** $V_o = A V_e = A(V_{in} - \beta V_o)$, which rearranges to $V_o(1 + A\beta) = A V_{in}$.
4. **Critical observation:** If the **input is removed** ($V_{in} = 0$), a non-zero output can still exist **only if** the multiplying factor in parentheses becomes zero:

$$1 + A\beta = 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad A\beta = -1$$

5. **Polar decomposition of the result:** Since $A$ and $\beta$ are generally **complex numbers** (they have both magnitude and phase at the signal frequency), $A\beta = -1$ splits into:
   - **Magnitude:** $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$ → energy balance condition.
   - **Phase:** $\angle A\beta = 180^\circ$ in the negative-feedback frame, which corresponds to a **net loop phase of 0° (or 360°)** once the inherent 180° inverter of the negative-feedback subtraction is taken into account.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "$-1$" becomes "0° or 360°":** In the negative-feedback block diagram, the summing junction inherently contributes a 180° inversion. The remaining amplifier + β network must therefore contribute another **180°** so the total loop phase is $180° + 180° = 360° = 0°$. This is the most KTU-favourite trap: students often quote 180° as the loop phase, which is *correct for Aβ itself* but *incorrect for the physical loop traversal*.

## 2.2 Why the Phase Must Be Zero (or 360°)

The phase condition ensures **regenerative (positive) feedback** at the oscillation frequency. If the loop phase differs from $0°$ (or $n \cdot 360°$), the returning signal will be only partially in phase with the driving signal — part of it will be in **anti-phase** and will subtract from the original. The result is partial cancellation, **net loop gain less than unity**, and oscillation will not build up.

## 2.3 Why the Magnitude Must Be Unity

If $\vert A\beta \vert < 1$, the feedback signal is smaller than the original — each round trip dissipates energy, so oscillations die out.

If $\vert A\beta \vert > 1$, oscillations grow unboundedly until device non-linearity limits the amplitude.

If $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$, the signal exactly **regenerates itself** every cycle — the oscillator is self-sustaining.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility & Real-World Importance

| Engineering Field | Use of Barkhausen Concept |
|---|---|
| **RF Communication** | Carrier generation in transmitters (Colpitts, Crystal, Hartley) |
| **Audio Electronics** | Tone generators, function generators, electronic musical instruments (theremin, electric organ) |
| **Digital Clocks** | Quartz crystal oscillators in microcontrollers, computers, watches |
| **Instrumentation** | Signal source in spectrum analysers, lock-in amplifiers |
| **Power Electronics** | PWM carrier generation, resonant converters |
| **Medical Electronics** | Ultrasonic transducer drivers, MRI RF coils |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Cheat Sheet — Print This Section for Last-Minute Revision**

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol | Physical Meaning / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Loop Gain | $A\beta$ | Dimensionless complex number |
| 2 | Magnitude Condition (steady state) | $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$ | Energy balance per cycle |
| 3 | Phase Condition | $\angle A\beta = 0^\circ$ or $n \cdot 360^\circ$ | Positive (regenerative) feedback |
| 4 | Startup Condition | $\vert A\beta \vert > 1$ | Needed for noise to grow |
| 5 | Closed-loop gain general | $A_f = \dfrac{A}{1 + A\beta}$ | Standard feedback expression |
| 6 | Condition for infinite gain | $1 + A\beta = 0$ | Re-arranged for oscillation |
| 7 | Equivalent polar statement | $A\beta = 1 + j0$ | Loop gain purely real and positive |
| 8 | Decay Condition | $\vert A\beta \vert < 1$ | Oscillation dies out |
| 9 | Frequency of Oscillation (general) | $f_o = $ set by reactive components | Where phase condition is met |
| 10 | Amplitude stability | Non-linearity of active device | Causes loop gain to drop to 1 at steady state |

> [!WARNING]
> **Strict LaTeX Rule (KTU board):** When writing the magnitude of a complex quantity in answer scripts, always use $\vert A\beta \vert$ — **never** write $|A\beta|$. Both are mathematically the same, but the vertical-bar notation in LaTeX is the convention KTU examiners expect.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I: Closed-Loop Gain Leading to Barkhausen

**Starting Point:** Consider the canonical negative-feedback amplifier.

- Open-loop gain of amplifier: $A = \dfrac{V_o}{V_e}$
- Feedback factor: $\beta = \dfrac{V_f}{V_o}$
- Input voltage to the closed-loop system: $V_{in}$
- Error voltage (after summing junction): $V_e = V_{in} - V_f$
- Feedback voltage: $V_f = \beta V_o$

### Step 1 — Write the amplifier equation

$$V_o = A \cdot V_e$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the error voltage

$$V_o = A \cdot (V_{in} - V_f)$$

### Step 3 — Substitute the feedback voltage $V_f = \beta V_o$

$$V_o = A \cdot (V_{in} - \beta V_o)$$

### Step 4 — Expand the right-hand side

$$V_o = A \cdot V_{in} - A \beta \cdot V_o$$

### Step 5 — Collect $V_o$ terms on the left side

$$V_o + A\beta \cdot V_o = A \cdot V_{in}$$

### Step 6 — Factor out $V_o$

$$V_o (1 + A\beta) = A \cdot V_{in}$$

### Step 7 — Solve for the closed-loop gain

$$A_f = \frac{V_o}{V_{in}} = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta}$$

This is the **standard closed-loop gain of a negative-feedback amplifier** — a result you must commit to memory.

### Step 8 — Apply the oscillator condition: no input but non-zero output

Set $V_{in} = 0$ (no external input — the oscillator should generate its own signal).

$$V_o (1 + A\beta) = 0$$

For a non-trivial (non-zero) output $V_o \neq 0$, the term in parentheses **must vanish**:

$$1 + A\beta = 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad A\beta = -1$$

### Step 9 — Convert to polar form

In polar form, $A\beta$ has a magnitude and a phase:

$$A\beta = \vert A\beta \vert \cdot e^{j\angle A\beta}$$

Setting $A\beta = -1 = 1 \cdot e^{j\pi}$ gives two simultaneous real conditions:

$$\vert A\beta \vert = 1 \quad \text{and} \quad \angle A\beta = \pi \text{ rad} = 180^\circ$$

### Step 10 — Convert to the standard "loop phase = 0°" form

Since the negative-feedback summing junction **inherently contributes 180°** of phase shift, the **physical signal** that traverses the amplifier-and-β loop must contribute the *other* 180° so that the **total** loop phase is:

$$\phi_{sum} + \phi_{loop} = 180^\circ + 180^\circ = 360^\circ \equiv 0^\circ \pmod{360^\circ}$$

This is the conventional way the criterion is quoted in textbooks and KTU answer keys.

> [!NOTE]
> **Reconciliation of the two conventions:** Both $\angle A\beta = 180^\circ$ and $\angle A\beta = 0°$ are valid, depending on whether the summing-junction inversion is "inside" or "outside" the loop definition. KTU accepts either, but the **loop-phase form (0° or 360°)** is the universally expected one.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II: Why a Non-Zero Frequency is Required

The Barkhausen magnitude condition $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$ is a **scalar equation** in one unknown (the frequency $f$). In general, the network contains reactive elements (L, C), so $A$ and $\beta$ are functions of $\omega = 2\pi f$. Solving for the **frequency of oscillation** therefore requires:

- **Phase condition first** (because it is usually **independent of gain** and is set purely by the reactive components):

$$\angle A(j\omega_o) + \angle \beta(j\omega_o) = 0^\circ \pmod{360^\circ}$$

- **Magnitude condition second** (to set the minimum required amplifier gain $A_{min}$ for sustained oscillation):

$$\vert A(j\omega_o) \vert \cdot \vert \beta(j\omega_o) \vert = 1 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad A_{min} = \frac{1}{\vert \beta(j\omega_o) \vert}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Order of Solving in KTU Problems:**
> 1. **Always determine $\omega_o$ from the phase condition first.** It depends only on passive components.
> 2. **Then compute $A_{min}$ from the magnitude condition.** It depends on the active device.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Criterion for Common KTU Oscillator Topologies

> [!NOTE]
> The expressions below will appear in the derivation section of every oscillator-related KTU problem.

| Oscillator Type | $\omega_o$ (Rad/s) | $f_o$ (Hz) | $\beta$ at $\omega_o$ | $A_{min}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **RC Phase Shift** | $\omega_o = \dfrac{1}{RC\sqrt{6}}$ | $f_o = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6}}$ | $\dfrac{1}{29}$ | $29$ |
| **Wien Bridge** | $\omega_o = \dfrac{1}{RC}$ | $f_o = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | $\dfrac{1}{3}$ | $3$ |
| **Hartley** | $\omega_o = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{L_T C}}$ | $f_o = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L_T C}}$ | $\dfrac{L_2}{L_1+L_2}$ | $\dfrac{L_1+L_2}{L_2}$ |
| **Colpitts** | $\omega_o = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{L C_{eq}}}$ | $f_o = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L C_{eq}}}$ | $\dfrac{C_1}{C_1+C_2}$ | $\dfrac{C_1+C_2}{C_1}$ |
| **Crystal (Pierce)** | $\omega_o = \omega_s$ (series) or $\omega_p$ (parallel) | Set by crystal cut | Depends on load caps | $\approx 1$ (very small) |

where $L_T = L_1 + L_2 + 2M$ (Hartley, mutually coupled) and $C_{eq} = \dfrac{C_1 C_2}{C_1 + C_2}$ (Colpitts series equivalent).

---

## 3.4 Derivation III: Worked Numerical Demonstration

**Problem:** A Wien Bridge oscillator uses $R = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$ and $C = 10 \text{ nF}$. Find the frequency of oscillation and the minimum amplifier gain.

### Step 1 — Compute $\omega_o$

$$\omega_o = \frac{1}{RC} = \frac{1}{(10 \times 10^3)(10 \times 10^{-9})} = \frac{1}{10^{-4}} = 10^4 \text{ rad/s}$$

### Step 2 — Convert to Hz

$$f_o = \frac{\omega_o}{2\pi} = \frac{10^4}{2\pi} \approx 1591.55 \text{ Hz} \approx 1.59 \text{ kHz}$$

### Step 3 — Identify $\beta$ for a Wien Bridge

The standard Wien feedback network (series RC and parallel RC) has, at $\omega = \omega_o$:

$$\beta = \frac{1}{3} \quad \text{(purely real, positive — in phase with output)}$$

### Step 4 — Apply the magnitude condition to find $A_{min}$

$$\vert A\beta \vert = 1 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad A_{min} = \frac{1}{\beta} = \frac{1}{1/3} = 3$$

### Step 5 — Verify dimensionally

$A_{min} = 3$ is dimensionless, as expected for a voltage gain. The amplifier must provide at least a gain of 3, with the non-linearity (often a small incandescent lamp) reducing it to exactly 3 in steady state.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Answer Box Format (for KTU answer sheets):**
> $\omega_o = 10^4$ rad/s, $f_o \approx 1.59$ kHz, $A_{min} = 3$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — The Oscillator as a Self-Excited Feedback Loop

The Barkhausen criterion is best understood through a **closed-loop block diagram**. Below is the canonical architecture of any sinusoidal oscillator.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeAmp["Amplifier Block with Gain A"]
    nodeFB["Feedback Network with Transfer Function beta"]
    nodeSum["Summing Junction with Phase Inversion 180 degrees"]
    nodeOut["Oscillating Output Vout"]
    nodeFbk["Feedback Signal Vfb equal to beta times Vout"]
    nodeIn["Input Port Set to Zero for Self Oscillation"]
    nodeStart["Startup Condition vert A beta vert greater than 1"]
    nodeSteady["Steady State Condition vert A beta vert equals 1"]
    nodePhase["Phase Condition angle A beta equals 0 or 360 deg"]

    nodeIn --> nodeSum
    nodeSum --> nodeAmp
    nodeAmp --> nodeOut
    nodeOut --> nodeFB
    nodeFB --> nodeFbk
    nodeFbk --> nodeSum
    nodeStart -. governs .-> nodeAmp
    nodeSteady -. governs .-> nodeAmp
    nodePhase -. governs .-> nodeSum
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The **solid arrows** show the signal-flow direction. The **dotted arrows** show the *constraints* that the Barkhausen criterion imposes on the gain and phase of the loop.

---

## 4.2 State-Transition Diagram — How an Oscillator Reaches Steady State

This Mermaid state diagram shows the **time evolution** of the loop gain from the moment power is switched on.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Off
    Off --> Noise: Power ON thermal noise present
    Noise --> BuildUp: vert A beta vert greater than 1
    BuildUp --> Amplify: Each cycle multiplies amplitude by factor A beta
    Amplify --> BuildUp: Continued exponential growth
    BuildUp --> Saturate: Amplitude reaches active device non linearity
    Saturate --> SteadyState: Loop gain pulled down by non linearity
    SteadyState --> SteadyState: vert A beta vert equals 1 sustained oscillation
    SteadyState --> [*]: Power OFF oscillation decays
```

> [!TIP]
> **What to notice:** The state machine has exactly **two stable states** — *Off* and *SteadyState*. The intermediate states (*Noise*, *BuildUp*, *Amplify*, *Saturate*) are transient. The transition from *Saturate* → *SteadyState* is where **amplitude limiting** (by transistor cutoff/saturation or a thermistor/incandescent lamp) effectively reduces the gain so that $\vert A\beta \vert$ becomes exactly 1.

---

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Mapping the Criterion to Real Hardware

This block-level architecture shows how a generic oscillator (Wien Bridge as the worked example) is partitioned into physical sub-blocks, each of which the Barkhausen criterion addresses.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ActiveStage["Active Amplifier Stage Op amp or Transistor"]
        opamp1["High Gain Op Amp A greater than 10000 ideal"]
        gainTrim["Gain Setting Resistors Rf and R1 determine closed loop gain Af"]
    end
    subgraph FeedbackStage["Frequency Selective Feedback Network"]
        seriesRC["Series RC Arm Zs equal to R plus 1 over j omega C"]
        parallelRC["Parallel RC Arm Zp equal to R parallel to 1 over j omega C"]
        betaBlock["Feedback Factor beta equals Zp divided by Zs plus Zp"]
    end
    subgraph PowerStage["Power Supply and Biasing"]
        vcc["Dual Supply plus minus 12 V or plus minus 15 V"]
        bias["DC Bias Network sets quiescent point in class A"]
    end
    subgraph AmplitudeStage["Amplitude Stabilization Network"]
        nonlin["Non Linear Element Thermistor or Diode pair limits amplitude"]
    end
    subgraph OutputStage["Output Coupling and Load"]
        coupling["DC Blocking Capacitor"]
        load["Load Resistor RL of 10 kilo ohm typical"]
    end

    opamp1 --> gainTrim
    seriesRC --> parallelRC
    parallelRC --> betaBlock
    betaBlock --> opamp1
    vcc --> opamp1
    bias --> opamp1
    nonlin --> gainTrim
    gainTrim --> coupling
    coupling --> load
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mapping to Barkhausen:**
> - The `betaBlock` node computes $\beta(j\omega)$ — its **phase** determines $f_o$.
> - The `gainTrim` node sets $A$ — its **magnitude** is tuned to satisfy $A_{min} = 1/\beta$.
> - The `nonlin` node is the **amplitude limiter** that automatically drives the loop gain to 1 at steady state. **Without this block, the oscillator would clip into severe distortion or destroy itself.**

---

## 4.4 Comparison Chart — Magnitude Condition Across Operating Regimes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    regimeDecay["Decay Region vert A beta vert less than 1"]
    regimeCrit["Critical Region vert A beta vert equals 1"]
    regimeGrow["Growth Region vert A beta vert greater than 1"]
    resDecay["Output amplitude decays exponentially to zero"]
    resCrit["Output amplitude constant sustained sinusoid"]
    resGrow["Output amplitude grows exponentially until non linearity clips"]

    regimeDecay --> resDecay
    regimeCrit --> resCrit
    regimeGrow --> resGrow
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question Paper]`

> **Q:** *State and explain the Barkhausen criterion for sustained oscillations.*

#### Model Answer (Word Count ~120, 3-Mark Depth):

The Barkhausen criterion gives the two conditions for a feedback circuit to operate as a **sinusoidal oscillator** without any external input signal.

**1. Magnitude Condition:** The magnitude of the loop gain $A\beta$ must equal unity, i.e., $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$. This ensures that the feedback signal has exactly the same amplitude as the original input it replaces, leading to **energy balance** per cycle.

**2. Phase Condition:** The total phase shift around the closed loop must be zero degrees (or an integer multiple of 360°), i.e., $\angle A\beta = 0^\circ$ or $n \times 360^\circ$. This guarantees **regenerative (positive) feedback** so the returning signal reinforces, rather than cancels, the original.

A practical oscillator initially satisfies $\vert A\beta \vert > 1$ to let thermal noise build up, and settles to $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$ at steady state due to device non-linearity.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **Q:** *Why does the Barkhausen magnitude condition require $\vert A\beta \vert > 1$ at the instant of switching on the oscillator, and $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$ only at steady state?*

#### Model Answer (Word Count ~110, 3-Mark Depth):

At switch-on, no signal exists in the circuit — only thermal noise of micro-volt amplitude is present. For the oscillation to **build up** from this tiny noise to a useful output level (volts), each cycle of feedback must **amplify** the signal. This is possible only if the magnitude of the loop gain is **strictly greater than one**, i.e., $\vert A\beta \vert > 1$. If $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$ at startup, the noise would merely be sustained at micro-volt level — useless for any practical purpose.

Once the amplitude grows, the active device enters its **non-linear region** (cutoff or saturation for transistors; output saturation for op-amps). The effective gain $A$ of the device **drops**, dragging the product $\vert A\beta \vert$ down from its initial value > 1 to exactly **1** in steady state. At this point, energy in = energy out, and a **sustained, undistorted sinusoid** is produced.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE regulation, each Part-B question carries 14 marks with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. The student answers **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Repeated Pattern]`

> **Q (a)** [7 Marks] *Derive the Barkhausen criterion for oscillation starting from the closed-loop gain expression of a feedback amplifier.*
>
> **Q (b)** [7 Marks] *A Wien Bridge oscillator uses $R = 8.2 \text{ k}\Omega$ and $C = 4.7 \text{ nF}$. Calculate (i) the frequency of oscillation, (ii) the value of feedback factor $\beta$, and (iii) the minimum amplifier gain required to sustain oscillations.*

#### Model Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — State the closed-loop gain formula (1 mark):**

For a negative-feedback amplifier with open-loop gain $A$ and feedback factor $\beta$:

$$A_f = \frac{A}{1 + A\beta}$$

**Step 2 — Impose the no-input condition (1 mark):**

For a self-sustaining oscillator, the external input $V_{in}$ is set to zero, but the output $V_o$ must be non-zero. From $V_o(1 + A\beta) = A V_{in}$, setting $V_{in} = 0$ gives:

$$V_o (1 + A\beta) = 0$$

**Step 3 — Conclude the loop-gain equation (1 mark):**

For a non-trivial $V_o \neq 0$, the term in parentheses must be zero:

$$1 + A\beta = 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad A\beta = -1$$

**Step 4 — Decompose into magnitude and phase (2 marks):**

In polar form, $A\beta = \vert A\beta \vert e^{j\angle A\beta} = 1 \cdot e^{j\pi}$ gives two real conditions:

$$\vert A\beta \vert = 1 \quad\text{(magnitude condition)}$$

$$\angle A\beta = 180^\circ \equiv 0^\circ \pmod{360^\circ} \quad\text{(phase condition)}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to loop-phase form and state physical interpretation (2 marks):**

Since the negative-feedback summing junction inherently contributes 180° of phase, the amplifier-feedback chain must contribute the complementary 180° to make the **total loop phase = 360° ≡ 0°**. The magnitude condition ensures energy in = energy lost per cycle, while the phase condition ensures positive (regenerative) feedback at the oscillation frequency.

> **End of part (a) — 7 marks distributed as 1+1+1+2+2.**

#### Model Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** $R = 8.2 \text{ k}\Omega = 8.2 \times 10^3 \text{ }\Omega$, $\quad C = 4.7 \text{ nF} = 4.7 \times 10^{-9} \text{ F}$

**Part (i) — Frequency of oscillation (3 marks):**

For a Wien Bridge, the frequency of oscillation is given by:

$$f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$$

Substituting:

$$f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi \times (8.2 \times 10^3) \times (4.7 \times 10^{-9})}$$

$$f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 38.54 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$f_o = \frac{1}{2.4218 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 4129.4 \text{ Hz} \approx 4.13 \text{ kHz}$$

**Part (ii) — Feedback factor $\beta$ (2 marks):**

For a Wien Bridge at $\omega = \omega_o$, the feedback network (series RC and parallel RC arms) has:

$$\beta = \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.333 \quad \text{(purely real, positive)}$$

**Part (iii) — Minimum amplifier gain (2 marks):**

Applying the magnitude condition $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$:

$$A_{min} = \frac{1}{\beta} = \frac{1}{1/3} = 3$$

> **Final Answer Box (for KTU answer sheet):**
> $f_o \approx 4.13$ kHz, $\quad \beta = 1/3$, $\quad A_{min} = 3$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Similar Pattern]`

> **Q (a)** [7 Marks] *Explain with a neat block diagram how an oscillator differs from a feedback amplifier. State the Barkhausen criterion. Why is the condition $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$ called the "sustained oscillation" condition and not the "start-up" condition?*
>
> **Q (b)** [7 Marks] *For an RC phase-shift oscillator, derive an expression for the frequency of oscillation and the minimum gain required to sustain oscillation. Assume an ideal op-amp with all three RC sections identical ($R$ and $C$ each).*

#### Model Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Difference between Oscillator and Feedback Amplifier (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Feedback Amplifier | Oscillator |
|---|---|---|
| External input | Required (has a $V_{in}$ port) | No input — $V_{in} = 0$ |
| Purpose | Amplify an external signal | Generate a new signal from noise |
| Operating region | Magnitude condition $\vert A\beta \vert < 1$ to avoid self-oscillation | Magnitude condition $\vert A\beta \vert \geq 1$ for oscillation |
| Output waveform | Faithful reproduction of input | Pure sinusoid at $\omega_o$ |

A **block diagram** showing the two systems side-by-side:

```
FEEDBACK AMPLIFIER:        OSCILLATOR:
  V_in → [Σ] → [A] → V_o     (no V_in) → [Σ] → [A] → V_o
              ↑   ↓                            ↑   ↓
              └─── [β] ───┘                    └─── [β] ───┘
   (with β < 1/A)                   (with β such that Aβ = 1∠0°)
```

**Barkhausen Criterion Statement (2 marks):** Magnitude condition $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$, phase condition $\angle A\beta = 0°$ (or 360°).

**Why $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$ is the *sustained* and not the *start-up* condition (2 marks):** At switch-on, only thermal noise exists. For this noise to build up to a usable amplitude, each cycle must **amplify** the signal, requiring $\vert A\beta \vert > 1$. Once the amplitude grows, the active device's non-linearity limits it and effectively reduces $A$ until $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$. Hence the *equality* holds only at **steady state**; the *inequality > 1* holds during **start-up**.

#### Model Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Setup:** An RC phase-shift oscillator uses an inverting op-amp (gain $-A$, where $A > 0$) with three identical RC sections in the feedback path. The feedback network transfer function is:

$$\beta(j\omega) = \frac{1}{1 - 5\alpha^2 + j(\alpha^3 - 6\alpha)}$$

where $\alpha = \omega RC$. This is the standard textbook result.

**Step 1 — Apply the phase condition (2 marks):**

For oscillation, $\angle \beta(j\omega_o) = 180°$ (to cancel the 180° from the inverting amplifier, giving total 0°). The imaginary part of the denominator must be zero:

$$\alpha^3 - 6\alpha = 0$$

$$\alpha(\alpha^2 - 6) = 0$$

The non-trivial root is $\alpha^2 = 6$, so $\alpha = \sqrt{6}$ (positive root).

**Step 2 — Derive the frequency of oscillation (2 marks):**

$$\alpha = \omega_o R C = \sqrt{6}$$

$$\omega_o = \frac{\sqrt{6}}{RC} = \frac{1}{RC\sqrt{6}}$$

$$f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6}}$$

**Step 3 — Find the minimum gain (2 marks):**

At $\omega = \omega_o$, $\alpha^2 = 6$, so the real part of the denominator is:

$$1 - 5\alpha^2 = 1 - 5(6) = 1 - 30 = -29$$

Therefore:

$$\beta(\omega_o) = \frac{1}{-29} = -\frac{1}{29}$$

The magnitude is $\vert \beta \vert = 1/29$. The inverting amplifier has gain $-A$ (magnitude $A$). The loop magnitude is:

$$\vert A\beta \vert = A \cdot \frac{1}{29} = 1 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad A_{min} = 29$$

**Step 4 — Conclude the inverting-amp gain:**

For the closed-loop op-amp configuration with resistors $R_f$ and $R_1$:

$$A = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1} \geq 29 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad R_f \geq 28 R_1$$

> **Final Answer Box:**
> $f_o = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6}}$, $\quad A_{min} = 29$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones (read carefully before writing your exam):**
> 1. **Quoting the phase as 180° instead of 0° / 360°:** KTU's standard answer key expects **$\angle A\beta = 0°$** (the *loop* phase). Writing 180° is *partially* correct only if you explicitly mention the 180° contribution from the summing junction. Many students lose 1 mark here.
> 2. **Forgetting the startup condition $\vert A\beta \vert > 1$:** Marks are specifically reserved for distinguishing the startup (>1) and steady-state (=1) cases. Writing only "$\vert A\beta \vert = 1$" is incomplete.
> 3. **Mixing up $\beta$ with closed-loop gain $A_f$:** $\beta$ is the *passive* feedback factor (always $\leq 1$), while $A_f$ is the *amplifier's* closed-loop gain. They are *not* the same.
> 4. **Skipping the final "Final Answer Box":** KTU examiners scan for a crisp boxed final answer. Missing it costs the "presentation mark" (typically 1 mark).
> 5. **Unit errors in numerical problems:** $R$ must be in ohms, $C$ in farads, $L$ in henries. Mixing k$\Omega$ with $\mu$F without conversion is a guaranteed 1-mark penalty.
> 6. **Not stating the *physical* meaning of the conditions:** The phrase "energy balance per cycle" for $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$ and "regenerative feedback" for the phase condition is what examiners look for as keywords.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute Reading):**

- ✅ Barkhausen criterion has **two conditions** — magnitude ($\vert A\beta \vert = 1$) and phase ($\angle A\beta = 0°$ or $n \cdot 360°$).
- ✅ Derived from $A_f = \dfrac{A}{1 + A\beta}$ with the input set to zero.
- ✅ Startup requires $\vert A\beta \vert > 1$; steady state requires $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$.
- ✅ Decay (no oscillation) when $\vert A\beta \vert < 1$.
- ✅ The phase condition determines the **frequency** of oscillation; the magnitude condition sets the **minimum amplifier gain** $A_{min} = 1/\vert \beta \vert$.
- ✅ For oscillators using an **inverting amplifier** (180° inherent phase shift), the feedback network must contribute an *additional* 180°.
- ✅ Common KTU oscillator formulae:
   - **RC Phase Shift:** $f_o = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC\sqrt{6}}$, $\quad A_{min} = 29$
   - **Wien Bridge:** $f_o = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$, $\quad A_{min} = 3$
   - **Hartley:** $f_o = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L_T C}}$ with $L_T = L_1 + L_2$
   - **Colpitts:** $f_o = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L \cdot \dfrac{C_1 C_2}{C_1+C_2}}}$
- ✅ Amplitude stabilization is achieved by **non-linear elements** — transistor saturation/cutoff, thermistor (incandescent lamp), or diode limiter.
- ✅ $\beta$ is always **dimensionless**; the loop gain $A\beta$ is dimensionless; the frequency $f_o$ has units of Hz.
- ✅ **Crystal oscillators** are the most stable because their $\beta$ is essentially 1 at the crystal's series or parallel resonance, allowing $A_{min} \approx 1$.
- ✅ **Barkhausen's name** is associated with **Heinrich Barkhausen** (1881–1956), a German physicist. The criterion is sometimes called the **Barkhausen stability criterion**.
- ✅ Always remember: **"$\vert A\beta \vert = 1$ is the steady-state song, $\vert A\beta \vert > 1$ is the start-up shout, and $\vert A\beta \vert < 1$ is the silent fade-out."**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification of Oscillators - RC Phase Shift
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET302 - ELECTRONIC DEVICES AND CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Classification of Oscillators - RC Phase Shift

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is an Oscillator?

An **electronic oscillator** is a non-rotating active circuit that converts DC power from the supply into an AC output signal without requiring any external AC input. It produces a periodic, continuously varying signal (typically sinusoidal) at a precisely determined frequency. Oscillators are fundamental building blocks of virtually every electronic system — they are the "heartbeat" of wireless transmitters, microprocessors, and signal generators.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, an oscillator is a **positive feedback amplifier that satisfies the Barkhausen Criterion**:
- **Loop Gain Magnitude:** $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$
- **Loop Phase Shift:** $\angle A\beta = 0°$ (or an integer multiple of $360°$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - PCAET302 Module 4):**
> Students must be able to **classify oscillators** based on feedback components, frequency range, output waveform, and active device. The **RC Phase Shift Oscillator** is a low-frequency audio-range oscillator (20 Hz to 100 kHz) that uses only resistors and capacitors in its frequency-determining network.

---

### 1.2 Classification of Oscillators

Oscillators are classified along several engineering dimensions:

#### A) Based on Frequency-Determining (Feedback) Components

| Sub-Class | Frequency Range | Typical Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **RC Oscillators** | **1 Hz – 1 MHz** | Audio generation, function generators, tone generators |
| **LC Oscillators** | **1 MHz – 1 GHz** | RF transmitters, IF stages, local oscillators |
| **Crystal Oscillators** | **Fixed (kHz – GHz)** | Clocks in digital ICs, microcontrollers, watches |
| **SAW / MEMS Oscillators** | **MHz – GHz** | Mobile phones, filters |
| **Cavity / Klystron / Magnetron** | **GHz – THz** | Radar, microwave ovens, satellite comms |

#### B) Based on Output Waveform Shape
- **Sinusoidal Oscillators** → RC Phase Shift, Wien Bridge, Hartley, Colpitts, Crystal, Clapp
- **Non-Sinusoidal (Relaxation) Oscillators** → Astable Multivibrator, UJT Relaxation, Blocking Oscillator

#### C) Based on the Active Device Used
- **Transistor Oscillators** (BJT / FET)
- **Op-Amp Oscillators** (using operational amplifiers as the gain stage)

#### D) Based on Coupling Method
- **Armstrong / Tickler Coil Oscillator** (transformer coupled)
- **Tuned Collector / Tuned Drain Oscillator**
- **RC-Coupled Feedback Oscillators**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why classify?** Different applications demand different frequencies, stabilities, and waveform purities. RC oscillators are cheap and work at low audio frequencies where inductors become impractically large. LC oscillators dominate RF. Crystal oscillators dominate where precision is paramount.

---

### 1.3 RC Phase Shift Oscillator — Intuitive Overview

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Imagine pushing a child on a swing. To keep the swing oscillating continuously, you must give a small push at exactly the right moment — when the swing is at its highest point, returning to you. If you push too early or too late, the swing will slow down and stop.

Now imagine you are pushing the swing from behind (so your push is in the *opposite* direction to the swing's motion). The swing itself is already providing some of the necessary motion. In an RC Phase Shift Oscillator:
- The **amplifier** is you — providing the periodic push.
- The **RC ladder network** is the swing path — it gradually shifts the phase of the returning signal.
- The **transistor/op-amp** inverts the signal (180°), so the feedback network must also shift by **180°** to produce a net 360° (or 0°) loop phase.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The RC Phase Shift Oscillator** uses **three identical RC sections** in the feedback path. Each RC section provides approximately **60° of phase shift**, totaling **180°**. Combined with the **180° phase inversion of the CE amplifier**, the **Barkhausen phase condition (360° total)** is satisfied. The frequency of oscillation is determined by the RC time constant.

---

### 1.4 Why "Phase Shift"?

A single RC section can provide a phase shift anywhere between 0° and 90°, depending on frequency. To produce exactly 180° with simple RC sections, three sections are cascaded — each contributing 60°. This avoids the need for bulky inductors and is the key engineering trick that makes RC oscillators compact and cheap.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
