## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Block Architecture of an Oscillator

Every sinusoidal oscillator consists of two essential blocks:

$$\boxed{\text{Oscillator} = \text{Amplifier (A)} + \text{Positive Feedback Network } (\beta)}$$

For oscillations to build up and sustain, **Barkhausen's Criterion** must be met:
$$|A\beta| \geq 1 \quad \text{and} \quad \angle A\beta = 2n\pi, \quad n = 0, 1, 2, \dots$$

In practice:
- At **start-up**, $|A\beta| > 1$ so oscillations grow.
- As amplitude increases, **non-linearities** (transistor saturation/cutoff, op-amp slew limiting) reduce the gain.
- At **steady state**, $|A\beta| = 1$ exactly — sinusoidal output.

---

### 2.2 RC Phase Shift Oscillator — Operating Principle

#### Circuit Topology

The RC Phase Shift Oscillator uses:
1. **One inverting amplifier** (BJT in Common Emitter, or Op-Amp in inverting configuration) — provides **180° phase shift** and voltage gain $A$.
2. **A cascaded RC ladder network** in the feedback path — provides an additional **180° phase shift** at the oscillation frequency.
3. The feedback network consists of **three identical RC sections**. Each section is typically a high-pass type (series C, shunt R) or low-pass type (series R, shunt C).

#### Why Three Sections?

- One RC section gives at most 90° phase shift — and at exactly 90° the attenuation is infinite.
- Two RC sections give 180° only at infinite frequency (impractical).
- **Three RC sections** give exactly 180° at a finite, useful frequency, with finite attenuation that the amplifier can compensate.

#### Frequency Selection Mechanism

Each RC section is a frequency-selective network. Only at one specific frequency does the total phase shift through all three sections equal exactly 180°. At this frequency, the feedback becomes **positive** (after the amplifier's 180° inversion), and oscillation occurs.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** Memorize the frequency formula and the minimum gain condition. Most numerical problems in Module 4 of PCAET302 revolve around these two equations.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Frequency of Oscillation** | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC \sqrt{6}}$ | $f_0$ = oscillation frequency | Hz |
| 2 | **Angular Frequency** | $\omega_0 = \dfrac{1}{RC\sqrt{6}}$ | $\omega_0 = 2\pi f_0$ | rad/s |
| 3 | **Phase Shift per RC Section** | $\phi_{\text{section}} = 60°$ | Total = $3 \times 60° = 180°$ | degrees |
| 4 | **Minimum Voltage Gain** (BJT, ideal) | $\vert A_v \vert_{\min} = 29$ | Neglecting loading | — (ratio) |
| 5 | **Minimum Voltage Gain** (Op-Amp) | $\dfrac{R_f}{R_1} \geq 29$ | $R_f$, $R_1$ are feedback resistors | — (ratio) |
| 6 | **Minimum $h_{fe}$ (BJT, ideal)** | $h_{fe(\min)} = 29$ | When $r_o \to \infty$, $R_C \to 0$ | — |
| 7 | **Realistic BJT Gain Condition** | $h_{fe} \geq 23 + \dfrac{4(R_C + R_E)}{R_E} + \dfrac{29 R_C}{R_E}$ | Including loading | — |
| 8 | **Attenuation of RC Ladder** | $\vert \beta \vert = \dfrac{1}{29}$ | At oscillation frequency | — |
| 9 | **Barkhausen Loop Gain** | $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$ | Sustained oscillation condition | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Mnemonic:** **"3 × 60° = 180°"** (RC network) **+ 180° (amplifier inversion) = 360° = 0°** (Barkhausen phase). And **$|A| = 29$, $|\beta| = 1/29$, so $|A\beta| = 1$**.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Audio Signal Generators:** Used as low-cost audio-frequency tone generators in laboratory instruments.
- **Musical Instruments:** Electronic organs, synthesizers use RC oscillators for tone generation.
- **Function Generators:** The classic HP/Agilent function generators used RC phase shift for sine waves.
- **Boat Sonar and Ultrasonic Cleaners:** Modified RC designs push into the low ultrasonic range.
- **Phase-Locked Loops (PLLs):** RC oscillator as the Voltage-Controlled Oscillator (VCO) for low-frequency PLLs.

---

### 2.5 Advantages and Limitations

**Advantages:**
- Simple, economical circuit using only R, C, and a transistor/op-amp.
- Good frequency stability at audio frequencies.
- Compact and lightweight (no bulky inductors).
- Easy to tune by varying R or C.

**Limitations:**
- Output is not purely sinusoidal — significant harmonic distortion (~5%).
- Poor frequency stability compared to LC or crystal oscillators.
- High attenuation by the RC network ($\vert \beta \vert = 1/29$) demands high amplifier gain.
- Sensitive to temperature and component tolerances.
- Not suitable for high frequencies (above ~1 MHz).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Frequency of Oscillation for RC Phase Shift Oscillator

#### Circuit Setup

Consider the standard configuration: a Common-Emitter (CE) transistor amplifier with three identical RC sections in the feedback network. Each section consists of a capacitor $C$ in series, followed by a resistor $R$ to ground (high-pass configuration).

**Assumptions (for simplified KTU-level derivation):**
1. The amplifier input impedance $R_i \to \infty$ (loading by the next RC section is neglected).
2. The amplifier output impedance $R_o \to 0$ (the source resistance to the ladder is neglected).
3. All three RC sections are identical: each with resistance $R$ and capacitance $C$.

#### Transfer Function of a Single RC Section

For one high-pass RC section (series $C$, shunt $R$), the voltage divider rule gives:

$$\frac{V_o}{V_i} = \frac{R}{R + \frac{1}{j\omega C}} = \frac{j\omega RC}{1 + j\omega RC}$$

**Phase angle** of this single section:

$$\phi_1 = 90° - \tan^{-1}(\omega RC)$$

#### Phase Condition

For oscillation, the total phase shift through the three RC sections must be exactly $-180°$ (i.e., the network inverts the signal at $f_0$):

$$\phi_{\text{total}} = 3 \phi_1 = -180°$$

$$\Rightarrow \phi_1 = -60°$$

Substituting:

$$90° - \tan^{-1}(\omega_0 RC) = -60°$$

$$\tan^{-1}(\omega_0 RC) = 150°$$

This leads to a contradiction because $\tan^{-1}$ cannot exceed 90°. This indicates our assumption about section type was wrong.

#### Correct Section Configuration

Use the **low-pass** configuration: series $R$, shunt $C$ (resistor $R$ in series with the signal, capacitor $C$ to ground).

Single-section transfer function:

$$\frac{V_o}{V_i} = \frac{1/j\omega C}{R + \frac{1}{j\omega C}} = \frac{1}{1 + j\omega RC}$$

Phase:

$$\phi_1 = -\tan^{-1}(\omega RC)$$

For total $-180°$:

$$-\tan^{-1}(\omega_0 RC) = -\frac{180°}{3} = -60°$$

$$\tan^{-1}(\omega_0 RC) = 60°$$

$$\omega_0 RC = \tan 60° = \sqrt{3}$$

$$\boxed{\omega_0 = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{RC}}$$

#### Accounting for Loading Between Sections

In reality, each RC section loads the previous one. Solving the full network (using KVL/KCL across all three sections) yields the **exact** textbook result.

Let the nodes in the ladder be labeled $V_A$, $V_B$, $V_C$ (output of section 1, 2, 3) and $V_i$ at input. Applying KCL at each node:

At node A (output of section 1):

$$\frac{V_i - V_A}{R} = \frac{V_A}{1/j\omega C} + \frac{V_A - V_B}{R}$$

At node B:

$$\frac{V_A - V_B}{R} = \frac{V_B}{1/j\omega C} + \frac{V_B - V_C}{R}$$

At node C (ladder output):

$$\frac{V_B - V_C}{R} = \frac{V_C}{1/j\omega C}$$

Define $\alpha = \omega RC$ and $\beta = j\alpha$. After algebraic simplification of these three equations, the overall transfer function of the ladder is:

$$\frac{V_C}{V_i} = \frac{1}{(j\alpha)^3 + 6(j\alpha)^2 + 5(j\alpha) + 1}$$

Wait — this is the form for one specific ladder topology. The correct standard textbook result is:

$$\beta(j\omega) = \frac{V_C}{V_i} = \frac{1}{\left(1 - 5\omega^2 R^2 C^2\right) + j\left(6\omega RC - \omega^3 R^3 C^3\right)}$$

For purely real $\beta$ (zero imaginary part) — i.e., phase $= -180°$ — set imaginary denominator to zero:

$$6\omega_0 RC - \omega_0^3 R^3 C^3 = 0$$

$$\omega_0 RC \left(6 - \omega_0^2 R^2 C^2\right) = 0$$

Since $\omega_0 \neq 0$:

$$\omega_0^2 R^2 C^2 = 6$$

$$\omega_0 = \frac{1}{RC\sqrt{6}}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC \sqrt{6}}}$$

#### Magnitude at Oscillation Frequency

Substitute $\omega_0 RC = \sqrt{6}$ into the real part of the denominator:

$$\text{Re}(\text{denominator}) = 1 - 5\omega_0^2 R^2 C^2 = 1 - 5(6) = 1 - 30 = -29$$

Magnitude of the transfer function:

$$\vert \beta \vert = \frac{1}{\vert 1 - 5\omega_0^2 R^2 C^2 \vert} = \frac{1}{29}$$

$$\boxed{\vert \beta \vert = \frac{1}{29}}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Minimum Gain Condition (Barkhausen Magnitude)

Applying the Barkhausen magnitude condition $\vert A \beta \vert = 1$:

$$\vert A \vert = \frac{1}{\vert \beta \vert} = 29$$

For a CE transistor amplifier with the simplified assumptions:

$$A_v = \frac{h_{fe} \cdot R_C}{R_E + h_{ie}}$$

For oscillation, $A_v \geq 29$, which yields approximately $h_{fe} \geq 29$ (when bias resistors and $r_o$ are large enough to be neglected — i.e., the "ideal" KTU textbook assumption).

For a non-ideal case (including loading), the more accurate result is:

$$\boxed{h_{fe} \geq 23 + \frac{4(R_C + R_E)}{R_E} + \frac{29 R_C}{R_E}}$$

For an op-amp based RC phase shift oscillator, the inverting amplifier has gain:

$$A_v = -\frac{R_f}{R_1}$$

For oscillation:

$$\frac{R_f}{R_1} \geq 29$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** Design an RC phase shift oscillator to generate a **2 kHz** sine wave using $C = 10 \text{ nF}$. Find $R$, the minimum gain, and the minimum $h_{fe}$.

**Solution:**

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi R C \sqrt{6}}$$

$$2000 = \frac{1}{2\pi \cdot R \cdot (10 \times 10^{-9}) \cdot \sqrt{6}}$$

Solving for $R$:

$$R = \frac{1}{2\pi \cdot 2000 \cdot 10^{-8} \cdot \sqrt{6}}$$

$$R = \frac{1}{4\pi \times 10^{-5} \cdot 2.449}$$

$$R = \frac{1}{3.078 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\boxed{R \approx 3.25 \text{ k}\Omega}$$

Minimum gain magnitude:

$$\vert A_v \vert_{\min} = 29$$

Minimum $h_{fe}$:

$$h_{fe(\min)} \approx 29$$

**Practical choice:** Use $h_{fe} \geq 50$ to ensure reliable start-up and compensate for component tolerances.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Step-by-Step Summary

| Step | Analysis Task | Output |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Identify topology (transistor CE vs op-amp) | Choose gain stage |
| 2 | Apply KCL/KVL at each RC ladder node | Set up 3 equations in 3 unknowns |
| 3 | Solve for transfer function $\beta(j\omega)$ | Complex rational function |
| 4 | Set imaginary part = 0 (phase = 180°) | Frequency $\omega_0 = \frac{1}{RC\sqrt{6}}$ |
| 5 | Substitute $\omega_0$ into real part | Magnitude $\vert \beta \vert = 1/29$ |
| 6 | Apply $|A\beta| = 1$ | Minimum gain $\vert A \vert = 29$ |
| 7 | Translate gain to component values | $h_{fe}$ for BJT, $R_f/R_1$ for op-amp |

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wien Bridge Oscillator (WBO):** A *sinusoidal low-frequency oscillator* that employs a **Wien Bridge network** (a frequency-selective positive feedback network comprising a series RC arm and a parallel RC arm) coupled with a non-inverting amplifier to generate low-distortion sine waves over the **audio frequency range (typically 1 Hz to 1 MHz)**.

The Wien Bridge is a special lead-lag network whose voltage transfer function exhibits a **maximum at a specific center frequency** $f_0$ and provides **zero phase shift** at that frequency. When embedded in the positive feedback loop of an amplifier satisfying the **Barkhausen Criterion**, the circuit self-oscillates at $f_0$ and produces a clean sinusoidal waveform.

The standard operational frequencies in KTU laboratory practice and examination questions are **1 kHz**, **10 kHz**, and **100 kHz**, making the Wien Bridge the canonical *audio-frequency sine wave generator* studied in electronic circuits.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Tuned Pendulum Clock"**
> Imagine a grandfather clock. It has a pendulum of a specific length that swings naturally at one frequency, and a tiny escapement mechanism (the amplifier + nonlinear element) pushes it just enough on every swing to keep it going without letting it die out or grow uncontrollably. The Wien Bridge Oscillator is essentially an *electronic pendulum*: the lead-lag network (Wien bridge) acts as the pendulum—vibrating naturally at one preferred frequency—and the op-amp (with its automatic gain control) acts as the escapement, providing just the right push on each cycle to sustain oscillation.

In simpler words:
- **Series RC network** = *the "spring" that pushes the signal ahead* (lead network, +phase)
- **Parallel RC network** = *the "damper" that pulls the signal back* (lag network, −phase)
- At one special frequency, **the two effects exactly cancel**, leaving the signal passing through the network **untouched in phase**, with exactly **1/3 of its original amplitude**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following standards are universally accepted in KTU examination practice:

- **Feedback factor at resonance:** $\beta = \dfrac{1}{3}$ (dimensionless)
- **Required closed-loop gain:** $A_v = 3$ (dimensionless)
- **Center / oscillation frequency:** $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ in **Hz**
- **Angular frequency:** $\omega_0 = \dfrac{1}{RC}$ in **rad/s**
- **Typical component matching:** $R_1 = R_2 = R$ and $C_1 = C_2 = C$ for textbook derivations

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Block-Level Operational Architecture

The Wien Bridge Oscillator consists of two functional blocks:

1. **Frequency-Selective Positive Feedback Network (Wien Bridge / Lead-Lag Network):**
   - **Series Arm:** $R_1$ in series with $C_1$ — this introduces a *leading* phase.
   - **Parallel Arm:** $R_2$ in parallel with $C_2$ — this introduces a *lagging* phase.
   - The output of this network is fed back to the **non-inverting input** of the op-amp.

2. **Non-Inverting Amplifier (Negative Feedback Amplifier):**
   - Standard op-amp configuration with $R_f$ (feedback) and $R_1'$ (ground) setting the gain.
   - Closed-loop gain: $A = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1'}$.
   - For sustained oscillation, $A = 3$, hence $R_f = 2R_1'$.

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Stage

| Step | Stage Action | Why it Matters |
|------|--------------|----------------|
| 1 | Series RC arm produces +phase (lead) | Compensates for the phase lag in the parallel arm |
| 2 | Parallel RC arm produces −phase (lag) | Cancels the lead at one specific frequency |
| 3 | At $f_0$, total phase = 0° | Satisfies **Barkhausen's phase condition** |
| 4 | Network gain is exactly 1/3 | Forces amplifier to provide gain of 3 to satisfy amplitude condition |
| 5 | Op-amp in non-inverting mode | Provides gain of 3, no phase inversion |
| 6 | Nonlinear amplitude stabilization (diodes / thermistor / JFET) | Prevents amplitude growth or decay — locks steady-state output |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **all high-yield formulas** for the Wien Bridge Oscillator. Memorize this table — it covers roughly 80% of Part A and Part B marks in KTU exams.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Unit | Boundary Condition |
|---|----------|---------|------|-------------------|
| 1 | Oscillation frequency | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | Hz | Valid when $R_1 = R_2 = R$, $C_1 = C_2 = C$ |
| 2 | Angular frequency | $\omega_0 = \dfrac{1}{RC}$ | rad/s | $2\pi f_0$ |
| 3 | Feedback factor (magnitude) | $\vert\beta\vert = \dfrac{1}{3}$ | dimensionless | Only at $f = f_0$ |
| 4 | Required amplifier gain | $A_v = 3$ | dimensionless | Barkhausen amplitude condition |
| 5 | Non-inverting amp gain | $A_v = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$ | dimensionless | $R_f = 2R_1$ for $A_v = 3$ |
| 6 | Transfer function of network | $\beta(j\omega) = \dfrac{j\omega RC}{1 - \omega^2 R^2 C^2 + 3j\omega RC}$ | dimensionless | General expression |
| 7 | Phase of $\beta$ | $\phi = 90° - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{3\omega RC}{1 - \omega^2 R^2 C^2}\right)$ | degrees | Zero only at $\omega_0$ |
| 8 | Barkhausen startup condition | $A_v \beta > 1$ | dimensionless | Needed for oscillation to build up |
| 9 | Barkhausen steady-state | $A_v \beta = 1$ | dimensionless | Maintains constant amplitude |
| 10 | Startup gain (typical) | $A_v \approx 3.001$ to $3.05$ | dimensionless | Slightly above 3 to overcome losses |
| 11 | Phase shift of amplifier (non-inv.) | $0°$ | degrees | No phase inversion |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> The Wien Bridge Oscillator is the **workhorse of audio test equipment** and **function generators** found in every KTU electronics laboratory. Practical applications include:
> - **Audio signal generators** for testing amplifiers, speakers, and filters (20 Hz – 20 kHz).
> - **Function generators** (HP/Agilent 33120A, Tektronix AFG1000 series) — internally use Wien-bridge-derived topologies.
> - **Tone detectors** in communication circuits.
> - **Vibration analyzers** and biomedical instrumentation.
> - **Phase-locked loops (PLL)** where the VCO is often Wien-bridge based.

The reason for its dominance in the audio range is its **inherent amplitude stability, low distortion, and ease of frequency tuning** (a single ganged variable resistor or capacitor can vary $f_0$ over a wide range).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!WARNING]
> **Important for KTU exam:** The derivation of the **oscillation frequency** and the **feedback factor** are the two most frequently asked 14-mark questions. Practice writing the steps in this exact sequence.

## 3.1 Derivation of the Transfer Function of the Wien Bridge Network

### Step 1: Define the Impedances

The Wien Bridge network is composed of two arms:
- **Series arm:** $Z_1 = R + \dfrac{1}{sC} = \dfrac{1 + sRC}{sC}$  
- **Parallel arm:** $Z_2 = R \parallel \dfrac{1}{sC} = \dfrac{R \cdot \frac{1}{sC}}{R + \frac{1}{sC}} = \dfrac{R}{1 + sRC}$

### Step 2: Apply the Voltage Divider Rule

The output of the network is taken across $Z_2$, and the input is applied across the series combination of $Z_1 + Z_2$. Hence the transfer function (feedback factor) is:

$$
\beta(s) = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \frac{Z_2}{Z_1 + Z_2}
$$

### Step 3: Substitute and Simplify

Substituting $Z_1$ and $Z_2$:

$$
\beta(s) = \frac{\dfrac{R}{1 + sRC}}{\dfrac{1 + sRC}{sC} + \dfrac{R}{1 + sRC}}
$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $sC(1 + sRC)$:

$$
\beta(s) = \frac{sRC}{1 + 2sRC + s^2 R^2 C^2 + sRC}
$$

### Step 4: Collect Like Terms

The denominator becomes:

$$
1 + 2sRC + s^2 R^2 C^2 + sRC = 1 + 3sRC + s^2 R^2 C^2
$$

Therefore:

$$
\beta(s) = \frac{sRC}{s^2 R^2 C^2 + 3sRC + 1}
$$

This is the **general transfer function** of the Wien Bridge network — a band-pass type response.

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** This is a second-order *band-pass* function. It passes only a narrow band of frequencies centered at $\omega_0$, with maximum amplitude at that center.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Oscillation Frequency

### Step 1: Convert to Frequency Domain

Substitute $s = j\omega$:

$$
\beta(j\omega) = \frac{j\omega RC}{1 - \omega^2 R^2 C^2 + 3j\omega RC}
$$

### Step 2: Apply Barkhausen's Phase Condition

For oscillation, the **total phase shift around the loop must be 0°** (or an integer multiple of $360°$). The non-inverting amplifier contributes $0°$ phase shift. Therefore, the Wien network must contribute **0° phase shift** at the oscillation frequency.

### Step 3: Set the Imaginary Part of the Denominator to Zero

For the overall phase of $\beta(j\omega)$ to be zero, the *real part* of the denominator must be zero (because the numerator $j\omega RC$ is purely imaginary and adds $90°$; we need the denominator to also add $-90°$ to give a total of $0°$).

Equating the real part to zero:

$$
1 - \omega^2 R^2 C^2 = 0
$$

### Step 4: Solve for $\omega_0$

$$
\omega_0^2 R^2 C^2 = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_0 = \frac{1}{RC}
$$

Converting to frequency in Hz:

$$
f_0 = \frac{\omega_0}{2\pi} = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the canonical KTU result:** $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ Hz.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Feedback Factor at Resonance

### Step 1: Substitute $\omega_0 = 1/(RC)$ into $\beta(j\omega)$

At $\omega_0$:

$$
\beta(j\omega_0) = \frac{j\omega_0 RC}{1 - \omega_0^2 R^2 C^2 + 3j\omega_0 RC}
$$

Since $\omega_0^2 R^2 C^2 = 1$, the real part of the denominator vanishes:

$$
\beta(j\omega_0) = \frac{j\omega_0 RC}{0 + 3j\omega_0 RC}
$$

### Step 2: Cancel the $j\omega_0 RC$ terms

$$
\beta(j\omega_0) = \frac{1}{3}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** At the oscillation frequency, the Wien Bridge passes exactly **1/3** of the input voltage back to the amplifier, with **zero phase shift**.

## 3.4 Derivation of the Required Amplifier Gain

### Step 1: Apply Barkhausen's Amplitude Condition

For sustained oscillation:

$$
|A_v \beta| = 1
$$

### Step 2: Substitute $\beta = 1/3$

$$
A_v \cdot \frac{1}{3} = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad A_v = 3
$$

### Step 3: Express in Terms of Op-Amp Components

The op-amp is in non-inverting configuration with gain:

$$
A_v = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1'}
$$

Setting $A_v = 3$:

$$
3 = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1'} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{R_f}{R_1'} = 2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_f = 2R_1'
$$

## 3.5 Combined Frequency-Dependent Feedback Factor (Full Magnitude)

For general frequency $f$, the magnitude of $\beta$ is:

$$
\vert\beta\vert = \frac{\omega RC}{\sqrt{(1 - \omega^2 R^2 C^2)^2 + (3\omega RC)^2}}
$$

At $\omega = \omega_0$:

$$
\vert\beta_{max}\vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{0 + 9}} = \frac{1}{3}
$$

This peak value of $\beta$ explains why the oscillator naturally locks to $\omega_0$ — any deviation from $\omega_0$ reduces $\beta$, attenuating those frequency components, and leaving only $f_0$ in the output.

## 3.6 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style Problem)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A Wien Bridge Oscillator uses $R = 10\text{ k}\Omega$ and $C = 0.01\text{ }\mu\text{F}$. Calculate the oscillation frequency and the required values of $R_f$ and $R_1'$ for the op-amp gain stage.

### Solution

**Step 1:** Identify the time constant:

$$
RC = 10 \times 10^3 \times 0.01 \times 10^{-6} = 10^{-4}\text{ s}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the frequency formula:

$$
f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{10^4}{2\pi} = 1591.55\text{ Hz} \approx 1.59\text{ kHz}
$$

**Step 3:** Required gain $A_v = 3$:

$$
A_v = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1'} = 3 \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_f = 2R_1'
$$

**Step 4:** Choose a standard value for $R_1'$. Let $R_1' = 10\text{ k}\Omega$. Then:

$$
R_f = 2 \times 10\text{ k}\Omega = 20\text{ k}\Omega
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** $f_0 \approx 1.59$ kHz, $R_f = 20$ k$\Omega$, $R_1' = 10$ k$\Omega$.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Wien Bridge Oscillator Architecture

> [!NOTE]
> The following diagram shows the **block-level functional architecture** of the Wien Bridge Oscillator, including the closed feedback loop, the lead-lag network, and the gain stage.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DC["DC Supply +Vcc and -Vee"] --> OP["Non-Inverting Amplifier Op-Amp 741 Gain Av equals 3"]
    OP --> OUT["Sine Wave Output Vo"]
    OP -- Negative Feedback via Rf and R1prime --> NFB["Negative Feedback Network Rf in series with R1prime to ground"]
    NFB --> OP
    OUT -- Rout --> NET["Wien Bridge Network Input Vfb"]
    NET["Series Arm R1 in series with C1 and Parallel Arm R2 in parallel with C2"] --> VFB["Feedback Voltage Vfb fed to Non-Inverting Input plus"]
    VFB --> OP
    OP -- BJT or Diode Amplitude Stabilizer --> STAB["Amplitude Control Circuit Thermistor or Zener Diodes"]
    STAB --> OP
    OP --> OUT
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Circuit Topology — Lead-Lag Network

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    IN["Input Node Vin"] --> S1["R1 Resistor in Series"]
    S1 --> S2["C1 Capacitor in Series"]
    S2 --> OUT["Output Node Vfb taken from here"]
    IN --> P1["R2 Resistor in Parallel Branch"]
    IN --> P2["C2 Capacitor in Parallel Branch"]
    P1 --> OUT
    P2 --> OUT
    OUT["Vfb node also marked as common point of both arms to ground reference"]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Frequency Response of the Wien Network

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Very Low Frequency f much less than f0"] -- Attenuates --> R["Network Output small magnitude, positive phase approaching plus 90"]
    B["At f0 equals 1 over 2 pi RC"] -- Maximum --> P["Network Output equals Vin divided by 3, Phase equals 0 degrees"]
    C["Very High Frequency f much greater than f0"] -- Attenuates --> Q["Network Output small magnitude, negative phase approaching minus 90"]
    P -- Oscillation occurs here --> OSC["Wien Bridge Oscillator Locks to this Frequency"]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Complete Op-Amp Wien Bridge Oscillator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VCC["Dual DC Supply plus Vcc and minus Vee"] --> OA["Op-Amp 741 Non-Inverting Configuration"]
    OA["Non-Inverting Input receives Vfb from Wien Network"] -- Inverting input receives negative feedback --> RFB["Rf Feedback Resistor 20 kilo ohm"]
    RFB --> G["R1prime Resistor 10 kilo ohm to ground"]
    G -- Sets gain of 3 --> OA
    OA -- Output Vo sine wave --> LO["Load Speaker or Oscilloscope"]
    OA -- Output fed back through Wien network --> WN["Wien Bridge Network R equals 10 kilo ohm, C equals 0.01 micro farad"]
    WN -- Vfb back to plus input --> OA
    OA -- Nonlinear element for amplitude control --> NL["Two Zener Diodes Anti-parallel in feedback path Rf"]
    NL --> RFB
```

## 4.5 Description of the Functional Blocks

| Block | Function | Typical Component |
|-------|----------|-------------------|
| DC Supply | Powers the op-amp symmetrically | $\pm 12$ V to $\pm 15$ V |
| Non-Inverting Amplifier | Provides gain of 3 with no phase shift | Op-amp 741 |
| Negative Feedback Network | Sets closed-loop gain via $R_f/R_1'$ | Resistors $R_f$ and $R_1'$ |
| Wien Bridge Network | Frequency-selective positive feedback | $R$–$C$ – $R$–$C$ ladder |
| Amplitude Stabilizer | Maintains constant output amplitude | Zener diodes, thermistor, or JFET |
| Output Load | Where the sine wave is delivered | Speaker, CRO, counter |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A QUESTIONS (3 MARKS EACH)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> *Define a Wien Bridge Oscillator. State the formula for its frequency of oscillation.*

**Model Answer:**

> A Wien Bridge Oscillator is a low-frequency sinusoidal oscillator that uses a Wien Bridge (a series-parallel RC lead-lag network) as the frequency-determining positive feedback element, and a non-inverting amplifier as the gain element, to generate low-distortion audio-frequency sine waves.

**Frequency of oscillation:**

$$
f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC} \quad \text{(with } R_1 = R_2 = R \text{ and } C_1 = C_2 = C\text{)}
$$

This is the **only formula a 3-mark Part A question requires** for a "define" question. Marks are split as: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for the formula, 1 mark for stating the conditions ($R_1 = R_2 = R$, $C_1 = C_2 = C$).

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> *What is the value of the feedback factor $\beta$ of a Wien Bridge at the frequency of oscillation? Justify your answer.*

**Model Answer:**

At the frequency of oscillation, the feedback factor of the Wien Bridge is:

$$
\beta = \frac{1}{3}
$$

**Justification:** Applying the voltage divider rule to the Wien network and substituting $\omega = 1/(RC)$ (the zero-phase condition), the transfer function reduces to exactly $1/3$ with zero phase shift, satisfying the Barkhausen criterion.

---

## PART B QUESTIONS — 14 MARKS

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

> **A Wien Bridge Oscillator uses an op-amp with components $R = 5\text{ k}\Omega$ and $C = 0.02\text{ }\mu\text{F}$ in the Wien network. The feedback resistors are $R_f$ and $R_1$.**

**(a) Draw the circuit diagram of a Wien Bridge Oscillator using an op-amp. Explain its working principle.**
**(7 marks)**

**(b) Derive the expression for the frequency of oscillation and the condition for sustained oscillation. Calculate the oscillation frequency and the value of $R_f$ for $R_1 = 10\text{ k}\Omega$.**
**(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.A(a)**

**Circuit Diagram:**

(Refer to the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.4 of these notes.)

**Working Principle:**

The Wien Bridge Oscillator consists of:

1. **Wien Bridge Network** (positive feedback path): Made of a series arm ($R$ in series with $C$) and a parallel arm ($R$ in parallel with $C$). This network acts as a band-pass filter that selects a specific frequency $f_0$.

2. **Non-Inverting Amplifier** (op-amp with gain $A_v = 1 + R_f/R_1$): Provides the necessary amplification without phase inversion.

3. **Negative Feedback Network** ($R_f$ and $R_1$): Stabilizes the gain and controls amplitude.

**Operation:**
- On power-up, random noise contains all frequencies.
- The Wien network allows only the frequency $f_0 = 1/(2\pi RC)$ to pass with maximum gain $\beta = 1/3$ and zero phase shift.
- The op-amp amplifies this signal by gain 3, giving loop gain $A_v \beta = 1$.
- The signal grows until nonlinear amplitude stabilization (diodes/thermistor) limits it to a steady-state sine wave at $f_0$.

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.A(b)**

**Step 1: Derive $\beta(s)$ of the Wien Network**

Series arm impedance:

$$
Z_1 = R + \frac{1}{sC} = \frac{1 + sRC}{sC}
$$

Parallel arm impedance:

$$
Z_2 = R \parallel \frac{1}{sC} = \frac{R}{1 + sRC}
$$

Voltage divider:

$$
\beta(s) = \frac{Z_2}{Z_1 + Z_2} = \frac{sRC}{s^2 R^2 C^2 + 3sRC + 1}
$$

**Step 2: Apply the phase condition**

Substituting $s = j\omega$:

$$
\beta(j\omega) = \frac{j\omega RC}{1 - \omega^2 R^2 C^2 + 3j\omega RC}
$$

For zero phase shift, the real part of the denominator must be zero:

$$
1 - \omega^2 R^2 C^2 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_0 = \frac{1}{RC}
$$

Therefore:

$$
f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}
$$

**Step 3: Magnitude at $f_0$**

$$
\beta(j\omega_0) = \frac{j\omega_0 RC}{3j\omega_0 RC} = \frac{1}{3}
$$

**Step 4: Condition for sustained oscillation (Barkhausen criterion)**

$$
A_v \beta = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad A_v \cdot \frac{1}{3} = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad A_v = 3
$$

$$
1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1} = 3 \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_f = 2R_1
$$

**Step 5: Numerical calculation**

Given $R = 5\text{ k}\Omega$ and $C = 0.02\text{ }\mu\text{F}$:

$$
RC = 5 \times 10^3 \times 0.02 \times 10^{-6} = 10^{-4}\text{ s}
$$

$$
f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^{-4}} = 1591.55\text{ Hz} \approx 1.59\text{ kHz}
$$

For $R_1 = 10\text{ k}\Omega$:

$$
R_f = 2 \times 10\text{ k}\Omega = 20\text{ k}\Omega
$$

> **Final Answer:** $f_0 \approx 1.59$ kHz, $R_f = 20$ k$\Omega$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

> **(a) With the help of a block diagram, explain the principle of operation of a Wien Bridge Oscillator. Discuss the role of the lead-lag network in selecting the frequency of oscillation.**
> **(7 marks)**

> **(b) Starting from the transfer function of the Wien Bridge network, derive the conditions for (i) frequency of oscillation and (ii) minimum amplifier gain required for sustained oscillation. A Wien Bridge Oscillator is to be designed for a frequency of 10 kHz. Choose $C = 0.01\text{ }\mu\text{F}$ and find the required value of $R$.**
> **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.B(a)**

**Block Diagram:**

(Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes.)

**Principle of Operation:**

The Wien Bridge Oscillator is built on the principle of **regenerative (positive) feedback** in which a frequency-selective network in the feedback path ensures that only one frequency is reinforced, leading to a sustained sinusoidal output.

The two functional blocks are:

1. **Lead-Lag Network (Wien Bridge)** — Acts as the frequency selector. The series arm ($R$-$C$ in series) introduces a *leading* phase shift, and the parallel arm ($R$-$C$ in parallel) introduces a *lagging* phase shift. The total phase shift is zero only at one specific frequency $f_0 = 1/(2\pi RC)$. At this frequency, the network passes a fraction $\beta = 1/3$ of the output back to the input.

2. **Non-Inverting Amplifier** — Provides a gain of 3 with no phase inversion, ensuring the total loop gain $A_v \beta = 1$ at $f_0$ (Barkhausen criterion).

**Role of the Lead-Lag Network:**

- The network is a **second-order band-pass filter** with center frequency $f_0$.
- It determines the **frequency of oscillation** through its components $R$ and $C$.
- It provides **zero phase shift at $f_0$**, satisfying the phase condition of Barkhausen.
- At any other frequency, the phase is non-zero and the magnitude is less than 1/3, so those components are rejected.

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.B(b)**

**Derivation of conditions:**

The transfer function of the Wien network (derived in Q.A):

$$
\beta(s) = \frac{sRC}{s^2 R^2 C^2 + 3sRC + 1}
$$

**Substituting $s = j\omega$:**

$$
\beta(j\omega) = \frac{j\omega RC}{1 - \omega^2 R^2 C^2 + 3j\omega RC}
$$

**(i) Frequency of oscillation (Phase condition):**

For the total phase shift around the loop to be 0° (or 360°), since the non-inverting amplifier contributes 0°, the Wien network must contribute 0°. This requires the real part of the denominator to be zero:

$$
1 - \omega_0^2 R^2 C^2 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_0 = \frac{1}{RC}
$$

$$
\boxed{f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}}
$$

**(ii) Minimum amplifier gain (Amplitude condition):**

At $\omega_0$, $\beta = 1/3$. For sustained oscillation:

$$
A_v \cdot \beta = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad A_{v,\min} = 3
$$

**Numerical Calculation for $f_0 = 10$ kHz, $C = 0.01\text{ }\mu\text{F}$:**

Rearranging the frequency formula:

$$
R = \frac{1}{2\pi f_0 C} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10 \times 10^3 \times 0.01 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
R = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{10^4}{2\pi} = 1591.55\text{ }\Omega \approx 1.59\text{ k}\Omega
$$

> **Final Answer:** $R \approx 1.59$ k$\Omega$

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to mention the matching condition** $R_1 = R_2 = R$ and $C_1 = C_2 = C$ when stating the formula $f_0 = 1/(2\pi RC)$. Examiners deduct **1 full mark** if this is omitted.
> 2. **Confusing the inverting and non-inverting inputs** of the op-amp. The Wien network must be connected to the **non-inverting (+)** input, and the $R_f$–$R_1$ network to the **inverting (−)** input. A wrong connection is a conceptual error worth **2 marks**.
> 3. **Using $A_v = 1 - R_f/R_1$ (inverting formula)** instead of $1 + R_f/R_1$. This is a frequent sign error worth **1 mark**.
> 4. **Not stating Barkhausen's two conditions** (loop gain = 1 AND phase shift = 0°). Half-mark deducted for each missing condition.
> 5. **Forgetting the units** in numerical answers (e.g., writing 1591 instead of 1.59 kHz or 1591 Hz).
> 6. **Writing "$\omega_0 = 1/(2\pi RC)$"** — confusing angular and linear frequency. Remember: $\omega_0 = 1/(RC)$ in rad/s, $f_0 = 1/(2\pi RC)$ in Hz.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Wien Bridge Oscillator**

- **Definition:** Sinusoidal oscillator using a Wien Bridge (lead-lag RC network) as the positive feedback element.
- **Key formula:** $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ Hz — **memorize verbatim**.
- **Feedback factor at resonance:** $\beta = \dfrac{1}{3}$ — **memorize verbatim**.
- **Required amplifier gain:** $A_v = 3$ — **memorize verbatim**.
- **Op-amp configuration:** **Non-inverting**; gain $A_v = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$, hence $R_f = 2R_1$.
- **Network type:** **Series RC** in series with **Parallel RC** — the series arm is a *lead* network, the parallel arm is a *lag* network.
- **Phase response:** $+\arctan(\omega RC)$ from the parallel arm cancelled by $-\arctan(1/\omega RC)$ from the series arm — net phase is zero only at $\omega_0 = 1/(RC)$.
- **Barkhausen Criterion:** Two conditions must be met — (1) loop gain $|A_v \beta| = 1$, (2) total phase shift = $0°$ (or $360°$).
- **Startup vs. steady-state:** Startup requires $A_v \beta > 1$ (e.g., $A_v = 3.05$); steady-state requires $A_v \beta = 1$ exactly.
- **Amplitude stabilization:** Use **Zener diodes** (anti-parallel), **thermistor** (in $R_f$ position), or **JFET** (as variable resistor) in the negative feedback path to automatically adjust gain and prevent amplitude runaway.
- **Phase shift of amplifier (non-inverting):** Exactly **0°** — this is why the non-inverting configuration is essential.
- **Frequency range:** Typically **1 Hz to 1 MHz** (audio range), making it the most widely used low-frequency oscillator.
- **Application areas:** Function generators, audio test equipment, sine wave inverters, tone generators, PLL VCOs.
- **Distinguishing feature:** Unlike RC phase shift oscillator (which uses three RC sections and an inverting amplifier), the Wien Bridge uses only **two RC sections** and a **non-inverting amplifier** — this is a frequently-asked comparison question worth 7 marks.
- **Why $R_1 = R_2$, $C_1 = C_2$?** Simplifies the math, gives a clean formula. If they are unequal, $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{R_1 R_2 C_1 C_2}}$ — examiners may give this as a "twist" question for extra marks.
- **Numerical tip:** Always keep the time constant $RC$ in **seconds** before plugging into $f_0 = 1/(2\pi RC)$. Common errors arise from unit mismatches (k$\Omega \times \mu$F = $10^{-3}$ s).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hartley
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET302 - ELECTRONIC DEVICES AND CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Hartley

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 — The Hartley Oscillator

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Hartley Oscillator** is a sinusoidal LC feedback oscillator invented by **Ralph V. L. Hartley in 1915**. It employs a **tank circuit** (frequency-determining network) consisting of **two inductors** $L_1$ and $L_2$ in series, mutually (or self) coupled, with a **single capacitor** $C$ placed in parallel across the series combination of the inductors. The feedback signal required to sustain oscillations is derived from the **voltage divider action** of the two inductor reactances.

> [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4 — Oscillators):**
> The Hartley Oscillator is grouped under **LC Feedback Oscillators**. Along with the Colpitts and Crystal oscillators, it forms the core of high-frequency waveform generation topics. Expect either a **direct 7-mark derivation** or a **14-mark "circuit + derivation" combined question** in the End Semester Examination (ESE).

> [!NOTE] > **Standard Nomenclature Used in KTU Textbooks:**
> - $L_1$: Inductor connected between the **collector/output** and the **tap point (emitter/ground reference)**.
> - $L_2$: Inductor connected between the **tap point** and the **base/feedback node**.
> - $C$: Tank capacitor across the series combination.
> - $M$: Mutual inductance between $L_1$ and $L_2$ (considering a tapped inductor with a common core).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Two-String Pendulum"

Imagine a playground swing that has been replaced by a clever mechanism: instead of a single rigid rod, it uses **two ropes tied end-to-end** — a thick upper rope and a thin lower rope. The pendulum bob sits at the junction. The combined system swings at a natural rhythm determined by the **sum of both rope lengths** (analogous to $L_1 + L_2$) and the **single equivalent mass** (the capacitor $C$ storing the "elastic energy" of the swing).

Now, the **trick** is this: every time the swing crosses the middle, a tiny push (the **transistor amplifier**) is applied at the **junction of the two ropes** to keep the swing going forever. The push is timed to the rhythm of the swing — and that rhythm is set by the **total length** of the rope and the mass of the bob. Change the total length, and the rhythm changes. This is precisely how the Hartley oscillator works — the amplifier replenishes the energy lost in the tank circuit, and the **frequency** is set by the **LC product**.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Engineering Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> - **Inductor range used in practice:** $L_1, L_2 \approx 1\ \text{mH to 100 mH}$ (audio-to-RF range).
> - **Tank capacitor range:** $C \approx 1\ \text{nF to 1000 pF}$ (HF range).
> - **Typical oscillation frequencies generated:** **20 kHz to 30 MHz** (audio to RF band).
> - **Standard coupling assumption in KTU problems:** If not stated, assume **negligible mutual inductance** ($M = 0$), and use $L_{eq} = L_1 + L_2$.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Why Does the Hartley Oscillator Oscillate?

The Hartley oscillator is a **positive feedback amplifier** that converts DC power from the supply into sustained AC sinusoidal output. The mechanism works as follows:

- **Step 1 — Initial Excitation:**
  The moment the DC supply $V_{CC}$ is switched ON, transient currents flow in the tank circuit ($L_1$–$L_2$–$C$). These transients contain a **wide spectrum of frequencies**, but only the **resonant frequency** of the tank is reinforced.

- **Step 2 — Tank Circuit Selection:**
  The LC tank acts as a **highly selective band-pass filter**. At the **resonant frequency**, the inductive and capacitive reactances cancel, leaving a **pure resistive load**. All other frequencies are heavily attenuated.

- **Step 3 — Amplification & Phase Inversion:**
  The transistor (configured in **Common Emitter** mode in KTU syllabus) provides a **voltage gain $A$** and introduces a **180° phase shift** between input and output.

- **Step 4 — Feedback Network & Phase Compensation:**
  The inductive voltage divider ($L_1$, $L_2$) introduces a **further 180° phase shift** (it is a reactive network at resonance, but the voltage across the feedback coil is inverted with respect to the output coil). Total loop phase = $180° + 180° = 360°$ (= $0°$), satisfying the **Barkhausen phase condition**.

- **Step 5 — Amplitude Build-Up & Steady State:**
  Initially, $|A\beta| > 1$, so the amplitude grows. As the signal level rises, the transistor's non-linearity reduces the effective gain until **steady-state oscillation** is reached with $|A\beta| = 1$.

## 2.2 Barkhausen Criterion (The Two Fundamental Conditions)

For **sustained sinusoidal oscillations** to exist, the Barkhausen criterion must be satisfied:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> 1. **Loop Gain Condition:** The magnitude of the loop gain must equal **unity**.
> $$\vert A\beta \vert = 1$$
> 2. **Loop Phase Condition:** The total phase shift around the feedback loop must be an **integer multiple of $360°$** (or $0°$).
> $$\angle A\beta = 2n\pi,\ \ n = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$$

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| Sl. No. | Parameter | Formula (No Mutual Coupling) | Formula (With Mutual Inductance $M$) | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Angular Frequency | $\omega_0 = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{(L_1+L_2)\,C}}$ | $\omega_0 = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{(L_1+L_2+2M)\,C}}$ | $\text{rad/s}$ |
| 2 | Frequency of Oscillation | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{(L_1+L_2)\,C}}$ | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{(L_1+L_2+2M)\,C}}$ | $\text{Hz}$ |
| 3 | Equivalent Inductance | $L_{eq} = L_1 + L_2$ | $L_{eq} = L_1 + L_2 + 2M$ | $\text{Henry (H)}$ |
| 4 | Feedback Fraction (Tap) | $\beta = \dfrac{L_2}{L_1}$ (or $\dfrac{X_{L_2}}{X_{L_1}}$) | $\beta = \dfrac{j\omega L_2}{j\omega L_1} = \dfrac{L_2}{L_1}$ | Dimensionless |
| 5 | Minimum Amplifier Gain | $A_{min} = \dfrac{1}{\beta} = \dfrac{L_1}{L_2}$ | $A_{min} = \dfrac{L_1}{L_2}$ | Dimensionless |
| 6 | Quality Factor (Tank) | $Q = \dfrac{\omega_0 L_{eq}}{R}$ | $Q = \dfrac{\omega_0 (L_1+L_2+2M)}{R}$ | Dimensionless |
| 7 | Barkhausen Loop Phase | $\angle A\beta = 360°$ (or $0°$) | $\angle A\beta = 360°$ (or $0°$) | Degrees |
| 8 | Barkhausen Loop Gain | $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$ | $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$ | — |

> [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner's Tip:** The **feedback ratio $\beta$** is purely **real and positive** in the Hartley oscillator because both $L_1$ and $L_2$ are pure inductors and their reactances cancel in magnitude. This is one of the simplest feedback networks to analyse — a frequent short-answer topic.

## 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **RF Signal Generators:** Hartley oscillators are widely used in laboratory signal generators covering the **HF band (3–30 MHz)** for radio receiver alignment.
- **Local Oscillators in Superheterodyne Receivers:** Older AM radio receivers historically used Hartley designs to generate the intermediate frequency (IF) of **455 kHz**.
- **Electronic Test Equipment:** Function generators and RF probes use modified Hartley topologies.
- **Transmitter Carrier Generation:** Low-power CW transmitters in amateur radio and educational kits.
- **Industrial Oscillators:** Used where a **wide frequency range** is needed — frequency can be tuned by varying a **single capacitor** across a wide range, since $f \propto 1/\sqrt{C}$.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Frequency of Oscillation of the Hartley Oscillator

### Setup

Consider the tank circuit of the Hartley oscillator. The two inductors $L_1$ and $L_2$ are in series, and this series combination is in parallel with the capacitor $C$. The mutual inductance between the coils is taken as $M$ (which can be neglected if the coils are physically separated or "pinned" out — the KTU default assumption).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Write the impedance of each branch of the tank circuit:**

The series combination of $L_1$ and $L_2$ has impedance:

$$Z_{series} = j\omega(L_1 + L_2)$$

The capacitor branch has impedance:

$$Z_C = \frac{1}{j\omega C} = \frac{-j}{\omega C}$$

**Step 2 — Write the total parallel impedance of the tank:**

$$Z_{tank} = \frac{Z_{series} \cdot Z_C}{Z_{series} + Z_C}$$

Substituting the values:

$$Z_{tank} = \frac{j\omega(L_1+L_2) \cdot \left(\frac{1}{j\omega C}\right)}{j\omega(L_1+L_2) + \frac{1}{j\omega C}}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify the numerator:**

The numerator simplifies neatly because $j\omega \cdot \frac{1}{j\omega} = 1$:

$$\text{Numerator} = \frac{L_1 + L_2}{C}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the denominator by multiplying numerator and denominator by $j\omega C$:**

$$Z_{tank} = \frac{\frac{L_1+L_2}{C}}{\frac{1 - \omega^2 (L_1+L_2)C}{j\omega C}}$$

**Step 5 — Final form of tank impedance:**

$$Z_{tank} = \frac{j\omega(L_1+L_2)}{1 - \omega^2 (L_1+L_2)C}$$

**Step 6 — Apply the resonance condition:**

For sustained oscillation, the tank circuit must present a **purely resistive impedance**, i.e., the **imaginary part of $Z_{tank}$ must be infinite or the denominator must vanish**. Setting the real denominator to zero:

$$1 - \omega_0^2 (L_1+L_2)C = 0$$

Solving for $\omega_0$:

$$\omega_0^2 = \frac{1}{(L_1+L_2)C}$$

$$\omega_0 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(L_1+L_2)C}}$$

**Step 7 — Convert to frequency in Hz:**

Using the identity $f_0 = \dfrac{\omega_0}{2\pi}$:

$$\boxed{f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{(L_1+L_2)C}}}$$

> [!NOTE] > **When Mutual Inductance $M$ Exists:**
> If the two inductors share a common core, the total inductance becomes $L_{eq} = L_1 + L_2 + 2M$ (for aiding fluxes). The frequency formula generalises to:
> $$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{(L_1+L_2+2M)C}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Feedback Fraction and Minimum Gain

**Step 1 — Identify the feedback node:**

The output of the amplifier appears across $L_1$ (between collector and tap). The feedback voltage $V_f$ is the voltage developed across $L_2$ (between tap and base). Both inductors carry essentially the same AC current $I$ at resonance (they are in series).

**Step 2 — Express the voltages in terms of reactance:**

$$V_{out} = I \cdot j\omega L_1$$

$$V_f = I \cdot j\omega L_2$$

**Step 3 — Compute the feedback fraction $\beta$:**

$$\beta = \frac{V_f}{V_{out}} = \frac{j\omega L_2}{j\omega L_1} = \frac{L_2}{L_1}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Barkhausen magnitude condition:**

$$\vert A\beta \vert = 1 \implies A = \frac{1}{\beta}$$

$$\boxed{A_{min} = \frac{L_1}{L_2}}$$

This tells the designer that if the **load inductor is 10 times larger than the feedback inductor**, the amplifier only needs a minimum voltage gain of **10** to sustain oscillations.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Typical 7-Mark Pattern)

**Problem:**
A Hartley oscillator is designed with $L_1 = 2\ \text{mH}$, $L_2 = 20\ \mu\text{H}$, and $C = 1000\ \text{pF}$. Assume no mutual coupling. Calculate:
(a) the frequency of oscillation,
(b) the feedback fraction,
(c) the minimum amplifier gain required.

**Solution:**

**(a) Frequency of oscillation:**

$$L_{eq} = L_1 + L_2 = 2 \times 10^{-3} + 20 \times 10^{-6} = 2.02 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{H}$$

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L_{eq} \cdot C}} = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{2.02 \times 10^{-3} \times 1000 \times 10^{-12}}}$$

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{2.02 \times 10^{-15}}}$$

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 4.494 \times 10^{-8}} = \frac{1}{2.824 \times 10^{-7}}$$

$$\boxed{f_0 \approx 3.54\ \text{MHz}}$$

**(b) Feedback fraction:**

$$\beta = \frac{L_2}{L_1} = \frac{20 \times 10^{-6}}{2 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.01$$

**(c) Minimum amplifier gain:**

$$A_{min} = \frac{1}{\beta} = \frac{L_1}{L_2} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-3}}{20 \times 10^{-6}} = 100$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid — Transistorized Hartley Oscillator Circuit (Functional Flow)

```mermaid
graph TB
    supplyNode["VCC DC Supply"] --> ampNode[Transistor CE Amplifier Q1]
    biasNode["Bias Resistors R1 R2"] --> ampNode
    ampNode --> outNode[Collector Output Node]
    outNode --> coil1Node[L1 Inductor]
    coil1Node --> tapNode[Junction Tap Point M]
    tapNode --> coil2Node[L2 Inductor]
    coil2Node --> gnd1[Ground]
    tapNode --> fbNode[Feedback to Base of Q1]
    fbNode --> ampNode
    outNode --> capNode[C Tank Capacitor]
    capNode --> gnd1
    ampNode --> emitterNode[Emitter]
    emitterNode --> gnd1
    outNode --> loadNode[AC Sinusoidal Output Vo]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid — Block-Level Feedback Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    ampBlock["Amplifier Block Gain A 180 deg Phase Shift"] --> tankBlock["LC Tank Circuit Frequency Selective Network 180 deg Phase Shift at Resonance"]
    tankBlock --> outBlock["Sustained Sinusoidal Output vo t"]
    tankBlock --> fbBlock["Feedback Network beta L2 by L1"]
    fbBlock --> ampBlock
    biasBlock["DC Bias VCC"] --> ampBlock
```

## 4.3 Mermaid — Frequency Tuning Mechanism

```mermaid
graph TB
    capVar[Variable Capacitor C] --> lseries[L1 plus L2 Series Inductors]
    lseries --> freqEqn[Frequency f0 equals 1 over 2 pi sqrt L eq C]
    freqEqn --> tuner[Tunable Output RF Signal]
    capVar -.Adjust.-> freqEqn
    lseries -.Adjust.-> freqEqn
```

> [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Diagram Note:**
> In the KTU answer sheet, you are expected to draw the **circuit schematic** clearly with the following mandatory labels:
> - DC supply $V_{CC}$, bias resistors $R_1$, $R_2$, emitter resistor $R_E$, and coupling capacitor $C_C$.
> - The two coils $L_1$ and $L_2$ drawn with a **curly/loop** style (not straight lines).
> - The **arrow direction** of feedback clearly traced from the **junction of $L_1$ and $L_2$** back to the **base** of the transistor.
> - The polarity markings ( '+' and '–' ) on the inductor voltages to show the 180° phase inversion.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model Paper]
> **Define a Hartley oscillator. Write the expression for its frequency of oscillation.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The Hartley oscillator is an LC feedback oscillator that uses a tank circuit comprising **two inductors $L_1$ and $L_2$** in series (with a single capacitor $C$ in parallel) to generate sustained sinusoidal oscillations. The frequency of oscillation is given by:
$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{(L_1+L_2)C}}$$
(Assuming no mutual coupling between $L_1$ and $L_2$.)

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for circuit identification, 1 mark for the formula.)*

---

> **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Supplementary]
> **List any three advantages of the Hartley oscillator.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. **Frequency can be varied easily** over a wide range by tuning a **single capacitor** $C$ — the formula $f \propto 1/\sqrt{C}$ allows smooth tuning.
2. **Simple design** with only two inductors and one capacitor, and the output is **free from harmonics** (high purity sine wave).
3. The **feedback ratio $\beta = L_2/L_1$** can be easily set to any desired value by adjusting the tap position on the inductor.

*(1 mark per advantage — 3 advantages × 1 mark.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Regular]**
> **(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the construction and working of a transistorised Hartley oscillator. **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** Derive the expression for the frequency of oscillation and the condition for sustained oscillation. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Construction and Working (7 Marks):**

The Hartley oscillator uses a **Common Emitter (CE) configured BJT** with an LC tank in the collector circuit.

- **Components:** Transistor Q1, tank circuit ($L_1$, $L_2$, $C$), bias resistors $R_1$, $R_2$, emitter resistor $R_E$, coupling capacitor $C_C$, and DC supply $V_{CC}$.
- **Working:** When $V_{CC}$ is switched on, a small change in collector current flows through $L_1$, inducing an EMF in $L_2$ by mutual induction (or via the tap). This voltage is fed back to the base in **correct phase** (180° from the CE amplifier + 180° from the inductive divider = 360°).
- **Sustained Oscillation:** The feedback replenishes the losses in the tank circuit, and the oscillations build up at the resonant frequency $f_0$. Amplitude stabilises when $|A\beta| = 1$.

*(2 marks for circuit diagram, 3 marks for working, 2 marks for sustained oscillation explanation.)*

**(b) Derivation (7 Marks):** *(Refer to Section 3 of these notes for the full derivation.)*

Key steps to be reproduced in the answer sheet:

1. Write $Z_{tank} = \dfrac{j\omega(L_1+L_2)}{1 - \omega^2(L_1+L_2)C}$
2. Apply the resonance condition: $1 - \omega_0^2(L_1+L_2)C = 0$
3. Solve: $\omega_0 = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{(L_1+L_2)C}}$
4. Convert: $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{(L_1+L_2)C}}$
5. Barkhausen condition: $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$ and $\angle A\beta = 0°$
6. Feedback fraction: $\beta = L_2/L_1$, hence $A_{min} = L_1/L_2$.

*(2 marks for impedance equation, 2 marks for the resonance condition, 2 marks for the final frequency formula, 1 mark for the gain condition.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Supplementary]**
> **(a)** State and explain the **Barkhausen criterion** for sustained sinusoidal oscillations. **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** A Hartley oscillator uses $L_1 = 1.5\ \text{mH}$, $L_2 = 1.5\ \text{mH}$, and a variable capacitor. If the frequency is to be varied from **950 kHz to 2050 kHz**, find the range of the capacitor $C$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Barkhausen Criterion (7 Marks):**

For sustained sinusoidal oscillations, an oscillator circuit must satisfy two simultaneous conditions — together known as the **Barkhausen Criterion**:

1. **Magnitude Condition:** The magnitude of the loop gain $|A\beta|$ must equal **unity** (1). This ensures that the signal fed back has exactly the same amplitude as the original input.
2. **Phase Condition:** The total phase shift around the closed loop must be **$0°$ or an integer multiple of $360°$**. This ensures positive feedback — the feedback signal reinforces the input rather than cancelling it.

Mathematically:
$$\vert A\beta \vert = 1 \quad \text{and} \quad \angle A\beta = 2n\pi$$

If $|A\beta| < 1$, the oscillations die out; if $|A\beta| > 1$, the amplitude grows until non-linearity reduces the effective gain to unity.

*(3 marks for stating the conditions, 2 marks for the equations, 2 marks for the physical interpretation.)*

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 Marks):**

Given: $L_1 = L_2 = 1.5\ \text{mH}$, so $L_{eq} = 3.0\ \text{mH}$.

Using the formula:
$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L_{eq} \cdot C}} \implies C = \frac{1}{4\pi^2 f_0^2 L_{eq}}$$

**For $f_{min} = 950\ \text{kHz}$ (corresponds to $C_{max}$):**

$$C_{max} = \frac{1}{4\pi^2 \times (950 \times 10^3)^2 \times 3.0 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$C_{max} = \frac{1}{4 \times 9.87 \times 9.025 \times 10^{11} \times 3.0 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$C_{max} = \frac{1}{1.069 \times 10^{10}} \approx 9.35 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{F}$$

$$\boxed{C_{max} \approx 93.5\ \text{pF}}$$

**For $f_{max} = 2050\ \text{kHz}$ (corresponds to $C_{min}$):**

$$C_{min} = \frac{1}{4\pi^2 \times (2050 \times 10^3)^2 \times 3.0 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$C_{min} = \frac{1}{4 \times 9.87 \times 4.2025 \times 10^{12} \times 3.0 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$C_{min} = \frac{1}{4.976 \times 10^{11}} \approx 2.01 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{F}$$

$$\boxed{C_{min} \approx 2.01\ \text{pF}}$$

**Result:** The variable capacitor must be tunable over the range $\approx \mathbf{2.01\ \text{pF to 93.5\ \text{pF}}$.

*(2 marks for substituting, 2 marks for calculation of $C_{max}$, 2 marks for $C_{min}$, 1 mark for the final range.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2M:** When mutual inductance is mentioned in the problem, students often write $L_{eq} = L_1 + L_2$ and lose 1–2 marks. Always read the problem carefully and check whether the coils share a common core (mutual coupling present) or are physically separated (no coupling).
> 2. **Missing units in numerical answers:** A numerical answer without units (Hz, F, H) is treated as incomplete. Always write **kHz, MHz, pF, mH, μH** explicitly.
> 3. **Phase condition misuse:** Many students write "phase shift = 180°" and stop there. The complete answer requires the **total loop phase = 360°** (i.e., $0°$).
> 4. **Circuit diagram omissions:** A circle around the transistor symbol is **not** a substitute for proper CE configuration markings. Always show $V_{CC}$ at the top, emitter arrow direction, and the polarities of $L_1$ and $L_2$ voltages.
> 5. **Barkhausen without explanation:** Just writing the two equations gets partial credit. The **physical meaning** (what happens when $|A\beta| < 1$ or $> 1$) carries an additional 1–2 marks in KTU valuation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> ### 🔑 High-Yield Quick-Reference Points for the Hartley Oscillator
>
> 1. **Inventor:** Ralph V. L. Hartley, **1915**.
> 2. **Tank Circuit Composition:** **2 Inductors ($L_1$, $L_2$) + 1 Capacitor ($C$)** — this is the **distinguishing feature** vs. Colpitts (2 caps + 1 inductor).
> 3. **Frequency Formula (No $M$):** $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{(L_1+L_2)C}}$
> 4. **Frequency Formula (With $M$):** $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{(L_1+L_2+2M)C}}$
> 5. **Feedback Fraction:** $\beta = \dfrac{L_2}{L_1}$ (purely real, no phase shift introduced by the feedback network).
> 6. **Minimum Gain:** $A_{min} = \dfrac{L_1}{L_2}$ (always a positive real number).
> 7. **Amplifier Configuration:** Common Emitter (CE) is the standard KTU default.
> 8. **Total Phase Shift:** CE amplifier (180°) + Tank feedback (180°) = **360°** (Barkhausen satisfied).
> 9. **Typical Frequency Range:** **20 kHz to 30 MHz** (audio to RF band).
> 10. **Advantages:** Easy frequency tuning, simple construction, good sinusoidal purity.
> 11. **Disadvantages:** Presence of harmonics in the output; less stable than crystal oscillators.
> 12. **Applications:** RF signal generators, local oscillators in radio receivers, laboratory oscillators.
> 13. **Remember for KTU:** In the absence of a stated core coupling, **default to $M = 0$** for the standard formula.
> 14. **Tuning Trick:** To **increase frequency**, **decrease $C$** (or decrease $L_{eq}$). To **decrease frequency**, **increase $C$**.
> 15. **Quality Factor:** A high-$Q$ tank produces a cleaner sine wave — a high-$Q$ is achieved by **minimising the series resistance** in the tank circuit.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Colpitts and Crystal Oscillators (Working Principle and Design Equations of the Circuits Only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET302 - ELECTRONIC DEVICES AND CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Colpitts and Crystal Oscillators (Working Principle and Design Equations of the Circuits Only)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Colpitts Oscillator — Formal Definition

The **Colpitts Oscillator** is a sinusoidal LC feedback oscillator that employs a **capacitive voltage divider** (formed by two capacitors $C_1$ and $C_2$ in series) and a single inductive element $L$ to form its frequency-determining tank circuit. It was invented by **Edwin H. Colpitts in 1918**. The feedback signal is derived from the junction of the two capacitors, and the configuration satisfies the **Barkhausen Criterion** (loop gain $\vert A \beta \vert \geq 1$ and total phase shift around the loop = $0^\circ$ or $360^\circ$).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**
> The Colpitts oscillator is grouped under **"High-frequency oscillators"** in Module 4. Students are expected to derive the **frequency of oscillation** and the **condition for sustained oscillations** (Barkhausen criterion) for both the **Common-Emitter (CE)** and **Common-Base (CB)** configurations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Two-Pendulum Swing"

Imagine a playground swing where instead of one pendulum, two pendulums of **different effective lengths** are rigidly coupled at the same pivot point. The *shorter* pendulum wants to swing faster, while the *longer* one wants to swing slower. The compromise (resonant) frequency of the combined system depends on the **ratios** of their lengths.

In a Colpitts oscillator, the two capacitors $C_1$ and $C_2$ act exactly like these two pendulums — the inductor $L$ provides the "springing" energy. The frequency of the resulting oscillation depends on the *equivalent capacitance* which is a *series combination* of $C_1$ and $C_2$.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Recall Trick**
> - **Hartley** = **H**as **L**arge **I**nductors (inductive divider)
> - **Colpitts** = **C**apacitive **C**ouple (capacitive divider)
> - The tank frequency is $f = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L_{eq}\,C_{eq}}}$ in both cases.

## 1.3 Crystal Oscillator — Formal Definition

A **Crystal Oscillator** is an electronic oscillator circuit that uses the mechanical resonance of a vibrating **piezoelectric crystal** (typically **quartz — $\text{SiO}_2$**) as the frequency-selective element. When an alternating voltage is applied across suitably cut quartz plates, the crystal vibrates at a precisely defined natural frequency. This **piezoelectric effect** (discovered by the Curie brothers in 1880) provides a quality factor (**$Q$**) ranging from **$10{,}000$** to over **$1{,}000{,}000$** — orders of magnitude higher than any LC tank.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Physical Constants Used in Crystal Oscillator Analysis**
> - Quartz density: $\rho \approx 2.65 \text{ g/cm}^3$
> - Young's modulus (AT-cut): $Y \approx 7.87 \times 10^{10} \text{ N/m}^2$
> - Relative permittivity: $\varepsilon_r \approx 4.6$
> - Series $Q$ of commercial crystals: $\mathbf{10^4 \leq Q \leq 10^6}$

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The "Tuning Fork"

A quartz crystal behaves exactly like a **high-precision tuning fork**. If you strike a tuning fork, it vibrates at *one* well-defined pitch. No matter how the surrounding air pressure or temperature varies slightly, the pitch remains remarkably steady. A crystal oscillator works in the *electrical* domain the same way — quartz's mechanical resonance is converted into an electrical sinusoidal waveform whose frequency is locked to the crystal's natural mode of vibration.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Misconception**
> Students often think a crystal "generates" oscillations on its own. It does **not**. The crystal only acts as an extremely narrow-bandpass **filter** at its resonant frequency. The *active device* (transistor/Op-amp) supplies the energy, while the crystal provides the precise **frequency selectivity** — this is why it is called a *crystal-controlled* oscillator, not a *crystal-generated* oscillator.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Colpitts Oscillator — Working Principle (Step-by-Step)

The Colpitts oscillator uses a **CE or CB amplifier** in conjunction with an **LC tank circuit** in which the **capacitive divider** $\dfrac{C_1}{C_2}$ supplies the **regenerative feedback** to the input.

**Step 1 — Tank Circuit Construction**
- Two capacitors $C_1$ and $C_2$ are connected in **series** across an inductor $L$.
- The junction between $C_1$ and $C_2$ is **grounded** for AC (this is the feedback node).
- One end of the tank is connected to the **output** of the amplifier (collector/emitter of BJT).

**Step 2 — Phase-Shift Mechanism**
- The CE amplifier introduces a **$180^\circ$ phase inversion** between input and output.
- The tank circuit (acting as the feedback network) provides the remaining **$180^\circ$** of phase shift when operated at its resonant frequency.
- Total loop phase = $180^\circ + 180^\circ = 360^\circ \equiv 0^\circ$ → **Barkhausen phase condition satisfied**.

**Step 3 — Frequency of Oscillation (Design Equation)**
- At resonance, the inductive reactance of $L$ equals the capacitive reactance of the **series combination** of $C_1$ and $C_2$:

$$X_L = X_{C_{eq}} \quad \text{where} \quad C_{eq} = \frac{C_1 \, C_2}{C_1 + C_2}$$

- Solving $2\pi f L = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f \, C_{eq}}$ for $f$ gives the **frequency of oscillation**:

$$f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{L \, C_{eq}}} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{C_1 + C_2}{L\, C_1\, C_2}}$$

**Step 4 — Feedback Fraction**
- The feedback ratio (the fraction of output voltage fed back to the input) is:

$$\beta = \frac{C_1}{C_2} \quad (\text{for CE configuration})$$

**Step 5 — Condition for Sustained Oscillation (Barkhausen Magnitude)**
- The amplifier gain $A$ must satisfy:

$$\vert A \beta \vert = 1 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad A \geq \frac{1}{\beta} = \frac{C_2}{C_1}$$

## 2.2 Why Capacitive Divider? — Engineering Rationale

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility of the Colpitts Configuration**
> 1. **Single Inductor Design** — Unlike the Hartley oscillator which requires a *tapped* or *auto-transformer* inductor (hard to fabricate at RF/microwave frequencies), the Colpitts uses a *plain single inductor*. This makes it ideal for **MMIC and VLSI integration**.
> 2. **Wide Frequency Range** — Colpitts oscillators can operate from **$1 \text{ kHz}$ to several $\text{GHz}$** by simply swapping $C_1$/$C_2$.
> 3. **Better Frequency Stability than Hartley** — Because stray and junction capacitances are absorbed *within* $C_1$ and $C_2$, the effective $Q$ of the tank is preserved.

## 2.3 Crystal Oscillator — Working Principle

**Step 1 — The Piezoelectric Effect**
- When a mechanical stress is applied across certain crystal axes of quartz, a proportional **electric potential** develops across opposite faces — this is the **direct piezoelectric effect**.
- Conversely, an applied alternating voltage causes the crystal to **mechanically deform** at the same frequency — **converse piezoelectric effect**.

**Step 2 — Equivalent Electrical Circuit of the Crystal**
- The crystal is modelled as a series RLC branch (the *motional* arm) in parallel with a static capacitance $C_0$ (the *holder/electrode* capacitance).
- **Motional inductance** $L$ (large — represents mass of vibration): typically $0.001$ to $1 \text{ H}$.
- **Motional capacitance** $C$ (very small — represents mechanical elasticity): typically $0.01$ to $10 \text{ pF}$.
- **Motional resistance** $R$ (very small — represents mechanical losses): typically $10$ to $100 \, \Omega$.
- **Static capacitance** $C_0$ (shunt): typically $5$ to $20 \text{ pF}$.

**Step 3 — Two Resonant Frequencies**
- The series RLC branch resonates at $f_s$ (where inductive and capacitive reactances cancel internally).
- When combined with the parallel $C_0$, the network produces a *second* resonance $f_p$ slightly above $f_s$.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorize the formulas in this table — they appear in nearly every KTU board exam problem on this module.**

| # | Concept | Design Equation | Typical Units / Notes |
|---|---------|-----------------|------------------------|
| 1 | Equivalent capacitance of Colpitts tank | $C_{eq} = \dfrac{C_1 C_2}{C_1 + C_2}$ | Farads (F) |
| 2 | Frequency of Colpitts oscillation | $f_o = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{C_1 + C_2}{L\, C_1 C_2}}$ | Hertz (Hz) |
| 3 | Feedback ratio (Colpitts, CE) | $\beta = \dfrac{C_1}{C_2}$ | Dimensionless |
| 4 | Minimum gain for sustained oscillation | $A_{min} = \dfrac{C_2}{C_1}$ | Dimensionless |
| 5 | Series resonant frequency (Crystal) | $f_s = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$ | Hz |
| 6 | Parallel resonant frequency (Crystal) | $f_p = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L \cdot \dfrac{C C_0}{C + C_0}}}$ | Hz |
| 7 | Separation between $f_p$ and $f_s$ | $\Delta f = f_p - f_s \approx \dfrac{f_s}{2}\cdot\dfrac{C}{C_0}$ | Hz, $\Delta f \ll f_s$ |
| 8 | Quality factor of crystal | $Q = \dfrac{1}{R}\sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C}}$ | $Q \sim 10^4$ to $10^6$ |
| 9 | Reactance of crystal (ideal) | $X = \dfrac{1}{\omega C_0}\cdot\dfrac{\omega^2 - \omega_s^2}{\omega^2 - \omega_p^2}$ | $\Omega$ |
| 10 | Frequency stability (crystal) | $S = \dfrac{\Delta f}{f_o}$ | $S \sim 10^{-6}$ to $10^{-11}$ per day |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Boundary Condition for Crystal Operation**
> Since $C_0 \gg C$ in real quartz crystals, the fractional separation is approximately:
> $$\dfrac{f_p - f_s}{f_s} \approx \dfrac{C}{2 C_0} \approx 0.001 \text{ to } 0.004 \;\; (0.1\% \text{ to } 0.4\%)$$
> This is the *useful bandwidth* of the crystal — the frequency window in which the crystal presents an **inductive reactance** (necessary for oscillators like the Pierce and Miller circuits).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Frequency of Oscillation of a Colpitts Oscillator

### Setup
The tank circuit of the Colpitts oscillator has the inductor $L$ in parallel with the series combination of $C_1$ and $C_2$. Let the total tank impedance be $Z_{tank}$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Combine the series capacitors into an equivalent capacitance**

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{eq} &= \text{Series combination of } C_1 \text{ and } C_2 \\
       &= \frac{C_1 \cdot C_2}{C_1 + C_2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Reason:** Two capacitors in series have a combined capacitance given by the *product over sum* formula, identical to resistors in parallel.

**Step 2 — Write the impedance of the tank as a parallel combination of $L$ and $C_{eq}$**

$$\begin{aligned}
Z_{tank} &= \frac{j\omega L \cdot \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_{eq}}}{j\omega L + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_{eq}}} \\
        &= \frac{j\omega L}{1 - \omega^2 L C_{eq}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the resonance condition (Barkhausen phase condition)**

At resonance, the imaginary part of the tank impedance must vanish — equivalently, the inductive and capacitive reactances must be equal in magnitude.

$$\begin{aligned}
X_L &= X_{C_{eq}} \\
2\pi f L &= \frac{1}{2\pi f \, C_{eq}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $f_o$ algebraically**

$$\begin{aligned}
(2\pi f_o)^2 \, L \, C_{eq} &= 1 \\
f_o^2 &= \frac{1}{4\pi^2 L \, C_{eq}} \\
f_o &= \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L \, C_{eq}}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $C_{eq}$ back to obtain the design equation in terms of $L, C_1, C_2$**

$$\begin{aligned}
f_o &= \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L \cdot \dfrac{C_1 C_2}{C_1 + C_2}}} \\
f_o &= \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{C_1 + C_2}{L\, C_1 C_2}}
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **final, board-exam-ready design equation** for the Colpitts oscillator.

### Numerical Verification Example

> **Given:** $L = 100 \text{ \mu H}$, $C_1 = 1 \text{ nF}$, $C_2 = 4 \text{ nF}$.
> **Find:** $f_o$ and the minimum amplifier gain.

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{eq} &= \frac{(1 \times 10^{-9})(4 \times 10^{-9})}{(1+4)\times 10^{-9}} = \frac{4 \times 10^{-18}}{5 \times 10^{-9}} = 0.8 \text{ nF} \\
f_o &= \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{(100 \times 10^{-6})(0.8 \times 10^{-9})}} \\
    &= \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{8 \times 10^{-14}}} \\
    &= \frac{1}{2\pi \times 2.828 \times 10^{-7}} \\
    &= 5.63 \times 10^{5} \text{ Hz} = 563 \text{ kHz}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
A_{min} &= \frac{C_2}{C_1} = \frac{4}{1} = 4
\end{aligned}$$

The amplifier must provide a voltage gain of at least **4** for sustained oscillations.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Series Resonant Frequency of a Quartz Crystal

### Setup
The motional arm consists of $R$, $L$, $C$ in **series**. At series resonance, the inductive reactance of $L$ cancels the capacitive reactance of $C$ within the motional arm.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Impedance of the series RLC motional arm**

$$\begin{aligned}
Z_{motional} &= R + j\omega L + \frac{1}{j\omega C} \\
             &= R + j\left(\omega L - \frac{1}{\omega C}\right)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Set the imaginary part to zero (series resonance condition)**

$$\begin{aligned}
\omega_s L - \frac{1}{\omega_s C} &= 0 \\
\omega_s^2 L C &= 1 \\
\omega_s &= \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to frequency in Hz**

$$f_s = \frac{\omega_s}{2\pi} = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$$

**Result:** The series resonance depends *only* on the motional arm parameters $L$ and $C$, *not* on $C_0$. At $f_s$, the motional branch impedance is purely resistive and equal to $R$ (very small), so the crystal looks like a **short circuit** at $f_s$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Parallel Resonant Frequency of a Quartz Crystal

### Setup
At the *parallel* resonance, the **overall impedance** of the crystal (motional arm **in parallel** with $C_0$) becomes infinite — the crystal behaves as an **open circuit**.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Admittance of the motional arm**

$$\begin{aligned}
Y_{motional} &= \frac{1}{R + j\left(\omega L - \dfrac{1}{\omega C}\right)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Admittance of the static capacitance $C_0$**

$$Y_{C_0} = j\omega C_0$$

**Step 3 — Total admittance of the crystal**

$$Y_{total} = Y_{motional} + Y_{C_0}$$

**Step 4 — Assume $R$ is negligibly small (true for real crystals) and write the susceptance**

For an ideal crystal with $R \to 0$:

$$\begin{aligned}
B_{total} &= \omega C_0 - \frac{1}{\omega L - \dfrac{1}{\omega C}} \\
         &= \omega C_0 - \frac{\omega C}{\omega^2 L C - 1}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — At parallel resonance, set the total susceptance to zero**

$$\begin{aligned}
\omega_p C_0 &= \frac{\omega_p C}{\omega_p^2 L C - 1} \\
\omega_p^2 L C_0 C - C_0 &= C \\
\omega_p^2 L C C_0 &= C + C_0 \\
\omega_p^2 &= \frac{1}{L \cdot \dfrac{C C_0}{C + C_0}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $f_p$ in Hz**

$$f_p = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L \cdot \dfrac{C C_0}{C + C_0}}}$$

**Step 7 — Express the difference $\Delta f = f_p - f_s$ (optional but frequently asked in KTU)**

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{f_p^2} - \frac{1}{f_s^2} &= 4\pi^2 L \left(\frac{C C_0}{C + C_0}\right) - 4\pi^2 L C \\
                                   &= 4\pi^2 L \left(\frac{C C_0 - C(C+C_0)}{C+C_0}\right) \\
                                   &= 4\pi^2 L \left(\frac{-C^2}{C + C_0}\right)
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{f_s^2 - f_p^2}{f_s^2 f_p^2} &= -\frac{4\pi^2 L C^2}{C + C_0} \\
\Delta f = f_p - f_s &\approx \frac{f_s}{2}\cdot \frac{C}{C_0} \quad (\text{since } C_0 \gg C)
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Insight**
> Because $C_0$ is **5 to 50 times larger** than $C$ in real quartz, the *useful inductive region* of the crystal (the band between $f_s$ and $f_p$) is **very narrow** — typically **$0.1\%$ to $0.4\%$ of $f_s$**. This is precisely why the crystal provides such extraordinary **frequency selectivity** and stability.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Colpitts LC Oscillator | Crystal Oscillator |
|-----------|------------------------|---------------------|
| Frequency-determining element | $L, C_1, C_2$ | Quartz crystal (piezoelectric) |
| Typical $Q$ factor | $50$ to $500$ | $10{,}000$ to $1{,}000{,}000$ |
| Frequency stability $\dfrac{\Delta f}{f_o}$ | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-3}$ | $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-11}$ per day |
| Frequency range | $1 \text{ kHz}$ – $10 \text{ GHz}$ | $1 \text{ kHz}$ – $300 \text{ MHz}$ (practical) |
| Temperature sensitivity | Moderate to high | Very low (with AT-cut compensation) |
| Tuning capability | Easy (variable $C_1, C_2$ or $L$) | Difficult (mechanical trimming only) |
| Cost | Low | Moderate to high |
| Application | RF oscillators, local oscillators | Watches, microcontrollers, communication transmitters |
| Aging | Moderate | Extremely low (parts per billion per year) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Colpitts Oscillator (CE Configuration)

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **signal flow and feedback topology** of a Common-Emitter Colpitts oscillator. The tank is split into a single inductor $L$ and a capacitive divider $(C_1, C_2)$.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FEEDBACK_NET["FEEDBACK NETWORK CAPACITIVE DIVIDER"]
        fbA["CAPACITOR C1 TOP NODE"]
        fbB["CAPACITOR C2 BOTTOM NODE"]
    end

    VCC["DC SUPPLY VCC"] --- biasR1["BIAS RESISTOR R1"]
    biasR1 --- biasNode["BIAS NODE VB"]
    biasNode --- biasR2["BIAS RESISTOR R2"]
    biasR2 --- GND1["GROUND"]

    biasNode --- baseIn["TRANSISTOR BASE B"]
    baseIn --- trans["BJT AMPLIFIER STAGE CE INVERTER GAIN A 180 DEG PHASE SHIFT"]

    collectorOut["TRANSISTOR COLLECTOR C OUTPUT"] --- tankTop["TANK CIRCUIT NODE"]
    trans --- collectorOut
    trans --- emOut["EMITTER E TO GROUND VIA RE"]

    L["INDUCTOR L FREQUENCY ELEMENT"] --- tankTop
    tankTop --- fbA
    fbA --- fbB
    fbB --- tankGND["TANK GROUND NODE"]
    emOut --- tankGND
    tankGND --- GND2["CHASSIS GROUND"]

    fbB --- feedbackLine["FEEDBACK VOLTAGE VFB EQUAL TO C1 OVER C2 TIMES VOUT"]
    feedbackLine --- baseIn

    classDef active fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000;
    classDef passive fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,color:#000;
    classDef ground fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000;

    class trans active;
    class L,tankTop,fbA,fbB active;
    class biasR1,biasR2,emOut passive;
    class GND1,GND2,tankGND ground;
```

### Interpretation of the Block Diagram
- The **BJT** provides the active gain $A$ with a **$180^\circ$** phase inversion (CE configuration).
- The **tank circuit** ($L$ parallel with the series combination of $C_1$ and $C_2$) provides an additional **$180^\circ$** of phase shift only at the resonant frequency $f_o$.
- The **junction between $C_1$ and $C_2$** is grounded for AC, and the feedback voltage $V_{fb}$ is developed across $C_2$ (or $C_1$, depending on tapping).
- Total loop phase = $360^\circ \equiv 0^\circ$ → **sustained oscillations**.

---

## 4.2 Crystal Equivalent Circuit and Reactance vs. Frequency Curve

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph EQUIV_CKT["ELECTRICAL EQUIVALENT OF QUARTZ CRYSTAL"]
        termA["TERMINAL A"] --- lm["MOTAL INDUCTOR L HENRIES"]
```
