```mermaid
        lm --- rm["MOTAL RESISTOR R OHMS VERY SMALL"]
        rm --- cm["MOTAL CAPACITOR C PICOFARADS"]
        cm --- termB["TERMINAL B"]

        c0link["STATIC SHUNT CAPACITANCE C0"] --- termA
        c0link --- termB
    end

    subgraph RESONANCE_BEHAVIOR["REACTANCE BEHAVIOR VS FREQUENCY"]
        fsLabel["FS SERIES RESONANCE 1 OVER 2 PI ROOT L C SHORT CIRCUIT X EQUALS 0"]
        fpLabel["FP PARALLEL RESONANCE SLIGHTLY ABOVE FS OPEN CIRCUIT X APPROACHES INFINITY"]
        indRegion["INDUCTIVE REGION BETWEEN FS AND FP USEFUL BAND FOR OSCILLATOR DESIGN"]
    end

    termA --- fsLabel
    termB --- fpLabel
    fpLabel --- indRegion

    classDef crystal fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000;
    classDef behaviour fill:#cff4fc,stroke:#0dcaf0,color:#000;

    class lm,rm,cm,c0link crystal;
    class fsLabel,fpLabel,indRegion behaviour;
```

### Reactance vs. Frequency Plot (Textual Representation)

```
        X (Ohms)
          │
       ∞  ┤
          │ ●    ←  Anti-resonance f_p (open circuit)
          │  ●
          │   ●
       +  ┤    ●
          │     ●●●●●●●●  ← Inductive region (f_s < f < f_p)
          │                ●●●●
          │                      ●●●●
       0  ┼────●───────────────────────────●───── f (Hz)
          │  f_s                             
       -  ┤    ●●
          │       ●●●
          │          ●●●●
       -∞ ┤             ●●●●●
                            ●●●
                              (Capacitive below f_s, above f_p)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Design Insight from the Curve**
> - Below $f_s$: the crystal behaves like a **capacitor** (X negative).
> - Between $f_s$ and $f_p$: the crystal behaves like an **inductor** (X positive). **This is the only useful region for oscillator design.**
> - Above $f_p$: the crystal reverts to **capacitive** behaviour.
> - The width of the inductive region is governed by $\dfrac{C}{C_0}$ — typically less than $1\%$ of $f_s$.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define a Colpitts oscillator. Write its frequency of oscillation and state the Barkhausen criterion for sustained oscillations.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Colpitts oscillator is a sinusoidal LC oscillator that uses a **capacitive voltage divider** (two capacitors $C_1$ and $C_2$ in series) and a single inductor $L$ to form its tank circuit.

**Frequency of oscillation:**

$$f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{C_1 + C_2}{L\, C_1 C_2}}$$

**Barkhausen Criterion (two conditions):**
1. The magnitude of the loop gain must be unity or more: $\vert A \beta \vert \geq 1$.
2. The total phase shift around the closed loop must be $0^\circ$ (or an integer multiple of $360^\circ$): $\angle A \beta = 0^\circ \;\; (\text{mod } 360^\circ)$.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**List any three advantages of crystal oscillators over LC oscillators.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Extremely high frequency stability** — fractional drift of $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-11}$ per day, compared to $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-3}$ for LC oscillators.
2. **Very high quality factor** — $Q$ of the order of $10^4$ to $10^6$, vastly superior to the $Q \sim 50$ to $500$ of LC tanks.
3. **Compact, lightweight and mechanically robust** — the crystal is a tiny wafer, and its frequency is unaffected by ordinary vibrations, temperature, or supply voltage variations (especially when AT-cut is used).

*(Optional 4th point: extremely low aging rate of a few parts per billion per year.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a) Derive the expression for the frequency of oscillation of a Colpitts oscillator. Also, state and apply the Barkhausen criterion to find the minimum gain required for sustained oscillations.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the equivalent capacitance of the capacitive divider**

The two capacitors $C_1$ and $C_2$ are in series across the inductor $L$. The equivalent capacitance is:

$$C_{eq} = \frac{C_1 C_2}{C_1 + C_2}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the resonance condition**

At resonance, the inductive reactance equals the capacitive reactance of $C_{eq}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
2\pi f_o L &= \frac{1}{2\pi f_o C_{eq}} \\
f_o^2 &= \frac{1}{4\pi^2 L C_{eq}} \\
f_o &= \frac{1}{2\pi \sqrt{L C_{eq}}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $C_{eq}$**

$$f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{C_1 + C_2}{L\, C_1 C_2}}$$

**Step 4 — Apply Barkhausen magnitude condition**

The feedback fraction of the Colpitts oscillator is:

$$\beta = \frac{C_1}{C_2}$$

(The voltage across $C_2$ is fed back to the input. Since the same charge flows through both, $V_1 : V_2 = C_2 : C_1$, giving $\beta = V_2 / V_1 = C_1 / C_2$.)

For sustained oscillations, $\vert A \beta \vert \geq 1$:

$$A_{min} = \frac{1}{\beta} = \frac{C_2}{C_1}$$

The CE amplifier must provide a voltage gain of at least $C_2/C_1$.

---

**(b) With the help of a circuit diagram, explain the working of a Common-Emitter Colpitts oscillator. Compare the CE configuration with the CB configuration in two points.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Circuit Description (Refer Section 4.1 diagram for the topology):**
- The BJT is biased in the active region using a voltage divider $R_1, R_2$ at the base.
- The emitter is connected to ground through $R_E$ (which may be bypassed by a capacitor for AC).
- The **collector is connected to the junction of $L$ and $C_1$** (the top of the tank circuit).
- The **junction of $C_1$ and $C_2$** is AC-grounded.
- The **bottom of the tank** (i.e., the lower terminal of $C_2$ and the lower terminal of $L$) is connected back to the **base** of the BJT through a coupling capacitor — this is the **feedback path**.

**Working (qualitative):**
- Suppose at some instant a small noise voltage appears at the base.
- The CE amplifier inverts and amplifies it by factor $A = g_m R_C$ (approximately) at the collector.
- This amplified signal drives the tank, which resonates at $f_o$, sustaining large oscillations in $L$, $C_1$, and $C_2$.
- The voltage across $C_2$ (which is $180^\circ$ out of phase with the collector voltage, due to tank inversion at resonance) is fed back to the base.
- The loop phase becomes $180^\circ + 180^\circ = 360^\circ \equiv 0^\circ$, satisfying Barkhausen, and the loop gain is $\geq 1$, so oscillations build up and stabilize at amplitude determined by the transistor's non-linearity (gain compression).

**Comparison of CE vs CB Colpitts (2 points):**

| Aspect | CE Configuration | CB Configuration |
|--------|------------------|------------------|
| Input impedance | Medium ($\sim$ k$\Omega$) | Very low ($\sim$ tens of $\Omega$) |
| Frequency of operation | Better suited for moderate RF ($\sim$ tens of MHz) | Preferred for VHF/UHF ($\sim$ hundreds of MHz to GHz) |
| Feedback arrangement | Output and feedback through capacitive divider | Output and feedback share the same tank connection |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a) Draw the equivalent circuit of a quartz crystal. Derive the expressions for its series and parallel resonant frequencies.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Equivalent Circuit (Refer Section 4.2 diagram):**

The quartz crystal is represented as a series RLC branch ($R$, $L$, $C$) representing the motional (vibrational) behaviour, in parallel with a static capacitance $C_0$ representing the electrodes and the holder.

**Step 1 — Derivation of Series Resonant Frequency $f_s$**

At series resonance, the imaginary part of the motional branch impedance is zero:

$$\begin{aligned}
\omega_s L - \frac{1}{\omega_s C} &= 0 \\
\omega_s^2 &= \frac{1}{LC} \\
f_s &= \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}
\end{aligned}$$

At $f_s$, the crystal impedance is a minimum (equal to $R$), and the crystal behaves as a **short circuit** (neglecting $R$).

**Step 2 — Derivation of Parallel Resonant Frequency $f_p$**

At parallel resonance, the total admittance of the crystal vanishes. Treating $R$ as negligible:

$$\begin{aligned}
Y_{total} &= j\omega C_0 + \frac{1}{j\omega L + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C}} \\
         &= j\omega C_0 - \frac{j\omega C}{\omega^2 L C - 1} = 0
\end{aligned}$$

Solving:

$$\omega_p^2 = \frac{1}{L \cdot \dfrac{C C_0}{C + C_0}}$$

$$f_p = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L \cdot \dfrac{C C_0}{C + C_0}}}$$

At $f_p$, the crystal impedance is a maximum, and the crystal behaves as an **open circuit**.

**Numerical note:** Since $C_0 \gg C$ in real quartz, $f_p$ is only slightly greater than $f_s$ — typically less than $0.4\%$ higher.

---

**(b) Explain the working of a Pierce crystal oscillator with a neat sketch. List any three advantages of crystal oscillators.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Pierce Oscillator (commonly used with the crystal in the *inductive* region $f_s < f < f_p$):**

The Pierce oscillator is essentially a **Colpitts oscillator in which the inductor is replaced by a crystal**. The crystal operates in its *inductive* region (between $f_s$ and $f_p$), and the two capacitors $C_1$ and $C_2$ form the capacitive divider as in a Colpitts.

**Working:**
- The transistor (or inverter) is biased in the active region.
- The crystal presents an inductive reactance, completing the LC tank with $C_1$ and $C_2$.
- At the resonant frequency of this combined tank, the Barkhausen criterion is satisfied and oscillations are sustained.
- The frequency of oscillation is precisely $f_s < f_o < f_p$, *locked* by the crystal's mechanical resonance.

**Sketch description (textual):**
- A BJT (or CMOS inverter) with base connected via a biasing resistor to $V_{CC}$.
- Output (collector/drain) connected to one terminal of the crystal.
- Other terminal of the crystal grounded.
- Two capacitors $C_1$ and $C_2$ in series from output to ground, with their junction fed back to the input (base/gate).

**Three Advantages of Crystal Oscillators (from the 3-mark question, expanded):**
1. **Frequency stability of $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-11}$ per day** — orders of magnitude better than LC oscillators.
2. **Quality factor $Q$ of $10^4$ to $10^6$** — far exceeds the $Q$ achievable with discrete LC tanks.
3. **Compact, rugged, and immune to environmental noise** — particularly with AT-cut quartz, the frequency variation with temperature is less than $\pm 0.005\%$ over $0$ to $70^\circ\text{C}$.
4. **Low aging** — typically less than $1$ part per million per year.
5. **Low cost in mass production** — quartz crystals are inexpensive when ordered in bulk for consumer electronics.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert — Colpitts and Crystal Oscillators**
> 1. **Forgetting the Series-Combination Formula:** When computing $C_{eq}$ for the Colpitts tank, students often *add* $C_1$ and $C_2$ instead of using the product-over-sum formula. The capacitors are in **series** in the tank — therefore use $\dfrac{C_1 C_2}{C_1 + C_2}$, **not** $C_1 + C_2$. *(1–2 marks lost per wrong derivation step.)*
> 2. **Mixing up $f_s$ and $f_p$:** The series resonance $f_s$ does **not** depend on $C_0$, while the parallel resonance $f_p$ **does**. Always state this explicitly — the KTU examiner awards half a mark for this distinction.
> 3. **Confusing Pierce with Miller:** In a Pierce oscillator, the crystal replaces the **inductor** of a Colpitts (operating in its inductive region). In a Miller oscillator, the crystal operates near its **series resonance** and is placed in the **feedback path** (not as an inductor). Mixing the two is a common 3-mark deduction.
> 4. **Skipping the Phase Diagram:** A common KTU pet peeve is students writing the loop equation without explicitly stating the **$180^\circ$ from CE amplifier + $180^\circ$ from tank = $360^\circ$** total phase shift. Always write this out clearly.
> 5. **Wrong Units in Numerical Problems:** Watch out for $\mu$H vs H, pF vs F, kHz vs Hz. Convert and write units at every step.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this as your last-night revision sheet before the KTU ESE exam.**

### Colpitts Oscillator — Key Takeaways
- ☐ It is a **capacitive divider** type LC oscillator (Hartley = inductive divider).
- ☐ Tank circuit = single inductor $L$ in parallel with the *series* combination of $C_1$ and $C_2$.
- ☐ **Frequency equation:** $f_o = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{C_1 + C_2}{L\, C_1 C_2}}$ — memorize this.
- ☐ **Equivalent tank capacitance:** $C_{eq} = \dfrac{C_1 C_2}{C_1 + C_2}$ (series combination).
- ☐ **Feedback fraction:** $\beta = \dfrac{C_1}{C_2}$ (in CE configuration).
- ☐ **Barkhausen magnitude condition:** $A \geq \dfrac{C_2}{C_1}$.
- ☐ **Barkhausen phase condition:** $180^\circ$ (CE amplifier) $+ 180^\circ$ (tank at resonance) $= 360^\circ$.
- ☐ Two configurations: **CE** (general purpose) and **CB** (high-frequency VHF/UHF).

### Quartz Crystal — Key Takeaways
- ☐ Crystal = series RLC (motional) **in parallel** with static $C_0$.
- ☐ **Piezoelectric effect** — converts mechanical ↔ electrical energy.
- ☐ **Series resonance:** $f_s = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$ — crystal is a **short circuit**.
- ☐ **Parallel resonance:** $f_p = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L \cdot \dfrac{C C_0}{C+C_0}}}$ — crystal is an **open circuit**.
- ☐ $f_p > f_s$, with the difference typically less than $0.4\%$ of $f_s$.
- ☐ **Inductive region** ($f_s < f < f_p$) is the *only* region useful for oscillator design.
- ☐ **Quality factor** $Q = \dfrac{1}{R}\sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C}} \sim 10^4$ to $10^6$.
- ☐ **Frequency stability** $\sim 10^{-6}$ to $10^{-11}$ per day.
- ☐ **Pierce oscillator** = Colpitts with crystal replacing $L$ (operates in inductive region).
- ☐ **Miller oscillator** = crystal in feedback path (operates at series resonance).

### Common Application Areas (write this in 1-mark KTU questions)
- ☐ Colpitts: RF signal generators, local oscillators in superheterodyne receivers, function generators.
- ☐ Crystal: digital watches, microcontroller clock sources (e.g., 16 MHz crystal in 8051/Arduino), cellular base stations, GPS modules.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analysis of RC Phase Shift Oscillator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET302 - ELECTRONIC DEVICES AND CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Analysis of RC Phase Shift Oscillator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **RC Phase Shift Oscillator** is a sinusoidal, low-frequency (audio range, typically **20 Hz to 100 kHz**) oscillator that uses a combination of an **inverting amplifier** and a **three-section RC ladder network (feedback network)** to generate sustained oscillations. The RC network produces a total phase shift of **180°**, which, when combined with the **180° phase shift of the inverting amplifier**, satisfies the **Barkhausen Criterion** for sustained oscillations (total loop phase = 0° or 360°).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):**
> The analysis of the RC Phase Shift Oscillator is a **high-weightage topic** in KTU ESE examinations. Students must master both the **BJT (CE) version** and the **Op-Amp version**, including the derivation of the frequency of oscillation and the minimum amplifier gain condition.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Three-Turn Steering" Analogy

Imagine you are pushing a child on a swing. The swing naturally moves back and forth in a periodic cycle. Now imagine a **rope with three loose knots** connecting your hand to the swing:

- Each knot introduces a small **delay** (analogous to a **60° phase lag**).
- Three knots together produce a total delay of **180°** (half a cycle).
- The amplifier then "flips" the signal (another 180°), giving a full **360° push** in sync with the swing.

> [!NOTE]
> If you push exactly in rhythm (Barkhausen phase condition) and push hard enough (Barkhausen magnitude condition: $\vert A \beta \vert \geq 1$), the swing oscillates forever. This is the essence of every sinusoidal oscillator.

## 1.3 Key Components of the RC Phase Shift Oscillator

| Component | Function | KTU Terminology |
|---|---|---|
| **Amplifier (CE BJT / Op-Amp)** | Provides **180° phase shift** and **gain** to overcome losses | Active Inverting Stage |
| **RC Section 1 (R₁, C₁)** | Produces **60° phase lag** | Lead-Lag Network |
| **RC Section 2 (R₂, C₂)** | Produces **60° phase lag** | Lead-Lag Network |
| **RC Section 3 (R₃, C₃)** | Produces **60° phase lag** | Lead-Lag Network |
| **Feedback Loop** | Routes output back to input | Positive Feedback Path |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Convention:** In the KTU syllabus, the three RC sections are assumed **identical** ($R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = R$ and $C_1 = C_2 = C_3 = C$) to simplify the derivation. This assumption is used in the standard formula sheet and is the expected approach in board examinations.
> 
> **Standard Frequency Range:** $\mathbf{20 \text{ Hz} \text{ to } 100 \text{ kHz}}$ (Audio frequency range).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — The Barkhausen Criterion

For any sinusoidal oscillator, the **Barkhausen Criterion** requires two simultaneous conditions to be satisfied:

1. **Loop Phase Shift Condition:** The total phase shift around the closed loop must be an integer multiple of $2\pi$ (i.e., $0°$ or $360°$).
$$ \angle A \beta = 0° \text{ or } 360° \text{ (i.e., } 2n\pi \text{ radians)} $$

2. **Loop Gain Magnitude Condition:** The magnitude of the loop gain must be at least unity.
$$ \vert A \beta \vert \geq 1 $$

For the RC Phase Shift Oscillator, the **inverting amplifier** contributes **180°**, and the **three-stage RC network** must contribute the remaining **180°** (i.e., $3 \times 60°$). This is the minimum number of RC sections required because a single RC stage can produce at most **90°** of phase shift (only at infinite frequency), and two stages can produce a maximum of **180°** (only at infinite frequency). Hence, **three identical RC sections** are used to obtain a practical 180° phase shift at a finite frequency.

## 2.2 Why Three Sections? — Engineering Rationale

- A single RC network can never reach 180° phase shift at a finite, real frequency.
- Two RC sections can theoretically reach 180° only at $\omega \to \infty$, which is impractical.
- Three sections provide a stable 180° at a well-defined, finite frequency — making the circuit physically realizable and stable.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Utility:** RC phase shift oscillators are used in **audio signal generators**, **musical synthesizers**, **tone generation circuits**, and as **local oscillators** in communication receivers operating in the audio band. They are inexpensive, simple to construct, and produce reasonably pure sine waves at low frequencies where LC oscillators become impractical (large inductors).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter / Quantity | Formula | Condition / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Frequency of Oscillation** (RC Phase Shift) | $f = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC \sqrt{6}}$ | Hz |
| 2 | **Angular Frequency** | $\omega = \dfrac{1}{RC\sqrt{6}}$ | rad/s |
| 3 | **Feedback Factor** $\beta$ (magnitude) | $\vert \beta \vert = \dfrac{1}{29}$ | Dimensionless |
| 4 | **Minimum Gain (Op-Amp Version)** | $A \geq 29$ | Dimensionless |
| 5 | **Op-Amp Feedback Resistor Condition** | $R_f \geq 29\, R_1$ | Ohms ($\Omega$) |
| 6 | **Minimum Gain (BJT CE Version)** | $h_{fe} \geq 29 + \dfrac{23}{h_{ie}}\cdot R$ | Dimensionless |
| 7 | **Phase Shift per RC Section** | $\phi = 60°$ | Degrees |
| 8 | **Total RC Network Phase** | $3 \times 60° = 180°$ | Degrees |
| 9 | **Amplifier Phase** (CE / Inverting Op-Amp) | $180°$ | Degrees |
| 10 | **Total Loop Phase** | $180° + 180° = 360° \equiv 0°$ | Degrees |
| 11 | **Operating Frequency Range** | $20 \text{ Hz} \text{ to } 100 \text{ kHz}$ | Audio band |
| 12 | **Required Condition for Equal Resistors/Capacitors** | $R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = R$, $C_1 = C_2 = C_3 = C$ | Identical sections |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Board Exams:** *"To oscillate, you need **29** friends and the frequency formula has **√6** inside."* — This single line covers both the gain condition and the frequency formula, the two most asked parts in the ESE.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Circuit Configuration (BJT CE Version)

Consider a Common Emitter (CE) amplifier using an NPN BJT with a collector resistor $R_C$ and an emitter resistor $R_E$ (bypassed by a capacitor). The output $V_o$ is taken from the collector. The feedback network consists of **three identical RC sections**, each with resistance $R$ and capacitance $C$, connected in a **ladder configuration**. The junction between RC sections and the input of the BJT completes the loop.

Let us define the nodes:
- Node $V_o$ — output of the amplifier (collector of BJT)
- Node $V_1$ — junction between third and second RC section
- Node $V_2$ — junction between second and first RC section
- Node $V_i$ — feedback signal returned to the BJT base (input)

The three RC sections, looking from output to input, form the network:

$$ V_o \;\longrightarrow\; R \;\longrightarrow\; (\text{Node } V_1) \;\longrightarrow\; R \;\longrightarrow\; (\text{Node } V_2) \;\longrightarrow\; R \;\longrightarrow\; V_i $$
$$ \text{with shunt capacitors } C \text{ from each node to ground.} $$

## 3.2 Loop Equations Using KCL (Kirchhoff's Current Law)

Let the input impedance of the BJT (looking into the base) be $R_i$ (assumed very high, ideally infinite, so we ignore the loading effect on the last RC section — this is a standard KTU assumption for the simplified derivation).

**At Node $V_1$ (KCL):**
The currents leaving node $V_1$ through the resistors and capacitor must sum to zero.

$$ \frac{V_1 - V_o}{R} + \frac{V_1 - V_2}{R} + j\omega C \cdot V_1 = 0 $$

Rearranging:

$$ V_1 \left( \frac{2}{R} + j\omega C \right) = \frac{V_o}{R} + \frac{V_2}{R} $$

Multiplying through by $R$:

$$ V_1 (2 + j\omega RC) = V_o + V_2 \quad \text{...(Equation 1)} $$

**At Node $V_2$ (KCL):**

$$ \frac{V_2 - V_1}{R} + \frac{V_2 - V_i}{R} + j\omega C \cdot V_2 = 0 $$

$$ V_2 (2 + j\omega RC) = V_1 + V_i \quad \text{...(Equation 2)} $$

**At Node $V_i$ (KCL):** Since the input impedance of the BJT is assumed infinite, no current flows into the base terminal.

$$ \frac{V_i - V_2}{R} + j\omega C \cdot V_i = 0 $$

$$ V_i \left( \frac{1}{R} + j\omega C \right) = \frac{V_2}{R} $$

Multiplying by $R$:

$$ V_i (1 + j\omega RC) = V_2 \quad \text{...(Equation 3)} $$

## 3.3 Solving the Loop Equations

From **Equation 3**:

$$ V_2 = V_i (1 + j\omega RC) $$

Substitute into **Equation 2**:

$$ V_i (1 + j\omega RC)(2 + j\omega RC) = V_1 + V_i $$

$$ V_1 = V_i \left[ (1 + j\omega RC)(2 + j\omega RC) - 1 \right] $$

Expanding $(1 + j\omega RC)(2 + j\omega RC)$:

$$ (1 + j\omega RC)(2 + j\omega RC) = 2 + j\omega RC + 2j\omega RC + (j\omega RC)^2 $$

$$ = 2 + 3j\omega RC + j^2 \omega^2 R^2 C^2 = 2 + 3j\omega RC - \omega^2 R^2 C^2 $$

Therefore:

$$ V_1 = V_i \left[ 2 + 3j\omega RC - \omega^2 R^2 C^2 - 1 \right] = V_i \left[ 1 + 3j\omega RC - \omega^2 R^2 C^2 \right] \quad \text{...(Equation 4)} $$

Substitute $V_1$ and $V_2$ into **Equation 1**:

$$ V_1 (2 + j\omega RC) = V_o + V_2 $$

$$ V_i \left[ 1 + 3j\omega RC - \omega^2 R^2 C^2 \right] (2 + j\omega RC) = V_o + V_i (1 + j\omega RC) $$

## 3.4 Expanding the Left-Hand Side

Let $a = \omega RC$ for brevity. Then:

$$ \left[ 1 + 3ja - a^2 \right] (2 + ja) $$

Expanding term by term:

$$ = 1 \cdot 2 + 1 \cdot ja + 3ja \cdot 2 + 3ja \cdot ja + (-a^2) \cdot 2 + (-a^2) \cdot ja $$

$$ = 2 + ja + 6ja + 3j^2 a^2 - 2a^2 - ja^3 $$

Recall $j^2 = -1$:

$$ = 2 + 7ja - 3a^2 - 2a^2 - ja^3 $$

$$ = 2 + 7ja - 5a^2 - ja^3 $$

Grouping real and imaginary parts:

$$ = (2 - 5a^2) + j(7a - a^3) $$

## 3.5 Assembling the Feedback Ratio

The full equation becomes:

$$ V_i \left[ (2 - 5a^2) + j(7a - a^3) \right] = V_o + V_i (1 + ja) $$

$$ V_i (2 - 5a^2) + jV_i(7a - a^3) - V_i(1 + ja) = V_o $$

$$ V_i \left[ (2 - 5a^2 - 1) + j(7a - a^3 - a) \right] = V_o $$

$$ V_i \left[ (1 - 5a^2) + j(6a - a^3) \right] = V_o $$

The feedback ratio $\beta = V_i / V_o$ is therefore:

$$ \beta = \frac{1}{(1 - 5a^2) + j(6a - a^3)} \quad \text{where } a = \omega RC $$

## 3.6 Applying the Barkhausen Phase Condition

For oscillation, $\beta$ must be **real** (i.e., purely resistive, no imaginary component), because the CE amplifier already provides the 180° phase shift — the RC network must contribute the remaining 180°, but the feedback signal at the oscillating frequency must be **in phase with the input** as seen by the amplifier.

Setting the imaginary part of the denominator to zero:

$$ 6a - a^3 = 0 $$

$$ a(6 - a^2) = 0 $$

Since $a = \omega RC \neq 0$ (we are not at DC), we have:

$$ a^2 = 6 \quad \Rightarrow \quad a = \sqrt{6} $$

Substituting back $a = \omega RC$:

$$ \omega^2 R^2 C^2 = 6 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega^2 = \frac{6}{R^2 C^2} $$

$$ \omega = \frac{\sqrt{6}}{RC} $$

The **frequency of oscillation** in Hz is:

$$ \boxed{f = \frac{1}{2\pi RC \sqrt{6}}} $$

## 3.7 Applying the Barkhausen Magnitude Condition

At the oscillating frequency, the imaginary part vanishes, so $\beta$ becomes purely real:

$$ \beta = \frac{1}{1 - 5a^2} $$

Substitute $a^2 = 6$:

$$ \beta = \frac{1}{1 - 5(6)} = \frac{1}{1 - 30} = \frac{1}{-29} $$

The negative sign indicates the **180° phase shift** of the RC network. The **magnitude** of the feedback factor is:

$$ \vert \beta \vert = \frac{1}{29} $$

For Barkhausen magnitude condition $\vert A \beta \vert \geq 1$:

$$ A \geq \frac{1}{\vert \beta \vert} = 29 $$

The amplifier must therefore have a **minimum voltage gain of 29**.

## 3.8 Summary Table — Key Derivation Results

| Quantity | Result | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Imaginary part set to zero: $6a - a^3 = 0$ | $a^2 = 6$ | Frequency condition |
| Frequency of oscillation | $f = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC \sqrt{6}}$ | Hz |
| Feedback factor magnitude | $\vert \beta \vert = 1/29$ | Network loss |
| Minimum amplifier gain | $A \geq 29$ | Gain condition |
| Op-Amp resistor condition | $R_f \geq 29 R_1$ | Practical design rule |

## 3.9 Numerical Example (KTU Board Pattern)

**Problem:** A RC phase shift oscillator uses $R = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$ and $C = 1 \text{ nF}$. Find the frequency of oscillation and the minimum gain required.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Substitute into the frequency formula:

$$ f = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10 \times 10^3 \times 1 \times 10^{-9} \times \sqrt{6}} $$

$$ = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^{-5} \times 2.449} $$

$$ = \frac{1}{1.538 \times 10^{-4}} $$

$$ f \approx 6.5 \text{ kHz} $$

Step 2 — Minimum gain:

$$ A \geq 29 $$

$$\boxed{f \approx 6.5 \text{ kHz}, \quad A_{\min} = 29}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram of RC Phase Shift Oscillator (Functional Architecture)

The following **Mermaid flowchart** maps the functional architecture of the oscillator, showing the signal flow from the amplifier through the three RC sections and back, including the phase shifts at each stage.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN[Input Node Vi] -->|Feedback Signal| AMP[Inverting Amplifier]
    AMP -->|180 Degree Phase Shift| GAIN[Gain Block A >= 29]
    GAIN -->|Output Vo| NODE1[Node 1]
    NODE1 -->|R| CAP1[Shunt Capacitor C]
    CAP1 -->|60 Degree Lag| NODE2[Node 2]
    NODE2 -->|R| CAP2[Shunt Capacitor C]
    CAP2 -->|60 Degree Lag| NODE3[Node 3]
    NODE3 -->|R| CAP3[Shunt Capacitor C]
    CAP3 -->|60 Degree Lag| IN
    NODE1 -.->|RC Section 1| NODE2
    NODE2 -.->|RC Section 2| NODE3
    NODE3 -.->|RC Section 3| IN
```

## 4.2 Detailed Circuit Schematic — BJT Version (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph AMP[Inverting Amplifier CE BJT]
        IN_B[Base Input] -->|Vin| BJT[Common Emitter BJT]
        BJT --> VOUT[Collector Output Vo]
        BJT -->|Emitter| GND1[Emitter Bypass Capacitor to GND]
    end
    subgraph RC[Three Stage RC Ladder Network]
        VOUT --> RC1[R Section 1]
        RC1 --> NODE1[Junction Node 1]
        NODE1 --> CC1[Capacitor C to GND]
        NODE1 --> RC2[R Section 2]
        RC2 --> NODE2[Junction Node 2]
        NODE2 --> CC2[Capacitor C to GND]
        NODE2 --> RC3[R Section 3]
        RC3 --> NODE3[Junction Node 3]
        NODE3 --> CC3[Capacitor C to GND]
    end
    NODE3 -->|Feedback Vi| IN_B
```

## 4.3 Detailed Circuit Schematic — Op-Amp Version (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph OPAMP[Non Inverting Op Amp Amplifier]
        V_N[Node 3 Feedback Signal] --> R1A[Resistor R1]
        R1A --> V_MINUS[Inverting Input]
        V_MINUS --> OP[Op Amp]
        OP --> VOA[Output Vo]
        OP -->|Feedback| RF[Resistor Rf]
        RF --> V_MINUS
    end
    subgraph RC3[RC Phase Shift Ladder]
        VOA --> RA1[R Section 1]
        RA1 --> NA1[Node 1 with Capacitor C]
        NA1 --> RA2[R Section 2]
        RA2 --> NA2[Node 2 with Capacitor C]
        NA2 --> RA3[R Section 3]
        RA3 --> V_N
        V_N --> CA3[Capacitor C to GND]
    end
```

## 4.4 Signal Phase and Magnitude Flow Chart

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Signal Vi] -->|Phase 0 Degree| B[Amplifier]
    B -->|Phase Shift 180 Degree| C[Output Vo]
    C -->|RC1 Phase Lag 60 Degree| D[Node 1]
    D -->|RC2 Phase Lag 60 Degree| E[Node 2]
    E -->|RC3 Phase Lag 60 Degree| F[Node 3]
    F -->|Total Phase 180 Degree| G[Feedback Vi to Amplifier]
    G -->|Loop Total 360 Degree = 0 Degree| A
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The diagrams are functional architecture maps — they show the **signal path** and **phase relationships** of the RC Phase Shift Oscillator. In a KTU exam, students may be asked to draw the circuit; the block diagrams above give the examiner's expected logical flow of the signal.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Q: State and explain the Barkhausen criterion for sustained oscillations.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The Barkhausen criterion states that for sustained sinusoidal oscillations, two conditions must be simultaneously satisfied:

1. **Loop Phase Condition:** The total phase shift around the closed loop must be an integer multiple of $2\pi$ (i.e., $0°$ or $360°$). Mathematically, $\angle A\beta = 2n\pi$ radians, where $n$ is an integer.
2. **Loop Gain Condition:** The magnitude of the loop gain must equal unity (for sustained oscillations) or be slightly greater than unity (to compensate for losses and ensure oscillation build-up). Mathematically, $\vert A\beta \vert \geq 1$.

In the RC Phase Shift Oscillator, the inverting amplifier provides $180°$ phase shift, and the three-stage RC network provides the remaining $180°$, giving a total loop phase of $360°$ (or $0°$). The amplifier gain must be at least 29 to satisfy the magnitude condition, since $\vert \beta \vert = 1/29$.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Q: Why are three RC sections used in an RC phase shift oscillator instead of one or two?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A single RC network can produce a maximum phase shift of $90°$, which is insufficient because the remaining $90°$ can never be achieved at any finite frequency. Two RC sections can theoretically produce $180°$ only at infinite frequency ($\omega \to \infty$), which is impractical and physically unrealizable.

Three identical RC sections can produce a stable phase shift of $180°$ at a well-defined, finite frequency $f = 1/(2\pi RC \sqrt{6})$. Each section contributes $60°$, and the network is both physically realizable and frequency-stable. This is the standard engineering compromise used in the KTU syllabus.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Part B questions carry 14 marks and offer internal choice. The question usually has two sub-parts: **(a)** for 7 marks and **(b)** for 7 marks. Students must answer **either** Choice A **or** Choice B in full.

---

### ✅ Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Question]

**Q: With the help of a neat circuit diagram, derive the expression for the frequency of oscillation and the minimum gain required for an RC phase shift oscillator using a BJT in CE configuration. Assume three identical RC sections.**

#### (a) Circuit Description and Frequency Derivation (7 Marks)

**Circuit Description:**
The RC phase shift oscillator using a CE-configured BJT consists of:
- An NPN transistor in **Common Emitter (CE)** configuration providing a $180°$ phase shift between base (input) and collector (output).
- A **three-stage RC ladder network** connected between the collector (output) and the base (input), providing an additional $180°$ phase shift.
- The RC sections are identical: $R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = R$ and $C_1 = C_2 = C_3 = C$.

**Derivation:**
The feedback ratio of the three-stage RC network (assuming the input impedance of the BJT is very high, so no loading) is derived using KCL at each node. Let $V_o$ be the output, $V_1, V_2$ the intermediate nodes, and $V_i$ the feedback voltage.

KCL at node $V_1$:

$$ V_1(2 + j\omega RC) = V_o + V_2 $$

KCL at node $V_2$:

$$ V_2(2 + j\omega RC) = V_1 + V_i $$

KCL at node $V_i$ (no loading by BJT):

$$ V_i(1 + j\omega RC) = V_2 $$

Substituting sequentially and simplifying (let $a = \omega RC$):

$$ \beta = \frac{V_i}{V_o} = \frac{1}{(1 - 5a^2) + j(6a - a^3)} $$

For oscillation, the imaginary part of the denominator must be zero:

$$ 6a - a^3 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad a^2 = 6 $$

Therefore, the **frequency of oscillation** is:

$$ \boxed{f = \frac{1}{2\pi RC \sqrt{6}} \text{ Hz}} $$

#### (b) Gain Derivation and Condition (7 Marks)

At the oscillation frequency, with $a^2 = 6$, the feedback factor becomes purely real:

$$ \beta = \frac{1}{1 - 5(6)} = \frac{1}{-29} $$

The magnitude is $\vert \beta \vert = 1/29$, and the negative sign indicates the $180°$ phase shift contributed by the RC network.

By the **Barkhausen magnitude condition**:

$$ \vert A \beta \vert \geq 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad A \geq \frac{1}{\vert \beta \vert} = 29 $$

For a CE BJT amplifier, the voltage gain is $A = h_{fe} R_C / h_{ie}$. The minimum gain condition translates to:

$$ \frac{h_{fe} R_C}{h_{ie}} \geq 29 \quad \Rightarrow \quad h_{fe} \geq \frac{29 h_{ie}}{R_C} $$

$$\boxed{A_{\min} = 29, \quad h_{fe,\min} = \frac{29 h_{ie}}{R_C}}$$

---

### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice — KTU Pattern]

**Q: Design an RC phase shift oscillator using an op-amp to generate a sine wave of frequency 1 kHz. Derive the frequency formula and the resistor condition for the feedback network.**

#### (a) Frequency Derivation for Op-Amp Version (7 Marks)

The op-amp version uses a **non-inverting amplifier** (inverting input is grounded through $R_1$, and feedback resistor $R_f$ is connected from output to inverting input). The same three-section RC ladder network provides $180°$ phase shift. The non-inverting amplifier provides $0°$ phase shift, so the RC network must contribute the full $180°$ in this configuration. (Note: Some KTU textbooks show the inverting version where the amplifier provides $180°$ and the RC network provides $180°$; the frequency formula is the same in both cases.)

**Derivation:** The same as the BJT case, with identical RC sections:

KCL at the three nodes yields:

$$ \beta = \frac{1}{(1 - 5a^2) + j(6a - a^3)} $$

Setting imaginary part to zero:

$$ a^2 = 6 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega RC = \sqrt{6} $$

$$\boxed{f = \frac{1}{2\pi RC \sqrt{6}}}$$

**Design Calculation for 1 kHz:**
Choose $C = 10 \text{ nF}$ (standard value), then:

$$ R = \frac{1}{2\pi f C \sqrt{6}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 1000 \times 10 \times 10^{-9} \times 2.449} $$

$$ R = \frac{1}{1.539 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 6.5 \text{ k}\Omega $$

#### (b) Gain Condition and Resistor Selection (7 Marks)

The non-inverting op-amp gain is:

$$ A = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1} $$

The Barkhausen magnitude condition requires $A \geq 29$:

$$ 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1} \geq 29 $$

$$ \frac{R_f}{R_1} \geq 28 $$

$$\boxed{R_f \geq 29 R_1 \text{ (approximately, for sustained oscillations)}}$$

**Design Example:** Choose $R_1 = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$, then $R_f \geq 29 \text{ k}\Omega$. Use $R_f = 33 \text{ k}\Omega$ (standard value) to ensure oscillation build-up.

**Final Design Summary:**
- $R = 6.5 \text{ k}\Omega$, $C = 10 \text{ nF}$, $R_1 = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_f = 33 \text{ k}\Omega$
- Oscillation frequency: $f \approx 1 \text{ kHz}$
- Output amplitude: stabilized by the saturation limit of the op-amp

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU ESE:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign in $\beta = -1/29$:** The negative sign carries the $180°$ phase information. Dropping it loses 1-2 marks.
> 
> 2. **Confusing the gain condition:** Writing "$A \geq 1$" instead of "$A \geq 29$" is a serious error — the RC network has significant attenuation ($1/29$), and the amplifier must compensate.
> 
> 3. **Missing the $\sqrt{6}$ in the frequency formula:** Writing $f = 1/(2\pi RC)$ is a classic KTU board trap. The $\sqrt{6}$ comes from the imaginary part equating to zero. **Always include $\sqrt{6}$**.
> 
> 4. **Ignoring the assumption of identical RC sections:** The standard KTU derivation assumes $R_1=R_2=R_3=R$ and $C_1=C_2=C_3=C$. State this assumption explicitly at the start of the derivation for full marks.
> 
> 5. **Confusing RC with LC oscillators:** RC phase shift oscillators work in the **audio range (20 Hz – 100 kHz)**. For frequencies above 100 kHz, LC or crystal oscillators are preferred. Mentioning this range earns appreciation marks.
> 
> 6. **Not mentioning the BJT input impedance assumption:** In the BJT derivation, the standard KTU assumption is that the input impedance of the BJT is **infinite** (or sufficiently high not to load the network). Forgetting this assumption may lead to a more complex equation that examiners may not accept.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** RC Phase Shift Oscillator = Inverting Amplifier + 3-stage Identical RC Ladder Network.
- **Operating Frequency Range:** $\mathbf{20 \text{ Hz} \text{ to } 100 \text{ kHz}}$ (audio band).
- **Frequency Formula:** $\mathbf{f = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC \sqrt{6}}}$.
- **Angular Frequency:** $\mathbf{\omega = \dfrac{\sqrt{6}}{RC}}$.
- **Feedback Factor:** $\mathbf{\beta = -\dfrac{1}{29}}$ (negative sign = $180°$ phase shift).
- **Magnitude of $\beta$:** $\mathbf{\vert \beta \vert = \dfrac{1}{29}}$.
- **Minimum Amplifier Gain:** $\mathbf{A \geq 29}$.
- **Op-Amp Resistor Condition:** $\mathbf{R_f \geq 29 R_1}$.
- **BJT Gain Condition:** $\mathbf{h_{fe} \geq \dfrac{29 h_{ie}}{R_C}}$.
- **Phase Shift per RC Section:** $60°$ (each).
- **Total RC Network Phase:** $180°$.
- **Amplifier Phase (Inverting):** $180°$.
- **Total Loop Phase:** $360° \equiv 0°$ (Barkhausen satisfied).
- **Barkhausen Criterion:** $\angle A\beta = 0°$ AND $\vert A\beta \vert \geq 1$.
- **Why three sections?** Two sections can only give $180°$ at infinite frequency; three sections give a stable, finite frequency.
- **Standard Assumptions for KTU Derivation:** Identical RC sections; infinite BJT input impedance; no loading effects.
- **Real-World Applications:** Audio generators, tone generators, musical synthesizers, local oscillators in communication receivers.
- **Mnemonic to Remember:** "**29 friends, √6 inside**" — minimum gain = 29, frequency formula contains √6.
- **Most-Asked KTU Sub-Question:** "Derive the frequency of oscillation" and "Find the minimum gain" — these two appear in nearly every KTU ESE paper on this topic.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Power Amplifiers - Classification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET302 - ELECTRONIC DEVICES AND CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Power Amplifiers - Classification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Power Amplifier** is a large-signal electronic amplifier specifically engineered to deliver substantial amounts of **electrical power** to a low-impedance load (such as a loudspeaker, antenna, motor, or transducer) rather than merely producing a large voltage or current. Power amplifiers operate in the **large-signal regime** where the transistor's output voltage and current swing over a wide range of the load line, demanding careful thermal design and bias-stability considerations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PCAET302 – Module 4):**
> *"Power Amplifiers are classified based on the portion of the input cycle during which the output current flows, i.e., the **conduction angle** (θ) of the active device, and the **position of the Q-point (quiescent operating point)** on the DC load line."*

## 1.2 The Five Primary Classes of Power Amplifiers

| Class | Q-Point Location | Conduction Angle (θ) | Ideal Maximum Efficiency |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **Class A** | Center of load line | **360°** | 25% (series-fed) / 50% (transformer) |
| **Class B** | Exactly at cutoff | **180°** | **78.6% (π/4)** |
| **Class AB** | Slightly above cutoff | **180° < θ < 360°** | 50% – 78.6% |
| **Class C** | Below cutoff bias | **θ < 180°** | > 78.6% (theoretical ∞ at θ→0) |
| **Class D** | Switched-mode operation | PWM controlled | **≈ 90–100%** (theoretical) |

> [!NOTE]
> The **conduction angle θ** is the angular duration (in electrical degrees) during one complete input cycle for which the transistor remains in the active (conducting) region and delivers current to the load.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Water Tap Intuition

Imagine a large water tank supplying water to a garden hose:
- A **Class A** amplifier is like a tap that is **always fully open**, continuously flowing water even when no one is using it. The water that flows when nobody is drinking represents *wasted DC power dissipated as heat*. Hence, efficiency is very low.
- A **Class B** amplifier is like a tap that is **closed by default** and only opens for exactly **half the duration** you press it (one transistor handles the positive half-cycle, the other handles the negative half-cycle in push-pull).
- A **Class C** amplifier opens for only a tiny sliver of the cycle, like a fast-tap that gives short bursts.
- A **Class D** amplifier is like a high-speed on/off solenoid valve that pulses so rapidly that the average flow still matches the desired output — a digital switching concept.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Trade-off:** Higher efficiency (Class D) means less heat dissipation but introduces **nonlinearity / distortion**. Lower efficiency (Class A) means the cleanest, most linear audio reproduction. The art of amplifier design lies in balancing **efficiency (η)**, **fidelity (THD)**, and **thermal management**.

## 1.4 Why Classification Matters in Engineering

In modern KTU-aligned curricula and real-world industries (audio engineering, RF transmitters, industrial motor drives, medical imaging, satellite communications), choosing the right amplifier class directly determines:
- **Battery life** in portable devices (favors Class D in smartphones, hearing aids).
- **Heat sink size and weight** in aerospace systems (favors Class B/AB).
- **Signal fidelity** in Hi-Fi audio and instrumentation (favors Class A).
- **Bandwidth and harmonic content** in RF/IF stages (favors Class C for narrow-band tuned amplifiers).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" Behind the Classification

The classification is fundamentally derived from the **position of the DC bias point (Q-point)** on the transistor's output characteristics. This position determines:

1. **How long the transistor conducts** during one cycle (the conduction angle θ).
2. **The amplitude of the quiescent current (I_CQ)** that flows even when no signal is present.
3. **The maximum theoretical conversion efficiency η**, defined as the ratio of AC power delivered to the load (P_out) to the DC power drawn from the supply (P_DC).

$$
\eta \;=\; \frac{P_{out\,(ac)}}{P_{DC}} \times 100\%
$$

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

### Class A — Full-Cycle Conduction (θ = 360°)
- The Q-point is biased at the **midpoint of the load line**, so I_CQ ≈ I_C(sat)/2.
- The transistor is *always ON* and conducts for the entire 360° of the input cycle.
- **Pros:** Excellent linearity, no crossover distortion, simplest biasing.
- **Cons:** Poor efficiency (the continuous I_CQ wastes power as heat even with no input signal).
- **Typical use:** High-fidelity audio pre-driver stages, RF input stages.

### Class B — Half-Cycle Conduction (θ = 180°)
- The Q-point is biased **exactly at cutoff** (I_CQ ≈ 0, V_BE ≈ 0 V).
- The transistor conducts for **only half** of the input cycle (180°).
- Almost always implemented as a **push-pull pair** (two complementary transistors: one NPN for positive half-cycle, one PNP for negative half-cycle) so that the full waveform is reconstructed.
- **Pros:** High efficiency (up to **78.6%**), zero quiescent power dissipation.
- **Cons:** Suffers from **crossover distortion** near the zero-crossing of the signal because of the transistor's V_BE threshold (~0.6–0.7 V for silicon BJTs).
- **Typical use:** Audio output stages, loudspeaker drivers, op-amp internal output stages.

### Class AB — The Practical Compromise (180° < θ < 360°)
- Q-point is biased **slightly above cutoff** using small V_BE biasing diodes / V_BE multiplier network.
- Each transistor conducts for **slightly more than 180°**, so both transistors overlap in a small region near zero-crossing, eliminating crossover distortion.
- **Pros:** Combines reasonable efficiency (50–70%) with low distortion.
- **Cons:** Small quiescent current still flows → some thermal loss.
- **Typical use:** Nearly all commercial audio amplifiers, op-amp outputs, battery-powered audio.

### Class C — Less Than Half-Cycle (θ < 180°)
- The Q-point is biased **well below cutoff** (negative V_BE for BJT, or below threshold for FET).
- The transistor conducts only for a narrow pulse near the **positive peak** of the input.
- Output is **highly distorted**, so a **tuned (LC) tank circuit** at the load is used to reconstruct a sinusoidal waveform via flywheel action.
- **Pros:** Highest theoretical efficiency (approaches 100% as θ → 0°).
- **Cons:** Cannot amplify without a tuned load; not suitable for audio.
- **Typical use:** RF power amplifiers in transmitters, radio-frequency oscillators, frequency multipliers.

### Class D — Switching-Mode Operation
- The transistor operates as a **switch** (either fully ON in saturation or fully OFF in cutoff), driven by a high-frequency **PWM (Pulse-Width Modulation)** signal whose duty cycle varies with the audio input.
- A **low-pass LC filter** at the output reconstructs the original audio waveform.
- **Pros:** Theoretically **100% efficiency** because the ideal switch dissipates zero power in both ON (V = 0) and OFF (I = 0) states.
- **Cons:** Requires complex PWM generation, suffers from switching losses at high frequencies and EMI.
- **Typical use:** Class-D audio amplifiers in smartphones, TVs, soundbars, subwoofers, and modern EV motor drives.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> All KTU board exam numericals on this topic reduce to one of the formulas below. Memorize the boxed entries.

| # | Parameter | Class A (Series-Fed) | Class A (Transformer) | Class B (Push-Pull) | Unit |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :---: |
| 1 | Conduction angle θ | 360° | 360° | 180° | degrees |
| 2 | Quiescent current I_CQ | V_CC / (2 R_L) | V_CC / R'_L | 0 | A |
| 3 | DC input power P_DC | V_CC² / (2 R_L) | V_CC² / R'_L | (2 V_CC²) / (π R_L) | W |
| 4 | Max AC output power P_out(max) | V_CC² / (8 R_L) | V_CC² / (2 R'_L) | V_CC² / (2 R_L) | W |
| 5 | Max collector dissipation P_C(max) | V_CC² / (8 R_L) | V_CC² / (2 R'_L) | V_CC² / (π² R_L) | W |
| 6 | Max conversion efficiency η_max | **25%** | **50%** | **78.6% (π/4)** | % |
| 7 | Figure of merit (utilization) | **0.125** | 0.25 | **0.4** | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Figure of Merit (FOM):** Defined as the ratio of maximum AC output power to the maximum collector power dissipation:
> $$
> \text{FOM} \;=\; \frac{P_{out(max)}}{P_{C(max)}}
> $$
> This tells the designer how much output power can be obtained per watt of transistor dissipation capacity. A **higher FOM is always better** (it means the transistor is "used" more efficiently).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Sector | Preferred Class | Reason |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Hi-Fi Audio Amplifiers (e.g., Marshall, McIntosh) | Class A / AB | Lowest harmonic distortion for music reproduction |
| Car Audio / Subwoofers | Class D | Compact, high efficiency, low heat |
| Mobile Phones / Hearing Aids | Class D | Battery life critical |
| Radio / TV Transmitters (RF) | Class C | Highest efficiency for CW & FM transmission |
| Servo Motor Drives / Robotics | Class D (PWM) | Precise digital control of motor torque |
| Medical (Pacemakers, Ultrasound) | Class AB / D | Balance of linearity and battery life |
| Test & Measurement Instrumentation | Class A | Highest linearity and accuracy |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Methodology Used:** Every derivation below assumes an **ideal transistor** (V_CE(sat) = 0, I_CEO = 0, perfect push-pull symmetry) and **sinusoidal excitation**. These assumptions are standard in KTU board solutions and the resulting efficiencies represent *theoretical upper limits*.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Class A Series-Fed Power Amplifier

**Circuit Description:** A common-emitter (or common-source) amplifier with the load R_L connected directly (in series) with the collector and a DC supply V_CC. No transformer is used.

**Step 1 — Position the Q-point at the center of the DC load line** for maximum symmetrical swing. The quiescent collector current is:

$$
I_{CQ} \;=\; \frac{V_{CC} - V_{CEQ}}{R_L} \;\approx\; \frac{V_{CC}}{2 R_L}
$$

(We assume V_CEQ = V_CC/2 for maximum swing.)

**Step 2 — DC input power drawn from the supply:**

$$
P_{DC} \;=\; V_{CC} \cdot I_{CQ} \;=\; V_{CC} \cdot \frac{V_{CC}}{2 R_L} \;=\; \frac{V_{CC}^{2}}{2 R_L}
$$

**Step 3 — AC output power delivered to the load.** With the Q-point at center, the maximum peak collector current swing is I_peak = I_CQ = V_CC/(2 R_L), and the maximum peak voltage swing is V_peak = I_CQ × R_L = V_CC/2. The RMS values are V_peak/√2 and I_peak/√2, so:

$$
P_{out} \;=\; \frac{V_{peak} \cdot I_{peak}}{2} \;=\; \frac{\left(\dfrac{V_{CC}}{2}\right) \cdot \left(\dfrac{V_{CC}}{2 R_L}\right)}{2} \;=\; \frac{V_{CC}^{2}}{8 R_L}
$$

**Step 4 — Maximum conversion efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{max} \;=\; \left.\frac{P_{out}}{P_{DC}}\right\vert_{max} \;=\; \frac{\dfrac{V_{CC}^{2}}{8 R_L}}{\dfrac{V_{CC}^{2}}{2 R_L}} \;=\; \frac{1}{4} \;=\; 25\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Even under ideal conditions, a series-fed Class A amplifier cannot exceed **25% efficiency**. The remaining 75% of the DC power is dissipated as heat in the transistor's collector junction — this is why Class A amplifiers require large heat sinks.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Class A Transformer-Coupled Power Amplifier

**Circuit Description:** The load R_L is coupled to the collector through an ideal step-down transformer with turns ratio n = N_1/N_2. The effective AC load seen at the collector is R'_L = n² R_L.

**Step 1 — Q-point biasing.** The DC resistance of the transformer's primary winding is nearly zero, so the DC load line is essentially **vertical**. The Q-point is set by the bias network such that V_CEQ = V_CC and I_CQ = V_CC / R'_L.

**Step 2 — DC input power:**

$$
P_{DC} \;=\; V_{CC} \cdot I_{CQ} \;=\; V_{CC} \cdot \frac{V_{CC}}{R'_L} \;=\; \frac{V_{CC}^{2}}{R'_L}
$$

**Step 3 — AC output power.** The collector voltage can swing from **0 to 2 V_CC** (because the transformer couples only the AC component, and the quiescent voltage is V_CC). So the peak AC voltage across the primary is V_peak = V_CC, and the peak current is I_peak = V_CC / R'_L.

$$
P_{out(max)} \;=\; \frac{V_{peak} \cdot I_{peak}}{2} \;=\; \frac{V_{CC} \cdot \left(\dfrac{V_{CC}}{R'_L}\right)}{2} \;=\; \frac{V_{CC}^{2}}{2 R'_L}
$$

**Step 4 — Maximum conversion efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{max} \;=\; \frac{P_{out(max)}}{P_{DC}} \;=\; \frac{\dfrac{V_{CC}^{2}}{2 R'_L}}{\dfrac{V_{CC}^{2}}{R'_L}} \;=\; \frac{1}{2} \;=\; 50\%
$$

> [!TIP]
> The transformer "frees" the collector voltage from being clamped at V_CC, allowing it to swing down to 0 V (and momentarily above V_CC), which **doubles the efficiency** of the Class A stage.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Class B Push-Pull Power Amplifier (The Most Important)

**Circuit Description:** Two complementary transistors (Q1 = NPN, Q2 = PNP) connected in a push-pull configuration at the output. Each transistor conducts for exactly half the input cycle (180°). The load R_L is connected between the joined emitters and ground.

**Step 1 — Output current waveform.** For a sinusoidal input, the output current through R_L is a full sine wave reconstructed by alternating halves from Q1 and Q2. Peak current:

$$
I_{peak} \;=\; \frac{V_{CC}}{R_L}
$$

**Step 2 — Maximum AC output power:**

$$
P_{out(max)} \;=\; \frac{V_{peak} \cdot I_{peak}}{2} \;=\; \frac{V_{CC} \cdot \left(\dfrac{V_{CC}}{R_L}\right)}{2} \;=\; \frac{V_{CC}^{2}}{2 R_L}
$$

**Step 3 — Average DC current drawn from V_CC.** Each transistor conducts for half a cycle. The average current over a full cycle from the supply is the average of a half-sinusoid:

$$
I_{DC} \;=\; \frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T/2} I_{peak} \sin(\omega t)\, dt \;=\; \frac{I_{peak}}{\pi} \;=\; \frac{V_{CC}}{\pi R_L}
$$

**Step 4 — DC input power:**

$$
P_{DC} \;=\; V_{CC} \cdot I_{DC} \;=\; V_{CC} \cdot \frac{V_{CC}}{\pi R_L} \;=\; \frac{V_{CC}^{2}}{\pi R_L}
$$

**Step 5 — Maximum conversion efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{max} \;=\; \frac{P_{out(max)}}{P_{DC}} \;=\; \frac{\dfrac{V_{CC}^{2}}{2 R_L}}{\dfrac{V_{CC}^{2}}{\pi R_L}} \;=\; \frac{\pi}{4} \;\approx\; 78.6\%
$$

**Step 6 — Maximum collector dissipation per transistor.** The dissipation is maximum at the condition V_out = (2/π) V_CC:

$$
P_{C(max)} \;=\; \frac{V_{CC}^{2}}{\pi^{2} R_L}
$$

**Step 7 — Figure of Merit (utilization):**

$$
\text{FOM} \;=\; \frac{P_{out(max)}}{P_{C(max)}} \;=\; \frac{\dfrac{V_{CC}^{2}}{2 R_L}}{\dfrac{V_{CC}^{2}}{\pi^{2} R_L}} \;=\; \frac{\pi^{2}}{2} \;\approx\; 4.93
$$

(Equivalently, expressed as a ratio: 0.4 when defined as P_C(max)/P_out(max).)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Asked:** The number **π/4 = 0.785 = 78.6%** is one of the most heavily tested values in the entire EDC syllabus. Always derive it cleanly: I_DC = I_peak/π, then η = (π/4).

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Class C Power Amplifier (Conceptual)

For a Class C amplifier with conduction angle θ, the efficiency is:

$$
\eta \;=\; \frac{\theta - \sin\theta}{4 \sin(\theta/2) - 2\theta \cos(\theta/2)}
$$

For θ = 180° (Class B), this reduces to π/4 ≈ 78.6%. As θ → 0°, the efficiency approaches 100% (but output power also approaches zero). Typical Class C operation uses θ ≈ 60°–120° with a tuned LC load to filter out the harmonic content and reconstruct the fundamental sinusoid.

---

## 3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Performance Metric | Class A (Series) | Class A (Transformer) | Class B | Class AB | Class C | Class D |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Conduction angle | 360° | 360° | 180° | 180°–360° | < 180° | Switched |
| Max theoretical efficiency | 25% | 50% | 78.6% | 50–78.6% | ≈ 90–100% | ≈ 100% |
| Distortion (THD) | Very Low | Very Low | High (crossover) | Low | Very High | Medium (PWM) |
| Power gain | High | High | Medium | Medium | Low | Low (switching) |
| Heat sink size | Largest | Large | Small | Small | Smallest | Smallest |
| Typical application | Audio pre-amp | Audio driver | Audio output | Commercial audio | RF transmitter | Modern audio, motor drives |
| Needs transformer/tuned load? | No | Yes | No | No | **Yes (LC tank)** | No |
| Quiescent power dissipation | **High** | High | **Zero** | Small | Zero | Zero |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagrams illustrate the **functional block-level architecture** of the various power amplifier classes. Mermaid flowcharts have been used (rather than physical schematic drawings) for maximum clarity in printed notes and to remain valid Mermaid v10 syntax (no reserved keywords used as node IDs).

## 4.1 Master Classification Flowchart of Power Amplifiers

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootA[Power Amplifiers] --> rootB[Classification by Conduction Angle]
    rootB --> classA[Class A : 360 degrees]
    rootB --> classB[Class B : 180 degrees]
    rootB --> classAB[Class AB : 180 to 360 degrees]
    rootB --> classC[Class C : less than 180 degrees]
    rootB --> classD[Class D : Switched PWM]
    classA --> classA1[Series Fed: 25 percent efficiency]
    classA --> classA2[Transformer Coupled: 50 percent efficiency]
    classB --> classB1[Push Pull Complementary Pair]
    classB --> classB2[Eta max equals 78.6 percent]
    classAB --> classAB1[Small Bias to Avoid Crossover Distortion]
    classC --> classC1[Requires Tuned LC Load]
    classD --> classD1[Requires LC Low Pass Filter]
```

## 4.2 Operational Block Diagram — Class B Push-Pull Stage

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputA[Input Sine Wave] --> splitter[Phase Splitter or Direct Drive]
    splitter --> q1path[NPN Transistor Q1]
    splitter --> q2path[PNP Transistor Q2]
    q1path --> joinNode[Joined Emitters Output Node]
    q2path --> joinNode
    joinNode --> load[Load R_L: Speaker]
    vccNode[Supply V_CC] -.-> q1path
    vccNode -.-> q2path
    q1path -. Conducts only positive half cycle .-> joinNode
    q2path -. Conducts only negative half cycle .-> joinNode
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Class D Switching Amplifier

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    audioIn[Audio Input Signal] --> comparator[Comparator with Triangle Wave]
    comparator --> pwmGen[PWM Signal Generation]
    pwmGen --> driver[Gate Driver Stage]
    driver --> fet1[Power MOSFET Half Bridge]
    fet1 --> lcFilter[LC Low Pass Filter]
    lcFilter --> speaker[Speaker Load]
    pwmGen -. Modulated duty cycle represents audio amplitude .-> fet1
```

## 4.4 Conduction-Angle Comparison Chart (Visual Reference)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sineA[Input Sine Wave Full Cycle 360 deg] --> aClamp[Class A: Conducts entire cycle]
    sineA --> bClamp[Class B: Conducts only second half 180 deg]
    sineA --> abClamp[Class AB: Conducts 200 to 220 deg]
    sineA --> cClamp[Class C: Conducts only 60 to 120 deg]
    aClamp --> aOut[Output: Full sine, low efficiency 25 to 50 percent]
    bClamp --> bOut[Output: Half waves, push pull reconstructs full sine, 78.6 percent]
    abClamp --> abOut[Output: Nearly full sine, no crossover distortion, 50 to 70 percent]
    cClamp --> cOut[Output: Narrow pulses, LC tank reconstructs sine, 90 plus percent]
```
