# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define a Class A power amplifier. Why is its maximum conversion efficiency limited to 25% in a series-fed configuration?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A Class A power amplifier is one in which the **transistor is biased such that the collector current flows for the entire 360° of the input cycle**, with the Q-point positioned at the center of the DC load line.

In a series-fed configuration, the maximum theoretical efficiency is limited to 25% because:
- **Even with zero input signal**, a continuous quiescent collector current I_CQ = V_CC/(2R_L) flows, drawing DC power P_DC = V_CC²/(2R_L) from the supply (1 mark).
- The maximum AC output power deliverable to R_L is P_out = V_CC²/(8R_L) (1 mark).
- The ratio η = P_out / P_DC = (1/4) = **25%**, since the Q-point center-biasing wastes half of the supply headroom on the quiescent condition (1 mark).

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is meant by "conduction angle" in a power amplifier? List the conduction angles for Class A, B, AB, and C operation.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The **conduction angle** is the angular measure (in electrical degrees) of the portion of the input signal cycle during which the transistor's output current flows (i.e., the device remains in the active/conducting region) (1 mark).

Conduction angles for each class (2 marks):
- **Class A:** 360° (full cycle)
- **Class B:** 180° (half cycle)
- **Class AB:** between 180° and 360° (typically 200°–220°)
- **Class C:** less than 180° (typically 60°–120°)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> The Part B question below is structured with internal choice (either-or) per KTU 2024 ESE regulations. Both alternatives are fully solved with model answers.

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — July 2024]

**A Class B push-pull power amplifier operates from a ±V_CC = ±20 V symmetrical supply and drives an 8 Ω loudspeaker load. Calculate:**
**(a) [7 Marks] The maximum AC output power, the DC input power drawn from the supply, and the maximum conversion efficiency. Also compute the maximum power dissipation per transistor.**
**(b) [7 Marks] The Figure of Merit (utilization) and explain what it signifies. Sketch the output current waveform and comment on the dominant distortion mechanism.**

---

#### Part (a) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**Given:** V_CC = 20 V, R_L = 8 Ω, Class B push-pull.

**Step 1 — Maximum peak output current:**

$$
I_{peak} \;=\; \frac{V_{CC}}{R_L} \;=\; \frac{20}{8} \;=\; 2.5 \;A
$$

**Step 2 — Maximum AC output power delivered to the load:**

$$
P_{out(max)} \;=\; \frac{V_{CC}^{2}}{2 R_L} \;=\; \frac{(20)^{2}}{2 \times 8} \;=\; \frac{400}{16} \;=\; 25 \;W
$$

**Step 3 — Average DC current drawn from the supply:**

$$
I_{DC} \;=\; \frac{I_{peak}}{\pi} \;=\; \frac{2.5}{\pi} \;\approx\; 0.796 \;A
$$

**Step 4 — DC input power:**

$$
P_{DC} \;=\; V_{CC} \cdot I_{DC} \;=\; 20 \times 0.796 \;\approx\; 15.92 \;W
$$

**Step 5 — Maximum conversion efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{max} \;=\; \frac{P_{out(max)}}{P_{DC}} \;=\; \frac{25}{15.92} \;\approx\; 1.571
$$

Wait — that exceeds 100%! Let me recheck. The error is that for **maximum efficiency** with a ±V_CC symmetrical supply, each side provides V_CC, and the total DC power is from both supplies combined, but the standard derivation for single-supply Class B gives P_DC = 2 V_CC I_DC for a dual supply.

Let me redo properly with **single-supply** V_CC = 20 V and R_L = 8 Ω:

**Step 3 (revised) — DC current for a single supply:**

$$
I_{DC} \;=\; \frac{I_{peak}}{\pi} \;=\; \frac{V_{CC}}{\pi R_L} \;=\; \frac{20}{\pi \times 8} \;\approx\; 0.796 \;A
$$

**Step 4 (revised) — DC input power:**

$$
P_{DC} \;=\; V_{CC} \cdot I_{DC} \;=\; 20 \times 0.796 \;\approx\; 15.92 \;W
$$

**Step 5 (revised) — Maximum conversion efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{max} \;=\; \frac{P_{out(max)}}{P_{DC}} \;=\; \frac{25}{15.92} \;\approx\; 1.571 \;\text{?}
$$

This is clearly wrong. The issue is that V_peak ≠ V_CC. For maximum sine output, V_peak is at most V_CC, so the formulas above are correct only when V_peak = V_CC. The maximum efficiency is always π/4 regardless of V_CC and R_L:

$$
\eta_{max} \;=\; \frac{\pi}{4} \;\approx\; 78.6\%
$$

**The correct final numbers (re-calculated properly):**

For maximum efficiency condition, V_out(peak) = V_CC = 20 V.

$$
P_{out(max)} \;=\; \frac{V_{CC}^{2}}{2 R_L} \;=\; \frac{400}{16} \;=\; 25 \;W \quad \text{(1.5 marks)}
$$

$$
P_{DC} \;=\; \frac{2 V_{CC}^{2}}{\pi R_L} \;=\; \frac{2 \times 400}{\pi \times 8} \;=\; \frac{800}{25.13} \;\approx\; 31.83 \;W \quad \text{(2 marks)}
$$

The correct formula for the **dual-supply ±V_CC** push-pull Class B gives:

$$
P_{DC} \;=\; \frac{2 V_{CC} \cdot I_{peak}}{\pi} \;=\; \frac{2 \times 20 \times 2.5}{\pi} \;\approx\; 31.83 \;W
$$

$$
\eta_{max} \;=\; \frac{P_{out}}{P_{DC}} \;=\; \frac{25}{31.83} \;\approx\; 78.6\% \;=\; \frac{\pi}{4} \quad \text{(2 marks)}
$$

**Step 6 — Maximum collector dissipation per transistor (occurs at V_out = (2/π) V_CC):**

$$
P_{C(max)} \;=\; \frac{V_{CC}^{2}}{\pi^{2} R_L} \;=\; \frac{400}{\pi^{2} \times 8} \;\approx\; 5.07 \;W \quad \text{(1.5 marks)}
$$

#### Part (b) — Figure of Merit and Distortion Analysis (7 Marks)

**Figure of Merit (utilization) calculation:**

$$
\text{FOM} \;=\; \frac{P_{out(max)}}{P_{C(max)}} \;=\; \frac{25}{5.07} \;\approx\; 4.93 \;=\; \frac{\pi^{2}}{2} \quad \text{(2 marks)}
$$

**Significance (2 marks):** The FOM indicates that for every watt of transistor dissipation rating, the amplifier can deliver **4.93 watts of output power**. A higher FOM means the output transistors are utilized more effectively. Class B (FOM ≈ 4.93) is vastly superior to Class A (FOM = 2) and transformer-coupled Class A (FOM = 4).

**Output current waveform (1 mark):** Each transistor produces a half-sinusoid of peak I_peak = 2.5 A; the two halves combine at the load to reconstruct a full sinusoidal current waveform.

**Dominant distortion mechanism (2 marks):** **Crossover distortion** near the zero-crossing of the waveform, caused by the dead zone of approximately ±0.6 V (the V_BE threshold of silicon BJTs) during which neither transistor conducts. This is mitigated in practice by biasing the transistors into Class AB operation with a small V_BE offset network.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Alternative Choice]

**A transformer-coupled Class A power amplifier delivers power to a 16 Ω loudspeaker through a transformer whose primary-to-secondary turns ratio is 10:1. The supply voltage is V_CC = 24 V. Calculate:**
**(a) [7 Marks] The effective AC load at the collector, the maximum AC output power delivered to the loudspeaker, the DC input power, and the maximum conversion efficiency.**
**(b) [7 Marks] The maximum power dissipated by the transistor and the Figure of Merit. Compare these results with a Class B push-pull amplifier delivering the same maximum output power.**

---

#### Part (a) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**Given:** V_CC = 24 V, R_L = 16 Ω (loudspeaker), n = N_1/N_2 = 10.

**Step 1 — Effective AC load impedance reflected to the primary:**

$$
R'_L \;=\; n^{2} \cdot R_L \;=\; (10)^{2} \times 16 \;=\; 1600 \;\Omega \quad \text{(1.5 marks)}
$$

**Step 2 — Q-point biasing (for maximum swing):** V_CEQ = V_CC = 24 V (DC load line is nearly vertical because the transformer primary has negligible DC resistance). The quiescent current is:

$$
I_{CQ} \;=\; \frac{V_{CC}}{R'_L} \;=\; \frac{24}{1600} \;=\; 0.015 \;A \;=\; 15 \;mA \quad \text{(1 mark)}
$$

**Step 3 — Maximum AC output power.** The collector voltage can swing from 0 to 2 V_CC, giving a peak AC voltage of V_peak = V_CC = 24 V across the primary. The corresponding peak current is I_peak = V_CC/R'_L = 15 mA.

$$
P_{out(max)} \;=\; \frac{V_{CC}^{2}}{2 R'_L} \;=\; \frac{(24)^{2}}{2 \times 1600} \;=\; \frac{576}{3200} \;=\; 0.18 \;W \;=\; 180 \;mW \quad \text{(2 marks)}
$$

**Step 4 — DC input power:**

$$
P_{DC} \;=\; V_{CC} \cdot I_{CQ} \;=\; 24 \times 0.015 \;=\; 0.36 \;W \;=\; 360 \;mW \quad \text{(1 mark)}
$$

**Step 5 — Maximum conversion efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{max} \;=\; \frac{P_{out(max)}}{P_{DC}} \;=\; \frac{0.18}{0.36} \;=\; 0.5 \;=\; 50\% \quad \text{(1.5 marks)}
$$

#### Part (b) — Transistor Dissipation, Figure of Merit, and Comparison (7 Marks)

**Maximum transistor dissipation (2 marks):** For a transformer-coupled Class A, the transistor dissipation is maximum when V_out = 0 (i.e., at the quiescent point with no signal):

$$
P_{C(max)} \;=\; P_{DC} \;=\; 360 \;mW
$$

Actually, more precisely, the maximum dissipation at the **quiescent** point is V_CC × I_CQ = 360 mW, and this occurs when there is no input signal. With signal applied, the dissipation decreases, so P_C(max) = 360 mW.

**Figure of Merit (2 marks):**

$$
\text{FOM} \;=\; \frac{P_{out(max)}}{P_{C(max)}} \;=\; \frac{180 \;mW}{360 \;mW} \;=\; 0.5
$$

(Equivalently, the utilization factor as conventionally defined is 0.25, or 25%.)

**Comparison with Class B (3 marks):** To deliver the same P_out(max) = 180 mW, a Class B push-pull amplifier would require:

- DC input power: P_DC = P_out/η = 180/0.786 ≈ 229 mW (much less than the 360 mW of Class A).
- Maximum transistor dissipation: P_C(max) = P_out/π²/2 ≈ 36.5 mW (about 10× lower than Class A's 360 mW).
- **Conclusion:** For the same output power, Class B requires **~6× less DC power** and transistors rated for **~10× less dissipation**, making it dramatically more efficient and compact. The trade-off is the need for a complementary push-pull pair and the presence of crossover distortion.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks on This Topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting the π in I_DC = I_peak/π for Class B.** Many students incorrectly write I_DC = I_peak/2, leading to a wrong efficiency of 50% instead of 78.6%. The half-sinusoid average is I_peak/π, NOT I_peak/2. (Loss: 2–3 marks per question)
> 2. **Confusing P_C(max) with P_DC.** The maximum transistor dissipation in Class B occurs at V_out = (2/π) V_CC, NOT at V_out = 0. Always derive: P_C(max) = V_CC²/(π² R_L). (Loss: 2 marks)
> 3. **Using the wrong η for Class A series-fed vs. transformer-coupled.** Series-fed = 25%; transformer-coupled = 50%. Students often mix these up. (Loss: 1–2 marks)
> 4. **Forgetting to mention crossover distortion** in any Class B answer. This is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction in KTU valuation if omitted.
> 5. **Wrong formula for the Figure of Merit.** It is P_out(max) / P_C(max), NOT η itself. (Loss: 1 mark)
> 6. **Not stating the condition for maximum efficiency** (V_out = V_CC, i.e., full voltage swing). Always specify the operating condition. (Loss: 1 mark)

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Memorize These Before Walking Into the Exam Hall:**

- [x] **Classification is based on conduction angle θ** (not on circuit topology alone).
- [x] **Class A** → θ = 360°, η_max = 25% (series) or 50% (transformer-coupled), FOM = 0.125 to 0.25.
- [x] **Class B** → θ = 180°, η_max = π/4 ≈ **78.6%**, I_DC = I_peak/π, FOM ≈ 0.4 (or π²/2 ≈ 4.93 in the alternative definition).
- [x] **Class AB** → 180° < θ < 360°, eliminates crossover distortion at small efficiency cost.
- [x] **Class C** → θ < 180°, requires **tuned LC load**, used in RF transmitters, η → 100% as θ → 0°.
- [x] **Class D** → switched-mode (PWM), η → 100% theoretical, dominant in modern portable audio.
- [x] **Master formulas to memorize verbatim:**
  - η = P_out / P_DC
  - P_out(max) for Class B = V_CC² / (2 R_L)
  - P_DC for Class B = 2 V_CC I_peak / π
  - I_DC for Class B = I_peak / π
  - P_C(max) for Class B = V_CC² / (π² R_L)
- [x] **Crossover distortion** is the Achilles' heel of Class B; Class AB is the practical cure.
- [x] **Transformer coupling** in Class A allows the collector voltage to swing up to 2 V_CC, doubling efficiency.
- [x] **Higher FOM = better transistor utilization = less heat sink mass = lower cost.**
- [x] Always state the **assumptions** (ideal transistor, sinusoidal input, no saturation) before starting any numerical derivation in the exam.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transformer Coupled Class A Power Amplifier
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET302 - ELECTRONIC DEVICES AND CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Transformer Coupled Class A Power Amplifier

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)

A **Transformer Coupled Class A Power Amplifier** is a large-signal Class A amplifier in which the loudspeaker (or any low-impedance load) is connected to the collector of the BJT through the primary winding of a step-down audio-frequency transformer. The transformer performs two simultaneous, indispensable functions:

1. **Impedance Matching** — It reflects the low speaker impedance ($R_L$, typically $4\ \Omega$–$8\ \Omega$) up to the much higher value ($R_L' = n^2 R_L$, typically a few hundred ohms) required by the transistor collector for efficient power transfer, where $n = N_1/N_2$ is the primary-to-secondary turns ratio.
2. **DC Blocking** — Because a transformer winding presents nearly **zero DC resistance** but a finite AC impedance, it allows the quiescent collector current to flow without dissipating DC power in the load, while still delivering the AC signal power to the speaker.

The transistor is biased such that its quiescent operating point $Q$ lies at the **midpoint of the AC load line** ($V_{CEQ} = V_{CC}$ and $I_{CQ} = V_{CC} / (n^2 R_L)$) so that the output voltage swing is maximum and symmetric.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — PCAET302 Module 4**
> This topic directly maps to **CO3** ("Design and analyze power amplifier circuits"). Mastery of the 50% maximum efficiency derivation is mandatory and carries high weightage in ESE questions.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Voltage Translator"

Imagine you are speaking into a small microphone, but the listener is a person standing 200 meters away. Your voice is too weak to reach them directly. So you shout into a **megaphone** (a step-up acoustic transformer) that projects your voice over the distance.

Now reverse the problem: a high-power water pump pushes water through a small nozzle — most of the pressure is wasted as turbulence because the hose is too narrow. If you connect a **wide-bore pipe** (low-impedance load) directly to a high-pressure narrow-bore pump, the pump's energy is reflected back and damages it.

A transformer is exactly this kind of **impedance translator** for electrical signals. The transistor is a "high-pressure, low-current" device; the speaker is a "low-pressure, high-current" device. The transformer matches these two worlds, allowing almost all the electrical energy to be transferred to the speaker as sound.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constant Used in This Topic**
> Maximum theoretical conversion efficiency of a **single-ended transformer-coupled Class A amplifier = 50%**. This figure is a direct consequence of the fact that the DC power drawn from the supply ($V_{CC} I_{CQ}$) is constant, while only the AC component delivers useful power, and the peak-to-mean ratio of a sinusoid is 2.

### Why Transformer Coupling? (The Real Motivation)

| Problem in Direct-Coupled Class A | How Transformer Solves It |
|---|---|
| Speaker ($4\ \Omega$) connected directly in collector dissipates huge DC power and severely limits swing. | Reflected load $R_L' = n^2 R_L$ is large (e.g., for $n = 10$, $R_L' = 400\ \Omega$). |
| DC voltage drop across speaker wastes supply. | Zero DC resistance in primary $\Rightarrow$ no DC drop, $V_{CEQ} = V_{CC}$. |
| Impedance mismatch causes poor power transfer. | Optimum matching gives maximum power theorem. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Circuit Topology and DC Analysis

The basic structure consists of:

- A BJT (commonly a power transistor such as 2N3055) with the **emitter grounded**.
- Base bias network ($R_1, R_2, R_E$) chosen to fix the Q-point.
- The **primary winding** ($N_1$ turns) of the audio transformer connected between the collector and $V_{CC}$.
- The **secondary winding** ($N_2$ turns) connected to the speaker load $R_L$.

Because the primary winding's DC resistance $r_{p1}$ is designed to be negligibly small (typically less than 1 $\Omega$), the **DC collector load is essentially a short circuit**. Therefore the DC load line is **vertical**, passing through $V_{CE} = V_{CC}$ for all values of $I_C$.

The Q-point coordinates are:
$$V_{CEQ} = V_{CC} - I_{CQ}\, r_{p1} \approx V_{CC}$$

$$I_{CQ} = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{BE}}{R_E}\ \text{(set by bias network)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Design Rule for Maximum Symmetric Swing**
> For the largest possible unclipped output, the Q-point must be placed so that the AC load line intersects the $V_{CE}$ axis at $2 V_{CC}$ and the $I_C$ axis at $2 I_{CQ}$. This requires $I_{CQ} = \dfrac{V_{CC}}{n^2 R_L}$.

### 2.2 AC Analysis — The Reflected Load Concept

When an AC signal $v_i$ is applied, the transformer reflects the secondary load $R_L$ into the primary as an equivalent AC resistance:

$$R_L' = n^2 R_L, \quad \text{where } n = \frac{N_1}{N_2}$$

This reflected load $R_L'$ is what the collector "sees" for AC signals. The AC load line therefore has slope $-1 / R_L'$ and passes through the Q-point $(V_{CC}, I_{CQ})$.

The instantaneous collector current and collector-emitter voltage become:

$$i_C(t) = I_{CQ} + I_m \sin(\omega t)$$

$$v_{CE}(t) = V_{CC} - I_m\, n^2 R_L \sin(\omega t)$$

The voltage appearing across the secondary (and hence across the speaker) is:
$$v_L(t) = \frac{I_m\, n^2 R_L}{n} \sin(\omega t) = n\, I_m R_L \sin(\omega t)$$

### 2.3 Power Calculations — The Three Powers

**(a) AC Power Delivered to the Load (Useful Output Power)**

For a sinusoidal output with peak current $I_m$ and peak secondary voltage $V_m = n I_m R_L$:

$$P_{ac} = P_{out} = \frac{V_m^2}{2 R_L} = \frac{I_m^2 R_L}{2} = \frac{V_m I_m}{2}$$

For **maximum** swing, $I_m = I_{CQ}$ and $V_m = V_{CC}$, so:

$$P_{ac(\max)} = \frac{V_{CC}\, I_{CQ}}{2} = \frac{V_{CC}^2}{2\, n^2 R_L}$$

**(b) DC Power Drawn from the Supply**

The supply $V_{CC}$ delivers a **constant** current $I_{CQ}$ (independent of signal), so:

$$P_{DC} = V_{CC} \cdot I_{CQ}$$

**(c) Power Dissipated in the Transistor (Wasted as Heat)**

By conservation of energy:

$$P_D = P_{DC} - P_{ac} = V_{CC} I_{CQ} - \frac{V_{CC} I_{CQ}}{2} = \frac{V_{CC} I_{CQ}}{2}$$

The transistor must be rated to dissipate this worst-case power safely (with a heatsink).

### 2.4 Conversion Efficiency

$$\eta = \frac{P_{ac}}{P_{DC}} = \frac{V_{CC} I_{CQ}/2}{V_{CC} I_{CQ}} = \frac{1}{2} = 50\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **50% is the THEORETICAL CEILING** — never exceeded in single-ended Class A, regardless of how cleverly you bias the transistor. Practical efficiencies are 30%–40% because of: (i) transformer losses (copper + core), (ii) $V_{CE(\text{sat})} \neq 0$, and (iii) saturation of the core.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Boundary / Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Turns ratio | $n = N_1 / N_2$ | dimensionless | $N_1 > N_2$ (step-down) |
| 2 | Reflected (AC) load | $R_L' = n^2 R_L$ | $\Omega$ | Seen at collector |
| 3 | Q-point voltage | $V_{CEQ} \approx V_{CC}$ | V | Ideal transformer |
| 4 | Q-point current (max swing) | $I_{CQ} = V_{CC} / (n^2 R_L)$ | A | Set by bias network |
| 5 | Max AC output power | $P_{ac(\max)} = V_{CC}^2 / (2\, n^2 R_L)$ | W | $V_m = V_{CC}$ |
| 6 | DC input power | $P_{DC} = V_{CC}\, I_{CQ}$ | W | Constant |
| 7 | Transistor dissipation | $P_D = V_{CC} I_{CQ} - P_{ac}$ | W | Worst case $\approx P_{ac(\max)}$ |
| 8 | Max efficiency (single-ended) | $\eta_{\max} = 50\%$ | — | Ideal, sinusoidal |
| 9 | Practical efficiency | $\eta \approx 30\%\text{–}40\%$ | — | Real transformer + losses |
| 10 | Required turns ratio | $n = \sqrt{R_L' / R_L}$ | — | For given $R_L'$ |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Transformer-coupled Class A amplifiers, despite their low efficiency, dominated audio power amplification from the 1930s to the 1960s because of:

- **Excellent impedance matching** to low-impedance speakers.
- **Galvanic isolation** between the DC supply and the speaker (protecting the speaker voice coil from DC offset).
- **Simple, single-transistor topology** — no complementary symmetry needed.
- **Low distortion** when operated well within the linear region.

They are still used today in niche applications: high-fidelity valve (tube) amplifiers, certain public-address (PA) systems, and high-power RF driver stages.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Reflected Load Theorem from First Principles

Consider an ideal transformer (coupling coefficient $k = 1$, no losses) with primary turns $N_1$, secondary turns $N_2$, and a load $R_L$ on the secondary.

The voltage and current transformation equations of an ideal transformer are:

$$v_1 = n\, v_2, \qquad i_1 = \frac{i_2}{n}, \qquad n = \frac{N_1}{N_2}$$

The instantaneous power must be conserved (ideal transformer, lossless):

$$p_1 = v_1 i_1 = (n v_2)\left(\frac{i_2}{n}\right) = v_2 i_2 = p_2$$

Now substitute the secondary Ohm's law $v_2 = i_2 R_L$ into the expression for $v_1$:

$$v_1 = n v_2 = n(i_2 R_L)$$

And from the current relation, $i_2 = n i_1$, therefore:

$$v_1 = n \cdot (n i_1) R_L = n^2 i_1 R_L$$

By Ohm's law on the primary side, the equivalent primary impedance is:

$$\boxed{R_L' = \frac{v_1}{i_1} = n^2 R_L}$$

> This is the foundational result: **a load $R_L$ on the secondary appears multiplied by $n^2$ when viewed from the primary**.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Maximum AC Output Power

Starting from the AC load line. Since the Q-point is at $(V_{CC}, I_{CQ})$ and the AC load line slope is $-1/R_L'$, the instantaneous collector voltage and current are:

$$v_{CE}(t) = V_{CC} + \Delta v_{CE}, \qquad i_C(t) = I_{CQ} + \Delta i_C$$

with $\Delta v_{CE} = -\Delta i_C \cdot R_L'$.

For a sinusoidal drive, let $\Delta i_C = I_m \sin(\omega t)$, then:

$$i_C(t) = I_{CQ} + I_m \sin(\omega t)$$

$$v_{CE}(t) = V_{CC} - I_m R_L' \sin(\omega t) = V_{CC} - I_m n^2 R_L \sin(\omega t)$$

The AC current delivered to the secondary is $i_L = n \cdot i_{\text{primary ac}} = n I_m \sin(\omega t)$, and the secondary voltage is $v_L = n^2 R_L \cdot I_m \sin(\omega t) / n = n R_L I_m \sin(\omega t)$.

The instantaneous power in the load:

$$p_L(t) = v_L(t) \cdot i_L(t) = (n R_L I_m \sin\omega t)(n I_m \sin\omega t) = n^2 R_L I_m^2 \sin^2 \omega t$$

Average over one full cycle (using $\langle \sin^2 \omega t \rangle = 1/2$):

$$P_{ac} = \frac{1}{T}\int_0^T p_L(t)\, dt = n^2 R_L I_m^2 \cdot \frac{1}{2} = \frac{n^2 R_L I_m^2}{2}$$

Since $n^2 R_L = R_L'$, this can also be written as $P_{ac} = I_m^2 R_L' / 2$. The **maximum** occurs when the swing is as large as possible, which means $I_m = I_{CQ}$ (the swing can go from 0 to $2 I_{CQ}$):

$$\boxed{P_{ac(\max)} = \frac{I_{CQ}^2 \cdot n^2 R_L}{2} = \frac{V_{CC} I_{CQ}}{2} = \frac{V_{CC}^2}{2\, n^2 R_L}}$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — DC Power and Efficiency

The supply voltage $V_{CC}$ is connected across the transformer primary (plus a tiny $r_{p1}$). The current drawn from the supply is the instantaneous collector current $i_C(t)$, which is **pulsating** (not constant) in reality, but for the DC (average) power calculation we use the **mean** value:

$$\langle i_C \rangle = \frac{1}{T}\int_0^T (I_{CQ} + I_m \sin\omega t)\, dt = I_{CQ} + 0 = I_{CQ}$$

So the average DC power drawn from the supply is:

$$P_{DC} = V_{CC} \cdot \langle i_C \rangle = V_{CC}\, I_{CQ}$$

The conversion efficiency (with $I_m = I_{CQ}$ for maximum swing) is:

$$\eta = \frac{P_{ac(\max)}}{P_{DC}} = \frac{V_{CC} I_{CQ}/2}{V_{CC} I_{CQ}} = \frac{1}{2}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{\max} = 50\%}$$

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Worst-Case Transistor Dissipation

The transistor dissipation is the difference between DC power supplied and AC power delivered to the load:

$$P_D = P_{DC} - P_{ac} = V_{CC} I_{CQ} - \frac{I_m^2\, n^2 R_L}{2}$$

To find the **worst case** (maximum $P_D$), differentiate with respect to $I_m$ and set to zero:

$$\frac{dP_D}{dI_m} = -I_m\, n^2 R_L = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad I_m = 0$$

This is a **minimum**, not a maximum. As $I_m$ increases from 0 to $I_{CQ}$, $P_D$ **decreases monotonically** from $V_{CC} I_{CQ}$ to $V_{CC} I_{CQ} / 2$. Therefore:

- **Maximum dissipation** occurs at $I_m = 0$ (no signal): $P_{D(\max)} = V_{CC} I_{CQ}$.
- **Minimum dissipation** occurs at $I_m = I_{CQ}$ (full signal): $P_{D(\min)} = V_{CC} I_{CQ} / 2$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Implication**: The transistor and heatsink must be rated for the **no-signal** condition, not the full-signal condition! This is counterintuitive but is a direct consequence of constant DC supply current. A Class A amplifier dissipates the most power when there is no music playing.

### 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Condition | $I_m$ | $P_{ac}$ | $P_{DC}$ | $P_D$ (Transistor) | $\eta$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| No signal | 0 | 0 | $V_{CC} I_{CQ}$ | $V_{CC} I_{CQ}$ | 0% |
| Half-swing | $I_{CQ}/2$ | $V_{CC} I_{CQ} / 8$ | $V_{CC} I_{CQ}$ | $7 V_{CC} I_{CQ} / 8$ | 12.5% |
| Full swing (max) | $I_{CQ}$ | $V_{CC} I_{CQ} / 2$ | $V_{CC} I_{CQ}$ | $V_{CC} I_{CQ} / 2$ | 50% |

### 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A transformer-coupled Class A amplifier uses a $V_{CC} = 20$ V supply and a step-down transformer with $N_1 : N_2 = 10 : 1$ to drive an $8\ \Omega$ speaker. The Q-point is set at the midpoint of the AC load line. Calculate (a) the reflected load, (b) the Q-point current, (c) the maximum AC power output, (d) the DC power drawn, and (e) the efficiency.

**Solution:**

(a) Reflected load:
$$R_L' = n^2 R_L = 10^2 \times 8 = 800\ \Omega$$

(b) Q-point current (for max swing):
$$I_{CQ} = \frac{V_{CC}}{R_L'} = \frac{20}{800} = 25\ \text{mA}$$

(c) Maximum AC power:
$$P_{ac(\max)} = \frac{V_{CC}^2}{2 R_L'} = \frac{20^2}{2 \times 800} = \frac{400}{1600} = 0.25\ \text{W} = 250\ \text{mW}$$

(d) DC power drawn:
$$P_{DC} = V_{CC}\, I_{CQ} = 20 \times 25\ \text{mA} = 500\ \text{mW}$$

(e) Efficiency:
$$\eta = \frac{250\ \text{mW}}{500\ \text{mW}} = 50\%$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Circuit Topology — Mermaid Block Diagram

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional signal flow** of a transformer-coupled Class A power amplifier. This avoids the limitations of ASCII circuit art while remaining fully compilable in standard Markdown renderers.

```mermaid
graph LR
    DC["DC Supply VCC"]:::supply
    BIAS["Base Bias Network R1 R2 RE"]:::bias
    BJT["Power BJT 2N3055"]:::transistor
    T1["Transformer Primary N1 turns"]:::xfmr
    T2["Transformer Secondary N2 turns"]:::xfmr
    SPK["Speaker Load RL 8 ohm"]:::load
    IN["AC Input Signal vi"]:::input
    OUT["Acoustic Output"]:::output

    DC ---|VCC rail| BJT
    DC ---|VCC rail| T1
    IN --> BIAS
    BIAS -->|VBE bias| BJT
    BJT -->|Collector current ic| T1
    T1 <-->|Magnetic coupling| T2
    T2 --> SPK
    SPK --> OUT

    classDef supply fill:#ffe5b4,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
    classDef bias fill:#d4f0ff,stroke:#0066aa,color:#000
    classDef transistor fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000
    classDef xfmr fill:#e0d4ff,stroke:#3300aa,color:#000
    classDef load fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600,color:#000
    classDef input fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#aa8800,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#ffd9f2,stroke:#aa0066,color:#000
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The DC supply feeds both the collector (via the transformer primary) and the bias network.
- The AC input modulates the base-emitter junction, which modulates the collector current.
- The collector current (pulsating DC) flows through the transformer primary, inducing an AC voltage in the secondary.
- The DC component is blocked by the transformer; only the AC component appears across the speaker.

### 4.2 Load Line Geometry — Mermaid Visual

This diagram illustrates the relationship between the **DC load line** (vertical) and the **AC load line** (sloped) on the $V_{CE}$–$I_C$ plane, with the Q-point marked at the centre.

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph LOADLINE["Collector Characteristic VCE vs IC"]
        P1["Point A: 2VCC on VCE axis at IC=0"]:::corner
        P2["Point B: 2ICQ on IC axis at VCE=0"]:::corner
        Q["Q point: VCC, ICQ at centre"]:::qpoint
        DCL["DC Load Line vertical through VCC"]:::dcline
        ACL["AC Load Line slope minus 1 over n squared RL"]:::acline
        P1 --- P2
        Q --- P1
        Q --- P2
    end

    classDef corner fill:#ffffff,stroke:#000000,color:#000
    classDef qpoint fill:#ffeb3b,stroke:#000000,color:#000
    classDef dcline fill:#0000ff,stroke:#0000ff,color:#fff
    classDef acline fill:#ff0000,stroke:#ff0000,color:#fff
```

### 4.3 Power Flow / Energy Budget Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    P_DC["PDC from VCC supply constant"]:::dc
    P_AC["Pac delivered to speaker"]:::ac
    P_D["PD dissipated in transistor heat"]:::heat
    EFF["Efficiency eta equals Pac over PDC"]:::eff
    THERMAL["Heatsink thermal path"]:::thermal

    P_DC -->|Portion Pac| P_AC
    P_DC -->|Portion PD| P_D
    P_DC --> EFF
    P_AC --> EFF
    P_D --> THERMAL

    classDef dc fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef ac fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600,color:#000
    classDef heat fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000,color:#000
    classDef eff fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#aa8800,color:#000
    classDef thermal fill:#e0d4ff,stroke:#330099,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> The energy flow above makes the **fundamental inefficiency of Class A** immediately obvious: even when the speaker is silent, the full $P_{DC} = V_{CC} I_{CQ}$ is being drawn from the supply and is converted to heat in the transistor. There is no "off" state.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**
*Why is a transformer used as a coupling device in a Class A power amplifier? Mention any two advantages.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A transformer is used to couple the load to the collector of a Class A power amplifier for the following reasons:

1. **Impedance matching**: A speaker typically has a very low impedance ($4\ \Omega$–$8\ \Omega$), while the collector of a transistor requires a much higher load (a few hundred ohms) for efficient power transfer. The transformer reflects the low load as $R_L' = n^2 R_L$, enabling optimum matching and maximum power transfer.

2. **DC blocking / Isolation**: The transformer primary has near-zero DC resistance, so the DC quiescent current flows without dissipating DC power in the load. The DC component is blocked from the speaker, protecting it from DC offset, while the AC component is delivered efficiently.

3. (Bonus) It also provides galvanic isolation and allows the Q-point voltage $V_{CEQ} = V_{CC}$ (no DC drop), maximizing the available swing.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question]**
*State the maximum theoretical efficiency of a transformer-coupled Class A power amplifier and explain why the practical efficiency is lower.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The maximum theoretical efficiency of a single-ended transformer-coupled Class A power amplifier is **50%**.

Practical efficiency is lower (typically 30%–40%) due to:
- **Copper losses** ($I^2 R$) in the transformer windings.
- **Core losses** (hysteresis and eddy currents) in the iron core.
- **Transistor saturation voltage** $V_{CE(\text{sat})}$ being non-zero, which reduces the maximum swing below $V_{CC}$.
- **Bias network currents** drawing additional power from $V_{CC}$.
- **Signal not always reaching peak amplitude** (music has a low average-to-peak ratio).

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing $R_L$ and $R_L'$**: Many students use the speaker resistance $R_L$ in the AC power formula instead of the **reflected** load $R_L' = n^2 R_L$. This gives a wrong answer **4 times smaller** (or larger, depending on $n$) and costs 7 of 14 marks. Always verify which side of the transformer you are on.
> 2. **Forgetting the Q-point placement condition**: For maximum efficiency, the Q-point MUST be at $I_{CQ} = V_{CC}/R_L'$. If you pick an arbitrary $I_{CQ}$, the efficiency will be less than 50%. Show this in your working.
> 3. **Calculating average DC power wrong**: The DC current drawn is $I_{CQ}$ (the **average** of the pulsating collector current), NOT the peak $I_{CQ} + I_m$.
> 4. **Not drawing the AC and DC load lines**: KTU examiners award 2 marks specifically for the **load line diagram** showing the vertical DC load line and the sloped AC load line with the Q-point marked.

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Adapted]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a transformer-coupled Class A power amplifier. Draw the DC and AC load lines and mark the Q-point. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the maximum efficiency of a single-ended transformer-coupled Class A power amplifier. Show that the transistor dissipation is maximum under no-signal condition. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution for Q-A(a) — 7 marks

**Circuit description (3 marks):**
The circuit consists of a power BJT (e.g., 2N3055) with:
- Emitter grounded (or with a small unbypassed $R_E$ for thermal stability).
- Base biased by a voltage divider $R_1, R_2$ from $V_{CC}$.
- Collector connected to the **primary** of an audio-frequency step-down transformer.
- The **secondary** of the transformer drives the speaker load $R_L$.
- A coupling capacitor $C_{in}$ applies the input AC signal to the base.

**DC analysis (2 marks):**
- The transformer primary DC resistance is negligible, so the DC load is essentially a short.
- DC load line: **vertical**, defined by $V_{CE} = V_{CC}$.
- The Q-point is fixed on this vertical line at $I_{CQ} = (V_{CC} - V_{BE})/R_E$ (ignoring base current).

**AC analysis and load line (2 marks):**
- The load $R_L$ is reflected to the primary as $R_L' = n^2 R_L$.
- The AC load line passes through the Q-point with slope $-1/R_L'$.
- For maximum swing, Q is placed at the centre: $I_{CQ} = V_{CC}/R_L'$, so the AC load line cuts the $V_{CE}$ axis at $2 V_{CC}$ and the $I_C$ axis at $2 I_{CQ}$.

**Operation (bonus, for full 7 marks):**
On a positive half-cycle of input, base current increases, $i_C$ rises above $I_{CQ}$, and $v_{CE}$ falls below $V_{CC}$. The transformer secondary delivers a corresponding half-cycle to the speaker. The opposite happens on the negative half-cycle. The transformer blocks the DC and delivers only the AC component to the load.

##### Model Solution for Q-A(b) — 7 marks

**Derivation of $P_{ac}$, $P_{DC}$ (4 marks):**
- Let the AC collector current be $i_c(t) = I_{CQ} + I_m \sin \omega t$.
- The secondary load current and voltage are sinusoidal with peak values $n I_m$ and $n R_L I_m$ respectively.
- Average AC power: $P_{ac} = \dfrac{n^2 R_L I_m^2}{2} = \dfrac{I_m^2 R_L'}{2}$.
- For maximum swing, $I_m = I_{CQ}$, so $P_{ac(\max)} = \dfrac{V_{CC} I_{CQ}}{2}$.
- DC power: $P_{DC} = V_{CC} \cdot I_{CQ}$ (constant, since supply current is the average collector current).

**Efficiency derivation (2 marks):**

$$\eta = \frac{P_{ac(\max)}}{P_{DC}} = \frac{V_{CC} I_{CQ} / 2}{V_{CC} I_{CQ}} = \frac{1}{2} = 50\%$$

**Transistor dissipation maximum (1 mark):**
$$P_D = P_{DC} - P_{ac} = V_{CC} I_{CQ} - \frac{I_m^2 R_L'}{2}$$

Differentiating with respect to $I_m$ shows $P_D$ **decreases** as $I_m$ increases. Hence $P_{D(\max)}$ occurs at $I_m = 0$ (no signal), where $P_{D(\max)} = V_{CC} I_{CQ}$.

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of **reflected load** in a transformer-coupled amplifier. A Class A amplifier has $V_{CC} = 24$ V and drives an $8\ \Omega$ speaker through a transformer with turns ratio $N_1 : N_2 = 12 : 1$. The Q-point is set for maximum swing. Calculate (i) the reflected load, (ii) the Q-point current, (iii) the maximum AC output power, (iv) the DC power drawn, and (v) the conversion efficiency. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare Class A, Class B, and Class C power amplifiers in terms of (i) conduction angle, (ii) maximum theoretical efficiency, (iii) position of Q-point on the load line, and (iv) typical application. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution for Q-B(a) — 7 marks

**Concept of reflected load (2 marks):**
A load $R_L$ connected across the secondary of an ideal transformer appears at the primary as $R_L' = n^2 R_L$, where $n = N_1/N_2$. This is derived from the power-conservation equations $v_1 = n v_2$ and $i_1 = i_2 / n$, which give $R_L' = v_1/i_1 = n^2 (v_2/i_2) = n^2 R_L$. It enables impedance matching between the high-impedance collector circuit and the low-impedance speaker.

**Calculations (5 marks):**
Given: $V_{CC} = 24$ V, $R_L = 8\ \Omega$, $n = 12/1 = 12$.

(i) Reflected load:
$$R_L' = n^2 R_L = 12^2 \times 8 = 144 \times 8 = 1152\ \Omega$$

(ii) Q-point current (for max swing):
$$I_{CQ} = \frac{V_{CC}}{R_L'} = \frac{24}{1152} = 20.83\ \text{mA}$$

(iii) Maximum AC output power:
$$P_{ac(\max)} = \frac{V_{CC}^2}{2 R_L'} = \frac{24^2}{2 \times 1152} = \frac{576}{2304} = 0.25\ \text{W} = 250\ \text{mW}$$

(iv) DC power drawn:
$$P_{DC} = V_{CC} \cdot I_{CQ} = 24 \times 20.83\ \text{mA} = 500\ \text{mW}$$

(v) Conversion efficiency:
$$\eta = \frac{P_{ac(\max)}}{P_{DC}} = \frac{250}{500} = 50\%$$

##### Model Solution for Q-B(b) — 7 marks

| Parameter | Class A | Class B | Class C |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) Conduction angle | $360°$ (full cycle) | $180°$ (half cycle) | $< 180°$ (less than half) |
| (ii) Max theoretical $\eta$ | 50% (single-ended) / 78.5% (push-pull with transformer) | 78.5% | $\approx 90\%$ (approaches 100% as conduction angle $\to 0$) |
| (iii) Q-point position | Centre of load line | At cutoff ($I_{CQ} = 0$) | Below cutoff (reverse bias on BE) |
| (iv) Typical application | High-fidelity audio pre-amps, small-signal stages | Audio output stages (push-pull), battery-operated devices | RF power amplifiers, IF stages, oscillators |

(1 mark for the table, 1 mark each row of explanation, plus 1 mark for stating the formulas for Class B efficiency $\eta = \pi/4 = 78.5\%$ and Class C $\eta = (\theta - \sin\theta)/[2(\sin(\theta/2) - (\theta/2)\cos(\theta/2))]$ where $\theta$ is the conduction angle.)

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Transformer-Coupled Class A**

- [ ] **Transformer serves two functions**: (1) impedance matching via $R_L' = n^2 R_L$, (2) DC blocking.
- [ ] **DC load line is vertical** at $V_{CE} = V_{CC}$ because the primary winding has $\approx 0$ DC resistance.
- [ ] **AC load line passes through Q-point with slope $-1/R_L'$**.
- [ ] **Q-point placement for maximum swing**: $I_{CQ} = V_{CC}/R_L'$ and $V_{CEQ} = V_{CC}$, so the load line cuts axes at $2V_{CC}$ and $2I_{CQ}$.
- [ ] **Maximum AC power formula**: $P_{ac(\max)} = V_{CC} I_{CQ}/2 = V_{CC}^2/(2 R_L')$.
- [ ] **DC power formula**: $P_{DC} = V_{CC} I_{CQ}$ (constant, independent of signal).
- [ ] **Maximum theoretical efficiency** = **50%** (single-ended). This is the **upper bound**.
- [ ] **Practical efficiency** = **30%–40%** due to copper loss, core loss, $V_{CE(\text{sat})}$, and bias losses.
- [ ] **Worst-case transistor dissipation occurs at ZERO signal** ($I_m = 0$), not at full signal. Heatsink must be sized for no-signal condition.
- [ ] **Power balance**: $P_{DC} = P_{ac} + P_D$ at all times.
- [ ] **Push-pull transformer-coupled Class A can theoretically reach 78.5%** (this is a common viva/quiz trap — Class A can exceed 50% only with push-pull).
- [ ] **Turns ratio formula**: $n = \sqrt{R_L'/R_L}$ — must always be greater than 1 for a low-impedance speaker.
- [ ] **Common exam trap**: Don't confuse the **reflected** load $R_L'$ (used in collector-side equations) with the **actual speaker** $R_L$ (used only on the secondary side).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Push Pull Class B and Class AB Power Amplifiers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET302 - ELECTRONIC DEVICES AND CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Push Pull Class B and Class AB Power Amplifiers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Class B Push-Pull Amplifier:** A power amplifier configuration in which two complementary (NPN + PNP) or matched (NPN + NPN with driver) transistors are biased such that each device conducts for exactly **180°** of the input signal cycle. The two transistors operate in alternating half-cycles, and their outputs are combined at a common load to reconstruct the full waveform.

> [!NOTE]
> **Class AB Push-Pull Amplifier:** A modified Class B topology where each transistor is biased slightly ON (with a small quiescent current $I_{CQ}$) so that the conduction angle lies between **180° and 360°**, typically **200°–220°**. This eliminates the cross-over distortion inherent to pure Class B operation while preserving most of its high efficiency.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Push-Pull Architecture:** The term "push-pull" describes the mechanism by which one transistor "pushes" (sources) current into the load during the positive half-cycle while the other "pulls" (sinks) current during the negative half-cycle. The two halves of the waveform are reconstructed at the common load, doubling the power handling capability versus a single-ended (Class A) stage.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine a **two-person rowing team** in a long boat (the load). One rower on the left side only pulls the oar when the boat needs to move forward (positive half of the signal), and the other on the right only pulls when the boat needs to move backward (negative half). Neither rower wastes energy pulling on a stroke that isn't needed — this is **Class B efficiency**.

However, there's a problem: at the exact moment when one rower stops and the other starts, there's a tiny "dead zone" where neither is pulling — the boat momentarily loses momentum. This is **cross-over distortion**.

The fix? Have both rowers lightly hold their oars in the water all the time, so when one stops, the other is *already* moving (no dead zone). This is **Class AB operation** — a tiny bias keeps both transistors slightly active, eliminating the dead zone while still being far more efficient than both pulling constantly (Class A).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Performance Metrics (Standard for KTU):**
> - **Maximum Theoretical Efficiency of Class B:** $\eta_{max} = 78.5\% = \pi/4$
> - **Maximum Theoretical Efficiency of Class AB:** Slightly less than Class B (≈ 70–78% depending on bias)
> - **Conduction Angle:** Class B = 180° | Class AB = 200°–220° | Class A = 360°
> - **Power Dissipation:** $P_D = P_{DC} - P_{AC}$ (difference between input DC power and AC signal power delivered to load)

### 1.3 Why Push-Pull is Necessary in Power Amplifiers

In a single-ended Class A amplifier, the transistor conducts for the full 360°, leading to enormous quiescent (standby) power dissipation and a maximum theoretical efficiency of only **25%** (for transformer-coupled) or **50%** (for direct-coupled/ideal). Push-pull configurations solve this by:
1. Halving the conduction period per device.
2. Eliminating the even harmonics in the output (complementary symmetry cancels them).
3. Doubling the effective voltage swing at the load.
4. Achieving up to **5π/24 ≈ 65.5%** maximum efficiency (transformer-coupled) or **78.5%** (ideal/transformerless) — a massive improvement.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Input Signal Splitting:** The input AC signal is applied simultaneously to the bases of two transistors — one NPN (Q1, upper) and one PNP (Q2, lower) in the complementary configuration.
2. **Biasing Condition:**
   - **Class B:** Both transistors are biased at cutoff ($V_{BE} = 0$ V). They only turn ON when $\vert v_{in} \vert \geq V_{BE(on)} \approx 0.7$ V.
   - **Class AB:** Both transistors are biased slightly above cutoff using diodes or a $V_{BE}$ multiplier. A small quiescent current $I_{CQ}$ flows through both devices.
3. **Conduction Cycle:**
   - **Positive Half-Cycle ($v_{in} > 0$):** Q1 (NPN) turns ON; Q2 (PNP) is OFF. Current flows from $+V_{CC}$ through Q1 into the load $R_L$ to ground.
   - **Negative Half-Cycle ($v_{in} < 0$):** Q2 (PNP) turns ON; Q1 (NPN) is OFF. Current flows from ground through $R_L$ and Q2 to $-V_{EE}$.
4. **Output Reconstruction:** The two half-cycles are seamlessly combined at the load to reproduce the full sine wave.
5. **Cross-over Distortion (Class B only):** During the small interval when $\vert v_{in} \vert < 0.7$ V, neither transistor conducts, causing a "notch" at the zero-crossing of the output.
6. **Class AB Fix:** The bias network (typically two diodes or a $V_{BE}$ multiplier) forward-biases both bases by ~1.2–1.4 V, so the transition between transistors is smooth.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are EXAM-CRITICAL for KTU ESE Module 4.**

| **Parameter** | **Class B Formula** | **Class AB Formula** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Conduction Angle per device | $\theta_c = 180°$ | $\theta_c = 200°\text{ to }220°$ | degrees |
| Maximum Output Power | $P_{o(max)} = \dfrac{V_{CC}^2}{2 R_L}$ | $P_{o(max)} = \dfrac{V_{CC}^2}{2 R_L}$ | Watts (W) |
| DC Input Power (ideal) | $P_{DC} = \dfrac{2 V_{CC}}{\pi R_L} \cdot V_{CC} = \dfrac{2 V_{CC}^2}{\pi R_L}$ | $P_{DC} = \dfrac{2 V_{CC}}{\pi R_L} \cdot V_{CC} = \dfrac{2 V_{CC}^2}{\pi R_L}$ | W |
| Power Dissipation per transistor | $P_{D(avg)} = \dfrac{V_{CC}^2}{\pi^2 R_L}$ | $P_{D(avg)} \approx \dfrac{V_{CC}^2}{\pi^2 R_L}$ (approx) | W |
| Maximum Efficiency (ideal) | $\eta_{max} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} = 78.5\%$ | $\eta_{max} \approx 70\text{–}78\%$ | % |
| Power Dissipation Maximum (at $V_p = \dfrac{2 V_{CC}}{\pi}$) | $P_{D(max)} = \dfrac{V_{CC}^2}{\pi^2 R_L}$ | $P_{D(max)} = \dfrac{V_{CC}^2}{\pi^2 R_L}$ | W |
| Quiescent Current | $I_{CQ} = 0$ (Class B) | $I_{CQ} > 0$ (small, ~mA) | A |
| Cross-over Distortion | **Present** (notch at zero crossing) | **Eliminated** (smooth transition) | — |
| Load Voltage Swing | $v_o = V_p \sin(\omega t)$, $V_p \leq V_{CC}$ | $v_o = V_p \sin(\omega t)$, $V_p \leq V_{CC}$ | V |
| Load Current (RMS) | $I_{L(rms)} = \dfrac{V_p}{\sqrt{2} R_L}$ | $I_{L(rms)} = \dfrac{V_p}{\sqrt{2} R_L}$ | A |
| Output Power (general) | $P_o = \dfrac{V_p^2}{2 R_L}$ | $P_o = \dfrac{V_p^2}{2 R_L}$ | W |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions for Maximum Power Transfer:**
> - $V_p \leq V_{CC}$ (peak output voltage cannot exceed supply rail)
> - $P_{o(max)}$ occurs at $V_p = V_{CC}$ (full voltage swing)
> - $P_{D(max)}$ occurs at $V_p = \dfrac{2 V_{CC}}{\pi} \approx 0.636 \, V_{CC}$ (NOT at $V_p = V_{CC}$!)

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Audio Amplifiers:** Class AB is the industry standard for audio power amplifiers in home theaters, car stereos, and PA systems because it offers the best trade-off between low distortion and high efficiency.
- **RF Transmitters:** Class B push-pull is used in RF power stages where the high efficiency reduces thermal stress and extends battery life in portable transmitters.
- **Operational Amplifier Output Stages:** Virtually every op-amp (LM741, TL081, NE5532) uses a Class AB complementary output stage to drive low-impedance loads.
- **Motor Drivers:** H-bridge motor controllers use push-pull pairs (NPN + PNP or N-channel + P-channel MOSFETs) to drive DC motors in both directions.
- **Communication Systems:** High-frequency push-pull amplifiers are used in mixers and modulators due to their inherent suppression of even-order harmonics.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Efficiency of Class B Push-Pull Amplifier

**Step 1 — Assume sinusoidal output voltage across the load.**

$$
v_o(t) = V_p \sin(\omega t)
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the AC (output) power delivered to the load.**

The RMS value of the output voltage is $V_{rms} = \dfrac{V_p}{\sqrt{2}}$. Therefore:

$$
P_{o} = \frac{V_{rms}^2}{R_L} = \frac{V_p^2}{2 R_L}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the average DC input power drawn from the supply.**

In a Class B push-pull stage, each transistor conducts for half the cycle. The average current drawn from each supply ($+V_{CC}$ and $-V_{EE}$) is the average of a half-sine wave over the full period. For a half-sinusoid with peak $I_p = V_p / R_L$, the average value is:

$$
I_{DC} = \frac{1}{\pi} \cdot I_p = \frac{V_p}{\pi R_L}
$$

Since current is drawn from both $+V_{CC}$ and $-V_{EE}$, the total DC power input is:

$$
P_{DC} = V_{CC} \cdot I_{DC} + V_{EE} \cdot I_{DC} = 2 \cdot \frac{V_{CC} \cdot V_p}{\pi R_L}
$$

For a symmetric supply ($V_{CC} = V_{EE} = V_{CC}$):

$$
P_{DC} = \frac{2 V_{CC} V_p}{\pi R_L}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the conversion efficiency.**

$$
\eta = \frac{P_o}{P_{DC}} = \frac{V_p^2 / (2 R_L)}{2 V_{CC} V_p / (\pi R_L)}
$$

**Step 5 — Simplify the expression.**

Cancel $R_L$ from numerator and denominator, then simplify:

$$
\eta = \frac{V_p^2}{2 R_L} \cdot \frac{\pi R_L}{2 V_{CC} V_p} = \frac{\pi V_p}{4 V_{CC}}
$$

**Step 6 — Determine maximum efficiency by maximizing $V_p$.**

The maximum undistorted output swing is $V_p = V_{CC}$. Substituting:

$$
\eta_{max} = \frac{\pi V_{CC}}{4 V_{CC}} = \frac{\pi}{4} = 0.785 = 78.5\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result:** $\boxed{\eta_{max} = 78.5\%}$ — This is the canonical result KTU examiners expect.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Power Dissipation Maximum for Class B

**Step 1 — Express total power dissipation as the difference between DC input and AC output power.**

$$
P_D = P_{DC} - P_o = \frac{2 V_{CC} V_p}{\pi R_L} - \frac{V_p^2}{2 R_L}
$$

**Step 2 — To find the maximum, differentiate $P_D$ with respect to $V_p$ and set it to zero.**

$$
\frac{d P_D}{d V_p} = \frac{2 V_{CC}}{\pi R_L} - \frac{2 V_p}{2 R_L} = \frac{2 V_{CC}}{\pi R_L} - \frac{V_p}{R_L} = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the critical $V_p$ value.**

$$
\frac{V_p}{R_L} = \frac{2 V_{CC}}{\pi R_L} \implies V_p = \frac{2 V_{CC}}{\pi} \approx 0.636 \, V_{CC}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back into $P_D$ to find the maximum dissipation.**

$$
P_{D(max)} = \frac{2 V_{CC}}{\pi R_L} \cdot \frac{2 V_{CC}}{\pi} - \frac{1}{2 R_L} \left( \frac{2 V_{CC}}{\pi} \right)^2
$$

$$
P_{D(max)} = \frac{4 V_{CC}^2}{\pi^2 R_L} - \frac{2 V_{CC}^2}{\pi^2 R_L} = \frac{2 V_{CC}^2}{\pi^2 R_L}
$$

**Step 5 — Power dissipation per transistor (since two transistors share the load):**

$$
P_{D(max)\text{, per transistor}} = \frac{V_{CC}^2}{\pi^2 R_L}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Output Power at Maximum Dissipation Point

Substitute $V_p = \dfrac{2 V_{CC}}{\pi}$ into the output power equation:

$$
P_o = \frac{V_p^2}{2 R_L} = \frac{1}{2 R_L} \left( \frac{2 V_{CC}}{\pi} \right)^2 = \frac{2 V_{CC}^2}{\pi^2 R_L}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight for KTU:** At the point of maximum transistor dissipation, $P_o = P_{D(max)} = \dfrac{2 V_{CC}^2}{\pi^2 R_L}$. The conversion efficiency at this operating point is:

$$
\eta = \frac{P_o}{P_{DC}} = \frac{2 V_{CC}^2 / (\pi^2 R_L)}{2 V_{CC} \cdot (2 V_{CC} / \pi) / (\pi R_L)} = \frac{1}{2} = 50\%
$$

So when each transistor dissipates maximum power, the overall efficiency is exactly **50%**.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Class A vs Class B vs Class AB

| **Parameter** | **Class A** | **Class B (Push-Pull)** | **Class AB (Push-Pull)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Conduction Angle | $360°$ | $180°$ | $200°\text{–}220°$ |
| Quiescent Bias | $I_{CQ} \approx I_{peak}/2$ | $I_{CQ} = 0$ | $I_{CQ} \approx 5\text{–}10\%$ of $I_{peak}$ |
| Max Efficiency (ideal) | $25\%$ (transformer) / $50\%$ (series-fed) | $78.5\%$ | $70\text{–}78\%$ |
| Cross-over Distortion | None | **Severe** | Negligible (eliminated) |
| Harmonic Distortion (THD) | Low | High (without compensation) | Low |
| Power Dissipation (idle) | **Highest** (always ON) | **Lowest** (zero idle) | Low (small bias current) |
| Power Supply Rejection | Good | Moderate | Moderate |
| Thermal Stability | Critical (self-heating) | Excellent | Good (needs thermal tracking) |
| Typical Application | High-fidelity pre-amps | RF transmitters | Audio power amplifiers |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Complementary Symmetry Class B Push-Pull (Transformerless)

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeVin["Vin AC Signal Source"] --> nodeC1["Coupling Capacitor Cin"]
    nodeC1 --> nodeBaseN["Base of Q1 NPN Upper Transistor"]
    nodeC1 --> nodeBaseP["Base of Q2 PNP Lower Transistor"]
    nodeVCC["+VCC Supply Rail"] --> nodeColN["Collector of Q1 NPN"]
    nodeVEE["-VEE Supply Rail"] --> nodeColP["Collector of Q2 PNP"]
    nodeBaseN --> nodeQ1["Q1 NPN Conducts During Positive Half Cycle"]
    nodeBaseP --> nodeQ2["Q2 PNP Conducts During Negative Half Cycle"]
    nodeQ1 --> nodeEmitterN["Emitter of Q1 Joins At Output Node"]
    nodeQ2 --> nodeEmitterP["Emitter of Q2 Joins At Output Node"]
    nodeEmitterN --> nodeCout["Coupling Capacitor Cout"]
    nodeEmitterP --> nodeCout
    nodeCout --> nodeRL["Load Resistor RL to Ground"]
    nodeRL --> nodeVout["Vout Reconstructed Full Sine Wave"]
    nodeBias["Bias Network D1 D2 Provides 1.2 to 1.4 V For Class AB"] -.-> nodeBaseN
    nodeBias -.-> nodeBaseP
```

### 4.2 Class AB Bias Network Using Diode Pair and $V_{BE}$ Multiplier

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeVCC["+VCC Supply"] --> nodeR1["Resistor R1 from VCC"]
    nodeR1 --> nodeD1["Diode D1 Forward Biased 0.7 V Drop"]

    nodeD2 --> nodeR2["Resistor R2 to Negative Rail or Ground"]
```
