Eta approaches 100 percent] style E1 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000 style E2 fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,color:#000 style E3 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000 style E4 fill:#ffab91,stroke:#d84315,color:#000 style E5 fill:#ff8a65,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000 style E6 fill:#ff7043,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Define a Class C power amplifier. State its conduction angle and maximum theoretical efficiency.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **Class C power amplifier** is an amplifier in which the transistor's Q-point is biased **below cutoff**, so the device conducts for **less than 180°** (typically 60° to 120°) of the input cycle. - **Conduction angle ($2\theta$):** Less than **$180°$**. - **Maximum theoretical efficiency:** Approaches **100%** as the conduction angle approaches zero. *(Award 1 mark each for: definition, conduction angle value, and efficiency statement.)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** **Why is an LC tank circuit essential in a Class C power amplifier? How does it affect the output waveform?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The LC tank circuit is essential because the Class C transistor output current is in the form of **narrow pulses rich in harmonics**, not a sine wave. The tank circuit performs two functions: 1. **Flywheel action** — The LC tank, once excited by a current pulse, continues to oscillate at its resonant frequency $f_r = 1/(2\pi\sqrt{LC})$ through many cycles between pulses. 2. **Harmonic filtering** — The parallel LC tank presents **very high impedance at $f_r$** (preserving the fundamental) and **very low impedance at harmonic frequencies** (shorting them to ground). **Net effect:** The output voltage across the tank is a **clean, near-sinusoidal waveform** at the fundamental frequency, even though the collector current is pulsed. *(Award 1 mark for "why needed", 1 mark for flywheel, 1 mark for harmonic filtering.)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - **Do NOT** attempt detailed Class C derivations — the syllabus explicitly says **"No Analysis Required"**. Examiners will **deduct marks** for wasted effort on un-syllabus derivations. > - **Always** mention the **role of the LC tank** in any Class C answer. Skipping this is the **#1 reason students lose 2–3 marks**. > - **Always** state the conduction angle range (**< 180°**, typically 60°–120°) when asked about Class C. Writing only "below cutoff" is incomplete. > - **Avoid** confusing Class C with Class B. Class C needs reverse bias; Class B has zero bias. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **Q.A. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]** **(a)** With the help of a functional block diagram, explain the **operating principle of a Class C tuned power amplifier**. Clearly show how the input sine wave is converted to a pulsed collector current and then back to a sinusoidal output. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss in detail the **efficiency characteristics** of a Class C amplifier. Explain why its efficiency is higher than Class A and Class B, and state the theoretical maximum efficiency. Why does the efficiency increase as the conduction angle decreases? **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — Operating Principle (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Biasing arrangement (1 mark):** The base of the transistor is given a **negative DC bias voltage** $V_{BB}$ (for an NPN BJT) such that the Q-point lies **below the cutoff line** ($I_{BQ} < 0$ at $V_{BB}$). The transistor is in cutoff at quiescence. **Step 2 — Input signal action (1 mark):** A sinusoidal input $v_i(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$ is applied on top of the reverse bias. The transistor turns ON only when the instantaneous input voltage exceeds the reverse bias, i.e., when $v_i(t) > \vert V_{BB} \vert$. **Step 3 — Pulsed collector current (1 mark):** The collector current flows as a **train of narrow pulses** centered at the positive peaks of the input. The width of each pulse is determined by the conduction angle $2\theta$, where $\theta = \cos^{-1}(\vert V_{BB} \vert / V_m)$. **Step 4 — LC tank load (1.5 marks):** The collector is connected to a **parallel LC tank** tuned to the input frequency. The tank's resonant frequency is $f_r = 1/(2\pi\sqrt{LC})$. At resonance, the tank presents **maximum (purely resistive) impedance** $Z = L/(CR)$ (the dynamic resistance). **Step 5 — Flywheel action (1.5 marks):** Each current pulse delivers a quantum of energy to the tank, which then "rings" (oscillates) freely at $f_r$ for the rest of the cycle. This produces a **near-sinusoidal output voltage** $v_o(t) = V_{om} \sin(\omega t + \phi)$ across the tank. **Step 6 — Harmonic suppression (1 mark):** The tank's impedance falls off sharply at harmonic frequencies ($2f_r$, $3f_r$, …), so harmonics in the current pulse are shorted to ground, leaving only the fundamental in the output. --- **Part (b) — Efficiency Characteristics (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Master efficiency equation (1 mark):** $$\eta = \frac{P_{ac}}{P_{DC}} \times 100\% = \frac{V_{om}^{2}/(2R_L)}{V_{CC} \cdot I_{DC}} \times 100\%$$ **Step 2 — Class A benchmark (1 mark):** Class A conducts for the full $360°$, so $I_{DC}$ is the average of a full sinusoid → $\eta_{max,A} = 50\%$. **Step 3 — Class B benchmark (1 mark):** Class B conducts for $180°$, so $I_{DC}$ is roughly half. The well-known result is $\eta_{max,B} = \pi/4 \approx 78.5\%$. **Step 4 — Class C advantage (1.5 marks):** In Class C, conduction is **< 180°**, so $I_{DC}$ is even smaller than in Class B. As $2\theta$ shrinks, $I_{DC} \to 0$, and since $P_{DC} = V_{CC} \cdot I_{DC}$ also shrinks, the ratio $P_{ac}/P_{DC}$ grows. **Step 5 — Theoretical maximum (1 mark):** $$\lim_{\theta_c \to 0°} \eta = \frac{P_{ac}(\text{finite})}{0^+} = 100\%$$ **Step 6 — Why efficiency rises with smaller $\theta_c$ (1 mark):** Fewer conduction cycles per second → smaller average current → smaller $P_{DC}$ → larger $\eta$. (The AC power delivered to the tank does not collapse as fast as $P_{DC}$ does.) **Step 7 — Practical range (0.5 marks):** Real circuits operate at $\theta_c = 80°$ to $120°$, achieving $\eta = 60\%$ to $80\%$, balancing efficiency with usable power output. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **Q.B. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]** **(a)** Compare **Class A, Class B, and Class C** power amplifiers in terms of **conduction angle, Q-point location, maximum efficiency, and typical application**. Present your answer in a **comparative table** with at least four parameters. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the **distortion characteristics** of a Class C amplifier in detail. Why is the output heavily distorted without a tank circuit? How does the tank circuit suppress this distortion? What are the limitations of using Class C for audio applications? **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — Comparative Table (7 marks):** | Parameter | Class A | Class B | Class C | | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | | Conduction angle | $360°$ | $180°$ | **< 180°** (60°–120°) | | Q-point location | Center of load line | At cutoff axis ($I_C = 0$) | **Below cutoff** (reverse biased) | | Maximum efficiency | **50%** | **78.5%** ($\pi/4$) | **Approaches 100%** (theoretical) | | Practical efficiency | 25%–35% | 50%–70% | **60%–80%** | | Output distortion | Lowest (high fidelity) | Crossover distortion present | **Highest** (heavy harmonic content) | | Need for tank circuit | Not required | Optional (for RF) | **Mandatory** (to filter & reshape) | | Typical application | Hi-Fi audio, small-signal preamps | Audio push-pull, op-amp output | **RF transmitters, oscillators, multipliers** | | Power rating | Small/medium | Medium | **Large** (high-power RF) | *(Award ~0.5 mark per correctly filled cell, 1 mark extra for clear Q-point distinction and tank requirement note.)* --- **Part (b) — Distortion Characteristics (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Source of distortion in Class C (1 mark):** The transistor conducts for less than half the input cycle, so the collector current is a **series of narrow pulses** rather than a sinusoid. The Fourier expansion of such a pulse train contains a strong **DC component**, a strong **fundamental component**, and significant **2nd, 3rd, 4th, … harmonics**. **Step 2 — Why resistive load fails (1.5 marks):** If this pulsed current were passed through a plain resistor, the voltage across the resistor would also be a pulsed waveform — **not a sine wave**. The Total Harmonic Distortion (THD) would be extremely high (often > 50%), and the load (e.g., a speaker) would receive a buzz-like, distorted signal. **Step 3 — Role of the LC tank (1.5 marks):** The parallel LC tank is tuned to resonate at the **fundamental frequency** $f_r$. At resonance: - It presents a **very high impedance** $R_d = L/(CR)$ to the fundamental → maximum voltage developed. - It presents a **very low impedance** (ideally zero) to all harmonics → harmonics are shorted. This **bandpass filtering** action reconstructs a clean sinusoid from the pulse train. **Step 4 — Quality factor governs selectivity (1 mark):** The tank's quality factor $Q = \omega_0 L / R$ determines how sharply it filters. Higher $Q$ → narrower bandwidth → cleaner output but more sensitive to detuning. For very narrow conduction angles (high distortion), a higher-$Q$ tank is mandatory. **Step 5 — Why Class C fails for audio (1 mark):** Audio signals span **20 Hz to 20 kHz**, a bandwidth ratio of 1000:1. A single LC tank can only resonate at **one** frequency (or a very narrow band around it). A Class C amplifier would only faithfully amplify a single audio tone, completely failing for music or speech. Hence, **Class C is strictly narrowband**, suitable only for RF, oscillators, and frequency multipliers. **Step 6 — Practical applications of distortion "feature" (1 mark):** Ironically, the same harmonic richness that makes Class C unsuitable for audio makes it **ideal for frequency multipliers** (doublers, triplers) — by tuning the tank to the 2nd or 3rd harmonic instead of the fundamental, the harmonic becomes the desired output. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Class C Power Amplifier (No Analysis Required):** - ✅ **Definition:** Transistor biased **below cutoff**, conducts for **< 180°** of the input cycle. - ✅ **Conduction angle range:** Typically **60° to 120°** (always < 180°). - ✅ **Bias requirement:** **Reverse bias** at the base (NPN: negative $V_{BB}$). - ✅ **Maximum theoretical efficiency:** **100%** (as $\theta_c \to 0°$). - ✅ **Practical efficiency:** **60% to 80%**. - ✅ **Efficiency ranking:** $\eta_A (50\%) < \eta_B (78.5\%) < \eta_C (\to 100\%)$. - ✅ **Why high efficiency:** Transistor OFF most of the time → $I_{DC}$ is small → $P_{DC}$ is small → $\eta = P_{ac}/P_{DC}$ is large. - ✅ **Why efficiency increases with smaller $\theta_c$:** Smaller angle → smaller $I_{DC}$ → smaller $P_{DC}$ → larger $\eta$. - ✅ **Distortion level:** **Highest among all classes** due to pulsed collector current. - ✅ **LC tank is MANDATORY** — it is **not optional** for Class C (unlike Class A/B). - ✅ **Tank function:** (i) Flywheel action (sustains oscillation), (ii) Bandpass filter (removes harmonics). - ✅ **Tank resonant frequency:** $f_r = 1/(2\pi\sqrt{LC})$. - ✅ **Tank quality factor:** $Q = \omega_0 L / R = 1/(\omega_0 R C)$ — higher $Q$ = sharper filtering. - ✅ **Narrowband amplifier** — only useful for a single frequency (or harmonics of it). - ✅ **Not suitable for audio** — bandwidth too large for a single tank to cover. - ✅ **Primary applications:** **RF power amplifiers, IF amplifiers, oscillators (Clapp/Colpitts), frequency multipliers, radar transmitters**. - ✅ **KTU 2024 caveat:** **No detailed mathematical analysis is required** — focus on concepts, qualitative efficiency trends, and distortion mechanism. - ✅ **Key formula to remember:** $\eta = (P_{ac}/P_{DC}) \times 100\%$; $P_{DC} = V_{CC} \cdot I_{DC}$; $f_r = 1/(2\pi\sqrt{LC})$. - ✅ **Common exam trap:** Confusing Class B (no bias, $180°$ conduction, $\eta = 78.5\%$) with Class C (reverse bias, < 180° conduction, $\eta > 78.5\%$).

topics.title: Regulated Power Supplies - Load and Line Regulation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET302 - ELECTRONIC DEVICES AND CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Regulated Power Supplies - Load and Line Regulation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Regulated Power Supply (RPS)** is an electronic circuit that converts an unregulated alternating current (AC) input into a **constant, stable direct current (DC) output voltage**, irrespective of variations in the input line voltage, the load current demand, or environmental conditions such as temperature.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, regulation is quantified by two principal performance metrics:

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4)**
> - **Line Regulation (also called Source Regulation):** The ability of the power supply to maintain its output voltage constant when the **input (line/AC mains) voltage** varies.
> - **Load Regulation:** The ability of the power supply to maintain its output voltage constant when the **load current drawn** by the connected device varies.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine a **municipal water tap** connected to an overhead tank. The tank acts as a regulated supply.

- **Scenario 1 (Line Regulation Analogy):** During peak hours, the pressure in the main city pipeline (line voltage) drops sharply, and during off-peak hours it surges. Despite these input pressure changes, a well-designed overhead tank maintains a nearly **constant water level (output voltage)** to your home.
- **Scenario 2 (Load Regulation Analogy):** When you open one tap in your house, the flow is strong. When you open five taps simultaneously (increased load current), the flow weakens and the pressure drops. A regulated supply acts like a **pressure-compensating pump (voltage regulator)** that instantly increases its output to keep the pressure (voltage) at every tap constant.

> [!NOTE] **Why Regulation Matters in Engineering**
> Sensitive electronic components such as microcontrollers, Op-Amps, sensors, and communication ICs require tightly controlled DC voltages. Even a **5% drift** in supply voltage can cause logic errors, signal distortion, or permanent hardware damage.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Units

- **Line Regulation** is expressed in **Volts per Volt (V/V)** or as a **percentage (%)**.
- **Load Regulation** is expressed in **Volts per Ampere (V/A)** or as a **percentage (%)**.
- Output Voltage stability is measured against the **nominal output voltage $V_{O(nom)}$** in **Volts (V)**.
- Load Current is measured in **Amperes (A)** or **milliamperes (mA)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Block-Level Working of a DC Regulated Power Supply

The complete signal flow of a regulated DC power supply is:

1. **Transformer** — Steps down the 230 V AC mains to a lower AC voltage.
2. **Rectifier** — Converts AC to pulsating DC (half-wave, full-wave, or bridge).
3. **Filter** — Smooths the pulsating DC using a capacitor (C-filter, LC-filter, or π-filter).
4. **Regulator** — A Zener diode, transistor series-pass, or dedicated 3-terminal IC (78xx, LM317) that holds the output voltage constant.

> [!NOTE] The **regulator block** is the sole component that actively performs **load and line regulation**. The preceding stages merely condition the raw AC input.

## 2.2 Line Regulation — Operational Theory

**Line Regulation** quantifies how much the DC output voltage $V_O$ changes when the AC input (mains) voltage $V_{IN}$ changes, while the load is held constant.

**Operational Logic (step-by-step):**

- The transformer's secondary voltage $V_S$ is a fixed fraction of the primary voltage $V_P$. Hence, any fluctuation in $V_P$ (mains) directly passes to $V_S$.
- After rectification and filtering, an unregulated DC voltage $V_{DC}$ is produced, which is **directly proportional** to $V_S$.
- The regulator (e.g., Zener or series-pass transistor) senses the change at its input and dynamically adjusts its internal resistance (or pass element) so that $V_O$ remains **pinned to a reference voltage**.

> [!IMPORTANT] A perfect regulator has **zero line regulation**, meaning $V_O$ is **completely independent** of $V_{IN}$.

## 2.3 Load Regulation — Operational Theory

**Load Regulation** quantifies how much the DC output voltage $V_O$ changes when the load current $I_L$ is varied, while the input line voltage is held constant.

**Operational Logic (step-by-step):**

- When load resistance $R_L$ decreases, load current $I_L$ increases.
- Without regulation, the increased current would cause a greater voltage drop across the internal source resistance $R_S$ of the unregulated supply, dropping $V_O$ according to $V_O = V_{DC} - I_L R_S$.
- The regulator senses the drop in $V_O$ and reduces the voltage drop across its own pass element, restoring $V_O$ to its nominal value.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Mathematical Definition** | **Units** | **Ideal Value** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Line Regulation ($LR_{line}$) | $\dfrac{\Delta V_O}{\Delta V_{IN}}$ | V/V or % | $0$ |
| Line Regulation (Percentage) | $\dfrac{\Delta V_O}{V_{O(nom)} \cdot \Delta V_{IN}} \times 100\,\%$ | % / V | $0\,\%/\text{V}$ |
| Load Regulation ($LR_{load}$) | $\dfrac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{I_{FL}} \times 1000$ | mV/A or % | $0$ |
| Load Regulation (Percentage) | $\dfrac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{FL}} \times 100\,\%$ | % | $0\,\%$ |
| Output Impedance ($Z_{out}$) | $\dfrac{\Delta V_O}{\Delta I_L}\Big\vert_{\Delta V_{IN}=0}$ | $\Omega$ | $0\,\Omega$ |
| Source Effect | $\dfrac{\Delta V_O}{\Delta V_{IN}}\Big\vert_{I_L=\text{const}}$ | V/V | $0$ |

> [!NOTE] In the above table, $V_{NL}$ = No-load output voltage, $V_{FL}$ = Full-load output voltage, and $I_{FL}$ = Full-load current.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Telecommunications:** Base stations and routers require $< 1\,\%$ load regulation to prevent signal dropout.
- **Medical Equipment:** ECG, MRI, and patient monitors demand ultra-strict line regulation to avoid misdiagnosis due to voltage drift.
- **Battery Chargers:** Smartphones and EVs rely on load regulation to deliver constant current during the entire charging profile.
- **Aerospace & Defence:** Avionics and missile guidance systems are powered by supplies with regulation better than $0.01\,\%$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Line Regulation

**Statement:** Line Regulation is the ratio of the change in DC output voltage to the change in input line voltage at a constant load.

**Starting Point — Unregulated DC Output:**

The unregulated DC output of a filtered bridge rectifier feeding a capacitive filter under no-load condition is:

$$
V_{DC(unreg)} \approx 1.414 \times V_{S(rms)}
$$

where $V_{S(rms)}$ is the secondary RMS voltage of the transformer.

**Step 1 — Express the change in $V_{DC}$ due to a change in $V_{S}$:**

$$
\Delta V_{DC} = 1.414 \times \Delta V_{S}
$$

**Step 2 — Relate the secondary voltage change to the primary (line) voltage change:**

Since the transformer turns ratio is fixed, $V_{S} = (N_2 / N_1) \cdot V_{P} = k \cdot V_{IN}$.

$$
\Delta V_{S} = k \cdot \Delta V_{IN}
$$

**Step 3 — Relate $\Delta V_O$ to $\Delta V_{DC}$ through regulator gain:**

Let the regulator have a small-signal line-to-output gain of $A_{LR}$ (typically $\ll 1$). Then:

$$
\Delta V_O = A_{LR} \cdot \Delta V_{DC} = A_{LR} \cdot 1.414 \cdot k \cdot \Delta V_{IN}
$$

**Step 4 — Final Line Regulation Expression:**

$$
\boxed{\,LR_{line} = \dfrac{\Delta V_O}{\Delta V_{IN}} = 1.414 \cdot k \cdot A_{LR}\,}
$$

**Units check:** (V/V), since all terms are dimensionless ratios. **Confirmed.**

## 3.2 Derivation of Load Regulation

**Starting Point — Equivalent Circuit Model:**

Model the unregulated source as a Thevenin equivalent: an ideal voltage source $V_{DC}$ in series with an internal source resistance $R_S$. The regulator holds $V_O$ constant, but in practice:

$$
V_O = V_{DC} - I_L \cdot R_S
$$

**Step 1 — Differentiate with respect to $I_L$:**

$$
\dfrac{dV_O}{dI_L} = -R_S
$$

This represents the **output impedance** $Z_{out}$ of the supply (negative sign indicates $V_O$ drops as $I_L$ increases).

**Step 2 — Load Regulation from No-Load to Full-Load:**

When $I_L = 0$ (no load), $V_O = V_{DC} = V_{NL}$.

When $I_L = I_{FL}$ (full load), $V_O = V_{FL}$.

Subtracting the two conditions:

$$
V_{NL} - V_{FL} = I_{FL} \cdot R_S
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for Load Regulation:**

$$
\boxed{\,LR_{load} = \dfrac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{I_{FL}} = R_S \quad [\text{in V/A}]\,}
$$

**Step 4 — Express in percentage form:**

$$
LR_{load}(\%) = \dfrac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{FL}} \times 100\,\%
$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A regulated DC supply delivers $V_O = 12\,\text{V}$ nominal. When the input mains varies from $200\,\text{V}$ to $250\,\text{V}$, the output varies from $11.95\,\text{V}$ to $12.05\,\text{V}$. When the load changes from no-load to $500\,\text{mA}$, the output drops from $12.00\,\text{V}$ to $11.90\,\text{V}$. Calculate: (a) Line Regulation in %/V, (b) Load Regulation in mV/mA, (c) Load Regulation in %.

**Given Data:**
- $\Delta V_{IN} = 250 - 200 = 50\,\text{V}$
- $\Delta V_O = 12.05 - 11.95 = 0.10\,\text{V}$
- $V_{NL} = 12.00\,\text{V}$, $V_{FL} = 11.90\,\text{V}$
- $I_{FL} = 500\,\text{mA} = 0.5\,\text{A}$
- $V_{O(nom)} = 12\,\text{V}$

**Solution (a) — Line Regulation in %/V:**

$$
LR_{line} = \dfrac{\Delta V_O}{\Delta V_{IN}} = \dfrac{0.10}{50} = 0.002\,\text{V/V}
$$

Converting to %/V:

$$
LR_{line}(\%/\text{V}) = \dfrac{0.10}{50 \times 12} \times 100 = \dfrac{0.10}{600} \times 100 = 0.0167\,\%/\text{V}
$$

**Solution (b) — Load Regulation in mV/mA:**

$$
LR_{load} = \dfrac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{I_{FL}} = \dfrac{12.00 - 11.90}{500} = \dfrac{0.10}{500} = 2 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{V/mA} = 0.2\,\text{mV/mA}
$$

**Solution (c) — Load Regulation in %:**

$$
LR_{load}(\%) = \dfrac{12.00 - 11.90}{11.90} \times 100 = \dfrac{0.10}{11.90} \times 100 \approx 0.8403\,\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **Verification of Sign Convention**
> Both regulation values are **positive**, which is the convention. A negative value would indicate an over-compensating regulator, which is rare in linear designs.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of a Complete Regulated Power Supply

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M[AC Mains 230V] --> T[Step Down Transformer]
    T --> R[Bridge Rectifier]
    R --> F[Smoothing Capacitor Filter]
    F --> VR[Voltage Regulator Zener or IC 78xx]
    VR --> OUT[Regulated DC Output to Load RL]
    FB[Feedback Network] -.Sense.-> VR
    VR -.Adjusts Pass Element.-> OUT
    OUT -.Load Current IL.-> RL[Variable Load RL]
```

## 4.2 Regulation Characteristics — Line vs Load

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Regulated Power Supply] --> B[Line Regulation Test]
    A --> C[Load Regulation Test]
    B --> B1[Hold IL constant]
    B1 --> B2[Vary VIN from Vmin to Vmax]
    B2 --> B3[Measure Delta VO]
    B3 --> B4[Compute LRline = Delta VO / Delta VIN]
    C --> C1[Hold VIN constant]
    C1 --> C2[Vary IL from 0 to IFL]
    C2 --> C3[Measure VNL and VFL]
    C3 --> C4[Compute LRload = VNL minus VFL / IFL]
    B4 --> R[Result: Output VO stable]
    C4 --> R
```

## 4.3 Comparison of Regulation Performance

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Unregulated
        U1[Output VO drops as IL increases]
        U2[Output VO rises as VIN increases]
    end
    subgraph Regulated
        R1[VO constant as IL varies]
        R2[VO constant as VIN varies]
    end
    Unregulated --> Regulated
    R1 --> S[Improved Performance]
    R2 --> S
```

> [!NOTE] **Reading the Diagrams:** The first diagram shows the **signal path** from mains to load with a feedback loop. The second diagram shows the **measurement procedure** for both regulation tests. The third diagram shows the **qualitative benefit** of adding regulation.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] Define Line Regulation and Load Regulation. State their ideal values.**

**Model Answer:**

- **Line Regulation:** It is defined as the ratio of the change in DC output voltage $\Delta V_O$ to the change in input line voltage $\Delta V_{IN}$, with the load current held constant.

$$
LR_{line} = \dfrac{\Delta V_O}{\Delta V_{IN}} \Big\vert_{I_L = \text{constant}}
$$

- **Load Regulation:** It is defined as the change in output voltage $V_{NL} - V_{FL}$ when the load current varies from no-load to full-load, divided by the full-load current.

$$
LR_{load} = \dfrac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{I_{FL}}
$$

**Ideal values:** Both Line Regulation and Load Regulation should be **zero** for a perfect regulated power supply, meaning the output voltage is completely independent of input voltage and load current.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] Why is a Zener diode used in a regulated power supply? What is its role with respect to line and load regulation?**

**Model Answer:**

A **Zener diode** operates in the reverse-breakdown (Zener) region, where the voltage across it remains **nearly constant** ($V_Z$) over a wide range of current. It is used in shunt configuration across the load in a regulated power supply.

- **For Line Regulation:** When input voltage increases, the Zener draws more current, but the voltage across it remains pinned at $V_Z$, keeping the output voltage constant.
- **For Load Regulation:** When load current changes, the Zener current adjusts automatically to absorb the difference, ensuring the load voltage stays at $V_Z$.

> [!WARNING] **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write "Zener diode converts AC to DC" — this is **WRONG**. The Zener only **regulates**; the rectifier performs AC-to-DC conversion.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for **Line Regulation** of a regulated power supply. Explain the role of the regulator in achieving low line regulation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A DC regulated supply has $V_O = 15\,\text{V}$ nominal. When the input line varies from $180\,\text{V}$ to $240\,\text{V}$, the output changes by $0.15\,\text{V}$. The output drops from $15.0\,\text{V}$ (no load) to $14.7\,\text{V}$ at full load of $600\,\text{mA}$. Calculate: (i) Line Regulation in $\%/\text{V}$, (ii) Load Regulation in $\text{mV}/\text{mA}$ and $\%$. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q-A(a):

**Line Regulation Derivation:**

The unregulated DC output after rectification and filtering is:

$$
V_{DC} \approx 1.414 \cdot V_{S(rms)}
$$

Let the transformer turns ratio be $k = N_2/N_1$, so $V_{S(rms)} = k \cdot V_{IN}$.

When $V_{IN}$ changes by $\Delta V_{IN}$:

$$
\Delta V_{DC} = 1.414 \cdot k \cdot \Delta V_{IN}
$$

If the regulator has a small-signal line-rejection gain $A_{LR}$, the output change is:

$$
\Delta V_O = A_{LR} \cdot \Delta V_{DC} = 1.414 \cdot k \cdot A_{LR} \cdot \Delta V_{IN}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\,LR_{line} = \dfrac{\Delta V_O}{\Delta V_{IN}} = 1.414 \cdot k \cdot A_{LR}\,}
$$

**Role of the Regulator:** The regulator senses the input voltage change through its feedback loop and dynamically adjusts the impedance of its series-pass element so that the voltage drop across the pass element compensates exactly for the input change. In an ideal regulator, $A_{LR} \to 0$, giving $LR_{line} = 0$.

#### Model Solution to Q-A(b):

**Given:**
- $\Delta V_{IN} = 240 - 180 = 60\,\text{V}$
- $\Delta V_O = 0.15\,\text{V}$
- $V_{NL} = 15.0\,\text{V}$, $V_{FL} = 14.7\,\text{V}$
- $I_{FL} = 600\,\text{mA}$
- $V_{O(nom)} = 15\,\text{V}$

**(i) Line Regulation in %/V:**

$$
LR_{line} = \dfrac{\Delta V_O}{V_{O(nom)} \cdot \Delta V_{IN}} \times 100 = \dfrac{0.15}{15 \times 60} \times 100 = \dfrac{0.15}{900} \times 100 = 0.0167\,\%/\text{V}
$$

**(ii) Load Regulation in mV/mA:**

$$
LR_{load} = \dfrac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{I_{FL}} = \dfrac{15.0 - 14.7}{600} = \dfrac{0.3}{600} = 5 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{V/mA} = 0.5\,\text{mV/mA}
$$

**Load Regulation in %:**

$$
LR_{load}(\%) = \dfrac{15.0 - 14.7}{14.7} \times 100 = \dfrac{0.3}{14.7} \times 100 \approx 2.041\,\%
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2023]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the working of a **DC regulated power supply**. Discuss how the regulator stage provides both load and line regulation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a circuit diagram, explain the working of a **Zener diode shunt regulator**. Derive the expression for load regulation. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to Q-B(a):

**Block Diagram:** Transformer → Rectifier → Filter → Regulator → Load (as shown in Section 4.1 above).

**Working Explanation:**

1. **Transformer:** Steps down 230 V AC to a lower AC voltage suitable for the required DC level.
2. **Rectifier:** Converts the stepped-down AC to pulsating DC using diodes (half-wave / full-wave / bridge).
3. **Filter:** The capacitor charges to the peak voltage and supplies current to the load during the off-cycles, producing a smooth but unregulated DC.
4. **Regulator:** Holds the output at a fixed reference voltage using either a Zener shunt regulator or a 3-terminal IC series regulator.

**How the Regulator Provides Both Regulations:**

- **Line Regulation:** When $V_{IN}$ rises, the unregulated DC $V_{DC}$ also rises. The regulator's feedback circuit senses this and increases the voltage drop across its pass element, keeping $V_O$ constant.
- **Load Regulation:** When $I_L$ increases, the regulator reduces its own internal voltage drop to maintain $V_O$ at the same level. Conversely, when $I_L$ decreases, the regulator absorbs the excess.

#### Model Solution to Q-B(b):

**Zener Shunt Regulator Circuit:**

A Zener diode $D_Z$ is connected in **reverse-bias** across the load $R_L$, with a **series resistor $R_S$** connecting the unregulated DC source to the load.

**Working:**

- The Zener operates in the breakdown region, maintaining $V_O = V_Z$ (constant).
- **Line Regulation:** If $V_{IN}$ increases, the current through $R_S$ increases, but the Zener absorbs the extra current ($I_Z$ increases), keeping $V_O$ constant.
- **Load Regulation:** If $I_L$ decreases, $I_Z$ increases to maintain $KCL$ at the node ($I_S = I_Z + I_L$). If $I_L$ increases, $I_Z$ decreases. $V_O = V_Z$ remains constant as long as $I_Z$ stays within $I_{Z(min)}$ and $I_{Z(max)}$.

**Derivation of Load Regulation:**

Applying KVL around the input loop:

$$
V_{IN} = I_S \cdot R_S + V_O
$$

Applying KCL at the output node:

$$
I_S = I_Z + I_L
$$

Substituting:

$$
V_{IN} = (I_Z + I_L) R_S + V_O
$$

Since $V_O = V_Z$ is constant, taking the differential with respect to $I_L$:

$$
0 = R_S \cdot \Delta I_Z + R_S \cdot \Delta I_L + \Delta V_O
$$

Since $\Delta I_Z = -\Delta I_L$ (to maintain $I_S$ constant if $V_{IN}$ is fixed):

$$
\Delta V_O = -R_S \cdot \Delta I_Z - R_S \cdot \Delta I_L = -R_S \cdot \Delta I_L + R_S \cdot \Delta I_L = 0
$$

For an **ideal** Zener, this gives $LR_{load} = 0$. In a **practical** Zener with dynamic resistance $r_z$:

$$
\boxed{\,LR_{load} = \dfrac{\Delta V_O}{\Delta I_L} = r_z \parallel R_S\,}
$$

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Units Mistake:** Writing load regulation as $0.2$ without specifying $\text{mV/mA}$ or % — full credit loss.
> 2. **Sign Confusion:** A negative load regulation is almost never acceptable in linear regulators.
> 3. **Forgetting $V_{O(nom)}$:** When asked for percentage line regulation, students forget to divide by $V_{O(nom)}$.
> 4. **Confusing Line and Load Regulation:** A common KTU failure point — remember: **Line = input side, Load = output side**.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Line Regulation** measures $V_O$ stability against $V_{IN}$ changes; ideal = $0$ V/V.
- **Load Regulation** measures $V_O$ stability against $I_L$ changes; ideal = $0$ V/A.
- The **regulator block** is solely responsible for achieving both regulations.
- **Zener diode** operates in reverse breakdown to act as a **shunt voltage reference**.
- **Series-pass transistor regulators** and **3-terminal ICs (78xx, LM317)** provide better regulation than simple Zener shunts.
- Units: Line Regulation in **% per Volt** or **V/V**; Load Regulation in **%** or **mV/mA**.
- For a **Thevenin equivalent** unregulated source, load regulation equals the source resistance $R_S$.
- Higher **dynamic Zener impedance $r_z$** → worse load regulation.
- **Switched-Mode Power Supplies (SMPS)** offer regulation better than $0.1\,\%$ but are studied in higher semesters.
- In KTU 2024 Scheme, expect a 3-mark definition question and a 7-mark numerical problem on this topic in every exam cycle.
- Always include **units** and **sign conventions** in your final numerical answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Series Voltage Regulator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET302 - ELECTRONIC DEVICES AND CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Series Voltage Regulator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Series Voltage Regulator** is a type of linear voltage regulator in which the **pass element (control element) is connected in series** between the unregulated input DC source and the regulated output load. The pass element — typically a power BJT, Darlington pair, or MOSFET — acts as a *variable resistance* whose dynamic value is automatically adjusted by a negative feedback loop so that the output voltage remains constant despite variations in input voltage, load current, or temperature.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Expectation)**
> *"A voltage regulator in which the control element is in series with the load is called a series voltage regulator. The output voltage is sampled, compared with a reference, and the error is used to control the conduction of the series pass transistor."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Variable Dam" of Electronics

Imagine a **water tank** supplying a village. The water level (regulated output voltage) must remain steady even when:
- The inlet river flow (input voltage) surges during monsoons, **OR**
- Villagers (load) suddenly open many taps (increased load current).

A **series regulator** is like installing a **smart, automatically-adjusting dam** *in line with the pipe* carrying water to the village. A water-level sensor (sampling network) measures the tank level. If the level rises above the setpoint, the dam's gate closes slightly, choking the flow. If the level falls, the gate opens more. The dam itself is the **pass transistor** placed *in series* with the supply.

This is fundamentally different from a **shunt regulator**, where the "dam" would be placed *in parallel*, diverting excess current to ground.

### 1.3 Key Functional Roles of the Series Regulator

| Subsystem | Real-World Analogy | Electronic Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Pass Transistor** (Series Element) | Adjustable dam in the main pipe | Variable resistance in series with load |
| **Reference Voltage** ($V_{REF}$) | Setpoint marker on the tank | Zener diode or bandgap reference |
| **Sampling Network** (R1, R2) | Water level sensor | Voltage divider across the output |
| **Comparator / Error Amplifier** | Tank-level decision controller | Op-amp comparing $V_{REF}$ to sampled voltage |
| **Current Sense Resistor** ($R_S$) | Flow meter | Detects overload condition for protection |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Highlight (2024 Scheme)**
> Series regulators are the **backbone of IC voltage regulators** (LM7805, LM7812, LM317, LM7905). The student MUST understand that the pass transistor operates in the **active (linear) region**, NOT in saturation or cutoff, for normal regulation.

### 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics (Bolded as Required)

- **Line Regulation** $\left(\dfrac{\Delta V_O}{\Delta V_{IN}}\right)_{I_L = \text{const}}$ — measured in **V/V** or **mV/V**
- **Load Regulation** $\left(\dfrac{\Delta V_O}{\Delta I_L}\right)_{V_{IN} = \text{const}}$ — measured in **V/A** or **mV/mA**
- **Ripple Rejection Ratio (PSRR)** — measured in **dB**
- **Dropout Voltage** $(V_{IN} - V_O)_{min}$ — typically **2 V to 3 V** for BJT-based regulators
- **Efficiency** $\eta = \dfrac{V_O}{V_{IN}} \times 100\%$ — typically **40% to 65%**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Architecture — The Four Functional Blocks

The series voltage regulator, regardless of complexity, contains **four indispensable blocks**:

1. **Pass Element (Series Control Transistor)**
   - Placed in series between $V_{IN}$ and $V_O$.
   - Operates in the **active region** of its $I_C$–$V_{CE}$ characteristics.
   - For an NPN emitter follower, the output is taken from the **emitter**, so:
$$V_O = V_{IN} - V_{CE(\text{pass})}$$

2. **Reference Voltage Source**
   - Typically a **Zener diode** ($V_Z$) biased in reverse breakdown.
   - Modern ICs use **bandgap references** ($\approx$ **1.25 V**, temperature-compensated).

3. **Sampling (Feedback) Network**
   - A resistive voltage divider: $R_1$ (upper, connected to $V_O$) and $R_2$ (lower, connected to ground).
   - Samples a fraction $\beta = \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$ of the output.

4. **Error Amplifier (Comparator)**
   - Compares sampled voltage $V_S = \beta V_O$ with reference $V_{REF}$.
   - Generates an error signal: $\epsilon = V_{REF} - V_S$.
   - This error drives the base of the pass transistor, altering its $V_{CE}$ to correct $V_O$.

### 2.2 Negative Feedback Loop — The "Why" Behind Regulation

The student must internalize the **causal chain**:
- Suppose $V_{IN}$ **increases** $\Rightarrow V_O$ tries to rise $\Rightarrow V_S$ rises $\Rightarrow (V_{REF} - V_S)$ decreases $\Rightarrow$ base drive to pass transistor reduces $\Rightarrow V_{CE}$ of pass transistor **increases** $\Rightarrow V_O$ falls back to nominal.
- Suppose load current $I_L$ **increases** $\Rightarrow$ voltage drop across pass transistor increases $\Rightarrow V_O$ falls $\Rightarrow V_S$ falls $\Rightarrow (V_{REF} - V_S)$ increases $\Rightarrow$ base drive increases $\Rightarrow V_{CE}$ drops $\Rightarrow V_O$ rises back to nominal.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the pass transistor is NPN (emitter follower) and not PNP?**
> In an emitter follower, the output at the emitter *follows* the base voltage minus $V_{BE}$, naturally providing low output impedance. This is critical for a regulator, which must behave like an ideal voltage source (zero output impedance).

### 2.3 Output Voltage Derivation — General Case

For an ideal op-amp error amplifier with reference $V_{REF}$ at the non-inverting input and sampled voltage at the inverting input, the **virtual short principle** gives:

$$V_{REF} = V_S = V_O \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

Solving for $V_O$:

$$V_O = V_{REF} \left(1 + \frac{R_1}{R_2}\right)$$

This is the **cornerstone equation** of any feedback-based series regulator.

### 2.4 Current Limiting (Short-Circuit Protection)

A real series regulator without current limiting will self-destruct under a short circuit. The standard protection uses a **current-sense resistor** $R_S$ in series with the emitter of the pass transistor, plus a **second BJT** (Q2) acting as a current sensor.

- The voltage across $R_S$ is $V_{R_S} = I_L \cdot R_S$.
- When $V_{R_S}$ exceeds the cut-in voltage of Q2's base-emitter junction ($\approx$ **0.7 V**), Q2 turns ON and **steals base current** from the pass transistor.
- The maximum load current is therefore:

$$I_{L(\max)} = \frac{V_{BE2(\text{on})}}{R_S} = \frac{0.7\text{ V}}{R_S}$$

### 2.5 Foldback Current Limiting (Advanced)

To **reduce** the short-circuit current below the full-load current (protecting both regulator and load), a **foldback network** adds $R_3$ in series with Q2's base from the output node.

The foldback limit equation (commonly tested):

$$I_{SC} = \frac{V_{BE2(\text{on})} (R_2 + R_3) - V_O R_2}{R_S R_3}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Foldback vs. Constant Current Limiting** — In constant current limiting, $I_{SC} = I_{L(\max)}$. In foldback, $I_{SC} < I_{L(\max)}$; the operating point "folds back" along the load line.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this table — every equation below has appeared in KTU university examinations.**

| # | Parameter / Quantity | Governing Equation | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Basic output voltage (Zener + BJT) | $V_O = V_Z + V_{BE}$ | V; for simple shunt-feedback series regulator |
| 2 | Feedback-based output voltage | $V_O = V_{REF} \left(1 + \dfrac{R_1}{R_2}\right)$ | V; op-amp driven, KTU **most-asked** |
| 3 | Sample fraction (feedback factor) | $\beta = \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$ | dimensionless |
| 4 | Closed-loop output impedance | $Z_O = \dfrac{Z_{o(\text{open})}}{1 + A \beta}$ | $\Omega$; $A$ is open-loop gain |
| 5 | Line regulation | $LR = \dfrac{\Delta V_O}{\Delta V_{IN}}$ | V/V or mV/V |
| 6 | Load regulation | $LD = \dfrac{\Delta V_O}{\Delta I_L}$ | V/A or mV/mA |
| 7 | Constant current limit | $I_{L(\max)} = \dfrac{V_{BE2(\text{on})}}{R_S}$ | A; $V_{BE2(\text{on})} \approx 0.7$ V |
| 8 | Foldback short-circuit current | $I_{SC} = \dfrac{V_{BE(\text{on})}(R_2+R_3) - V_O R_2}{R_S R_3}$ | A; requires $R_3$ |
| 9 | Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{V_O}{V_{IN}} \times 100\%$ | %; BJT regulator |
| 10 | Dropout condition | $V_{CE(\text{pass})} \geq V_{CE(\text{sat})}$ | V; $V_{CE(\text{sat})} \approx 0.2$ V |
| 11 | LM317 adjustable regulator | $V_O = V_{REF}\left(1 + \dfrac{R_2}{R_1}\right) + I_{ADJ} R_2$ | V; $V_{REF} = 1.25$ V, $I_{ADJ} \approx 50\ \mu$A |
| 12 | Power dissipation in pass transistor | $P_D = (V_{IN} - V_O) I_L$ | W; critical for **heat sink** design |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **On-board DC regulation** in every microcontroller/microprocessor system (LM7805, LM7812).
- **Battery chargers** requiring constant voltage.
- **Bench power supplies** in laboratories.
- **Automotive electronics** (post-alternator regulation before DC-DC conversion).
- **Telecommunications base stations** where ripple rejection is paramount.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Output Voltage of Feedback Series Regulator

**Problem Statement:** Derive the expression for the regulated output voltage $V_O$ of a series regulator that uses an op-amp error amplifier, a reference voltage $V_{REF}$, and a feedback divider $R_1$ (top) and $R_2$ (bottom).

**Assumptions:**
- Op-amp is ideal: $A_{OL} \to \infty$, input bias current $\to 0$, $V^+ = V^-$ (virtual short).
- Feedback is negative.
- Pass transistor is an emitter follower with unity voltage gain from base to emitter.

**Derivation (Exhaustive Step-by-Step):**

**Step 1:** The voltage at the non-inverting input of the op-amp is fixed by the reference:

$$V^+ = V_{REF}$$

**Step 2:** The voltage at the inverting input is determined by the voltage divider across $V_O$ formed by $R_1$ and $R_2$:

$$V^- = V_O \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the **virtual short principle** (since the op-amp is in a negative feedback loop, the differential input must collapse to zero for finite output):

$$V^+ = V^-$$

**Step 4:** Substitute the expressions from Step 1 and Step 2:

$$V_{REF} = V_O \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

**Step 5:** Solve for $V_O$ by inverting the fraction:

$$V_O = V_{REF} \cdot \frac{R_1 + R_2}{R_2}$$

**Step 6:** Expand the fraction by splitting into a sum:

$$V_O = V_{REF} \cdot \left(\frac{R_1}{R_2} + \frac{R_2}{R_2}\right) = V_{REF} \cdot \left(\frac{R_1}{R_2} + 1\right)$$

**Step 7:** Final canonical form:

$$V_O = V_{REF} \left(1 + \frac{R_1}{R_2}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The output is always *larger* than the reference (for $R_1 > 0$). This is the principle of a **non-inverting amplifier** — the regulator "amplifies" the stable reference to a higher, equally stable output voltage.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Line Regulation in a Closed-Loop Series Regulator

**Problem Statement:** Derive the line regulation (sensitivity of $V_O$ to changes in $V_{IN}$) of a feedback series regulator, given that the open-loop line regulation (without feedback) is $\left(\dfrac{\partial V_O}{\partial V_{IN}}\right)_{OL}$.

**Derivation:**

**Step 1:** Express the total open-loop variation of the output as the sum of input-induced and feedback-induced components:

$$dV_O = \left(\frac{\partial V_O}{\partial V_{IN}}\right)_{OL} dV_{IN} + A \cdot dV_{REF} - A \beta \cdot dV_O$$

where $A$ is the op-amp open-loop gain and $\beta = \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$ is the feedback factor.

**Step 2:** Group the $dV_O$ terms on the left side:

$$dV_O + A\beta \cdot dV_O = \left(\frac{\partial V_O}{\partial V_{IN}}\right)_{OL} dV_{IN} + A \cdot dV_{REF}$$

**Step 3:** Factor $dV_O$:

$$dV_O (1 + A\beta) = \left(\frac{\partial V_O}{\partial V_{IN}}\right)_{OL} dV_{IN} + A \cdot dV_{REF}$$

**Step 4:** Solve for the closed-loop line regulation (with $V_{REF}$ held constant, $dV_{REF} = 0$):

$$\left(\frac{\partial V_O}{\partial V_{IN}}\right)_{CL} = \frac{1}{1 + A\beta} \left(\frac{\partial V_O}{\partial V_{IN}}\right)_{OL}$$

**Step 5:** Interpretation: The feedback loop reduces line regulation by the **loop gain** $A\beta$:

$$LR_{CL} = \frac{LR_{OL}}{1 + A\beta}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Numerical Example:** If $A = 10^5$ and $\beta = 0.1$, then $A\beta = 10^4$, and line regulation improves by a factor of **10,000**. This is why op-amp feedback regulators are vastly superior to simple Zener-based designs.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Output Impedance of Feedback Regulator

**Derivation:**

**Step 1:** Define open-loop output impedance $Z_{O(OL)}$ as the impedance seen looking into the emitter of the pass transistor (with feedback loop broken).

**Step 2:** With feedback closed, the output voltage tries to stay constant against load current changes. The closed-loop output impedance is reduced by $(1 + A\beta)$:

$$Z_{O(CL)} = \frac{Z_{O(OL)}}{1 + A\beta}$$

**Step 3:** This is the dual of the line regulation result. Both equations demonstrate the same underlying principle: **negative feedback improves regulation in proportion to loop gain**.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Numerical Design Problem (KTU Standard)

**Given:** A series regulator with op-amp error amplifier. Reference voltage $V_{REF} = 5.0$ V. Required output voltage $V_O = 15$ V. Available resistors: $R_1$ and $R_2$ are to be chosen with $R_2 = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$. Also determine the maximum load current if $R_S = 0.5\ \Omega$ and $V_{BE(\text{on})} = 0.7$ V.

**Step 1:** Apply the output voltage formula:

$$V_O = V_{REF}\left(1 + \frac{R_1}{R_2}\right)$$

**Step 2:** Substitute known values:

$$15 = 5\left(1 + \frac{R_1}{10{,}000}\right)$$

**Step 3:** Divide both sides by 5:

$$3 = 1 + \frac{R_1}{10{,}000}$$

**Step 4:** Subtract 1:

$$2 = \frac{R_1}{10{,}000}$$

**Step 5:** Solve for $R_1$:

$$R_1 = 20{,}000\ \Omega = 20\ \text{k}\Omega$$

**Step 6:** Maximum load current (constant current limiting):

$$I_{L(\max)} = \frac{V_{BE(\text{on})}}{R_S} = \frac{0.7\ \text{V}}{0.5\ \Omega} = 1.4\ \text{A}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Board Answer Format:** Always show the substitution step. Examiners reward students who explicitly substitute numerical values — skipping directly to the answer costs 1 mark per derivation.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Series vs. Shunt Regulator (Highest KTU Yield)

| Feature | Series Regulator | Shunt Regulator |
|---|---|---|
| **Pass element position** | In series with load | In parallel with load |
| **Current through pass element** | $I_L$ (load current) | $I_L + I_{shunt}$ (always higher) |
| **Efficiency** | Higher (40–65%) | Lower (10–30%) |
| **Short-circuit behaviour** | Requires explicit current limit | Naturally protected (current limited by series resistor) |
| **Ripple rejection** | Excellent with feedback | Moderate |
| **Load regulation** | Excellent | Poor |
| **Heat dissipation** | $(V_{IN} - V_O) I_L$ | $V_{IN} \cdot I_{shunt}$ |
| **Typical applications** | IC regulators (LM78xx) | Low-current references, op-amp rails |
| **Complexity** | Higher (needs feedback) | Lower (passive zener) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of a Series Voltage Regulator

> [!NOTE]
> The block diagram below is the **most frequently drawn figure** in KTU Module 4 examination answers. Memorize its signal flow direction.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcDC[Unregulated DC Input V_IN] --> passBlock{Series Pass Transistor Q1}
    passBlock --> outNode[Regulated Output V_O]
    outNode --> loadRL[Load R_L]
    outNode --> sampDiv[Sampling Network R1 R2]
    sampDiv --> sampTap[Sampled Voltage V_S]
    sampTap --> compInv[Error Amplifier Inverting Input]
    refSrc[Reference Voltage V_REF Zener] --> compNon[Error Amplifier Non Inverting Input]
    compInv --> errAmp[Error Amplifier Op-Amp]
    compNon --> errAmp
    errAmp --> ctrlSig[Error Signal Epsilon]
    ctrlSig --> passBlock
    outNode --> curSense[Current Sense Resistor R_S]
    curSense --> protQ[Protection BJT Q2]
    protQ --> ctrlSig
```

**Reading the diagram:** The unregulated DC enters Q1 (the pass element). The output is sampled by the divider, compared with the reference, and the resulting error signal feeds back to Q1's base, completing the negative feedback loop. The protection branch (Q2, $R_S$) shunts base drive away from Q1 when current exceeds the safe limit.

### 4.2 Detailed Circuit Schematic (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph inputStage["INPUT STAGE"]
        V1[Unregulated DC Source V_IN] --> Rseries[Series Pass Transistor Q1 NPN]
    end
    subgraph referenceStage["REFERENCE STAGE"]
        Vz[Zener Diode D_Z] --> Rbias[Bias Resistor R_B from V_IN]
        Vz --> VrefNode[Reference Voltage V_REF Node]
    end
    subgraph feedbackStage["FEEDBACK STAGE"]
        Ro1[Output V_O] --> Rsense1[Current Sense Resistor R_S]
        Rsense1 --> R1[Feedback Resistor R1 Upper]
        R1 --> R2[Feedback Resistor R2 Lower]
        R2 --> Gnd1[Ground]
        R1 --> Vtap[Tap Point V_minus]
    end
    subgraph errorStage["ERROR AMPLIFIER STAGE"]
        VrefNode --> Vplus[Op-Amp Non Inverting Input V_plus]
        Vtap --> Vminus[Op-Amp Inverting Input V_minus]
        Vplus --> OpAmp[Operational Amplifier]
        Vminus --> OpAmp
        OpAmp --> ErrOut[Error Output Epsilon to Q1 Base]
    end
    subgraph protectionStage["PROTECTION STAGE"]
        Rsense1 --> Q2prot[Protection BJT Q2 Base]
        Q2prot --> Steal[Steals Base Current from Q1]
    end
    Rseries --> Ro1
    ErrOut --> Rseries
```

**Explanation of signal flow:**
- The pass transistor Q1 sits in series between $V_{IN}$ and $V_O$ (top rail).
- The Zener diode sets a fixed $V_{REF}$ at the op-amp's non-inverting input.
- $R_1$ and $R_2$ form a divider, sending a fraction of $V_O$ to the op-amp's inverting input.
- The op-amp's output drives Q1's base, controlling its conduction and thus $V_O$.
- $R_S$ (between $V_O$ and the actual load) drops a voltage proportional to $I_L$. When this drop exceeds **0.7 V**, Q2 turns on, clamping the regulator.

### 4.3 Closed-Loop Feedback Signal Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[V_IN Disturbance] --> B[V_O Tries to Change]
    B --> C[Sampling Network R1 R2]
    C --> D[Comparator Compares V_S with V_REF]
    D --> E[Error Epsilon Generated]
    E --> F[Q1 Base Drive Adjusted]
    F --> G[V_CE of Q1 Changes]
    G --> H[V_O Corrected Back to Nominal]
    H --> C
```

This **closed-loop causal chain** is what differentiates a regulated supply from an unregulated one.

### 4.4 Current-Limiting Sub-Circuit

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    LoadCurrent[Load Current I_L] --> SenseRes[R_S Voltage Drop I_L times R_S]
    SenseRes --> CompareThreshold{Threshold Check V_R_S greater than 0.7 V}
    CompareThreshold -->|No Normal| Reg[Regulator Operates Normally]
    CompareThreshold -->|Yes Overload| Q2TurnOn[Q2 Turns ON]
    Q2TurnOn --> StealBase[Steals Base Drive from Q1]
    StealBase --> Limit[I_L Held at I_L max equals 0.7 over R_S]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tip for KTU Drawing:** Always label **polarities** of $V_{IN}$, $V_{REF}$, and $V_O$. Mark the pass transistor explicitly as **NPN** and indicate that $V_O$ is taken from the **emitter** (emitter follower configuration).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: Define a series voltage regulator. Mention its main advantage over a shunt regulator. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A series voltage regulator is a type of linear voltage regulator in which the **pass element (control transistor) is connected in series** between the unregulated input supply and the regulated output load. The output voltage is continuously sampled by a feedback network, compared with a fixed reference voltage, and the resulting error signal controls the conduction of the pass transistor such that $V_O$ remains constant.

**Main advantage over shunt regulator:** In a series regulator, the current through the pass element is equal to the load current $I_L$, whereas in a shunt regulator, the pass element carries $I_L + I_{shunt}$ continuously. Therefore, the series regulator has **higher efficiency** and **less power dissipation** in the control element, making it the preferred topology for IC regulators like the LM78xx series.

*(Mark distribution: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for working principle, 1 mark for advantage.)*

---

#### Question 2: What is meant by line regulation and load regulation? State the standard unit for each. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Line Regulation** is defined as the change in regulated output voltage $V_O$ per unit change in unregulated input voltage $V_{IN}$, with the load current held constant:

$$LR = \left(\frac{\Delta V_O}{\Delta V_{IN}}\right)_{I_L = \text{const}}$$

It is measured in **V/V** (or more practically, **mV/V**). A low value of line regulation is desirable.

**Load Regulation** is defined as the change in regulated output voltage $V_O$ per unit change in load current $I_L$, with the input voltage held constant:

$$LD = \left(\frac{\Delta V_O}{\Delta I_L}\right)_{V_{IN} = \text{const}}$$

It is measured in **V/A** (or practically, **mV/mA** or even **% of $V_O$**). Again, a low value indicates good regulation.

*(Mark distribution: 1 mark each for definitions + 1 mark for units.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Draw the block diagram of a series voltage regulator using an op-amp error amplifier. Explain the function of each block in detail. **(7 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(b)** Derive the expression for the regulated output voltage of a feedback series regulator with reference $V_{REF}$ and feedback resistors $R_1$, $R_2$. A regulator is designed with $V_{REF} = 6$ V, $R_2 = 5$ k$\Omega$, and desired output $V_O = 18$ V. Find the required value of $R_1$. **(7 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

---

**Model Solution for Question A(a):**

**Block Diagram (refer to Section 4.1 above for the rendered Mermaid version).** In a written exam, draw the following four functional blocks connected by arrows showing signal flow:

1. **Series Pass Element (Q1):** An NPN transistor with collector tied to $V_{IN}$ and emitter tied to $V_O$. Operates as an emitter follower. The output voltage is:

$$V_O = V_{IN} - V_{CE1}$$

2. **Reference Voltage Source:** A Zener diode $D_Z$ biased in reverse breakdown by a resistor $R_B$ connected to $V_{IN}$. Provides a temperature-stable $V_{REF}$ at the non-inverting input of the op-amp.

3. **Sampling Network:** A resistive divider $R_1$ (top) and $R_2$ (bottom) connected across the output. Samples a fraction of $V_O$:

$$V_S = V_O \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

4. **Error Amplifier (Op-Amp):** Compares $V_S$ at the inverting input with $V_{REF}$ at the non-inverting input. The output error voltage:

$$\epsilon = A_{OL} (V_{REF} - V_S)$$

drives the base of Q1. As $V_{REF} > V_S$ increases, $\epsilon$ rises, increasing Q1's base drive, lowering $V_{CE1}$, and raising $V_O$. The loop is closed.

**Working Principle:** When $V_{IN}$ rises or $I_L$ decreases, $V_O$ tries to rise $\Rightarrow V_S$ rises $\Rightarrow \epsilon$ falls $\Rightarrow$ Q1 conducts less $\Rightarrow V_{CE1}$ increases $\Rightarrow V_O$ falls back to the set value. This is **negative feedback regulation**.

*(Mark allocation: 2 marks for block diagram, 3 marks for block functions, 2 marks for working explanation.)*

---

**Model Solution for Question A(b):**

**Derivation:**

From the virtual short principle of the op-amp:

$$V_{REF} = V_O \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

Solving for $V_O$:

$$V_O = V_{REF} \left(\frac{R_1 + R_2}{R_2}\right) = V_{REF}\left(1 + \frac{R_1}{R_2}\right)$$

**Numerical Substitution:**

Given: $V_{REF} = 6$ V, $R_2 = 5\ \text{k}\Omega$, $V_O = 18$ V.

$$18 = 6\left(1 + \frac{R_1}{5{,}000}\right)$$

Divide both sides by 6:

$$3 = 1 + \frac{R_1}{5{,}000}$$

Subtract 1:

$$2 = \frac{R_1}{5{,}000}$$

Solve:

$$R_1 = 10{,}000\ \Omega = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$$

**Answer:** $R_1 = 10$ k$\Omega$.

*(Mark allocation: 2 marks for derivation, 2 marks for formula substitution, 2 marks for arithmetic, 1 mark for final answer with units.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the current limiting and foldback current limiting techniques used in series voltage regulators with neat diagrams. **(7 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(b)** A series regulator with op-amp error amplifier uses a current-sense resistor $R_S = 0.33\ \Omega$ and a BJT protection transistor with $V_{BE(\text{on})} = 0.7$ V. Calculate (i) the maximum load current, and (ii) the power dissipated in the pass transistor when $V_{IN} = 20$ V, $V_O = 12$ V, and $I_L = 1$ A. **(7 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

---

**Model Solution for Question B(a):**

**Current Limiting (Constant Current Type):**

In a basic current-limiting scheme, a small resistor $R_S$ is placed in series with the load, and a second BJT (Q2) has its base-emitter connected across $R_S$. Under normal operation, $V_{R_S} = I_L R_S < 0.7$ V, so Q2 remains OFF and does not affect regulation. When $I_L$ rises such that $V_{R_S}$ reaches 0.7 V, Q2 turns ON. Q2's collector is connected to the base of the pass transistor Q1. Once Q2 conducts, it **shunts base current away from Q1**, preventing Q1 from delivering any further current to the load. The maximum load current is therefore clamped at:

$$I_{L(\max)} = \frac{V_{BE2(\text{on})}}{R_S} \approx \frac{0.7\ \text{V}}{R_S}$$

**Foldback Current Limiting:**

The disadvantage of simple current limiting is that during a short circuit, the full $I_{L(\max)}$ continues to flow, dissipating $I_{L(\max)} \times V_{IN}$ watts in the pass transistor — often exceeding its safe operating area (SOA). Foldback limiting adds a resistor $R_3$ from the output to Q2's base, such that as $V_O$ falls (due to overload), the voltage across Q2's base-emitter increases, **further reducing** the available output current. The short-circuit current $I_{SC}$ is therefore less than $I_{L(\max)}$.

The foldback equation is:

$$I_{SC} = \frac{V_{BE(\text{on})}(R_2 + R_3) - V_O R_2}{R_S R_3}$$

where $R_2$ is the lower feedback resistor. The operating point "folds back" along the load line toward a safe $I_{SC}$ at $V_O = 0$ V.

*(Mark allocation: 2 marks for constant current diagram + explanation, 2 marks for equation, 2 marks for foldback diagram + explanation, 1 mark for foldback equation.)*

---

**Model Solution for Question B(b):**

**Part (i) — Maximum Load Current:**

Using the constant current limit formula:

$$I_{L(\max)} = \frac{V_{BE(\text{on})}}{R_S} = \frac{0.7\ \text{V}}{0.33\ \Omega} = 2.12\ \text{A}$$

**Part (ii) — Power Dissipation in the Pass Transistor:**

The voltage across the pass transistor is:

$$V_{CE(\text{pass})} = V_{IN} - V_O = 20 - 12 = 8\ \text{V}$$

The current through the pass transistor is approximately equal to the load current (base current is negligible):

$$I_{C(\text{pass})} \approx I_L = 1\ \text{A}$$

Therefore, the power dissipation is:

$$P_D = V_{CE(\text{pass})} \times I_{C(\text{pass})} = 8 \times 1 = 8\ \text{W}$$

**Answer:** (i) $I_{L(\max)} = 2.12$ A. (ii) $P_D = 8$ W.

*(Mark allocation: 2 marks for formula, 1 mark for Part (i) answer, 2 marks for $V_{CE}$ calculation, 2 marks for $P_D$ calculation with units.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Confusing the pass transistor configuration:** The pass transistor is in the **common-collector (emitter follower)** configuration, NOT common-emitter. Marks are deducted if the student writes the wrong configuration.
> 2. **Forgetting the "+1" in the output formula:** The expression $V_O = V_{REF} \left(1 + \dfrac{R_1}{R_2}\right)$ is sometimes written as $V_O = V_{REF} \cdot \dfrac{R_1}{R_2}$ (missing the unity term). This is a **classic 1-mark deduction error**.
> 3. **Using $V_{BE} = 0.6$ V instead of 0.7 V:** KTU board key uniformly accepts $V_{BE(\text{on})} = 0.7$ V. Using 0.6 V may still be marked, but 0.7 V is the safer standard.
> 4. **Neglecting the $I_{ADJ}$ term in LM317 problems:** For the LM317 specifically, the term $I_{ADJ} \cdot R_2$ ($\approx 50\ \mu$A $\times R_2$) appears in the output formula. Ignoring it is acceptable only if the question explicitly says "neglect $I_{ADJ}$".
> 5. **Failing to draw arrow direction in block diagram:** The KTU examiner expects the signal flow to be **clockwise** (input $\to$ pass $\to$ output $\to$ sampling $\to$ error amp $\to$ pass). Reversed arrows lose one mark.
> 6. **Forgetting the heat-sink comment:** A regulator dissipating more than **1–2 W** absolutely requires a heat sink. If the numerical question yields a high $P_D$, mentioning "heat sink is required" earns a free 0.5–1 mark.
> 7. **Confusing foldback with simple current limit:** Foldback has TWO resistors in the protection network ($R_S$ and $R_3$), simple limiting has only ONE ($R_S$). Mark this distinction clearly in diagrams.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Series Voltage Regulator**

- A series voltage regulator has its **pass element in series** with the load; a shunt regulator has it in **parallel**.
- The four essential blocks are: **Pass Transistor, Reference, Sampling Divider, Error Amplifier**.
- The pass transistor operates in the **active (linear) region**, NOT saturation or cutoff.
- Output voltage formula: $V_O = V_{REF} \left(1 + \dfrac{R_1}{R_2}\right)$.
- Feedback factor: $\beta = \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$.
- Closed-loop line regulation improves by $(1 + A\beta)$: $LR_{CL} = \dfrac{LR_{OL}}{1 + A\beta}$.
- Closed-loop output impedance is reduced by $(1 + A\beta)$: $Z_{O(CL)} = \dfrac{Z_{O(OL)}}{1 + A\beta}$.
- Constant current limit: $I_{L(\max)} = \dfrac{V_{BE(\text{on})}}{R_S} = \dfrac{0.7}{R_S}$.
- Foldback current limit: $I_{SC} = \dfrac{V_{BE(\text{on})}(R_2 + R_3) - V_O R_2}{R_S R_3}$ (with $R_3$ extra).
- Power dissipation in pass transistor: $P_D = (V_{IN} - V_O) \cdot I_L$.
- Efficiency: $\eta = \dfrac{V_O}{V_{IN}} \times 100\%$ (typically 40–65%).
- **LM7805** is a fixed +5 V regulator; **LM317** is an adjustable regulator with $V_{REF} = 1.25$ V.
- **LM7905** is a fixed **negative** –5 V regulator (mentioned occasionally).
- **Dropout voltage** is the minimum $(V_{IN} - V_O)$ for regulation, typically **2 V to 3 V** for BJT regulators.
- **Ripple Rejection Ratio (PSRR)** is typically **60–80 dB** in IC regulators.
- **Always check thermal SOA:** $P_D$ must be within the transistor's safe operating area, otherwise a heat sink is mandatory.
- **Series regulators are preferred for high-current, high-efficiency applications** (LM78xx family).
- **Shunt regulators are preferred for low-noise, low-current reference applications**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET302 - ELECTRONIC DEVICES AND CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Shunt Voltage Regulator**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Shunt Voltage Regulator** is a type of linear voltage regulator in which the **controlling/regulating element is connected in parallel (shunt) with the load**, and a **series dropping resistor (R_S)** is placed between the unregulated input DC source and the load. Any excess current that is not required by the load is **diverted (shunted) away** through the regulating element, thereby keeping the voltage across the load essentially constant.

The most common implementation of a shunt regulator uses a **Zener diode** operated in its **reverse breakdown (avalanche/Zener) region**, where the device behaves as a near-constant voltage source.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The shunt regulator is essentially a **two-terminal parallel-connected controlling device** (Zener, transistor, or IC reference) that absorbs the difference between the supply current and the load current to maintain a stable $V_{out}$ across the load.

> [!NOTE]  
> **Operating Principle:** The regulator does **NOT** block excess current like a series regulator; instead, it **diverts (bleeds)** the surplus current to ground. The series resistor R_S absorbs the corresponding excess voltage drop.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine a **water tank with an overflow pipe** (the shunt element) connected at a fixed height:

- The **incoming pipe** (R_S) brings variable water pressure (unregulated input).
- The **tank level** (load voltage) must stay at a fixed height.
- If too much water comes in, the **overflow pipe bleeds off the excess**, keeping the tank level constant.
- If the demand (load) increases, less water overflows; if demand decreases, more overflows.

The **Zener diode is that overflow pipe**: it opens up (conducts more) whenever the load tries to use less current, dumping the surplus current to ground and clamping the voltage at $V_Z$.

## 1.3 Key Terminology

| Term | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| $V_{in}$ | Unregulated DC input voltage (can vary) |
| $V_{out} = V_L$ | Regulated output voltage across the load |
| $R_S$ | Series dropping resistor |
| $I_S$ | Current through $R_S$ |
| $I_Z$ | Current through the Zener (shunt element) |
| $I_L$ | Load current |
| $V_Z$ | Zener (breakdown) voltage — sets $V_{out}$ |
| $I_{ZK}$ | Minimum Zener current to maintain regulation (knee current) |
| $I_{ZM}$ | Maximum allowable Zener current (power limit) |

## 1.4 Why Use a Shunt Regulator?

- **Simple, cheap, and rugged** — needs only one Zener and one resistor.
- **Excellent for low-power, fixed-reference applications** (reference voltages, sensor biasing, op-amp supplies, A/D reference ladders).
- **Built-in short-circuit protection** — when load is shorted, all current flows through the Zener (must be rated for it or protected).
- Inherent **noise filtering** due to the Zener's low dynamic impedance in breakdown.

> [!NOTE]  
> **Limitation:** A shunt regulator is **inherently inefficient** because the full $I_S$ always flows, even when the load draws zero current. Maximum theoretical efficiency $\approx 50\%$. For high-power regulation, **series regulators** or **switch-mode regulators** are preferred.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Circuit Topology

The fundamental Zener shunt regulator has three branches connected across the unregulated DC source $V_{in}$:

1. **Series resistor $R_S$** — between $V_{in}$ and the regulated node.
2. **Zener diode (reverse-biased)** — cathode at the regulated node, anode at ground.
3. **Load $R_L$** — connected in parallel with the Zener.

All three branches share the same node voltage: the **regulated output $V_{out}$**.

## 2.2 Operating Conditions (The 'How' Behind Each Mode)

### Mode 1 — Zener OFF (Unregulated)
If $V_{in}$ is so small that $V_{out}$ would fall below $V_Z$, the Zener is non-conducting. The circuit reduces to a simple **voltage divider**:
$$V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{R_L}{R_S + R_L}$$
Regulation is lost in this mode.

### Mode 2 — Zener ON (Regulated, Active)
When $V_{in}$ is large enough that $V_{out}$ would tend to exceed $V_Z$, the Zener enters breakdown and **clamps** the node at $V_Z$:
$$V_{out} = V_Z$$
This is the **desired mode of operation**. Any extra current automatically diverts into the Zener.

### Mode 3 — Load Open Circuit
If $R_L \to \infty$ ($I_L = 0$), the entire $I_S$ flows through the Zener. The Zener must tolerate this current without exceeding $P_{ZM} = V_Z \cdot I_{ZM}$.

## 2.3 Kirchhoff's Current Law at the Regulated Node

The single most important KCL equation of the shunt regulator:

$$I_S = I_Z + I_L$$

where
$$I_S = \frac{V_{in} - V_Z}{R_S}, \quad I_L = \frac{V_Z}{R_L}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Why this works:** If $I_L$ increases (load draws more), $I_S$ stays roughly fixed (set by $R_S$ and $V_{in}$), so $I_Z$ must **decrease** to satisfy KCL. Conversely, if $I_L$ decreases, $I_Z$ increases to absorb the surplus.

## 2.4 Design of the Series Resistor $R_S$

$R_S$ must be chosen so that for any combination of $V_{in}^{min}$, $V_{in}^{max}$, $I_L^{min}$, and $I_L^{max}$, the Zener current $I_Z$ stays within the safe window:
$$I_{ZK} \le I_Z \le I_{ZM}$$

The two extreme cases give the bounds on $R_S$:

### Worst Case 1: $V_{in}$ is MAXIMUM and $I_L$ is MINIMUM
This forces the **maximum Zener current**. We must keep $I_Z \le I_{ZM}$:
$$R_S \ge \frac{V_{in}^{max} - V_Z}{I_{ZM} + I_L^{min}}$$

### Worst Case 2: $V_{in}$ is MINIMUM and $I_L$ is MAXIMUM
This forces the **minimum Zener current**. We must keep $I_Z \ge I_{ZK}$:
$$R_S \le \frac{V_{in}^{min} - V_Z}{I_{ZK} + I_L^{max}}$$

The **valid design range** for $R_S$ is therefore:
$$\frac{V_{in}^{min} - V_Z}{I_{ZK} + I_L^{max}} \;\le\; R_S \;\le\; \frac{V_{in}^{max} - V_Z}{I_{ZM} + I_L^{min}}$$

> [!WARNING]  
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Forgetting the $I_L$ terms in the numerator-denominator pairing. Always remember: **MAX $V_{in}$ pairs with MIN $I_L$** (to bound $I_Z$ from above), and **MIN $V_{in}$ pairs with MAX $I_L$** (to bound $I_Z$ from below).

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $V_{out} = V_Z$ | Regulated output voltage (Zener ON) | V |
| 2 | $I_S = I_Z + I_L$ | KCL at the regulated node | A |
| 3 | $I_S = \dfrac{V_{in} - V_Z}{R_S}$ | Current through series resistor | A |
| 4 | $I_L = \dfrac{V_Z}{R_L}$ | Load current | A |
| 5 | $I_Z = I_S - I_L$ | Zener (shunt) current | A |
| 6 | $P_Z = V_Z \cdot I_Z$ | Power dissipated by Zener | W |
| 7 | $R_S^{min} = \dfrac{V_{in}^{max} - V_Z}{I_{ZM} + I_L^{min}}$ | Lower bound on $R_S$ (Zener current limit) | $\Omega$ |
| 8 | $R_S^{max} = \dfrac{V_{in}^{min} - V_Z}{I_{ZK} + I_L^{max}}$ | Upper bound on $R_S$ (Zener knee limit) | $\Omega$ |
| 9 | $\eta_{max} = \dfrac{V_Z \cdot I_L}{(V_{in}^{min})(I_S)}$ | Regulator efficiency | unitless |
| 10 | $\eta_{max}^{theoretical} \approx 50\%$ | Theoretical max for ideal shunt regulator | unitless |
| 11 | $Line\,Reg. = \dfrac{\Delta V_{out}}{\Delta V_{in}} \bigg\vert_{R_L\,fixed}$ | Line regulation ratio | V/V |
| 12 | $Load\,Reg. = \dfrac{\Delta V_{out}}{\Delta I_L} \bigg\vert_{V_{in}\,fixed}$ | Load regulation ratio | V/A |

## 2.6 Regulation Performance Metrics

### Line Regulation
With $R_L$ constant, as $V_{in}$ changes, ideally $V_{out}$ should not change. In a Zener shunt regulator, line regulation is governed by the Zener's **dynamic impedance $Z_Z$**:

$$\frac{\Delta V_{out}}{\Delta V_{in}} = \frac{Z_Z}{R_S + Z_Z}$$

For a well-chosen Zener, $Z_Z \ll R_S$, so the ratio approaches zero → excellent line regulation.

### Load Regulation
With $V_{in}$ constant, $\Delta V_{out}$ arises from the finite $Z_Z$:
$$\Delta V_{out} = \Delta I_Z \cdot Z_Z$$
Smaller $Z_Z$ → better load regulation.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Voltage references** for A/D and D/A converters (precision $V_{REF}$).
- **Input protection** of sensitive instrumentation.
- **Bias supplies** for op-amps, comparators, and transistor stages.
- **Waveform clippers and limiters** in communication electronics.
- **Crowbar protection** (when combined with an SCR across the supply rail).
