# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Output Voltage in the Regulated Mode

**Given:** A Zener diode with breakdown voltage $V_Z$ is connected with its cathode at the output node and anode at ground; the input $V_{in}$ exceeds the threshold required to drive the Zener into breakdown.

**Step 1 — Identify the operating region.**  
If the Zener is reverse-biased and $V_{in} \ge V_Z$, the Zener enters the **avalanche/Zener breakdown region**.

**Step 2 — Apply the Zener's defining property.**  
In breakdown, the voltage across the Zener is (approximately) constant:
$$V_{Zener} = V_Z$$

**Step 3 — Recognize the parallel connection.**  
Since the load $R_L$ is in parallel with the Zener, both share the same voltage:
$$V_{out} = V_{Zener} = V_Z$$

$$\boxed{\,V_{out} = V_Z\,}$$

> **Conclusion:** The output voltage is set **entirely by the Zener breakdown voltage**, independent of $V_{in}$ (as long as regulation is maintained) and independent of $R_L$ (as long as $I_Z$ stays within bounds).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Derivation of the Series Resistor $R_S$ Design Equation

**Given:**  
- Input variation: $V_{in}^{min} \le V_{in} \le V_{in}^{max}$  
- Load variation: $I_L^{min} \le I_L \le I_L^{max}$  
- Zener safe operating window: $I_{ZK} \le I_Z \le I_{ZM}$

**Step 1 — Start from KCL at the output node.**
$$I_S = I_Z + I_L$$

**Step 2 — Express $I_S$ using Ohm's law across $R_S$.**  
The voltage across $R_S$ is $V_{in} - V_Z$, so:
$$I_S = \frac{V_{in} - V_Z}{R_S}$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to solve for $R_S$.**
$$R_S = \frac{V_{in} - V_Z}{I_S} = \frac{V_{in} - V_Z}{I_Z + I_L}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the worst-case condition for MAXIMUM Zener current.**  
The largest $I_Z$ occurs when $V_{in}$ is at its **maximum** and $I_L$ is at its **minimum**:
$$I_Z^{max} = \frac{V_{in}^{max} - V_Z}{R_S} - I_L^{min}$$

For safe operation we need $I_Z^{max} \le I_{ZM}$, so:
$$R_S \ge \frac{V_{in}^{max} - V_Z}{I_{ZM} + I_L^{min}}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the worst-case condition for MINIMUM Zener current.**  
The smallest $I_Z$ occurs when $V_{in}$ is at its **minimum** and $I_L$ is at its **maximum**:
$$I_Z^{min} = \frac{V_{in}^{min} - V_Z}{R_S} - I_L^{max}$$

For regulation we need $I_Z^{min} \ge I_{ZK}$, so:
$$R_S \le \frac{V_{in}^{min} - V_Z}{I_{ZK} + I_L^{max}}$$

**Step 6 — Combine the two inequalities.**  
The design is feasible only if the interval is non-empty:

$$\frac{V_{in}^{min} - V_Z}{I_{ZK} + I_L^{max}} \;\le\; R_S \;\le\; \frac{V_{in}^{max} - V_Z}{I_{ZM} + I_L^{min}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Design Rule:** Pick $R_S$ in the geometric/arithmetical centre of this interval to give maximum design margin. If no valid $R_S$ exists, you must use a Zener with higher $I_{ZM}$ or higher power rating.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Maximum Efficiency of an Ideal Shunt Regulator

**Step 1 — Define efficiency.**  
$$\eta = \frac{P_{load}}{P_{in}} = \frac{V_Z \cdot I_L}{V_{in} \cdot I_S}$$

**Step 2 — Use KCL to relate $I_S$ to $I_L$.**  
At minimum loss scenario, $V_{in} = V_{in}^{min}$ and $I_Z = 0$ (idealised, no Zener standby current):
$$I_S = I_L$$
Then:
$$\eta = \frac{V_Z \cdot I_L}{V_{in}^{min} \cdot I_L} = \frac{V_Z}{V_{in}^{min}}$$

**Step 3 — Find the maximum of $\eta$ with respect to $V_{in}^{min}$.**  
The smallest allowable $V_{in}^{min}$ is exactly $V_Z$ (otherwise the Zener cannot be driven into breakdown). So the maximum $\eta$ is:
$$\eta_{max} = \frac{V_Z}{V_Z} = 1 \;\text{(idealised, no $I_{ZK}$)}$$

**Step 4 — Account for practical $I_{ZK}$ drain.**  
In a real circuit, the Zener needs a minimum $I_{ZK}$ to stay in regulation, so $I_S \ge I_{ZK} + I_L$ at all times. As $I_L \to 0$, the efficiency $\to 0$. The peak efficiency occurs at $I_L^{max}$:
$$\eta_{peak} = \frac{V_Z \cdot I_L^{max}}{V_{in}^{min} \cdot (I_L^{max} + I_{ZK})}$$

In the worst-case (full $I_{ZM}$ bleed, $V_{in}^{max} = 2 V_Z$), efficiency is bounded by approximately **50%** — this is the classical textbook upper limit.

$$\boxed{\,\eta_{shunt}^{practical} \le 50\%\,}$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** Design a Zener shunt regulator with $V_{out} = 12\,\text{V}$ for a load that draws $I_L = 10\,\text{mA}$ to $50\,\text{mA}$ from a supply that varies between $V_{in} = 15\,\text{V}$ and $20\,\text{V}$. Use a Zener with $I_{ZK} = 5\,\text{mA}$ and $I_{ZM} = 100\,\text{mA}$.

**Step 1 — Calculate lower bound on $R_S$.**
$$R_S^{min} = \frac{V_{in}^{max} - V_Z}{I_{ZM} + I_L^{min}} = \frac{20 - 12}{(100 + 10)\,\text{mA}} = \frac{8}{0.110} \approx 72.7\,\Omega$$

**Step 2 — Calculate upper bound on $R_S$.**
$$R_S^{max} = \frac{V_{in}^{min} - V_Z}{I_{ZK} + I_L^{max}} = \frac{15 - 12}{(5 + 50)\,\text{mA}} = \frac{3}{0.055} \approx 54.5\,\Omega$$

**Step 3 — Check feasibility.**  
We need $R_S^{min} \le R_S^{max}$, but $72.7\,\Omega \le 54.5\,\Omega$ is **FALSE**. No valid $R_S$ exists.

**Step 4 — Remedy.** Either use a Zener with higher $I_{ZM}$ (e.g., $I_{ZM} = 200\,\text{mA}$), or reduce the input range. If we use $I_{ZM} = 200\,\text{mA}$:
$$R_S^{min} = \frac{8}{0.210} \approx 38.1\,\Omega, \quad R_S^{max} \approx 54.5\,\Omega$$

**Step 5 — Choose $R_S = 47\,\Omega$ (standard value, lies in the feasible range).**  
**Step 6 — Verify worst-case $I_Z$.**  
- At $V_{in} = 20\,\text{V}$, $I_L = 10\,\text{mA}$: $I_S = (20-12)/47 = 170.2\,\text{mA}$, so $I_Z = 160.2\,\text{mA} < 200\,\text{mA}$ ✓  
- At $V_{in} = 15\,\text{V}$, $I_L = 50\,\text{mA}$: $I_S = (15-12)/47 = 63.8\,\text{mA}$, so $I_Z = 13.8\,\text{mA} > 5\,\text{mA}$ ✓

> **Design validated.**

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison: Shunt vs. Series Regulator

| Parameter | Shunt Regulator | Series (Pass) Regulator |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Position of control element | Parallel to load | In series with load |
| $I_S$ with $V_{in}$ variation | Stays constant | Stays constant |
| $I_S$ with $I_L$ variation | Stays constant | **Changes** with $I_L$ |
| Efficiency | Poor (≤ 50%) | Good (can exceed 90%) |
| Short-circuit behaviour | Self-protective (Zener limits current) | Pass transistor can be destroyed |
| Complexity | Trivial (R + Zener) | Higher (needs feedback loop) |
| Best use | Low-current references, high-noise immunity | General-purpose power supplies |
| Load regulation | Poor (depends on $Z_Z$) | Excellent (feedback driven) |

> **How the transistor version works:** The Zener sets a precise reference at the base of $Q_1$. As $V_{out}$ tries to rise above $V_Z + V_{BE}$, $Q_1$ conducts more, shunting the excess current through its collector–emitter junction to ground. The transistor multiplies the Zener's current-handling capability by $\beta$, allowing much higher load currents with the same reference Zener.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]
**Define a shunt voltage regulator. With a neat sketch, explain its working using a Zener diode.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **shunt voltage regulator** is a regulator in which the controlling element is connected in **parallel (shunt) with the load** and a **series resistor $R_S$** is placed between the unregulated input and the load.

**Working (sketch — 1 mark):** The unregulated DC input $V_{in}$ is applied across the series combination of $R_S$ and the parallel combination of (Zener + load). The Zener is reverse-biased and operated in its **breakdown region**.

**Operation (2 marks):** When $V_{in}$ rises, the voltage across the Zener tends to increase, but the Zener holds it constant at $V_Z$, diverting the excess current through itself. The KCL at the output node is $I_S = I_Z + I_L$. Thus, $V_{out} = V_Z$ is held constant irrespective of variations in $V_{in}$ or $R_L$, provided the Zener current $I_Z$ stays between $I_{ZK}$ and $I_{ZM}$.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]
**State and justify the KCL equation governing the Zener shunt regulator. Why is the efficiency of a shunt regulator inherently low?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**KCL equation (1.5 marks):** At the regulated output node, applying Kirchhoff's Current Law:
$$I_S = I_Z + I_L$$
where $I_S = \dfrac{V_{in} - V_Z}{R_S}$ is the current entering through the series resistor, $I_Z$ is the current diverted through the Zener, and $I_L$ is the current delivered to the load. This equation is justified because the regulated node is a single node with three branches; charge conservation demands that the inflow equals the sum of outflows.

**Low efficiency (1.5 marks):** A shunt regulator always carries the **full series current $I_S$**, regardless of how much current the load actually draws. When $I_L$ is small, the excess current is wasted as heat in the Zener and $R_S$. The maximum practical efficiency is approximately **50%**, making it suitable only for low-power, high-precision reference applications.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – July 2024, Model]

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of a **Zener diode shunt regulator**. Derive the expression for the **series resistance $R_S$** required for proper regulation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Zener shunt regulator has the following specifications:  
- $V_{in}$ varies from **15 V to 20 V**  
- $V_Z = 9.1\,\text{V}$, $I_{ZK} = 5\,\text{mA}$, $I_{ZM} = 50\,\text{mA}$  
- Load current $I_L$ varies from **10 mA to 30 mA**

Determine: (i) the range of permissible values of $R_S$, (ii) the power rating required for the Zener, and (iii) the maximum efficiency at the worst-case operating point. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Working and Derivation of $R_S$ (7 marks):**

**Circuit Diagram (2 marks):**  
[Show $V_{in}$ → $R_S$ → node → (Zener $\|$ Load) → Ground, with Zener cathode at the node.]

**Working (2 marks):** The Zener is reverse-biased. When $V_{in}$ exceeds $V_Z$, the Zener enters breakdown and clamps the output node to $V_Z$. The series resistor drops the excess voltage $(V_{in} - V_Z)$ and limits the current. KCL: $I_S = I_Z + I_L$.

**Derivation of $R_S$ (3 marks):** As shown in Section 3.2 above, applying the worst-case bounds:
- **Max $V_{in}$, Min $I_L$** → maximum $I_Z$ → bound $R_S$ from below:
$$R_S \ge \frac{V_{in}^{max} - V_Z}{I_{ZM} + I_L^{min}}$$
- **Min $V_{in}$, Max $I_L$** → minimum $I_Z$ → bound $R_S$ from above:
$$R_S \le \frac{V_{in}^{min} - V_Z}{I_{ZK} + I_L^{max}}$$

Combining:
$$\frac{V_{in}^{min} - V_Z}{I_{ZK} + I_L^{max}} \;\le\; R_S \;\le\; \frac{V_{in}^{max} - V_Z}{I_{ZM} + I_L^{min}}$$

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Range of $R_S$ (3 marks):**

Lower bound:
$$R_S^{min} = \frac{V_{in}^{max} - V_Z}{I_{ZM} + I_L^{min}} = \frac{20 - 9.1}{(50 + 10)\,\text{mA}} = \frac{10.9}{0.060} \approx 181.7\,\Omega$$

Upper bound:
$$R_S^{max} = \frac{V_{in}^{min} - V_Z}{I_{ZK} + I_L^{max}} = \frac{15 - 9.1}{(5 + 30)\,\text{mA}} = \frac{5.9}{0.035} \approx 168.6\,\Omega$$

**Observation:** The interval is **inverted** ($181.7 > 168.6$). **No feasible $R_S$ exists** with the given Zener. We must either choose a Zener with a higher $I_{ZM}$ rating (e.g., $I_{ZM} \ge 70\,\text{mA}$) or use a different Zener voltage closer to $V_{in}^{min}$. Suppose we use a Zener with $I_{ZM} = 80\,\text{mA}$: then $R_S^{min} = 10.9/0.090 = 121.1\,\Omega$, and a valid range $121.1 \le R_S \le 168.6$ opens up. Choose $R_S = 150\,\Omega$ (standard value).

**(ii) Zener Power Rating (2 marks):**

Maximum Zener current occurs at $V_{in}^{max}$, $I_L^{min}$:
$$I_Z^{max} = \frac{V_{in}^{max} - V_Z}{R_S} - I_L^{min} = \frac{20 - 9.1}{150} - 0.010 = 0.0727 - 0.010 = 62.7\,\text{mA}$$

Maximum Zener power:
$$P_Z^{max} = V_Z \cdot I_Z^{max} = 9.1 \times 0.0627 \approx 0.571\,\text{W}$$

Choose a Zener rated for at least **1 W** (with 50% safety margin).

**(iii) Maximum Efficiency (2 marks):**

Worst case (lowest efficiency) is at $V_{in}^{max} = 20\,\text{V}$, $I_L = I_L^{min} = 10\,\text{mA}$:
$$I_S = \frac{20 - 9.1}{150} = 72.7\,\text{mA}$$
$$\eta = \frac{V_Z \cdot I_L}{V_{in} \cdot I_S} = \frac{9.1 \times 0.010}{20 \times 0.0727} = \frac{0.091}{1.454} \approx 6.26\%$$

**Best-case efficiency** at $V_{in}^{min} = 15\,\text{V}$, $I_L^{max} = 30\,\text{mA}$:
$$I_S = \frac{15 - 9.1}{150} = 39.3\,\text{mA}$$
$$\eta_{max} = \frac{9.1 \times 0.030}{15 \times 0.0393} = \frac{0.273}{0.590} \approx 46.3\%$$

The efficiency is far below 50% in practice — confirming the inherent inefficiency of shunt regulators.

---

### QUESTION B (Choice 2 — Alternative) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]

**(a)** Draw the circuit of a **Zener shunt regulator** and explain its **V-I characteristics**. Discuss its **line regulation** and **load regulation** performance with supporting equations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a Zener shunt regulator to deliver a regulated output of **$V_{out} = 5.6\,\text{V}$** at a load current of **20 mA**, given that the input varies from **10 V to 15 V**. The Zener has $I_{ZK} = 1\,\text{mA}$ and $P_{ZM} = 500\,\text{mW}$. Verify your design. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution Outline

**(a) Circuit, V-I characteristics, and regulation (7 marks):**

- **Circuit (2 marks):** $V_{in} \to R_S \to$ node; Zener reverse-biased (cathode at node); load in parallel with Zener.
- **V-I Characteristics (2 marks):** Plot $V_{out}$ vs. $I_L$. For $I_L < I_L^{min}$ (region of operation), $V_{out} = V_Z$ is flat. For $I_L > I_L^{max}$ (Zener current falls below $I_{ZK}$), regulation collapses and $V_{out}$ drops below $V_Z$. The flat region is the **regulation band**.
- **Line regulation (1.5 marks):**  
  $$\text{Line Reg.} = \frac{\Delta V_{out}}{\Delta V_{in}} = \frac{Z_Z}{R_S + Z_Z}$$
- **Load regulation (1.5 marks):**  
  $$\text{Load Reg.} = \frac{\Delta V_{out}}{\Delta I_L} = -Z_Z$$
  (The minus sign indicates $V_{out}$ decreases as $I_L$ increases beyond the regulation band.)

---

**(b) Numerical Design (7 marks):**

Given: $V_{out} = V_Z = 5.6\,\text{V}$, $I_L = 20\,\text{mA}$, $V_{in} \in [10, 15]\,\text{V}$, $I_{ZK} = 1\,\text{mA}$, $P_{ZM} = 500\,\text{mW}$.

**Step 1 — Compute $I_{ZM}$.**
$$I_{ZM} = \frac{P_{ZM}}{V_Z} = \frac{0.5}{5.6} \approx 89.3\,\text{mA}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the bounds on $R_S$.**
$$R_S^{min} = \frac{15 - 5.6}{(89.3 + 20)\,\text{mA}} = \frac{9.4}{0.1093} \approx 86.0\,\Omega$$
$$R_S^{max} = \frac{10 - 5.6}{(1 + 20)\,\text{mA}} = \frac{4.4}{0.021} \approx 209.5\,\Omega$$

**Step 3 — Choose $R_S = 120\,\Omega$ (standard E12 value within the feasible range).**

**Step 4 — Verify at the worst cases.**

- Worst case 1 — $V_{in} = 15\,\text{V}$, $I_L = 20\,\text{mA}$ (highest $I_Z$ case if $I_L$ min is 20 mA as per single value, but here $I_L$ is fixed):
  $$I_S = \frac{15 - 5.6}{120} = 78.3\,\text{mA}, \quad I_Z = 78.3 - 20 = 58.3\,\text{mA} \le I_{ZM} = 89.3\,\text{mA} \quad ✓$$

- Worst case 2 — $V_{in} = 10\,\text{V}$, $I_L = 20\,\text{mA}$:
  $$I_S = \frac{10 - 5.6}{120} = 36.7\,\text{mA}, \quad I_Z = 36.7 - 20 = 16.7\,\text{mA} \ge I_{ZK} = 1\,\text{mA} \quad ✓$$

**Design validated. Use $R_S = 120\,\Omega$, $1/2\,\text{W}$ rating, and a 5.6 V Zener of $P_{ZM} \ge 500\,\text{mW}$.**

**Power dissipation check on $R_S$:** $P_{R_S}^{max} = I_S^2 \cdot R_S = (0.0783)^2 \times 120 \approx 0.736\,\text{W}$. **Upgrade $R_S$ to a 1 W rating** for safety.

---

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 
> 1. **Mixing the worst-case pairs** (using MIN $V_{in}$ with MIN $I_L$): this is the single most common error. Always remember: **MAX $V_{in}$ + MIN $I_L$** for upper Zener current; **MIN $V_{in}$ + MAX $I_L$** for lower Zener current.
> 2. **Forgetting the $I_L$ term in the $R_S$ formula** (writing only $(V_{in}^{max} - V_Z)/I_{ZM}$): this leads to under-rated $R_S$ and Zener failure.
> 3. **Ignoring the $R_S$ power rating**: a $120\,\Omega$ resistor carrying 78 mA dissipates 0.73 W — a $1/4\,\text{W}$ resistor will burn. Always compute and specify the wattage.
> 4. **Not verifying the interval** $R_S^{min} \le R_S^{max}$: if the interval is empty, the design is infeasible and you must either change the Zener or change the input range. Marks are awarded for identifying infeasibility and proposing a remedy.
> 5. **Confusing shunt and series regulators**: in a series regulator, the control element is in series with the load; in a shunt regulator, it is in parallel. Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
> 6. **Skipping the KCL derivation** in part-(a) of 7-mark questions: the equation $I_S = I_Z + I_L$ must be **derived**, not merely stated.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Core definition (must-memorise):** A shunt regulator places the **controlling element in parallel with the load** and a **series resistor $R_S$** between the unregulated input and the load.
- **Operating principle:** The Zener **diverts (bleeds) the excess current** to maintain $V_{out} = V_Z$. The regulator does **not** block current — it **absorbs** the difference.
- **The single most important equation:**
$$I_S = I_Z + I_L$$
- **Series resistor design bounds** (KTU's favourite 14-mark derivation):
$$\frac{V_{in}^{min} - V_Z}{I_{ZK} + I_L^{max}} \le R_S \le \frac{V_{in}^{max} - V_Z}{I_{ZM} + I_L^{min}}$$
- **Worst-case pairing rule:** MAX $V_{in}$ ↔ MIN $I_L$ (upper Zener current limit); MIN $V_{in}$ ↔ MAX $I_L$ (lower Zener current / knee limit).
- **Efficiency ceiling:** $\eta_{max}^{practical} \approx 50\%$ — an inherent limitation of shunt topology.
- **Power dissipation checks** are mandatory: $P_Z = V_Z \cdot I_Z$ and $P_{R_S} = I_S^2 R_S$.
- **Line regulation** depends on the Zener's dynamic impedance $Z_Z$ — smaller $Z_Z$ gives better line regulation.
- **Load regulation:** $\Delta V_{out} = \Delta I_Z \cdot Z_Z$ — also better with smaller $Z_Z$.
- **Three operating modes:** Zener OFF (unregulated, $V_{out} < V_Z$), Zener ON (regulated, $V_{out} = V_Z$), and overload (Zener exceeds $I_{ZM}$).
- **Transistor-shunt variant** uses a pass transistor whose base is biased by the Zener, multiplying current-handling capacity by $\beta$.
- **Applications:** voltage references, A/D reference ladders, op-amp supply rails, sensor biasing, crowbar protection.
- **Comparisons to know cold:** Shunt vs. Series — current path, efficiency, complexity, short-circuit behaviour, regulation quality.
- **Always verify interval feasibility** before finalising $R_S$ — empty interval ⇒ redesign the Zener or the supply range.
- **Standard E12/E24 resistor values** within the design range should be chosen; never specify a non-standard value without justification.
- **Always overspec power ratings** by at least 50% for both the Zener and $R_S$ (engineering safety margin).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Short Circuit Protection and Fold Back Protection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAET302 - ELECTRONIC DEVICES AND CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Short Circuit Protection and Fold Back Protection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Short Circuit Protection (SCP)** is a current-limiting mechanism embedded within a regulated DC power supply (typically a series or shunt voltage regulator) that prevents the source current from exceeding a safe maximum value when the load is accidentally shorted (i.e., $V_L = 0$ V). It safeguards the **pass transistor**, **rectifier diodes**, and **transformer windings** from thermally destructive fault currents.

Within the KTU 2024 syllabus (Module 4 – Power Supplies and Regulators), two dominant SCP topologies are mandated:

1. **Constant Current (CC) Limiting** – also called *current fold-over*.
2. **Foldback Current Limiting** – also called *re-entrant or cutback protection*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why SCP is not optional:** A 15 V / 1 A series regulator with a hard short can attempt to deliver $I_{SC} \to \infty$. Without protection, the pass transistor's $P_D = V_{CE} \times I_C$ can exceed its $P_{D(\max)}$ rating, causing **secondary breakdown** and catastrophic failure.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Tap" Metaphor

Imagine a municipal water pipeline feeding your house:

- **No protection** → a burst pipe downstream causes the entire street's pressure to crash and the pump to overheat.
- **Constant Current Protection** → a *pressure-limiting valve* that holds the flow rate constant at 10 L/min, no matter how much the tap is opened. The pump works *just as hard* during a burst.
- **Foldback Protection** → a *smart valve* that, sensing a burst, simultaneously **reduces both the pressure AND the flow rate** so that the pump idles comfortably until the burst is repaired.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** In Constant Current limiting, the regulator dissipates its **maximum power** *exactly at the short-circuit point*. In Foldback limiting, the **short-circuit power dissipation is deliberately much smaller** — this is the central engineering reason foldback is preferred in high-current supplies.

### 1.3 Key Engineering Metrics (Memorise for KTU)

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|
| Maximum load current | $I_{L(\max)}$ | $0.5$ – $10$ A |
| Short-circuit current | $I_{SC}$ | $0.3 \, I_{L(\max)}$ – $1.0 \, I_{L(\max)}$ |
| Sense resistor | $R_S$ | $0.05$ – $1.0 \, \Omega$ |
| Sense voltage threshold | $V_{SENSE}$ | $\approx 0.6$ V ($V_{BE(on)}$) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a Protected Series Regulator

A series regulator with SCP is built from four functional blocks:

1. **Pass Element** ($Q_1$, typically an NPN Darlington) — carries the load current.
2. **Sampling Network** ($R_1, R_2$) — senses the output voltage.
3. **Error Amplifier** — compares sampled voltage with a reference ($V_{REF}$).
4. **Current Sense / Limit Sub-system** ($R_S$ + sense transistor $Q_2$) — monitors $I_L$ and overrides the error amplifier when $I_L$ exceeds threshold.

### 2.2 Operating Principle — Constant Current (CC) Limiting

The sense resistor $R_S$ is placed in series with the load. The voltage developed across it is $V_{R_S} = I_L \cdot R_S$. A sense transistor $Q_2$ monitors this voltage:

- **Normal region** ($I_L < I_{L(\max)}$): $V_{R_S} < V_{BE2(on)} \approx 0.6$ V → $Q_2$ OFF → regulator behaves as a normal series regulator.
- **Current-limit region** ($I_L \ge I_{L(\max)}$): $V_{R_S} \ge 0.6$ V → $Q_2$ ON → $Q_2$ robs base drive from $Q_1$ → $V_{OUT}$ collapses → load current is **clamped** at $I_{L(\max)}$.
- **Short-circuit point** ($V_L = 0$): $I_L = I_{L(\max)}$ (no further reduction).

**V-I Characteristic Shape:** a *rectangular knee* — output stays at $V_{NOM}$ for $I_L < I_{L(\max)}$, then drops vertically to zero while current remains pegged at $I_{L(\max)}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Power Dissipation Hot-Spot:** At the short-circuit point, $P_D = V_{IN} \times I_{L(\max)}$ — this is the *worst-case* dissipation the pass transistor must survive, *and it occurs continuously* during a fault. Heat-sink design must be sized for this condition.

### 2.3 Operating Principle — Foldback Current Limiting

Foldback adds a *voltage-dependent* feedback path. The sense transistor's base is driven by a divider $R_1$–$R_2$ that samples the *output* voltage, not just the load current.

- **Normal region** ($I_L < I_{L(\max)}$): the divider holds the base of $Q_2$ low enough that $V_{BE2} < 0.6$ V → $Q_2$ OFF.
- **Knee point** ($I_L = I_{L(\max)}$, $V_{OUT} = V_{NOM}$): $V_{BE2}$ reaches $0.6$ V → $Q_2$ starts to conduct.
- **Foldback region** ($V_{OUT}$ falling due to $Q_2$ action): the divider voltage *decreases*, so to keep $V_{BE2} = 0.6$ V, the required $V_{R_S}$ must *also decrease* — meaning $I_L$ falls. **Positive feedback** causes the operating point to *fold back* along a curved trajectory.
- **Short-circuit point** ($V_L = 0$): $I_L = I_{SC}$, where $I_{SC} < I_{L(\max)}$.

**V-I Characteristic Shape:** a *re-entrant arc* — output falls along a curved locus toward a point $(V_{OUT} \approx 0,\; I_{SC})$ that lies **below** the $I_{L(\max)}$ line.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL — Read this before using the table below:** the vertical pipe symbol `\vert` is rendered literally; in handwritten KTU scripts use $\vert$ or "such that" phrasing. These equations are the *only* set you need for any SCP numerical problem.

| # | Quantity | Equation | Conditions / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sense voltage threshold | $V_{SENSE} = V_{BE(on)} \approx 0.6$ V | Si BJT @ 25 °C |
| 2 | Constant-current $I_{L(\max)}$ | $I_{L(\max)} = \dfrac{V_{BE}}{R_S}$ | $V_{OUT} \approx V_{NOM}$ |
| 3 | Foldback $I_{L(\max)}$ | $I_{L(\max)} = \dfrac{V_{BE}}{R_S} + \dfrac{V_{NOM} \cdot R_1}{R_S \cdot (R_1 + R_2)}$ | At $V_{OUT} = V_{NOM}$ |
| 4 | Foldback $I_{SC}$ | $I_{SC} = \dfrac{V_{BE} \cdot (R_1 + R_2)}{R_2 \cdot R_S}$ | At $V_L = 0$ |
| 5 | Foldback condition | $I_{SC} < I_{L(\max)}$ | Required for re-entrant shape |
| 6 | Pass-transistor dissipation (CC) | $P_{D,CC} = V_{IN} \cdot I_{L(\max)}$ | Worst case, at short |
| 7 | Pass-transistor dissipation (FB) | $P_{D,FB} = V_{IN} \cdot I_{SC}$ | Worst case, at short |
| 8 | Power saving ratio | $\dfrac{P_{D,FB}}{P_{D,CC}} = \dfrac{I_{SC}}{I_{L(\max)}}$ | Dimensionless, $< 1$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Foldback protection is *the* industry standard for laboratory bench power supplies (e.g., Agilent E3631A, Rigol DP832), telecommunications -48 V rectifier plants, and EV onboard chargers. The reduced $I_{SC}$ prevents the wire harness and connector pins from welding during a sustained short, and lets the supply *ride through* transient faults (e.g., motor inrush of a faulty load) without nuisance tripping.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Constant Current Limit Magnitude

**Setup:** A sense resistor $R_S$ is placed between the regulator output node $V_{OUT}$ and the load terminal $V_L$. The base–emitter junction of the sense transistor $Q_2$ is connected directly across $R_S$ (with proper polarity).

**Step 1.** Apply Ohm's law across $R_S$ when current-limit just begins:

$$V_{R_S} = I_L \cdot R_S$$

**Step 2.** $Q_2$ turns on when its base–emitter voltage reaches the cut-in voltage:

$$V_{R_S} = V_{BE2(on)} = 0.6 \, \text{V}$$

**Step 3.** Solve for the limiting current:

$$I_{L(\max)} \cdot R_S = 0.6$$

$$\boxed{\,I_{L(\max)} = \dfrac{V_{BE}}{R_S} = \dfrac{0.6}{R_S}\,}$$

**Worked Check:** For $R_S = 0.5 \, \Omega$, $I_{L(\max)} = 0.6 / 0.5 = 1.2$ A. ✔

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Foldback $I_{L(\max)}$ and $I_{SC}$

**Topology (adopt this on your answer sheet):**
- $R_1$ is wired from the regulator output node $V_{OUT}$ to the base of $Q_2$.
- $R_2$ is wired from the base of $Q_2$ to the load terminal $V_L$.
- $R_S$ is in series between $V_{OUT}$ and $V_L$.
- The emitter of $Q_2$ is tied to the load terminal $V_L$.

**Step 1 — Voltage at the base of $Q_2$ (node analysis):**
Applying KCL at the base node (no base current assumption; $I_B \approx 0$):

$$\dfrac{V_{OUT} - V_{B2}}{R_1} = \dfrac{V_{B2} - V_L}{R_2}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $V_{B2}$:**

$$R_2 (V_{OUT} - V_{B2}) = R_1 (V_{B2} - V_L)$$

$$R_2 V_{OUT} + R_1 V_L = V_{B2} (R_1 + R_2)$$

$$V_{B2} = \dfrac{R_2 \, V_{OUT} + R_1 \, V_L}{R_1 + R_2}$$

**Step 3 — Express $V_{BE2}$:**

$$V_{BE2} = V_{B2} - V_L = \dfrac{R_2 \, V_{OUT} + R_1 \, V_L}{R_1 + R_2} - V_L$$

**Step 4 — Simplify using $V_{OUT} - V_L = I_L R_S$:**

$$V_{BE2} = \dfrac{R_2 (V_{OUT} - V_L)}{R_1 + R_2} = \dfrac{R_2 \, I_L \, R_S}{R_1 + R_2}$$

**Step 5 — Impose the turn-on condition $V_{BE2} = V_{BE}$:**

$$\dfrac{R_2 \, I_L \, R_S}{R_1 + R_2} = V_{BE}$$

$$I_L = \dfrac{V_{BE} (R_1 + R_2)}{R_2 \, R_S}$$

This is the *constant-current* result — the divider must therefore be tied differently to obtain foldback. Re-route $R_2$ from the base of $Q_2$ to **ground** instead of $V_L$ (the standard foldback topology):

**Step 1′ — Re-derived with $R_2$ to ground:**

$$V_{B2} = \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} \cdot V_{OUT}$$

**Step 2′ — Base–emitter voltage:**

$$V_{BE2} = V_{B2} - V_L = \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} V_{OUT} - V_L$$

**Step 3′ — Substitute $V_L = V_{OUT} - I_L R_S$:**

$$V_{BE2} = \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} V_{OUT} - V_{OUT} + I_L R_S = I_L R_S - \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} V_{OUT}$$

**Step 4′ — Set $V_{BE2} = V_{BE}$ and solve for $I_L$ (the *operating* locus of the foldback curve):**

$$I_L = \dfrac{V_{BE}}{R_S} + \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} \cdot \dfrac{V_{OUT}}{R_S}$$

**Step 5′ — Evaluate at the two anchor points of the characteristic:**

**(a) Knee point** — regulator still in regulation, $V_{OUT} = V_{NOM}$:

$$\boxed{\,I_{L(\max)} = \dfrac{V_{BE}}{R_S} + \dfrac{R_1 \, V_{NOM}}{R_S \,(R_1 + R_2)}\,}$$

**(b) Short-circuit point** — load terminal is at ground, $V_L = 0$, so $V_{OUT} = I_{SC} R_S$. Substituting back into Step 4′:

$$V_{BE} = I_{SC} R_S - \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} \cdot I_{SC} R_S = I_{SC} R_S \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

$$\boxed{\,I_{SC} = \dfrac{V_{BE} \, (R_1 + R_2)}{R_2 \, R_S}\,}$$

**Step 6 — Verify foldback condition $I_{SC} < I_{L(\max)}$:**

$$\dfrac{V_{BE} (R_1 + R_2)}{R_2 R_S} < \dfrac{V_{BE}}{R_S} + \dfrac{R_1 V_{NOM}}{R_S (R_1 + R_2)}$$

Multiply by $R_S (R_1 + R_2)$:

$$V_{BE} (R_1 + R_2)^2 / R_2 < V_{BE} (R_1 + R_2) + R_1 V_{NOM}$$

Rearranging yields the **design rule of thumb**:

$$\dfrac{R_1}{R_2} < \dfrac{V_{NOM} - V_{BE}}{V_{BE}}$$

For $V_{NOM} = 12$ V, $V_{BE} = 0.6$ V: $R_1 / R_2 < 19$.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Power Dissipation Comparison

**Step 1.** Worst-case dissipation in CC limiter (at $V_L = 0$, $I_L = I_{L(\max)}$):

$$P_{D,CC} = (V_{IN} - V_{OUT}) \cdot I_L \big\vert_{V_{OUT} = 0} = V_{IN} \cdot I_{L(\max)}$$

**Step 2.** Worst-case dissipation in Foldback limiter (at $V_L = 0$, $I_L = I_{SC}$):

$$P_{D,FB} = V_{IN} \cdot I_{SC}$$

**Step 3.** Ratio:

$$\dfrac{P_{D,FB}}{P_{D,CC}} = \dfrac{I_{SC}}{I_{L(\max)}} < 1$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Example:** If $I_{SC} = 0.4 \, I_{L(\max)}$, the foldback pass transistor runs at **40 %** of the CC-limiter dissipation. The heat-sink can be downsized by ~60 % — a major cost saving in high-current industrial SMPS.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Architecture — Protected Series Regulator

The Mermaid diagram below maps the *signal and power flow* through a typical protected series regulator. This is the diagram examiners expect to see in Part B answers.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA[DC Input Vin] --> passA[Pass Element Q1]
    passA --> senseA[Current Sense Resistor RS]
    senseA --> outA[Output Node Vout]
    outA --> sampA[Sampling Network R1 R2]
    sampA --> errA[Error Amplifier]
    refA[Reference Vref] --> errA
    errA --> passA
    senseA --> limA[Sense Transistor Q2 with Divider]
    limA --> passA
    outA --> loadA[Load RL]
    loadA --> gndA[Ground]

    classDef power fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#c33,color:#000
    classDef ctrl  fill:#e2ecfd,stroke:#36c,color:#000
    classDef ground fill:#222,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    class srcA,passA,senseA,outA,loadA power
    class sampA,errA,refA,limA ctrl
    class gndA ground
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- *Red nodes* = power path ($V_{IN} \to V_{OUT}$).
- *Blue nodes* = control path (feedback + current limit override).
- The blue path from `limA` to `passA` is the **override line** that collapses the pass transistor's drive when the current limit fires.

### 4.2 Operating State Machine — Regulator Behaviour

The regulator cycles through three states as $I_L$ changes. This is the conceptual map behind the V-I characteristic curve.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Normal
    Normal --> Knee: IL reaches ILmax and VBE2 turns Q2 on
    Knee --> Foldback: Vout decreases, VBE divider reduces, IL drops
    Foldback --> ShortCircuit: Vout to zero, IL stabilises at ISC
    ShortCircuit --> Foldback: Fault cleared, Vout recovers
    Foldback --> Knee: IL rises along curved locus
    Knee --> Normal: Vout reaches Vnom, Q2 turns off
    ShortCircuit --> Normal: Hard restart via power cycle
```

### 4.3 V-I Characteristic Curves (Rendered Schematic)

The figure below renders the **V-I output characteristic** of both protection methods. Although Mermaid cannot plot Cartesian curves directly, the flowchart uses ASCII-style description boxes with the actual numerical envelopes so the student can re-draw them in the exam hall.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CC["CONSTANT CURRENT LIMITING"]
        cc1[Region 1: Vout = Vnom, IL varies from 0 to ILmax]
        cc2[Region 2: IL = ILmax constant, Vout drops from Vnom to 0]
        cc1 --> cc2
    end

    subgraph FB["FOLDBACK LIMITING"]
        fb1[Region 1: Vout = Vnom, IL varies from 0 to ILmax]
        fb2[Region 2: Curved locus - both Vout and IL decrease]
        fb3[Region 3: Short circuit - Vout = 0, IL = ISC where ISC less than ILmax]
        fb1 --> fb2
        fb2 --> fb3
    end

    noteCC[Note: pass transistor dissipation at short is Pmax = Vin x ILmax]
    noteFB[Note: pass transistor dissipation at short is Pshort = Vin x ISC much less than Pmax]
    CC -.- noteCC
    FB -.- noteFB
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Drawing Tip:** When asked for the V-I characteristic, always draw *both* curves on the same axes: horizontal axis = $I_L$, vertical axis = $V_{OUT}$. Mark the knee $(I_{L(\max)}, V_{NOM})$ and the short-circuit point $(I_{SC}, 0)$. The foldback curve must clearly lie *below* the constant-current locus in the $V_{OUT} < V_{NOM}$ region.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**Q1. What is short-circuit protection in a DC regulator? Why is it necessary?**

**Model Answer:**
Short-circuit protection (SCP) is a current-limiting feature built into regulated DC power supplies. It prevents the load current from exceeding a safe maximum $I_{L(\max)}$ when the output terminals are shorted ($V_L = 0$ V). It is necessary because, without SCP, the pass transistor would dissipate $P_D = V_{CE} \cdot I_C$ far beyond its safe operating area (SOA), leading to thermal runaway, secondary breakdown, and permanent destruction of the regulator. SCP is implemented using a current-sense resistor $R_S$ and a sense transistor that overrides the error amplifier during fault conditions. *(3 marks)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary]**

**Q2. Distinguish between constant current limiting and foldback current limiting.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Constant Current Limiting | Foldback Current Limiting |
|---|---|---|
| Behaviour at short circuit | $I_L$ stays at $I_{L(\max)}$ | $I_L$ reduces to $I_{SC} < I_{L(\max)}$ |
| V-I shape | Rectangular knee | Re-entrant (curved foldback) |
| Pass-transistor dissipation at fault | Maximum: $V_{IN} \cdot I_{L(\max)}$ | Lower: $V_{IN} \cdot I_{SC}$ |
| Circuit complexity | Simple (one transistor + $R_S$) | Complex (divider + sense transistor) |
| Application | Low-power, cost-sensitive supplies | High-power industrial / lab supplies |

*(3 marks — awarding scheme: 1 mark per correct contrasting row × 3 rows)*

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **Choose ONE of the two alternatives below. Each carries 7 + 7 = 14 marks.**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — KTU University Exam Pattern, Dec 2022**

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a series voltage regulator employing **constant current short-circuit protection**. Derive the expression for the maximum load current $I_{L(\max)}$ in terms of the sense resistor $R_S$ and the base–emitter voltage $V_{BE}$.  *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Circuit Description (2 marks):**
The regulator consists of a pass transistor $Q_1$ (NPN Darlington), a Zener reference $V_Z$, an error amplifier formed by $Q_3$, a sampling divider $R_A$–$R_B$, and a current-sense sub-system comprising resistor $R_S$ in series with the load and an NPN sense transistor $Q_2$. The base of $Q_2$ is connected to the regulator side of $R_S$ and its emitter to the load side, so that $V_{BE2} = V_{R_S} = I_L R_S$.

**Step 2 — Normal Operation (1 mark):**
For $I_L < I_{L(\max)}$, $V_{R_S} = I_L R_S < 0.6$ V → $Q_2$ is OFF → the regulator behaves conventionally, holding $V_{OUT} = V_{NOM}$ via the feedback loop.

**Step 3 — Current Limit Action (2 marks):**
When the load is reduced, $I_L$ rises. As soon as $I_L R_S$ reaches $V_{BE(on)} = 0.6$ V, $Q_2$ turns ON, diverting base drive away from $Q_1$. The pass transistor desaturates, $V_{OUT}$ collapses, and the load voltage falls toward zero while $I_L$ is held constant at the limiting value.

**Step 4 — Derivation (2 marks):**
At the boundary of current limit:

$$V_{R_S} = V_{BE(on)}$$

$$I_{L(\max)} \cdot R_S = V_{BE(on)}$$

$$\boxed{\,I_{L(\max)} = \dfrac{V_{BE(on)}}{R_S} = \dfrac{0.6}{R_S}\,}$$

---

**(b)** Explain the principle of **foldback current limiting** with a circuit diagram and characteristic curve. Derive the expressions for $I_{L(\max)}$ and $I_{SC}$, and state the design condition that ensures $I_{SC} < I_{L(\max)}$.  *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Topology (1 mark):**
Foldback adds the resistor divider $R_1$ (from $V_{OUT}$) and $R_2$ (to ground) feeding the base of $Q_2$. The emitter of $Q_2$ sits at the load terminal. Thus the base voltage of $Q_2$ becomes a function of $V_{OUT}$, not just of $I_L$.

**Step 2 — Operating Equation (2 marks):**
Following the derivation of Section 3.2 (Step 4′):

$$I_L = \dfrac{V_{BE}}{R_S} + \dfrac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} \cdot \dfrac{V_{OUT}}{R_S}$$

**Step 3 — Knee Point (1 mark):** Substituting $V_{OUT} = V_{NOM}$:

$$I_{L(\max)} = \dfrac{V_{BE}}{R_S} + \dfrac{R_1 V_{NOM}}{R_S (R_1 + R_2)}$$

**Step 4 — Short-Circuit Point (2 marks):** Setting $V_L = 0$ so that $V_{OUT} = I_{SC} R_S$ and substituting back:

$$I_{SC} = \dfrac{V_{BE} (R_1 + R_2)}{R_2 R_S}$$

**Step 5 — Design Condition (1 mark):**
The foldback requirement $I_{SC} < I_{L(\max)}$ reduces to:

$$\dfrac{R_1}{R_2} < \dfrac{V_{NOM} - V_{BE}}{V_{BE}}$$

For $V_{NOM} = 12$ V, $V_{BE} = 0.6$ V: $R_1 / R_2 < 19$. The V-I characteristic is a re-entrant arc from $(I_{L(\max)}, V_{NOM})$ curving down to $(I_{SC}, 0)$ with $I_{SC} < I_{L(\max)}$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — KTU University Exam Pattern, May 2023**

**(a)** A series regulator uses **foldback current limiting** with $R_S = 0.2 \, \Omega$, $R_1 = 1.0 \, \text{k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 100 \, \Omega$, $V_{BE} = 0.6$ V, and nominal output $V_{NOM} = 10$ V. Calculate:
(i) the maximum load current $I_{L(\max)}$,
(ii) the short-circuit current $I_{SC}$,
(iii) the pass-transistor dissipation at the short-circuit point if $V_{IN} = 15$ V.  *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — $I_{L(\max)}$ (3 marks):**
Using the foldback formula:

$$I_{L(\max)} = \dfrac{V_{BE}}{R_S} + \dfrac{R_1 \, V_{NOM}}{R_S \, (R_1 + R_2)}$$

$$I_{L(\max)} = \dfrac{0.6}{0.2} + \dfrac{1000 \times 10}{0.2 \times 1100}$$

$$I_{L(\max)} = 3.0 + \dfrac{10000}{220} = 3.0 + 45.45$$

$$\boxed{\,I_{L(\max)} = 48.45 \, \text{A}\,}$$

**Step 2 — $I_{SC}$ (2 marks):**

$$I_{SC} = \dfrac{V_{BE} (R_1 + R_2)}{R_2 R_S} = \dfrac{0.6 \times 1100}{100 \times 0.2} = \dfrac{660}{20}$$

$$\boxed{\,I_{SC} = 33 \, \text{A}\,}$$

**Step 3 — Power Dissipation (2 marks):**

$$P_D = V_{IN} \cdot I_{SC} = 15 \times 33$$

$$\boxed{\,P_D = 495 \, \text{W}\,}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls in Foldback Numerical Problems:**
> 1. **Mixing up the two formulas.** Students often interchange the $I_{L(\max)}$ and $I_{SC}$ equations, swapping $R_1$ and $(R_1 + R_2)$. *Always* confirm: $I_{SC}$ uses the factor $(R_1 + R_2) / R_2$, which is greater than 1, so $I_{SC}$ *alone* would exceed $I_{L(\max)}$ *without* the subtraction term — the division happens *only at the knee*; the $I_{SC}$ value comes from a different limiting condition (output shorted).
> 2. **Ignoring the $V_{BE}/R_S$ term in $I_{L(\max)}$.** Examiners *specifically* test whether you remembered the base-emitter offset. Dropping it costs 1 mark.
> 3. **Unit mismatch on $R_S$.** Convert milliohms to ohms consistently. A 200 m$\Omega$ sense resistor is $R_S = 0.2$ $\Omega$, not $0.2$ m$\Omega$.
> 4. **Forgetting the foldback condition.** If $R_1 / R_2 \ge (V_{NOM} - V_{BE})/V_{BE}$, the circuit degenerates to constant-current limiting — examiners will deduct 1 mark if you do not check the inequality.

---

**(b)** Compare constant current and foldback current limiting with respect to (i) V-I characteristic, (ii) short-circuit current, (iii) pass-transistor power dissipation, (iv) circuit complexity. State **two practical applications** where foldback is preferred.  *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | Constant Current | Foldback |
|---|---|---|
| (i) V-I curve | Rectangular knee | Re-entrant curve |
| (ii) $I_{SC}$ | Equals $I_{L(\max)}$ | Less than $I_{L(\max)}$ |
| (iii) $P_D$ at fault | $V_{IN} I_{L(\max)}$ (worst case) | $V_{IN} I_{SC}$ (much less) |
| (iv) Complexity | One transistor + $R_S$ | Plus $R_1, R_2$ divider |

*(1 mark per row × 4 = 4 marks)*

**Two practical applications where foldback is preferred (1.5 marks each = 3 marks):**

1. **Laboratory bench power supplies:** A shorted test lead during prototyping must not destroy the supply. Foldback limits both current *and* dissipation, allowing continuous short-circuit survival (e.g., Agilent E3631A, Rigol DP832).

2. **Telecom -48 V rectifier plants:** A sustained short on a distribution feeder would otherwise weld connector pins and trip upstream breakers. Foldback lets the plant *current-limit* gracefully while the battery plant and load fuse coordinate protection.

> [!WARNING]
> **General KTU Mark-Loss Traps Across This Topic:**
> 1. Drawing the V-I characteristic *without axes labels* ($I_L$ on x-axis, $V_{OUT}$ on y-axis): −1 mark.
> 2. Failing to *state the assumption* $I_B \approx 0$ in the divider analysis: −0.5 mark.
> 3. Omitting the *design condition* $R_1 / R_2 < (V_{NOM} - V_{BE}) / V_{BE}$ in foldback derivations: −1 mark.
> 4. Confusing the "knee point" coordinates: it is $(I_{L(\max)}, V_{NOM})$, *not* $(I_{L(\max)}, 0)$. The short-circuit point is $(I_{SC}, 0)$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (last 60 seconds before the exam):**

- **Definition (1 line):** SCP is a current-limiting mechanism that prevents the load current from exceeding a safe maximum during a short-circuit fault.
- **Constant Current Limit:** $I_{L(\max)} = V_{BE} / R_S$. $I_{SC} = I_{L(\max)}$. Rectangular V-I curve. Worst-case dissipation at short = $V_{IN} I_{L(\max)}$.
- **Foldback Limit:** Two equations — at the *knee* ($V_{OUT} = V_{NOM}$) and at the *short* ($V_L = 0$). $I_{SC} < I_{L(\max)}$. Re-entrant V-I curve. Dissipation at short = $V_{IN} I_{SC} \ll V_{IN} I_{L(\max)}$.
- **Design rule for foldback:** $\dfrac{R_1}{R_2} < \dfrac{V_{NOM} - V_{BE}}{V_{BE}}$.
- **Key topology detail:** $R_2$ of the divider is tied to **ground** (not to $V_L$) for foldback; tying it to $V_L$ yields plain constant-current limiting.
- **V-I curve must show:** axes labelled, knee point $(I_{L(\max)}, V_{NOM})$, short-circuit point $(I_{SC}, 0)$, and the curved locus between them.
- **Engineering trade-off:** Foldback has higher circuit complexity but ~40–80 % lower fault dissipation — preferred for high-current / mission-critical supplies.
- **Standard KTU numerical sub-questions ask for:** $I_{L(\max)}$, $I_{SC}$, design condition check, and $P_D$ at the fault.
- **Bloom's-tag this topic:** *Apply* (numerical design), *Analyse* (V-I curve behaviour), *Evaluate* (CC vs Foldback choice). KTU Part A typically tests *Remember/Understand*; Part B tests *Apply/Analyse*.
- **Course Outcome (CO) mapping:** PCAET302 / Module 4 / CO4 — *"Design regulated power supplies with over-current and short-circuit protection."*
